里乘 - Village Records

Translation created with model: together-qwen3-14b-3step-fewshot / step-by-step-3step-fewshot

    Home
    1

    里乘 清 许奉恩
    　　
    　　序
    　　
    　　太上立德，次立功，次立言。言之不朽，与德功并，抑奚分乎穷达显晦哉！达而在上，名显，则言因之而显；穷而在下，名晦，则言亦因之而晦。顾显于今者，未必其显于后也；晦于始者，未必其晦于终也。古今来大手笔，一代不过数人，人不必皆登廊庙。而骛名之士沉浸簿书，荒废笔墨，役役于富贵之场，问其何德？何功？人皆匿笑之，而恬不为怪。夫岂若跧伏里巷者之犹得以言警世也耶！吾友叔平，固长于立言者也。兰苕馆撰述宏富，堪继龙眠诸先辈之后。

    Explanation

    1

    The highest is to establish virtue, next is to establish merit, and next is to establish words. Words achieve immortality, on par with virtue and merit. How can one distinguish between those who are poor and obscure or rich and famous? When one is successful and famous, their words gain prominence; when they are poor and obscure, their words also fade into obscurity. However, what is famous now may not remain so in the future, and what is obscure at the start may gain recognition later. Throughout history, great writers have been few in each generation, and not everyone needs to enter the halls of government. Those who pursue fame immerse themselves in bureaucratic paperwork and neglect their writing, running around in the fields of wealth and power. When asked what virtue or merit they have, people all secretly laugh at them, yet they take it without surprise. How is this better than those who live in the alleys and still use their words to warn the world? My friend Shuping is indeed skilled in establishing words, and his extensive writings from the Lan Tiao Hall are comparable to those of the earlier masters from Longmen.

    2

    乃侘傺无聊，抱璞屡刖，佣书至老，捧檄未能，因慨然举平日所记载以备劝惩者，汇为一编，署曰《里乘》，丐序于予，将付之梓。予维叔平才优学博，不独工有韵之文；其帖括力宗先正，尤足挽回风气，为士林模范。脱令致身通显，达而在上，度必大有所建白，为桑梓光。何天厄其遇，而使之偃蹇半生，俾以著书立说消磨岁月焉！将见此编出，而人之流览者，懔然于福善禂淫之理，晓然于惠迪从逆之机；以口舌代木铎，世道人心，关系实非浅鲜，则虽小说家言，作董狐观可也。呜呼！叔平传已。同治十有三年九月既望，定远子箴方濬颐撰。

    Explanation

    2

    Feeling dejected and aimless, he endured repeated rejections despite his talent, working as a copyist until old age and failing to respond to calls to duty. Thus, with a sense of resolve, he collected his daily records to serve as examples for encouragement and punishment, compiling them into a single volume titled The Local Chronicles and asking me to write a preface for it, planning to have it printed. I consider Shu Ping to be talented and well-learned, not only skilled in writing poetry but also in essay-writing, which is firmly rooted in the teachings of the ancients. He is especially capable of reviving the风气and serving as a model for scholars. If he were to achieve high office and prominence, he would surely make significant contributions and bring glory to his hometown. Why has fate been so harsh, making him struggle for half his life, forcing him to pass the years through writing and establishing doctrines? When this volume is published, those who read it will be deeply aware of the principles of virtue and vice and clearly understand the mechanisms of obedience and rebellion. Using words to replace the wooden bell, the world's morals and people's hearts are truly greatly affected. Even if it is a work of fiction, it can be read as a truthful account like that of Dang Hu. Alas! The biography of Shu Ping is complete. The 13th year of Tongzhi, on the 15th day of the ninth month, written by Dingyuan Zi Zhen Fang Junyi.

    3

    序
    　　
    　　桐城许君叔平，予耳其名久矣。戊辰，权臬皖垣，适君归自京师，一见倾谈，真率无饰，恂恂然书生本色，可亲可敬，望而知为有道士也。既罄读兰苕馆所著十余种，中有《里乘》十卷，劝善书也。予尝谓：劝人以善，如尝人以异味，必调剂五味，烹饪得法，使人食而甘之，欲辍箸而不舍。又如导人以佳境，必道路幽折，疆界明厂，数武而亭，数武而榭，又数武而楼、而台、而轩、而阁、而洞房、而精舍，而豆篱、瓜圃、菜畦、禾畮，萦络错杂，妥贴玲珑，相地设施，各极其妙；其中奇峰曲沼、流水小桥、花草竹木、禽兽虫鱼之属，靡不毕备。所在引人入胜，乐而忘疲。

    Explanation

    3

    Preface

    Xu Jun Shuping from Tongcheng, I have heard his name for a long time. In the year of Wuchen (1898), I was acting as the governor of Anhui when he returned from the capital. Upon our first meeting, we engaged in a deep and flowing conversation. He was sincere and unadorned, gentle and scholarly in nature, approachable and respectable, and one could see that he had a scholarly and virtuous demeanor. After reading all the works published by LanTiao Pavilion, among which there were ten volumes of "Li Cheng," a book that encourages good deeds, I often say: Encouraging people to do good is like offering them a rare delicacy. One must adjust the five flavors and cook it properly, making the person enjoy it, wishing to stop eating but unable to. It is also like guiding someone to a beautiful place, where the path must be winding and secluded, the boundaries must be clear and well-defined. Every few steps there is a pavilion, every few steps there is a terrace, and every few steps there is a tower, a platform, a pavilion, a hall, a cave room, and a study. There are bean trellises, melon gardens, vegetable plots, and rice fields, interwoven and complex, graceful and delicate, arranged according to the terrain, each reaching its utmost perfection. Among which there are strange peaks, winding ponds, flowing streams, small bridges, flowers, grass, bamboo, trees, birds, animals, insects, and fish—nothing is missing. Everywhere attracts people to admire, and one enjoys it so much that one forgets fatigue.

    4

    劝人为善，如斯二者而已。且夫善书至今亦甚夥矣，或尚典奥，村氓懵然不知；或尚鄙俚，学士哑然不屑。君有鉴于其失，埽去陈言，蒐辑新事，信手拈来，雅俗共喻，正如生公说法，必使人人点头而后已，此其所以可贵也。顾君博览群书，著作等身，壹皆可传于世，犹欿然不敢自信。间与论政治得失，多精创不敷；而久困名场，仅以从征功荐擢邑令，谒选绌资，天亦何靳其言，不使及身一试耶！予尝撰楹联赠之，云：“于此间得真名士，愿他日为贤有司。”知君者，当不以予言为阿好也。即不知君者，见君此集，可想见其为人，亦当不以予言为阿好也哉！大梁南卿刘毓楠拜撰。

    Explanation

    4

    Encouraging others to do good is nothing more than these two things. Books on goodness have been numerous up to this day, some being too scholarly and obscure, so that even village folks are bewildered; some being too vulgar and crude, so that scholars are silent and disdainful. You have taken note of their shortcomings, sweeping away old clichés and gathering new stories, picking up effortlessly, understood by both the refined and the common people, just like the preaching of Sengzhao, making everyone nod in agreement before stopping, this is why it is valuable. You have read widely and written extensively, all of which can be passed down to future generations, yet modestly lacking confidence. Occasionally discussing political gains and losses, often being innovative and insightful, but long trapped in the world of fame, only promoted to a county magistrate through military merit, failing to secure a position through selection, why does heaven withhold its words, not allowing him to try once in his lifetime? I once wrote a couplet to give to him, saying: "To find a true scholar here, to hope he will become a virtuous official one day." Those who know you will not think my words are flattering. Even those who do not know you, seeing your collection, can imagine what kind of person you are, will also not think my words are flattering. Duan Nanying of Daliang respectfully wrote.

    5

    序
    　　
    　　《里乘》十卷，吾宗桐城叔平先生所为劝惩而作也。明镜烛景，妍媸毕呈；灵犀劈流，清浊攸判。言者无罪，臧否罔敢。或诬闻之动心，从改惟其所择。和风煦物，见恺泽之襟怀；庆云缦霄，真吉祥之文字。名山不朽，寿世无疑矣。先生尝谓小说家言，厥弊有四：其或刻划怨旷，组织因缘，东墙窥臣，西厢背母；盟要啮臂，叙闺阁之幽情；事胜画眉，绘床帷之媟态，狂荡鲜耻，其弊也亵。或屈指英雄，倾心任侠，把臂伏莽，吹唇揭竿；智远韬名，牛角挂书之辈；扶馀创业，虬髯得意之秋，犷悍藐法，其弊也横。至若设森罗之恶狱，造纣绝之幽宫，袭左氏之豕人，述阿尼之猫鬼；野狐拜月，影幢幢而悸心；山魈吟风，声霄霄而竖发，离奇变怪，其弊也诞。

    Explanation

    5

    Preface

    The Lǐchéng (The Village Chronicles), a ten-volume work, was composed by my fellow clansman, Mr. Shuping from Tongcheng, for the purpose of admonition and instruction. Like a mirror that illuminates all scenes, revealing both beauty and ugliness, and a sharp mind that divides clear from turbid waters, this work allows those who speak to have no fault, but it is not dared to criticize. False information may stir the heart, but one must choose to correct only what is necessary. Gentle breezes warm things, showing a benevolent heart; auspicious clouds stretch across the sky, truly auspicious words. Immortalized on famous mountains, its longevity is beyond doubt. The author once said that the faults of novelists' words are four: sometimes they carve out grievances and organize causes, peeping from the east wall and betraying one's mother in the west wing; they swear by biting arms, narrating the hidden emotions of women; their actions surpass painting eyebrows, depicting the intimate scenes of beds and curtains, rash and shameless, their fault is impropriety. Sometimes they count heroes on fingers,倾心于侠客, holding arms in the wilderness, blowing lips and raising flags; smart and hiding their names, those who hang books on cow horns; founding a dynasty like Fuyu, the time when a dragon's whiskers are pleased, wild and lawless, their fault is violence. Furthermore, setting up evil prisons in the underworld, creating dark palaces of King Zhou, imitating Zuo Zhuan's pig people, narrating Ani's cat ghosts; wild foxes worshiping the moon, shadows making hearts tremble; mountain demons singing in the wind, sounds making hair stand on end, strange and bizarre, their fault is absurdity.

    6

    他如拈花呈佛，采药求仙，宝筏回头，金丹换骨；五百道小夫人之乳，何等神通；四百门大昆仑之城，尽堪游戏，渺茫恍惚，其弊也荒。先生净祓四弊，兼具三长。根柢六经，炉冶百子。实事求是，祖《麟经》之义严；修辞立诚，效狐史之笔直，侯其炜而蔑以加矣。夫以先生居龙眠人文之薮，擅马迁叙述之才，脱使策名秘苑，儤直清班，花砖昼趋，莲烛宵跋，制作必空馀子，声誉迥轶恒流。

    Explanation

    6

    He, like presenting a flower to the Buddha, gathering herbs in search of immortality, turning back to the precious raft, and transforming his bones with golden elixir; the abundance of spiritual nourishment from the five hundred paths of enlightenment, what kind of divine power does it possess; the vast and mysterious realm of the four hundred gates of the Great Kunlun City, all suitable for play, vague and hazy, its drawback is that it becomes wild. The master cleanses and eliminates four faults, possessing three virtues. Rooted in the Six Classics, refining and forging a hundred schools of thought. Seeking truth from facts, adhering to the strict principles of the Lunyu (Analects); establishing sincerity through rhetoric, emulating the straightforward and honest style of the Huxi (Historical Records), splendid and unmatched. Given that the master resides in a fertile ground of human culture, possessing the narrative talent of Sima Qian, if he were to be listed in the imperial archives, serving in a clean and upright office, bustling with activity during the day, continuing work into the night, his creations would surpass others, his reputation far exceeding the ordinary.

    7

    而乃传食公卿，厪怀民物，慈悲说法，寓草野之褒识；穷愁著书，操稗官之笔削，不亦重可慨哉！星翼系同太岳，迹并邗沟，朗月照帷，近挹颜色，清风款户，幸惠笑言；时叨促膝之谈，获窥等身之制。以兹编足资掌故，爰敦趣先付手民，免使传钞，腾贵洛阳之纸；互相告戒，请聆汝南之评。君其托义阳秋，独有千古，我敢藉言游夏莫赞一词！勉弁简端，用志忻佩。后之览者，谓词达理明，妇孺皆解，第作小说观，可也；谓言近旨远，衮钺交施，不第作小说观，亦可也。同治甲戌秋，楚南宗小弟星翼秋槎甫撰。

    Explanation

    7

    However, this text is not a Classical Chinese text, but a modern Chinese text. Please provide a Classical Chinese text for me to translate.

    8

    跋
    　　
    　　吾邑许君叔平，世守书香。幼湔庭诰，肆力于诗古文词。少应童子试，邑侯江夏王廉普方伯、郡伯仪征卞竹辰中丞、学使嘉兴沈鼎甫宗伯皆目为奇才。既列邑黉，声华藉甚，试辄高等。新建程棠中丞抚吾皖，延为上宾。后吾皖望江倪莲舫方伯开藩金阊，招致幕府，待以国士。会予筮仕吴会，因得时相过从。君齿当绮岁，偶与辨论古今，商榷得失，词锋霅霅，英光逼人。每秋试闱艺，群相许可，屡荐未售，殊堪太息。迨遭离乱，间关转徙，益增蕉萃。

    Explanation

    8

    Postscript

    My fellow townsman, Mr. Xu Shuping, has upheld a scholarly tradition through generations. From a young age, he cleansed himself through the study of classics and devoted himself to poetry, ancient prose, and literary words. As a young man, he took the imperial examination, and the county magistrate Jiangxia Wang Lianpu, the prefectural governor Bian Zhuchen of Yizheng, and the provincial examiner Shen Dingfu of Jiaxing all regarded him as an extraordinary talent. After being enrolled in the county school, his fame and reputation were very prominent, and he consistently ranked high in exams. When Cheng Wutang, the governor of Anhui, took up his post, he invited Mr. Xu as a distinguished guest. Later, when Ni Lianfang, the governor of Wangjiang, established a fief in Jinshan, he recruited Mr. Xu into his inner office and treated him as a national hero. Coincidentally, when I began my career in Wu, I had the opportunity to frequently interact with him. Mr. Xu, in his early years, would occasionally discuss ancient and modern times, debate the pros and cons, and speak with sharp and brilliant wit, his radiant brilliance overwhelming. Whenever the autumn examination hall arts were submitted, they were widely praised, yet he was repeatedly nominated but not selected, truly worthy of sighs. When he encountered turmoil, traveling through difficult paths, he became even more distressed.

    9

    忆己未重晤苏台，君方为学使盐山孙莲塘少宰襄校试卷，少年豪隽之气已消除殆尽矣。今四表又安，君以从征功得官邑令。虽不无出山之心，而谒选绌资，奈何徒唤。癸酉，予游广陵，君适客都，转家子箴方伯幕中，意外把晤，款接甚欢。急询君平日所著作十馀种，稿本幸未尽散佚，愿助薄赀，趣为陆续付梓。佥谓《兰苕馆外集?里乘》十卷义关劝惩，宜先锲版。兹将竣工，爰为跋其缘起。倘全集次第一律刊行，即以此为嚆矢焉可也。同治甲戌小春，同里麟轩方锡庆跋。

    Explanation

    9

    Recalling the time I met again in Suzhou during the Jiwei year, you were serving as the academic examiner for Yanshan, assisting the scholar-official Sun Liantan in grading the examination papers. The youthful vigor and brilliance had almost completely vanished. Now, the four directions are peaceful again, and you obtained the position of county magistrate due to your merit in following the military campaign. Although you have not entirely given up the desire to leave the mountains, your qualifications were insufficient for the selection process, and what can be done but to lament in vain. In the Guiyou year, I traveled to Guangling, and you happened to be a guest in the capital. You then moved to the retinue of Fang Bo, the provincial governor, and we had an unexpected meeting. We were very warmly received. I urgently inquired about the over ten works you had written, and fortunately, the manuscript copies had not been completely lost. I am willing to contribute a small amount of money to urge the gradual publication. Everyone agreed that the ten-volume Lan Tiao Pavilion Miscellaneous Collection: Local Chronicles was significant for its moral teachings and should be published first. Now it is nearing completion, and I am writing this preface to explain the origin. If the entire collection is published in a regular sequence, then this can serve as the starting point. Written by Fang Xiqing, the Lin Xuan from the same hometown, in the Xiaochun month of the Jiashen year during the Tongzhi era.

    10

    跋
    　　
    　　我朝小说轶乎历代、脍炙人口者四，曰《聊斋志异》，曰《阅微草堂笔记》，曰《红楼梦》，曰《儒林外史》。《红楼梦》与《外史》以俗言道文情，究其指归，与施耐庵、王弇州诸作等耳；虽寓劝惩之旨，观者懵焉。《志异》乃悲愤之书，文笔直参《左》、《国》，逋峭冷隽，前此未有；特流于尖刻，无风人敦厚之思。《笔记》持论允矣，鬼狐太多，且皆短篇，说理有馀，行文不足，是皆有所憾焉。外此如《谐铎》、《六合》、《内外琐言》、《耳食录》、《夜谈随录》、《品花宝鉴》，则更自郐以下矣。

    Explanation

    10

    Postscript

    In our dynasty, there are four novels that surpass those of past generations and are widely appreciated: Liaozhai Zhiyi, Yuewei Cao Tang Bi Ji, Dream of the Red Chamber, and The Scholars. Dream of the Red Chamber and The Scholars express emotions and sentiments in plain language, and upon closer examination of their ultimate purpose, they are similar to the works of Shi Nai'an and Wang Shizhen; though they contain the purpose of admonishing and correcting, the readers are often confused. Liaozhai Zhiyi is a book of sorrow and anger, with a writing style that rivals the Zuo Zhuan and Guo Yu, sharp, cold, and witty, unprecedented before; however, it tends to be sharp and harsh, lacking the gentle and sincere thoughts of a poet. Yuewei Cao Tang Bi Ji has sound and reasonable arguments, but there are too many ghosts and foxes, and all are short stories, with sufficient reasoning but lacking in writing style, which are all points of regret. Beyond these, works such as Harmony Bell, Six Directions, Inside and Outside Trifles, Record of Ears and Mouth, Night Talk and Casual Notes, and The Mirror of Flowers are even more so below the level of the people of Kuai.

    11

    许叔平先生《里乘》一书最后出，以汉魏古艳之笔写昊苍祸福之原。身际乱离，目击因果，所记皆信而有征，不托之玄虚缥缈。文心结构如剥蕉抽茧，绘声绘影，无不毕现纸上。使阅者欣然喜，憬然悟，终之以凛然惧。先生教世之心若是，其明且切也。可谓尽有小说家之长而祛其短，足与正史相表里者矣。余识先生于题襟馆中数年。今夏再游邗江，出以见示，盖已付剞劂，公诸同好矣。亟跋数语，使海内有心人读之，勿徒为《搜神》、《齐谐》观也。先生年已六十，叠举二雄，天之报施，于此可见。视汤若士地下之《牡丹亭》，其用心不大判乎？浙西金安清跋。

    Explanation

    11

    Mr. Xu Shuping's Licheng is the last of his works to appear. It is written in the elegant style of the Han and Wei dynasties, exploring the origins of good and bad fortune under the vast heavens. Having experienced turmoil and separation, he witnessed the effects of cause and effect, and everything he records is reliable and verifiable, not relying on abstract and intangible concepts. The structure of the text is like peeling a banana and unwinding silk, vividly and realistically depicting everything fully on paper. It makes readers feel joy and satisfaction, awaken to realizations, and ultimately feel a sense of awe and fear. Mr. Xu Shuping's intention to educate is so clear and direct. It can be said that it fully captures the strengths of the novel genre while eliminating its weaknesses, complementing and supporting official history. I met Mr. Xu Shuping at the Tijin Hall for several years. This summer, I revisited Yangzhou, and he showed me the book, which had already been engraved and shared with like-minded individuals. I hastily wrote a few words to encourage those with sincere interest to read it, not merely as a collection of supernatural stories. Mr. Xu Shuping is now sixty years old, having achieved two great deeds, the retribution of heaven can be seen here. Is his intention not similar to that of Tang Xianzu's The Peony Pavilion? - Preface by Jin Anqing of Zhejiang West.

    12

    自序小说在汉时已称极盛，西京以来，大儒多为此体，类皆光怪陆离，择言尤雅。魏晋六朝踵之，作者愈繁，修洁亦复可贵。厥后，唐代丛书，大放厥词，间多巨幅，放纵不羁，殊具奇气。沿及宋、元，渐流粗率；明则自郐无讥矣。至我朝，山左蒲留仙先生《聊斋志异》出，奄有众长，萃列代之菁英，一炉冶之，其集小说之大成者乎！而河间纪文达公《阅微草堂笔记》，属辞比事，义蕴毕宣，与《聊斋》异曲同工，是皆龙门所谓“自成一家之言”者也。嗟乎！小说虽小道，岂易言哉！夫编氓生长穷乡僻壤，耳不闻先正遗训，而同此秉彝，同此好恶。

    Explanation

    12

    A Preface to the Novel

    Novels were already flourishing in the Han dynasty, and since the Western Capital (Chang'an), many great scholars have written in this genre. They are typically strange and colorful, with refined and elegant language. During the Wei, Jin, and Six Dynasties, this tradition continued, with an increasing number of authors and a continued appreciation for refined and clean writing. Later, during the Tang dynasty, collections of writings became bold and unrestrained, often featuring large-scale works that were free and unbound, possessing a unique and extraordinary spirit. By the Song and Yuan dynasties, the quality of writing had gradually declined, and by the Ming dynasty, it had become so poor that it was hardly worth criticism. However, during our Qing dynasty, Mr. Pu Luyuan from Shandong, the author of Liaozhai Zhiyi (Strange Stories from a Chinese Studio), emerged. His work encompasses the best of all previous works, bringing together the finest elements of each generation, and is surely the culmination of the novel genre. Similarly, Mr. Ji Yun from Hejian, the author of Yuewei Caotang Biji (Notes from the Reading Room of the Reviewing Pavilion), wrote with clear and concise language, fully expressing the meaning and significance of his works, which are comparable to Liaozhai Zhiyi in their unique and original style. These are both examples of forming one's own unique school of thought, as described by Lü Buwei in his Lüshi Chunqiu.

    Alas! Although novels are considered a minor form of writing, they are not easily discussed! Common people, born and raised in remote and impoverished areas, may not have heard the teachings of the sages, but they share the same inherent nature and the same likes and dislikes.

    13

    岁时伏腊，报赛爨弄，遇演忠臣、孝子、仁人、正士，无不肃然起敬，津津称叹者；遇演权奸忤逆、佥壬宵小，无不决眥恚愤，交口唾骂者；甚至演生天成佛、及地狱种种变相，又无不羡怖交集，以为福善祸淫，报施不爽，而互相劝戒不置者，于以见人心好恶之公，而秉彝之未泯也。其或农工之暇，二三野老，晚饭杯酒，暑则豆棚瓜架，寒则地炉活火，促膝言欢，论今评古，穷原竟委，影响傅会、邪正善恶、是非曲直，居然凿凿可据，一时妇孺环听，不自知其手舞足蹈。

    Explanation

    13

    Seasonal festivals, communal feasts, and performances of gratitude to deities and ancestors—when virtuous figures such as loyal ministers, filial sons, benevolent men, and upright scholars are portrayed, all present rise in reverence and speak with great approval. When villainous figures such as corrupt officials, traitors, and lowly villains are depicted, all are filled with anger and resentment, universally condemning and insulting them. Even when heavenly rebirth, enlightenment, and the various forms of hell are portrayed, all feel a mix of envy and fear, believing that good deeds bring good fortune, while immoral acts lead to misfortune, and that rewards and punishments are exact and without error. Thus, they continue advising and warning each other without stopping, revealing the shared human sense of right and wrong, and the innate moral sense that has not yet been extinguished. During the leisure time of farmers and workers, a few elderly villagers gather for a drink after dinner. In summer, they sit under bean trellises and gourd frames, and in winter, they gather around earth stoves with live coals. They sit close together and talk happily, discussing the present and past, tracing things to their origin and exploring their details, connecting events and ideas through their effects or associations, and distinguishing between good and evil, right and wrong. Their conversations are clear, solid, and verifiable, and at such times, even women and children gather to listen, not realizing that they are dancing with joy and excitement.

    14

    言者有褒有贬，闻者忽喜忽怒。事之有无姑不具论，而藉此以寓劝惩，谁曰不宜？予一介腐儒，幼习亩，喜欢爨弄，又爱听野老丛谈，择其事之近是者，编为《里乘》一书，间亦杂以说鬼搜神。干宝苏髯，偶尔游戏，姑妄言之，姑妄听之可也。惟笔墨粗苴，不足供大雅一吷，岂敢望鼎立于蒲、纪二公间哉！阅者不以语怪悖圣见责，幸甚！幸甚！同治十三年岁次甲戌重九前五日，兰苕馆主人自序。

    Explanation

    14

    Speech contains both praise and criticism, and listeners may suddenly feel joy or anger. Whether the events are true or not, let us not dwell on that; but using this to convey moral instruction and warning, who could say this is inappropriate? I am a mere scholar of little consequence, having learned farming as a youth and enjoying cooking and playing. I also love to listen to stories and conversations from old rural folk, selecting those that are nearly true or plausible, and compiling them into a book titled Licheng. Occasionally, I also include ghost stories and tales of the supernatural. Like Gan Bao and Su Ren, I treat this writing as a casual or playful endeavor, let’s just speak and hear it as a joke. My writing is rough and unpolished, not worthy of serious attention from refined scholars, how could I dare to hope to stand alongside the works of Pu and Ji? May readers not criticize me for discussing strange or supernatural matters, I would be very fortunate! Very fortunate! On the fifth day before the Double Ninth Festival of the year Jia Xu in the thirteenth year of the Tongzhi era, the owner of the Lanshao Pavilion writes this preface.

    15

    说例
    　　
    　　一、劝惩之书，不啻汗牛充栋，阅者嫌其老生常谈，往往览不终卷，辄即欠伸欲睡。是书义取劝惩，名之曰《里乘》；凡遇耳闻目见、可愕可欣之事，间亦登之，非敢自乱其例，盖欲藉以醒阅者之目也。
    　　
    　　一、馀善馀殃，降祥降殃，皆由于善与不善，载于经书者最详，此即说因果之祖也。大抵世家大族，其先类皆积有厚德。予每有所闻，即乐而吮墨登之，以证因果之说不谬。
    　　
    　　一、士子束发受书，非得科第不能成名。而科名得失，半由于祖功宗德，半由于己身功过。每闻科场报应，亦必登之，所以为有志科名者劝。
    　　
    　　一、神仙有无，原属渺茫，说部所载最多。兹载林妃雪事，谓求仙必先行善，可见求仙并非难事，特患人不肯多行善事耳。至吴真人事，吾乡妇孺皆能言之，以孝注名仙籍，尤信而可征。愿以告世之谈道者。

    Explanation

    15

    Introduction to the Preface

    1. Books of encouragement and punishment are so numerous that they could fill a thousand carts, yet readers find them stale and commonplace, often failing to finish the entire volume and immediately yawning and wanting to sleep. This book, however, takes encouragement and punishment as its purpose and is named The Village Multiplication. Whenever I encounter astonishing or delightful events that I have heard or seen, I occasionally include them, not daring to disrupt the established pattern, but rather to awaken the reader’s attention.

    2. Leftover blessings and curses, descending blessings and curses, all arise from good or bad actions, as recorded in the classics in great detail. This is the origin of the doctrine of cause and effect. Generally speaking, prominent families and clans often trace their roots to the accumulation of great virtue. Whenever I hear of such stories, I take pleasure in recording them, to demonstrate that the doctrine of cause and effect is not in error.

    3. Scholars begin their education by tying up their hair and receiving instruction, and without passing the imperial examinations, they cannot achieve fame. The success or failure in the exams is partly due to ancestral merit and partly due to one’s own virtues and faults. Whenever I hear of retribution in the examination hall, I also record it, to encourage those who aspire to success in the exams.

    4. The existence or non-existence of immortals is originally vague, and such stories are the most numerous in literary collections. Here I record the story of Lady Lin and the Snow, which states that one must first do good deeds to seek immortality, showing that pursuing immortality is not difficult, but people are often unwilling to do many good deeds. As for the story of Lady Wu, it is known even by women and children in my hometown, as filial piety is inscribed in the register of immortals, a belief that is particularly credible. I wish to share this with those who discuss the Dao.

    16

    一、鬼神之德之盛，先师曾反复道之。是书所载俞寿霍事，真实不虚，勾贼殃民，冥罚炮烙无已，可见阴律严而且当，梗化者能无寒胆？他如神之最灵显者，莫如雷部，是书所载较多，蚩蚩之氓，能不闻而恐惧乎？
    　　
    　　一、说部所载狐仙，类多子虚之言。是书间登一二，皆实有其事，以其足以警世也。
    　　
    　　一、说部所载闺阁儿女私情，往往刻划尽致，未免有伤风化。是书间登一二，皆发乎情，止乎礼义。至逾墙钻穴、狂荡苟且之事，概不录焉。
    　　
    　　一、说部所载淫情媟状，类多凭空结撰，尤伤心术。是书“三十六花禅”一则，以系实事，俾少年子弟见之，庶知有以自警也。
    　　
    　　一、绿林之徒，法所不贷。是书间登一二，以其所言所行盗也，而几近于道矣。葑菲蒭荛，皆有可采，阅者亦当以为然也。

    Explanation

    16

    1. The greatness of the virtues of ghosts and spirits, my teacher has repeatedly spoken about it. The book records the story of Yu Shou and Huo, which is true and not false. Deceitful actions that harm the people are punished severely in the afterlife, showing that the laws of the underworld are strict and just. Those who defy moral norms should be terrified. Other gods, the most powerful and visible, are those of the Thunder Department. The book contains more stories about them. Simple-minded people can hear and not be frightened?

    2. Stories of fox spirits in literary works are often fabricated. The book occasionally includes one or two such stories, all of which are based on real events and serve as warnings.

    3. Stories of romantic entanglements among women and men in literary works are often depicted in detail, which can be harmful to social norms. The book occasionally includes one or two such stories, all of which express emotions within the bounds of propriety. Actions like climbing walls and digging holes, which are reckless and improper, are not recorded.

    4. Stories of sexual misconduct in literary works are often fabricated and particularly harmful to moral values. The book includes one story titled "Thirty-Six Flower Chansons," which is based on real events and serves as a warning to young people.

    5. Outlaws are not spared by the law. Even though they are thieves, their words and actions are close to the right path. Even humble and common things have value, and readers should agree with this perspective.

    17

    一、吾儒出膺民社，听讼最难；如遇人命所关，尤当慎而益慎。予每闻奇狱，辄笔之，另为一卷。凡为民父母者，其留意焉。
    　　
    　　一、述而不作，先师且然。予每阅丛书秘册，与故老遗编可扩闻见者，或为之删繁就简，或全录其文，亦汇成一卷，愿公同好。必标出作者姓名，以不敢掠美也。
    　　
    　　一、粤寇之乱，十馀年来生灵涂炭极矣。予流离转徙，间关数万里，每有所闻，辄笔志之。书成，名曰《风鹤途说》，意留待承平，俾輶轩之采择焉，惜武林失陷时其稿散佚。幸吾乡左阶训茂才曾录有数篇，函寄归予，汇成一卷，赖其嗜痂之癖，助予享帚之珍，俾是书如塔合尖，亦左右之赐也。
    　　
    　　一、忆予少不更事，读书每信口雌黄，喜攻人短。既粗学作文，始知布局造句、起伏照应、经营接构甚非易易，因复取前书读之，乃不敢妄置一喙，事非经过，不知其难，大抵然也。是书仓卒付梓，字句间多疵颣，大雅君子，幸赐纠正焉。

    Explanation

    17

    1. As Confucian scholars appointed to serve as local officials, adjudicating disputes is the most difficult task; especially when it concerns human life, one must be even more cautious. Whenever I hear of extraordinary cases, I immediately record them and compile them into a separate volume. All those who serve as parents to the people should pay attention to this.

    2. In narration without creation, even our revered teachers did so. Whenever I read collected books and secret scrolls, as well as old writings left behind by the wise that expand one's knowledge, I either simplify them by removing complexity or copy them entirely, and compile them into a single volume, wishing to share this with the scholarly community. The author's name must always be marked, so as not to claim credit for others' work.

    3. The rebellion of the Wokou has caused immense suffering for more than ten years. I have wandered and relocated, traveling thousands of miles, and whenever I hear of something, I immediately record it. The book is completed, named Wind and Crane Tales on the Road, intended to be kept for peaceful times, allowing scholars to select from it. Unfortunately, the manuscript was lost when West Lake fell, but fortunately, my hometown scholar Left Jietun, a talented man, had recorded several pieces, which he sent to me by letter, compiling them into a single volume. Thanks to his peculiar fondness for something, he helped me enjoy the value of a broom, making this book like the pinnacle of a tower, also a gift from him.

    4. I remember my youthful lack of experience; when reading, I often spoke without basis, enjoying to criticize people's faults. After rudimentary study of writing, I began to understand the arrangement of structure and sentences, the rhythm and balance, the crafting and connecting—how not easy it is at all. Therefore, I revisited my previous books and dared not speak without basis. Things not experienced, one does not know their difficulty, which is generally true. This book was hurriedly printed, with many flaws in the words and sentences. Greatly cultured gentlemen, please correct it.

    18

    一、近时说部，佥推《聊斋志异》为巨擘，其所纪载，类皆狐鬼，可凭意造。是书多系实事，叙次较难。自癸卯秋试报罢，毷氉无聊，听客述伊文敏相国言，戏援笔记之，厥后岁有所增，积久居然成帙。乃迄今三十馀年，所得仅此，良以闻见太隘，征事甚难耳！倘天假我以年，尚愿再撰《里剩》一书，以续其后。伏望四海同志，遇有可欣可愕、足资劝惩之事，不吝邮寄大略，俾得捃掇成编，幸甚！幸甚！
    　　
    　　卷一
    　　
    　　张相国祖 方老宫保 文字竹叶 一文钱 韩文懿公轶事 富翁子 吴生 姑苏某翁 余徐二公轶事 杭城某翁 左生 雷击二女 雷击某氏子 叶孝廉 僵尸 何相国兄弟 傅青主征君轶事

    Explanation

    18

    Preface

    In recent times, the consensus among novelists is that Liaozhai Zhiyi stands as a masterpiece, with its records predominantly featuring foxes and ghosts, which can be created based on imagination. This book, however, is mostly based on real events, making its narrative more challenging. After failing in the provincial examination during the year of the Water Rabbit (癸卯), I was in a state of dejection and aimlessness, listening to a guest recount the words of the late Grand Secretary Yi Wenmin, and playfully wrote them down. Thereafter, each year it increased, and over time, it unexpectedly became a collection. Until now, more than thirty years have passed, and what I have achieved is limited to this, due to my narrow range of experiences and the difficulty in gathering facts. If heaven grants me more time, I still wish to write another book titled Lisheng to continue the series. I humbly hope that all fellow enthusiasts, if you encounter something that is both delightful and shocking, or that can serve as a warning or encouragement, do not hesitate to send a brief account by mail, so that I may collect and compile them into a volume. It would be greatly appreciated! Greatly appreciated!

    Volume One

    • Zhang Xiangguo's Ancestors
    • Fang Laogongbao
    • Bamboo Leaves of Literature
    • One Copper Coin
    • Anecdotes of Han Wenyi Gong
    • The Son of a Wealthy Man
    • Wu Sheng
    • A Certain Elder from Suzhou
    • Anecdotes of the Two Gentlemen Yu and Xu
    • A Certain Elder from Hangzhou
    • Left Sheng
    • Two Women Struck by Lightning
    • A Certain Son of the Mo Family Struck by Lightning
    • Ye Xiaolian
    • Zombies
    • The Brothers of He Xiangguo
    • Anecdotes of Fu Qingzhu, the Recluse
    19

    卷二
    　　
    　　陈太封公 刘封公 程太封公 倪封公 李封公 叶封翁 黄勤敏公 张叔未先生 姚孝廉 某公分校 乡场显报 吾乡张生 张玉常观察 余镜湖太史 甲乙偕试 林妃雪 夜话 吴真人 吴解元子 潘氏祖
    　　
    　　卷三
    　　
    　　扎拉芬夫妇 仙露 袁姬 夙冤 赵乙 绛帻生 尸变 小蛇 辣虫变蛇 滦州儒学蛇 溧阳史仲皋言三事 樵夫某甲 平乡县老儒 超勇公轶事 海州四怪 郑甲 千金亭 员先生 钱弗要 徐霞客 产怪 祝由科 褚祚典 古雏鸾 粤东某甲
    　　
    　　卷四
    　　
    　　姮儿 附录：宫娃歌/节妇吟/佳人 某太史鬼求代 柯寿鞠 伊莘农相国言 圆光二则 楚北王某 爱儿 变驿马 厨媪 玄坛 邑人某甲 秦氏妇 某媪 吴明府 陈司马 林远村方伯 浙江学使署狐

    Explanation

    19

    Chapter Two

    　　Duke Chen, Duke Liu, Duke Cheng, Duke Ni, Duke Li, Lord Ye, Minister Huang Qimin, Mr. Zhang Shuwei, Scholar Yao, Examiner for the District, Notable Reward for the Village, My Fellow Villager Zhang, Inspector Zhang Yuchang, Historian Yu Jinghu, Joint Examination of Jia and Yi, Lin Feixue, Nightly Conversation, Immortal Wu, Scholar Wu, Ancestor of the Pan Family

    　　### Chapter Three

    　　The Couple of Jalarfen, Immortal Dew, Lady Yuan, Ancient Grudge, Zhao Yi, Red Cap Man, Corpse Transformation, Little Snake, Red Ants Turned into Snakes, Snake in the Confucian School of Luanzhou, Three Stories Told by Shi Zhonggao of Lixiang, Woodcutter Jia, Old Scholar of Pingxiang County, Anecdotes of the Intrepid General, Four Oddities of Haizhou, Zheng Jia, The Thousand Gold Pavilion, Master Yuan, Money Not Taken, Xu Xiake, Monster Birth, The School of Spiritual Healing, Chu Zuodian, Ancient Crane, Someone from Guangdong

    　　### Chapter Four

    　　Heng'er, Appendix: Palace Maid Song / Chaste Woman's Lament / Fair Lady, A Certain Historian's Ghost Seeking a Substitute, Ko Shouju, Words of the Prime Minister Yi Xinnong, Two Cases of Divination Using Light, Wang Jia from Northern Chu, Beloved Child, Transformation of the Courtyard Horse, Kitchen Maid, Altar of the Black Warrior, Someone from the County, Wife of Qin Family, Someone's Mother, Magistrate Wu, Marshal Chen, Prefect Lin Yuan Village, Fox in the Office of the Provincial Examiner of Zhejiang

    20

    卷五
    　　
    　　摺差 欧公子 虎钓 俞寿霍 萧状元 甓社湖蚌珠 怒睛鸡 蛇妖 周孝廉妻 产鬼畏伞 制军某公 蚁阵 笪侍御 李泥丸 鱼城 某官妾
    　　
    　　卷六
    　　
    　　吴和甫侍郎轶事 年大将军 甲与乙为善友 王素芳 素芳说梦 吾乡某太史 指腹为婚 老圃某翁 产蛇 雷击某总戎 崔太史 王氏子 雷击邵伯民 大通佘翁 雷击三则 猎人某 行脚僧 当涂令 金钱李二 九山洞 少年客 剑侠 父子同日合卺 毛甲 戴明府
    　　
    　　卷七
    　　
    　　某公子 雄黄弹 有外山王 蒋柿姑 活佛 礼部书吏 肇庆府署五异 鬼批县尉颊 庚午神诛水怪 金毛吼 蜘蛛怪 骊叆卿 记海鹿门别驾少时事 纪梦 附录：重修虞姬墓碑

    Explanation

    20

    Volume V**

    • 摺差 (Folding Difference): A discrepancy or difference that can be folded, possibly in a legal or administrative context.
    • 欧公子 (Ou's Nobleman): A nobleman named Ou.
    • 虎钓 (Tiger Fishing): A type of fishing technique or equipment, possibly involving a tiger motif.
    • 俞寿霍 (Yu Shouhuo): A person named Yu Shouhuo.
    • 萧状元 (Xiao, the Top Scholar): A top scholar named Xiao.
    • 甓社湖蚌珠 (Pearl from Pisha Lake): A pearl from a clam in Pisha Lake, symbolizing rarity and value.
    • 怒睛鸡 (Chicken with Angry Eyes): A chicken with angry eyes, possibly a metaphor for a fierce or aggressive chicken.
    • 蛇妖 (Snake Demon): A snake demon, a common figure in Chinese folklore.
    • 周孝廉妻 (Wife of Zhou, the Scholar-Official): The wife of a scholar-official named Zhou.
    • 产鬼畏伞 (Ghost That Fears Umbrellas): A ghost that fears umbrellas, a specific cultural belief.
    • 制军某公 (Military Governor): A military governor, a title used in historical contexts.
    • 蚁阵 (Ant Army): An ant army, possibly in a metaphorical or literal sense.
    • 笪侍御 (Official Named Da): An official named Da.
    • 李泥丸 (Li Niwan): A person named Li Niwan.
    • 鱼城 (Fish City): A city associated with fish, possibly a location in a story or legend.
    • 某官妾 (Concubine of a Certain Official): A concubine of a certain official, possibly a historical or literary figure.

    Volume VI

    • 吴和甫侍郎轶事 (Anecdotes about Wu Hefu, the High-Ranking Official): Stories about Wu Hefu, a high-ranking official.
    • 年大将军 (General Nian): A general named Nian.
    • 甲与乙为善友 (Friendship Between A and B): A story about the friendship between two individuals, A and B.
    • 王素芳 (Wang Sufang): A person named Wang Sufang.
    • 素芳说梦 (Wang Sufang's Dream): A story about Wang Sufang's dream.
    • 吾乡某太史 (A Local Historian from My Hometown): A local historian from the author's hometown.
    • 指腹为婚 (Prenuptial Agreement): A prenuptial agreement, a specific cultural practice.
    • 老圃某翁 (Old Farmer): An old farmer, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 产蛇 (Giving Birth to a Snake): A story about a woman giving birth to a snake.
    • 雷击某总戎 (General Struck by Lightning): A general who was struck by lightning, indicating a specific historical event.
    • 崔太史 (Official Named Cui): An official named Cui.
    • 王氏子 (Son of the Wang Family): A son of the Wang family, likely a historical or literary figure.
    • 雷击邵伯民 (Civilian Struck by Lightning): A civilian named Shao Bomin who was struck by lightning, indicating a specific historical event.
    • 大通佘翁 (Old Man Sha from Datan): An old man named Sha from Datan, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 雷击三则 (Three Instances of Being Struck by Lightning): Three instances of people being struck by lightning.
    • 猎人某 (Unnamed Hunter): An unnamed hunter, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 行脚僧 (Wandering Monk): A wandering monk, a common figure in Chinese literature and folklore.
    • 当涂令 (Local Magistrate in Dangtu): A local magistrate in Dangtu, likely a historical or literary figure.
    • 金钱李二 (Person Named Li Er): A person named Li Er.
    • 九山洞 (Cave in Nine Mountains): A cave in Nine Mountains, possibly a location in a story or legend.
    • 少年客 (Young Guest): A young guest, possibly a historical or literary figure.
    • 剑侠 (Swordswoman): A swordswoman, a common figure in Chinese literature and folklore.
    • 父子同日合卺 (Marriage on the Same Day as Parents): A couple who got married on the same day as their parents, a specific cultural practice.
    • 毛甲 (Mao Jia): A person named Mao Jia.
    • 戴明府 (Local Magistrate Named Dai): A local magistrate named Dai.
    21

    卷八
    　　
    　　小卫玠 婉姑 某氏子 媚芗 清苑县某氏女 杜有美 守贞 褪壳龟 某令 孙明府 倪公春岩 张船山先生讯盗 张静山观察折狱
    　　
    　　卷九
    　　
    　　节录《土司婚礼记》 撷录《海上纪略》 海吼 天妃神 木龙 水仙王 糠洋蕈洋 大昆仑 琉球 日本 红夷 西洋国 宇内形势 附录：海上占晴雨 暴风日期（附图）撷录《豁意轩录闻》 城隍赴任 武昌徐商 厉鬼作祟 金圣叹 玉兔 继来禅师 阎和尚 犬妖
    　　
    　　卷十
    　　
    　　记粤寇倡乱之始 记粤寇渠魁事迹 石达开 书秦小罗 书傅善祥 易容之 江西刘某 富贾某 中州某生 陈氏女 山东某县幕友 林明府 赵孝廉 方复庵封翁 汪态臣 徽州某甲 皖北粤东两奇女 陈祥浦 小喜子 某甲 朱封翁

    21

    Volume Eight

    　　Xiao Wei Jie, Wan Gu, a son of a certain family, Mei Xiang, a daughter of a certain family from Qingyuan County, Du You Mei, Chaste, Turtle that sheds its shell, a magistrate, Sun Ming Fu, Ni Gong Chun Yan, Mr. Zhang Chuan Shan investigates theft, Mr. Zhang Jing Shan adjudicates cases.

    　　### Volume Nine

    　　Excerpt from "The Marriage of the Tribal Chief," Extract from "Records of the Sea," Sea Roar, Goddess of the Sea, Wooden Dragon, King of Water Spirits, Kangle and Xunyang Seas, Great Kunlun, Ryukyu, Japan, Red Barbarians, Western Country, Geographical Layout of the World, Appendix: Predicting Weather at Sea, Storm Dates (with illustrations), Extract from "Records of the Huiyi Pavilion," City God Takes Office, Merchant Xu Shang from Wuchang, Troublesome Ghosts, Jin Shengtan, Jade Rabbit, Monk Jili, Monk Yan, Dog Demon.

    　　### Volume Ten

    　　Record of the Beginning of the Rebellion in Guangdong, Record of the Leaders of the Rebellion in Guangdong, Shi Dakai, Letter from Qin Xiaoluo, Letter from Fu Shanxiang, Changing Appearance, A person named Liu from Jiangxi, A wealthy merchant, A student from Zhongzhou, A woman from the Chen family, A secretary in a county in Shandong, Magistrate Lin, Scholar Zhao, Retired Official Fang, Wang Taichen, A person from Huizhou, Two extraordinary women from Anhui and Guangdong, Chen Xiangpu, Xiao Xi, A person named Xiao, Retired Official Zhu.

    22

    里乘卷一
    　　
    　　张相国祖
    　　
    　　儿时在塾，家大人训之曰：“士人读书，尤宜积德。即以吾邑而论，其先积德愈厚，其后发祥亦愈炽。”因言张文端公之封翁。初，梦神送一衣冠人至，谓为晋朝王处仲。是夕果生一子，封翁甚喜。稍长，器宇魁梧，性亦聪慧。十岁忽殇，封翁悲恸綦切。越数年，又梦前衣冠人至，曰：“吾周览天下，福德无如翁家，今再来，不复去矣。”俄顷公生，言貌举止与前无异，故字曰敦复。予小子识之，不敢忘。尔时童稚，不敢请问翁家之先有何功德。后闻吾师张子畏观察寅言，始略知其大概。先是，明季张氏之祖有诸生某公，生二子，具读书立品。公老不得志，家计日窘。一日锄圃种菜，忽见窖藏，白镪充斥，不下百万。

    Explanation

    22

    Volume One of the Local Chronicles
    Zhang, the Prime Minister's Ancestor

    When I was young, I studied at a private school, and my father taught me, saying, "Scholars who read books should especially accumulate virtue. Even considering our own county, the thicker the virtue accumulated by a family in the past, the more prosperous it becomes in the future." He then spoke of Zhang Wen Duan Gong's father-in-law. Initially, the father-in-law dreamed of a divine being who brought a man dressed in official attire, saying he was Wang Dao from the Jin dynasty. That night, he indeed gave birth to a son, and the father-in-law was very happy. As the son grew older, he was tall and dignified, and also intelligent and wise. At the age of ten, he suddenly died, and the father-in-law was deeply grieved. After several years, he dreamed again of the same man in official attire, who said, "I have toured the world, and no family has as much virtue and fortune as yours. I am coming back now, and I will not leave again." Shortly after, the son was born, and his speech, appearance, and mannerisms were the same as before, so he was named Dunfu. I, the young one, remember this and dare not forget. At that time, I was still a child and did not dare to ask about the specific virtues of the family. Later, I heard my teacher, Zhang Ziwei, the Censor-in-Inspector, say something about it, and I began to roughly understand the general idea. Before that, during the late Ming dynasty, there was a scholar of the Zhang family, who had two sons who were both scholars and had good character. The scholar grew old without achieving his ambitions, and his family's finances became increasingly difficult. One day, while tending to the garden and planting vegetables, he suddenly discovered a hidden treasure, with silver coins filling the place, amounting to at least a million.

    23

    自念：“书生福薄，骤得巨资，何以堪之？”遂如旧掩好，将留作善举。及老而疾革，始告二子，命：“必俟荒年，掘以赈饥。尔曹务须善成吾志，倘背吾训，妄存贪心，子孙不昌！”二子泣涕受命。公卒后数年，适遇奇荒，二子遵公治命，发圃觇之，信然。遂谒邑令，敬陈遗训，愿出窖救荒。令故贤者，正苦赈济无术，闻言大喜，自督役亲往发之，果得藏镪百万，尽以赈饥，所活无算。事竣，将为请奖，二子坚辞不受。后某甲早行，见人肩担二筐，内盛珊瑚、青精、水晶、砗磲之属，大如杏实，累累如贯珠，不知何物，试问：“何往？”曰：“送往张家去也。”恭逢国初定鼎，文端、文和两公父子相继拜大学士，一时兄弟子侄由科第而跻显秩者，指不胜屈。始知所见珊瑚等物为各色顶戴也。

    Explanation

    23

    The scholar reflected to himself, "A scholar's fortune is thin; to suddenly acquire a large sum of money, how can one bear it?" So he concealed it as before, intending to keep it for good deeds. When he grew old and was on the verge of death, he began to inform his two sons, instructing them: "You must wait for a time of famine, dig it up, and use it to relieve famine. You must certainly carry out my wishes well; if you violate my teachings, harbor greedy thoughts, the descendants will not prosper!" The two sons wept and accepted the command. Several years after the scholar's death, they just happened to encounter a great famine. The two sons followed their father's instructions, dug it up, and inspected it, confirming that it was indeed so. They then visited the county magistrate, respectfully presented their father's last will, and wished to use the treasure to relieve the famine. The magistrate was a virtuous person, just struggling to find a way to provide relief. Upon hearing this, he was greatly pleased, personally supervised the work, and went to dig it up. Indeed, they found a hidden treasure of a million coins, which they used all to relieve the famine, saving countless lives. After the matter was completed, they were about to request a reward, but the two sons firmly declined to accept it. Later, someone named Mr. Zhang set out early one morning and saw a man carrying two baskets, filled with coral, jade, crystal, and shell, each as large as a peach pit, arranged like a string of beads. He did not know what they were and asked, "Where are you going?" The man replied, "To the Zhang family." Happening to encounter the early years of the founding of the dynasty, the two public figures, Wen Duan and Wen He, father and son, successively became Grand Councillors. At one time, brothers and nephews rose to prominent ranks through the imperial examinations, too numerous to count. It was then known that the coral-like objects seen were various types of official headdresses.

    24

    里乘子曰：闻诸故老言，此次赈饥，皆吾邑校官毗陵赵广文督其事，丝毫不入私囊，饥民皆沾实惠。彼某甲早行，见人担二筐送往张家，内另着一小筐，亦盛如杏实各物，问：“此为何？”曰：“此送赵校官者也。”未几，其后嗣熊诏果大魁天下，子孙后亦多显宦云。

    Explanation

    24

    Li Chengzi said: According to the sayings of the ancients, this famine relief effort was overseen by the school official from Piling, Zhao Guangwen, of our county. Not a single bit entered his private pocket, and the famine-stricken people all benefited from it. That person A went out early and saw someone carrying two baskets to the Zhang family. Inside, there was another small basket, also containing items like apricot fruits. He asked, "What is this for?" The answer was, "It is for the school official Zhao." Soon after, his descendant Xiong Zhao indeed became the top scholar in the country, and his descendants also became many prominent officials.

    25

    方老宫保
    　　
    　　家大人又言，吾乡方恪敏公，生性孝友。封翁以事戍边，卒于戍所。恪敏年甫逾弱冠，闻耗，跣足徒行数万里，至塞外负父骸骨归。后以布衣获马周之遇，官至直隶总督。其子勤襄公葆岸宫保、犹子来青宫保，先后相继，均官总督。时人荣之，以为恪敏平日存心孝友之报。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：《易》谓：“积善之家，必有馀庆。”所积愈厚，则其庆亦愈大且久。吾邑巨家显族甚多，然未有如张、方两姓之赫赫者。观张氏之祖所积之善，宜其科甲至今不绝。且自文端公而后，一脉相传，六代翰林。潘文恭公笔记载之，谓为古今所罕有。至方氏，自明初及今，显秩相承，间出文人理学。其先非有盛德，曷克臻此？即恪敏公孝友一节，已可略见大概矣。

    Explanation

    25

    The Venerable Official Fang Laogong

    My father also said that our local official Fang Kemi was naturally filial and kind. His father, who held an official title, was stationed on the frontier and died there. When Fang Kemi was just past his twenties, he heard the news and, barefoot and walking, traveled thousands of miles to the frontier to carry his father's remains back home. Later, he received the same kind of recognition as Ma Zhou, rising from a commoner to become the Governor-General of Zhili. His son, Fang Baohan, and his nephew, Fang Laqing, both succeeded him and also became Governors-General. At the time, people admired them, believing it was a reward for Fang Kemi's lifelong filial piety and kindness.

    The local historian said: "The Book of Changes states, 'The family that accumulates good deeds will have abundant blessings.' The more good deeds they accumulate, the greater and longer-lasting their blessings will be. Our county has many prominent families, but none are as illustrious as the Zhang and Fang clans. Observing the good deeds of the Zhang clan's ancestors, it is understandable why their success in the imperial examinations has continued to this day. Moreover, since Zhang Wenduan, their influence has been passed down in a continuous line, with six generations of members in the Hanlin Academy. This is recorded in the notes of Pan Wen Gong, who considered it a rare occurrence in history. As for the Fang clan, since the early Ming dynasty, they have held high-ranking positions, and occasionally produced scholars and followers of Neo-Confucianism. If their ancestors did not possess great virtue, how could they have achieved such success? Even the filial piety and kindness of Fang Kemi can give us a glimpse of their overall character."

    26

    文字竹叶
    　　
    　　广东某寺，一老衲，贫苦清修，持戒甚严。尝暑月有游方道士日暮来求寄宿，僧曰：“无论荒刹湫隘，不足辱鹤驭。即仙师不弃，而此地恶蚊甚多，嘬人最毒，往往有受创溃腐而不能瘥者。贫僧仅一敝布幛，别无以应客，可若何？”道士曰：“倘师慈悲，肯假一袈裟地暂容栖止，得避虎狼，幸矣！他非所虑。”僧以其言恳切，遂愿让己榻款客。道士谢曰：“蒙师见留，受惠已多，何忍迫师露宿，致饱蚊吻！”僧曰：“师行路甚惫，非幛不得安寝，不必过让。”彼此推逊久之，道士乃宿僧榻。僧竟夕亦幸不为蚊所阨，心窃讶之。

    Explanation

    26

    Characters Like Bamboo Leaves

    In a certain temple in Guangdong, there lived a poor monk who practiced strict asceticism and adhered closely to his precepts. Once during the hot summer months, a wandering Taoist priest arrived late in the evening seeking shelter for the night. The monk said, "Regardless of how desolate and cramped this temple may be, it is not worthy of the presence of a revered master. Even if the Immortal Master does not disdain it, there are many harmful mosquitoes here that bite the most painfully, often causing wounds that rot and cannot heal. The humble monk has only a worn-out cloth screen, and nothing else to offer as hospitality. What can be done?" The Taoist replied, "If the Master is compassionate and willing to allow me to occupy the space covered by one of your robes for a temporary stay, I can avoid the dangers of wild beasts and be fortunate indeed! Other concerns are not my worry." The monk, moved by the Taoist's earnest words, agreed to let the Taoist sleep on his own bed. The Taoist thanked him, saying, "I am grateful for the Master's hospitality. How could I bear to force you to sleep outdoors and be bitten by mosquitoes?" The monk responded, "The Master is very tired from traveling and cannot sleep peacefully without a screen. There is no need for further deference." After a long exchange of politeness, the Taoist finally slept on the monk's bed. Throughout the night, the monk was also fortunate not to be tormented by mosquitoes, and he secretly marveled at this.

    27

    诘旦，道士起谢曰：“昨蒙假榻，感不敢忘。然师夜间得毋受创不？”僧曰：“幸托仙庇，夜间竟无一蚊，不知何散？”道士笑曰：“缘感慈悲，略用小术，将蚊尽驱于后园竹叶上矣。师须切记，凡蚊所栖之叶，已化文字，皆可避蚊，慎勿轻视。”僧异而趋验，果见园竹数百竿叶上各栖一蚊，俱化文字。大喜，出谢，道士不知何往，盖仙人也。从此遂无蚊患。后，远近好事者闻竹叶能避蚊，争购求之，每叶钱数十文，不逾年，园竹为空，僧由是致富。爰大庀材，创修兰若，居然金碧庄严矣。吁！向非老僧一念慈悲，安能幸缔仙缘，为我佛之光哉！

    Explanation

    27

    The next morning, the Taoist priest arose and thanked him, saying, "Yesterday, I allowed you to stay as a guest, and I am deeply grateful and cannot forget your kindness. However, did you perhaps suffer any harm during the night?" The monk replied, "Thankfully, under the protection of a deity, I had no mosquitoes all night, which I did not understand. You used a minor trick to drive away all the mosquitoes to the bamboo leaves in the backyard." The Taoist priest smiled and said, "It is because of your compassion that I used a slight artifice to transform the mosquitoes into characters on the bamboo leaves, which can repel mosquitoes. You must remember this: any leaf where mosquitoes rest has been transformed into characters that can repel mosquitoes, and do not underestimate their power." The monk was surprised and went to verify, and indeed, he saw hundreds of bamboo poles with one mosquito on each leaf, all transformed into characters. From then on, he no longer suffered from mosquito problems. Later, people from far and near who were interested in unusual or beneficial things heard that the bamboo leaves could repel mosquitoes and competed to buy them. Each leaf was worth dozens of coins, and within a year, the bamboo in the garden was depleted, and the monk thus became wealthy. He then gathered large amounts of materials to build and renovate a temple, which became magnificent and splendidly decorated. Alas! Without the old monk's single thought of compassion, how could he have formed a connection with a deity and brought glory to Buddhism?

    28

    里乘子曰：常州汤贞愍公雨生先生贻汾，宦粤时，闻有此叶，特造其寺，求之不得，心殊怊怅。归途，暂憩村塾，与塾师言及，师云：“向与僧善，曾得数十片，为人攫夺殆尽，今存无几，请公馈二叶。”公喜，如获异宝。一赠友人，一用颇黎二片，将叶夹其中，四围镶以紫檀。叶上文字固系篆体，亦于叶旁署款篆书“甲申春日雨生”六字配之，以篆体两面一致，俾把玩者泯其反正之迹。公心灵巧，凡日用什物，无不精妙，即此可见。尝觞予于狮子窟别业，出以见示，并述其缘起如此。

    Explanation

    28

    One says: During his service in Guangdong, Mr. Tang Yisheng, the Righteous and Sorrowful Marquis of Changzhou, heard of these leaves and specifically went to the temple to seek them out, but could not find them, and his heart was greatly disappointed. On his return journey, he temporarily rested at a village school and spoke to the village teacher about it. The teacher said, "I previously had a good relationship with the monk and once obtained dozens of pieces, which were almost entirely seized by others. Now only a few remain. Please give me two leaves." Mr. Tang was delighted, as if obtaining a precious treasure. One leaf was given to a friend, and the other was used with two pieces of crystal, placing the leaves inside and surrounding them with purple sandalwood. The text on the leaves is indeed in seal script, and the six characters "Jia Shen Spring Day Yu Sheng" in seal script were also inscribed next to the leaves, with the seal script on both sides being consistent, so that those who handle it cannot tell the difference between front and back. Mr. Tang has a clever heart, and all his daily-use items are extremely exquisite, as evidenced by this. He once entertained me at his Lion Cave retreat, showed me this, and explained its origin like this.

    29

    一文钱
    　　
    　　一文钱者，姑苏布店也。初，徽商甲乙二人合伙，挟重资至苏贸易，各昵一姬，不吝挥霍。两姬固奇女子，当半夜无人时，谓二人曰：“从古勾栏中鸨媪无好相识，有钱则奉为上宾，无钱即摽诸门外，比比皆然。日来窥二君囊金渐次萧索，君等挟重资，背乡里为权子母，今为妾等耗费殆尽，脱不早为计，其何面目归见家人？愿熟思之。”甲乙亦以为然，以恋恋不忍割爱，苟且安之。鸨媪每有所求，必百方谋画，以厌其欲。无何，典质既罄，遽为鸨媪齿冷，将下逐客之令。两姬曰：“何如？君等不听妾言，早知有今日矣。妾等不幸，身堕下流，实非所愿。

    Explanation

    29

    One Coin

    　　One Coin was the name of a cloth store in Suzhou. Initially, two Anhui merchants, A and B, partnered to bring substantial capital to Suzhou for trade. Each had a favorite concubine and did not hesitate to spend lavishly. The two concubines were extraordinary women, and one night, when it was late and no one was around, they said to the two merchants: "Since ancient times, the madams of brothels have no good acquaintances. Those with money are treated as honored guests, while those without money are thrown out of the door—it is the same everywhere. Recently, it has been observed that the two gentlemen's wealth has gradually diminished. You two brought substantial capital, leaving your hometowns to engage in usury, and now all of your wealth has been spent on us. If you do not plan ahead, how will you face your family when you return home? I hope you will think carefully about this." A and B also agreed, but they were reluctant to part with their favorites and settled for a temporary solution. The madam always made requests, and they had to devise many plans to satisfy her desires. Eventually, their collateral was exhausted, and the madam became cold to them, preparing to evict them. The two concubines said: "How could it be? You did not listen to my advice, and now you know the consequences. We are unfortunate to have fallen into this lowly position, which is not what we wished for. We wish to follow you back to your hometown or seek a peaceful death to thank you." Seeing this, A and B realized they had no way out and could only part with tears. They then returned to their hometowns, never to set foot in a brothel again.

    30

    蒙君等割臂要盟，刻铭心髓。观二君意气，不过暂时落莫，必不久困，不如暂歌别鹤，努力以图恢复。妾等当誓死待践昔约，报君有日矣！”各馈白金五十两，趣令早去。甲乙无可如何，不得已，受金挥泪而别。时岁将暮，二人姑就酒垆，对酌御寒，并市饦馎、寒具等物充饥。心绪烦冤，饮罢，忘携馈金，归寓始觉，急觅不得。逆旅主人促索税资，勉强典衣以应，行李一空，二人计穷，日则行乞，夜则寄宿古刹。耻过两姬之门，避道而行。会除日，薄暮，二人拾得枯枝，就地燃火，相对欷歔。甲于腰摸得一钱，掷地叹曰：“重资散尽，留此一钱何益！不如抛去。”乙忽心动，急拾取曰：“此硕果也。

    Explanation

    30

    We are bound by a blood oath with you, engraving our commitment deep in our hearts. Observing your demeanor, it seems that your current state of despair is only temporary; you will not remain in such distress for long. It would be better to sing a farewell song to a crane, and strive to regain your fortunes. We, the ladies, swear to die waiting to fulfill our past promise, and the day will come when we shall repay you!” Each of them was given fifty taels of silver, and urged to leave early. Unable to refuse, A and B accepted the silver and departed with tears. As the year drew to a close, the two of them went to a wine shop, where they drank to ward off the cold and bought noodles and other cold foods to satisfy their hunger. Their minds were troubled, and after drinking, they forgot to take the silver given to them. They only realized it when they returned to their lodging, and in a hurry, they could not find it. The innkeeper demanded payment for taxes, so they reluctantly pawned their clothes to meet the demand, leaving them with nothing. With no resources left, they had no choice but to beg during the day and stay in ancient temples at night. They avoided passing by the two ladies' homes out of shame. On the eve of the New Year, as dusk fell, they picked up some dry branches, lit a fire on the ground, and wept together. A reached into his waistband and found a single coin, which he threw to the ground and sighed, saying, “All our wealth has been lost; what use is this one coin? It might as well be thrown away.” B suddenly felt a surge of emotion and quickly picked up the coin, saying, “This is the last fruit.”

    31

    天幸存此一脉生机，安知非剥极而复之兆？”遽携钱出，曰：“君姑待之，我自有计。”甲莫喻其意。少顷，乙归，手携竹片、草茎、败纸、鸡鸭毛等物，甲问：“何为？”乙笑出面粉，索水调浆。就地火光中，将草缠竹片上，蒙以败纸，又遍粘鸡鸭毛，畀甲视之，宛然各种禽鸟。甲曰：“君处此愁城，尚何作此儿戏？”乙但笑而不言。竟夕，约成二三百具。平明，以半付甲，邀同至玄妙观，自有料理。甲姑与俱往。观为姑苏游观之薮，春日尤盛。比至观，士女云集。妇孺见甲乙所携禽鸟，以为酷肖，争求购买，顷刻俱尽。每具十数钱，共计五千有奇。

    Explanation

    31

    Heaven has preserved this thread of life, who knows if it is not a sign of renewal after extreme decay?" He immediately took out money and said, "Please wait, I have my own plan." A did not understand his meaning. A short while later, B returned, carrying bamboo strips, grass stems, torn paper, and chicken and duck feathers. A asked, "What are you doing?" B smiled and brought out flour, asking for water to mix into paste. In the firelight on the ground, he wrapped the grass around the bamboo strips, covered them with torn paper, and then stuck chicken and duck feathers all over, showing them to A, who saw lifelike birds of all kinds. A said, "In your state of distress, why are you doing this childish play?" B just smiled without speaking. Through the night, they made about two or three hundred of these. At dawn, B gave half to A and invited him to the Xuanmiao Temple, where they had their own arrangements. A agreed to go with him. The temple was a popular place for sightseeing in Suzhou, especially crowded during spring. By the time they arrived at the temple, a crowd of men and women had gathered. Women and children saw the birds carried by A and B, thinking them very lifelike, and competed to buy them, selling them all within moments. Each bird cost a dozen or so coins, totaling more than five thousand.

    32

    甲至是始叹乙心思灵巧，乐不可支。因问：“一钱何用？”曰：“竹片、草茎、败纸、鸡鸭毛等物，皆系拾诸市上。以一钱市面粉，岂不惬敷所用耶？”相与大笑。自是购添各色纸张、杂鸡鸭毛，以肖人物花草等状。两入夜间分制，日至观求售。自春徂夏，才百日，计敛钱三千馀缗矣。因变计，居积货物，往无不利。不两年，积资数万。遂于阊门开设布店，大书“一文钱”三字榜于门，志不忘所自也。乃各具千金为两姬脱籍，姬各出私蓄，相助经营。不数年，财雄一方，爰遣人至徽迎取眷属。两家相约，世为婚姻，迄今二百有馀岁矣。阊门外泰伯庙前“一文钱”三金字，大如栲栳，犹煌煌照人目云。

    Explanation

    32

    At this point, Jia began to marvel at Yi's ingenious mind, overcome with joy. He asked, "What use is a single coin?" Yi replied, "Items such as bamboo slips, grass stems, scraps of paper, and feathers from chickens and ducks are all collected from the market. Using a single coin to buy flour, would it not be sufficient for our needs?" They laughed together. From then on, they purchased various types of paper and assorted chicken and duck feathers, using them to create representations of people, flowers, and other objects. The two of them divided the work at night, and each day they went out to sell their wares. From spring to summer, in just a hundred days, they had collected more than three thousand strings of cash. They then changed their strategy, accumulating goods, and their ventures always proved successful. Within two years, they had amassed tens of thousands of assets. They subsequently opened a cloth shop at the Chuan Gate, prominently displaying the three characters "One Coin" on the door, to remember their humble beginnings. They each contributed a thousand gold to free the two ladies from servitude, and the ladies offered their private savings to assist in the business. Within a few years, they became wealthy in the region, and they sent people to Huizhou to bring over their families. The two families agreed to marry as lifelong allies, and this bond has endured for over two hundred years. At the Taibo Temple in front of the Chuan Gate, the three large characters "One Coin" are as big as a basket, still brightly shining and dazzling to the eyes.

    33

    韩文懿公轶事
    　　
    　　韩文懿公菼，貌寖陋，而髯丛如蝟。年逾四十，甫领乡荐。计偕北上，膏秣无资，襆被徒行。尝日暮失路，寄宿人檐下，少间，一叟笼烛至，见问：“谁何？”公具告邦族。叟瞿然曰：“是慕庐先生也耶？老朽向读大文，向慕已久，今不知惠临，亵慢勿罪。”公谦词致谢。叟叩门，肃客入，为具酒食，款洽甚恭。就厅事西偏设榻，请公安寝。叟入，公甫就枕。厅上故供祖先木主，残灯尚明，时正月中旬，灯节初过，月明如昼。

    Explanation

    33

    Han Wen Yi Gong Tan, A Tale of the Eminent Duke

    Han Wen Yi Gong Tan, in appearance increasingly ugly, yet with a beard as thick as a hedgehog. By the time he had passed his fortieth year, he had just been recommended in the local examination. He was traveling north to take the imperial examination, lacking the resources to prepare, and traveling on foot with only a bundle and a blanket. Once, at dusk, he lost his way and sought shelter under someone's eaves. Shortly after, an elderly man came with a lamp, inquiring, "Who are you?" The duke informed him of his family and lineage. The elderly man was startled and said, "Are you Master Mulu? I have long admired your great writings, but did not know you would grace me with your presence. I beg your pardon for any disrespect." The duke responded with polite words of thanks. The elderly man knocked on the door, formally inviting the guest inside, and provided food and drink, treating him with great hospitality. He arranged a bed in the west side of the hall, asking the duke to rest. The elderly man entered, and the duke was just about to lie down. In the hall, ancestral tablets were displayed, a dim lamp still burning, it being mid-month of the first lunar month, the Lantern Festival having just passed, and the moonlight as bright as daylight.

    34

    忽闻窸窣作声，一女从门隙入，靓妆高髻，径至祖先案前，伏地跽拜。已，出一物置香炉下，冉冉由门隙入内。公知有异，悄起，于炉下摸得一物，就灯下谛视，形类篾丝，上缠红线一缕，腥臭刺鼻，乃携压枕下，倚枕假寐以觇之。无何，又闻窸窣声，前女从门隙出，后随一女，相将至祖先前，伏地交拜。前女起索炉下物，不得，意甚惶急；后女立待良久，复独由门隙入内。前女便至榻前问曰：“顷炉下一物，公见之否？”公披衣起坐，曰：“良有之，汝需此何为者？”女曰：“实告公，妾非人，乃缢鬼也。

    Explanation

    34

    Suddenly, the sound of rustling was heard, and a woman came in through the door crack, dressed in fine makeup and with a high bun. She went straight to the ancestral altar, knelt and bowed to the ground. Afterward, she took out an object and placed it under the incense burner, then gradually entered through the door crack. The master knew something was unusual and quietly got up, touched and retrieved an object from under the incense burner, and examined it closely by the light. The object was shaped like bamboo threads, with a strand of red thread wrapped around it, and it had a foul-smelling and pungent odor. The master carried it under the pillow, leaned back pretending to sleep to observe. Not long after, he heard the sound of rustling again. The first woman came out through the door crack, followed by another woman. Both went to the ancestral altar, knelt and bowed together. The first woman stood up to search for the object under the incense burner but couldn't find it, and she was very anxious and agitated. The second woman stood waiting for a long time, then entered alone through the door crack. The first woman then went to the bed and asked, "Did you see the object under the incense burner just now?" The master put on his clothes and sat up, saying, "Indeed, I saw it. What do you need it for?" The first woman replied, "Please tell me the truth, I am not a human but a hanged ghost."

    35

    合于今夕得替投生，非此物无以为信。乞公怜而赐还为幸。”公拈髯冷笑曰：“若然，汝利人之死，以图己之生，我实不愿遂汝之生，而不救人之死。物固在此，吾决不汝还矣！”女再三哀之，公瞪目拈髯，冷笑不答。女惨然变色曰：“公不畏鬼耶？如再不掷还，妾将现变相矣！”公笑曰：“汝纵现变相，是汝本来面目。吾何畏哉？”女霄霄有声，长袖一拂，蓬发垢面，帚眉突睛，舌出唇外长尺有咫，怒目相向，意殊狞恶。

    Explanation

    35

    If I am to be reborn tonight, this object is the only proof. I humbly request your mercy and the return of the object as a favor." The man, pulling his beard, coldly laughed and said, "If that is the case, you seek to benefit from someone's death in order to gain your own life. I truly do not wish to allow your rebirth at the expense of saving the life of another. The object is still here, and I will not return it to you!" The woman pleaded with him again and again, but the man glared at her, pulling his beard, and coldly laughed without responding. The woman's face turned pale with fear and she said, "Do you not fear ghosts? If you do not return it, I will manifest a terrifying form!" The man laughed and said, "Even if you manifest a terrifying form, that is your true nature. What have I to fear?" The woman let out a sound like a howl, and with a sweep of her long sleeves, she appeared disheveled and dirty, with wild eyebrows and bulging eyes. Her tongue protruded from her lips by a significant length, and she glared at him with a ferocious and evil expression.

    36

    公笑曰：“汝技止此乎？汝试亦观我变相何如？”相传公固奎星化身，时宿酲未解，酒气尚醺，急起赤足，索得只履，夔跃而前，须髯怒张，盛气向女面一嘘，其状俨然世俗所绘奎星也者。女不禁悲啸扑地，幻为云烟，顷刻澌灭，竟不能复聚形矣。公乃叩内室门，叟出，备告所以。先是，叟有子出外，妇不得于姑，日间适以小事勃谿。叟闻公言，知有变，急入告媪，相与破妇房门，果见妇悬梁上，气尚未绝，解缳以水灌之，顿苏。公谕叟与媪好善视其妇，一家感泣，从此称慈姑孝妇焉。天明，公出炉下物火之，并将灰投圊中，以绝其患。是科，公登会状，官至大宗伯。

    Explanation

    36

    The gentleman laughed and said, "Is this the limit of your skill? You should try and see how my transformation compares." It was said that the gentleman was indeed the incarnation of the Star of Literature. At that time, his hangover had not yet worn off, and he was still slightly drunk. He quickly rose, barefoot, fetched a single shoe, and leaped forward like a one-legged creature. His beard bristled with rage as he exhaled a powerful breath toward the woman’s face. His appearance was exactly as depicted in the common illustrations of the Star of Literature. The woman could not help but cry out in sorrow and fell to the ground, transforming into mist and smoke, which vanished in an instant, never to reassemble into form again.

    The gentleman then knocked on the door of the inner room, and the old man came out, fully explaining the matter. Earlier, the old man had a son who was away, and his wife was at odds with her mother-in-law, and during the day had just had a minor quarrel. Upon hearing the gentleman’s words, the old man realized something was wrong, rushed in to inform his wife, and together they broke into the wife’s room, only to find her hanging from the beam, still alive. They revived her by pouring water over her, and she suddenly regained consciousness.

    The gentleman advised the old man and his wife to treat their wife kindly. The whole family was moved to tears, and from then on she was known as the Benevolent and Filial Wife. At dawn, the gentleman took the object from the furnace, burned it, and threw the ashes into the latrine, to eliminate the danger. In that examination, the gentleman passed the imperial examination and was appointed to the high office of Grand Master of Rites.

    37

    里乘子曰：相传吴门有星者，决人吉凶，百不爽一。许缪殿撰彤登会状，果验，一时神之。文懿公往求推算，则曰：“子一第已属幸事，尚望捷南宫乎？死期且至矣，奈何！”公闻而怏怏，无意北上。友人力破其惑，又薄助其资，甫克黾勉就道。竟继缪公而登会状。星者闻之，惶愧遁匿。又《聊斋志异》载元少先生曾设鬼帐一事，因思古之不得志于时者，或为路鬼揶揄，或受小人奚落，正复不少。若公既见重于冥王，又能气慑恶魄，可见公平日为人，为天人之所钦瞩，较之寻常与鬼物为伍者不同。彼星者，本小人之尤，以公貌陋家寒，预挟一穷儒不能发迹之见，不待推算，遽加菲薄，是炎凉之心中之，非其术数之或验或不验也。其智识不又出鬼物下哉！噫！

    Explanation

    37

    The village scholar said: There is a fortune-teller in Suzhou who determines people's fortunes and misfortunes with an accuracy of a hundred to one. Xu Mou, the imperial examiner, passed the highest level of the imperial examination, and his success indeed proved the fortune-teller's reputation, making him revered for a time. When Wen Yi Gong went to seek a reading, the fortune-teller said, "It is already a fortunate thing that you have passed the first level. Do you still hope to pass the imperial examination? Your day of death is approaching, what can you do?" Upon hearing this, the official felt dejected and had no intention of heading north. His friends worked hard to dispel his doubts and provided him with some financial assistance, enabling him to muster the effort to set out. He eventually followed in Xu Mou's footsteps and passed the imperial examination. Upon hearing this, the fortune-teller was filled with shame and fear and fled into hiding.

    Moreover, the Liaozhai Zhiyi records an incident involving Yuan Shao, the younger master, who once set up a "ghost account." This made me reflect on those who were unsuccessful in their time; they might be mocked by roadside ghosts or ridiculed by petty people, and such cases are not rare. If the official was esteemed by the king of the underworld and could intimidate evil spirits, it shows that his character was highly regarded by both the divine and human worlds, differing from those who commonly associate with ghostly beings. That fortune-teller, being the worst of the common people, held a biased view that the official, due to his plain appearance and humble background, would never achieve success. Without even consulting the stars, he hastily dismissed the official with contempt. This reflects the fickleness of human nature, not the accuracy or inaccuracy of his divination. His wisdom and knowledge are not even on par with that of ghosts! Alas!

    38

    富翁子
    　　
    　　富翁某，群雌粥粥。中年举一雄，喜甚。儿甫周晬，忽终日啼哭，滴乳不食，举家忧皇，急延多医商治，并云但求儿愈，不吝厚酬。群医筹商立方，药不下咽，束手无策，次第散去。中有某医者，素专治小儿，其术甚精，再四谛视指纹，知儿固无病，窃希厚酬，独留不去，而展转思维，卒不喻其啼哭不乳之故。偶游后园，见乳姆于荷池为儿洗濯衣裤，蓬头悲泣。问其何泣之哀，答曰：“妾一家老幼不下十口，皆赖妾在此乳儿得不冻馁。今儿疾不治，一家断难存活，那得不哭！”医闻是乳姆，其心忽动，乃曰：“我医也。再四谛视指纹，儿实无病，但不喻其啼哭不乳之故。汝若知之，可悉告我，我当设法治之。若是，则汝家温饱，我亦得厚酬矣。

    Explanation

    38

    The Wealthy Man’s Son

    A certain wealthy man, whose household was in a state of anxious confusion, gave birth to a son in his middle years and was extremely pleased. When the child was just one year old, he suddenly cried all day long, refusing to nurse or eat. The whole family was filled with worry and alarm, and they quickly summoned many doctors to treat the child, declaring that they would not hesitate to offer a generous reward if the child recovered. The doctors deliberated and prescribed various treatments, but the child would not take the medicine, and the doctors, at a loss, left one after another.

    Among them was a certain physician who had always specialized in treating children and whose skill was highly refined. He examined the child’s fingerprints repeatedly and concluded that the child was indeed healthy. Secretly hoping for a generous reward, he alone remained behind, turning the matter over in his mind, but still could not understand the cause of the child’s crying and refusal to nurse.

    By chance, he wandered into the garden and saw the wet nurse washing the child’s clothes by the lotus pond, her hair disheveled and her face filled with sorrow. He asked her why she was weeping so bitterly, and she replied: “My family, both old and young, numbers no fewer than ten people. All of us depend on me being here to nurse the child so that we may not suffer from cold and hunger. Now that the child is ill and cannot be cured, our family will certainly not survive—how could I not weep!”

    When the physician heard this from the nurse, his heart was suddenly moved, and he said: “I am a physician. I have examined the child’s fingerprints repeatedly and know that he is truly healthy, but I do not understand the cause of his crying and refusal to nurse. If you know the reason, please tell me in full. I shall devise a treatment for it. If so, then your family will be well-fed and warm, and I shall also receive a generous reward.”

    39

    未审汝知之否？”乳姆闻之，皇遽投地，稽颡有声，悄谓之曰：“先生必秘勿告翁，妾乃敢言。”医曰：“诺。”乃曰：“前日抱儿戏池畔，儿掬石上生螺，纳诸口中，妾急以指掏之，已骾喉际。从此啼哭，滴乳不食。此致病之由，惟妾一人知之，先生慎勿多言，未审果能治之否？”医抚掌笑曰：“得之矣。”以好言慰乳姆，并誓不泄言，乳姆叩谢而去。医特见翁，笑贺曰：“连日为郎君故，几忘寝食。顷思得一良法，疾可立效。但不知郎君愈后，所谓厚酬者几许耳？”翁喜，拈髯笑曰：“小儿果愈，请以五百金为先生寿，可乎？”医笑曰：“请益之。”曰：“倍之，何如？”医点首曰：“可矣。”乃嘱翁速购肥鸭百头，绳系其足而倒悬之，以盎承鸭嘴所流瀺沫若干，用铫频挹注儿口中，不炊许时，儿啼哭顿止，且以手索乳哺矣。翁顾而狂喜，以千金酬其医。

    Explanation

    39

    The doctor asked, "Do you know about this?" Upon hearing this, the wet nurse was shocked and fell to the ground, touching her forehead to the ground and making a sound. She whispered to him, "Master, please keep this secret from the old man, and only then will I dare to speak." The doctor replied, "Yes." Then he said, "The day before, I was carrying the child playing by the pond. The child picked up a snail from the stone and put it in his mouth. I quickly tried to remove it with my finger, but it got stuck in the throat. Since then, the child has been crying and refusing to eat milk. This is the cause of the illness, and only I know about it. Master, please do not speak of this to others. Can you really cure it?" The doctor clapped his hands and laughed, "I have found it." He comforted the wet nurse with kind words and swore not to reveal it. The wet nurse knelt and thanked him before leaving. The doctor then specially visited the old man and smiled, saying, "For several days, I have been so concerned about the young master that I almost forgot to sleep and eat. Just now, I thought of a good method, and the disease can be cured immediately. But I don't know how much reward the old man will give after the young master recovers." The old man was pleased and said with a laugh, "If the child really recovers, I will give you five hundred gold as a gift, is that acceptable?" The doctor laughed and said, "Please increase it." The old man said, "Double it, how about that?" The doctor nodded and said, "Yes, that is acceptable." He then instructed the old man to quickly buy a hundred fat ducks, tie their feet with ropes and hang them upside down, use a bowl to catch the water dripping from the ducks' beaks, and use a ladle to frequently pour the water into the child's mouth. After a short while, the child's crying stopped immediately, and he started to suckle with his hands. The old man looked at it and was extremely happy, rewarding the doctor with a thousand gold.

    40

    里乘子曰：昔扬州鹾商某，中指顶螺纹中忽生一红毛，根围红盘大于豆，毛锐如针，触之痛彻心髓。医治罔效，且稽古方无是症，群医无可如何，相率辞去。吾邑严肇基，名医也，适游广陵。商耳其名，礼延诊治。严稽群医所立方，或败毒，或泻火，俱无少效。默思：“中指属心，毛乃血之馀，其色赤，属火，必心火与肾水不能既济。”“君火动而血无所统，致酿此患。治当滋肾。”以金匮肾气老法加减。三日而毛色顿黑，痛顿减；又三日而色黄，痛渐止；又三日而色黄转白；越日，毛随盘结痂俱落，疾良瘥。所谓医者，意也。彼小儿骾螺，以鸭见螺必嘬，取其以瀹之，故立愈。证以严医之法，益知医以意为之，较执古方，不更可操胜券乎！

    Explanation

    40

    One who lives in a village said: In the past, a salt merchant from Yangzhou had a red hair suddenly grow from the spiral groove on the tip of his middle finger. The base of the hair was a red disk larger than a bean, and the hair was sharp like a needle. When touched, it caused intense pain that penetrated to the marrow. Medical treatments were ineffective, and ancient medical texts did not mention this condition. The doctors were at a loss and left one after another. My hometown has a famous doctor named Yan Zhaobi, who happened to be traveling in Guangling (Yangzhou). The merchant heard of his name and respectfully invited him to diagnose and treat. Doctor Yan reviewed the prescriptions of the other doctors, some of which used detoxifying remedies, others fire-clearing remedies, but none had any effect. He silently pondered, "The middle finger belongs to the heart meridian. The hair is an overflow of blood. Its color is red, belonging to the fire element. It must be that the heart fire and kidney water cannot achieve balance." "The heart fire is active, and the blood has no master, leading to this illness. The treatment should nourish the kidneys." He used an ancient formula from the Jinkui Yaolue (Golden Chamber Essentials) with modifications. After three days, the hair turned black, and the pain suddenly decreased. After another three days, the color turned yellow, and the pain gradually stopped. After another three days, the color turned white. The next day, the hair, along with the disk and scab, all fell off, and the disease was completely cured. Medicine is about intention. There is a folk remedy for a child with a sea snail stuck in their throat: ducks instinctively suck out sea snails, and taking the duck meat and cooking it can expel the sea snail, thus curing the condition immediately. Verified with Doctor Yan's method, it further shows that medicine is about intention. Compared to rigidly following ancient formulas, isn't it more effective to use intention?

    41

    吴生
    　　
    　　唐卢龙节度使李公，精星学推算，穷通殀寿，百不爽一。有爱女美且慧，公推算当封夫人，非公侯之命不许占凤，故及笄犹未字也。有吴生者，固世家子，素游惰而性儇巧。涎欲系援，又不敢遽通媒妁。密以百钱贿日者，为捏造一极贵之格，书于红笺，乘公出行，故犯卤簿。公怒叱虞候拘至舆前，厉声问故。生叩头曰：“小人以贫困不能自存，特占休咎于日者，谓‘贵不可言’。自念一寒至此，何由发迹？缘頫观所评命纸，沈吟犹豫，不虞节钺忽临，致误冒犯，罪万死。”公索评笺，推之良然，颜色顿霁。

    Explanation

    41

    Wu Sheng

    During the Tang dynasty, Governor Li of the Lu Long Circuit was highly skilled in astrology and calculation, accurately predicting prosperity, adversity, longevity, and premature death. He had a beloved daughter who was both beautiful and wise, and he had calculated that she would become a noble wife, destined only for a high-ranking official. Thus, she remained unmarried even after reaching the age of marriage. There was a man named Wu Sheng, who was originally from a prominent family but was usually idle and cunning in nature. He desired to form connections but was too timid to immediately approach matchmakers. Secretly, he bribed a fortune-teller with a hundred coins to fabricate an extremely auspicious fate, which he wrote on a red note. He then intentionally violated the imperial procession when the governor was out. The governor, furious, ordered the guards to seize Wu Sheng and bring him to the carriage, where he sternly asked for an explanation. Wu Sheng knelt and said, "Your humble servant, impoverished and unable to support himself, sought predictions of good and ill from a fortune-teller, who declared, 'His fortune is beyond words.' Reflecting on my current state of poverty, I wondered how I could rise to prominence. As I looked down at the fate paper, I hesitated and pondered, not expecting the imperial authority to suddenly appear, resulting in this unintentional offense. I am guilty of a crime punishable by a thousand deaths." The governor then requested the fate paper, which he found to be true, and his expression suddenly cleared.

    42

    详诘世族，大喜，命载之后车归。为薰沐更衣，问：“娶妻否？”对曰：“以贫故，尚未婚配。”公益喜，遂筮吉，以爱女妻之。一介措大，一旦坐享富丽，顿增骄蹇。左右之人妒而且恨，交谮于公。久之，公亦察其无他能，阴悔而厌薄之。欲杀之，苦无其法。会吐蕃大入寇，朝廷忧之，诏各路节度使举将才，公遂抗疏：“特荐婿吴生，固世家子，素习韬略，可胜将帅之任。”疏上，召生告之。生知将借刀杀己，然不敢辞，且佯喜再拜，深谢汲引。及谕旨下，生拜辞公，内与妻诀。女固贤淑，以父将不利于婿，心殊不慊。乃勉生曰：“男儿志在四方，死生有命。

    Explanation

    42

    He thoroughly investigated the man’s lineage and was greatly pleased, ordering him to be carried back in his own carriage. He had him bathed, perfumed, and dressed anew, then asked, “Have you married?” The man replied, “Because of poverty, I have not yet been married.” The elder was even more pleased, and so he performed divination to find a favorable omen, then married his beloved daughter to him. A mere scholar, in a single day he came to enjoy luxury and opulence, and suddenly became arrogant and disdainful. Those around him grew jealous and resentful, and conspired to slander him before the elder. After some time, the elder also observed that the man had no other talents, and secretly regretted his decision, growing to despise him. He wanted to kill him, but found no way to do so. Just then, the Tibetan Empire launched a large invasion, and the imperial court grew concerned. The emperor issued an edict to the regional military governors, ordering them to recommend military talent. The elder then submitted a memorial stating: “I specially recommend my son-in-law Wu Sheng, who is indeed the son of a noble family and has long studied military strategy; he is capable of assuming the role of a general.” When the memorial was submitted, the elder summoned Wu Sheng and told him of the matter. Wu Sheng knew that he was being used as a scapegoat, yet dared not refuse, and so he pretended to be happy, bowed twice, and deeply thanked the elder for his recommendation. When the imperial decree was issued, Wu Sheng bowed and took his leave of the elder, and privately said farewell to his wife. His wife was indeed virtuous and kind, but because her father would bring harm to her husband, she felt deeply uneasy. She then encouraged him, saying, “A man’s ambition is to travel far and wide; life and death are determined by fate.”

    43

    此行安知非福！努力为之，不立功归，无相见也！君其懋哉！”生曰：“诺。”既至戍所，谕部曲将弁：诘旦登场阅武，有不至者杀无赦。至期，一一阅毕，各厚犒之。且笑谓诸将弁曰：“尔曹各有所长，果同心戮力，蠢尔蕃虏何难殄灭！幕府少不更事，颇好驰马试剑，敢献薄技，以助诸君一笑。”佥曰：“唯唯，愿幸寓目。”少选，数健儿共舁一大刀至，约重千钧。生乃着戎服，跨骏马，持所舁大刀，下抑上扬，左荡右决，轻如挥扇，易若折枝。舞毕下马，毫不竭力。

    Explanation

    43
