    Who knows if this journey is not a blessing? Strive to do it. If you do not achieve success and return, we shall not meet again. You must be diligent! The soldier replied, "Yes." Upon arriving at the garrison, he commanded the troops: "Review the troops early the next morning. Those who do not attend shall be killed without mercy." On the appointed day, he reviewed each unit, rewarding them generously. He then smiled and said to the generals, "Each of you has your own strengths. If we are truly united and work together, how difficult is it to annihilate these barbaric enemies? The general staff has little experience, but I particularly enjoy riding and practicing with the sword. I dare to present a small skill to entertain you all." They all replied, "Yes, yes, we wish to see it." After a short while, several strong men carried a large sword, weighing about a thousand catties. He then mounted his horse, holding the large sword, and performed a display: downward and upward, left and right, as light as waving a fan, as easy as breaking a branch. After finishing the dance, he dismounted without showing any sign of fatigue.

    44

    合营罗拜，欢声雷动，贺曰：“公神威，真天人也！”生命以刀悬诸营门，择日挞伐。初，生阅武时，吐蕃潜遣谍者侦之，见生舞刀，大惊，舌挢几不能下。深夜，悄就营门举之，直如蚍蜉撼树，牢不能动。谍报，吐蕃闻之相顾失色，君臣筹议，以为不早自量力，强与交绥，是螳臂当车，徒自取死。急上表谢罪，愿岁岁朝贡，永誓不反。捷闻，朝廷嘉悦，以李公所荐得人，晋左仆射，封代国公；以生征虏有功，授岭南节度使，封万户侯，妻封凉国夫人。至是，生得官归，遂为翁婿、夫妇如初。后，女问生，始知前所舞大刀，以木片饰锡箔为之。又预如式铸千钧铁刀，使悬营门，故令其谍者侦报，以慑其心，而投诚输款也。

    Explanation

    44

    The camp gathered in homage, the crowd’s joyous cries echoing like thunder, and they praised, saying, “Your divine power, truly a being of heaven and man!” Life itself seemed to hang by a blade at the camp gate, and a day was chosen for the punitive campaign. At first, when the man was reviewing military exercises, the Tibetan Empire secretly sent spies to observe him. They saw him wielding the sword and were greatly startled, their tongues so stunned they could scarcely descend. One night, a spy quietly approached the camp gate and attempted to lift the blade, but it was as if an ant tried to shake a tree — he could not budge it even an inch. The spies reported back, and upon hearing this, the Tibetans looked at one another in alarm. The emperor and his ministers deliberated, concluding that the Tibetans had not measured their strength in time, had recklessly engaged in battle, and thus were like a mantis blocking a chariot — a doomed endeavor that would only bring death upon themselves. In haste, they submitted a petition of apology, vowing to send tribute year after year and swear eternal loyalty, never to rebel again. When news of the victory reached the court, the imperial authorities were greatly pleased. Because of the recommendation of General Li, the man was promoted to Left Chancellor of the Secretariat and granted the noble title of Duke of Dai. For his success in subduing the enemy, he was appointed Military Governor of Lingnan, granted the title of Lord of Ten Thousand Households, and his wife was ennobled as Lady of Liang. When he returned home with his official post, he was once again husband and wife, as before. Later, the woman asked him, and only then did she learn that the great sword he had previously wielded was made of wooden pieces covered in tin foil. He had also prepared in advance a massive iron sword weighing a thousand jin, hung it at the camp gate, and thus sent his spies to report on it, in order to intimidate the enemy’s heart and bring them to submission and surrender.

    45

    里乘子曰：或谓吴生一生工于用诈，始也，以诈得妇；卒也，以诈得功，亦何狡狯乃尔也。予谓必其命应如此，故天牖其衷，福至心灵。向使吐蕃之役应变无谋，则翁将借刀以杀其婿。夫且不能终有其妻，匪寇婚媾能不为生危乎？方入赘时，公虽信命，竟不免为人言所摇，赖女也能贤，安命不贰，“安知非福”，一言幸中，果尔塞上捷闻，朝中命下，翁既徼宠，妻亦分荣。自是生得官归，遂为翁婿、夫妇如初，是盖有幸存焉。予旧过卢生祠，见题壁诗甚夥，类皆艳羡卢生得遇吕仙，作此一场好梦。予谓卢生若无封侯骨，何能入梦？因口占三绝以调侃之，有云：“卢生自有封侯骨，才得邯郸梦一场。”即此意也。武侯尝言：“谋事在人，成事在天。”天即命也。否则，命之不犹，而妄希非分，天下之百般奸巧、百般穷者，岂少也哉！况就用兵而论，所谓兵不厌诈，此事即采入智囊，亦奚不可？

    Explanation

    45

    Li Chengzi said: Some say that Wu Sheng was skilled in using deceit throughout his life. At first, he gained a wife through deceit; in the end, he gained merit through deceit. How cunning is he? I believe that his fate must have been like this, so Heaven opened his heart, and blessings brought him insight. If the campaign against the Tibetans had lacked strategy, then the father-in-law would have used someone else's hand to kill his son-in-law. He would not have been able to keep his wife, and if the marriage was not genuine, how could it not be dangerous for him? When he was first taking up residence with his wife's family, although he believed in fate, he could not help but be swayed by others' words. Fortunately, his wife was virtuous and accepted her fate without wavering. She said, "How can one know it is not a blessing?" Her words proved true, and indeed, news of a victory on the frontier came, followed by an imperial decree from the court. The father-in-law then sought favor, and the wife also shared in his honor. Afterward, Wu Sheng returned home with his official title, and the relationship between father-in-law and son-in-law, as well as husband and wife, returned to its original state. There was still a chance for survival. I once passed by the temple of Lu Sheng and saw many poems inscribed on the walls, all of which envied Lu Sheng for meeting Lü Xian and having this pleasant dream. I believe that if Lu Sheng did not have the bone of a marquis, how could he have entered the dream? Therefore, I composed three poems in jest, one of which says, "Lu Sheng has the bone of a marquis, so he could only have one good dream in Handan." This is the meaning. Zhuge Liang once said, "Plans are made by people, but success depends on Heaven." Heaven is fate. Otherwise, if one's fate is not favorable and one foolishly hopes for something beyond one's station, there are certainly many in the world who are both cunning and poor! Moreover, in terms of warfare, it is said that "deceit is not to be despised in war." Even if this incident were to be included in a book of stratagems, what would be wrong with that?

    46

    姑苏某翁
    　　
    　　姑苏某翁，赴饮夜归，见邻人某甲河畔磨刀，诧问：“何为？”不答。再三研诘，始勃然谓：“妇与某生有私，适瞰生来，将掩执而并杀之，以洗门户之羞！”翁把玩其刀曰：“此固当杀，奈刀不利何？我有宝刀，如新发于硎，请暂相假，何如？”甲谢曰：“甚善。”翁笑问：“汝曾杀人也未？”曰：“承平世界，焉敢妄杀人？”曰：“然则汝初次杀人，亦须薄饮壮胆。”乃邀甲至家，出酒一瓶，佐以菹豆，嘱其自酌无躁，爰托如厕，悄往呼妇，告之故，妇与生惶愧叩谢。翁急麾生曰：“去，去！毋缓！”生仓皇遁。翁又嘱妇扃户。归，视甲酒甫罄，问：“尚需酒乎？”曰：“足矣。”遂假刀别翁急归，破扉而入，索生不得，意翁漏泄，恨甚。返叩翁，翁笑曰：“良然，汝且稍安，试听老夫一言。

    Explanation

    46

    In Suzhou, there was an old man named Mr. Wu who returned home from a drinking party at night. He saw his neighbor Mr. Jia grinding a knife by the river and asked, "What are you doing?" Mr. Jia did not answer. Mr. Wu repeatedly questioned him, and finally, Mr. Jia became angry and said, "My wife has been having an affair with someone, and just now I saw him coming. I was going to catch them both and kill them to cleanse my family's shame!" Mr. Wu handled the knife and said, "This person certainly deserves to die, but what can be done with such a dull knife? I have a precious knife, as if freshly sharpened on a whetstone. May I temporarily lend it to you? How about that?" Mr. Jia thanked him and said, "Very good."

    Mr. Wu then asked, "Have you ever killed someone before?" Mr. Jia replied, "In a time of peace, how dare I kill someone recklessly?" Mr. Wu said, "Then, since this is your first time killing someone, you should have a light drink to bolster your courage." He invited Mr. Jia to his home, took out a bottle of wine, served it with pickled vegetables and beans, and told him to drink by himself without haste. He then pretended to go to the toilet and quietly went to call his wife, telling her the reason. The wife and the man bowed in fear and gratitude. Mr. Wu hurriedly told the man to go, saying, "Go, go! Don't delay!" The man fled in panic. Mr. Wu then instructed the wife to lock the door.

    When Mr. Wu returned, he saw that Mr. Jia's wine had just been finished and asked, "Do you need more wine?" Mr. Jia said, "That's enough." Mr. Wu lent the knife and hurried back to Mr. Wu. Mr. Jia broke the door and entered, searching for the man but not finding him. He suspected that Mr. Wu had revealed the plan and was very angry. He returned to Mr. Wu, and Mr. Wu smiled and said, "That's true. You should calm down and listen to what I have to say."

    47

    窭人子娶妇不易，因泄一朝忿杀之，固差快意。但杀之必须鸣官，鸣官即不免笞责，且须薄费。为汝计，亦甚不利。今为汝画一万全策：妇既不贞，不如鬻去；藉得其资，亦可再娶，不较为尽善乎？汝其思之。”甲沉思久之，曰：“翁计诚善。倘其父兄不肯奈何？”翁谓：“但坐以私生一事，即指老夫为左证，计无不谐。”甲如言往诉妇之父兄，果耻其不贞，听甲处分。遂将妇鬻去。越数年，翁以岁饥家落，丐食如秦，藁宿寺中。一日，见群婢拥一丽人，珠冠绣帔，入寺礼佛。健仆三五辈，皆华服、着吉莫靴，伺立门外，屏息耳语，意是命妇。少选，礼佛毕，丽人升舆，瞥见翁，命从者诣翁，备问邦族，遽令携翁归。见其阀阅闬闳，粉垩焜耀。其良人出，年可三十许，容止甚都。丽人谓翁是表叔行，良人慰问殷情。

    Explanation

    47

    A poor man's son finds it difficult to marry a wife, and because of venting a momentary anger, he killed her, which is certainly somewhat satisfying. However, killing someone must involve reporting to the authorities, and reporting to the authorities will inevitably lead to corporal punishment, and it will also require a small expense. For your sake, it is also very disadvantageous. Now, I will propose a perfect plan for you: since the woman was not chaste, it is better to sell her; by obtaining her money, you can remarry, which is not better than the best solution? You should think about it." After a long period of deep thought, A said, "The old man's plan is truly good. If his father and brothers do not agree, what can be done?" The old man said, "Just blame it on the fact that she was born out of wedlock, and point to me as a witness, the plan will definitely work." A followed his advice and went to report to the woman's father and brothers, who indeed were ashamed of her lack of chastity and agreed to let A deal with the matter. Thus, A sold the woman. After several years, the old man, due to famine, fell into poverty, begging for food like in Qin, sleeping in a temple with straw. One day, he saw a group of maids surrounding a beautiful woman, with a pearl crown and embroidered robe, entering the temple to worship Buddha. Three or five strong servants, all dressed in fine clothes and wearing Gimo boots, were waiting outside, holding their breath and whispering, seeming to be a lady-in-waiting. After a short while, after finishing the worship, the beautiful woman climbed into the carriage, glancing at the old man, and ordered the attendants to go to the old man, asking about his family background, and quickly ordered to bring the old man back. He saw that his family was noble and splendidly decorated. His husband came out, around thirty years old, with a very handsome appearance. The beautiful woman said the old man was her cousin, and the husband comforted him with great sincerity.

    48

    翁谛视丽人非他，即某甲之鬻妇也。以其良人在侧，彼此约略寒暄，两心默喻，不敢絮说。良人命左右为翁具汤沐，更衣进馔，栖以精舍。漏二下，两婢秉华烛，导妇至翁寝所，裣叩地。翁急掖起，妇喟然叹曰：“妾曩以一时之误，微翁，白骨已朽，妾之身，翁之赐也！再生之恩，久恨未报，今幸相遇，谨先具黄金二百两为翁寿。”乃命婢列金几上，谓：“不腆微物，出自私忱，聊酬万一。翁请安心宽住，他日言旋，良人自别有馈赆也。”翁喜出非望，凡妇所言，竟不知所答，唯唯而已。先是，其良人以无子，命纪纲吴下买妾，适得妇归；连举双雏，良人大喜，使乳母字之。前岁嫡死，妇已正位，逑好甚敦。良人富固敌国，以妻党故，遇翁甚厚。

    Explanation

    48

    The elderly man looked intently at the beautiful woman, recognizing her as the one he had previously saved, who was now the wife of someone else. With her husband present, they exchanged polite greetings, and though their hearts understood each other, they dared not speak at length. The husband ordered his attendants to prepare baths for the elderly man, change his clothes, and serve him food, lodging him in a refined dwelling. At the second watch, two maids carried lit candles, leading the woman to the elderly man's sleeping quarters, where she bowed respectfully. The elderly man quickly helped her up, and she sighed deeply, saying, "I was once in a dire situation, but for you, I would have perished long ago. My life is your gift! The debt of life I owe you has long gone unrepaid. Now that we have met again, I humbly offer you two hundred taels of gold as a token of my gratitude." She then ordered the maids to place the gold on a table, saying, "This modest gift comes from my sincere heart, and I hope it slightly repays your kindness. Please stay at ease and live comfortably. When I return, my husband will have additional gifts for you." The elderly man was overjoyed beyond expectation, and he could not respond to everything the woman said, only nodding in agreement. Previously, his son-in-law, lacking a child, had ordered a servant to buy a concubine from Wu, and just as the woman returned, she bore twins, which greatly pleased her husband, who entrusted a wet nurse to care for them. Last year, his first wife died, and the woman had become the principal wife, with a very harmonious relationship. Her husband was extremely wealthy, and due to his wife's family, he treated the elderly man with great kindness.

    49

    翁居此半载，左右给役，皆二八俊童；饮食起居，靡不称心，心不自安，屡欲辞归。夫妇以翁年老，不敢久留，为具四时衣服，裘葛单袷悉备，其良人又赆以三千金，命仆马送归，行色甚壮。抵家后，知子为人佣工，招归，出资命其贸易。不数年，居然巨富，往来江湖，称为大贾。后子至楚北，以人命株连逮系，苦不能脱。会堂上官虑囚，阅爰书，见其子姓氏籍贯，问：“翁是否同族？”答谓：“是罪人之父。”官色顿霁。不日，出子于狱，且谓之曰：“汝可速归，烦寄语而翁，某生问讯矣。”其子再拜而出，心甚德之，而莫知其由。又念父固乡民，平日未尝与显者交，何得官言及此？而翁闻子系狱，深以为忧，忽见子归，悲喜交集。子乃述官问讯之言，翁瞿然惊曰：“是矣，是即与某妇有私之某生也。今贵也耶？”遂为子具告往事，父子交庆，设两人木主，尸祝之。

    Explanation

    49

    The old man lived here for half a year, and all the servants on the left and right were handsome youths. In eating, drinking, and daily routines, nothing was unsatisfactory, but he could not rest peacefully and often wanted to return home. His wife, seeing that the old man was elderly, dared not keep him long, so she prepared clothes for all four seasons, including fur, coarse hemp, single-layered, and double-layered garments. His wife also gave him a gift of three thousand gold pieces and ordered servants and horses to send him back, and the departure was very grand. After returning home, he learned that his son was working as a laborer, so he recalled him home, provided capital, and instructed him to engage in trade. In no time, he became a great wealthy man, traveling the rivers and lakes, and was known as a major merchant.

    Later, the son went to the north of Chu and was implicated in a case involving a death and arrested, unable to escape his troubles. When the higher official in the hall was reviewing prisoners, he read the legal documents and saw the son's name, surname, and place of origin. He asked, "Is the old man related to you?" The son replied that he was the old man's son. The official's expression suddenly brightened. In no time, he released the son from prison and told him, "You may return quickly, please convey my greetings to your father, Mr. So-and-so is inquiring after him." The son bowed twice and left, deeply grateful but unaware of the reason. He wondered, "He had never associated with prominent people, so how could the official mention this?" Meanwhile, the old man, hearing that his son was in prison, was deeply worried. Suddenly, he saw his son return, and he was overwhelmed with both sorrow and joy. The son then recounted the official's words, and the old man was startled and exclaimed, "Yes, yes, this is the man who had an affair with a certain woman. Is he now in high position?" He then fully recounted the past events to his son, and father and son rejoiced together. They set up wooden tablets for two ancestors and performed ancestral worship.

    50

    里乘子曰：本夫诛奸，世间尽有，使朴诚畏祸者闻之，恐波及其身，则必掩耳疾走矣；使忠厚不忍者闻之，或谏劝阻止，而不善立言，当其人盛怒之下，必致反唇相稽，不惟不能阻止，且可增其忿焰矣；抑使浮躁好事者闻之，则必幸灾乐祸，从旁哓哓撺掇、怂恿之不暇矣。虽本夫诛奸，律所不禁，而见死不救，亦非所以体上天好生之心。

    Explanation

    50

    Li Chengzi said: The original intent of punishing evil-doers is common in the world. If those who are simple and honest, fearing calamity, hear this, they might be implicated themselves and would cover their ears and flee quickly. If those who are loyal and compassionate, unable to bear it, hear this, they might advise and try to stop it, but if they do not know how to express themselves well, when the person is in a state of intense anger, they will inevitably retaliate with sharp words. Not only will they fail to stop it, but they will also fuel the fire of their anger. Moreover, if those who are impulsive and eager to stir up trouble hear this, they will certainly take pleasure in others' misfortunes and be busy urging and inciting from the sidelines. Although the original intent of punishing evil-doers is not prohibited by law, failing to help someone in danger is also not in line with the benevolent nature of Heaven.

    51

    我不预闻，于我无尤也；我闻之，固不必谏劝阻止，以逆彼之听，又何必撺掇怂恿，以助彼之虐乎？某翁洞悉人情，窥某甲怒气方张，愿假刀以利其用，又劝饮以壮其胆，语语中肯，甲焉得不堕其术中？而于是托如厕，悄往告妇，寓谏劝阻止于撺掇怂恿之中，斟酌可谓尽善矣。及甲恨而返叩，即直承不讳，并为剖陈利害，策画万全，甲妇与某生之命，固赖翁而生，即甲他日苟得再娶，而幸延一线之嗣者，亦出翁之赐也。大抵遇此等事，谏劝阻止者少，撺掇怂恿者多，有损于人而无益于己，不知是何居心也！问有能不动声色、排难解纷，如翁之热肠为人、从容周密者乎？予尝谓：人心即天心也，顺天者昌。观翁后日，父子两受其报，天心不大可见哉！

    Explanation

    51

    If I am not informed beforehand, I have no fault in it; if I hear of it, I certainly do not need to advise, dissuade, or stop it, nor should I go against his hearing. Why should I incite or encourage him to aid his cruelty? A certain old man deeply understands human nature and observed that Mr. A's anger was just beginning to rise. He willingly lent a knife to sharpen his use and encouraged drinking to bolster his courage. Every word was to the point, and how could Mr. A not fall into his scheme? Pretending to go to the toilet, he secretly went to inform the wife, embedding advice, dissuasion, and prevention within incitement and encouragement. His consideration can be said to be perfect. When Mr. A returned in anger, the old man immediately confessed without reservation and explained the benefits and harms, planning for absolute safety. The wife of Mr. A and the life of Mr. B were indeed saved by the old man. Even if Mr. A were to remarry in the future and fortunately continue the family line, it would also be due to the old man's favor. Generally, in such matters, those who advise, dissuade, or stop are few, while those who incite or encourage are many. This is harmful to others and beneficial to oneself; one cannot fathom what kind of heart this is! Is there anyone who can resolve difficulties and disputes without raising a fuss, like the old man's warm-hearted and meticulous nature? I have often said: human heart is the heart of heaven, and those who follow heaven will prosper. Seeing how the old man and his son both received their rewards in the future, can the heart of heaven not be clearly seen?

    52

    余徐二公轶事
    　　
    　　徽州黟县余公梦岩，名毓祥。微时授徒，馆谷甚菲。岁除，无资祀先，夫妇枵腹，愁对太息。公身仅着一敝缊袍，一旧羊皮短褕。鸡鸣而起，拟趁早墟，贳短褕可得三千钱，市牲酒薪米之属，聊以卒岁。独行五里许，路经一岭，隐约见树林中有人影，叱之不答，固疑是鬼。迫而视之，则一男子投缳树技也。大骇，急解缳放卧地上，移时顿苏。诘其自经之由，其人忸怩泣对曰：“小人负佃租若干，主人迫索，倘不急偿，便撺取妻相抵。妻去，儿在襁褓，失乳必死。小人既不忍妻之生离，又不忍儿之短折，左右思维，不如先填沟壑为得也。”问：“租值须钱几何？”曰：“三千足矣。

    Explanation

    52

    The Extraordinary Deeds of Mr. Yu and Mr. Xu

    In the county of Yi in Huizhou, there was a man named Yu Mengyan, whose given name was Yuyang. When he was poor, he taught students and received very little remuneration. On New Year's Eve, he had no funds to offer sacrifices to his ancestors, and his wife and he were starving, sighing in deep distress. Mr. Yu was dressed in only one tattered, worn-out robe and an old sheepskin jacket. At cockcrow, he rose early, intending to go to the early market to rent out his short jacket, which could earn him three thousand coins, with which he could buy meat, wine, firewood, and rice to just get through the year. He walked alone for about five miles, passing by a mountain ridge, when he vaguely saw a human shadow in the forest. He scolded it, but it did not respond, and he was certain it was a ghost. Approaching to look at it, he saw a man hanging himself on a tree branch. He was greatly frightened, quickly untied the noose, and laid him down on the ground. After a while, the man suddenly came to. Mr. Yu interrogated him about the reason for his hanging, and the man, embarrassed and crying, replied: "I owe a certain amount of rent to my landlord, who is pressing for payment. If I do not quickly repay it, he will take my wife as compensation. If my wife leaves, my child, still in swaddling clothes, will lose its milk and die. I cannot bear the separation of my wife and the premature death of my child. After thinking back and forth, I decided that it would be better to fill the ditch first." Mr. Yu asked, "How much money is needed for the rent?" The man replied, "Three thousand coins will suffice."

    53

    ”公乃以短褕付之，曰：“速将去贳钱偿主人，慎勿出此下策。”其人崩角在地，叩问姓名。公麾令速去：“勿多言，吾不责尔偿，问姓名何为者！”其人叩头起，携短褕而去。公日晡归家，夫人问：“衣已贳乎？”曰：“否否，吾不自慎，为人窃取去矣。”夫人亦无怒词，反以笑言相慰。时夫妇年俱逾五十，尚无子。未几，夫人竟有娠，生辛伯司马兆元。是年为嘉庆丙子科，公领乡荐。丁丑，联捷成进士，观政礼部，擢郎中，在官有政声。生平不苟取予，不轻然诺，乡人以贤者称之。后，投缳男子贸易小阜，欲报曩德，苦不知姓名，遍访乡党，群悬揣非公不能。姑备仪诣谢，公峻拒之曰：“若误矣，我无是也。

    Explanation

    53

    The official handed over the short robe to the man, saying, "Hurry to go and borrow money to repay the owner, and be careful not to resort to this low strategy." The man knelt on the ground with his head bowed, asking for the name. The official waved his hand and ordered him to leave quickly, saying, "Do not speak more, I do not blame you for repayment, why ask for the name?" The man rose and left with the short robe. The official returned home in the afternoon, and his wife asked, "Has the robe been borrowed?" He replied, "No, no, I was not careful, and it was stolen by someone." His wife did not scold him, but comforted him with words of laughter. At the time, both the husband and wife were over fifty years old and still had no children. Soon after, the wife finally became pregnant and gave birth to Xin Bo, Sima Zhao Yuan. That year was the Jiaqing Bizi year, and the husband passed the provincial examination. In the following year, he successively passed the imperial examination, served in the Ministry of Rites, was promoted to the rank of Langzhong, and had a good reputation in office. Throughout his life, he did not take things lightly, did not lightly promise, and was called a virtuous person by the local people. Later, a man who hanged himself engaged in small trade, wanting to repay past kindness, but struggled to know the name, and searched the local community. All guessed that it could not be anyone but the official. Temporarily preparing to visit and thank, the official resolutely rejected him, saying, "You are mistaken, I did not do that."

    54

    ”公年登大耋，告归林下。易箦时，辛伯叩问是事，曰：“此盛德事，吾何能为？大抵乡人以我平日迂方，或拟议及之耳。”予与辛伯交最昵，问之果然。嗟乎！观余公已事，叹造物试验贤者，可谓至巧、至酷。彼索逋者，必须钱三千，若暗中计，短褕之值恰以相抵；少一钱不可，多一钱亦不可。在凡人处此，岂能一钱不留，竟如公慨然持赠，空拳而归，直行所无事乎？而夫人闻之，绝无怨言，反以笑语相慰，亦可以谓难矣！世谓行阴德事，不使人知，余公有焉。后，吾友汉军徐公可司马同善言，其尊人铁孙观察为孝廉时，岁暮，存馆金三十两，归家途中，值索逋鬻妻事，价恰符馆金之数，亦慨以相赠。

    Explanation

    54

    The old gentleman, having reached the age of eighty, retired to the countryside. At the moment of his death, Xin Bo asked him about this matter, and he replied, "This is a great virtuous deed, how could I do it? The villagers probably thought of me as old-fashioned in daily life, so they might have speculated about it." I was closest friends with Xin Bo, and when I asked him, he confirmed it. Alas! Seeing what happened to the old gentleman, I lamented that Heaven tests the virtuous, and it can be said that it is both ingenious and cruel. The moneylender needed three thousand coins, and if calculated in secret, the value of the short jacket just matched the amount; one coin less was unacceptable, one coin more was also unacceptable. For an ordinary person in this situation, how could they not leave even one coin, yet the old gentleman gave it away generously and returned empty-handed, as if nothing had happened? And the lady heard about it and had no complaints, instead comforting him with laughter and words, which can also be considered difficult! The world says that performing good deeds in secret, not letting others know, the old gentleman had such virtue. Later, my friend Hanjun Xu Gong Ke, who was a military司马 and well-versed in the classics, told me that his father, who was an observer and a filial and honest man, at the end of the year, left thirty taels of silver for the school. On his way home, he encountered a case of a moneylender selling his wife, and the price just matched the amount of the school money, so he also gave it away generously.

    55

    徐公平生乐善不倦，笔难尽述，以此与余公相似，故连类及之，而不特书也。徐公讳荣，丙申进士。由县令起家，洊晋福建汀漳龙道。抑予闻之，我朝黟县进士，自余公始；广东驻防汉军举人，自嘉庆丙子科徐公始。余公五十后始得子，且多孙焉。考终，祀乡贤、名宦等祠。徐公居官，善政不可枚举。其最著者：守绍兴时，创修壖堤，活数十百万生灵，万世利赖。公尝曰：“吾所在有功德于民，子孙必昌！”信然。公督兵新安，殉难黟县之渔亭，赐甚厚，凡建专祠尸祝者数十处。今长子伯安虑善，权浙江金华府知府；次公可同善，即选通判，并加同知衔；次春漪传善，现官四川会理州知州。孙十人，皆能以诗书世其家。

    Explanation

    55

    Mr. Xu devoted his entire life to doing good without tiring, and it is difficult to describe all his deeds in writing. For this reason, he is similar to Mr. Yu, and I mention him along with others, not just recording his story. Mr. Xu's name was Rong, and he was a Jinshi (imperial scholar) in the Bing Shen year. He began his career as a county magistrate and later served as the governor of the Ting Zhang Long Circuit in Fujian. I have heard that in our dynasty, the first Jinshi from Yi County was Mr. Yu, and the first Han army scholar in Guangdong was Mr. Xu in the Jiaqing Bingsubai year. Mr. Yu had children after the age of fifty and many grandchildren. He died a peaceful death in old age and was worshiped in the temples of local sages and famous officials. Mr. Xu's good governance cannot be counted, and his most notable achievement was when he served as governor of Shaoxing, where he created and repaired dikes, saving tens of millions of lives, and benefiting future generations. He once said, "Wherever I serve, if I have done good for the people, my descendants will surely flourish!" Indeed, he did. He was in charge of troops in Xin'an and died a martyr in Yixian's Yu Ting. He received generous rewards, and dozens of places built special temples to honor him. His eldest son Bo'an is concerned with good deeds and currently serves as the governor of Jinhua Prefecture in Zhejiang. His second son Kotong is selected as a deputy magistrate and has been promoted to the rank of Tongzhi. His third son Chunyi continues the tradition of good deeds and currently serves as the governor of Huili Prefecture in Sichuan. He has ten grandchildren who can maintain their family's reputation through poetry and books.

    56

    里乘子曰：予尝谓：天下至善之事，非有厚德、厚福者不能遇。二公福德过人，故所遇若合符节。当其初时，造物之所以试验之者，不可谓非至巧至酷，而其所以报之者，不可谓不厚。若我辈庸碌无奇，造物不甚留意，遂亦不必试验。予自知德凉福薄，断不能几及二公之万一，然不敢不勉也！

    Explanation

    56

    text
    Li Chengzi said: I have often said that the most perfect things in the world can only be encountered by those with great virtue and great fortune. The two sirs possess extraordinary virtue and fortune, so what they encounter fits perfectly like a seal and its impression. When they were first tested by the Creator, it cannot be called anything but extremely ingenious and cruel, yet the rewards they received cannot be called anything but abundant. As for us, who are ordinary and unremarkable, the Creator does not pay much attention to us, so there is no need for such trials. I know I have little virtue and little fortune, certainly cannot come close to even a tiny fraction of what the two sirs have, yet I dare not not strive.

    57

    杭城某翁
    　　
    　　杭城某翁，富埒王侯，而艰于子。姬妾甚众，卒无兰兆。翁年已逾曰艾，自念无嗣，何需多金？遂矢行善事，且不求人知。逾年，某姬果举一雄，方颐丰下，贺者群称英物，翁心颇慰。儿七岁就傅，徇齐殊众，一家珍爱之。无何，儿环唇生七疔，痛彻心髓，症甚危殆。凡精岐黄者，皆罗致家中，翁署券：“患愈，酬白金三千镒。”诸医涎其赏，商榷立方，卒无效。创且日甚，水浆不入。医谓唇疔最毒难治，此多至七枚，遍稽古书，皆无此症。群谢无能，相率辞去。翁愁思无策，惟率诸姬环榻相向而泣。儿仅存息一丝，坐待其毙而已。

    Explanation

    57

    A certain elderly gentleman from Hangzhou was extremely wealthy, rivaling kings and dukes, but had difficulty in having children. He had many concubines, yet finally, there was no sign of a male heir. After a year, one of his concubines indeed gave birth to a son, who had a square jaw and full lower face. Those who saw him praised him as a person of great talent and virtue, and the old man felt greatly relieved. When the child was seven years old, he began his education and showed exceptional qualities, earning the love and admiration of the entire family. However, soon after, seven carbuncles grew around his lips, causing him extreme pain that penetrated to the bone marrow. The condition was very critical. The old man sought out all those skilled in the art of medicine and made a formal agreement: if the illness was cured, a reward of 3,000 taels of silver would be given. The doctors coveted the reward and discussed and formulated a treatment plan, but ultimately, it was ineffective. The sores worsened day by day, and the child could not take in any food or drink. The doctors said that lip carbuncles are the most toxic and difficult to treat, and there is no record of such a condition in ancient books. They all admitted their inability to help and left one after another. The old man felt helpless and distressed, leading all the concubines to sit around the bed and weep together. Only a thread of life remained in the child, and they waited for him to die.

    58

    忽有媪丐于门，聒求无厌，阍者以少主垂危，谯呵之。翁闻，出责阍者，如言给媪。媪合手称谢，见翁泪承睫，诘知儿疾，曰：“老妇有儿，幼亦患此，曾遇异人，谓名‘七星攒月’，危症也。惟十二岁内小儿所下蚘虫百条，捣饼叠敷之可治。试之，良瘥。后以方传人，皆效。今公子得遇老妇，合是有缘，敢为翁贺。”翁喜，如媪言，悬格征求。凡有小儿者，咸以药下蚘虫争献求赏。敷之果愈。先是，翁闻媪言，入谕于众；比出延媪，不知所往，而所给之物固在。或谓翁素虔奉天竺观音，此盖菩萨化身，以旌善人云。儿弱冠成进士，事亲不仕。生子五，皆读书成名。至今科第不绝，尚称素封焉。

    Explanation

    58

    Suddenly, an old woman began begging at the door, persistently demanding without satisfaction. The gatekeeper, thinking the young master was gravely ill, shouted at her. The master heard and came out to scold the gatekeeper, giving the old woman what she asked for. The old woman clasped her hands and thanked him, seeing the master with tears in his eyes, she inquired about the illness of the child. She said, "I once had a son who also suffered from this condition. I met a strange person who named it 'Seven Stars Gathered Under the Moon,' a dangerous illness. Only if one hundred roundworms from a child under twelve years old are crushed into a paste and layered on can it be cured. I tried it, and it proved effective. Later, the prescription was passed on to others, and it all worked. Now the young master has met this old woman, it seems to be a matter of fate, dare I congratulate you, sir." The master was pleased and, as the old woman said, posted a notice to seek the remedy. All those with children competed to offer the medicine to expel roundworms and seek reward. Applied it, and it indeed healed. Before this, the master heard the old woman's words and told everyone inside. When he went out to invite the old woman, she was nowhere to be found. But the things given to her were still there. Someone said that the master had always been devoutly worshipping the Bodhisattva Guanyin from India. This must be the incarnation of the Bodhisattva, coming to reward the virtuous. The son, upon reaching adulthood, became a Jinshi (imperial scholar) and took care of his parents without seeking official position. He had five sons, all of whom became famous through their studies. Until now, the family's success in the imperial examinations continues, and they are still considered wealthy in learning.

    59

    里乘子曰：翁年已逾曰艾，忽自念无嗣，何需多金？是天牖其衷也。其矢行善事，难；其不求人知，尤难。盖初存心固施不望报，而天必报之，彼苍苍者，何尝丝毫负人哉！观此，叹“为富不仁”是阳虎之说耳。而天下之为富翁者，闻此翁之风，亦可以兴矣。

    Explanation

    59

    Li Chengzi said: The old man, having surpassed the age of sixty, suddenly reflected upon himself that he had no heir, questioning why one needed much wealth. This was heaven opening his heart. He sincerely performed good deeds, which was difficult; even more difficult was not seeking recognition from others. Initially, he sincerely performed good deeds without expecting a reward, but heaven would certainly reward him. Heaven, how could it ever fail anyone? Considering this, one might lament that 'being wealthy and unkind' is merely the view of Yang Hu. But those who are wealthy throughout the world, hearing of this old man's example, can also be inspired.

    60

    左生
    　　
    　　邑诸生左泰，年少，才貌双俊，而苦无行。邻翁某走无常，性方正，喜规人过。尝谓生曰：“昨至阴司，窃觇吾邑士人禄籍，君贵居极品，寿享期颐，子孙昌盛。以曾挑某寡妇致失节，又与某处女有私，干怒冥王，已镌禄秩二级，减寿二纪矣。君宜自爱，后福尚未可量也。”生闻而骇惧，迹为少歛。未几，故态复萌，荡检益甚。翁见之，愠曰：“前老夫为郎君言，将以为妄耶？昨又见君籍，不但淫恶甚多，且又唆某甲健讼，某翁倾家，兼毙多命。冥王大怒，镌君五级，寿仅花甲矣。不速悔而痛改之，不可挽也！”无何，又遇翁于途，责之曰：“君不听吾言，冥王以积恶多端，罚仅以布衣终，子孙亦不能显达。是真不可以救药矣！”生俱漫应之。

    Explanation

    60

    Left Scholar

    A young scholar from the locality named Zuo Tai was exceptionally talented and good-looking, but he lacked proper conduct. A neighbor, an old man who served as a messenger of the dead, was upright in character and liked to point out others' faults. He once told the scholar, "Yesterday, I went to the underworld and secretly observed the register of scholars in our locality. You are destined to occupy the highest rank, enjoy a long life of a hundred years, and have a flourishing family lineage. However, because you once seduced a widow, causing her to lose her virtue, and had an affair with a maiden, you angered the ruler of the underworld. As a result, your official rank has been reduced by two levels, and your lifespan has been shortened by twenty-four years. You should take care of yourself, as your future blessings are still uncertain." Upon hearing this, the scholar was filled with fear and slightly restrained his behavior. However, after a short while, his old habits returned, and he became even more unrestrained. The old man saw this and angrily said, "Before, I warned you, did you think I was lying? Yesterday, I saw your register again, and it shows that you have committed many acts of lust and evil, and you have incited lawsuits, caused a man to lose his family, and even caused multiple deaths. The ruler of the underworld is furious and has reduced your rank by five levels, and your lifespan is now only sixty years. If you do not quickly repent and reform, it cannot be reversed!" Shortly after, the old man met the scholar on the road and scolded him, "You did not listen to my words, and the ruler of the underworld, due to your accumulated sins, has punished you to end your life as a commoner, and your descendants will also not be able to achieve prominence. This is truly beyond redemption!" The scholar merely responded indifferently.

    61

    既而又遇，翁直唾生面曰：“孺子真不可教，今死期至矣！可若何？”生始大恐，跽叩其由。翁曰：“昨见冥王稽君籍，令鬼吏权衡功过。吏检君恶籍，每恶以寸纸书之，纸片累累，堆积如山。冥王立叱勾摄君魂，削君寿禄，且斩嗣续。吏称：‘左某尚有善籍可抵。’王颔之，命稽善籍。须臾，吏呈寸纸，大声唱曰：‘左某生平奉事继母至孝。仅此一善。’王令试权其轻重，见吏持一天平至，将恶籍纸片数百张堆置于左，复置善籍寸纸于右，权之轻重相埒。王色顿霁曰：‘孝之为德，如此其盛乎！’旋命吏但削君禄，寿于某年月日时，勾摄结案。姑念事继母孝，留一子以延宗祧。”生闻，赧汗如雨，自言：“愿痛自改过，尚可挽回否？”翁曰：“悔已晚矣。”掉头不顾而去。后生果如期而卒，三子仅存一焉。

    Explanation

    61

    Encountered again shortly, the old man spat directly at the boy's face and said, "The boy truly cannot be taught; now the time of death has come! What can be done?" The boy was greatly frightened and knelt, asking for the reason. The old man said, "Yesterday, I saw the King of the Underworld examine your records and ordered the ghost officials to weigh your merits and faults. The official checked your record of sins, each sin written on a small piece of paper, the pieces of paper piled up like a mountain. The King of the Underworld immediately ordered the soul to be taken, to cut your lifespan and fortune, and to cut off your descendants. The official said, 'There is still a record of good deeds that can offset this.' The King nodded and ordered to check the record of good deeds. In a moment, the official presented a small piece of paper and shouted loudly, 'Zuo was filial to his stepmother throughout his life. This is the only good deed.' The King ordered to weigh the merits and faults, and saw an official holding a scale, placing hundreds of pieces of paper from the record of sins on the left side and the small piece of paper from the record of good deeds on the right side. The weights were equal. The King's expression suddenly brightened and said, 'Filial piety as a virtue is so great!' Immediately, the King ordered the official to only cut your fortune, your lifespan to a certain date and time, and to finalize the case. Out of consideration for your filial piety to your stepmother, one son is left to continue the family line." The boy heard this and was filled with shame, sweating profusely, and said, "I wish to deeply repent and ask if it is still possible to change?" The old man said, "It is too late to regret." He turned away without looking back and left. The boy indeed died at the appointed time, and three sons, only one survived.

    62

    里乘子曰：左生幸遇邻翁，屡进药石，以攻其疾，诚为难得。乃始闻骇惧，继则故态复萌，且加甚焉，一误再误，六州铁直不能铸此大错。天罚所及，孽由自作，夫复何怨？而一念之孝，权其轻重，善恶两平，卒赖此以延一线，可见天道赏罚之公也。信乎冥王之言曰：“孝之为德，如此其盛乎！”

    Explanation

    62

    Li Chengzi said: Left Master was fortunate to meet a neighbor-elder who repeatedly offered sharp advice to treat his moral flaws, truly a rare and valuable encounter. Initially, he felt shock and fear, but then his old habits resurfaced and became even worse, making one mistake after another. Even six states' worth of iron couldn't forge such a great mistake. The punishment of heaven has arrived, and the sins were self-inflicted; what is there to complain about? Ultimately, he relied on a single thought of filial piety to weigh the pros and cons, finding a balance between good and evil, and thus prolonging life by a thread. This shows the fairness of heaven's rewards and punishments. Indeed, the words of the underworld king say: "The virtue of filial piety, how great it is!"

    63

    雷击二女
    　　
    　　江苏葛菊人言，太湖西洞庭山有村，曰后堡，人烟辐辏，多以织绸为业。绵绸以茧网为之，谓茧面第一层茸丝，其薄如网也。先是，有湖州某叟，每年蚕熟，辄舟载茧网至后堡贩卖。村有某媪，其子外出傭工，家惟孙女十二岁、外孙女十四岁，相依过活。道光十三年六月，某叟来贩蚕网，担货到村，子十三岁，留以守舟。二女恒至舟，与叟子戏，日久益密无猜。叟售货既罄，将归，以风逆不能解缆，歛佛番十二元纳橐，置舟中，仍携拣剩残网到村，贬价卖讫，旋舟，索橐不得，怒诘其子，鞭之几死。岸人佥为缓颊，咎叟疏虞，不能专责乃子。叟无词，含泪刺舟，怏怏而去。是月二十四日，某媪将午炊，以石敲火不得，出门乞火。

    Explanation

    63

    Title: The Two Girls Struck by Lightning

    Text:
    According to Ge Juren from Jiangsu, there is a village called Houbao on the West Dongting Mountain in Taihu Lake. The village is densely populated, and most of its inhabitants are engaged in silk weaving as their profession. Silk is made from the fine, thin layer of silk from silkworm cocoons, which is as delicate as a net. Previously, there was an old man from Huzhou who, every year when the silkworms had finished spinning their cocoons, would load his boat with silkworm cocoons and travel to Houbao to sell them. In the village, there was an old woman whose son had gone out to work, leaving only her twelve-year-old granddaughter and her fourteen-year-old granddaughter-in-law to live together. In the sixth month of the thirteenth year of the Daoguang era (1833), the old man came to sell silkworm cocoons. He brought his goods to the village, left his thirteen-year-old son behind to guard the boat, and the two girls often visited the boat, playing with the old man's son. Over time, they became closer and trusted each other more. After the old man sold all his goods, he was about to return home, but the wind was against him, preventing him from untying the ropes. He collected twelve yuan in small coins and placed them in his bag, which he put in the boat. He then carried the remaining, less desirable silkworm cocoons to the village, selling them at a reduced price until finished. As he turned to leave, he could not find his bag. Angered, he questioned his son, whipping him almost to death. The people on the shore all tried to mediate, blaming the old man for carelessness and not solely holding his son responsible. The old man had no defense, with tears in his eyes, he rowed away in a sullen mood. On the twenty-fourth day of that month, the old woman was about to start her midday meal, but could not strike a fire with a stone, so she went out to borrow a fire.

    64

    忽烈风暴雨，雷电大作，村民某甲见媪宅火起，趋报媪。媪骂曰：“促狭儿，无妄咒人！我敲火不得，始出求火，家中那得火发？”甲曰：“此何等事，敢作诳语！谓予不信，可自觇之。”媪急冒雨趋归，果见火焚屋内。哭央村众扑灭，火顿息，雷雨亦止。入视，二女手扶瓷坛，跽死院中，两太阳穴各洞如针孔，血水涔涔然流出，面不改色。众甚讶之，坛故盛爆豆，试共发之，则豆下叟之橐资存焉。村民多与叟善，急遣人驰送湖州。至，则叟与妻已于昨夜投缳死矣。盖叟归途，复痛责其子，逼迫投河。到家，妻询得其故，既痛子死，又以失资无以为生，夫妻交谪，半夜俱自经云。村人回，始知雷击两女为有由也。吁！可畏哉！

    Explanation

    64

    A Sudden Storm and Its Aftermath

    A sudden, violent storm erupted with fierce winds and heavy rain, accompanied by thunder and lightning. A villager named Jia saw smoke rising from the old woman's house and rushed to report it. The old woman scolded him, saying, "You mischievous boy, you're cursing me without cause! I couldn't strike a fire, so I went out to find one—how could there be a fire in my house?" Jia replied, "What kind of thing is this? How dare you speak falsely? If you don't believe me, you can check it yourself." The old woman, fearing the truth, hurried home in the rain and indeed saw the house burning inside. She cried out and begged the villagers to put it out, and the fire was quickly extinguished, the thunderstorm also ceasing. Upon entering, they found two women kneeling and holding a porcelain jar in the courtyard, both temples pierced like needle holes, blood flowing continuously, yet their faces remained unchanged. The villagers were greatly astonished. The jar was found to contain exploding beans, and when they tried to open it, the old man's purse and funds were discovered beneath the beans. The villagers, who were friendly with the old man, quickly sent someone to deliver the treasure to his hometown in Huzhou. Upon arrival, they learned that the old man and his wife had both hanged themselves the previous night. It turned out that on his way home, the old man had severely scolded his son, forcing him to throw himself into the river. When he returned home, his wife learned the reason and, grieving the death of her son and the loss of their funds, which left them unable to make a living, the couple blamed each other. In the middle of the night, they both hanged themselves. The villagers, upon returning, realized that the lightning striking the two women was not without cause. Alas, how terrible it is!

    65

    里乘子曰：或谓二女憨稚无知，戏攘橐资，初未尝出于有心，天之所罚，无乃太刻。予谓不然，大抵上天好生，于人命尤极珍惜。无论二女有心无心，而某叟一门由失资绝灭净尽，则惨莫惨于此矣。似此恶作剧，天怒已极，虽在童稚，顾可恕哉！
    　　
    　　雷击某氏子
    　　
    　　皖城怀宁某媪，孀居。一子年弱冠，貌甚朴愿，为某官傔从，服役勤慎，能得主人意。同治七年三月十八日，夜漏二下，其子启户为主人瀹茗，忽暴雷一声击死，僵跽户外。媪闻之来，抚尸哭曰：“吾儿素朴愿，天乎冤哉！何罪而遭此惨也！”雷又震震有声，佥戒媪勿妄语干神怒，雷乃止。后有人言其子曾盗贩陶器某甲钱五百文，甲夫妇诟怨，无以营生，俱投缳死。事已隔一年，雷始击之，尚是恕也。吁！以五百钱毙二命，天怒之烈，不亦宜乎！

    Explanation

    65

    Li Chengzi said: Some people say that the two girls were naive and ignorant, playfully stealing money, and initially did not act with intent. Is it not too harsh for heaven to punish them? I do not agree. Generally, heaven favors life and especially values human life. Whether the two girls acted with or without intent, the entire family of the old man was completely wiped out due to losing money, which is nothing more tragic than this. Such mischief has reached the peak of heaven's anger. Even if they were young children, can they be forgiven?

    Lightning struck the son of a certain person.

    In Huaining, Wan Cheng, there lived a widow. She had one son who was in his early twenties, looking very simple and honest. He served as an attendant for a certain official, working diligently and cautiously, and was able to please his master. On the 18th day of the third month in the seventh year of Tongzhi, at two hours past midnight, the son opened the door to prepare tea for his master. Suddenly, a loud thunder struck him dead, and he lay stiffly kneeling outside the door. The widow heard about it and came running, embracing the body and crying, "My son was always simple and honest. Oh, heaven, how unjust! What sin did he commit to suffer such a tragedy?" The thunder continued to rumble, and everyone warned the widow not to speak rashly and offend the gods. The thunder then stopped. Later, someone said that the son had stolen and sold pottery worth five hundred coins to a certain person named A. The couple of person A complained bitterly and could not make a living, so they both hanged themselves. The incident had occurred a year ago, and the thunder finally struck him, which was still a lenient punishment. Alas! With five hundred coins, two lives were taken. The intensity of heaven's anger, isn't it appropriate?

    66

    里乘子曰：此子盗某甲钱，岂其母不知也耶？观母之呼天鸣冤，且言无罪，是其平日貌为朴愿，不惟欺人，且直欺其母矣！而以五百钱毙二命，致干天罚，谚谓“雷惯击老实人”，信然。
    　　
    　　叶孝廉
    　　
    　　陈西堂言，其中表清苑叶孝廉某，家居，忽有人赍书至，函签写叶某开拆，姓名与己相符，发函视之，盖母与子家报也。略云：“闻汝登贤书，甚喜。但数年未接汝只字，心甚悬念。我日形衰老，盼汝甚切。汝妇贤孝，经营菽水，心劳力竭，目下益难搘持，若不将汝妇改醮，势必俱死。然汝妇不忍去，我亦不忍舍也。兹乘便特寄汝知，有信即给原人带回。盼切盼切。汝妇去留，亦候汝信定夺也。”云云。孝廉得书，心知误投，以书词迫切，不从权赒济，恐他日其子归，不免破镜之悲。遂备白金二十两，并具复函给来人带回。略云：“自违膝下，无日不思。白云在天，瞻望泣血。

    Explanation

    66

    The village elder said: "This son stole money from someone named Jia. How could his mother not know? Seeing the mother cry out to heaven and lament her innocence, and claim to be innocent, it is that he usually appears simple and honest, not only deceiving people, but also directly deceiving his mother! And with five hundred coins, he killed two lives, leading to divine punishment. It is said that thunder often strikes honest people, indeed so."

    Ye Scholar-official

    Chen Xitang said: Among the officials of Qingyuan, there was a scholar-official named Ye. At home, suddenly someone brought a letter, the envelope was signed to be opened by Ye, and the name matched his own. He opened the letter to read it, and it was a letter from the mother and son's household. It roughly says: "Hearing that you have passed the examination for the literati, very happy. But for several years, I have not received a single word from you, my heart is very anxious. I am growing old day by day, I miss you very much. Your wife is virtuous and filial, managing the household and providing for daily needs, tired both mentally and physically, it is even more difficult to support the household now. If we do not remarry your wife, we will all die. However, your wife cannot bear to leave, I also cannot bear to let her go. I am taking this opportunity to inform you, if you have a reply, please send it back to the original person. I look forward to it eagerly. Whether your wife stays or leaves, please decide after receiving your reply." The scholar-official received the letter and knew it was mistakenly delivered, because the letter's words are urgent, if he does not act with compassion and provide assistance, fearing that one day the son will return, he will inevitably face the sorrow of a broken mirror. He then prepared twenty taels of silver, and wrote a reply to be returned by the messenger. It roughly says: "Since leaving my parents, I have not a day without thinking of them. White clouds in the sky, looking up and weeping blood."

    67

    顷奉慈谕，欣悉起居康健，下怀顿慰。儿幸叨一第，急欲回家，因事纠缠，猝难遄发，兹备白金二十两，托原人带回，付儿妇暂供甘旨。目下窘况，不言可喻，儿妇贤孝，素所深信，姑且耐贫，儿归自有料理。团聚不远，决不再事稽迟，致劳倚闾盼望也。”其母得回书，以为果己也子者。乃以二十金作资斧，携妇到清苑，径投某宅。相视愕然，孝廉具陈崖末，爰另屋居之，并给日用，嘱姑妇安居，勿事忧虑，当留心为访其子音耗。后，其子果领顺天乡荐，回家访母与妻不得，迹至清苑，母子夫妻相见，悲喜交集。母为道孝廉高义，其子感泣，与孝廉约为兄弟。从此休戚相关，有逾骨肉。孝廉子若孙，今已相继举于乡矣。谓非好善之报哉！

    Explanation

    67

    I have just received your kind message, and I am glad to learn that you are in good health, which greatly comforts my heart. I am fortunate to have obtained a degree, and I am eager to return home. However, due to various matters, it is suddenly difficult to depart quickly. I have prepared twenty taels of silver and entrusted the original messenger to bring it back, giving it to my wife temporarily to provide for her needs. The current difficult situation does not need to be explained; my wife is virtuous and filial, and I have always deeply believed in her. She will temporarily endure poverty, and when I return, I will handle everything. Reunion is not far off, and I will definitely not delay again, causing you to wait anxiously by the door. Her mother received the reply and believed it to be from her son. She then took the twenty taels of silver as travel expenses, accompanied by her daughter-in-law, and went directly to Qingyuan, where they went straight to a certain residence. They looked at each other in surprise, and the Filial and Honest Man explained the situation. He then provided them with a separate house to live in, supplied their daily necessities, and instructed the mother and daughter-in-law to settle down and not worry, promising to look for news of her son. Later, her son indeed passed the provincial examination in Shuntian and returned home to search for his mother and wife but could not find them. He traced them to Qingyuan, where the mother, son, and daughter-in-law met, and they were overwhelmed with mixed feelings of joy and sorrow. The mother spoke of the Filial and Honest Man's high virtue, and her son was deeply moved and wept, agreeing to become brothers with the Filial and Honest Man. From then on, they shared both good and bad times, becoming closer than blood relatives. The Filial and Honest Man's son and grandson have now successively passed the provincial examinations. Is this not a reward for doing good deeds?

    68

    里乘子曰：人心即天心也。初误以姓名之同，鬼神使之，径投其书，情词迫切，惨不忍闻。天意以为：安得阅书者善为行权，巧事斡旋，庶不使鸾凤分飞乎？乃孝廉果能暗合天意，函金婉答，措词不即不离，极为得体。他日其人果领乡荐归来，母妻团聚。询知孝廉高义，不惟其人感泣，即百世后耳其事者，亦无不为之同声感泣也。天道报施善人，子孙世济其美，不蓍可券矣！

    Explanation

    68

    Master Li Cheng said: The human heart is none other than the Heavenly heart. At first, he was mistaken, thinking that the similarity of names was the work of spirits, and so directly sent the letter, its words filled with urgent emotion, so sorrowful that it was unbearable to hear. Heaven's will pondered: How can the one who reads this letter be skilled in exercising discretion, adept at handling the matter, so as not to cause the phoenix and the crane to part ways? Indeed, the filial and upright scholar was able to secretly align with Heaven's will, sending a letter with a gift of gold and answering in a tactful and gentle manner, his words neither too direct nor too distant, extremely proper in tone. Later, that person indeed returned after receiving the local recommendation, and mother and wife were reunited. Upon learning of the scholar's noble virtue, not only did he himself weep with emotion, but even those who would hear of this story a hundred generations later would also weep together in sympathy. The way of Heaven rewards the virtuous; the descendants will continue to carry forth their excellence—it can be confidently guaranteed without the need for divination.

    69

    僵尸
    　　
    　　家大人尝训之曰：“尔曹客游日多，如遇生地，必使僮仆同室宿，且切不可灭灯，以防不测。”因言壮年客姑孰，寓某庵中，时方初秋，天气嫩凉。夜就枕，反侧不能成寐，目微启，见案上灯光暗如萤；距榻尺有咫，一物从地出半身，长尺许，黄毛毧毧，状类弥猴，掉头望榻上，气咻咻然，目碧色，炯如猫睛。其半身尚藏地下，闻人转侧声，歘遁不见。大骇，以为目昏瞀，所见不确，起挑灯，再就枕。心烦躁，复不能寐，姑启目觇之。灯复暗，前物复自地出，闻人声，依旧遁去。如是者三。灯光益暗，前物出地益高。遂不敢复就枕，起呼仆叩僧门，告以所见。僧点首笑曰：“良有之。老衲以君文星，当无患。不虞其亦敢乃尔也。”爰为移榻别室，方得安寝。越数岁，闻姑孰人言，前室地下故有枯棺，年久成僵尸，今已掘去矣。然当日幸未睡熟，且未灭灯，故不致罹意外患也。

    Explanation

    69

    A Zombie Encounter

    My father once taught me: "You all travel increasingly as guests, so if you encounter unfamiliar places, you must have your servants sleep in the same room, and absolutely must not extinguish the lamp to guard against unforeseen events." He recounted that in my youth, I traveled to Gushu and stayed at a certain temple. It was early autumn, and the weather was mild and cool. At night, I lay down to sleep, but I was restless and could not fall asleep. With my eyes slightly open, I saw the light on the table was dim like a firefly; about a foot away from the bed, an object emerged from the ground halfway, about a foot long, with yellow, fluffy fur, resembling a monkey. It turned its head to look at the bed, breathing heavily, with green eyes that were bright like a cat's. Its other half was still hidden underground. When it heard someone moving, it suddenly disappeared. I was greatly frightened, thinking that my eyes were blurred and what I saw was not clear. I got up to relight the lamp and lay down again. Feeling restless and agitated, I could not fall asleep again, and cautiously opened my eyes to peek. The light became dim again, and the previous object emerged from the ground again, and disappeared as before. This happened three times. The light became even dimmer, and the previous object emerged from the ground even higher. I dared not lie down again, so I got up and called the servant to knock on the monk's door, telling them what I had seen. The monk nodded and smiled, saying, "Indeed, there is such a thing. Old monk, you are a scholar, should have no worry. Did not expect it to be so bold." He moved the bed to another room, and I finally managed to sleep peacefully. After several years, I heard people from Gushu say that there was an old coffin underground in the previous room, which had turned into a zombie after many years, and it has now been dug up. Fortunately, I was not fully asleep, and the lamp was not extinguished, so I did not suffer any unexpected harm.

    70

    何相国兄弟
    　　
    　　吾家有石屋寺，在青山之阳。相传吾乡何文端相国如宠与兄方伯如申，微时读书其中，穷困几无以自存。会除夕，闻家家祀祖爆竹声，兄弟以难备牲酒，竟不能归家度岁，相对愁叹。忽闻声震如雷，急同出视之，则青山中裂，金光璀璨。就视，皆白镪累累，充牣其中。方伯大喜，以为天怜其穷，将谋运归。相国急止之曰：“不可！君子当固穷，暴得此横财，不祥。且安知非天之所以试汝我也？子姑赖之。”方伯曰：“唯唯。虽然，穷甚矣，第暂假少许，何害？”遂取白金五十两，而书券投其中，山顿合如故。后方伯官布政时，稽核库帑，少白金五十两，正诘责间，吏忽见畸角一纸券，上书“某年月日，何某假天帑白金一铤，计重五十两”，以呈，方伯大惊，验之，盖即前所自书投山中者。因备述前事，自如数出金偿库中。一时闻者莫不称叹。观此，益信货财有定，不可苟得也。相国字芝岳，晚年予告归，号西畴老人。

    Explanation

    70

    The Brothers of Grand Secretary He

    Our family has a Stone House Temple on the southern slope of Green Mountain. It is said that during their humble times, Grand Secretary He Wencheng and his elder brother, Provincial Governor He Rushen, studied there and were extremely poor, barely able to survive.

    On New Year's Eve, they heard the sounds of ancestral worship and firecrackers from every household. Unable to prepare sacrificial meat and wine, they could not return home to celebrate the New Year. They worried and sighed together.

    Suddenly, they heard a sound like thunder. They rushed out to see what it was and found that Green Mountain had split open, revealing golden light shining brilliantly. Inside, they saw pile upon pile of silver, filling the space.

    He Rushen was overjoyed, thinking that heaven had taken pity on their poverty and given them this unexpected wealth. He planned to take it home. However, He Wencheng quickly stopped him, saying, "No! A gentleman should be steadfast in poverty. Suddenly obtaining this unexpected wealth is unlucky. Moreover, how do we know it is not the way heaven tests you and me? You should just rely on it."

    He Rushen replied, "Yes, yes. Although, we are extremely poor. Just borrow a little bit, what harm is there?"

    He Rushen then took fifty taels of silver, wrote a promissory note, and threw it into the mountain. The mountain suddenly closed up as before.

    Later, when He Rushen was an official in the provincial administration, he audited the treasury and found that there was a shortage of fifty taels of silver. During the interrogation, the official suddenly saw a paper note at the corner. Written on it was "On a certain date, He Rucheng borrowed fifty taels of silver from the heavenly treasury." He Rucheng was greatly surprised and verified that it was indeed the note he had written and thrown into the mountain earlier. He then fully recounted the previous events and paid the treasury the full amount.

    Those who heard about it all praised it. From this, one can see that wealth and property have their destiny and cannot be obtained lightly. Grand Secretary He Wencheng, whose courtesy name was Zhiyue, retired home in his later years and called himself the Old Man of the Western Field.

    71

    傅青主征君轶事
    　　
    　　山右傅青主征君山，以书画著名一时，而不肯轻为之作。尝有执友某求画，请之谆谆，意不可却。征君谓：“画虽末艺，然必须笔补造化。我每作画，先择其时，非遇良辰，不肯下笔。今重违君意，约以中秋夕为期。如是日天气晴爽，风定月明，当准备纸笔，惟命是听。”其友笑诺。待至某日，果晴爽如所言。友大喜，知征君善饮，乃备肴酒，迎与痛饮，自晡至映，始罢席。征君命侍者为研浓墨，骈两几，铺丈长玉版纸其上，又取铁界尺镇纸四角，谓俟月上，东向秉烛为之作画。少焉，月出东山，光鉴毛发，征君乐甚，命侍者取所研浓墨一巨钵置旁几，屏退诸人，独自命笔。友遥遥窃窥，但见征君手舞足蹈，或踊或跃，其状若狂。友大惊，径趋至背后，以手力抱其腰。征君狂叫，叹曰：“孺子败吾清兴，奈何！”遂掷笔搓纸，竟作罢论。

    Explanation

    71

    Famous Calligrapher and Painter Fu Qingzhu's Anecdote

    Fu Qingzhu, a renowned calligrapher and painter from Shanxi, was famous for his calligraphy and painting during his time, but he was unwilling to lightly undertake such tasks. Once, a close friend of his requested a painting and pleaded earnestly, making it difficult to refuse. Fu Qingzhu said, "Although calligraphy and painting are minor arts, they must make up for the natural world with a brush. I always choose the right time to work, and if it is not a favorable occasion, I will not start. Now, I will not disappoint you, and we have agreed to the Mid-Autumn Festival night as the deadline. If the weather is clear, fresh, calm, and moonlit on that day, please prepare the paper and ink, and I will do as you wish." His friend laughed and agreed.

    On the agreed day, the weather was indeed clear, fresh, calm, and moonlit as described. The friend was overjoyed and, knowing that Fu Qingzhu enjoyed drinking, prepared a feast and invited him to drink heartily. They drank from afternoon until evening. Fu Qingzhu then ordered his servant to grind thick ink, place two tables side by side, and lay out a long piece of fine paper on top. He also used iron rulers to press down the corners of the paper, saying he would wait until the moon rose, face east, and hold a candle to begin painting. Soon, the moon rose over the eastern mountains, its light so bright it illuminated even the finest details. Fu Qingzhu was greatly delighted and ordered his servant to bring a large inkstone filled with the thick ink he had prepared. He dismissed everyone else and began painting alone. His friend, watching from a distance, saw Fu Qingzhu dancing with his hands and feet, leaping and jumping, as if he were in a state of madness. The friend was greatly alarmed and rushed to the back of Fu Qingzhu, grabbing him by the waist. Fu Qingzhu let out a wild cry and lamented, "You have ruined my clear兴致, what can I do!" He then threw away his brush, crumpled the paper, and finally gave up on the painting.

    72

    友见征君发髩、须眉、满头皆墨，竟体汗下如雨，以征君酒醉，不能强事丹青，急取水为之浣濯，遣人送归。所画废纸上惟浓墨一团，大于釜口，以征君手笔，不忍捐弃，姑叠折庋诸架上。一夜，天阴月黑，室内隐约放光，急往察之，见光出自废纸，始悟征君画果通神，可惜败兴中辍，未竟厥事也。京师打钟庵募修落成，僧慕征君名，丐书庵额，以僧无行，辞不许。僧稔某甲与征君善，啖以重金，求为转乞。甲知征君为人，不敢遽达，又虑无以报僧。既思得一法，乃沽佳醖，招征君饮，又预作五绝诗一首，将“打钟庵”三字嵌于诗中，乘征君微醺，自握笔书此诗，屡书屡自拉弃之，征君睨之而笑。甲曰：“家有屏，欲书此诗刻其上，顾不善涂鸦，致贻君笑。”时征君已醉，笑曰：“我为汝代笔如何？”甲喜曰：“本不敢相烦，果尔，幸甚！”征君遽索纸，纵笔为之一挥，较常尤胜。

    Explanation

    72

    A Friend Saw the Reclusive Scholar's Hair, Beard, and Eyebrows Blackened, Sweat Pouring Down Like Rain

    A friend saw the reclusive scholar's hair, beard, and eyebrows all blackened, sweat pouring down his body like rain. Because the reclusive scholar was drunk, he could not force himself to engage in painting. The friend quickly took water to wash it off and sent someone to send him back home. The painting on the discarded paper was just a mass of thick ink, larger than the mouth of a pot. Because it was the reclusive scholar's handwriting, he was unwilling to discard it, so he temporarily folded and placed it on a shelf. One night, the sky was dark and the moon was obscured, and a faint light was emitted indoors. The friend hurriedly went to investigate and saw that the light was coming from the discarded paper. He finally realized that the reclusive scholar's painting truly was divine, it was a pity that it was interrupted in a disappointing manner, and the matter was not completed.

    The Bell Temple in the Capital Was Rebuilt and Completed, the Monk Admired the Reclusive Scholar's Name

    The Bell Temple in the capital was rebuilt and completed. The monk admired the reclusive scholar's name and asked to write the name of the temple. Because the monk had no virtue, the reclusive scholar refused to allow it. The monk knew that someone named Jia was friends with the reclusive scholar and offered a large sum of money, asking to be interceded. Jia knew the reclusive scholar's character and did not dare to immediately convey the request, also worried about how to repay the monk. After thinking of a method, he then bought good wine, invited the reclusive scholar to drink, and also prepared a five-line poem in advance, embedding the three characters "Bell Temple" in the poem. When the reclusive scholar was slightly drunk, he wrote the poem himself, writing it repeatedly and then tearing it up each time. The reclusive scholar glanced at it and laughed. Jia said, "I have a screen at home and want to write this poem on it, but I'm not good at painting, which caused you to laugh." At that time, the reclusive scholar was already drunk, laughed and said, "How about I write it for you?" Jia happily said, "I didn't dare to trouble you, but if you do, it would be very fortunate!" The reclusive scholar quickly asked for paper, wrote it in one stroke, and it was even better than usual.

    73

    甲请曰：“既蒙赐书，即求署款，以为蓬舍光，尤深欣感。”征君笑而许之。后甲刓此三字授僧，榜于门。征君偶过庵前，讶额署己款，笔意确是。注视沈思良久，忽忆前为甲书屏中有此三字，始悟为甲所卖，遂与绝交。征君精医，今所传世者，仅妇科书，顾不徒精妇科也。有同乡某客都中，忽患头痛，经多医无效。闻太医院某公为国手，断人生死不爽，特造请诊视。公按脉毕，命之曰：“此一月症也。可速归家料理后事，迟无及矣。”某闻，怏怏归寓，急治任，兼程旋里。会征君入都，遇诸途。问某归意，以疾告。曰：“太医院某公，今国手也。盍请治之？”某叹曰：“仆此归，正遵某公命也。”乃具告所言。征君曰：“果尔，奈何！我试为汝诊之。”按脉良久，叹曰：“某公真国手也！其言不谬。

    Explanation

    73

    甲请求道：“既然承蒙您赐予书信，我就请求在上面签名，以使我的茅舍增光，我感到特别欣喜和感激。”征君微笑着答应了他。后来，甲将这三个字刻好交给一位僧人，挂在了门上。征君偶然路过庵前，惊讶地发现门额上签着自己的名字，笔迹确实是他自己的。他凝视良久，深思之后，忽然想起之前为甲写屏风时曾用过这三个字，这才意识到自己被甲欺骗了，于是与他断绝了往来。征君精通医术，如今流传于世的只有妇科书籍，但他的医术并非仅限于妇科。有位同乡某人到京城，突然患了头痛，看了许多医生都不见效。听说太医院的某公是国手，断人生死从不失误，便特意去请求他诊治。某公诊脉完毕，对他说：“这是一个月的病症。你可以赶快回家料理后事，晚了就来不及了。”那人听了，郁郁寡欢地回到住处，急忙收拾行李，加快速度返回家乡。恰逢征君进京，两人在路上相遇。征君问那人为什么急着回家，那人告知了病情。征君说：“太医院的某公，现在是国手。为什么不请他诊治呢？”那人叹息道：“我这次回来，正是遵照某公的命令。”于是详细告知了事情的经过。征君叹了口气说：“如果是这样，那怎么办！我来为你诊断一下。”他诊脉良久，叹息道：“某公真是国手啊！他的判断没有错。”

    74

    ”某固知征君技不在某公下，泫然泣曰：“诚如君言，某真无生望矣！然君久著和缓名，竟不能生死人而肉白骨乎？”征君又沈思久之，谓曰：“汝疾万无生理。今思得一法，愈则不任功；不愈，亦不任过。汝如法试之何如？”某大喜，求方。征君命归家，遍觅健少所着旧毡笠十余枚，煎浓汤，漉成膏，旦夕服之，当有效。“万一幸愈，可速至都中谒某公，当云何也。”某谢诺而别，归家如法治之，疾果瘥。寻至都中，见征君，喜慰异常，趣往谒某公。公见某至，瞿然曰：“君犹无恙耶？”某具以征君所治之法告之，公叹曰：“傅君神医，吾不及也！吾初诊汝疾，系脑髓亏耗。按古方，惟生人脑可疗，顾万不能致，则疾亦别无治法。今傅君以健少旧毡笠多枚代之，真神医！吾不及也！若非傅君，汝白骨寒矣。”谓：“非为鄙人所语耶。然则医虽小道，攻之不精，是直以人命为儿戏也！吾尚敢业此哉？”公送某出，即乞休，闭门谢客，绝口不谈医矣。

    Explanation

    74

    "I firmly know that the Master's skill is not inferior to that of Master Gong. With tears streaming down his face, he said, 'If what you say is true, I truly have no hope of survival! However, you have long been renowned for your healing skills. Could you not bring the dead back to life and make white bones flesh?' The Master pondered deeply for a long time and said, 'Your illness has no chance of recovery. I have now thought of a method. If you recover, I cannot claim credit; if you do not, I cannot be blamed. How about you try this method?' I was greatly pleased and asked for the prescription. The Master instructed me to go home and collect more than ten old felt hats worn by strong young men. Boil them into a thick decoction, strain it into a paste, and take it morning and evening. It should be effective. 'If you are fortunate enough to recover, you should quickly go to the capital to see Master Gong and say what I have told you.' I thanked him and left, then followed his instructions at home, and my illness indeed recovered. Soon, I went to the capital to see the Master, who was extremely happy, and hurried to see Master Gong. When Master Gong saw me, he was startled and said, 'You are still unharmed?' I told him in detail about the method the Master used to treat me. He sighed, 'Master Fu is a divine physician; I cannot match him! When I first diagnosed your illness, it was due to a depletion of brain marrow. According to ancient prescriptions, only fresh human brain could treat it, but it is impossible to obtain. Now Master Fu has used many old felt hats worn by strong young men as a substitute. He is truly a divine physician! I cannot match him! If it were not for Master Fu, you would have been dead.' He said, 'Was this not what I told you? However, medicine is a minor art. If one does not master it, it is merely treating life and death as a game! How dare I continue in this profession!' Master Gong saw me out, then immediately retired, shut himself in, refused visitors, and never spoke of medicine again."

    75

    里乘子曰：予幼时喜玩征君妇科书，见前胪载轶事数则，今刻本已不载矣。犹忆载征君所为《艳词》，有“欢床如天，欢身如云。登天抱云，欢堕侬身”之句，叹其不愧为才人吐属。后觅得所著《霜红龛全集》，阅之多不称意。或谓征君稿多散佚，其《霜红龛集》乃村伧凑缀为之，是或然欤？征君书画传者甚少，予曾见方子箴方伯藏所书大草立幅，笔意在张颠、米颠之间，洵可宝贵；而画则未之见也。曩在京城，与李子皆同游琉璃厂，见青主墨画牡丹立幅，甚精妙。子皆以京钱十二千购得之。方子箴识。

    Explanation

    75

    The author of Li Cheng Zi says: In my youth, I enjoyed reading the works of a respected scholar on gynecology, and I saw several anecdotes listed in the preface. These stories are no longer included in the current printed editions. I still remember one of the Yan Ci (Elegant Words) written by the scholar, which contains the lines: "The bed is like the sky, the body is like clouds. Climb the sky and embrace the clouds, and I fall into your embrace." I admired that it does not fail to be the expression of a talented person. Later, I found the complete works of the scholar, titled Shuang Hong Kan Quan Ji (Complete Works of Shuang Hong Kan), but found much of it unsatisfactory. Some say that many of the scholar's manuscripts have been lost or dispersed, and that the Shuang Hong Kan Ji was compiled by unrefined individuals, which may be true? The works of calligraphy and painting by the scholar are very rare. I once saw a vertical scroll of cursive script by the scholar, which Fang Zi Zhen, a respected official, had in his collection. The style of the calligraphy is between that of Zhang Xu and Mi Fu, truly precious; however, I have not seen any of his paintings. In the past, while in the capital, I visited the Luyu Factory with Li Zi Jie and saw a vertical scroll of ink paintings of peonies by Qing Zhu, which was very exquisite. Li Zi Jie purchased it for twelve thousand coins in Beijing currency. Fang Zi Zhen identified it.

    76

    里乘卷二
    　　
    　　陈太封公
    　　
    　　山右陈翁，相国文贞公祖也。家贫，以舌耕为业。年至不惑，计累岁所积修金共四五十金，镕小铤十余枚，每夜灯下，一一摩挲，聊以自娱。媪尝戏而揶揄之，亦不以为侮也。村有族子某，困阨无以自存，素知翁所积，尝伏窗窥之，欲胠箧窃取，而苦无间。一夜，媪启户如厕，某乘间潜入，稔知积金固置褥下，急探手暗中摸索。翁睡固未熟，觉而擥其袂，就炕炉取火烛之，见是某，大惊，仍息其烛，低声问曰：“汝胡为者？奈何作此丑事，为宗族羞！”某愧且慄，答曰：“岁暮，饥寒交迫，实逼处此。

    Explanation

    76

    Volume Two of Li Cheng
    Tian Tai Gong Chen
    The Old Master Chen from Shanxi was the ancestor of the Grand Secretary Wen Zhen. He came from a poor family and made a living by teaching. By the time he reached his forties, he had accumulated a total of forty to fifty taels of gold over the years, which he had melted into more than ten small ingots. Each night, under the lamp, he would stroke each one carefully, finding pleasure in this simple act. His wife once teased him playfully, but he did not take it as an insult. In the village, there was a distant relative named Mr. Someone who was in dire straits and unable to support himself. He had always known about the old master’s savings and once peered through the window, intending to open the chest and steal the gold, but found no opportunity. One night, when the old master’s wife went to the bathroom, Mr. Someone took the chance to slip in secretly. Knowing that the gold was firmly placed under the mattress, he quickly reached out in the dark to feel for it. The old master, who was not yet fully asleep, woke up and grabbed his sleeve. He went to the stove to light a fire and illuminate the room. Seeing it was Mr. Someone, he was greatly startled, extinguished the light again, and asked in a low voice, “What are you doing? Why do such a disgraceful thing and bring shame to the family?” Mr. Someone was ashamed and trembling, and replied, “It is the end of the year, and I am starving and freezing. I am driven to this desperate act.”

    77

    ”翁曰：“汝休矣！”遂取所积金尽付之，麾令：“速去，好为之。我不汝瑕疵也。”某不遑叩谢，匆匆携金径去。翁乃大声呼曰：“有贼！”媪闻急返，问：“如何？”曰：“适有贼入室，已惊窜矣。未知失物也未？”命媪烛之遍察，惟失积金。失色懊怨，翁谓得失有命，反慰籍之。时翁方苦无嗣，自后，媪忽有娠，连举数子，家亦渐裕。某自得金后，勤俭经营，居然小康，娶妻甚贤。某尝向妻述旧事，欲报翁德，苦未得当。会秋谷将登，某防盗获，夜起侦伺。时月明如昼，见二人称娖行阡陌间，意是盗瓜豆者，姑屏息觇之。但闻哝哝小语，一曰：“在此。

    Explanation

    77

    The old man said, "You stop!" and then took the accumulated wealth he had and gave it all to him, waving his hand and commanding, "Leave quickly, and do well. I will not find fault with you." The person did not have time to bow and thank him, and hurriedly took the gold and left. The old man then shouted loudly, "Thieves!" The old woman heard and rushed back, asking, "What happened?" He replied, "Just now, thieves entered the house and have already fled in fear. I don't know if anything was stolen." He ordered the old woman to light candles and thoroughly search the house, and only the accumulated gold was missing. The old woman was shocked and resentful, but the old man said that gains and losses are fated, and instead comforted her. At the time, the old man was suffering from having no offspring, and afterward, the old woman suddenly became pregnant and gave birth to several children in succession, and the family also gradually became prosperous. After receiving the gold, the person diligently and thriftily managed his affairs, and unexpectedly became moderately well-off, marrying a very virtuous wife. The person once told his wife about the old man's kindness and wanted to repay his virtue, but had not found the right way. When the autumn harvest was about to be gathered, the person guarded against theft, and rose at night to spy. The moonlight was as bright as daylight, and he saw two people walking in a measured and orderly manner through the fields, thinking they were thieves stealing melons and beans, he temporarily held his breath and watched them. He only heard them murmuring softly, and one said, "Here."

    78

    ”一争曰：“否否，吾审之最确，毕竟在彼不在此。君如不信，试折枝插之，十日不枯，便验真伪。”一人曰：“诺。”又相将行数武，插枝而去。某知二人为形家者流，急迹其插枝处，固己新购之业。留心识之，果十日其枝不枯，大喜。商之妻，将谋葬亲，妻尼之曰：“吾侪小人，猝得吉壤，恐无德以堪之。君尝言欲报翁德，闻翁所葬亲地甚凶，将谋改葬，不如即以此穴相赠，我亲附葬其旁足矣。”某曰：“汝言良是。但翁长厚，明言相赠，彼必不受，奈何？”夫妻沈思久之，某忽跃起，拊妻背笑曰：“得之矣。翁昔葬亲，掘穴不深，我所目见。

    Explanation

    78

    One person argued, "No, no, I have examined it most accurately, it is definitely there and not here. If you do not believe me, try breaking off a branch and planting it; if it does not wither in ten days, then we can verify the truth or falsity." Another person said, "Okay." They walked together for several steps, planted the branch, and left. The narrator realized that the two people were geomancers and quickly traced the place where the branch was planted, which was already a newly purchased property. Paying close attention, he observed that the branch indeed did not wither in ten days and was greatly pleased. The businessman's wife planned to bury her parents, but she objected and said, "We common folk, having suddenly obtained a favorable plot of land, fear that we lack the virtue to bear it. You once said you wanted to repay your father-in-law's kindness, and I heard that the place where your father-in-law buried his parents is very inauspicious, so you are planning to re-bury them. It would be better to give this site to you as a gift, and my parents can be buried next to them, which is sufficient." The narrator said, "Your words are indeed right. But your father-in-law is kind and generous, and if we clearly state it as a gift, he will definitely not accept it, what to do?" The couple pondered for a long time, and the narrator suddenly jumped up, patted his wife's back, and laughed, "I've got it. Your father-in-law once buried his parents, but he did not dig the grave deep enough, as I saw with my own eyes."

    79

    趁夤夜人静，我两人潜为迁葬，附亲其旁，仍将旧穴填好，不使翁知，不亦可乎？”妻曰：“善。”遂如言部署讫，而翁果竟不知也。越岁，相国生，以年少登科甲、跻显秩。翁年期颐，矍铄异常。每春秋展祭，尚在旧穴，凡精形家术者，皆谓此地子孙不当发迹。又有为翁谋者，谓某之某地最吉，如改葬，莫善于此。翁亦甚欲之，以前事恐某介意，反赧于启齿。后另择数处，皆云不吉。不得已，托人风意于某，某笑曰：“若然，则小子已代翁改葬久矣。”遂宛转向来人告其巅末，使转达翁。翁感激往谢，酬以重金，不受。再延形家相之，佥谓封拜之地。乃伐石封墓，气象益尊。不数年，相国入阁，果如形家言。

    Explanation

    79

    Taking advantage of the quiet midnight hours, my wife and I secretly relocated the burial, placing it next to the original grave and filling in the old site properly, ensuring that the old man remained unaware, was it not a good plan? She replied, "Good." We then proceeded as instructed and completed the arrangements, and indeed, the old man never knew. The following year, the son was born and, at a young age, passed the imperial examinations and rose to a high rank. The old man, at the age of 100, was exceptionally lively and vigorous. Every spring and autumn during the ritual of paying respects to ancestors, the ceremony still took place at the old grave. All geomancy experts agreed that this place was not auspicious for the descendants. There were also those who suggested to the old man that a certain place was the most favorable, and if the burial were to be changed, nothing would be better than this. The old man also greatly desired this, but fearing that the previous matter might offend someone, he was ashamed to speak about it. Later, several other places were chosen, but all were said to be inauspicious. In the end, someone was entrusted to convey the intention to the old man, who laughed and said, "If that is the case, then your son has already secretly relocated the burial for me for a long time." He then gently informed the person who came before about the entire matter and had the message delivered to the old man. The old man expressed gratitude and visited to thank, offering a large sum of money, but it was refused. The geomancers were then consulted again, and all agreed that it was a place for ennoblement. The tomb was then marked with carved stone, and its appearance became more dignified. Not many years later, the son entered the cabinet, indeed as the geomancers had said.

    80

    里乘子曰：此金铁杉太守为予言者。谚云：“阴地不如心地。”观陈封翁之遇族子某，所谓阴德者非耶？若某者，亦可谓善于报德者矣。闻某亲附葬其旁，至今子孙亦甚藩衍，且多殷富。在翁与某，一则施德不望报，一则受德不忘报，两贤相遇，皆足以风世。
    　　
    　　刘封公
    　　
    　　山东诸城刘封翁，素饶于财。值岁荒，斗米千钱，民不聊生。封翁计：“拥厚资，饥民未必甘心坐视而不发难者。”遂决意毁家救荒，活人无算。后其子文正公统勋、孙文清公墉，相继为宰相；曾孙文恭公镮之，官至尚书。仕宦科第，至今不绝。佥谓为善之报。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：封翁决意毁家救荒，可谓卓识。向使封翁当日稍事悭吝，其家业亦未必能保，反得为富不仁之名。由是观之，人不亦乐得为善乎哉！后直隶宝坻李封翁毁家救荒，大略相同，子孙科第仕宦，亦一时称盛。

    Explanation

    80

    The commentator says: This is a story told by the governor of Jin, Tie, and Shan. It is said that "A place of burial is not as good as a place of the heart." Observe how Elder Chen treated his clan member, which exemplifies hidden virtue, does it not? If this clan member, then, can also be said to be good at repaying virtue. I have heard that this clan member buried himself near Elder Chen, and his descendants have since flourished greatly and many are prosperous. Between Elder Chen and this clan member, one bestowed virtue without expecting a return, while the other received virtue and did not forget to repay it. Two virtuous people meeting, both are sufficient to set an example for the world.

    Elder Liu
    Elder Liu from Zhucheng in Shandong has always been rich. During a year of famine, a measure of rice cost a thousand coins, and the people could not make ends meet. Elder Liu thought: "With such abundant wealth, the starving people might not be willing to sit idly by and not cause trouble." So he decided to sacrifice his family fortune to relieve the famine, saving countless lives. Later, his son, Wen Zheng Gong Tongxun, and his grandson, Wen Qing Gong Yong, successively became prime ministers; his great-grandson, Wen Gong Gong Ruanzhi, served as a minister. Official careers and scholarly success have continued to this day. All agree that this is the reward for doing good.

    The commentator says: Elder Liu's decision to sacrifice his family fortune to relieve the famine can be called insightful. If Elder Liu had been slightly stingy at that time, his family fortune might not have been preserved, and instead, he would have gained a reputation for being wealthy but unkind. Thus, we can see that people should not also enjoy doing good, right? Later, Elder Liu from Baodi in Zhili also sacrificed his family fortune to relieve the famine, with similar results. His descendants' scholarly success and official careers were also highly praised for a time.

    81

    程太封公
    　　
    　　江西新建程太封翁，性耿介，躬耕自食其力。娶太夫人某氏，井臼亲操，雍雍然有梁孟、鲍桓之风。后家道日裕，夫妻力行善事。所制升斗，俱有复底，籴则加板一层，粜则去之。晚年盈资累万，儿孙绕膝。双庆古稀，是日，戚党毕集，太夫人受贺毕，忽入房端坐，仰药而逝。时方暑月，举家悲泣，惶恐无措。以天热不能备礼，草草殡殓；又虑被人口实，仓卒葬于由陇。后有形家过其地，见之，叹曰：“此吉穴也。必热葬易于得气，子孙发祥乃速，且贵不可言。”不数年间，其孙晴峰先生矞采，辛未进士，官至两湖总督；憩棠先生楙采，甲戌翰林，官至浙江巡抚；霁亭先生奂采，庚辰翰林，官至江苏藩司，兼摄巡抚。其他曾孙，科第仕宦，至今不绝。益服堪舆之言不谬。憩棠先生巡抚吾皖时，予馆于署中，亲为予言之。

    Explanation

    81

    Cheng, the Venerable Official of Jiangxi's Xinxian County

    The venerable official of Jiangxi's Xinxian County, Cheng, was upright and incorruptible, tilling the land personally to earn his own living. He married a lady of high rank, surnamed Mou, who handled the tasks of drawing water and grinding grain herself, and their relationship was harmonious, much like that of the exemplary couples Liang Hong and Meng Guang, and Bao Shuya and his wife. Later, as the family's fortunes grew, Cheng and his wife diligently performed good deeds. The measuring vessels they used had double bottoms; when buying grain, an extra layer was added, and when selling, it was removed. In his old age, Cheng accumulated wealth in the tens of thousands, and his children and grandchildren surrounded him. On his 70th birthday, family and friends gathered to celebrate, and after receiving their congratulations, Cheng's wife suddenly entered the room, sat down, and took poison, passing away. It was the height of summer, and the entire family was in deep sorrow, feeling helpless and fearful. Due to the heat, they could not prepare a proper funeral, so they hastily buried her; fearing rumors, they hastily interred her in a place called Youlong. Later, a geomancer passed through the area and saw the site, exclaiming, "This is an auspicious burial site. A quick burial is easier to acquire qi, and the descendants will prosper quickly, and their wealth and status will be too great to describe." Within a few years, Cheng's grandson, Mr. Qingfeng Yucai, passed the imperial examinations in 1821 and became the governor of Hubei and Hunan provinces; Mr. Qiting Maocai passed the imperial examinations in 1824 and became a member of the Hanlin Academy, eventually becoming the governor of Zhejiang province; Mr. Jiting Huancai passed the imperial examinations in 1830 and became a member of the Hanlin Academy, eventually becoming the provincial finance commissioner of Jiangsu, with additional duties as the governor. Other great-grandchildren also passed the imperial examinations and entered government service, with success continuing to the present day. Increasingly, they believed that the words of geomancers were true. When Mr. Qiting was the governor of Anhui, I stayed in his official residence and he personally told me this story.

    82

    里乘子曰：予尝谓，天生吉壤，非福德兼隆者不能享；如葬不得法，即发祥也亦不速。程太夫人伉俪甚敦，子孙贤孝。向使非变出意外，其家决不肯草草殡殓，仓卒葬于田陇。可见其仰药时，正天之促其享此吉穴，且使葬之如法也。吁！吉穴讵可妄求哉！

    Explanation

    82

    里乘子说：我曾经说过，天生的吉壤，不是福德兼隆的人不能享有；如果葬法不当，即使发祥也不迅速。程太夫人的丈夫非常恩爱，子孙贤孝。如果不是发生意外，她的家人决不会草草地殡殓，仓促地葬在田垄中。可见她在仰药时，正是天意促使她享有这个吉穴，并且使葬法如法。唉！吉穴岂可妄求啊！

    83

    倪封公
    　　
    　　吾皖望江倪封翁，为濂舫方伯之父，次郊大令之祖也。尝客金陵。有星者善观气色，决吉凶，百不失一。相公之面，谓气色晦暗，不出一月寿终，促早归部署，迟则无及。公闻之，心甚怏怏，急买舟归。过芜湖，舟江浒。薄暮，登岸野眺，见一少妇抱婴儿，垂涕临江，意欲投水。公问：“汝何人？欲寻短见？”妇拭泪曰：“妾生不辰，良人嗜博，昨赌败，将鬻妾以偿博徒。妾上难舍慈姑，下难抛幼子，展转思维，不如一死。”公问：“身价几何？”曰：“言定二十千矣。”公曰：“此亦细事，汝第抱子回家，我明早携钱给汝夫偿债可也。”妇犹豫不信，公指江为誓，并问姓氏及里居甚详。妇具告之，拭泪叩谢而去。公归舟，戒榜人勿遽解缆。天明，怀数十金，访至妇家。

    83

    Mr. Ni of Wàngjiāng

    Mr. Ni of Wàngjiāng, a respected elder from Anhui Province, was the father of Fang Bo, the Magistrate of Liánfǎng, and the grandfather of Dàjū, the Magistrate of Cìjiāo. Once, he traveled to Jinling. There was a fortune-teller who was skilled in observing facial features and predicting good or bad fortune with absolute accuracy. Upon examining Mr. Ni's face, the fortune-teller predicted that his complexion was dark and he would die within a month, advising him to return home immediately to arrange his affairs, as waiting would be too late. Mr. Ni felt very disappointed upon hearing this and quickly bought a boat to return home. Passing through Wuhu, he moored his boat on the shore. In the evening, he went ashore to enjoy the scenery and saw a young woman holding a baby, crying by the river, intending to drown herself. He asked her who she was and why she wanted to end her life.
