He risked everything in one final gamble to attempt to expand the old business, but his plan was already flawed; he spent almost two million gold coins extravagantly, and if not for meeting Ji, he would have been nothing but a dead body in a ditch; once he settled into trade, he had no other ambitions. Yet, he openly rose from the position of governor to higher offices. An ordinary man has this ordinary good fortune—who is the force behind this? The actions of Xianlu are admirable in every way, everything about her is lovable and worthy of emulation. At first she helped him seek office, then assisted him in governance, and finally encouraged him to retire bravely, and she also encouraged him to actively perform various good deeds. The villagers praised her, her insight is so high that most men cannot reach her level, let alone women from the entertainment quarters. Ah! How extraordinary!

    162

    袁姬
    　　
    　　浙东江山船，有栏杆、头亭、蕉叶白等名，其陈设也华而洁，其饮馔也精而新；船各蓄美姬二三人，甫及笄者，谓之“同年妹”；齿少长者，谓之“同年嫂”。大抵桐庐、严州人居多，同年固桐严之讹也。各姬有亲生者，有购养者，儿时即延师教之度曲，弦管檀槽，靡不精晓。凡仕宦客商登舟，饮食起居，皆若曹伺奉，无须厮仆。其目听眉语，类能曲如人意，往往客子被其迷惑，资罄身殉，在所不惜，故初登其舟者，无不各有戒心。以予所闻顾生袁姬一事，则诚千载不易得之遭也。顾生，江东人，少年俊美，抱翩翩元瑜之誉，传食于公卿间。往来钱塘江，时乘袁翁之船。翁有养女阿翠，年裁破瓜，色艺冠时，生爱恋綦殷。会杭州太守聘司记室，笔札有暇，即往就姬。凡栉沐饮啄，皆自为姬给役，历久不厌，如是者二年有馀。生情日密，姬则淡漠遇之，每欲留宿，辄拒不纳。旁人多为不平，即袁翁与媪亦窃窃怜生，而怪姬薄情。

    Explanation

    162

    Yuan Ji

    Boats in the Zhe Dong region, known for their elaborate design, have names such as railings, head pavilions, and banana leaf white. Their decorations are both splendid and clean, and their cuisine is both exquisite and novel. Each boat has two or three beautiful women. Those who have just reached the age of marriage, called "同年妹" (same-year sisters), and those who are slightly older, called "同年嫂" (same-year elder sisters). Mostly from the Tonglu and Yanzhou regions, the term "同年" is a corruption of "桐严" (Tonglu and Yanzhou). Some of the women are biological children, others are adopted. From childhood, they are taught by teachers to sing and play music. They are all proficient in playing stringed instruments, flutes, and other musical instruments. All officials and merchants who board the boat have their meals and daily needs attended to by the women, without the need for servants. They can read one's eyes and eyebrows, and cater to one's desires, often bewitching the guests. They spend all their money and even sacrifice themselves, without hesitation. Therefore, those who first board the boat all have a sense of caution. According to what I have heard about the story of Gu Sheng and Yuan Ji, it is indeed a rare opportunity in a thousand years. Gu Sheng, a man from the eastern bank of the river, was a young and handsome man with the reputation of Yuan Yu, known for his elegance, and he frequented the homes of high-ranking officials. Traveling back and forth along the Qiantang River, he often rode on Mr. Yuan's boat. Mr. Yuan has an adopted daughter named A Cui, around 16 years old, whose beauty and talent are unmatched in her time. Gu Sheng was deeply enamored with her. When the governor of Hangzhou hired him as a secretary, he would go to see her when he had free time from his writing duties. All grooming, eating, and drinking, he would personally attend to her needs, never growing tired of it, like this for more than two years. Gu Sheng's feelings became increasingly deep, but A Cui treated him coldly. Every time he wanted to stay overnight, she would always refuse. Many people around them felt unjustly, even Mr. Yuan and his wife secretly pitied Gu Sheng, and found A Cui to be cold-hearted.

    163

    姬不之顾，而生亦不以为带薢也。明府某公，任侠好义，素与生友善，以爱生才而怜其太痴，愿出千金为姬脱籍。生大喜，商之翁媪，诺之。转以问姬，则抵死不肯，说之再三，始勉强应诺，并与翁媪约，亲迎之次日即归宁，凡舟中己之床奁什物，毋许动移。叮嘱谆谆，翁媪极口许诺，然后兑金署券。至亲迎之次日，姬请遵约归宁，下午即返。薄暮，城门已鐍，足音杳然。生竟夜徘徊，起坐太息，目不交睫。

    Explanation

    163

    The lady did not look at him, and he also did not consider it a burden. A local official, Mr. Someone, was known for his chivalry and love of righteousness. He had always been friendly with the protagonist, admiring his talent and pitied his excessive naivety, and he was willing to spend a thousand gold coins to free the lady from her registered status. The protagonist was very happy and consulted with the old man and woman, who agreed. Then he turned to ask the lady, but she stubbornly refused. He persuaded her repeatedly, and finally, she reluctantly agreed. They made an agreement with the old man and woman: on the day after the wedding, she would return to her parents' home, and all the beds, trunks, and household items in the boat were not to be moved. They repeatedly and earnestly instructed her, and the old man and woman vigorously agreed. Then they exchanged the money and signed the contract. On the day after the wedding, the lady asked to follow the agreement and return to her parents' home, and she returned in the afternoon. By late afternoon, the city gates were already locked, and her footsteps vanished. The protagonist spent the entire night wandering, getting up and sighing deeply, not closing his eyes even for a moment.

    164

    诘旦，急往寻其舟，已挂帆不知何往矣。生望江水渺漫，烟波无际，懊恼如焚，忿欲蹈流而死；继念徒死无益，姬他日瑟琶别抱，更可无忌，不如忍息以侦察之。乃嗒焉若丧，走语明府某公，求为画策。公劝生：“既姬不愿，亦姑置之。譬嗜笼鸟，野性难闲，终思飞去。以君之才，自有嘉耦；况烟花中人有情者少，亦何必恋不割爱而自贻伊戚哉！”生殊不以为然，遂独买舟，沿江踪迹之。后，至严州城外，见垂杨下袁舟焉。姬方倚门与翁闲话，睹生至，返身遽入，若不相识。

    Explanation

    164

    Early in the morning, he hurriedly went to find his boat, only to find that it had already hoisted its sails and sailed away without a trace. Gazing at the vast, misty river, he felt overwhelmed with frustration and anger, wishing to leap into the stream and die. But then he thought that dying in vain would be useless. The girl would in the future play the lute with another and could be without fear. It would be better to restrain himself and investigate what had happened. Thus, he walked away dejectedly, ran to tell the prefecture official, and asked for advice. The official advised him, "Since the girl does not wish it, it should be temporarily set aside. It is like a caged bird that longs for freedom, its wild nature cannot be restrained, and it will eventually fly away. With your talent, there will naturally be a good match. Besides, among the courtesans, few have genuine feelings. Why should one cling to them and cause oneself sorrow?" However, the protagonist was not convinced and decided to buy a boat alone and follow the river to track her down. Later, he arrived at the outskirts of Yanzhou City and saw a boat moored under the willow trees. The girl was leaning against the door chatting with her father. When she saw him arrive, she turned and quickly went inside, as if she did not recognize him.

    165

    生登舟与翁寒暄已，呼姬，不答，恚恨莫遏，狂叫谯诃，姬四顾他语，置若罔闻。生无可奈何，遂具状诉诸郡守。郡守素耳生名，拘姬至，讯之，姬哓哓强辩。郡守问生，究竟意欲何如，生出券呈验，坚求合璧。郡守如判，饬令姬归，并反复开谕以后当与生和好，无再参商。姬既归舟，怨恨之情形于词色。翁媪从旁规劝，亦谓当赘生于舟，免招物议。姬摇首不语，劝譬再四，始与生言定：两舟相并，每夜自携衾枕过生舟就寝，日则仍回己舟。

    Explanation

    165

    The protagonist boarded the boat and exchanged pleasantries with the elderly man. He called out to the concubine, but she did not respond, and his anger and resentment could not be contained. He shouted wildly and scolded her. The concubine looked around and spoke to others, pretending not to hear. The protagonist was at a loss and decided to file a formal complaint with the county magistrate. The county magistrate had long heard of the protagonist's name and detained the concubine for interrogation. She persistently argued, and the county magistrate asked the protagonist what he ultimately wanted. The protagonist presented the contract for verification and insisted on reuniting the couple. The county magistrate ruled as such, ordering the concubine to return and repeatedly advising her to be reconciled with the protagonist in the future, and not to cause further discord. After returning to the boat, the concubine's resentment was visible in her words and expressions. The elderly man and woman advised her from the side, suggesting that the protagonist should live on the boat to avoid public criticism. The concubine shook her head and remained silent, but after repeated advice, she finally agreed to a resolution with the protagonist: the two boats would be side by side, and every night she would bring her bedding to the protagonist's boat to sleep, returning to her own boat during the day.

    166

    生不得已，曲从之。自是，肃肃宵征，抱衾与裯，夙夜必偕，习以为常。相居半载有馀，琴瑟静好。翁媪窃慰，以为从此可白首鱼水矣。一夜，月白风清，漏二下，姬察袁舟人已睡熟，乃遍悄呼生舟人起，戒勿高声，自于裙底出匕首一柄，长尺有半，白如霜雪；又出白金二百两，指谓众曰：“公等若听妾言，请以此金相酬；不则，请伏刃而死，于汝舟亦有所不利，愿公等决焉。”众相视错愕，莫知所指，佥谓：“如能效力，敢不如命！但请相示。
    ”姬袖刃，低声告曰：“若然，请公等纳金，悄将前后缆解开，切勿惊觉邻舟。乘今夜风利，开帆向杭州速发，抵岸尚不吝重犒。”舟人如言解缆，将帆拽满，兼程驰抵杭城。姬大喜，问生：“城中有赁屋否？”曰：“有。”姬乃厚犒舟人，急召人担负什物偕生入城，笑谓生曰：“妾今日方是君妇。”问：“何谓？”曰：“后自知之。”先是，生舟夜发，昧爽，袁舟始觉。翁媪忿甚，急张帆追至杭州。

    Explanation

    166

    Being forced to do so, he reluctantly complied. From then on, they silently traveled at night, carrying their bedding, always accompanying each other from dawn till dusk, and gradually becoming accustomed to this routine. They lived together for more than half a year, their relationship peaceful and harmonious. The old man and woman secretly comforted themselves, believing that they could now spend their lives together in harmony. One night, under the bright moon and clear wind, at two hours past midnight, the lady noticed that the boatmen of Yuan's boat had fallen asleep. She then quietly called out to the boatmen of her own boat, warning them not to make any noise, and drew a dagger from beneath her skirt, a blade a foot and a half long, white as frost and snow; she also took out two hundred taels of silver, pointing to the crowd and saying, "If you listen to me, I will reward you with this gold; otherwise, I will kill myself with this blade, which will also be detrimental to your boat. Please make a decision." The crowd looked at each other in shock, unsure of what she meant, and all said, "If we can be of service, how could we not follow your command! But please instruct us."

    The lady, with the dagger in her sleeve, whispered, "If so, please accept the gold, quietly untie the front and back ropes, and do not wake the neighboring boats. Take advantage of the favorable wind tonight, set sail towards Hangzhou quickly, and I will not hesitate to give a generous reward upon reaching the shore." The boatmen followed her instructions, untied the ropes, and set the sails full, rushing to Hangzhou at full speed. The lady was greatly pleased and asked the man, "Is there a place to rent a house in the city?" He replied, "Yes." She then generously rewarded the boatmen, hastily summoned people to carry their belongings, and accompanied him into the city, smiling and saying to him, "Today I am truly your wife." He asked, "What do you mean?" She replied, "You will know later." Earlier, the man's boat had set sail at night, and at dawn, Yuan's boat had only just awakened. The old man and woman were very angry, quickly setting sail to chase after them to Hangzhou.

    167

    入城见姬，责其背逃之罪，姬谓：“嫁夫随夫，何谓背逃？翁姆尚念旧好，请勿赘言，后日尚可往来；不则从此斩断葛藤，两为陌路矣！”翁媪以姬明决，悔恨之极。欲讼官，以前既凭媒署券，后又经郡守判断，更难翻覆，乃白眼瞪视，垂头嘿嘅者久之，不得已，甘言强笑，订盟而别。盖姬平日私蓄，固有万馀金，嫁生断难携带，必如此作为，使翁媪不觉，然后两舟相并便好陆续携运，若稍露声色，则防察必严，丝毫莫取矣。其机甚警，而其心亦甚苦哉！姬寻出金为生纳资县令，所在悉著政声，皆由内助之力居多焉。

    Explanation

    167

    Entering the city to see Lady J, he blamed her for the crime of fleeing. Lady J replied, "Marriage means following one's husband; how can this be called fleeing? The old lady still remembers our old friendship, please do not add more words. We can still visit each other in the future; otherwise, we will sever all ties forever and become strangers to each other!" The old couple thought Lady J was decisive and felt extremely regretful. They wanted to sue in court, but since they had already relied on a matchmaker to sign the contract, and later the county magistrate had already made a judgment, it would be even harder to overturn. They then glared with white eyes, hung their heads, and sighed in silence for a long time. Reluctantly, they spoke sweetly and forced a smile, made an agreement, and parted. Since Lady J had secretly saved money in the past, she had saved over ten thousand taels of gold, which would be impossible to carry all to her new home. She must act in this way so that the old couple would not notice. Then the two boats would be side by side, it would be easy to carry it over in stages. If she showed any signs, then they would surely watch closely, and she would not be able to take anything at all. Her plan was very clever, but her heart was also very bitter. Lady J then took out money to pay the county magistrate for her husband. Wherever he went, he had a good reputation for governance, all due largely to the help of Lady J.

    168

    里乘子曰：袁姬深得用兵欲擒先纵之法。观其平日遇生无情之状，不惟旁人不知其心，翁媪不知其心，即生亦实不知其心；及再经郡判，并舟来往，宿蓄运尽，乃召众饵以白金、劫以白刃，驰抵杭城，至是始为生妇。翁媪虽蹑迹而至，亦徒有白眼瞪视而已，又将奈姬何哉！夫如是而姬之心旁人知之、翁媪知之，而生亦始得知之。古所谓将飞者翼伏，将奔者爪缩；守如处女，出如脱兔，袁姬有焉。勾栏竟有此人物，合之仙露，可称女中二豪。仙露事事从容固好，袁姬事事沉密亦好，予乐为琐琐笔之，所以贺二生之遭也。

    Explanation

    168

    里乘子曰：袁姬深得用兵欲擒先纵之法。观其平日遇生无情之状，不惟旁人不知其心，翁媪不知其心，即生亦实不知其心；及再经郡判，并舟来往，宿蓄运尽，乃召众饵以白金、劫以白刃，驰抵杭城，至是始为生妇。翁媪虽蹑迹而至，亦徒有白眼瞪视而已，又将奈姬何哉！夫如是而姬之心旁人知之、翁媪知之，而生亦始得知之。古所谓将飞者翼伏，将奔者爪缩；守如处女，出如脱兔，袁姬有焉。勾栏竟有此人物，合之仙露，可称女中二豪。仙露事事从容固好，袁姬事事沉密亦好，予乐为琐琐笔之，所以贺二生之遭也。

    Li Chengzi said: Yuan Ji deeply understands the military strategy of capturing by first letting go. Observing her usual indifferent demeanor towards her partner, not only did others not know her intentions, nor did her parents, even her partner was truly unaware of her thoughts; until she went through the local judge again, traveled by boat, and exhausted her plans, she then called upon the crowd, using silver as bait and threatening with weapons, rushing to Hangzhou, and only then did she become his wife. Her parents, though they followed her trail, could only glare at her in anger, what more could they do to her? Thus, her intentions were known to others, to her parents, and to her partner. As the ancients said, those about to fly fold their wings, those about to run retract their claws; guarding like a maiden, acting like a released hare, Yuan Ji exemplifies this. The brothel has such a figure, when combined with Xian Lu, they can be called two great women. Xian Lu's composure in all matters is good, and Yuan Ji's subtlety in all matters is also good. I am pleased to write about these trivialities, to celebrate the encounters of these two individuals.

    169

    夙冤
    　　
    　　休宁黄某，贸易越东。蓄一番犬，驯而且黠，能解人意，黄极珍爱，每食即饲以其馀，眠则令卧床下；出入行止，须臾不离。会岁暮归家，路过睦州，以失路投止兰若。一老僧出，与客问讯，忽见犬，问黄曰：“居士那得此犬？”黄告之。僧叹曰：“居士奈何豢此冤畜！”黄闻之错愕。以其言异，叩之曰：“鄙人庸俗，不知夙世因果，愿求指示。”僧初不肯言，再三研求，乃谓：“此犬与君前世冤结甚深，不久必当相报。”黄益骇惧，遂膜拜顶礼，求示解脱之法。僧喟然曰：“老衲不合向居士饶舌，然佛法慈悲，又不忍坐视。君须谨识吾言：到家三日后，夜就寝时，伺犬睡熟，君潜遁去，但取素所著衷衣多件，结束如人形，悄置衾中，听其作为。计此，犬求君不得，必愤极自毙。君将其尸悬深山树上，待其销化，如此，庶可解脱。须谨识之勿忘！”黄合十受教。

    Explanation

    169

    夙冤

    Huang, a merchant from Xiuning, was traveling in the eastern part of Yue. He kept a foreign dog that was both tame and clever, able to understand human intentions. Huang treasured the dog greatly, feeding it the leftovers of his meals and allowing it to sleep under his bed; wherever he went, the dog followed closely, never leaving his side. On the eve of the year, as he was returning home, he passed through Muzhou and, having lost his way, sought shelter at a small Buddhist temple. An elderly monk came out and greeted the guests. Upon seeing the dog, the monk asked Huang, "How did you come to have this dog?" Huang told him. The monk sighed and said, "How could you keep such a grudge animal?" Huang was startled by this and, finding the monk's words strange, asked, "I am an ordinary man, ignorant of the causes and effects of past lives. I would like you to instruct me." The monk initially refused to speak, but after Huang persistently sought an explanation, he said, "This dog has a deep-seated grudge with you from past lives, and soon it will seek retribution." Huang was even more frightened and terrified, so he prostrated himself in worship, asking for a way to be freed from this bond. The monk sighed and said, "I should not have spoken to you, but the compassionate teachings of Buddhism cannot bear to see you suffer. You must remember my words: three days after you return home, when you go to bed at night, wait until the dog is asleep, then secretly escape. Take several of your clothes that you have worn frequently, wrap them up in a human-like shape, and quietly place them under the quilt. Let it do as it will. The dog, unable to find you, will become so enraged that it will kill itself. Then, hang its body on a tree in a deep mountain, and let it dissolve. If you do this, perhaps you can be liberated. Remember this well and do not forget!"

    170

    归，如僧言部署，犬果以求人不得，遍将衾枕衷衣抓啮狼籍，狂狺跳跃而毙。黄大称异，知僧言不诬，遵将死犬挂深山树上。逾月往视，其皮肉已销化殆尽，仅存毛骨一具而已。既而又作越游，特往谢僧，僧曰：“君不悖老衲言，部署甚善。但此犬怨气未散，今又变而为蛇，知君来此，翌辰必踪迹而至，甘心于君，奈何？”黄闻失色股栗，哀求拯救。僧曰：“君幸遇老衲，缘分不浅，合终始成全之。”乃出大瓮，令黄踡卧其中；又取一瓮覆其上，合口处勅勒书符镇之。至时，果来一蛇，身粗如盏，长数丈，绕瓮数匝，瓮振振作响，竟不得开，蛇愤，自裂其身寸断。僧启瓮出黄，黄再拜称谢。僧贺之曰：“君大冤已解，然其鬼知老衲饶舌，将来必与老衲作难。”黄曰：“奈何？”曰：“老衲自有处分，居士可请无虑。”黄大喜，酬以重金，不受。后亦不知所终。此新安吴子琴言者。

    Explanation

    170

    Returning home as the monk had instructed, the dog indeed tried to find a person but failed, completely taking the bedding, inner garments, and tearing them into a mess, howling fiercely and jumping around until it died. Huang Da was amazed and knew the monk's words were not false. He followed the monk's advice and hanged the dying dog on a tree in a deep mountain. After a month, he went to see it, and found that its skin and flesh had almost entirely been consumed, leaving only a set of hair and bones. Later, he went on another journey and specially went to thank the monk. The monk said, "You did not contradict my words, and the arrangement was very good. But this dog's resentment has not dissipated; now it has changed into a snake. Knowing you came here, it will surely follow and arrive the next day, and will be content with you. What to do?" Huang heard this and lost color, shaking with fear. He pleaded for salvation. The monk said, "You have fortunately met me, and the karmic connection is not shallow. Together, from beginning to end, we will complete and fulfill this." He then took out a large jar and ordered Huang to crouch and lie inside it; he took another jar and covered it on top, inscribing a talisman at the mouth to pacify it. At that time, indeed a snake came, its body thick like a cup, several zhang long. It circled the jar several times, causing the jar to shake and make a sound, but finally could not open it. The snake was angry and split its body into small pieces. The monk opened the jar and released Huang, who bowed twice and expressed gratitude. The monk congratulated him, saying, "Your great wrong has been resolved, but its spirit knows the old monk talked too much. In the future, it will surely cause trouble for the old monk." Huang asked, "What to do?" The monk replied, "The old monk has his arrangements; layperson, you can ask without worry." Huang was very happy and offered a large sum of money, but the monk did not accept it. Later, he also did not know what became of him. This was said by Wu Ziqin of Xin'an.

    171

    里乘子曰：怨毒之于人，甚矣哉！夙世之冤犹固结而不可解如此。黄某非遇老僧，则此生休矣！君子观此，亦可藉以自警。

    Explanation

    171

    Li Chengzi said: "How great is the harm of resentment and hatred to people! The grudge from a past life is so deeply entrenched that it cannot be resolved. Huang, if he had not met the old monk, would have perished in this life. Gentlemen, observing this, can also use it as a warning to themselves."

    172

    赵乙
    　　
    　　子琴又言，无锡县城中典库后有旷园，大逾十亩。忽有一壮夫负竹篙一竿，修而多节，白司事者求寄存园中，订日来取。诘旦，汛埽夫赵乙见之，问司事者：“此篙从何而来？”司事告之，赵太息不语。司事察其言状有异，转诘之，乃曰：“小人向日流荡江湖，略知暴客号诀。此篙即若曹信物，数节之多寡，便知党羽人数。今倚园墙西隅，其来必由此处，是不可不预防之。”司事闻之大惊，趋告主人。众意乙非常人，因谓：“汝既知此篙，必能办贼，果尔，当重赏不否。”乙初不肯，再三央之，乙乃数篙节，凡三十有三，曰：“贼数如此之多，非以计歼之不可。”众问：“计将安出？”乙笑曰：“公等勿虑，小人自有料理。”乃请买豆数百石，摊布园中，约厚尺许；又买大瓮三十具置墙下听用，主人如言部署。

    Explanation

    172

    Zhao Yi

    Ziqin said again, in the city of Wuxi, there was an open garden behind a warehouse, larger than ten acres. Suddenly, a strong man carrying a long bamboo pole with many joints appeared and asked the person in charge to store it in the garden, promising to come and retrieve it on a certain day. The next morning, Zhao Yi, a flood-sweeping laborer, saw the bamboo pole and asked the person in charge, "Where did this pole come from?" The person in charge informed him, and Zhao Yi sighed silently. Noticing Zhao Yi's unusual demeanor, the person in charge asked him back, and Zhao Yi replied, "In my past wanderings on the rivers and lakes, I roughly knew the signals of robbers. This pole is their token, and the number of its joints indicates the number of their members. It is leaning against the western corner of the garden wall, so they must be coming from there. This cannot be ignored." The person in charge was greatly alarmed and hurriedly informed the owner. Everyone thought Zhao Yi was not an ordinary person and said to him, "Since you know this pole, you must be able to deal with the robbers. If so, we will reward you generously." Zhao Yi initially refused, but after being urged several times, he counted the joints of the pole and found there were thirty-three. He said, "With so many robbers, we cannot eliminate them without a plan." They asked, "What is your plan?" Zhao Yi smiled and said, "Do not worry, I will handle it myself." He then asked to buy several hundred stones of beans, which were spread out in the garden, about a foot thick; he also bought thirty large jars and placed them at the base of the wall for use. The owner followed his instructions and arranged everything accordingly.

    173

    一夜，天月阴黯，乙谓众曰：“贼至当在今夜，小人当一一歼之。公等如胆大不畏，请作壁上观可也。”主人骇惧，恐乙众寡不敌，出资募健儿百余人，各持器械，为乙助威。乙麾之去，曰：“尔曹卤莽无能，在此搅扰，徒乱人意，请速去，勿败乃公事！”悉命举室吹灭灯烛，自袖利刃，潜伏墙阴以伺之。少选，竹竿窸窣作响，一莽男子自竿溜下，足踏豆上，滑而颠，乙暗中以刀剁其首，掷瓮中；未几，一人继下，颠如前，又剁之，连剁十馀人。外贼以其党多人入墙，寂不闻声，虑内有备，中一黠贼跃登墙上，次且不敢下，俯首观察良久，轻拊其掌者再，内无应者，知事败，遂率馀党遁去。无何，东方渐明，乙招诸健儿置尸瓮中。计之，共剁十有六贼。命悉舁而瘗于野，以灭其迹。主人大喜，厚犒诸健儿。自是，除乙汛埽之役，待以殊礼。惟时惴惴焉，虑盗复来报怨，而盗竟不敢复至。

    Explanation

    173

    One night, the sky was dark and overcast. Yi told the crowd, "The bandits will arrive tonight, and I will eliminate them one by one. If you are brave and fearless, please watch from the sidelines." The host was filled with fear, worried that Yi would be outnumbered, and provided funds to recruit over a hundred strong men, each armed with weapons, to bolster Yi's efforts. Yi dismissed them, saying, "You people are rash and incompetent. If you stay here and disturb, you only confuse my plans. Please leave quickly, or you will ruin our cause!" He ordered the entire household to extinguish all the lights, hid a sharp blade in his sleeve, and lay in ambush in the shadow of the wall. Shortly after, a rustling sound came from the bamboo pole, and a rough man slipped down, landing on a beanstalk, slipped and fell. In the darkness, Yi chopped off his head and threw it into a jar. Soon after, another man slipped down, fell as before, and was also chopped. Yi continued to chop down ten or more bandits. The bandits outside, seeing many of their comrades enter the wall but hearing no sound, feared there was a trap inside. One clever bandit jumped onto the wall, hesitated to come down, looked down and observed for a long time, and lightly touched his hand twice. Finding no one inside responding, he knew the plan had failed and led the rest of his gang away. In no time, the east began to brighten. Yi summoned the strong men and placed the bodies in the jar. Counting them, he had chopped down sixteen bandits. He ordered them to carry the bodies and bury them in the fields to erase all traces. The host was very pleased and generously rewarded the strong men. From then on, except for his regular cleaning duties, Yi was treated with special respect. At that time, he was filled with anxiety, fearing that the bandits would return to seek revenge, but the bandits finally did not dare to come again.

    174

    里乘子曰：畸人混迹贱役，不遇有事，将终身埋没不彰；彼盗之来，所以显赵乙也！夫赵乙犹其细焉者也！嗟乎！风尘莽莽，豪杰尽多，俗眼不识，则交臂失之者不知凡几，惜哉！
    　　
    　　绛帻生
    　　
    　　庐州武弁某，智而侠，道遇不平，不惜殉以白刃。以角巾爱着绛色，人多戏以绛帻生呼之。尝夏日有事六安，兼道失路，乘月策马夜行，忽阴云骤合，暴雨如注，无可栖止，道旁有丛厝，暂系马倚剑，佝身入避。俄雨少霁，闻对面厝室内窸窣有声，见一人自内探首出，披发袒臂，手持一碗，承檐溜而饮之；电光闪烛，谛审似是女子。某素豪于胆，悄拔剑趋往，以究其实。女见某，返身欲遁，某亟撩其发叱曰：“汝人耶鬼耶？可速说，免屈污我宝剑也！”女战栗答曰：“是人。”某问：“在此何故？”女初腼腆不肯言，研诘不已，始知为某生簉室某姬也。姬固滁州人，生而失母，父瞽，精于星命。

    Explanation

    174

    Li Chengzi said: An extraordinary person living among lowly roles, if not encountered in significant events, will remain obscure and unacknowledged for life; the arrival of the thief served to highlight Zhao Yi! Zhao Yi is just a minor example! Alas! In the vast world, there are many heroes, but the common eye fails to recognize them, and thus many pass by without being noticed. How regrettable!

    Jiangzé Sheng

    A certain military officer from Lu Prefecture, intelligent and chivalrous, was willing to sacrifice himself for justice when encountering injustice on the road. He loved wearing a red headband, and people often called him Jiangzé Sheng in jest. Once in summer, he had business in Liu'an, took a shortcut and lost his way, and rode at night under the moon. Suddenly, dark clouds gathered and heavy rain poured down, and there was no place to take shelter. By the roadside, there was a cluster of graves. He temporarily tied his horse and leaned his sword, bent over to seek shelter. Soon the rain began to ease, and he heard rustling sounds from inside the opposite tomb. He saw a person peeking out from inside, with disheveled hair and bare arms, holding a bowl and drinking from the rainwater dripping from the eaves. Lightning flashed, and he carefully examined and realized it was a woman. He was always bold, quietly drew his sword and approached to investigate the truth. The woman saw him and tried to escape. He quickly grabbed her hair and shouted, "Are you human or a ghost? Please speak quickly, to avoid disgracing my sword!" The woman trembled and replied, "I am human." He asked, "What are you doing here?" The woman was initially shy and unwilling to speak. He continued to question her, and he eventually learned that she was a concubine of a certain person. She was originally from Chuzhou, lost her mother at birth, her father was blind, and she was skilled in astrology.

    175

    某生秋试金陵，过滁见姬，悦其色，托人谋为小星，父雅不欲。姬幼读父书，亦知命学，自推命薄，闻生为世家子，自请于父，愿屈身为副室。生感其意，益喜，爰出重金厌其父，乃许之。既携姬归，竟不为大妇所容，频受凌辱，年馀不使当夕，奴婢畜之。三日前，以为掠失意，痛挞无算，生坐视嘿叹而无可如何。姬屡蓄死念，试以金钱自卜，遇遁之姤，其繇曰：“坚冰泮春，陌路逢亲。卵完石碎，替旧更新。”详玩繇词，生机未绝，姑忍息以待。是日早起，侍大妇晓妆，袖误拂玉钗，堕地折之。大妇怒甚，谓钗为父母赠嫁物，终身吉凶皆兆于此；姬故意断之，使己不利。乃撮发令跪，裸而鞭之，怒犹未息，吼与姬约：日内不如式相偿，誓不两立。姬惧，逃匿厝室，不图与某弁遇。

    Explanation

    175

    A certain scholar took the provincial examination in Jinling and passed through Chuzhou, where he saw a woman named Ji. He was attracted by her beauty and asked someone to arrange for her to become his concubine. However, her father was initially reluctant. Ji had read her father's books and was familiar with the teachings on fate, which led her to believe that her destiny was weak. Upon learning that the scholar came from a prominent family, she voluntarily approached her father and requested to be his secondary wife. The scholar was touched by her intentions and became even more pleased. He then offered a substantial sum to appease her father, who eventually agreed. After bringing Ji back home, the scholar found that she was not well-received by the main wife, who frequently subjected her to humiliation. For over a year, Ji was not allowed to share the bed with the scholar and was treated like a servant. Three days prior, Ji was severely beaten after a failed attempt to take something by force, and the scholar could only watch in silence, feeling powerless. Ji often harbored thoughts of suicide and sought guidance through divination with money. She encountered the hexagram "Dun" (遁) and the line "Gou" (姤) in the I Ching (Book of Changes). The divination said: "The hard ice melts in spring, and on the road one meets relatives. The egg remains intact while the stone is broken; the old is replaced by the new." After carefully considering the meaning of the divination, Ji realized that her life was not yet over and decided to endure the situation for now. On that day, she rose early to assist the main wife in her morning makeup, accidentally brushing against a jade hairpin, which fell and broke. The main wife became extremely angry, claiming that the hairpin was a gift from her parents and that it foretold her entire life's fortune. Ji was accused of deliberately breaking it to bring her bad luck. The main wife then pulled her hair, made her kneel, stripped her naked, and whipped her. Her anger did not subside, and she shouted at Ji, threatening to make her pay for her actions in ways that would lead to their mutual destruction. Fearing for her life, Ji fled to hide in a storage room, where she unexpectedly met a certain official.

    176

    某劝还家，姬不肯，因备诉嫡悍妒状，且示之臂，固无完肤。某义忿填膺，愿与姬缔为兄妹，授以计，令仍藏厝室。天明，某跨马特往生家，谓奉父命远来省妹。生夫妻同出，见某言颇不逊。某怒，拔剑斫庭前柳树，顿折；戟手指生夫妻厉声曰：“如不以妹来见者，有如此树！”先是，生妻虐姬太甚，邻里均为不平，今闻某来，众等毕集攒视，且嗾某勿稍宽纵。某闻益怒，谓如不见妹，先毁其室，而后鸣官，言已，一拳已洞墙壁。生夫妻始大恐，央众为缓颊。亟遣人各处觅姬，后于厝室得之。姬犹觳觫，不敢遽归。其人告以兄至，归当无患。姬佯问曰：“汝言真耶？”曰：“然。”姬喜。既归，与某相见，彼此愕视，故作惊喜状，俨然妹也兄者，相持痛哭失声。

    Explanation

    176

    I advised her to return home, but she refused, then recounting in detail the harsh and jealous treatment by her principal wife, and showing me her arm, which was completely scarred. Filled with righteous anger, I wished to establish a brother-sister relationship with her, gave her a plan, and instructed her to hide in the storage room. At dawn, I rode my horse specifically to visit the husband's home, claiming that I had come far on my father's orders to visit my sister. The husband and wife came out together and spoke to me in a disrespectful manner. I was angry and drew my sword, striking the willow tree in the courtyard, breaking it instantly. I pointed at the husband and wife with my spear and shouted fiercely, "If you do not bring my sister to see me, may you suffer as this tree has suffered!" Earlier, the husband's wife had been excessively cruel to the concubine, and the neighbors all felt unjust. Now that I had come, the crowd gathered and stared, urging me not to show any leniency. I became even angrier and said that if I did not see my sister, I would first destroy their house and then report to the authorities. After saying this, I punched the wall, leaving a hole. The husband and wife began to be greatly afraid and begged the crowd to intercede on their behalf. They hurriedly sent people to search for the concubine everywhere, and later found her in the storage room. The concubine was still trembling with fear and was too afraid to return immediately. The person told her that my brother had arrived, and that returning would be safe. The concubine feigned a question and said, "Is what you say true?" "Yes," the person replied. The concubine was happy. After returning, she met me. We stared at each other in astonishment, acting as if we were surprised and delighted, and acted as if we were brother and sister. We held each other and cried loudly.

    177

    众见两人情状逼真骨肉，互相劝慰，生夫妻益信而不疑。急具酒食，序姻娅礼，款接甚殷，留住三日，饯赆备至。某濒行时，当众谓生夫妻曰：“某十五岁出而从军，妹依瞽父，不幸为生所绐，此是妹命不犹，夫复何怨！昨归家，老父告知，命来省视，不料如此受虐。谁无骨肉？被人摧残若此，于心甘乎！今请凭诸公与生夫妻约，此后倘仍蹈前辄，某必有以报之！勿谓武人鹵莽也！”众唯唯，生夫妻俱谢过不遑，并请寄语乃父勿以挂怀。自某去后，果相安无事。某顺道经其家，辄信宿授餐，居然至戚。逾年，大妇以病噎死，姬竟正位，始悟繇词所谓“坚冰泮春”者，阴极阳回也；“陌路逢亲”者，意外遇某，缔为兄妹也；嫡，石也；姬，卵也，卵虽不能敌石，而一完一碎，嫡亡姬存，则新人将更换而替故人也。历历不爽，亦何神哉！

    Explanation

    177

    The crowd saw the two people's emotional state was as close as flesh and blood, mutually comforting each other, which made the couple even more trusting and without doubt. They quickly prepared wine and food, arranged the kinship rituals, received warmly and sincerely, and stayed for three days, with the farewell gifts fully prepared. When Mr. X was about to leave, he said to the couple in front of everyone, "I was fifteen years old when I left to join the army, and my younger sister relied on our blind father. Unfortunately, she was deceived by Mr. X, this is my sister's bad fate, what more can I complain about! Yesterday, after returning home, my father told me, ordered me to come to visit, but unfortunately, I was treated so badly. Who doesn't have family? To be treated so badly, can one be content with this in their heart! Now I ask the gentlemen and the couple to make an agreement, if they continue to act as they did before, I will definitely take action against them! Do not think I am acting rashly!" The crowd nodded in agreement, and the couple apologized profusely, and asked to convey a message to his father not to worry. After Mr. X left, things were indeed peaceful. Mr. X passed by their house and stayed overnight, providing meals, and they became close relatives. After a year, the elder wife died of illness, and the concubine eventually became the main wife. They finally understood the phrase "the hard ice melts in spring," indicating the transition from the extreme of yin to yang; "meeting a relative on the road," an unexpected encounter with Mr. X, becoming brother and sister; the legitimate wife is like a stone; the concubine is like an egg, an egg cannot compete with a stone, but one is whole and one is broken, the legitimate wife is gone and the concubine remains, the new person will replace the old one. Everything happened as predicted, what a wonder!

    178

    里乘子曰：绛帻生自是黄衫客一流人，所以处分某生夫妻，不愧智而且侠，快人快事，惜冯小青当日未遇此君耳！
    　　
    　　尸变
    　　
    　　江苏葛生，客游楚南，为观察某公记室。公展觐入都，以期迫兼程前驰，生与厮仆督装在后。一日，趁程较远，投旅舍过晚，宿客已满，再三商之主人，只求一榻地聊以栖止，主人沉思久之始首肯。导生至后一室，内故有南北两炕，科浪反，北中设一方土床也。几，上置灯檠，光荧荧如豆，剔而亮之，襆被南炕。时方暑月，见北炕已有一客仰卧其上，头蒙毡笠，不辨老少。以天热尚着毡笠，疑是病夫，姑呼而问之，不答，意其睡熟，遂置之。枯坐取管吸建烟，渐觉倦怠，将拂簟就寝，忽见北炕客坐起，瞪目直视。生谛审之，面色惨白，不类生人；试问之，寂然不语，虑是尸变，心且惧，欲遁匿床下，计僵尸身不能佝，或可幸免，奈砖炕四围砌密，无从窜入，益复惶悚。

    Explanation

    178

    Li Chengzi said: "Jiangzé Sheng is indeed a man of the same caliber as the Huangshan Guest — in dealing with the couple of a certain scholar, he displayed no lack of wisdom and chivalry. It was a swift and righteous act. One can only regret that Feng Xiaqing did not meet such a man in her time!"

    Corpse Transformation

    Jiangsu native Ge Sheng was a guest traveling in the southern part of Chu, serving as a literary assistant to a certain official of the guancha rank. When the official set out to present his credentials in the capital, due to the urgency of the schedule, he traveled at double speed. Ge Sheng and his attendants remained behind to oversee the packing. One day, as the journey was long, they arrived at an inn too late in the evening. The lodging was already full, and after repeatedly asking the innkeeper, they only sought a single place to rest. The innkeeper pondered for a long time before finally agreeing. He led Ge Sheng to a back room, which had two kang beds, one to the north and one to the south. In the middle of the northern one, there was a square earthen bed. There was a table, on which stood a lamp stand, its light dim as a bean. He cleaned it and lit it, then placed his bedding on the southern kang. It was the height of summer, and as he looked toward the northern kang, he saw a guest lying on his back, with a felt hat covering his head — it was impossible to tell if he was old or young. Since it was so hot, the man still wore the felt hat, which made Ge Sheng suspect he might be ill. He called out to him, but received no answer, and assuming he was asleep, he left him be. He sat in silence, took up a pipe, and began to smoke. Gradually, he felt tired and was about to brush the mat and go to sleep when suddenly, the guest on the northern kang sat up and stared directly at him. Ge Sheng closely examined him — his face was pale, unlike that of a living person. He tried to ask him, but the man remained silent. Suspecting that this was a corpse transformation, his heart trembled with fear. He wanted to flee and hide under the bed, thinking that a zombie’s body could not bend and that he might thus escape. But the brick kang was surrounded by tight masonry, with no way to crawl in. He became even more terrified and anxious.

    179

    尝闻俗言：僵尸必借人生气方得起，其瞪目视人，人亦瞪目视之，四目相向，阳克阴永不能动；如目少眨，则阳气便散，彼即乘势而起。生忆其说，遂亦瞪目对视，不敢稍瞬。良久，渐难搘持。幸南炕旁近双扉，反手潜以烟管拨其，顿脱，急起拔关趋而出，尸亦跃起直追，缘生出户匆遽，双扉为衣风拽掩，尸撞触之，遂阖，阻不得出。方生之出也迫急，失足颠踣，晕不能兴；逾时始力疾起，呼厮仆召主人至，厉声叱责。主人忸怩谢过，且言客家小康，距此不过一舍地，今午过此地，得暴疾卒，已遣人召其妻孥，会当即至。正哗论间，妻孥果至，主人历诉其故，见生颧青鼻肿，并损二齿，俱稽颡谢罪。急欲入室，双扉为尸撑拄，猝莫能开。同破壁入，见尸直仆门上，十指入木如钉，牢不可拔。妻孥环哭，斧扉以停其尸。又见几灯为尸触翻，油泼南炕衾席，污渍狼藉，乃厚偿生资，相将舁尸而去。葛生与吾友葛菊人同族，菊人历历言之如此。

    Explanation

    179

    I once heard a folk saying: a zombie can only rise if it borrows human vitality. If a zombie stares at a person, the person should also stare back, and with their eyes locked, the yang energy will overcome the yin energy, preventing the zombie from moving. If the person blinks, the yang energy will dissipate, and the zombie will take advantage of this to rise. I remembered this saying and decided to stare at the zombie without blinking. Gradually, it became difficult to maintain. Fortunately, there were double doors near the south炕, and I used a pipe to push the latch, and it suddenly opened. I quickly pulled the door and rushed out. The zombie jumped up and chased me, but I had just escaped, and the doors closed behind me, blocking the zombie. I stumbled and fell, becoming dizzy and unable to get up. The owner shouted angrily. The owner felt embarrassed and apologized, saying that the guest's family was a small household, not far from here, and they had passed by earlier and suddenly fell ill, dying. They had already sent someone to summon his wife and children, who would arrive soon. While we were arguing, the wife and children indeed arrived. The owner explained the situation, and I saw that my cheeks were bruised, my nose swollen, and I had lost two teeth. They all knelt and begged for forgiveness. They wanted to enter the room, but the doors were blocked by the zombie, and they couldn't open them. They broke through the wall and saw the zombie stuck to the door, its fingers embedded like nails, and it couldn't be moved. The wife and children cried around it, and they used an axe to stop the zombie. They also saw that the lamp had been knocked over, spilling oil onto the south炕's bedding, making a mess. They paid me a large sum of money as compensation and carried the zombie away. I am from the same clan as my friend Ge JuRen, and he told me this story in detail.

    180

    小蛇
    　　
    　　黄稼田司马言，其乡某孝廉，礼闱下第南归，乘一薄笨车，途中天气暴热，当午歇凉，车子坐白杨树下。见一小蛇，长尺许，竟体褐灰色，昂头向上；树上则蟠一大蛇，身粗如碗，垂头向下，两头相向。见小蛇口中呼吸，大蛇为气所摄，渐觉瘫软，不能存身。车子嘿嘿诧异，以小反制大，心颇不平，急起，以足向小蛇颈上极力踏之，小蛇负痛，掉尾鞭其足背，车子固赤足着屦，足顿肿。小蛇既殪，大蛇屈伸久之，始蜿蜒穿树而去。乃车子之足，不炊许时已肿过脐，痛彻心髓，寻卒。

    Explanation

    180

    A Small Snake

    The military officer Huang Jiatian reported that a scholar-official from his hometown, who had failed the imperial examination and was returning home, was traveling in a simple and slow cart. The weather was extremely hot, and he decided to rest in the shade of a white poplar tree during the midday heat. He saw a small snake, about a foot long, with a brownish-gray body, raising its head upward. On the tree, there was a large snake coiled around it, as thick as a bowl, lowering its head downward, facing the small snake. The small snake was seen breathing, and the large snake was entranced by its breath, gradually becoming paralyzed and unable to maintain its position. The scholar-official muttered in astonishment, feeling quite unfair that the small snake could control the large one. He quickly stood up and stepped on the small snake's neck with all his might. The small snake, feeling pain, whipped its tail against his foot. The scholar-official was wearing sandals on his bare feet, and his foot immediately swelled. The small snake died, and the large snake stretched and contracted for a long time before slithering through the tree and leaving. The scholar-official's foot, within a short while, had swollen above the navel, and the pain penetrated to the bone marrow. Soon after, he died.

    181

    辣虫变蛇
    　　
    　　夏日有虫，长寸许，竟体皆毛，刺人痛于蜂螫，吾乡呼为辣虫。黄稼田司马家有园，树木甚多。尝夏晚纳凉，见一辣虫栖桐树干上，以草拨之不动，遂置之。越日，又一虫来衔其尾，凡七日，陆续相接，共七虫，长不及尺。司马留心以察其异。日见蠕蠕微动，渐自胶续，不十日，竟共化成一蛇，蜿蜒而去。以数虫化一虫，奇哉！噫！七日七虫，次第如有约而至，是果谁使之哉？

    Explanation

    181

    In the summer, there was an insect about an inch long, covered in hair, and its sting was as painful as a bee's. In my hometown, we call it a "spicy insect." Huang Jiatian, a Marquis, had a garden with many trees. One evening in the summer, while seeking relief from the heat, I saw a spicy insect resting on the trunk of a Chinese parasol tree. When I prodded it with grass, it did not move, so I left it alone. The next day, another insect came and bit the tail of the first one. Over the next seven days, more insects arrived in succession, each biting the tail of the previous one. After seven days, there were seven insects linked together, forming a creature less than a foot long. Huang Jiatian paid close attention to this unusual phenomenon. Each day, the insects moved slowly, gradually adhering to each other. After about ten days, they had transformed into a single snake, which slithered away. To see several insects transform into one was truly amazing! Over seven days, seven insects arrived in succession, as if by agreement, but who could have caused this phenomenon?

    182

    滦州儒学蛇
    　　
    　　滦州儒学，有舆夫见大成殿外树下一小蛇，长七八寸，粗裁如箸，竟体红光耀目，诧为罕见。以其小也易之，戏搦其尾，将携以示人；乃手甫近尾，蛇昂头直立，身暴长，尾略一摆，已将舆夫颠踣倒地，晕不能行。间壁一门子，闻声伏窗窥之，见一大赤蛇，其长不知几许，蜿蜒入大成殿内去，其身粗巨，塞门几满，逾时始竟。门子骇异，急入殿寻之，杳然无迹，见舆夫卧地呻吟，扶归，臀股青肿不良于行。彼此言蛇之异，猜为神物，命舆夫禳祷之，寻瘥。

    Explanation

    182

    In Luanzhou, at the Confucian school, a laborer saw a small snake under a tree outside the Great Hall of Accomplishment. The snake was about seven or eight inches long and as thick as a chopstick, with its entire body covered in bright red light, which was strikingly unusual. The laborer, thinking it was insignificant, playfully grasped its tail and intended to show it to others. As soon as his hand approached the tail, the snake raised its head, stood upright, and its body suddenly grew longer. Its tail swayed slightly, and the laborer was thrown head over heels to the ground, falling heavily. A gatekeeper in the adjacent building heard the commotion and peered out the window. He saw a large red snake, whose length was unknown, moving in a winding manner into the Great Hall of Accomplishment. The snake was so large that it almost blocked the door. After a long time, it finally disappeared. The gatekeeper was shocked and hurried into the hall to search for it, but found no trace. He then saw the laborer lying on the ground, moaning in pain. The laborer was helped back to his home, where he had severe bruising and swelling in his buttocks and thighs, making it difficult for him to walk. Both the gatekeeper and the laborer speculated that the snake was a divine being. They ordered the laborer to perform rites to appease the snake, and he soon recovered.

    183

    溧阳史仲皋言三事
    　　
    　　乡里有某生者，素好习武，凡击刺超距，无所不能，尝授徒乡里。暑月，辄逾墙出，盗瓜解热。一夜摘瓜回，忽见一人，匡躟尾其后，审其步趋与人异，意是僵尸，急迁道斜行以避之。乃彼亦蹑迹，相从迫逐，不少宽纵。生大恐，踉跄疾奔三四里许，甫至己塾，急飞步跃登墙上；尸亦三踊三跃，所不及墙者盈咫。墙上故覆以瓦，后一跃用力较猛，落地时颏挂仰瓦上，虚悬空中，竟不能动。生俯首谛视，且駴且笑，知其不能为患，掌掴其面者再而去。翌晨，趋往观之，一无所有，墙下但馀黑水一洼，其臭刺鼻，不可向迩。某生读书寺中，间壁有空舍，中停枯柩，历年既久，尝出为祟。生素豪于胆，夜伏窗隙窥之，漏二下，果闻舍内窸窣作响，一老者衣冠自内出，蹀躞向外而去。

    Explanation

    183

    Shi Zhonggao of Liyang spoke of three matters:
    In his hometown, there was a certain student who had always been fond of practicing martial arts. He was capable of all martial techniques, including striking, thrusting, and leaping. He once taught students in his hometown. During the hot summer months, he would climb over walls to steal melons to cool down. One night, after picking some melons and returning, he suddenly saw a person following him, walking with a hunched posture. Observing that its gait was different from that of a normal person, he thought it might be a zombie. He quickly changed his path and walked diagonally to avoid it. However, it also followed closely, pursuing him relentlessly without any leniency. The protagonist was greatly frightened and ran hastily for several miles. Upon reaching his own school, he quickly leaped onto the wall. The figure also jumped three times and leaped three times, barely failing to reach the wall by a few inches. The wall was covered with tiles. On the final leap, the figure exerted more force, and upon landing, its chin caught on the upward-facing tile, leaving it suspended in mid-air, unable to move. The protagonist looked down, both shocked and amused, realizing that it could not cause harm, and slapped its face twice before leaving. The next morning, he rushed to check and found nothing. Under the wall, there was only a small pool of black water, which had a foul odor that was unbearable to approach.

    Another student was studying in a temple, and there was an empty room next to it. Inside the room, there was a dried-up coffin that had been there for many years, and it was said to have caused disturbances. The student, known for his bravery, lay in wait at the window crack to observe it. At the second strike of the night bell, he indeed heard rustling sounds inside the room. An old man, dressed in formal attire, emerged from inside and walked slowly outwards.

    184

    生烛其舍，见棺盖已开，遂为合之，料其不能再入；又恐与己作难，乃返室猱升梁上，伏而察之。少选，老者归舍，果以棺合不能再入，迹至生室，意甚愤怒。遍室搜寻，见生伏梁上，瞪而仰视，目光绿若猫睛，三踊三跃，愈跃愈高，去梁几不盈尺。生大恐，袖携《易经》，急俯以掷其首，老者仆地，顿僵。天明人来，生始敢下，历述其异，共视其尸，已幻为棺盖，焚之，臭闻数里。自是毁柩埽舍，怪异遂绝。某处有厝柩，白日尝出为怪，一乡患之。有木工某甲，笑谓众曰：“诸君如醵金作筵饮我，当为除怪。”众果如言，作筵招甲。饮毕，问：“何时除怪？”甲踌躇久之，乃曰：“必俟天气晴爽、日方中时乃可，然须诸君往佐之。”众诺，订约而去。至日，甲来邀众同往。袖出尺周量棺盖，取墨绳弹于盖之当中者三，三弹而棺中皆霄霄有声，意似痛楚，初声较厉，后以次递杀；又取斧微削棺之四隅，谓众曰：“怪已除矣！”自是果绝。众大喜，乃厚酬甲。

    Explanation

    184

    The Man and the Coffin

    He lit a candle in the room and saw that the coffin lid was already open. He closed it, judging that it could not enter again. Yet fearing that it might cause trouble for him, he returned to the room and climbed up the beam like a monkey, crouching to observe it. Soon after, the old man returned to his dwelling. Indeed, the coffin was closed again and could not be re-entered. He traced the signs to the man's room and became very angry. He searched the entire room and saw the man crouched on the beam, staring upward with eyes green as a cat's eyes. He leaped three times, each time higher than the last, nearly reaching the beam within a foot. The man was greatly terrified. He pulled the I Ching from his sleeve, bent down quickly, and hurled it at the old man’s head. The old man fell to the ground and lay stiff. At dawn, when people arrived, the man finally dared to descend. He recounted the strange events, and together they examined the body, which had transformed into the coffin lid. They burned it, and the stench spread for several li. From that day on, the coffin was destroyed and the room swept clean; the strange phenomena ceased.

    In a certain place, a coffin was placed, and during the day it would often emerge to cause strange occurrences, troubling the entire village. There was a carpenter named Jia, who laughed and said to the people, “If you all contribute money to host a banquet for me, I will help you get rid of the ghost.” The people indeed followed his words and prepared a banquet to invite him. After the banquet, they asked, “When will you get rid of the ghost?” Jia hesitated for a long time and then said, “It can only be done on a clear, sunny day when the sun is high in the sky. However, you all must come to assist me.” The people agreed and made a pact to return on that day. On the appointed day, Jia came to invite the people to go together. He took out a measuring rod to measure the coffin lid and, using a chalk line, marked it three times in the center. Each time he struck it, a sound like wind echoed from within the coffin, as if in pain—the first sound was the loudest, and each subsequent one grew quieter. Then he took an axe and slightly trimmed the four corners of the coffin. He told the people, “The ghost has been removed!” From that day on, the strange occurrences indeed ceased. The people were greatly delighted and gave Jia a generous reward.

    185

    里乘子曰：仲皋又言圬工之刀、石工之锥、木工之斧、尺、绳、墨各物，均极宝贵，皆能镇压怪异。凡房舍之梁、柱、楹、桷，楶、等类，及诸木器无故自爆作声，皆墨绳刨刮未净，其精灵日久自鸣也。然耶否耶？
    　　
    　　樵夫某甲
    　　
    　　苏州樵夫某甲，娶妻某氏，伉俪甚敦，惟日相对忧贫。甲尝忍饥以媚其妻。一日，卖柴毕，腹饥，入市食锞，吝钱，仅食三颗。旁座一人叹其太少，力劝加餐，愿为纳资，甲以素不识面，辞之。其人再三固让，乃各食十馀颗。其人纳资讫，问甲所业，怜之曰：“君业良苦，何不贸易？”甲以无资对。曰：“是不难，但从我去，愿以相助。”偕行四五里，出齐门，至一处，有瓷坛三，皆土封其口。其人指曰：“此中皆藏镪，任君取携，不靳也。”命俱去其封，视之果朱提充牣。因趣甲自取。甲廉，只取两铤。

    Explanation

    185

    Master Li Cheng said: Zhong Gao again remarked that the mason’s knife, the stonemason’s awl, the carpenter’s axe, the ruler, the rope, and the compass—all these tools are extremely valuable, for they are all capable of subduing strange and supernatural forces. Any wooden beams, pillars, doorframes, rafters, brackets, or other wooden structures in a house, as well as various wooden objects that suddenly burst or make noise without cause, are all due to the measuring rope not being fully scraped clean. Over time, the spirit within the wood becomes active and causes the sounds. Is this true, or is it not?

    A Woodcutter Named Mr. Jia

    Mr. Jia, a woodcutter from Suzhou, married a woman from a certain family. Their marriage was very harmonious, but they lived in constant worry over poverty. Mr. Jia once endured hunger to please his wife. One day, after selling his firewood, he was very hungry and went into the market to buy some food. He was very stingy with his money and only ate three pieces. A man sitting nearby sighed at how little he ate and urged him to eat more, offering to pay for it. Mr. Jia, not knowing him, declined. The man insisted several times, and in the end, they each ate more than ten pieces. After paying, the man asked Mr. Jia what he did for a living, and feeling pity, said, “Your profession is very arduous. Why don’t you try trading instead?” Mr. Jia replied that he had no money. The man said, “That is not difficult. Just come with me, and I will help you.” They walked together for several miles, exiting through Qi Gate, and arrived at a place where there were three ceramic jars, all sealed with earth. The man pointed and said, “Inside these jars are all hidden gold. You may take whatever you like, without any hesitation.” He ordered them to remove the seals, and upon opening them, they found the jars filled with red gold. He then urged Mr. Jia to take what he wanted. Mr. Jia, being cautious, took only two bars.

    186

    曰：“何不多取？”固请益之。甲又取二铤，其人乃自取十馀铤付甲，使以衣盛之，约计不下千金，喜出望外，称谢而归，倾置床上。妻方在灶下作晚炊，甲趋拊其背曰：“卿勿复尔，当作富家媪，享用有日矣，何必作苦！”妻嗤之曰：“君颠也耶？未睡何得作梦语？”甲笑曰：“来来！”遂拉妻袂同至房中，指床上谓之曰：“卿视此累累者，犹不足为富家媪耶？”妻视之，但见纸灰一堆而已，骇问：“此物何来？”甲茫然若失，具告其事，知为鬼所揶揄。翌辰，往寻三坛，亦为乌有。自是，夫妻安命食苦，不再忧贫。久之，日渐自给，今则居然小康矣。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：一经鬼物揶揄，即安命食苦，不再忧贫，如此夫妇，可谓贤矣！居然小康，不亦宜乎？

    Explanation

    186

    Jia asked, "Why not take more?" The person persisted and gave him more. Jia took two ingots, and the person then took over ten ingots and gave them to Jia, telling him to put them in his clothes. The total was estimated to be no less than a thousand gold coins. Jia was extremely delighted, thanked the person, and returned home, dumping the ingots on the bed. His wife was in the kitchen preparing dinner when Jia rushed over, patted her back, and said, "You must not do this anymore. We will become wealthy old ladies, and there will be a day to enjoy it. Why endure hardship?" His wife laughed and said, "Are you crazy? Why are you speaking in your sleep?" Jia smiled and said, "Come here!" He pulled her sleeve and led her to the bedroom, pointing to the bed and saying, "Look at these piles here. Still not enough to be a wealthy old lady?" His wife looked and saw only a pile of paper ashes. She was shocked and asked, "Where did these things come from?" Jia was dazed and lost, told her the whole story, and realized it was a trick by ghosts. The next day, he went to look for the three altars, but they had also vanished. From then on, the couple accepted their fate and endured hardship, no longer worrying about poverty. After a while, they gradually became self-sufficient, and now they have achieved a comfortable life.

    The Village Historian said: Once tricked by ghosts, they accepted their fate and endured hardship, no longer worrying about poverty. Such a couple can be called virtuous. Eventually achieving a comfortable life, isn't that appropriate?

    187

    平乡县老儒
    　　
    　　直隶平乡县老儒某，年逾五十，授徒村塾。一夜，月白风清，挑灯独坐，忽有二女款关入室，韶龄媚姿，世所罕有。某目炫心摇，诘所自来。二女掩口笑曰：“我姊妹皆仙人也，与君有夙缘，愿侍枕席。”某狂喜，不暇研究，遂与绸缪，情好綦笃。自是，每日诸徒散后，二女即至，风雨无间。如是年馀，某神情日渐惝怳。家人察其有异，叩之，秘不肯言。某有二子，皆邑诸生，夜伏窗潜窥之，一无所睹，但闻某喃喃呓语，音殊模糊，一字不辨。家人忧之，禳祷无效。忽一夜，二女谓某曰：“妾等侍君年馀，情好无猜，不可不使君知我家里。君肯从我游，当使君一睹天界。”某忻然诺之。二女遂左右挟某行，登山涉水如履平地，终夜跋涉，不少停趾，道路修阻，不知几何由旬。未几，天渐达曙，入一大山，树木森茂，中有兰若。某闻晨钟，心顿清爽。

    Explanation

    187

    An Elderly Scholar from Pingle County

    An elderly scholar from Pingle County, whose name is not mentioned, was over fifty years old and taught in a village school. One night, the moon was bright and the wind was clear. He picked up a lamp and sat alone, when suddenly two young women knocked on the door and entered the room. They were in their beautiful youth, with charming appearances, rare in the world. The scholar's eyes were dazzled and his heart was shaken, and he asked where they had come from. The two women covered their mouths and laughed, saying, "We sisters are all immortals, and we have a predestined connection with you. We wish to serve you in bed." The scholar was wildly delighted and did not have time to think it through, so he formed a close relationship with them, and their affection was deeply strong. From then on, every day after all the students had dispersed, the two women would come, regardless of wind or rain. Like this for over a year, the scholar's spiritual appearance gradually became bewildered. Family members noticed something unusual and questioned him, but he kept it secret and would not speak. The scholar had two sons, both local scholars, who lay in wait at the window to secretly observe him, but saw nothing, hearing only the scholar mumbling and mumbling, the sound very unclear, not a single word could be distinguished. Family members were worried about him, and rituals and prayers were ineffective. Suddenly one night, the two women said to the scholar, "We have served you for over a year, our affection without suspicion, it is impossible not to let you know our home. If you agree to travel with us, we will let you see the heavenly realm." The scholar happily agreed. The two women then took the scholar by the sides and led him to climb mountains and cross rivers as if walking on level ground, traveling all night without stopping, the road long and obstructed, the distance unknown. Soon, the sky gradually became light, and they entered a large mountain, where the trees were dense and lush, and there was a Buddhist temple in the middle. The scholar heard the morning bell, and his heart suddenly became clear and refreshed.

    188

    一老僧策杖而来，二女见之，却不敢前。僧问某将何往，某心佝愗，噤不能答。僧以袖拂其面，喝声曰，“疾！如律令勅。”如雷震耳，某豁然如梦初醒，回视二女，已不知何往矣。知二女为妖，以僧非常人，膜拜求救。僧谓：“二女皆天狐，与君固有夙缘，此来亦无祸君意。脱不遇老僧，恐还乡无望耳。”某益骇惧，力求拯救。僧乃戟指于某胸背，勅勒书符，讫，送某出山，指示归路。又袖出碎镪若干，聊作资斧，且谓：“此地为永平世界，距君家已千馀里。途中但凝神守志，勿涉邪想，妖自不敢近。归家，日晨起净口，端坐诵《易经》一通，当永保无患矣。谨识勿忘，慎之！慎之！”某再拜受教归。如僧言，二女果不再至。同治癸亥，予北征过平乡，某尚无恙，年已七十矣。人有问其事者，自言不讳。惜某愦愦，当日未曾详诘僧为何名，所至之地为何山、何寺耳！

    Explanation

    188

    An elderly monk approached, staff in hand. The two women saw him but dared not come forward. The monk asked where the man was going, but the man, confused and bewildered, could not answer. The monk brushed his face with his sleeve and shouted, "Quickly! As by law and decree!" The command was like thunder in his ears, and the man suddenly awoke as if from a dream. He looked back at the two women, who had vanished without a trace. Realizing the two women were demons, he knelt and prayed for help, knowing the monk was no ordinary man. The monk said, "The two women are celestial foxes. You and they have a predestined connection, and their intent is not to harm you. If not for me, you might have no hope of returning home." The man was even more terrified and begged for salvation. The monk pointed at the man's chest and back with his finger, commanded and wrote a talisman, and then sent the man out of the mountain, pointing out the way home. He also pulled out some small coins from his sleeve, giving them as provisions, and said, "This place is the Land of Eternal Peace, and it is already more than a thousand miles from your home. On the journey, focus your mind and maintain your resolve, do not entertain evil thoughts, and the demons will not dare to approach. When you return home, wake up early each morning, cleanse your mouth, sit upright, and recite the Yijing (Book of Changes) once. You will always remain safe and secure. Remember carefully and do not forget, be careful! Be very careful!" The man bowed twice and took the monk's teachings, returning home. Following the monk's instructions, the two women did indeed not return. In the year of Gui Hai during the Tongzhi era, I was on a northern campaign and passed through Pingxiang. The man was still alive, now seventy years old. When people asked him about the incident, he spoke of it without hesitation. Unfortunately, the man was dull and did not inquire further about the monk's name or the name of the mountain and temple where the event took place.

    189

    超勇公轶事
    　　
    　　超勇公，索伦人，即伊彻满洲也，少以军功累晋公爵。闻公出征时，沿途供亿，必遣心腹偏将某甲前驱预备，公平日嗜好惟某甲能知之。凡启节，每到驿馆，下马即顾某甲问：“所需各物俱备不？”某甲鞠躬对曰：“诺。”公色然喜曰：“好孩子，狠会办事。”甫入门，某甲手捧髹漆红盒一，屈膝以献。去其盖，内盛径寸大蜘蛛百枚，蝍蛆、蝾螈、虿蝎等物称是。公一一去其钳爪，生啖毕，某甲侍公登堂梁，左右悬两巨蛇，粗如盏，长丈有奇，公睨而嬉笑，拔金错刀，寸断大嚼，如咬甘蔗，食讫，某甲屈单膝告退。

    Explanation

    189

    The Anecdotes of the Duke of Chaoyong

    The Duke of Chaoyong was a Solon man, also known as Yichi Manchu. He rose to the rank of duke through military achievements. During his military campaigns, he would send a trusted subordinate, referred to as "Mr. A," ahead to prepare the route. His daily preferences and habits were known only to this trusted subordinate.

    Whenever he traveled, upon arriving at an inn, he would dismount and immediately turn to Mr. A, asking, "Are all the necessary items prepared?" Mr. A would bow and respond, "Yes, everything is ready." The duke would then smile and say, "Good boy, you know how to handle things well."

    Upon entering the inn, Mr. A would present a lacquered red box, kneeling to offer it. The duke would remove the lid, revealing inside a hundred large spiders, centipedes, salamanders, and scorpions. The duke would remove the limbs of each creature and eat them raw. Mr. A would then help the duke climb onto a beam, where two large snakes, as thick as a wine cup and over ten feet long, were hanging. The duke would look at them playfully, laugh, and draw out a golden knife. He would cut the snakes into pieces and eat them voraciously, as if biting into sugar cane. After finishing his meal, Mr. A would kneel on one knee and respectfully withdraw.

    190

    公独入后室，内有蠢胖村妇八人，年皆二十以来，裸体以待，公一一递接已，乃呼从者具汤沐、进冠服，出见客。客退，又呼某甲来，赞之曰：“好孩子，狠会办事。”凡沿途供亿，必如此方惬公意，不则竟日忽忽不乐，戏下部曲，诃责鞭箠，无所不至矣。又闻公过戈壁瀚海等地，常数百里无人烟，村妇难致，则以肥壮水牛代之，故出征必多带水牛听用。按日轮交四牛，牛辄不能与公敌，公恨其不能胜任，则手刃剐而生飨之。观公所为，可谓天人，彼樊舞阳之生食豚肩，常开平之与虎豹交，以公较之，皆不足为奇矣！

    Explanation

    190

    The official entered the back chamber alone. Inside were eight dull, plump village women, all in their twenties, naked and waiting. He received each one in turn, then called his attendants to prepare a bath and bring ceremonial clothing, after which he emerged to greet the guests. When the guests departed, he summoned a certain person named Jia and praised him, saying, "Good child, you really know how to handle things." For all provisions along the way, it had to be done this way to satisfy the official's will; otherwise, he would spend the entire day listless and unhappy, and his subordinates and soldiers would be scolded and beaten without limit. It was also said that when the official passed through regions such as the Gobi Desert and the vast, empty瀚海, where there were often hundreds of miles without a soul in sight, it was difficult to obtain village women, so they used strong, fat water buffalo as substitutes. Hence, on military campaigns, he always brought many water buffalo along for use. Each day, four buffalo were rotated; yet the buffalo could not withstand the official’s strength. Frustrated by their inability to perform, he would personally skin them alive and eat them. To witness the official’s actions, one might call him a divine being. Compared to Fan Wuyang, who ate raw pork shoulder, or Chang Kaiping, who fought tigers and leopards, the official’s deeds, by contrast, are not remarkable at all.

    191

    里乘子曰：相传公能伏地听百里外马驼声。且知敌军有无多寡。人皆谓公天授，不知北魏时斛律金行军，用匈奴法，望尘知马步多少；嗅地知军远近，公既久于戎行，阅历较多，不难留心师其遗意耳。又闻公英姿盖世，气慑三军，而平生最惮阿文成一人。当从阿文成出师时，每召计议军务，公辄兢栗异常，汗多透甲。故阿所运筹，一以命公，无或违误。阿公所在克奏肤功，皆公之力居多云。

    Explanation

    191

    Li Chengzi said: It is said that the public could lie on the ground and hear the sounds of horses and camels from a hundred miles away. Moreover, they could know whether the enemy army existed and its size. People all believed that the public was divinely endowed, not knowing that during the Northern Wei dynasty, Hu Lü Jin used the Xiongnu method when leading troops, being able to estimate the number of horse and foot soldiers by looking at the dust; and to know the distance of the army by smelling the ground. Since the public had long experience in military service and had more experience, it was not difficult for them to pay attention and follow the intentions left behind. Furthermore, it was heard that the public had an heroic appearance that surpassed the world, aweing the entire army with their presence. However, throughout their life, they feared only Ah Wencheng. When they went to war with Ah Wencheng, every time they were called to discuss military affairs, the public would tremble abnormally, sweating so much that it seeped through their armor. Therefore, Ah Wencheng's plans were entrusted entirely to the public, with no deviation or error. Ah Wencheng achieved great success wherever they went, all due to the public's efforts.

    192

    海州四怪
    　　
    　　海州四怪，一鳢，一蜘蛛，一蜈蚣，一蚂蚁也。鳢长丈有半，竟体鳞甲灿然，本草所谓穿山甲者是也。蜈蚣亦长丈许，有翅能飞，尝天晴风静，飞戾半空，夭矫自如，人多误为纸鸢。蚂蚁大若栲栳，臀坚似铁，五兵不能入。四者之中蜘蛛尤为灵异，其大如箕，丝粗如小儿臂，好与龙斗，吐丝缚龙，胶不可解，必火龙来焚其丝乃已。滨海人常于山野拾得断丝，尺许之丝，两健儿持两端极力扯之，长可盈丈，利刃不能断，人恒宝之。四怪常幻人形，出游市廛，不为人祸。

    Explanation

    192

    The Four Monsters of Haizhou

    The Four Monsters of Haizhou are a fish, a spider, a centipede, and an ant. The fish is half a zhang long, its entire body covered in shiny scales, what the Bencao (Herbal Classic) refers to as the pangolin. The centipede is also about a zhang long, with wings that enable it to fly. It often flies into the sky on clear, windless days, twisting and turning with ease, often mistaken for a paper kite. The ant is as large as a wicker basket, its rear as hard as iron, impervious to the five types of weapons. Among the four, the spider is particularly mysterious and extraordinary. It is as large as a winnowing basket, its silk as thick as a child's arm. It enjoys fighting dragons, spitting silk to bind them, the silk being so strong that it cannot be untangled. Only a fire-breathing dragon can burn through its silk. Coastal inhabitants often find broken strands of its silk in the mountains and wilds. A strip of silk about a foot long, when two strong men pull it from both ends with all their might, can stretch to a full zhang in length. Sharp blades cannot cut it, and people always treasure it. The four monsters often transform into human form, wandering the bustling marketplaces, causing no harm to people.

    193

    蜘蛛出时尤多，每出则化形老者，白髯垂胸，道气盎然，最喜与小儿戏。出时，小儿多依其前后左右，老者出钱市梨枣饼饵之属分啖群儿，人多识之，呼为朱道人；遇久早，为人求雨辄应，地方颇受其福。顾四物同出，雷恒欲击之，往往片刻间雨雹骤至，雷电交作，四物即遁，则鳢居前，以头钻山，山洞如腐，鳢即入洞，蜘蛛啣尾继进，蜈蚣又次之，蚂蚁殿后，以臀堵洞口。丰隆左执锥，右秉钺，目灼灼观望，竟不能施其一击之威；徘徊刻许，天晴雨霁，四物亦不知何往矣。人以蚂蚁臀肉之坚，皆称为“铁屁股蚂蚁”云。

    Explanation

    193

    When spiders come out, they are especially numerous. Each time they appear, they transform into an elderly person with white beard hanging down to the chest, exuding a strong spiritual aura, and most enjoy playing with children. When they appear, children often follow them around. The elderly person spends money to buy pears, jujubes, and other snacks, distributing them to the children. People recognize them and call them Zhu Daoist. When there is a long drought, they pray for rain and it always responds, greatly benefiting the local area. However, when the four creatures come out together, the thunder god always wants to strike them. Often within a short time, hail and rain come suddenly, and lightning and thunder occur simultaneously. The four creatures immediately flee. The eel leads the way, using its head to drill through the mountain, and the mountain hole is like a rotten one, so the eel enters the hole. The spider follows, carrying its tail, and the centipede follows next. The ant follows last, blocking the hole with its buttocks. The thunder god, Fenglong, holds a needle in his left hand and a halberd in his right, with sharp eyes watching, but he ultimately cannot exert his power of a single strike. After a moment of hesitation, the sky clears and the rain stops, and the four creatures also disappear without a trace. People say the ant's buttocks are very hard and call them 'iron-buttocked ants.'

    194

    郑甲
    　　
    　　郑甲者，汴梁人，固绿林之魁也。中年改行，至老，乡党称善人。与人接，谦和惟谨，见者不知其能武也。去发一撮，圆如钱，光如镜，似僧之受戒火者。叩之，自言少时两臂能开二百石弓，取十砖累叠之，剖以掌，画然中开，利如刀劈；日可行六百里，捷过奔马；尤工弹术，百步外击物，百不失一。壮时驰骋齐鲁燕赵之郊，短衣匹马，藉探丸渔猎过客，往无不利，自以为无敌于天下矣。一日，有解饷官辇银百万如京师，车驮甚盛，末一少年为后殿，年约二十以来，美皙温存，弱如处女，展绣褥趺坐车唇；衣服华灿，首戴角巾，上缀猫睛宝石，大于龙眼核，精光上烛，与太阳相激射，洵希世珍也。以其为纨公子附从入都，殊不介意。惟心涎重资，计必有好建儿护卫，不敢孟浪从事。爰勼同道中之好身手者四十余人，沿途尾之，蹈隙而动。

    Explanation

    194

    Zhang Jia, a man from Bianliang, was originally the leader of the Green Forest bandits. In his middle age, he changed his ways and, by the time he grew old, was known in his hometown as a virtuous person. When dealing with others, he was modest, polite, and cautious, and those who saw him did not know that he was capable of martial arts. He had a tuft of hair removed, which was round like a coin, smooth like a mirror, and resembled the hair of a monk who had undergone a ritual burning. When asked about it, he said that in his youth, he could draw a bow that required a force of two hundred catties, split ten bricks stacked on top of each other with his palm, cleanly dividing them as if they were cut by a knife; he could travel six hundred li in a day, swift beyond a galloping horse; he was especially skilled in archery, hitting a target from a hundred steps away without missing once. In his prime, he roamed the outskirts of Qi, Lu, Yan, and Zhao, wearing a short coat and riding a single horse, preying on travelers by robbing them, never failing in his endeavors, and thought himself invincible in the world. One day, an official carrying a million taels of silver to the capital had a large convoy of heavily loaded carts, with a young man at the rear, about twenty years old, fair-skinned, gentle, and frail as a maiden, sitting cross-legged on a cushioned mat at the edge of the carriage; he wore splendid and glittering clothes, a square cap with a cat's eye gemstone larger than a dragon eye nut, whose brilliance shone upward, competing with the sun, truly a rare treasure in the world. Because he was accompanied by a young nobleman, Zhang Jia did not take it seriously. He was only envious of the heavy wealth, thinking that there must be good guards protecting it, and dared not act rashly. He gathered more than forty skilled fighters along the way and followed the convoy, waiting for an opportunity to act.

    195

    顾车驮过重，日行不过八十里，他日以遇雨失程，不及投店，道旁有兰若，遂栖止焉。予与众窃喜，曰，“此天授机会，不可失也。”于是伏莽以待。时新晴，湿云归山，少选，皎月东上，朗若白昼。漏二下，行踪已绝，万籁俱静，佥日：“可矣。”各褫外衣，着短褐，身藏利器，约次第逾墙入，拊掌吹唇为号，量技分遣，入者半，留者半。计议已定，同道中二十馀人鼓勇争先，一跃入墙，疾如飞鸟，堕地无声。予同二十馀人屏息墙阴，静听好音，准备运物。乃待至一时之久，墙内寂然。互相猜疑，吉凶莫决。又遣五人登墙探察消息，五人登墙不敢遽下，遥见少年秉烛檐梧，南向危坐，似知有人上墙，袖出白光一道，闪如电发。五人知是剑术，大骇，急厉声呼曰：“败，败矣！去去，勿缓！”吾侪闻之，急返身奔驰，墙头白光旋出，冷气逼人，猝难回避，各

    Explanation

    195

    The carriage was overloaded, limiting its daily travel to no more than eighty miles. On another day, due to rain, they lost their way and did not make it to an inn in time. By the roadside, there was a temple, and they decided to stay there for the night. The speaker and others secretly rejoiced, saying, "This is an opportunity granted by heaven and should not be missed." They then hid in the bushes to wait. The weather had just cleared up, and the wet clouds were returning to the mountains. Shortly after, the bright moon rose in the east, illuminating the area as if it were daytime. By the second hour of the night, all signs of life had ceased, and everything was silent. Everyone agreed that it was time. They each removed their outer clothes, put on short jackets, and hid their weapons. They agreed to enter in order, clapping and blowing to signal. They divided themselves according to their skills, with half entering and the other half remaining outside. After the plan was decided, more than twenty people from the group bravely competed to go first. They jumped over the wall quickly like birds, landing silently. The speaker and the other twenty or so people held their breath behind the wall, quietly listening for any sound, ready to move things. They waited for a long time, but the inside of the wall remained silent. They began to doubt each other, unsure of whether the outcome would be good or bad. They sent five more people to climb the wall to check the situation. The five people who climbed the wall were afraid to come down immediately. They saw a young man holding a candle under a eave, sitting upright facing south. He seemed to know someone had climbed the wall, and he pulled out a white light from his sleeve, which flashed like lightning. The five people recognized this as swordplay and were greatly frightened. They shouted loudly, "We are defeated, defeated! Go, go, don't delay!" The group heard this and quickly turned around to run away. A white light suddenly appeared from the top of the wall, and a cold air hit them. They could not avoid it in time, and each...

    196

    谋奔命。予见路旁有土圊，急投其中，秽深灭顶，仅露顖门，已为所髡，痛彻心髓。亡何，白光渐缩敛入墙。二十馀人已各身首异处，无一获免者。是役也，予幸为秽物所厌，得占硕果，不然，亦与四十馀人把臂为伍，白骨早朽矣。予既出圊，觉顶上血流涔涔，骤不可止，爰匍匐往丛莽中脱出秽服，别取他衣着之；又抽佩刀割襟以裹其创。自是嘿庆再生，不敢再为冯妇。今七十矣，每手摩剑瘢，犹觉电光在顶，为之齿击不置也。

    Explanation

    196

    I planned to translate the source text into English, ensuring that I handled idiomatic expressions, cultural allusions, syntactic ambiguities, and proper nouns as planned during the pre-drafting research. I aimed to produce a final translation that was both faithful to the original Classical Chinese and read naturally in English.

    Here is the final translation:

    I planned to flee in a hurry. I saw a latrine by the roadside and quickly threw myself into it. The filth was deep, submerging my head, leaving only my fontanelle exposed. I was shaved, and the pain was intense. Shortly after, a white light gradually contracted and disappeared into the wall. More than twenty people had their heads and bodies separated, and none escaped. In this incident, I was fortunate to be protected by the filth and survived, otherwise, I would have been among the more than forty people holding arms together, and my bones would have rotted early. I emerged from the latrine and felt blood flowing constantly from the top of my head, which I could not stop. I then crawled into a thicket, took off my dirty clothes, and put on others. I also drew my sword, cut my sleeve, and wrapped it around my wound. From then on, I silently rejoiced at being reborn and did not dare to be a fighter again. Now at seventy, every time I touch the scars on my sword, I still feel lightning on my head, and I cannot stop biting down on this memory.

    197

    千金亭
    　　
    　　吾乡方恪敏公，微时，屡试不第，行年五十，犹以笔耕为业。有精风鉴者，谓公大运将至，不十年可位至封圻。公问：“何之而可？”其人曰：“公利西北，不利东南，当以入都为可。”公固善书，素欲赴部谋为议叙，闻言，遂决意北上。襆被徒行，至保定界，资斧已绝，路旁有茅店，聊坐少憩，老妪提壶茶置公前，渴，不敢饮；案堆角黍累累，公正苦饥，涎视不语。老妪觇公意，笑问曰：“客欲食耶？”公笑曰：“然，奈无资何！”妪笑曰：“此细事耳，无资何害？”遂命子妇取角黍一盘，劝公饱啖，公亦不辞。啖毕，索纸笔书券付老妪藏之，谓：“他日过此必酬汝资，此券慎无失也。

    Explanation

    197

    Qianjin Pavilion

    In my hometown, there was a man named Fang Kemin, who was in humble circumstances and had failed the imperial exams repeatedly. At the age of fifty, he still made a living by writing. There was an expert in fortune-telling who told him that his great destiny was about to arrive, and within ten years, he could reach a high official position. Fang asked, "What should I do to achieve this?" The fortune-teller replied, "Your destiny is favorable in the northwest and unfavorable in the southeast. It is advisable for you to go to the capital." Fang was indeed good at calligraphy and had always wanted to go to the capital to seek official recognition. Upon hearing this, he decided to go north. Traveling with only a bag and a blanket, on foot, he reached the border of Baoding and ran out of money. Along the road, there was a thatched inn where he sat down briefly to rest. An old woman brought a pot of tea and placed it in front of him. He was too thirsty to drink. A tray was piled with glutinous rice dumplings, and he was starving, staring hungrily without speaking. The old woman observed his intentions and smiled, asking, "Do you want to eat?" Fang smiled and said, "Yes, but what to do without money?" The old woman smiled and said, "This is a small matter, having no money does not matter." She then ordered her daughter-in-law to bring a tray of glutinous rice dumplings and urged him to eat heartily. Fang did not refuse. After eating, he asked for paper and ink to write a receipt and gave it to the old woman to keep, saying, "Next time I pass by, I will definitely repay you. Please keep this receipt safe."

    198

    ”乃未及十年，公果为直隶总督。履任时，百官郊迎，观者夹道。公命清苑县令召老妪至，老妪不解所谓，战傈跪伏舆前，叩首请罪。公命左右扶起，温言抚慰，笑问曰：“一别多年，汝发已尽白矣，茅店犹无恙耶？饱啖角黍，今当酬汝资，所书券可缴还也。”老妪闻公言，始忆及前事，乃笑对曰：“向察公颜色，敬献不腆，未敢求谢。公今为贵人，尚忆及老妇，死且不朽。前券什袭藏之，当即奉缴，戋戋微物，那得幸邀赏赉，敢辞。”公笑曰：“汝归休，保卫眠食，吾当酌给汝资，令汝子小权子母，以娱馀年可也。”乃命人赍赐千金，送其还家。老妪感公德，作亭，奉公栗主而尸祝之。土人至今艳称其事，皆呼其亭曰“千金亭”。

    Explanation

    198

    Before ten years had passed, the official indeed became the governor-general of Zhili. When he assumed office, officials greeted him at the outskirts of the city, and onlookers lined the streets. The official ordered the magistrate of Qingyuan County to summon an elderly woman. The woman did not understand what was meant and, trembling with fear, knelt in front of the carriage, begging for forgiveness. The official ordered his attendants to help her up, comforted her with gentle words, and smiled as he asked, "It has been many years since we last met. Your hair is now completely white, and is your thatched inn still in good condition? I have eaten heartily of zongzi, and now I will repay you with funds. The bond you wrote can be returned to you." Upon hearing the official's words, the woman remembered the past and smiled, saying, "I observed your expression and humbly offered a small gift, but did not dare to ask for gratitude. You are now a person of high status, yet you still remember the old woman, and this will not perish even in death. I have carefully preserved the previous bond and will immediately return it. It is a small thing, how could I expect to receive a reward? I dare not decline." The official laughed and said, "You return to rest, take care of your sleep and meals, and I will provide you with funds. Let your son manage small affairs, so you can enjoy the remaining years." He then ordered someone to give her a thousand gold coins and send her back home. The woman was so grateful for the official's virtue that she built a pavilion and worshiped him with a spirit tablet. The local people still speak highly of the story and call the pavilion the "Thousand Gold Pavilion."

    199

    员先生
    　　
    　　粤东冯崧湖宫詹誉骥言，少侍封公游宦秦中，会母夫人有疾，百药罔效。时封翁宰某邑，或谓：“邑有员先生者，乡居好道，少得真仙秘传，能知过去未来，素精医理，不轻为人治疾，治辄奇效。如诸公子自往延请，渠惠然肯来，可保毋虑。”封翁乃命宫詹兄弟，翌辰徒步踵门延请。甫出城半里许，一人御车迎面而来，见宫詹兄弟，拱手问曰：“君等为冯公子耶？”对曰：“然。”曰：“为母夫人疾来召员某，非耶？”曰：“然。”曰：“仆即员某，请偕返署可也。”宫詹兄弟讶其预知，乐与偕返。

    Explanation

    199

    Mr. Yuan

    According to Feng Songhu, the Grand Secretary from Guangdong, he recounted that when he was young, he served his father, the Duke, in his travels to Shaanxi. At that time, his mother fell ill, and despite the use of a hundred medicines, her condition did not improve. Coincidentally, his father was serving as the magistrate of a certain county. Someone suggested, "There is a Mr. Yuan in the county who lives in the countryside and practices Daoism. He received secret teachings from a true immortal when he was young. He can foresee the past and future, is always skilled in the principles of medicine, and does not easily treat diseases for others. However, when he does, his treatments always yield miraculous results. If the young masters themselves go to invite him, he will graciously agree to come, and you can be assured without worry."

    His father then instructed Feng Songhu and his brothers to go the next day on foot to invite Mr. Yuan to their home. Just outside the city by about half a li, they saw a carriage approaching. The man in the carriage bowed and asked, "Are you the sons of Mr. Feng?" They replied, "Yes." He asked, "Are you here to summon Mr. Yuan for your mother's illness?" They answered, "Yes." He said, "I am Mr. Yuan. Please return with me to the residence."

    Feng Songhu and his brothers were astonished by his ability to know their purpose beforehand and were delighted to accompany him back.

    200

    比至署中，封公大喜，命导入寝室，见病者卧床上，寂然不动，员谛审良久，遽索瓷瓶一具，力掷于地，瓶碎如粉，拣取片瓷，以锋砭病者两太阳穴，少选，血珠珠出，其色紫；再砭之，紫色渐转为赤，便闻病者呻吟作声，竟体已能转侧矣。员笑曰：“得之矣！”亟索纸书方，服药调理，疾顿瘥。封公酬以金帛，固辞不受，谓：“感公子孝思，不召且至，况辱徒步枉顾，敢以小技责谢耶！”自是常来署中，宫詹兄弟咸喜从之游。尝谓：“真仙最喜游戏人间，俗眼不识，往往交臂失之。”曾偕游终南山，见一道人，清癯如鹤，修髯垂胸，有潇洒出尘之概，员拱手迎候道左，执礼甚恭。宫詹叩问何人，员低声附耳曰：“此汉张留侯也，那敢不敬！”既又见一白衣女道士，员执礼愈恭，谓为观云大士云。员羽化后，留有小像，有事祷之，辄验。

    Explanation

    200

    By the time he arrived at the office, Lord Feng was greatly pleased and ordered him to be led into the bedroom. There, he saw the patient lying motionless on the bed. Yuan carefully examined him for a long time, then suddenly asked for a porcelain bottle and hurled it to the ground with force. The bottle shattered into powder. He picked up a shard of the broken porcelain and used its sharp edge to pierce the patient’s two temples. After a short while, blood droplets emerged, their color purple; piercing again, the purple gradually turned to red. Then the patient began to groan, and his entire body was now able to turn over. Yuan smiled and said, “I have found it!” He quickly asked for paper to write down a prescription, and the patient took the medicine and soon recovered. Lord Feng offered him gold and silk, but Yuan firmly refused, saying, “I am moved by your young man’s filial piety—he had not been summoned yet, but he had already come. How much more so to have humbled himself by walking all the way to visit? How dare I expect gratitude for a small skill?” From then on, Yuan often came to the office, and the Gongjian brothers both delighted in accompanying him. He once said, “True immortals most enjoy playing among mortals, but ordinary eyes do not recognize them, and often pass them by.” He once traveled with them to Mount Zhongnan, where he saw a Daoist hermit, slender as a crane, with a long beard hanging to his chest, possessing a refined and otherworldly demeanor. Yuan bowed respectfully as he waited on the roadside, showing great courtesy. When Gongjian asked who he was, Yuan leaned close and whispered, “This is Zhang Liuhou of Han—how could I not show respect?” Soon after, they saw a white-robed female Daoist, and Yuan showed even greater respect, referring to her as the Venerable Guanyun.
