    The Scholar Who Became a Top Scorer

    Xiao, a scholar from Chaling, was obscure before achieving success. Once, on New Year's Eve, he dreamed of ascending the heavenly gate and entering an official residence. On a table was a scroll listing the names of scholars from all over the world. The first line of the first scroll listed "Xiao Jinzhong," and below it, it stated the top scorer of a certain examination. There were other characters folded at the corner that could not be seen, and a clerk revealed them to him, which were the three characters "died by fire." He was greatly startled and woke up. Xiao initially had another name, but because of the dream's omen, he changed his name to Jinzhong. Indeed, he became the top scorer in a certain examination. He always enjoyed drinking and got drunk easily. One day during winter, relatives invited him to drink, and he stayed overnight late at night, when it was extremely cold. He burned charcoal in the room, closed the door, hugged the stove, and sat alone, very tired, and fell asleep with his head drooping. His feet stepped on the charcoal, and he was drunk and unaware. Indeed, he died in a fire.

    324

    甓社湖蚌珠
    　　
    　　高邮甓社湖，大三十里。尝有物夜吐光，能照行人，朗若白昼。忽来一番僧，僦居湖干，镇日缘湖审视，如是者有年。一日，折柬遍招邻众，肆筵设席，酒殽备极丰腆。众问：“何求？曰：“求诸君翌晨助老僧一臂。”众莫测所以，姑漫应曰：“诺。如期毕至，僧出钲鼓数百具授众，使分立湖四隅，求为挝鼓敲钲，以助声威，毋少停止。自冠毗卢，着袈裟，仗剑跃入湖中。少选，狂风暴作，湖水奔腾澎湃，势如千军万马，众心惊魄骇，遵其所嘱，奋勇挝鼓，自晨至于日中昃，僧始踏浪而出。摇手喻众停止钲鼓，登岸，喘汗良久，满袈裟血渍淋漓，腥气刺鼻。众问：“何为？”曰：“此中有老蚌，自开辟以来胎养宝珠，光夺日月，老僧欲仗法力攘劫之，奈彼道行甚高，几为所吞；今右壳被宝剑斫伤，遁往东海，竟无法可制，再待千年，留为后图可也。稽首别众而去。

    Explanation

    324

    The Pitshe Lake Pearl

    The Pitshe Lake in Gaoyou is thirty li in size. Once, something would emit light at night, illuminating the path like daylight. Suddenly, a monk appeared and rented a house by the lakeside. He spent the whole day inspecting the lake for many years. One day, he wrote letters to invite all the neighbors and laid out a feast. The wine and dishes were extremely abundant and rich. The guests asked, "What do you seek?" The monk replied, "I ask for your help the next morning." The guests were puzzled but agreed, "Sure." As promised, they all arrived the next morning. The monk gave them hundreds of drums and gongs and instructed them to stand at the four corners of the lake to beat the drums and gongs to aid his efforts, without stopping. He put on his hat and robe, took a sword, and leaped into the lake. Soon, a fierce storm arose, and the lake water roared and surged like a thousand armies and ten thousand horses. The guests were terrified but followed his instructions and bravely beat the drums. From morning until noon, the monk finally emerged from the lake, shaking his hand to stop the drums and gongs. He climbed ashore, sweating profusely, with his robe stained with blood, the smell of which was pungent. The guests asked, "What happened?" The monk replied, "There is an old mussel in the lake that has been nurturing a pearl since the beginning of time. The pearl's light surpasses that of the sun and moon. I wanted to use my spiritual power to seize it, but it is too powerful and almost swallowed me. Now, its right shell has been injured by my sword, and it has fled to the East Sea, beyond my control. I will have to wait another thousand years to deal with it." He bowed respectfully and left the crowd.

    325

    里乘子曰：《统志》载：孙莘老家于湖阴，夜读书，觉窗明如昼，循湖求之，见大珠，其光烛天。是年，莘老及第，婿黄涪翁呈诗云：“甓社湖中有明月，淮南草木借光辉。”即谓此也。今湖中绝不再见吐光，岂蚌果遁往东海欤？或曰珠已为番僧所劫，其所云云，乃诡词以欺众也。

    Explanation

    325

    Li Chengzi said: According to the Comprehensive Records, Shen Lao lived in Huayin, and one night while reading, he noticed the window was as bright as day. Following the lake, he found a large pearl whose light illuminated the sky. That year, Shen Lao passed the imperial examination, and his son-in-law Huang Fuweng presented a poem saying, "In Pishi Lake, there is a bright moon; the grass and trees of Huainan borrow its light." This refers to that event. Now, the lake no longer sees the light being emitted. Could it be that the pearl has indeed fled to the East Sea? Some say the pearl has been stolen by foreign monks. What they say is merely deceptive words to deceive the masses.

    326

    怒睛鸡
    　　
    　　嵩山之阳，春日启蛰之后，居民尝夜见少室之巅红光两道，一长六七尺，一长四五尺，蜿蜒夭矫，若火龙然；鸡鸣遂隐，经秋即不得见，莫测其故。初，山下有农家蓄一雄鸡，气象赳赳，重可十斤，所种之卵无不者，主人宝之，呼曰“老雄”，十馀年不肯杀。岁又值乳鸡之时，忽数十卵仅一雄，其余尽毈，主人懊怨，以为不祥。一日，有番贾来，注视老雄与新雏，问主人肯市否。主人正虑老雄年久无用，姑漫应之曰：“客若肯出重价，那得不市？”客问：“此两雏索价几何？”曰：“五百足矣。”客喜曰：“诺。”主人初固索五百钱，见客遽喜诺，戏反齿绐之曰：“我所言固五百银，非钱也。

    Explanation

    326

    A Belligerent-Eyed Rooster

    On the southern slope of Mount Song, after the Awakening of Insects in spring, the local residents often saw two streaks of red light at the summit of Shaoshi Mountain. One streak was about six or seven feet long, the other about four or five feet long, twisting and coiling like a fire dragon. The light disappeared at dawn and was not seen again until autumn, its cause remaining a mystery.

    Initially, there was a farmer living at the foot of the mountain who kept a rooster. The rooster was imposing and dignified, weighing about ten pounds, and all the eggs it laid hatched successfully. The owner treasured the rooster and affectionately called it "Old Rooster." He had kept it for over ten years without slaughtering it. One year, during the season when chickens were typically hatched, out of dozens of eggs, only one male chick hatched, while the rest failed to hatch. The owner was disappointed and frustrated, believing the situation to be unlucky.

    One day, a foreign merchant arrived and closely examined the old rooster and the new chicks. He asked the owner if he was willing to sell them. The owner, considering that the old rooster was no longer useful due to its age, tentatively responded, "If the guest is willing to pay a high price, how could I refuse to sell?" The merchant then asked, "How much do you want for these two chicks?" The owner replied, "Five hundred units of currency." The merchant happily said, "Agreed." Initially, the owner had demanded five hundred silver units, not coins. Seeing the merchant's quick agreement, he playfully clarified, "I meant five hundred silver units, not coins."

    327

    ”客沉思久之，曰：“果尔，五百银亦所不吝，毋再翻悔！”主人大喜过望，答曰：“君如数将银来，誓不翻悔。”客喜。翌日，果携银五百来付主人，主人乃笼两鸡付之，笑拉客袂问曰：“我初固戏君耳，不谓果肯如数，敢问需此何为？”客笑曰：“君既见问，不敢不告。君不见少室之巅红光两道乎？”曰：“然。”曰：“此蜈蚣精也。一父一子，再百年后，少者长成，一方禽兽，蚕食无遗，且不免灾及小儿，实为大患，雷且难治。今少者尚稚，老者势孤，尚不敢公然肆虐，惟此两鸡足以制之。老雄固无足虑，惟新雏初，当饲以珍物，庶可速丰其毛羽，壮其筋力。矧闻数十卵仅得此雏，可知精气独钟，无怪其余尽毈也。计明年此时，新雏当亦可为老雄之助，制两妖不难矣。”曰：“此两鸡与他鸡何异？”曰：“凡鸡皆睫皮上掩，此则相反，名曰怒睛，是凤种也。

    Explanation

    327

    The guest pondered for a long time and said, "If so, I will not hesitate to give you five hundred silver pieces, and I will not change my mind again!" The host was overjoyed and replied, "If you bring the silver as agreed, I swear I won't change my mind." The guest was pleased. The next day, he indeed brought five hundred silver pieces to the host, and the host then handed over two chickens to him, smiling and pulling his sleeve, asking, "I initially just played with you, I didn't expect you to actually bring the money as agreed, so I dare ask what you need them for?" The guest smiled and said, "Since you have asked me, I dare not fail to tell you. Have you not seen the two rays of red light at the top of Shaoshi Mountain?" "Yes," the host replied. "These are centipede spirits. A father and a son, after another hundred years, when the younger one grows up, it will become a local monster, consuming everything like silkworms, and it will not be possible to avoid disasters affecting children. It is indeed a great calamity, even thunder is difficult to control. Now the younger one is still young, and the older one is isolated in power, so it still dares not openly wreak havoc. Only these two chickens are enough to subdue them. The old rooster is not a concern, but the new chick is just starting, and should be fed with precious things so as to quickly enrich its feathers and strengthen its muscles and strength. Especially since it is known that out of dozens of eggs, only this chick was hatched, it can be seen that the essence and energy were uniquely focused, it is no wonder that the rest all died. By this time next year, the new chick should also be able to assist the old male, and it will not be difficult to subdue the two monsters." "What is the difference between these two chickens and other chickens?" the host asked. "All chickens have their eyelids covering their eyes, but this is the opposite. It is called 'angry eyes' and is a phoenix breed."

    328

    ”别去岁星一周，客果携两鸡来访主人，其雏已长成，居然与老雄相等。客即下榻主人之家。他日，又见少室红光两道，客喜呼主人曰：“妖物又出矣！”越日薄暮，客携鸡独往，主人欲同往观之，客止之曰：“君不能胜妖气，中毒可虑。”客去，主人留心遥察。二更后，见少室之巅红光复灼，犹之掣电两股，以闪以烁，或东或西，或南或朔；或抑或扬，或分或合；或屈诎如环，或直伸如索；或回旋如鹰盘，或奋激如鱼跃；或少卷而骤舒，或将前而顿却；曤曤焉，爚爚焉，忽诧五尺孛芒疾驰斜掠，半明半灭，陡万丈而一落。主人色骇心喜，知小妖已告歼。尚有红光一道，忽高之忽低之，忽即之忽离之，气渐披靡，知其亦无能为，果不炊黍时，宛然败叶漾空，惨为狂飚之所摧，飘荡萧飒，站站然而下坠荒畦，红光悉绝。

    Explanation

    328

    After one orbit of Jupiter, the guest indeed brought two chickens to visit the host, and the chicks had grown up to be as large as adult roosters. The guest stayed overnight at the host's house. One day, he saw two red beams of light emanating from Mount Shaoshi and happily called out to the host, "The monster has appeared again!" The next day at dusk, the guest went alone with the chickens, and the host wanted to go with him to observe, but the guest stopped him, saying, "You cannot withstand the aura of the monster, and poisoning is a concern." After the guest left, the host kept a watchful eye from afar. Around 10 PM, he saw the red light reappear on the peak of Mount Shaoshi, like two streaks of lightning, flickering and darting in various directions—east, west, south, and north; rising and falling, splitting and merging; curling like a ring or stretching like a rope; swirling like an eagle circling or leaping like a fish. Sometimes it would curl slightly and then quickly uncurl, or advance and then suddenly retreat. It shone brightly and flickered, and suddenly a five-foot-long beam of light shot across at a diagonal, partially bright and partially dim, and then dropped sharply from a great height. The host was both surprised and delighted, knowing that the smaller monster had been defeated. There was still a single red light remaining, flickering up and down, coming and going, gradually weakening. Knowing it was no longer a threat, by the time rice was cooked, it resembled a withered leaf floating in the air, crushed by a fierce wind, fluttering and rustling, slowly descending into a field, and finally, the red light completely disappeared.

    329

    东方欲白，主人知两妖并除，姑烹茶以待客。俄焉，见客左手笼鸡，右手以树条贯拽两妖而至。主人迎而贺曰：“知大功告成，喜为君贺。”客叹曰：“两妖虽除，惜两鸡皆受重伤，奈何！”主人视小鸡竟体毛羽脱落殆尽，仅存一息；老雄亦毛羽褷，精神沮丧。又视其蜈蚣，大者长约六尺，左钳已脱，足尚有一二蠕蠕动者；小者长五尺许，双钳并去，足已夷其大半，僵如枯木矣。主人问：“此尚有用否？”曰：“红光外烛，珠当不少；即两躯壳以制刀剑鞘，亦值千金也。”乃以两鸡授主人，属善视之，且谓：“出力过甚，小鸡不过十日，老鸡不过半年皆当羽化；有功于人，尚其瘗之。其身受重毒，切不可食，慎之！慎之！”越日，客辞主人，又以二百金相谢，以木匣盛二妖，负之而去。后两鸡果如期先后俱斃，主人谨遵客所属，并瘗之。

    Explanation

    329

    The east was about to turn white, and the host knew that the two demons had been eliminated, so he decided to boil tea to wait for the guest. Soon, the guest arrived, holding a chicken in his left hand and dragging the two demons with a tree branch in his right hand. The host welcomed him and congratulated him, saying, "I am delighted to know that the great task is accomplished, and I congratulate you." The guest sighed and said, "Although the two demons are eliminated, it is regrettable that the two chickens are severely injured, what shall we do?" The host saw that the small chicken had almost completely lost its feathers and was barely alive, while the old rooster's feathers were disheveled and unkempt, and its spirit was depressed and discouraged. He then examined the scorpions: the larger one was about six feet long, with its left claw missing and only a couple of its feet still moving slightly; the smaller one was about five feet long, with both claws gone and most of its feet damaged, lying stiff like a piece of dead wood. The host asked, "Are these still useful?" The guest replied, "The red glow illuminates from the outside, there should be many pearls; even the shells can be used to make sword sheaths, which are also worth thousands of gold." He then gave the two chickens to the host, instructing him to take good care of them, and said, "They have exerted too much effort, the small chicken will not last more than ten days, and the old rooster will not last more than half a year before transforming into a spirit; for their contribution to people, they should be buried. Having suffered severe poison, they must not be eaten, be cautious! Be cautious!" The next day, the guest bade farewell to the host, thanked him with two hundred gold coins, and carried the two demons away in a wooden box. Later, the two chickens indeed died as expected, one after the other, and the host carefully followed the guest's instructions and buried them.

    330

    蛇妖
    　　
    　　广东驻防汉军刘协领溥，奇侅士也。生平正直不阿，尤以胆略自负。弱冠时落托无聊，偶游三元宫，有道士见而奇之，因与订方外交，款洽甚密。刘有缓急，必竭力相济，毫无吝色。刘不自安，时愧无以为报。一日访道士，见其频蹙默坐，似有忧色。刘叩之曰：“师何为者，而郁郁若此？脱有用小生处，万死不辞也。”道士闻之，瞿然起执刘手曰：“居士所言，是苍生之幸，非贫道一人之幸也！请上坐受贫道一拜，乃敢相求为助。”刘曰：“师休矣！固已言之，脱用小生，万死不辞，何琐琐效儿女态也！第言何事，容共图之。”道士谢曰：“若然，居士第随我来，倘有所见，有贫道在，勿惊勿恐。

    Explanation

    330

    The Snake Demon

    In Guangdong, there was a Han Chinese officer named Liu Xieling Pu, a man of extraordinary talent. Throughout his life, he was upright and unbending, and particularly proud of his courage and strategic insight. In his youth, he felt aimless and unfulfilled, and one day he visited the Sanyuan Temple. A Taoist saw him and was impressed by his qualities, and thus they formed a close friendship. Whenever Liu was in urgent need, the Taoist would always help him to the best of his ability, without any hesitation. Liu felt uneasy and often felt guilty for having nothing to repay the Taoist. One day, Liu visited the Taoist and saw him with furrowed brows, deep in thought, as if troubled. Liu asked, "Master, why do you look so troubled? If there is a use for me, I will not hesitate to die a thousand deaths!" The Taoist, hearing this, suddenly stood up and grasped Liu's hand, saying, "What you have said is fortunate for all living beings, not just for me alone! Please sit down, and I will pay my respects to you, and then I will ask for your help." Liu said, "Master, you need not do that! I have already said it, if there is a use for me, I will not hesitate to die a thousand deaths, why act so formally like a child? Just tell me what it is, and we will plan together." The Taoist thanked him and said, "If so, please follow me, and if you see anything, I will be here, do not be surprised or afraid."

    331

    ”刘问：“何如？”不答；固问，固不答，姑从之行。出北门五里许，去天房教先贤古墓数百步，道士乃结草为坛，立刘于其中，自顶至胸背及手足心，均为勅勒书符已；出一麦草笼，大尺许，而揭其盖授之，曰：“听吾引磬声响，急阖其盖，切记毋违。倘有所见，有贫道在，勿惊勿恐。”刘不知所以，姑漫应之，以觇其异。道士乃披发禹步，左手执剑，右手执引磬，口中喃喃诵咒。时二月中旬，月夜晴朗，天无纤云。三更时，腥风陡作，月色惨澹，忽闻声涌如潮，一蛇驰至，头大如箕，身巨如瓮，长十余丈，竟体金鳞，两目光灼如电，来伏坛下，稽首者三，道士以剑麾之曰：“去！”又一蛇至，长与前蛇相将，竟体明亮，洞见肠胃，伏坛稽首如前，道士亦以剑麾去之。

    Explanation

    331

    D asked, "How is it?" The Taoist priest did not reply. D insisted, but the priest still did not answer, so D reluctantly followed him. They walked about five miles from the north gate, then a few hundred steps from the ancient tomb of the sages of the Dajiao sect. The Taoist priest then tied grass to make an altar and stood D in the center of it. From his head to his back and the palms of his hands and feet, the priest inscribed magical talismans. He then took out a wheat-straw basket, about a foot in size, and lifted its lid to give it to D, saying, "Listen for the sound of my small bell. When you hear it, quickly close the lid. Remember not to disobey. If you see anything, I am here, so do not be afraid." D did not understand the purpose, so he casually agreed, hoping to witness something unusual. The Taoist priest then let his hair loose and performed the ritualistic step, holding a sword in his left hand and a small bell in his right, while murmuring incantations. It was mid-February, and the night was clear with a bright moon and no clouds in the sky. At the third watch, a foul wind suddenly arose, and the moonlight became dim. Suddenly, a sound like a surging tide was heard, and a large snake rushed toward them. Its head was as big as a winnowing basket, its body as large as a jar, and it was over ten zhang long. Its entire body was covered in golden scales, and its two eyes gleamed like lightning. The snake knelt three times at the altar, and the Taoist priest waved his sword at it, saying, "Go!" Another snake then appeared, of the same length as the first, but its body was bright, and its insides could be seen. It also knelt three times at the altar, and the Taoist priest waved his sword at it to drive it away as well.

    332

    以次，群蛇毕至，一蛇枭吻鹿角，竟体黄毛；一蛇龙首凤尾，前后四爪；一蛇头圆如毬，两胁有翼，伏地效蝶拍板；一蛇长不满丈，鳞甲斓，口吐五色气，纷纠若朝霞之彩；一蛇长数十丈，竟体红亮，熊熊若初日浴海，口吐火光，明可烛物。其余或青或黑，或黄或白，其色不一，其大小长短亦不一，咸次第伏坛稽首，不下百馀种。道士一一以剑麾去之，鸡三唱乃已。越日薄暮，道士又偕刘至坛，如前作法，三更，群蛇又络绎衔尾而至。有肖鼋鼍者，有肖蜥蜴者；有虿之双箝者，有蝦之蜷曲者；有虎豹首者，有牛马首者，有犬豕首者，有狐兔首者；有鹤顶者，有鸡冠者；有体方如印者，有身扁如带者；有无鳞如鱿鳗者，有多足如蝍蛆者，一切奇形怪状，又不下百馀种，鸡三唱乃已。第三日至坛，道士戒刘曰：“大功之成，端在今夕，居士须切记前语，不可疏忽。”刘谨受教。

    Explanation

    332

    In order, all the snakes arrived. One snake had an eagle-like snout and antler-like horns, its entire body covered in yellow hair; another had a dragon's head and a phoenix's tail, with four claws in front and behind; another had a round head like a ball, with wings on its sides, crouching on the ground and mimicking a butterfly's percussion board; another was less than a zhang in length, with a colorful scale pattern, spewing five-colored breath that intertwined like the hues of the morning sky; another was dozens of zhang long, its entire body bright red, blazing like the sun rising over the sea, spewing firelight that was bright enough to illuminate objects. The rest were either green, black, yellow, or white, each with a different color, and varying in size and length. They all knelt in sequence on the altar, paying homage, numbering no less than a hundred or more. The Taoist master dismissed them one by one with his sword, and the rooster crowed three times before they left. The next day at dusk, the Taoist master again accompanied Liu to the altar, performing the same rituals as before. At the third watch of the night, the snakes once more arrived in a continuous line, their tails trailing. Some resembled turtles and alligators, others lizards; some had double pincers like scorpions, others were curled like shrimp; some had tiger and leopard heads, others cow and horse heads, or dog and pig heads, or fox and rabbit heads; some had crane-like heads, others rooster-like combs; some had bodies shaped like seals, others flat like belts; some had no scales like cuttlefish and eels, others many feet like centipedes. All were of strange and unusual forms, numbering no less than a hundred or more, and the rooster crowed three times before they left. On the third day, they arrived at the altar, and the Taoist master warned Liu, saying, "The great work will be completed tonight, layman. You must remember my previous words and not neglect them." Liu respectfully accepted the instruction.

    333

    时至三更，群蛇又至，较前尤异，或肖夜叉，或肖罗汉；或肖猛士，或肖美人；或嘤嘤声如儿啼，或格格鸣若竹裂，所谓人首蛇身者计可数十种；他如首尾两头者，一颔两头者，一身三头、五头、九头者，道士皆一一以剑麾去。约近四更，忽沙石飞鸣，山谷响震，天色愁惨，星月顿暗，一蛇至坛，长仅丈许，鳞甲五色，首类雉卵，张口吐舌，两目突出，光烱如炬，怒视道士，屈身一跃，直扑其面。

    Explanation

    333

    When it was three o'clock at night, a group of snakes appeared again, more unusual than before. Some resembled demons, others resembled Buddhist arhats; some looked like fierce warriors, others like beautiful women. Some made soft crying sounds like a child's, while others made cracking noises like bamboo breaking. There were human-headed, snake-bodied creatures, numbering in the dozens. Others had two heads at the end of their bodies, two heads from the neck, or even three, five, or nine heads on one body. The Taoist priest drove them away one by one with his sword. By about four o'clock, suddenly sand and stones flew and the mountains and valleys echoed. The sky turned gloomy and dark, and the stars and moon suddenly dimmed. A large snake appeared at the altar, measuring about ten feet in length. Its scales were of five colors, its head resembled a chicken egg, and it opened its mouth and extended its tongue. Its eyes bulged out, glowing like torches, glaring angrily at the priest. It bent its body and leaped straight at the priest's face.

    334

    道士叱曰：“孽畜，焉敢无礼！”急以剑麾之曰：“止！”三扑三止，乃退伏坛下，稽首者三，身顿缩小不满五寸，一跃径至笼中。道士急击引磬，刘闻磬声，急阖笼盖，以付。道士袖出硃符，贴笼四隅，喜谓刘曰：“贫道奉师命捕收此妖，五年于兹，甫幸成功。不尔，东南滨海生灵，十年后受其屠毒，恐无噍类矣。”刘问作何处分，曰：“此去献俘于师。藉子一臂之助，必予厚赏，富贵寿考，后福无量，子其勉之。暂此作别，他日灵山会上，未必无再晤时也。”言毕，拂袖携笼而去。刘后官协领，夫妇齐眉寿逾九旬，目见五代。子孙世为膴仕，至今不绝。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：昔吾家旌阳敬明真君，歼孽龙而拔宅上升，说者谓其功德不在禹下。今道士捕收妖蛇，免使生灵屠毒，可见圣贤仙佛，同一去害安良之心也。嗟乎！仙岂易求哉！此事吾友汉军徐公可司马笔其大略。以告予者。盖刘为公可外祖李芳园协领同学契友，尝亲与李协领历历言之如此。

    Explanation

    334

    The Taoist priest scolded, "Evil beast, how dare you be disrespectful!" He quickly brandished his sword and commanded, "Stop!" The creature made three attempts to attack, but was stopped three times. It then retreated, bowed deeply three times, suddenly shrank to a size less than five inches, and leaped directly into the cage. The priest quickly struck a bell, and Liu, hearing the sound, swiftly closed the cage lid and handed it over. The priest took out a red talisman from his sleeve, attached it to the four corners of the cage, and said with joy to Liu, "I have captured this demon under my master's command after five years, and only now has the mission been successful. If not, the living beings along the southeastern coast would suffer from its slaughter and poison in ten years, and likely be extinct." Liu asked what would be done with it, and the priest replied, "This will be presented to the master. With your help, I will certainly give you a generous reward, wealth, nobility, long life, and good health, with infinite future blessings. You must strive for it. We part ways for now, but it is not impossible that we will meet again on the spiritual mountain someday." After finishing his speech, the priest waved his sleeve and left with the cage. Later, Liu became an official leader, lived a long and harmonious life with his wife, witnessed five generations, and his descendants continued to hold high positions until now.

    The author of the story said: In ancient times, my family's revered deity, the True Lord Jingyang, slew a dragon and ascended to heaven, and it is said that his virtue and merit were no less than those of Yu the Great. Now, the Taoist priest has captured this demon, preventing future harm to the living beings, showing that sages, saints, and deities share the same heart to eliminate evil and protect the good. Alas! How difficult it is to seek such a Taoist priest! This story was written by my friend General Xu Gongke, and it was told to me by Liu, who was a close friend of Li Fangyuan, the uncle of General Xu Gongke, and he once told me in detail about the events.

    335

    周孝廉妻
    　　
    　　成都周孝廉绍銮，年少倜傥。妻某氏，美而贤，琴瑟静好，秦徐不啻也。某氏体素弱，孕将分娩，殊形委顿。一日昼寝，忽见一村妇至榻前谓曰：“妾求代多年，今甫得娘子，此系帝命，莫怨妾也。”某氏讶其语不伦，力疾起坐问：“汝何人？所言何谓？我殊不解。”村妇笑曰：“娘子不知也耶？妾前村某氏妇，以产难死。今娘子不幸亦当罹此厄，妾奉帝命在此候代，娘子不知也耶？”某氏闻之大惊，婉语哀求，谓：“向无冤仇，乞赐怜宥。”村妇不肯。再三哀之，怒谓：“娘子休得饶舌！此何等事而可徇情也耶！”由此日坐榻前，刻不少离，他人不见，惟某氏独见之。孝廉知之，欲延僧忏解。村妇笑谓周妻曰：“汝家官人，大不怀好意，欲延僧驱我。我奉帝命，虽释迦如来，又将如我何哉！”某氏闻之，以告孝廉，相对愁闷，而病因之日益加剧。

    Explanation

    335

    The Wife of Zhou Shoulian

    In Chengdu, there was a scholar-official named Zhou Shoulian, who was young and unconventional. His wife, surnamed Mrs. Zhou, was beautiful and virtuous, and their relationship was harmonious and peaceful, no less so than that of Qin and Xu. Mrs. Zhou usually had a weak body and was about to give birth, which made her very much exhausted. One day, while she was taking a daytime nap, she suddenly saw a village woman come to her bed and say, "I have been seeking to replace for many years, and now I just got the lady. This is the decree of the emperor; do not blame me." Mrs. Zhou was surprised by the strange words and, with great effort, stood up and sat to ask, "Who are you? What do you mean? I do not understand at all." The village woman smiled and said, "Lady, do you not know? I am the wife of someone from a nearby village who died due to childbirth difficulties. Now, unfortunately, Lady, you too will face this misfortune. I am here following the decree of the emperor, waiting to take your place. Do you not know this?" Upon hearing this, Mrs. Zhou was greatly shocked and pleaded gently, saying, "There is no grudge between us; please bestow mercy and forgiveness." The village woman refused. Mrs. Zhou pleaded with her repeatedly, and the village woman angrily said, "Lady, do not talk nonsense! What kind of thing can be lenient about?" From then on, the village woman sat in front of the bed daily, not a moment away. Others did not see her, only Mrs. Zhou saw her alone. Zhou Shoulian learned about this and wanted to invite a monk for confession and absolution. The village woman smiled and told Mrs. Zhou, "Your husband has no good intentions; he wants to invite a monk to drive me away. I am following the decree of the emperor. Even if it is Sakyamuni Buddha, what can he do to me?" Upon hearing this, Mrs. Zhou told Zhou Shoulian, and they faced each other in grief and depression, and the illness worsened gradually because of it.

    336

    居亡何，又一女子搴帷入，年可二十许，靓妆高髻，风致翩婉，径至榻前，笑谓某氏曰：“夫人别来无恙？”某氏欠伸答曰：“姊姊何人？素昧平生，枉顾何为？乞赐明告。”女曰：“妾苏州惠氏，嫁夫周某，以产难死。今奉帝命，特来候代。”村妇闻之，瞪目让曰：“我奉帝命在先，汝不得横来妄争！”惠氏笑曰：“汝奉帝命，我独非奉帝命耶？请与汝约，汝代于昼，我代于夜，视产期以判，彼此无相争也。”村妇语塞。某氏闻之益忧。然相处日熟，知其是鬼，即亦不畏。

    Explanation

    336

    Not long after, another woman drew aside the curtain and entered. She was around twenty years old, with elegant makeup and a high hairstyle, her demeanor graceful and charming. She went directly to the bed and smiled at the woman, saying, "Madam, have you been well since we last met?" The woman yawned and replied, "Who are you, sister? I have never met you before. Why have you come? Please be clear with me." The woman said, "I am Hu from Suzhou, married to a man named Zhou, and I died due to childbirth difficulties. Now I have received an imperial order and have come to wait for a replacement." The village woman, upon hearing this, stared and reproached her, saying, "I received the imperial order first; you cannot come and unjustly challenge me!" Hu smiled and said, "You received the imperial order, and am I not also receiving the imperial order? Let me propose a compromise: you will replace her during the day, and I will replace her at night. We will decide based on the delivery date, and there will be no conflict between us." The village woman was speechless. The woman heard this and became even more worried. However, as they spent more time together, she realized that the woman was a ghost and no longer feared her.

    337

    久之，察惠氏意似不甚恶，瞷村妇不在侧，辄哀求救护，惠亦深为太息，意似首肯。邻生有扶鸾者，惠氏忽降坛书一绝云：“昨觞阿母赴瑶京，游遍昆仑第五城。戏掷硃砂填恨海，人间多少不平平。”款署：“妾吴门周惠氏也，慕诸君风雅，戏题小诗以博一噱。”邻生故与孝廉善，素耳孝廉家事，因讽之曰：“伏读佳什，足见婆心，岂畴昔周孝廉家坐守索命者，非子也耶？”乩书：“惭愧惭愧！天乎冤哉！妾本死于产难，甚恶此苦，不肯求代，且时阴以救人。上帝嘉妾一念之善，册为鬼仙，无所拘束。

    Explanation

    337

    After a long time, it became apparent that Lady Huishì's intentions seemed not very harsh. When she noticed that the village woman was not nearby, she would immediately plead for help and rescue. Lady Huishì also sighed deeply, seemingly nodding in agreement. A neighbor who practiced fúluán (a form of spiritual communication) suddenly had Lady Huishì descend to the altar and write a quatrain: "Yesterday, I bade farewell to my mother and traveled to the celestial Yáo Jīng, visiting the fifth city of Kūnlún. I playfully threw cinnabar to fill the sea of resentment, how many injustices exist in the world." She signed her name: "I am Lady Zhoú Huishì of Wú Mén. I admire the refined tastes of all of you, and I casually wrote this little poem to provoke a laugh." The neighbor, who was friendly with Scholar Zhoú, had always heard about his family's affairs, and thus advised him: "After bowing and reading your excellent poem, it is clear that you have a compassionate heart. Could it be that the one who once waited and demanded death in the house of Scholar Zhoú in the past, was not you?" The spirit wrote: "I am ashamed, I am ashamed! Heaven, how unjust! I originally died during childbirth, deeply disliking this suffering, I refused to ask for substitution, and sometimes secretly saved people. The supreme deity praised my single good thought, enrolling me as a guǐxiān (a ghost who has achieved a higher spiritual state), free from all constraints."

    338

    夙与周夫人有缘，知其将为村妇之代，欲救之而苦无法，君等何不谅予心也！”孝廉闻之，急来拜求，乩曰：“大难大难！倘夫人产于夜分，万一可救，然不可必也。子姑待之。”一日，某氏方倚枕与惠氏闲话，见门外一蓬首女鬼招村妇去，某氏问：“何事？”惠氏以目止之，会邻生又扶鸾，惠氏又降坛曰：“久欲救周夫人，今幸得一策，君可为孝廉贺。”生急招孝廉至，叩其所以。曰：“此事甚密，君须秘之。

    Explanation

    338

    From a long time ago, I have had a karmic connection with Lady Zhou, knowing that she will become a village woman. I wish to save her but am desperately unable to do so. Why can't you understand my heart?" Hearing this, the Xianglian hurriedly came to pay his respects and seek help. The spirit board replied, "Great difficulty! If Lady Zhou gives birth at midnight, there might be a chance to save her, but it is not certain. You should wait and see." One day, a certain person was leaning on a pillow and chatting with Huishi when they saw a ghost with unkempt hair summon a village woman. The certain person asked, "What is happening?" Huishi stopped it with a glance. The neighbor again supported the spirit board, and Huishi descended from the altar and said, "I have long wanted to save Lady Zhou, and now I have found a plan. You can congratulate the person with the title of Xianglian." The neighbor hurriedly called the Xianglian over and asked him the reason. Huishi said, "This matter is very secret; you must keep it confidential."

    339

    村妇之来，实奉帝命，今君对门卖浆者妇，蠢健有力，亦应以产难死，数应某妇求代，某妇力弱不敌，招村妇为助，始毕其命。君可为卖浆者具词控于城隍，谓村妇事不干己，横伤人命。冥法至公，恶其多事，必拘村妇不准求代。如此，则夫人无事矣。”孝廉大喜，使人侦于卖浆者之家，其妻日昳果以产难死。乃如惠氏言，秘为具词，控于城隍。越日，某氏见两鬼役持锒铛拘村妇去，自是不复再至。惠氏拊掌笑曰：“此大快事！夫人可安心，静候弄璋，保无他虑矣。”不数日，果举一子，一家欢庆。
    然某氏以体弱，产后血崩不止，势甚危殆，孝廉心甚忧之。某氏又见一青面鬼，紫髯如磔，口称要命，但蹀躞户外，不能进房。孝廉又央邻生扶鸾请惠氏降坛，求为营救。惠氏书云：“妾为夫人费煞心力，甫得安枕，不料又变生意外，此系宿孽，妾实不能为计。”孝廉再三哀求，久之，又书云：“怜君诚恳，心殊不忍。

    Explanation

    339

    A village woman came, truly fulfilling an imperial command. Now, the woman who sells drinks to your neighbor is strong and capable but should have died during childbirth. She was supposed to die in place of another woman, who was too weak to resist. The village woman was called upon to help complete the task. You can prepare a written complaint and take it to the city god, claiming that the village woman had no involvement and unjustly harmed someone's life. The laws of the afterlife are very fair; they dislike those who meddle too much and will surely detain the village woman, preventing her from seeking to die in place of others. In this way, the lady will be safe." The scholar-official was very pleased and sent someone to investigate the seller of drinks' household. Her wife indeed died during childbirth. Following Huì's advice, a secret written complaint was prepared and taken to the city god. The next day, the woman saw two ghost officers holding shackles and taking the village woman away. She never came back again. Huì clapped her hands and said, "This is a great joy! The lady can rest easy, quietly await the birth of a son, and be assured there will be no other worries." In a few days, she indeed gave birth to a son, and the whole family celebrated.

    However, the woman was weak and experienced uncontrollable bleeding after childbirth, which was very dangerous. The scholar-official was very worried. The woman also saw a ghost with a green face and a purple beard that looked like it had been torn, who claimed to want her life, but could only pace back and forth outside the door, unable to enter the room. The scholar-official again asked a neighbor to use divination to summon Huì to descend to the altar, seeking her help to save the woman. Huì wrote, "I have exhausted my strength and effort for the lady, just managing to get some rest, but unexpectedly, another unexpected event occurred. This is due to past karma; I truly cannot devise a plan." The scholar-official pleaded earnestly for a long time, and after a while, Huì wrote again, "I pity your sincerity and earnestness, and my heart is particularly unwilling."

    340

    顷思得一法：次月朔日，太白星君道过成都，君于是日平明，衣冠至西门敬候，见有白髯老者，将葫芦剖开，分挂两胁，两手拄杖，俟其入城，迎面祝求，听所言云何，再看缘法。君须切记，成败在此一举，断勿有忽！”孝廉拜谢。届日，遵往西门，晓关初放，行人如蚁，内果有一丐，白髯垂胸，如惠氏所云者，急迎面拜祝，老者不顾，孝廉追随约半里许，但闻老者低头自语：“放他去罢，放他去罢。”俄顷，市人益众，一转瞬间，忽失老者所在。孝廉回家，某氏喜谓曰：“顷闻户外鬼云：‘太白星君有命“放他去罢”，吾不敢在此矣。’踉跄而去。”孝廉大喜，妻病寻瘥。此亦公可所目睹，为予言者。

    Explanation

    340

    Recently, I thought of a method: On the first day of the next month, the Lord of the White Star will pass through Chengdu. On that day at dawn, dressed in formal attire, I waited respectfully at the western gate. I saw an old man with a white beard, carrying a gourd split open, hung on both sides, holding a walking stick with both hands. I waited for him to enter the city, approached and prayed face to face, listened to what he said, and then observed the karmic connection. You must remember this carefully; success or failure depends on this one action. Do not neglect this.

    On the appointed day, I followed to the western gate. At dawn when the gates were just opening, people were as numerous as ants. Indeed, there was a beggar with a white beard hanging down to his chest, as described by Master Hui. I rushed forward to greet and pray, but the old man ignored me. I followed him for about half a mile, but heard the old man speaking to himself while looking down: "Let him go. Let him go." In a short while, the crowd grew even larger. In an instant, I suddenly lost sight of the old man. I returned home, and someone said with joy: "Just now, I heard a ghost outside the door say, 'The Lord of the White Star has ordered, "Let him go," I dare not stay here.' He stumbled away." I was greatly pleased, and my wife's illness soon improved. This is also something that Master Gong has witnessed and told me.

    341

    里乘子曰：天下之最惨最苦者，莫过于产妇，况由此毕命，则惨苦尤有不忍言者。兹得太白星君低首自语，厉鬼即听命而去，诚为幸事。安得星君尝游戏人间，度一切苦厄哉！吾独怪李元《独异志》载太白星窃织女侍儿梁玉清、卫承庄淫奔事，未免污蔑上界星君，实甚荒渺不经，何足以信！

    Explanation

    341

    Master Li Cheng said: Among all the most miserable and suffering beings in the world, none are more so than those who give birth, and when this leads to their death, the suffering is even more unbearable to speak of. Here, the Star God of Venus lowered his head and spoke to himself, and the fierce ghosts immediately obeyed and departed, which is indeed a fortunate matter. How could the Star God ever play among the people and liberate all from suffering! I alone find it strange that Li Yuan's Unique Records recounts a story of the Star God of Venus secretly taking the maidservants of the Weaving Maid, Liang Yuqing and Wei Chengzhuang, and engaging in immoral behavior. This story不免污蔑上界星君，实甚荒渺不经，何足以信！

    342

    产鬼畏伞
    　　
    　　乡民毕酉，素有胆识。尝以妻有娠将产，月夜趁墟回家。道逢一女子，蹒跚独行，同路数里，略不闻其鼻息，心窃异之。试叩其氏族，当此午夜，独行何之？女子答曰：“妾非人，乃产鬼也。前村毕家妇分娩在即，特往讨替去。”酉大惊，默筹所以制之。佯笑答曰：“此大好事，汝得替投生好人家，可贺也。”曰：“此非所望，然得脱鬼趣，即为万幸。”因问酉姓名，乃诡对之，谈论甚洽。酉又问：“汝为鬼几何年矣？”曰：“于今十有三年矣。”曰：“求替何迟迟也？”曰：“阴曹必计平生善恶以判迟速，孽满方准求替，故迟迟以至于今也。”曰：“求替亦有术乎？”曰：“有。凡产鬼喉间各有红丝一缕，名曰‘血饵’。以此缒入产妇腹

    Explanation

    342

    A Ghost of Childbirth Fears an Umbrella

    A villager named Bi You, known for his courage and insight, once returned home under the moonlight after the market had closed because his wife was about to give birth. On the way, he encountered a woman who was walking slowly and alone. They traveled together for several miles, and he barely heard her breathing. He was secretly surprised by this. He tried to ask her about her family and why she was walking alone at midnight. The woman replied, "I am not human; I am a ghost of childbirth. The woman in the Bi family in the village is about to give birth, and I have come to seek a replacement." You was greatly alarmed and silently thought of a way to control her. He feigned a smile and said, "This is a great good fortune. You will be reborn in a good family, which is something to celebrate." She said, "This is not what I hope for, but if I can escape the realm of ghosts, it will be a great blessing." She then asked You his name, and he lied to her. They talked quite amicably. You then asked, "How many years have you been a ghost?" She replied, "It has been thirteen years since I became a ghost." "Why do you seek a replacement so slowly?" "The underworld must calculate one's lifetime of good and evil deeds to determine the speed of the replacement. Only when one's sins are complete can they seek a replacement, hence it has taken so long until now." "Is there a method to seek a replacement?" "Yes. All ghosts of childbirth have a red thread in their throats called 'Blood Bait.' Use this to lower it into the belly of the woman giving birth..."

    343

    中，系其婴胞，不使遽下，又暗中频频抽掣之，令其痛彻心髓，虽健妇，只三五抽掣，则命毕矣。”酉佯笑曰：“此术诚巧，未审有法制之否？”鬼但笑而不言。酉又固诘之，则曰：“制之亦自有法，但君切不可告人。”酉指天申誓，决不泄语。鬼悄谓曰：“产鬼最畏雨伞，以一伞置户后，即不敢入房矣。”酉曰：“然则更无别术乎？”曰：“君必勿泄，乃敢毕其词。”酉曰：“固申誓矣。

    Explanation

    343

    In the midst of the childbirth, the ghost tied the infant's umbilical cord, preventing it from dropping immediately, and then repeatedly pulled it in the darkness, causing the mother to suffer excruciating pain that penetrated to her very marrow. Even a strong woman would lose her life after only three or five such pulls. "酉" feigned a smile and said, "This technique is indeed ingenious, but I wonder if there is a way to control it?" The ghost merely laughed without speaking. "酉" persisted in questioning, and the ghost replied, "There is a way to control it, but you must not reveal it to anyone." "酉" pointed to the sky and swore solemnly, vowing never to disclose the secret. The ghost whispered, "The birthing ghost is most afraid of umbrellas. If you place an umbrella behind the door, it will not dare to enter the room." "酉" asked, "Then is there no other method?" The ghost said, "You must not reveal it, and I will finish my words." "酉" replied, "I have already taken the oath."

    344

    倘泄语，即与汝等！”鬼喜其诚，又曰：“如不能入房，则伏屋上，以血饵缒入产妇口中亦可，倘于床顶再张一伞，使血饵不能下缒，则鬼术穷矣。以君长者，故敢质告，倘泄语，则我无生望矣，愿君谅之。”酉曰：“诺。”既至家，妻正以难产，势甚危急。酉如鬼言，急以一伞置户后，又张一伞于床顶，不逾时，果呱呱堕地，而妻得无患。少选，闻空中呼酉名而詈之曰：“促狭儿，我不幸为汝所绐，又要迟此一次！汝如再告他人，致我永无生望，则天良丧尽矣！”叹息恨恨而去。酉闻而匿笑，为妻细述之。妻甚恶此苦，嘱遍告于人。凡有娠之家，各如法预防之，果皆无恙。

    Explanation

    344

    If you reveal this, it will be against you!" The ghost was pleased with his sincerity and said, "If you cannot enter the room, then lie on the roof and lower the blood bait into the mouth of the pregnant woman. If you place an umbrella on the ceiling, preventing the blood bait from reaching her, then my power is exhausted. Since you are a noble person, I dare to tell you this truth. If you reveal it, I will have no hope of life, so please understand me." The man replied, "Agreed." Upon arriving home, his wife was in labor, and the situation was very critical. Following the ghost's advice, he quickly placed an umbrella behind the door and another on the ceiling. Soon after, the baby was born with a cry, and his wife was relieved. Shortly afterward, they heard the ghost calling out the man's name and scolding him, "Narrow-minded child, I was unfortunately deceived by you, and I had to wait for this once again! If you reveal this to others, causing me to lose all hope of life, then your conscience is completely lost!" The ghost sighed angrily and left. The man heard this and secretly smiled, then told his wife in detail. His wife was very displeased with this ordeal and instructed him to tell everyone. All families expecting a child followed the method to prevent such incidents, and indeed, no harm came to them.

    345

    里乘子曰：乡民亦大诙诡，然非素有胆识，亦不能玩弄鬼物而酬对裕如也。嗟乎！此法若能遍传，则纣绝阴天之宫，消除无限怨气矣！不亦快哉！或谓鬼物甚灵，一时竟为乡民所绐，傥亦上天好生之心，默有以使之欤？
    　　
    　　制军某公
    　　
    　　制军某公，少孤贫，事母至孝。遭遇坎壈，年逾弱冠，尚不能博一衿。戚党以其衣被须捷，见辄避道。公大度豁达，殊不介意。众谓其委靡无耻，益姗诮之。尝以应童子试被黜归，道经古刹，遇一道士，紫髯掀鼻，貌甚怪陋，藉地趺坐。见公至，遽起为礼，笑谓：“贫道待贵人久矣。”公闻而愕眙，仓卒不知所对。道士又曰：“贫道赦凡，姓万，道号蜕云，由峨眉浪游至此，阅人甚多，富贵福泽，无如公者。以夙有香火之缘，故此相待。”公疑其戏己，笑谓：“小生凡庸，幸邀仙盼，如师所言，岂精姑布术乎？”曰：“子卿则吾岂敢，窃信平日甄识人物、鉴别贤否，颇无差谬。

    Explanation

    345

    Li Chengzi said: The villagers are indeed very clever and resourceful, but without a certain boldness and intelligence, they would not be able to handle spirits so easily and confidently. Alas! If this method could be widely spread, it would purify the underworld, eliminating endless grievances! Is that not a relief! Some say that the spirits are very powerful, and for a while, they were tricked by the villagers. Perhaps Heaven has a benevolent will, silently guiding them to do so?

    The Military Governor of a Certain Region

    The Military Governor of a Certain Region, when young, was an orphan and poor, but he served his mother with great filial piety. He encountered many misfortunes, and by the time he was over twenty, he still had not achieved scholarly success. His relatives and close associates criticized him for not showing immediate results, and people avoided him when they saw him. However, he was broad-minded and open-hearted, not taking their criticism to heart. People said he was weak and shameless, mocking him even more. Once, he participated in the imperial examination for young scholars but was dismissed and returned home. On his way back, he passed by an ancient temple and met a Taoist priest with a purple beard and a large nose, who looked strange and unattractive. The priest was sitting cross-legged on the ground. When he saw the official arrive, he quickly stood up to pay respects and said with a smile, "I have been waiting for a noble person for a long time." The official was startled and stared in surprise, at a loss for words. The priest then said, "I am called Shèfán, my surname is Wàn, and my Taoist name is Tuìyún. I came from Mount Emei and wandered here, having seen many people. There is no one like you in terms of wealth and blessings. Because we have a long-standing connection through religious practices, I am waiting for you." The official doubted that the priest was joking and laughed, saying, "I am just an ordinary and mediocre person, fortunate to be noticed by immortals. As you say, do you specialize in the art of fortune-telling?" The priest replied, "How dare I compare myself to Zi Qīng? I merely believe that in my daily life, I have a good sense of distinguishing people and identifying their virtues and flaws, and I am generally accurate."

    346

    ”公曰：“然则师相小生究竟何如？”道士掐指轮算，谓：“公某年入运，应入学；某年联捷登科第，入词馆；某年视学蜀中，某年擢侍御，出膺监司，陈臬开藩，荐领封圻，官至协揆，寿享期颐，夫妇齐眉，子孙贵显。”一一按年细推，令公牢记，以验后效。公谓：“小生一寒至此，老母菽水尚缺，行将弃儒而贾，复何奢望？”道士急摇手曰：“切勿！切勿！公如忧不给，贫道薄有所积，愿助膏火。

    Explanation

    346

    The official said, "Then, what will be the future of the young scholar under the guidance of the teacher?" The Taoist priest calculated by pinching his fingers and said, "In the year when the official enters his favorable period, he should attend school; in another year, he will pass the imperial examinations with distinction and enter the Hanlin Academy; in yet another year, he will inspect schools in Shu; in the following year, he will be promoted to a censor and appointed as an inspector; later, he will be appointed as a governor or military commander and govern a frontier region, eventually reaching the position of chancellor. He will enjoy a long life, live to a hundred years, have a harmonious relationship with his spouse, and have children who achieve high status." He detailed the events year by year, instructing the official to remember them well, so that he could verify the predictions in the future. The official said, "I have fallen into poverty, and my elderly mother still lacks the basic necessities. I am about to abandon my studies and become a merchant. What further hopes can I have?" The Taoist priest quickly shook his hand and said, "Do not! Do not! If the official is worried about not having enough, I have a small amount saved, and I am willing to assist with the expenses for education."

    347

    ”袖出黄金一流归之，且谓：“愿公富贵，长毋相忘，幸甚！”公以无处而馈，圣贤所戒，却不肯受。道士见公固辞，谓：“如不肯受馈，请暂称贷，他日相还亦无不可。”公曰：“如斯较可，奈无券何？”道士曰：“君子券以口，小人券以手。君子不券不负，小人虽券亦负。负不负在人，券何与焉！”公乃拜受，并问后会，且谓：“信如师言，他日宦海风波，得毋涉险否？”道士沉吟久之，曰：“公后入宦途，一无盘错，惟某年日月时，当有意外之虞。至期，贫道自来相探，可勿虑也。”言讫，珍重丁宁而别。公归，疑信参半，秘志于牍，以俟后验。将金易布粟，差免冻馁。

    Explanation

    347

    The Taoist priest pulled out a stream of gold from his sleeve and returned it, saying, "May you achieve wealth and honor, never forget each other, and I am very fortunate!" The man, considering it inappropriate to accept a gift without a proper reason, refused to take it. The Taoist priest, seeing that the man firmly declined, said, "If you do not accept the gift, please borrow it temporarily, and it can be returned later if necessary." The man replied, "That would be acceptable, but how can we ensure the loan is repaid?" The Taoist priest said, "Gentlemen use words as their bond, while petty people use hands as their bond. Whether one breaks a promise depends on the person, not on the contract. The contract is irrelevant in this case!" The man then bowed and accepted the gold, asking about their future meeting and saying, "If you are as wise as you say, will I not face dangers in the turbulent waves of officialdom?" The Taoist priest pondered for a long time and said, "After you enter the ranks of officials, you will face no obstacles, except for a certain year, month, day, and hour when you will encounter unexpected danger. At that time, I will come to visit you, so do not worry." With these words, he parted with great care and repeated instructions. The man returned home, half believing and half doubting, secretly recorded the event in a document to be verified later. He exchanged the gold for cloth and grain, barely avoiding freezing and starving.

    348

    益下帷攻苦，果于某年入学，某年联捷登科第，由词馆而荐领封圻，扬历中外，一一如道士言。既督两粤，初娶夫人，生丈夫子二。长公子十九岁已入词馆，次公子甫成童，赐孝廉，与试春闱。时太夫人尚健，板舆迎养，一家团聚，备极荣显。一日，阍者来报：有道士万赦凡号蜕云者踵门求见。公大喜，亟命开门迎入，执手道故，各叙调饥。公谓：“师言谨志于牍，无不应验；不知所谓意外之虞，究竟何若？”道士曰：“前事既验，后事可知。所可虞者，近在翌日。贫道此来，正为公也。”公稽所志牍，果然。急拜叩所以。道士曰：“公前世学道青城，道行甚高，以矢志放生，误践毙一蛙。翌日午时，合遭雷谴，以了宿孽。贫道旧与公同学，不敢不预以相报。”公闻大骇，急求救免。道士笑曰：“贫道思之烂熟，公请宽心。”乃附耳授以解免之策。

    Explanation

    348

    The official immersed himself in diligent study and indeed entered school in a certain year, passing the imperial examinations the following year. He rose from serving in the Literary Academy to being recommended for high office in the administration, serving both inside and outside the capital, all as the Taoist priest had predicted. After being appointed governor of the two Guang provinces, he first married a wife and had two sons. The eldest son, at nineteen, had already entered the Literary Academy, while the younger son, just a child, was granted the title of xiao lian (a scholar-official) and participated in the spring metropolitan examination. At that time, his grandmother was still healthy, and she was welcomed back in a sedan chair for care. The family was united, and they enjoyed the utmost honor and prestige.

    One day, the gatekeeper reported that a Taoist priest named Wan Shefa, also known as Tui Yun, had come to request an audience. The official was very pleased, immediately ordered the door to be opened to welcome him in, shook hands, and exchanged greetings, each recounting their recent experiences. The official said, "Your words were carefully recorded in the document, and none of them failed to come true; I wonder what the unexpected danger you mentioned actually is?" The Taoist priest replied, "Since the previous events have been verified, the future can be known. The thing to be wary of is very close, happening the next day. I came here specifically for you."

    The official checked the record and found it to be true. He hastily bowed and asked for the reason. The Taoist priest said, "In your past life, you studied Taoism in Qingcheng Mountain, and your spiritual practice was very high. However, you accidentally stepped on and killed a frog while vowing to release living creatures. On the next day at noon, you should be struck by lightning to resolve your past karma. I was your fellow student, and I dare not fail to warn you in advance." The official was greatly alarmed and urgently sought a way to avoid the fate. The Taoist priest smiled and said, "I have thought about it thoroughly, please be at ease." He then whispered a method to avoid the calamity in the official's ear.

    349

    公如言，晨兴具冠服，捧印绶坐堂皇，又遍召文武属吏，各冠服佩印，按班环坐。时当仲秋，天气晴爽，蔚蓝一抹，不留纤翳。日晡，黑云骤合，电蛇怒掣，烈风悲啸，暴雨奔注，雷震虩虩，声烧堂皇，屋瓦飞鸣，四壁摆动。公正襟危坐，色若死灰，属吏莫知所事，环视惴惴，局促各不自安。方皇遽间，忽一婢喘汗驰至，口称：“殆、殆矣！雷、雷击太夫人，奈何！”公本至孝，闻之大恸失声，急携印起，随婢奔入省视；属吏见公出，各追从其后。公至寝门，见太夫人方与诸姬妾作叶子戏，嘻嘻无恙。公殊诧异，遽闻霹雳一声，轰若山裂。奴子来曰：“堂皇被雷击塌，公顷所坐地坎深丈许，一蝎大于人，僵毙其中。”公大惊，使人觅道士，不知所在。始悟道士固蝎妖，欲藉公以避雷劫也。观其数十年前即已绸缪未雨，其心甚苦，其术亦甚神矣！而丰隆威灵，应变有法，彼妖物虽狡狯，究何能逃天诛哉！

    Explanation

    349

    The official, as he said, rose early and put on his ceremonial robes and hat. He held his seal and ribbon and sat on the main hall. He then summoned all the civil and military officials, each dressed in their official robes and wearing their seals, and they sat in their respective positions in a circle. It was mid-autumn, and the weather was clear and pleasant, with a clear blue sky without a cloud. By noon, dark clouds suddenly gathered, and lightning flashed like angry snakes, while the strong wind howled mournfully. Heavy rain poured down, and thunder roared, shaking the main hall, the roof tiles flew and rang, and the walls swayed. The official sat upright, his face pale as ash, and the officials were at a loss, looking around nervously, and each felt uneasy. Just as they were in a state of panic, a servant girl came running, panting and sweating, and said, "Danger! Danger! Lightning has struck Lady Grandmother, what shall we do!" The official, who was extremely filial, was deeply moved and cried out, quickly took his seal, and followed the servant into the room to see. The officials saw the official leave and followed him. When the official reached the bedroom door, he saw Lady Grandmother playing a card game with her maids, laughing and seemingly unhurt. The official was very surprised, and suddenly heard a loud thunderclap, as if a mountain had split. A servant came and said, "The main hall was struck by lightning and collapsed. The ground where you were sitting has a hole about a zhang deep, and a scorpion larger than a person lies dead in it." The official was greatly shocked and sent someone to find the Taoist, but he was nowhere to be found. He then realized that the Taoist was actually a scorpion demon who wanted to use the official to avoid the lightning disaster. Observing that the scorpion demon had been preparing for this event for decades, it was clear that his intentions were deeply troubling and his methods were extremely cunning! However, the power of the thunder god, responding to changes with law, was such that even though the demon was cunning, it could not escape divine retribution!

    350

    蚁阵
    　　
    　　曩在毗陵市上观蚁阵，见一人藉地坐，左右置黑赤髹漆方盒各一具，盒各三层，宽各盈尺，旁各有小孔出入，如蜂房然。观者次第敛钱毕，其人将黑赤盒各取首一层列地上，彼此相距约二尺许；又取黑赤小旗各三竿，插左右，相距尺有咫；又出小竹筒，长五寸许，吹之呜呜然作簸逻声；又击细腰鼓三通，但见每盒所蓄蚁络绎自孔出，约有千馀，各投所插色旗下。中旗蚁帅，约长一米；左右两副将，长稍杀；馀俱细小与凡蚁等。赤左黑右列阵毕，其人又击鼓数声，各帅乃督蚁兵争前交绥，前后进退，与鼓声缓急抑扬相应。

    Explanation

    350

    In the past, I watched an ant formation in Changzhou city. I saw a person sitting on the ground, with lacquered black and red square boxes placed on both sides, each box having three layers, about a foot wide, with small holes for entry and exit on the sides, resembling beehives. The spectators collected money in order. The person then took the top layers of the black and red boxes and arranged them on the ground, with a distance of about two feet between them. They then took three black and red small flags each and inserted them on both sides, with a distance of about a foot between them. They also took out a small bamboo tube, about five inches long, and blew it to produce a humming and rustling sound. They then struck the narrow-waisted drum three times, and the ants in each box emerged continuously from the holes, about a thousand in number, each going to the corresponding color flag. The ant commander under the central flag was about one meter long; the two deputy commanders on the left and right were slightly shorter; the rest were small and equal to ordinary ants. The formation was completed with red on the left and black on the right. The person then struck the drum several times, and the commanders supervised the ant soldiers to rush forward to engage, advancing and retreating, in response to the varying tempo and rhythm of the drum beats.

    351

    两军相持良久，忽见赤队蚁帅率众便退，左右两副亦率众分路俱退，黑队蚁帅便统左右两副率众前追，约逾赤旗界才二寸许，忽见赤帅又统众转战，两副亦从两翼截出，三面合攻，黑队陷入重围。帅知中计，情甚窘迫，同两副竭力冲突，竟不得出，亡者甚众，势穷援绝。鼓声顿死，其人急出小铜钲连敲数杵，忽见赤蚁让路一线，黑帅急同两副率败残馀兵仓皇夺路而出。又鸣钲数声，两帅各统众次第由孔入盒。是役也，黑蚁阵亡共计三百三十有五头，赤蚁亡者仅二十有七头。其人手拈蔑丝，定目视地，拨数战骨，太息谓众曰：“顷者倘不急鸣金收队，则黑蚁全军覆没矣！”便将赤盒盖揭开，请众观献俘。

    Explanation

    351

    The two armies faced each other for a long time. Suddenly, the commander of the red ant army led his troops to retreat immediately, and the two deputy commanders also led their troops to retreat in separate directions. The commander of the black ant army then led the two deputy commanders to pursue the retreating red ants. They crossed the boundary of the red flag by about two inches, when suddenly the red ant commander led his troops to turn and fight again, and the two deputy commanders also cut out from the two wings, attacking from three sides, and the black ant army became surrounded. The commander realized that he was outmaneuvered, and the situation was very dire. He fought desperately with the two deputy commanders to break through, but they could not escape, and many died. The situation was desperate and there was no help. The drumming suddenly stopped, and the person quickly took out a small bronze gong and struck it several times. Suddenly, the red ants made a narrow path, and the black ant commander, along with the two deputy commanders, rushed out in panic with the remaining troops. They sounded the gong several times, and the two commanders led their troops in order to enter the box through the holes. In this battle, the black ant army had a total of 335 casualties, while the red ant army had only 27 casualties. The person pulled out a piece of straw, fixed his gaze on the ground, picked up a few bones, and sighed, saying to the people, "If we had not quickly sounded the retreat signal, then the entire black ant army would have been wiped out!" He then lifted the lid of the red box and asked the people to see the captured ants.

    352

    但见盒中罫界纵横，凡二十有五方，中一罫又划分前后各半，前为蚁王听政之所，后为妃嫔所居，似箬叶为庐，蔽不可见。但见蚁王巨若赤豆，气象英杰，端拱殿中。帅同两副匍匐诣王前，交头隐隐不知作何语，蚁王首肯，似甚嘉悦。须曳，帅同两副退下，群蚁迎上，交头隐隐，亦不知云何。但见群蚁争前献俘，将所噬黑蚁之首若足，各衔置殿前，筑若京观。王顾左右，似有所论，两蚁似是近侍，下殿不知传何旨。蚁帅又独上殿诣王前，伏听谕旨毕，缓缓退下；两副同群蚁迎上，又交头隐隐不知云何，似俱欣欣有喜色。群蚁便趋至殿前，将所筑京观各衔出摈弃盒外，始各归列。

    Explanation

    352

    Only see the grid in the box, a total of twenty-five squares, with the central square divided into front and back halves. The front part is where the ant king listens to politics, and the back part is where the queen and concubines reside. It resembles a bamboo leaf shelter, hidden from view. Only see the ant king, large as a red bean, with an impressive and heroic appearance, sitting upright in the hall. The ant general and two deputies crawl to the ant king's presence, speaking in low voices, and it's unclear what they are saying. The ant king nods in agreement, appearing very pleased. After a while, the ant general and two deputies leave, and the ants gather, speaking in low voices, and it's unclear what they are discussing. Only see the ants rushing forward to present the captured black ants, bringing the heads and feet of the black ants they have killed and placing them in front of the hall, forming a pile like a mound. The ant king looks around, as if discussing something. Two ants, likely courtiers, leave the hall without knowing what command they are conveying. The ant general returns to the hall, kneeling to listen to the ant king's instructions, and then slowly leaves. The two deputies and the ants gather again, speaking in low voices, and appear very happy. The ants move to the front of the hall, taking the pile of heads and feet out of the box and discarding them, then returning to their places.

    353

    其人便将赤盒盖好。观者欲散，其人曰：“未也，有赏即有罚，既观献俘，不可不观治罪。”便又将黑盒盖揭开，请众观治罪。但见盒中方罫亦与赤者等，其王亦大如豆，端拱殿中，似甚震怒；正副三帅伏殿前不少动，似甚惶恐。少选，王顾左右，似有所谕，但见王旁群蚁类近侍者二十馀头，分半下殿，将三帅后股约嘬有数十许，三帅崩角乞命，但见王仰首不知作何语，始罢嘬。三帅稽首谢罪，缓缓退下，王亦退朝，群臣亦各归列。其人又将黑盒盖好，观者咸叹息称奇而散。予询其人曰：“蚁乃极微之物，君以何术驯致若此？”其人曰：“蚁虽微物，其性极灵。尽其性而导之，不半月便可演熟。

    Explanation

    353

    The person then covered the red box. The spectators wanted to disperse, but the person said, "Not yet, there is reward, and there is punishment. Having seen the presentation of captives, one cannot miss the punishment." The person then opened the black box and invited everyone to witness the punishment. Inside the black box, the grid was similar to that of the red box. The ant king, also large like a bean, stood upright in the hall, seeming very angry. The three commanders, with a main commander and two deputies, crouched before the hall without moving, appearing very fearful. A short while later, the ant king looked around, seeming to give some instructions. About twenty ants resembling attendants gathered around the ant king, half of them descended from the hall and bit the back of the three commanders several times. The commanders bowed down and begged for mercy, while the ant king looked up without knowing what to say, then stopped biting. The commanders bowed deeply and apologized, slowly withdrawing, while the ant king and officials returned to their places. The person then covered the black box again, and the spectators expressed amazement before dispersing. I asked the person, "Ants are extremely small creatures; what technique do you use to tame them like this?" The person replied, "Ants, though small, have extremely intelligent natures. By fully understanding their nature and guiding them, within half a month they can be trained to perform well."

    354

    ”予曰：“各插小旗三竿何也？”曰：“使之辨识各归各队也。”“吹竹筒何也？”曰：“使闻声之多寡，知派几许队伍也。”“击鼓三通何也？”曰：“促其出盒列阵也。再击鼓以作其气焉，兵法所谓‘鸣鼓进攻’也。既战酣而停鼓敲钲，亦犹鸣金收兵之义也。”予曰：“君言是矣。顾每阵无论孰胜孰负，必有所伤，如今日黑部阵亡不少，日日如此，不将澌灭无噍类乎？”曰：“每阵亡之多者休息五日，少者三日，故将列阵，必先敛钱，计得小人五日粮，乃可博一战。

    Explanation

    354

    I said, "What is the purpose of each person carrying three small flags?" He replied, "To make them distinguish and return to their respective teams." I asked, "What is the purpose of blowing bamboo tubes?" He said, "To make them know the number of teams by the number of sounds heard." I inquired, "What is the purpose of striking the drum three times?" He explained, "To urge them to come out of the box and form an array. Striking the drum again boosts their morale, as in the military tactic 'sounding the drum to attack.' After the battle is in full swing, stopping the drum and sounding the gong is also like the signal 'sounding the metal to withdraw the troops.'" I said, "Your words are correct. But in every battle, regardless of who wins or loses, there will always be casualties. Like today, the Black Team has lost many members. If this continues every day, won't they be completely wiped out?" He responded, "Those who lose many in each battle rest for five days, and those who lose fewer rest for three days. Therefore, before forming an array, they must first collect money, calculate enough for five days' rations for the soldiers, before engaging in a battle."

    355

    ”曰：“必须五日何也？”曰：“两部皆日月将雏，雏出，一日乳之，二日哺之，三日便知自食，四日便成立，五日便列队，故必须以五日也。”曰：“每部所蓄蚁几何也？”曰：“每罫仅容百头，除中罫王宫，计之当各二千四百有奇也。”曰：“今日所出队伍几何也？”曰：“吹筒六声，系派六成队伍，计每部应出一千四百有馀也。”曰：“每盒三层何也？”曰：“上层为众蚁所居，中层将雏，下层储胥。”曰：“盒中方罫二十有五，何也？”曰：“中罫为王宫；环王宫之八罫，将相、百官、各执事居之；缘边十六罫，庶民居之。”曰：“王宫后半以箬叶为庐蔽之，何也？”曰：“是妃嫔所居，不使人见，所以别内外、明尊卑也。蚁虽微物，固礼、义、廉、耻四维毕张也。

    Explanation

    355

    "Asked, 'Why is it necessary for five days?' It was replied, 'Both groups are involved in raising young every day and month. The young come out, and on the first day they are nursed, on the second day they are fed solid food, on the third day they begin to know how to feed themselves, on the fourth day they become mature, and on the fifth day they form a line. Therefore, it is necessary for five days.' Asked, 'How many ants does each group keep?' It was replied, 'Each grid can only hold a hundred ants, except for the central grid which is the king's palace. The calculation should be two thousand four hundred and more.' Asked, 'How many formations come out today?' It was replied, 'Blowing the tube six times, six formations are formed. The calculation is one thousand four hundred and more for each group.' Asked, 'Why are there three layers in each box?' It was replied, 'The top layer is where the ants live, the middle layer is for raising young, and the bottom layer is for storing supplies.' Asked, 'There are twenty-five grids inside the box, why is that?' It was replied, 'The central grid is the king's palace; the eight grids surrounding the king's palace are where the generals, ministers, officials, and various attendants live; the sixteen grids along the edge are where the common people live.' Asked, 'The back half of the king's palace is covered with bamboo leaves, why is that?' It was replied, 'This is where the consorts live, not to be seen by others, so as to distinguish between inside and outside and to make clear the hierarchy. Ants, though small creatures, fully uphold the four pillars of propriety, righteousness, integrity, and shame.'

    356

    ”予肃然向曰：“何谓也？”曰：“王宫居中，各官早晚两朝，时无少愆；每罫男左女右，序无少紊。凡所供亿，皆取给于庶民，无敢或怨，是其礼也；有功必赏，有过必罚，号令严明，毫无宽贷，一遇急难，互相护援，是其义也；凡出觅食，一有所得，归鸣于众，同往舁运，上之于官，官又上之于王，命储库中以济公用，不敢少有所私，倘王赏有功，即私存志之，众亦不敢攘夺，是其廉也；凡蚁交皆有时，不令人见，其性最警，人欲窃窥，即便分散，是真知耻也。”又示以盒之中层，亦分二十有五罫，中罫为妃嫔将雏之地，外八罫为命妇将雏之地，再外十有六罫，为民妇将雏之地。谛视所生之子，累累然与虱虮相类。

    Explanation

    356

    I respectfully responded, "What do you mean?" He said, "The king's palace is centrally located, and officials attend morning and evening court sessions, with no slight delay. Each grid has men on the left and women on the right, with no slight disorder. All supplies are taken from the common people, and no one dares to complain, which is their ritual; merit is rewarded, and faults are punished, with commands that are clear and strict, with no leniency. When faced with urgent difficulties, they protect and support each other, which is their righteousness. When they go out to find food, if they find anything, they call out to the group upon returning, and they all go together to carry it to the officials, who then take it to the king. It is ordered to be stored in the treasury for public use, and no one dares to keep even a little for themselves. If the king rewards someone for their merit, they will privately remember it, and the group also does not dare to seize it, which is their integrity. All ant interactions occur at specific times and are not seen by humans, and their nature is most alert. If humans try to secretly observe, they immediately disperse, which is truly knowing shame." He then showed the middle layer of the box, which is also divided into twenty-five grids. The central grid is for the empresses and their offspring, the outer eight grids are for the consorts and their offspring, and the outer sixteen grids are for the common wives and their offspring. Carefully examining the offspring, they resemble lice and fleas.

    357

    又示以盒之下层，但分三罫，都有旨蓄。予曰：“是人为之，抑自取之欤？”曰：“蚁贵自食其力，人所供者不贵。故日出觅食，预置各物于外，以待其飞挽，斯可矣。”曰：“蚁以何物为贵？”曰：“最贵者龙眼肉，其次胡桃肉，再其次羶腻各物，再其次枯死虫豸。而谷粒亦所珍惜，故谷粒另置一罫；日食粒几许，皆有定额，不敢虚糜。凡日所将雏，必报王，乃可给粮。”予曰：“其王亦出阵否？”曰：“间或有之。然两王出阵，彼此皆持重，不甚荡决，反不如命将督师之能鏖战也。”曰：“今日赤部大捷，其王赏有功，当赉何物？”曰：“今日赏赉必重，非龙眼即胡桃也。”其人言毕，拱手携盒，款缓而去。

    Explanation

    357

    He then showed me the lower layer of the box, which was divided into three sections, each containing carefully stored items. I asked, "Were these items placed here by someone else, or did they take them themselves?" He replied, "Ants value self-reliance, and what humans provide is not valued. Therefore, they go out to forage for food each day, placing various items outside to await their transportation, and this is sufficient." I asked, "What do ants value most?" He said, "The most valued are lychee flesh, followed by walnut meat, then fatty and greasy items, and lastly dried dead insects. Grains are also cherished, so they are placed in a separate section. The amount of grains consumed daily is fixed, and they do not waste any. Every day, the young ones must report to the king, and only then can they receive food." I asked, "Does the king also go out to battle?" He said, "Sometimes he does. However, when two kings go out to battle, they both act cautiously and do not engage in reckless fighting; it is actually not as effective as commanding generals and leading troops in battle." I asked, "Today, the Red Department has achieved a great victory. The king will reward the meritorious, what will he give?" He said, "Today's rewards will be significant, either lychee or walnuts." After finishing his words, he bowed his hand, carried the box, and left slowly.

    358

    里乘子曰：君子观于蜂而知君臣，蜂固有之，蚁亦宜然。其人谓礼、义、廉、耻四维毕张，然以战论之，诈败取胜，何其智也！争前合攻，何其勇也！重围让路，何其仁也！闻筒派队，闻鼓进，闻金退，何其信也！匪直张四维，并能立八极，以彼微物而能若此，何以人而不如蚁！

    Explanation

    358

    Li Chengzi said: A gentleman, by observing bees, understands the relationship between ruler and minister. Bees indeed have a social hierarchy, and ants should also have a similar structure. That person believes that the four pillars of propriety, righteousness, integrity, and shame are fully established. Yet, using deceit to win, how intelligent is that! Fighting together, how courageous is that! Yielding a path in a tight situation, how kind is that! Following commands to advance, retreat, or form ranks, how trustworthy is that! Not only are the four pillars established, but also the eight directions are upheld. Given that such small creatures can be so organized and disciplined, how can humans not be as organized and disciplined as ants?

    359

    笪侍御
    　　
    　　镇江吴次垣观察台朗言，其乡笪侍御重光，世称江上先生，生平铁面无私。时权贵某公把持朝政，其子为江西监司，倚父势，极贪墨，声名狼籍，上官侧目而无如何。会先生巡按江西，特具白简劾之，监司寻置于法，而先生直声倾动海内矣。权贵嗛之，屡欲蹈先生隙以图报复，先生知难自存，既疏求罢归，隐居山中，宦橐萧然，但以书画自给。四方不远千里，争求恐后，户屦常满。先生藉以自娱，不论润笔之有无，皆曲应其求，无不满意。每岁笔耕有馀，尽赒穷乏，囊中不留一钱，远近颂声不绝。一日，有客来谒，广颡环眼，丰准修髯，年约四十许，口操秦音，自言耳名来求书画。先生异其状，以礼遇之，留住三日。客请问曰：“先生知予来意否？”曰：“不知。”曰：“先生真长者也！予非他，乃权贵某公使来，甘心于先生者也。

    Explanation

    359

    Master Da Shiyu Chongguang, respectfully known as Jiangshang Xiansheng, was renowned for his impartiality and incorruptibility. During the reign of a powerful official, whose name we will not specify, this official's son served as the governor of Jiangxi and was notorious for his corruption, which had earned him a bad reputation. Despite the governor's misdeeds, the superiors could not act against him due to his father's influence. However, when Master Da was appointed to inspect Jiangxi, he submitted a special petition to impeach the governor, who was subsequently punished according to the law. Master Da's integrity and courage gained widespread fame and respect.

    The powerful official bore a grudge against Master Da and repeatedly tried to take advantage of his weaknesses to seek revenge. Knowing that it was difficult to survive and protect himself, Master Da submitted a petition requesting to be dismissed from office and retired to live in seclusion in the mountains. His official belongings were meager, but his home was always full of visitors who sought his paintings and calligraphy. Regardless of whether there was a fee, Master Da always accommodated their requests in a flexible manner, and everyone was satisfied. Each year, he earned more than enough from his writing and used the surplus to help those in need, leaving no money in his purse. Praise for him was constantly heard.

    One day, a guest came to visit. The guest had a broad forehead, round eyes, a prominent nose, and a long beard, and was about forty years old. He spoke with a Qin accent and said he came to request paintings and calligraphy. Master Da found the guest's appearance unusual and treated him with respect, keeping him for three days. The guest then asked, "Master Da, do you know my purpose for coming?" Master Da replied, "I do not." The guest said, "Master Da, you are truly a gentleman! I am not someone else; I have been sent by the powerful official to harm you."

    360

    ”先生笑曰：“老夫束发读书，粗有所得，寿天穷通，吉凶祸福，一切听之于天。今君既为某公所使远来图我，此亦天命使然，复何尤怨？即请速为勾当了事，无庸游移濡滞也！”客笑曰：“予果欲不利于先生，不待今日矣！方予来时，一路颂先生者甚夥，及始至于境，而颂者益众，老幼妇孺，如出一口，非大贤能若是乎？若害大贤，是悖天也！害大贤不义，悖天不祥，不义不祥，予不为也！今请为先生贺。”问：“何所贺？”曰：“此次幸使予来，倘使他人，则先生危矣。”曰：“此后保无有继君而来者乎？”曰：“先生请毋虑，馀俱予弟子行。吾侪极重义气，予既以义释先生，谁不知者？而肯为某公用乎！”乃索所求书画，并解腰缠，取兼金一百陈几上，揖曰：“先生以此自给，戋戋不腆，伏惟笑纳。予自有以复某公，先生请毋虑。”一拱手，掉头不顾而去。先生为之爽然若失者数日。

    Explanation

    360

    The scholar laughed and said, "Since my early years of studying, I have gained some basic understanding. Whether one lives long or short, experiences poverty or prosperity, good or bad fortune, I leave all to fate. Now that you have come from afar to harm me, it is also as if ordained by heaven. What more can one complain about? Please handle this matter quickly; there is no need to hesitate or delay!" The guest laughed and said, "If I truly wanted to harm you, I would not have waited until today! On my way here, many people praised you. When I first arrived at the destination, the praise grew even more, from the elderly to the young, men and women, all speaking in unison. Only a great sage could inspire such universal admiration. To harm a great sage is to defy heaven! To harm a great sage is unjust, to defy heaven is inauspicious. Unjust and inauspicious, I will not do it! Now I ask you to celebrate." The scholar asked, "What do you celebrate?" The guest replied, "This time, I was fortunate enough to come. If someone else had come, you would be in danger." The scholar asked, "Can I be sure that no one else will follow you?" The guest said, "Please do not worry, all my followers share the same principles. We place great value on righteousness. I have released you out of righteousness, and who does not know this? Would we dare to serve someone else?" He then retrieved the requested paintings and calligraphy, removed the money from his waist, and placed a hundred pieces of gold on the table. He bowed and said, "Please use this for your needs. It is a small amount, but I hope you will accept it with a smile. I will find a way to report back to someone, please do not worry." With a bow, he turned and left without looking back. The scholar felt a sense of loss that lasted several days.

    361

    李泥丸
    　　
    　　吴介臣侍御台寿言，湖州闵小艮司马，素好学道，得真仙李泥丸秘传，后以尸解上升。李泥丸者，初乞食于市，衣须捷而身垢秽，人不能迩。会有巨绅子患消渴疾，百医罔效，其妻祷于神，遇李于庙中，笑谓曰：“娘子欲活郎君耶？祷神无益，曷不求我？”从者陋其状，诃叱之。妻遽止之曰：“否否！我闻风尘中偶有真仙游戏，或有缘幸遇不可知，尔曹勿以貌失之也。”遽前裣衽，叩李求方。李笑曰：“娘子既诚心来求，亦易易耳。”乃掬地上泥，自吐沫搓为丸，授之曰：“归以白汤进病者吞之，当立愈。”妻谢而受之，从者吃吃匿笑。妻归，思病者历试诸方皆不效，姑以此投之何害？遂进白汤趣吞其丸，巨绅子果一汗而瘳。

    Explanation

    361

    R Li, the Immortal with Mud Pills

    According to Wu Jiechen, an official of the Imperial Censorate, Mr. Min Xiaogen of Huzhou, who had always been fond of learning the Dao, received secret teachings from the true immortal Li Niwan and later ascended through the method of shiji (spiritual transformation). Li Niwan, in his early years, begged for food in the marketplace, wearing tattered clothes and being unclean, which made people avoid him. Once, a wealthy family's son was suffering from diabetes, and despite being treated by many doctors, his condition showed no improvement. His wife prayed to the gods, and she encountered Li Niwan in a temple. He smiled and asked her, "Do you want to save your husband? Praying to the gods will not help; why not ask me?" The servants, seeing his appearance, mocked him. The wife quickly stopped them, saying, "No, no! I have heard that among the common people, there might occasionally be a true immortal playing, and it is uncertain whether one might have the good fortune to meet such a being. You must not let his appearance deceive you." She then approached him, bowed deeply, and asked for a remedy. Li Niwan laughed and said, "Since you come with sincerity, it is easy for me." He took some mud from the ground, spat on it, rolled it into a pill, and gave it to her, saying, "Take this pill with plain water to the patient and have him swallow it; he will be immediately cured." The wife thanked him and accepted the pill, while the servants hid their laughter. When she returned home, she thought that since all previous treatments had failed, trying this might not harm her husband. She gave him a cup of plain water and urged him to swallow the pill. Indeed, the wealthy family's son broke out in a sweat and was cured.

    362

    自此，人皆以为遇仙，远近就李求方者日踵相接，俱以沫团泥丸予之，无不立效，佥称为李泥丸云。司马闻之，拜求为师，李相司马，谓有仙骨，可以入道，许之。司马尝具汤沐、奉新衣请李易之，笑却不受。每行市上，喜与小儿戏。群儿亦乐从之游，皆呼曰：“李神仙。”李戏拾败叶，呵之即成钱，分给群儿，使市果饵。钱上字幕分明，历久不变，何其神也！司马尝叩拔宅飞升之说，一日卓午，李携司马立日中，取自着破毡笠置司马头上，又取司马角巾自着之，属司马视其影，李则但见帽影而不见人影，己则但见人影而不见帽影。李谓之曰：“所谓拔宅者，只就本身所御之物而言。身果能仙，平日即身所御之物皆随之而仙，非必宅果可拔也。”司马恍然，道以日进。或曰李泥丸即李八百。

    Explanation

    362

    From this point on, everyone believed they had encountered a fairy. People from far and near came to Li seeking remedies, one after another, like a continuous stream. Li gave them all remedies made from foam and clay, and all showed immediate results. They all called him Li Mudan. When Sima heard of this, he knelt and requested to be taken as a teacher. Li, looking at Sima, said he had the bones of a fairy and could enter the Dao, and agreed to him. Sima once prepared a bath and new clothes to ask Li to change into, but Li smiled and refused. Whenever Li walked in the market, he liked to play with children. The children also happily followed him around and called him "Li the Immortal." Li playfully picked up fallen leaves, and when he blew on them, they turned into coins, which he distributed to the children to buy fruit and snacks. The text on the coins was clearly visible and remained unchanged for a long time, how miraculous it is! Sima once inquired about the concept of lifting one's entire house and ascending to heaven. One day at noon, Li took Sima and stood them in the sunlight, took his own worn-out felt hat and placed it on Sima's head, then took Sima's headband and put it on himself. He instructed Sima to look at his shadow. Li saw only the shadow of the hat but not the shadow of the person, while Sima saw only the shadow of the person but not the shadow of the hat. Li said to him, "What is meant by lifting one's house is to consider only the objects one uses. If one truly becomes an immortal, the objects one uses in daily life will also become immortal, not necessarily that the house can be lifted." Sima suddenly understood and progressed in the Dao day by day. Some say Li Mudan is the same as Li Baibai.

    363

    里乘子曰：予初识吴氏昆仲于方子箴都转扬州官署。尝与次垣论古今书家，意见不合，次垣攘臂相争，自折其齿，举座皆笑；逾时意气俱平，谈笑自若，固各无蒂蓟也。介臣喜谈道，一日，都转招饮，介臣席间谈李泥丸事，并述司马尸解后，以道传袁太太。某宅素凶，主人请袁治之，袁以绳连系七鬼，宅乃转凶为吉。词锋霅霅，四座口为之噤。会道州何子贞先生在座，素不喜人谈怪，枯坐欲睡，介臣方刺刺不休，亦不以为意也。乃曾几何时，不兼旬而昆仲竟相继下世，追忆朋友聚散存殁之感，为之愀然。先大夫守湖州时，小艮司马居金盖山。会先妣杨太夫人有疾，先大夫携予宿金盖礼忏，求丹药疗之，果瘳。司马遇蘸坛则易道装，平时酬酢往来仍著冠服。尝至署中，先大夫觞之，予亦侍坐，司马茹荤饮酒，谈道娓娓不倦，惜予方幼稚，不能叩其旨趣也。方子箴识。

    Explanation

    363

    The Master of the Village said: I first met the brothers of the Wu family at Fang Zizhen's official residence in Yangzhou. I once debated with Ciyuan about the calligraphers of past and present, and our opinions clashed. Ciyuan raised his arms in a heated argument, biting off his own teeth, and everyone in the room laughed; after a while, their tempers calmed down, and they continued to talk and laugh as if nothing had happened, truly showing that there was no lingering resentment. Jiechen enjoyed discussing the Dao. One day, Fang Zizhen invited us to a banquet, and Jiechen recounted the story of Li Niman, as well as the tale of Master Sima's spiritual transformation after his physical death, and how he passed the Dao to Lady Yuan. A certain house was always unlucky, and the owner asked Lady Yuan to help. She tied seven ghosts with ropes, and the house then turned from misfortune to good fortune. His words were sharp and piercing, and everyone in the room was speechless. At the same banquet, Mr. He Zizhen of Daozhou was present, and he always disliked people talking about strange things. He sat there dully, about to fall asleep, while Jiechen talked incessantly, and he did not pay attention to it. And yet, not long after, within a few days, the brothers eventually died one after another. Reflecting on the feelings of friends gathering and scattering, living and dead, I was filled with sadness. When my father was governor of Huzhou, Master Xiaogen lived on Jinguai Mountain. When my mother, Lady Yang, fell ill, my father took me to stay at Jinguai Mountain to perform a repentance ceremony, seeking elixirs to treat her, and she indeed recovered. When Master Xiaogen met a sacrificial altar, he changed into Taoist robes, but in daily social interactions, he still wore official attire. He once came to the official residence, and my father hosted him, and I also attended the gathering. Master Xiaogen ate meat and drank wine, discussing the Dao in a continuous and engaging manner, but unfortunately, I was still young and unable to probe into its deeper meaning. Fang Zizhen recorded this.

    364

    鱼城
    　　
    　　洞庭湖滨渔人，户蓄鸬鹚，即杜诗所谓乌鬼也。其嘴曲如钩者最佳，一头可值五十金。小鱼至数斤重者，一乌鬼可取；重数十百斤者，只四五乌鬼亦足舁而出之。其下水时，皆绳约其颈，不则自饱咽之，即不肯为役矣。相传某年岁暮，正值需鱼之时，渔人日纵乌鬼下湖，连日竟一无所得。有善泅者下水侦之，知鱼已成城矣。鱼成城者，大鱼互衔其尾，层累打围于外，众小鱼实其中，又有极大之鱼，衔尾封盖于上，居然众志成城，撄守甚固，牢不可破。渔人聚议，无所为计。

    Explanation

    364

    Fish City

    The fishermen who dwell by Dongting Lake keep cormorants in every household—these are the "black demons" (wugui) mentioned in the poetry of Du Fu. Those with beaks curved like hooks are the finest, and one such bird can be worth as much as fifty gold pieces. Small fish weighing several jin can be caught by a single cormorant; for fish weighing dozens or even hundreds of jin, only four or five cormorants are needed to lift them out of the water. When they go underwater, they are all tethered around the neck with ropes; otherwise, they would eat the fish themselves and refuse to serve as laborers. It is said that one year, at the end of the year, when fish were most needed, the fishermen sent their cormorants into the lake every day, but for several days they caught nothing. A skilled swimmer then went underwater to investigate and discovered that the fish had formed a city. When fish form a city, the larger fish interlock their tails, layering themselves around the outside to encircle the smaller fish within. There is also a very large fish that locks its tail at the top, sealing the structure shut. Thus, through collective effort, they form a city, fiercely guarded and unbreakably secure. The fishermen gathered to discuss the matter, but could think of no solution.

    365

    忽有人言宜昌老渔翁家蓄一乌鬼，鸷而且智，号为鬼帅，非此不可以破鱼城。遂醵金具重币，使人往求某翁暂假鬼帅一用，并约得鱼不吝厚酬，某翁许诺。不日携至，众视鬼帅，枭目鹰吻，雕翎鹗爪，真神俊物也。重刑牲酾酒，祭祷湖神，试先纵鬼帅入湖，以觇其技。乃既达鱼城，四围循视，复遍察于上，见衔尾处有微隙可乘，以首锐入，摇身遽下，遇小鱼横乱啄之，小鱼惶骇，互相奔逃，势甚汹汹，大鱼猝难坚持，顷刻其城立溃。渔人见众鳞陡跃，湖波顿兴，知鬼帅得胜，急纵群乌鬼下湖，一鼓而聚擒之。

    Explanation

    365

    Suddenly, it was reported that an elderly fisherman from Yichang kept a black ghost, which was ferocious and intelligent, and was called the ghost leader. Only this one could break the fish city. They then pooled money and prepared rich gifts, sending someone to ask the old man to temporarily lend the ghost leader for use, and agreed that if they caught fish, they would not hesitate to offer generous rewards. The old man agreed. In no time, they brought the ghost leader.
