Everyone looked at the ghost leader, which had eagle-like eyes, hawk-like beak, carved feathers, and falcon-like claws, truly a divine and magnificent creature. They sacrificed animals and poured wine, praying to the lake god, and first released the ghost leader into the lake to test its skills. Once it reached the fish city, it circled around and looked all around, then thoroughly inspected the top, seeing a small gap at the tail where it could enter. It used its sharp head to enter, quickly descending, meeting the small fish and pecking them wildly. The small fish were terrified, fleeing from each other, the situation was very chaotic, the big fish could not hold on suddenly, and in a moment, the city collapsed. The fishermen saw the fish suddenly leap, the lake waves suddenly surged, knowing the ghost leader had won, they quickly released the group of black ghosts into the lake, and in one go, they captured them all.

    366

    某官妾
    　　
    　　某官出都赴任，至袁浦，舍车而舟。有爱妾某氏，登舟失足堕水，某官恸甚，乃扬言谕诸三老曰：“有能入水得尸者，犒白金五十镒；如拯出尚未气绝者，倍之。”诸三老贪赏，争泅水寻觅。有篙师某甲，独于港汊得之，居然气尚未绝，大喜。既见某氏两腕俱约金玉跳脱，其余簪钏首饰，俱系金玉珠宝，所值何止百镒？计不如戕其命而纳其物后再献尸求赏，不较算无遗策耶？乃将某氏复拉至水深处，察其气绝，曳藏芦苇中，自独归家，具以告妻，夫妇交相庆慰。趣妻为具晚炊，饱餐毕，甲趁暮夜无人，约先往取物送回。妻待至翌日薄暮，踪迹杳然，心殊疑虑，不得已，以告他三老求往视之，果见某氏尸藏芦苇中，面目如生，怒容可掬；甲跪其旁，抚之，则已僵矣。急趋白某官，始共知某甲险恶，为鬼索命而毙。爰舁某氏尸殡于舟。即取白金五十镒并某氏金玉珠宝等物，分犒诸三老，而某甲独不与焉。

    Explanation

    366

    A Certain Official's Concubine

    A certain official left the capital to take up his post, arriving at Yuanpu, where he abandoned the carriage for a boat. He had a beloved concubine named Ms. [Name], who climbed onto the boat, lost her footing, and fell into the water. The official was extremely grief-stricken and thus declared to the village elders, saying, "Whoever can dive into the water and retrieve the body will be rewarded with fifty taels of white gold; if they rescue her before she has died, the reward will be doubled." The village elders, greedy for the reward, rushed to dive into the water to search for her. There was a boatman named Mr. [Name], who alone found her in a branch of the river. She was still alive, and he was greatly pleased. When he saw that both her wrists were adorned with gold and jade bracelets, and the rest of her hairpins, bracelets, and ornaments were all made of gold, jade, and precious jewels, the value was far more than a hundred taels. He thought it would be better to kill her, take her valuables, and then present her body for the reward, wouldn't that be the perfect plan? He then dragged her back to the deep part of the water, checked if she was dead, dragged her into the reeds and hid her, and returned home alone, telling his wife in detail. The couple rejoiced together. He urged his wife to prepare dinner, and after a hearty meal, Mr. [Name] took advantage of the late night when no one was around, planning to go first to take the valuables back. His wife waited until the next evening, but he had disappeared without a trace, and she was deeply suspicious. With no choice, she told the other village elders to go and check on her. They indeed found Ms. [Name]'s body hidden in the reeds, her face looked as if she were alive, with a look of anger on her face. Mr. [Name] knelt beside her, touched her, and found that she was already stiff. They hurriedly reported this to the official, and everyone then realized how wicked Mr. [Name] was, who was killed by the spirit of Ms. [Name]. They then carried Ms. [Name]'s body to the boat for burial. They took the fifty taels of silver and the gold, jade, and precious jewels belonging to Ms. [Name], and distributed the reward among the village elders, but Mr. [Name] was not included.

    367

    里乘子曰：某甲得尸，气尚未绝，大喜，向使将去求赏，百镒之外，即另求某氏金玉珠宝等物，未尝不可，观后仍以各物分镐诸三老，可知某官并非悭吝人也。乃某甲贪念一转，自谓算无遗策，究之致人于死，而己亦卒为鬼索命而毙。某官分犒诸三老，甲独不与，然则险恶之人，复何益哉！某氏一弱女子耳，使非其鬼有灵，几致沉冤莫白。观其面目如生、怒容可掬，报仇雪愤，何其神也！噫嘻！阳有官刑，阴有冥罚，自古及今，焉有杀人而能漏网者哉！

    Explanation

    367

    里乘子曰：某甲得尸，气尚未绝，大喜，向使将去求赏，百镒之外，即另求某氏金玉珠宝等物，未尝不可，观后仍以各物分镐诸三老，可知某官并非悭吝人也。乃某甲贪念一转，自谓算无遗策，究之致人于死，而己亦卒为鬼索命而毙。某官分犒诸三老，甲独不与，然则险恶之人，复何益哉！某氏一弱女子耳，使非其鬼有灵，几致沉冤莫白。观其面目如生、怒容可掬，报仇雪愤，何其神也！噫嘻！阳有官刑，阴有冥罚，自古及今，焉有杀人而能漏网者哉！

    The narrator said: Someone found a corpse, the breath was not yet extinguished, and he was greatly pleased. If he had taken the body away to seek a reward, beyond a hundred yi, he could have sought other valuable items from a certain person, which would not have been impossible. Later, he still distributed the various items among the three elders of the village, thus it is known that the official was not a stingy person. But someone's greedy thoughts changed, and he thought his plan was foolproof. In the end, he caused someone's death, and he himself was eventually killed by the ghost seeking revenge. The official distributed rewards among the three elders, but someone was excluded. Thus, what good does a wicked person do? Someone was just a weak woman. If it were not for the spirit of the ghost, almost led to a deep injustice remaining unresolved. Seeing that the face was still alive-looking, with a visible expression of anger, avenging and clearing one's anger, how divine it is! Ah! There is official punishment in the light, and divine retribution in the darkness. From ancient times to the present, how can there be anyone who kills and escapes unpunished?

    368

    里乘卷六
    　　
    　　吴和甫侍郎轶事
    　　
    　　吾乡吴公和甫，讳存义，本休宁人，寄籍泰兴。由翰林起家，官至吏部侍郎。公正廉明，爱才如命。督学滇甫，前后八年，士民爱戴。当回民逆命时，滇省大小官署及缙绅巨室，悉罹屠毒，淫掠甚惨，独相戒不犯学使者署。时盐巡道署中货财衣物攘劫一空，并将插架书籍以刀截为两段。案有公诗集四本，一贼方将加刃，一贼急止之曰：“此吴学使诗也，慎勿损坏。”所藏书数万卷，惟公集独占硕果，亦一奇也。公按临永昌，试竣出城，甫数里，回视城内火光烛天，沿途回匪，白布缠头，戈甲森列，见公至，皆跪拜道左。公式舆训之曰：“汝曹皆天朝好百姓，幸各自爱。

    Explanation

    368

    Volume Six of Li Cheng

    The Remarkable Deeds of Vice Minister Wu He Fu

    My fellow townsman, Vice Minister Wu He Fu, whose given name was Cunyi, was originally from Xiuning but officially registered in Taixing. He began his career as a Hanlin scholar and eventually rose to the position of Vice Minister of the Ministry of Personnel. Wu was upright, just, and clear-minded, and he cherished talent as if it were his life. He served as the provincial governor of education in Yunnan for eight years, and during that time, he was beloved by scholars and the common people.

    During the rebellion of the Hui people, all government offices and the homes of prominent officials in Yunnan Province were subjected to slaughter and cruelty, with rampant rape and looting. However, the rebels mutually warned each other not to attack the office of the provincial governor of education. At the time, the office of the salt inspectorate was completely plundered of its goods, clothing, and valuables, and the books on the shelves were cut in half with a knife. There were four volumes of Wu’s poetry collection on the desk, and as one bandit was about to slash it with a knife, another bandit quickly stopped him, saying, "These are the poems of the provincial governor of education Wu; be careful not to damage them." Among the tens of thousands of books he collected, only his own collection remained intact, which was also a remarkable thing.

    Wu arrived in Yongchang and, after the exams were completed, left the city. Just a few miles outside the city, he looked back and saw the city ablaze with fire. Along the way, Hui rebels with white cloth wrapped around their heads and spears and armor arranged in rows knelt and bowed on the left side of the road when they saw Wu. Wu spoke to them from his carriage, saying, "You all are good citizens of the Heavenly Court; please take care of yourselves."

    369

    ”众唯唯噭应，稽颡有声。盖汉回誓不两立，必俟公出城乃敢互相格斗，居民多诡称为公傔从，幸免于难。公按临迤西时，有举人李某攀辕，诉称丽江县属盐井，有横天都御史者谋叛，聚众数十万，尅日举事。公以事关重大，密檄丽江太守暨巡绰官侦察虚实，复自易服潜访。乃知李前曾以首告谋逆疆吏要功，诬伤二千馀命，李侥幸得钦赐举人，兹仍欲袭故智。其所胪列为首者，皆本地富家巨族，所称聚众，即井旁烧盐灶丁；所谓横天都御史，则彼处供奉之神时降巫者之身，乡民遂称巫为横天都御史，其余所言茫无影响。公据实咨明督抚，惟将巫者惩责；复咨部将李某斥革，一时士民额手感颂，皆称之曰“吴青天”焉。

    Explanation

    369

    The crowd responded with loud, submissive agreement, bowing their heads with audible reverence. It was said that the Han and the Hui had sworn to be irreconcilable, only daring to fight each other once the public had left the city. Many residents pretended to be the public's attendants, thus escaping the conflict. When the public was inspecting the western regions, a scholar named Li grabbed the chariot's pole, pleading that in Lijiang County, there was a "Tian Duyushi" (Heavenly Magistrate) who was plotting rebellion, gathering tens of thousands of people, planning to act on a specific day. The public, considering the matter serious, secretly issued orders to the magistrate of Lijiang and the patrol officers to investigate the truth, and personally changed clothes to secretly investigate. It was then discovered that Li had previously falsely accused a border official of plotting rebellion to gain merit, wrongfully accusing over two thousand people. Li had luckily been granted the title of scholar by imperial decree, and now still wanted to repeat the old trick. The people he listed as leaders were all local wealthy families and influential clans, the so-called gathering of people referred to the salt workers near the well; the so-called "Tian Duyushi" was actually the body of a local deity who occasionally descended as a medium, and the villagers called the medium "Tian Duyushi." The rest of what he said was vague and without impact. The public reported the facts to the provincial governors, punishing and reprimanding only the medium; he also consulted the ministry to dismiss and remove Li from office. At that time, the gentry and common people felt relieved and praised him, all calling him "Mr. Wu, the Clear Sky."

    370

    后回匪诱丽江人从逆，皆拒之曰：“吾侪小人沐吴青天之德，幸得保全首领。若犯上作乱，其何面目以见公乎？”故滇省变乱相循者多年，而丽江从贼者卒鲜。其督学浙江也，按临嘉兴时，海盐某生以非罪系狱，受刑甚酷。公廉知其诬，点名至生，问提调官：“某生何在？”提调官以系狱对，公叱曰：“秀才有罪，应详准学使褫革，今未详革而敢擅下于狱，是何例也！如某生不来，老夫当停试奏办。”提调大恐，亟谕邑令出生于狱。比生至，发蓬面垢，形同重犯。公太息抚慰之曰：“汝发如此，种种皆老夫教令不行所至也。”提调、邑令相视忸怩，噤不敢语。及榜发，某生以优等食饩焉。

    Explanation

    370

    The bandits lured the people of Lijiang to join the rebellion, but they all refused, saying, "We common people have been bathed in the virtue of Wu Qingtian. Fortunately, we have managed to preserve our lives. If we defy authority and cause disorder, how can we face the public?" Thus, although the province of Yunnan experienced prolonged turmoil, few people from Lijiang joined the rebels. When he was overseeing education in Zhejiang, while inspecting Jiaxing, a student from Haixi was unjustly imprisoned and suffered very severe punishment. The official discovered that it was a false accusation. He called out the student's name and asked the examination official, "Where is the student?" The examination official answered that the student was imprisoned. The official scolded, saying, "If a scholar has committed a crime, it should be properly investigated and confirmed by the provincial examination supervisor before being dismissed. Now, without proper investigation, how dare you arbitrarily imprison him in prison? What precedent is this! If the student does not come, I will stop the examination and report this matter." The examination official was greatly frightened and quickly ordered the county magistrate to release the student from prison. When the student arrived, he had disheveled hair and a dirty face, looking like a serious criminal. The official sighed deeply and comforted him, saying, "Your hair is like this. All of this is due to my instructions not being carried out as ordered." The examination official and the county magistrate looked at each other in embarrassment and were silent and dared not speak. When the results were announced, the student was admitted as an outstanding candidate.

    371

    又公试杭州，有商籍童生陈某卷甚佳，越日，面试二题，顷刻走笔而成，斐然可诵。公益疑非童生所能办，密使人访之，果新昌秀才俞某顶冒捉刀。后试新昌，点名至俞，乃训之曰：“凡为秀才者，有学尤贵有品。汝前次为陈某捉刀，吾怜汝才而姑贷之，若遇他人，则汝殆矣。是所得者少而失者甚巨，智者不为也。汝其戒之。”俞感激涕零。榜发，以优等食饩，自是改行，不敢再蹈前辙矣。公生性俭约，官至卿贰，在京邸只蓄二仆，典学时倍之。到处严戒仆夫，毋许悉索供亿，除廉奉外，一切陋规丝毫不取。浙省学租岁数千金，悉捐于各郡创建育婴堂，所活嫛婗不可胜数。今滇、浙两省，皆建公祠以尸祝之。浙祠滨于西湖，每届乡试，各郡士子，订期醵资聚祀者千馀人。于戏！非盛德感人之深，恶能若是哉！

    Explanation

    371

    During the public examination in Hangzhou, there was a merchant's son named Teng who submitted an exceptionally good paper. The next day, during the oral examination, he was asked two questions and completed his answers with remarkable speed and elegance, which were worthy of being read aloud. The official was suspicious that such a paper could not have been written by a mere candidate and secretly investigated the matter. It turned out that Teng's paper had been written by a scholar from Xinchang named Yu, who had impersonated Teng. Later, when the examination was held in Xinchang, the official called out Yu's name and admonished him, saying, "For those who are scholars, having knowledge is important, but having character is even more so. Your previous act of impersonating Teng, I have forgiven you out of sympathy for your talent, but if you were to encounter someone else, you would be in great danger. What you gain is little, but what you lose is enormous. A wise person would not do such a thing. You should take this as a warning." Yu was moved to tears by the official's words. When the results were announced, Yu was listed as an excellent candidate and received a stipend, and from then on, he reformed his behavior and did not dare to repeat his mistake. The official was by nature thrifty and simple, and despite reaching a high-ranking position as a vice-minister, he only kept two servants in the capital. When he was a scholar-official, he had twice as many servants. He strictly warned his servants not to demand excessive supplies and took only a modest salary, refusing all corrupt practices. The annual rental income from educational properties in Zhejiang Province was several thousand taels of silver, which he donated entirely to establish orphanages in various counties, saving countless abandoned children. Today, both Yunnan and Zhejiang provinces have built temples in his honor. The temple in Zhejiang is located by West Lake, and every time the provincial examinations are held, thousands of scholars from various counties gather to worship him. Indeed, it is not due to the profound virtue that moved people, but how could it be otherwise?

    372

    里乘子曰：公直南书房时，文庙见所着貂服甚敝，问之。公叩首曰：“臣自授编修，仅此一袭，服之已二十馀年矣。”上微笑颔之。寻赐貂服，并拜典试之命。及任满入觐，上问滇省变乱原委甚详。既问：“汝按临几郡？”公叩首对曰：“以道梗均未按试。”上叹曰：“苦煞汝矣。”翌日，命兼摄顺天府丞事。谢恩时，上笑曰：“顺天府丞，每届考期，卷资例有千金，聊偿在滇之苦可矣。”其为文庙优眷如此。公每述及先朝高厚之恩，未尝不泣数行下也。

    Explanation

    372

    里乘子曰：公直南书房时，文庙见所着貂服甚敝，问之。公叩首曰：“臣自授编修，仅此一袭，服之已二十馀年矣。”上微笑颔之。寻赐貂服，并拜典试之命。及任满入觐，上问滇省变乱原委甚详。既问：“汝按临几郡？”公叩首对曰：“以道梗均未按试。”上叹曰：“苦煞汝矣。”翌日，命兼摄顺天府丞事。谢恩时，上笑曰：“顺天府丞，每届考期，卷资例有千金，聊偿在滇之苦可矣。”其为文庙优眷如此。公每述及先朝高厚之恩，未尝不泣数行下也。

    It is said by Li Chengzi: When the official was serving in the Southern Study, the emperor noticed his fur robe was very worn out and asked about it. The official bowed and said, "I have only this one fur robe, which I have worn for more than twenty years since being appointed as a compiler." The emperor smiled and nodded. Soon after, the emperor bestowed a new fur robe upon him and appointed him as the chief examiner. When his term was up and he came to pay homage to the emperor, the emperor asked in great detail about the causes and consequences of the unrest in Yunnan province. The emperor then asked, "How many prefectures did you inspect?" The official bowed and replied, "Due to road blockages, I was unable to conduct examinations in all the prefectures." The emperor sighed, "You must have suffered greatly." The next day, the emperor appointed him as the acting vice-governor of Shuntian Prefecture. When the official thanked the emperor, the emperor laughed and said, "As the vice-governor of Shuntian Prefecture, you will receive a substantial income during each exam period, which can compensate for the hardships in Yunnan." The emperor treated him with such favor. Whenever the official recounted the emperor's kindness, he could not help but cry.

    373

    庚申秋，外夷至京师，乘舆北幸，都城汹汹，大小臣工纷纷携眷迁避，各道胡同曲突绝烟。公时抱疴，闻难痛哭，谓所亲陆眉生给谏日：“子有老母，其行也乎哉！宁慎持之勿妄动，免悔噬脐也。”陆慷慨誓死勿去，公壮之。事定后，既出复入者，见公咸有愧色焉。江阴何廉昉观察拭，侨居维扬，筑壶园，座客常满。予尝于壶园座上得识诸公子，公次子礼园郎中宝清，博览群书，尤肆力于诗古文词；三子农上茂才宝让，何之聓也，皆能世公之学。予幸与纪群交稔，知公生平大略，爰著于编，藉以风世。《诗》有之曰：“虽无老成人，尚有典刑。”惟公有焉。

    Explanation

    373

    In the autumn of the Gengshen year, foreign barbarians invaded the capital, and the imperial carriage fled north. The capital was in a state of panic, with officials of all ranks and their families fleeing in haste. The streets and alleys were deserted, with no smoke rising from chimneys. At the time, the subject was ill, and upon hearing of the crisis, he wept bitterly. He told his close friend Lu Meisheng, an advisor, "Your mother is old; should you flee? It is better to remain cautious and not act rashly, so as to avoid future regret." Lu Meisheng, with great resolve, vowed to stay despite the danger, and the subject admired his courage. After the crisis had passed, those who had fled and returned later felt ashamed when they saw the subject. From Jiangyin, there was He Lianfang, an observer who lived in Weiyang (present-day Yangzhou). He built a garden called Hutu Yuan, which was often full of guests. The author once met the subject's sons at a gathering in Hutu Yuan. The subject's second son, Baohuaqing, was a medical officer who was well-read and especially dedicated to poetry and classical prose. The third son, Baorang, was a talented scholar and He Lianfang's son-in-law. Both sons were able to carry on the subject's scholarly tradition. The author was fortunate to have a close relationship with a group of scholars and knew the subject's general life story. He wrote about the subject in a compilation, using this as an example to inspire and influence the world. As the Book of Songs says, "Even though there are no old sages, there are still examples to follow." The subject was one such example.

    374

    年大将军
    　　
    　　幼听野老谈年大将军羹尧轶事，或谓其祖籍本安徽怀远县，后隶汉军。父遐龄，初为驻防都统；母极悍妒，都统私婢有身，母知之，大怒，以婢给仆，生羹尧。术者相其必贵，复收育之。儿时性黠犷，力复过人。入塾不肯读书，师偶威以夏楚，反受其侮。齿已舞勺，尚不识一丁字。都统患之，遍托人物色名师，有能教化其子者，不吝厚俸。有叟突然不介来谒，自称愿为公子师。都统出见，叟年可七十许，白髯布袍，举止不俗，心异之。

    Explanation

    374

    A Story of General Geng Yao

    When I was young, I heard old villagers talk about the extraordinary life of General Geng Yao. It is said that he originally came from Huaiyuan County, Anhui Province, and later became part of the Han army. His father, Xialing, was initially a commander of a garrison. His mother was extremely fierce and jealous. One day, the commander had a concubine who was pregnant. When the mother found out, she was very angry and gave the concubine to a servant, and Geng Yao was born. A fortune-teller predicted that he would surely become noble, and his mother took him back to raise him. As a child, Geng Yao was cunning and wild, with strength beyond ordinary. When he entered school, he refused to read, and the teacher occasionally threatened him with punishment, but instead, he was humiliated by Geng Yao. By the time he was old enough to dance the wushao (a ritual dance for young boys), he still couldn't recognize even a single character. The commander was worried about this and entrusted many people to find a good teacher. Anyone who could educate his son would not be stingy with high pay. Suddenly, an old man came without any introduction and claimed to be willing to be the young master's teacher. The commander went out to meet him. The old man was about seventy years old, with a white beard and a plain robe, and he had a dignified manner. The commander found him unusual.

    375

    因谓叟曰：“豚儿蠢劣异常，先生惠然肯来，敢问教化当用何法？”叟曰：“如公不弃老朽，请择乡村僻地，筑花园一所，池沼山石、竹木花草毕具，且备设经史图籍，及一切兵器、日用各物。园中惟居师弟二人，不用仆从。四围高其墙垣，不必设门，仅留圭窦，按时以进饮食。比及三年，老朽自有以报公也。”都统如言部署。叟与羹尧居园中，日自兀坐观书，听羹尧所为，绝不过问。羹尧日濬沼填池，移山运石，种竹木，裁花草，嬉戏自得，亦与叟从不通一语。自春徂秋，园中池沼、山石、竹木、花草，或自东迁西，或至南徙北，迁徙既遍，羹尧颇自厌烦。一日饭毕，见叟观书孜孜不倦，旁窥良久，似有羡心，卒然问曰：“先生竟日看书，其中果有味耶？”叟漫应曰：“书味极好，非汝所知也。汝第戏耳，勿来相嬲！”曰：“然则我亦可学乎？”叟曰：“有何不可？但恐汝不肯用心耳。

    Explanation

    375

    The official said to the old man, "My son is extremely stupid and clumsy. The master has kindly come, dare I ask what method of education and guidance should be used?" The old man replied, "If you do not disdain this old man, please choose a remote rural area and build a garden, with ponds, mountains, stones, bamboo, trees, flowers, and grass all included. Also prepare books of classics and history, as well as all kinds of weapons and daily necessities. Only the teacher and the disciple will live in the garden, without any servants. Surround the garden with high walls, no need to set up a gate, just leave a small opening. Provide food and drink at regular times. By the end of three years, the old man will have something to report to you." The official followed his advice. The old man and Geng Yao lived in the garden, sitting alone and reading books every day, listening to what Geng Yao did but never interfering. Geng Yao dug ponds, filled pools, moved mountains, transported stones, planted bamboo and trees, and pruned flowers and grass, playing and enjoying himself, but did not speak to the old man at all. From spring to autumn, the ponds, mountains, stones, bamboo, trees, flowers, and grass in the garden either moved from east to west or from south to north. After all the changes, Geng Yao became quite bored. One day after finishing his meal, he saw the old man reading books diligently without tiring, looking at him for a long time, seeming to have a sense of envy. He suddenly asked, "The master reads books all day, is there really any flavor in it?" The old man casually replied, "The taste of books is extremely good, it's not something you can understand. You're just playing around, don't come and bother me!" Geng Yao asked, "Then can I also learn?" The old man said, "There's nothing wrong with that, but I'm afraid you won't put your heart into it."

    376

    ”曰：“我若用心，汝肯教否乎？”叟笑曰：“汝如肯用心，我有何不肯教？”羹尧喜曰：“请从今日始，愿师有以教我，幸甚！然究竟读书有何好处？敢请。”叟正色曰：“书中好处甚多。上焉者为圣贤，其次立功名，又其次取富贵。不知汝欲自居何等也？”曰：“敢问何谓为圣贤？”曰：“则古称先，躬行不倦，明体达用，可仕可隐，道重一时，教垂百世，是谓为圣贤。”曰：“何谓立功名？”曰：“贯通天人，经纬文武，伐罪戡乱，拓地开疆，带砺山河，勋铭钟鼎，是谓立功名。”曰：“何谓取富贵？”曰：“简练揣摩，务趋时尚，弋获科第，雍容华，妇嬉子笑，温饱自甘，是谓取富贵。

    Explanation

    376

    "Rui said, 'If I use my heart, will you teach me?' The old man smiled and said, 'If you are willing to use your heart, how could I not teach you?' Geng Yao was delighted and said, 'Please start from today, I wish for my teacher to instruct me, and I am very fortunate! However, what are the benefits of reading books? I dare to ask.' The old man spoke seriously, 'There are many benefits in books. The highest is to become a sage and virtuous person, next is to establish fame and glory, and then to attain wealth and social status. Do you know what level you wish to achieve?' Rui asked, 'What does it mean to become a sage and virtuous person?' The old man replied, 'To follow the examples of the ancients, to practice diligently without tiring, to understand the principles and apply them, to be able to serve in office or retire to seclusion, to be valued in one's time, and to have one's teachings endure for generations, this is what it means to become a sage and virtuous person.' Rui asked, 'What does it mean to establish fame and glory?' The old man replied, 'To connect heaven and humanity, to weave through literature and military affairs, to punish crimes and suppress chaos, to expand territory and open new frontiers, to secure the mountains and rivers, to have one's achievements inscribed on bells and tripods, this is what it means to establish fame and glory.' Rui asked, 'What does it mean to attain wealth and social status?' The old man replied, 'To refine and practice, to strive for current trends, to gain success in the imperial examinations, to live in luxury and refinement, to have wives laugh and children smile, to be content with warmth and food, this is what it means to attain wealth and social status.'

    377

    ”羹尧沉吟久之，曰：“圣贤非所敢望，寻常富贵又非所屑，愿师教我以立功名者，可乎？”叟曰：“汝果真心从学乎？”羹尧乃拔剑斫树，誓之曰：“如不真心从学，有如此树！”叟知其志已决，大喜。于是先取经史，日与讲论；又教其攻习举业，暇则谈论兵法，早晚或习射，或舞刀剑干戈，相与为乐。羹尧天资固优，凡事一学即精，三年学果有成。其齿甫成童也。会仆由窦进馔，叟笑谓曰：“烦寄语若主人，公子学成，可以开门矣。”仆告都统开门，宾主相见，执手慰庆。都统见羹尧恂恂知礼，不似从前跳荡，知其学果有得，大喜，乃设筵款叟，酬以千金。叟固辞曰：“俟公子功成名立，再来索谢。”言毕兴辞，再三留之不可。羹尧雅不忍舍，独送一舍之地。师弟相对，依依难别。叟赠诗一首，令谨识之，洒泪挥手飘然而去。诗曰：“海水摇青云四垂，瞳晓日照旌旗。愿君熟读淮阴传，毋忘低头胯下时。”

    Explanation

    377

    Gong Yao pondered for a long time and said, "I dare not aspire to be a sage or a virtuous person, and ordinary wealth and nobility are not worth considering. May I ask my teacher to instruct me on how to achieve fame and success?" The old man asked, "Are you truly sincere in your desire to learn?" Gong Yao then drew his sword and cut down a tree, swearing, "If I am not truly sincere in my desire to learn, may I be like this tree!" Knowing that his resolve was firm, the old man greatly pleased. He then began by selecting the classics and histories, discussing them daily; he also taught him to study the imperial examinations, and in his spare time, they discussed military strategy. In the morning or evening, he practiced archery, swordsmanship, and martial arts, enjoying each other's company. Gong Yao had naturally superior talent, excelling at everything he learned, and after three years, his studies were successful. He was just a young boy at the time. When the servant brought food through a small door, the old man smiled and said, "Please convey to your master that the young master has completed his studies, so you can open the door." The servant informed the general to open the door, and the host and guest met, clasping hands and celebrating. The general saw that Gong Yao was modest and well-mannered, unlike before when he was wild and unrestrained, knowing that his studies had indeed borne fruit, greatly pleased, and set up a feast to entertain the old man, rewarding him with a thousand gold pieces. The old man declined, saying, "Wait until the young master has achieved fame and success, then come back to claim your reward." After finishing his words, he took his leave, and despite repeated attempts to keep him, he could not be persuaded to stay. Gong Yao was very reluctant to part, walking alone for a mile. The teacher and student stood facing each other, reluctant to part. The old man gave him a poem, instructing him to memorize it carefully, shedding tears, waving goodbye, and departing lightly. The poem reads: "The sea waves rock under the four corners of the sky, the morning sun shines on the banners. I hope you will thoroughly read the biography of Han Xin, never forgetting the time when you knelt under the bridge."

    378

    里乘子曰：叟突然而来，飘然而去，神龙见首不见尾，其殆仙乎？羹尧他日仗钺专征，威震万里，功名显赫，皆叟之教也。迹其生平为人，卒不能去一骄字，伏法自取，夫复何怨？然则叟以教化自居，抗颜三年，意者教则有馀，化犹不足欤？临别赠言，具有深意，听之藐藐，良可嗟叹。抑又闻之羹尧与吾乡张文和相国齐年登第，过从甚密。文瑞相国尝谓文和曰：“是儿音洪而厉，目炯而怒，纵使功名显达，亦难免祸，汝其远之。”噫！先达观人于微，亦何神哉！

    Explanation

    378

    The translator Li Chengzi said: The old man suddenly appeared and then vanished mysteriously, like a dragon showing only its head and not its tail. Could he be a deity or immortal? In the future, Gengyao will hold a military command and lead troops, striking fear across vast distances, and achieving great fame and glory, all due to the old man's teachings. Tracing his life, he could not rid himself of the trait of pride, and thus brought his own downfall. What more can he complain about? The old man took pride in his role as a teacher and stood up to him for three years. Perhaps his teaching was sufficient, but his influence was still lacking? His final words before parting had deep meaning, but they were taken lightly, which is truly regrettable. Additionally, it is known that Gengyao and my fellow townsman Zhang Wenhe, the chancellor, passed the imperial examinations in the same year and had a very close relationship. Chancellor Wenru once told Wenhe: "This person has a loud and fierce voice, glaring and angry eyes. Even if he achieves great fame and success, he will inevitably bring disaster upon himself. You should keep your distance." Alas! Wise people can discern character from small details, how remarkable is their ability!

    379

    甲与乙为善友
    　　
    　　甲与乙为善友，甲贫而乙富。甲将远出贸易，托家室于乙，乙毅然诺之。甲既去匝月，妻以食用不给，遣子往乙求助。乙冷笑曰：“曩与而翁言，特戏耳。若眷口多人，将仰给于我，来日方长，但供若坐享，虽铜山亦易崩也。请别为计。”子闻言觖望。不得已，又哀恳之，乙拒益力，子怏怏归，返命于母。甲妻叹曰：“今天下所谓金兰之友者，类如此矣！”米罄薪绝，举室愁对，计无所之。忽乙之老仆来，甲妻数其主人负诺之非。

    Explanation

    379

    甲 and 乙 are good friends, with 甲 being poor and 乙 being rich. 甲 is about to go far away for trade and entrusts his family to 乙. 乙 firmly agrees. After 甲 has been gone for a month, his wife finds food and supplies insufficient and sends the son to 乙 for help. 乙 coldly laughs and says, "What I said to your father was just a joke. If you have many dependents, you will rely on me, but there is plenty of time ahead, and to just let you sit and enjoy, even a copper mountain would easily collapse. Please think of another plan." The son hears this and is disappointed. Unwillingly, he pleads again, but 乙 rejects even more strongly. The son reluctantly returns and reports to the mother. 甲's wife sighs and says, "Today's so-called bosom friends are like this." Rice is gone and firewood is exhausted, and the whole family is in despair, with no plan to take. Suddenly, 乙's old servant comes, and 甲's wife accuses her husband of breaking his promise.

    380

    仆亦颇为不平，义形于色，且曰：“人情反覆如此，焉用友？夫人第请息怒，老奴闻夫人一家皆精女红，曷不以针黹生活，较胜求人。”甲妻曰：“汝言固善，奈无资何？”仆曰：“果尔，老奴自有良策。老奴常为主人会计，各店颇蒙取信。夫人应需何物，老奴可暂往各店支取，俟鬻物偿资，亦无不可。”甲妻大喜称谢。遂央仆贷得针线布帛等类，日督妾女子妇诸人壹意刺绣，自旦达夜，不肯少休。每业一物，仆即携去代鬻于乙，乙赏其精巧，不吝厚价。甲一家食用赖以不乏。久之，渐有赢馀，举家甚德老仆，而益不直乙。乙自甲去后，亦绝不过问。

    Explanation

    380

    The servant was also quite discontented, and his righteousness was evident on his face. He said, "Human nature is so fickle; what use are friends? Madam, please calm down. I have heard that your family is skilled in needlework. Why not make a living through needlework, which is better than seeking others?" Madam A replied, "Your words are indeed good, but what if there is no money?" The servant said, "If so, I have a good plan. I often handle the master's accounts and am trusted by all the stores. What items do you need? I can temporarily go to various stores to draw out funds and wait until selling items to repay the funds, and there is no problem." Madam A was greatly pleased and thanked him. She then asked the servant to borrow needles, threads, and fabrics. He supervised the maids, wives, and women to focus on embroidery from morning till night, not resting a little. Each time they finished a piece, he would carry it to sell on behalf of Party B, who appreciated its delicacy and did not spare a high price. The family's food and supplies depended on it. Gradually, they had a surplus, and the whole family greatly appreciated the old servant, becoming even more disdainful of Party B. After Party A left, Party B also completely stopped asking about it.

    381

    初甲出门，同人合权子母，三年，客囊充牣。既归，见家室无恙，衣食丰腴，意是乙所赒恤。询之妻，妻唾且骂曰：“君休矣！君若徒恃金兰之友，则一家之骨不填沟壑也几希矣！”乃痛数乙所为，并颂仆德，备述缕覼。甲不胜诧异，将诣乙诘责。乙见甲归，大喜，执手叙阔，情谊殷拳。甲忿不能遏，作色曰：“别后以家室相累，今幸不致饿莩，微君之惠不至此！”乙笑曰：“君疑仆耶诚然。老仆之代夫人经营者，皆鄙人之所筹画而指使者也。

    Explanation

    381

    Initially, Jia left home, and together with others, he managed the principal and interest. After three years, his travel bag was full. Upon returning, he found his family safe and well-fed, and he thought it was due to Yi's charity. He asked his wife, who spat and cursed, saying, "You're done! If you only relied on your sworn brother, our family would have been dead long ago!" She then bitterly recounted Yi's deeds, praising his virtues, and detailed everything. Jia was greatly surprised and decided to go to Yi to confront him. When Yi saw Jia return, he was very happy, grasped his hand, and talked about their long separation, showing deep affection. Jia, unable to suppress his anger, said sternly, "After we parted, you took care of my family. Now I'm glad they didn't starve, thanks to your kindness!" Yi laughed and said, "Do you doubt me? Indeed, it is true. The old servant who managed things for you, all the planning and directing were done by me."

    382

    鄙意如夫人暨诸弱息皆在妙龄，君既远出，举家无主，若使坐食偷安，反恐逸荡生事。故藉针黹使之作苦，闲束身心，不有以难之，则有所恃而业不专，又高其值而利诱之，则更有所贪而益忘倦。仆之为君谋者不可谓不忠矣，岂真需绣饰、藏为玩好者耶？乃使左右舁一箱至，见频年所购各物堆积其中，灿然如新。顾谓甲曰：“仆留此实无所用，请仍携归，俟女公子迨吉，小助妆奁可也。”甲至此始知乙用心之深，用情之挚，把臂痛哭，再拜谢过。归述于妻妾子女，始各恍然，无不感激涕零。嗟乎！如乙之于甲，是真不愧为友矣。安得今天下所谓金兰之友者尽如是耶！
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：我闻乙平生轻财重义，甲则不轻取予，皆正人君子也。至今两姓子孙蕃衍昌盛，世为婚好，休戚相关，有逾骨肉焉。此事惜传者逸其姓名。然管鲍、羊左、范张诸公之后又得甲乙二人，使友道之中添兹一段佳话，快甚快甚

    Explanation

    382

    My humble opinion is that since the lady and her young children are all in their youthful years, and you are away, the whole family is without a head. If they were to live off others' labor without effort, they would likely become idle and cause trouble. Therefore, I have had them engage in needlework to make them work hard and restrain their minds and bodies. If there is nothing to challenge them, they might rely on something else and not focus on their work. By increasing the value of the work and tempting them with profit, they might become greedy and forget their fatigue. My advice for you cannot be considered unloyal; do I truly need embroidered decorations to be kept as playthings? I then had someone bring a box containing various items purchased over the years, which were still new and shiny. I told person A, "I have no use for these items. Please take them back, and when it is appropriate, they can help with your daughter's dowry." Person A then realized the depth of person B's intentions and the sincerity of their feelings, and they embraced each other, wept bitterly, and bowed twice to apologize. Upon returning home, they told their wives, concubines, and children, and they all suddenly understood, moved to tears of gratitude. How wonderful! As person B is to person A, they truly do not disappoint in being friends. How I wish that all close friends in the world could be as true as person B and person A!

    The commentator said: I have heard that person B is not concerned with wealth but values righteousness, and person A does not easily take or give, both being upright and virtuous men. To this day, the descendants of both families have prospered and flourished, marrying each other's families for generations, sharing the same joys and sorrows, even closer than blood relations. It is a pity that the names of those involved are not recorded. Nevertheless, after the examples of close friendship such as Guan and Bao, Yang and Zuo, and Fan and Zhang, we now have person A and person B, adding a new chapter to the story of true friendship, which is very pleasing indeed.

    383

    王素芳
    　　
    　　清江名妓王素芳，字晓秋。尝薄暮假寐，忽一女郎披帷入，年可十八九，衣裙钗饰皆吴下妆束，来坐榻上。素芳起问邦族，女遽捉其臂，冷如鬼手馨，拽往外走曰：“汝随我去。”素芳姑从之。甫及户，见门外立一鬼，头与身各长尺许，戴皂冠峨峨然，目睒内向，素芳大骇踣地。家人闻声，毕集问讯。素芳神情恍惚，但见女在旁，言要报冤。素芳谓素不相识，何报怨之与有？女曰：“汝不知耶？汝前世广陵汪氏，家素丰，十八岁举于乡，与妾中表。妾幼失怙恃，寄养汝家。年及笄，汝以诗相挑，谓愿效玉镜台故事。妾怜才心切，雅不欲拒。从此情均伉俪，酬唱甚多。后汝从亲命，另婚他氏，遂背前盟。亲迎之日，妾忿乳药而死。如此负心，我岂汝恕耶？”素芳谓前世事非所能知，可求见谅。

    Explanation

    383

    A Courtesan Named Wang Sufang

    In Qingjiang, there was a renowned courtesan named Wang Sufang, whose courtesy name was Xiaoxiu. One evening, as she was about to doze off, a young lady suddenly entered by pulling aside the curtain. She was around eighteen or nineteen years old, dressed in the distinctive attire and adorned with the jewelry of women from Wu. She sat down on the bed. Wang Sufang asked about her family, but the woman quickly grabbed her arm, which felt as cold as a ghost's hand. She dragged her outside, saying, "You must come with me." Wang Sufang reluctantly followed. Just as they reached the door, she saw a ghost standing outside, its head and body each about a foot long, wearing a tall black hat, and its eyes glancing inward. Wang Sufang was so terrified that she fell to the ground. Her family heard the noise and gathered to ask what was happening. Wang Sufang was in a daze, but she saw the woman beside her, who said she wanted to seek revenge. Wang Sufang replied, "I don't know you. How can you have any grievances against me?" The woman said, "Do you not know? In a past life, you were a member of the Wang family from Guangling, a wealthy family. At eighteen, you passed the local examinations, and I was your cousin. I lost my parents at a young age and was raised in your family. When I reached the age of marriage, you courted me with poetry, saying you wished to follow the example of the Jade Mirror Pavilion. I was deeply moved by your talent and did not wish to refuse. From then on, our feelings were equal to those of a married couple, and we exchanged many poems. Later, you followed your family's command and married another family, thus betraying your previous vows. On the day of your wedding, I took poison out of resentment and died. Such a betrayal of heart, how could I forgive you?" Wang Sufang said, "Past lives are not something we can know, but can you seek forgiveness?"

    384

    正辩让间，忽一白发老者衣冠朱绂而至，谓是素芳前世之父，便谓女曰：“汝二人事，曲故在彼，但汝亦不能无过。当日老夫若知其由，必不另为议婚。稽渠前世册籍，禄寿俱丰，坐是罚削殆尽。以老夫平日为人，不应绝嗣，姑留为延一线，至二十三岁即已短折，今世谴变女身，且堕落为娼。阴曹法律至公，汝亦可以无憾。况渠所私处子尚有五人，不独负汝，不过汝死非命为可怜耳。然报应已彰，既往不咎，汝亦不必过嬲，可随我去。”女不肯。初老者来时，从一苍头，便命留事女郎，劝其速去。老者既去，苍头便从旁苦口劝解，素芳亦再三哀免，女坚执前说。素芳无可如何，忽思得一调停之法，乃谓女曰：“汝言我前世相负，我至今世悔之已晚，但我已贱而为娼，并无所归，薄命极矣！此后誓不从良，情愿奉汝为婿，设主祀之，以赎前愆，何如？”女沉思良久乃诺。素芳遂设女郎木主，夫事之，后果不

    Explanation

    384

    In the midst of a debate, suddenly an elderly man with white hair, dressed in formal attire and wearing a red sash, appeared. He claimed to be Su Fang's father from a previous life and addressed the woman, saying, "The fault lies with the two of you, but you too cannot be without blame. If I had known the reason back then, I would not have arranged another marriage. Upon examining his records from a previous life, it was found that he had both wealth and longevity, but as a result of his actions, almost all were punished and reduced. Considering my usual character, I should not allow the line to end, so I temporarily left to prolong the line, but by the age of twenty-three, he had already died prematurely. In this life, he is punished by being reborn as a woman and descending to prostitution. The laws of the underworld are extremely fair, so you can also have no regrets. Especially since he had five unmarried girls, not only did he wrong you, but it is also pitiful that you died an unnatural death. However, retribution has been made clear, and past wrongs will not be punished. You also do not need to persist in your demands, you can come with me." The woman refused. When the old man first arrived, he was accompanied by a servant who was instructed to stay and care for the woman, urging her to leave quickly. After the old man left, the servant earnestly tried to persuade her, and Su Fang also repeatedly pleaded for forgiveness, but the woman firmly adhered to her previous statement. Su Fang had no choice but to suddenly think of a way to mediate and said to the woman, "You say I wronged you in a previous life, and I regret it now, but it is too late. But I am now a prostitute and have no place to go, my fate is extremely unfortunate! From now on, I swear not to remarry, and I am willing to serve you as a husband, setting up a memorial to atone for my past sins, how about that?" The woman thought deeply for a long time and finally agreed. Su Fang then set up a memorial tablet for the woman and treated her as a husband, but the result was not clear.

    385

    复为祟。此咸丰己未年事。毗陵徐镜三明府游清江，见素芳自作笔记，中载前世与女郎唱和诗词甚多。其时素芳年裁三十许耳。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：老者所言，亦殊娓娓动听，乃鬼坚执前说，竟不遵命；及素芳许以夫事，设主祀之，誓不从良，鬼沉思良久乃诺，岂犹念前世恩爱而不忍割耶？吁嗟！鬼亦可谓多情矣！然素芳所自调停，殊觉曲而奇、奇而当。

    Explanation

    385

    The ghost continued to cause trouble. This was an event from the year Jiwu in the Xianfeng era. Mister Xu Jing-san of Piling visited Qingjiang and saw Su Fang's self-written notes, which contained many poems written in harmony with a lady from a previous life. At that time, Su Fang was just around thirty years old.

    The town chronicler said: The old man's words were also very charming and engaging, but the ghost insisted on its previous statement and did not obey the command; when Su Fang agreed to marry her and established a shrine to worship her, swearing never to marry again, the ghost thought deeply for a long time before finally agreeing. Could it be that she still remembered the love from a past life and could not bear to part? Ah! The ghost can also be called a lover! However, Su Fang's own arrangement was particularly subtle, strange, and fitting.

    386

    素芳说梦
    　　
    　　镜三又言素芳笔记载一梦，甚奇。咸丰庚申暮春，倚枕假寐，见前苍头来言，今日公子初度，家人设筵为寿，请归受贺。身不觉随苍头出，乘舆将至一处，高门巨第，苍头导之升堂。内设三席，中席则前所见老者与一媪并肩上坐，西席处女六人，女郎预焉。己便欲阑入西席，苍头曳其裾，使独坐东席。水陆杂陈，备极丰腆，自顾己身，居然男子而衣冠也者。俄见一壮岁秀才稽首筵下，既退，又一老妪入，年可古稀，裣衽伏地，且哭且诉，音甚颟顸，不辨何语。心颇厌其絮聒，起身出席欲麾之去，妪以臂相格，忽然蹉跌，头触屏风上，砰訇有声，顿醒。后悟初度为前世冥寿，中席乃前世父母，西席乃前世所私六处子，壮岁秀才及老妪乃前世妻子也。素芳醒后，发内甚痛，摸之一瘤坟起，即梦中屏风所触者。使镜三验之，尚未尽消。

    Explanation

    386

    Sufang's Dream

    Jing San also recounted that Sufang had recorded a dream, which was quite extraordinary. In the late spring of the year 1850 during the Xianfeng era, Sufang was leaning against a pillow and dozing lightly when a servant approached and said, "Today is the birthday of the young master, and the family has set up a banquet to celebrate. Please return to receive the congratulations." Without realizing it, Sufang followed the servant out and was taken by carriage to a grand mansion. The servant led her into a hall where three seats were arranged. In the central seat sat the elderly man and an old woman whom Sufang had seen before. In the western seat were six maiden women, among whom was Sufang herself. Sufang wanted to join the western seat but the servant pulled her skirt, making her sit alone on the eastern seat. A variety of dishes, both from land and sea, were served, and the feast was abundant and luxurious. Looking at herself, Sufang realized she was a man in formal attire. Soon, a young scholar bowed deeply before the banquet table and then left. An elderly woman entered, around seventy years old, and knelt down, clasping her sleeves over her chest. She cried and spoke, but her voice was unclear and indistinct, making it hard to understand what she was saying. Sufang was very annoyed by her incessant chatter and stood up, wanting to shoo her away. The elderly woman blocked her with her arm, and suddenly Sufang stumbled, hitting her head on the screen, which made a loud noise, and she woke up abruptly. Later, Sufang realized that the birthday celebration was a posthumous birthday from a past life, the central seat was occupied by her past-life parents, the western seat was occupied by the six maiden women from her past life, and the young scholar and the elderly woman were her past-life wife and children. After waking up, Sufang felt a severe pain in her hair and touched a lump that had formed, which was the spot where she had hit her head on the screen in the dream. She asked Jing San to examine it, and he found that the lump had not yet completely disappeared.

    387

    吾乡某太史
    　　
    　　京师都总管庙，其神为狐族之长。吾乡某太史登第时，年甫逾冠，少年选事，戏为文一通，就庙焚之。谓闻尔族灵与人同，野乘所载仙缘不少，心窃羡之。鲰生独居京邸，旷然寡俦，如不以尘俗见弃，愿赐系援，传之后世，亦为美谈云云。越日薄暮，一叟款关来谒，自言胡姓，昨蒙垂赐佳文，不以异类见斥，曷胜荣宠，今特送儿女子来，望君始终温煦之，幸甚。即以手招之曰：“来来，儿曹可善事贵人，吾去矣。”太史闻言，仓促不能致词。但见粲者林立，九女二男，女固妖娆，男亦婉娈，目炫神摇，不能自主。由此闭门谢客，镇日与群美周旋，颇幸奇遇。匝月后，精神怠尪，罢于奔命；而群美轮替值宿，苦无虚日，默自危虑。欲掬心告哀，又恐反遭侮虐。再四筹画，乃背书一疏，独诣前门关帝庙祈祷，大略控都总管纵妖作祟，求神驱佑。

    Explanation

    387

    A Certain Grand Historian from My Hometown

    In the capital, there is a temple dedicated to the Lord of the Capital, whose deity is the leader of the fox clan. When a certain Grand Historian from my hometown passed the imperial examinations, he was just past the age of twenty. As a young man selected for official duties, he playfully wrote an essay and burned it at the temple. He said that he had heard the spirits of your clan are the same as humans, and there are many accounts of divine connections in the records. He secretly admired this. Living alone in the capital, he felt lonely and without companions. If you do not disdain his earthly nature, he wished to be granted assistance, so that it might be passed down to future generations and become a beautiful tale. The next day at dusk, an old man knocked on the door to visit, saying his name was Hu. He said that he had received the Grand Historian's generous gift of a fine essay the day before, and did not disdain him as an outsider. How could he bear such honor! Now he specially sends his children and grandchildren, hoping the Grand Historian will always treat them kindly. Very fortunate. He immediately gestured and said, "Come, come, my children can serve the noble person well. I am leaving now." The Grand Historian, hearing this, was in a hurry and could not reply. He only saw beautiful women standing in a row, nine girls and two boys. The girls were indeed charming, and the boys were also graceful. He was blinded and dizzy, unable to control himself. Therefore, he closed the door to guests and spent the whole day with the group of beauties, quite fortunate to have such an encounter. After a month, his spirit was exhausted, exhausted from running around. And the group of beauties took turns to stay overnight, hardly had a day off. He silently worried. He wanted to pour out his heart in sorrow, fearing that he might be further abused. He thought about it repeatedly and then wrote a petition. Alone, he went to the Guan Di Temple in front of the palace to pray, roughly accusing the Lord of the Capital of allowing demons to cause mischief, and requested the gods to drive them away and protect him.

    388

    是夜就寝，见金甲神拘至一处，宫殿崇閎，上坐一人，气象尊严；左右唱名，令跽墀下。旋又唱胡某名，便闻银铛声响，一人跽于右侧，睨之，即是胡叟。上坐叱问：“老狐何故纵妖作祟？”叟与太史互相争诉。上坐怒责太史不知自重，殊玷清班。姑念平日尚无大过，暂从宽典，急宜治任归里，毋得逗留辇下。言讫，叱之令去。霍然惊寤，见残灯犹明，群美不知何时俱已遁去。窃自庆慰，而回忆所梦，又复骇汗如雨。向晨，即见叟拄杖蹩躄来前，戟手指而数之曰：“无理、无情莫汝为甚！汝初以姻好是求，予故以儿女相托。纵使反悔，亦应熟商，何得率尔妄渎上神？幸蒙圣帝仁明，仅予薄谴，臀股笞责三百，肉尽见骨，痛彻于心；合家老幼罚戍西羌三年。俟赦归，再与计较也！”言毕，恨恨而去。太史骇惧，从此告归故乡，不敢复作春明之梦矣。

    Explanation

    388

    That night, as I lay down to sleep, I saw a god in golden armor lead me to a place where the palace was grand and magnificent. On the throne sat a person of majestic and solemn appearance. The attendants called out names, ordering me to kneel at the steps below. Soon after, the name of Hu was called, and I heard the sound of silver bells ringing. A person knelt on the right side, glancing at me, and it was Old Hu. The person on the throne angrily asked, "Why did you, old fox, allow a demon to cause mischief?" Old Hu and the historian argued back and forth. The person on the throne rebuked the historian for not valuing himself, severely tarnishing the reputation of the court. However, considering there were no serious offenses in the past, leniency was shown for now. The historian was ordered to prepare immediately to return home and not to linger in the capital. After the words were spoken, the historian was ordered to leave. Suddenly, I awoke from my dream, seeing the dim light still burning, and the beautiful women had all disappeared without knowing when. I secretly felt relieved and happy, but recalling the dream, I was once again drenched in cold sweat. Early in the morning, I saw Old Hu come limping forward with a cane, pointing at me and scolding, "You are unreasonable and heartless! You initially sought a marriage alliance, so I entrusted my children to you. Even if you regret it, you should have discussed it thoroughly. How could you rashly offend the gods? Fortunately, I received the benevolence of the Holy Emperor, who only gave me mild punishment—three hundred lashes on the buttocks, flesh completely stripped off, bones exposed, pain penetrating deeply into the heart; the entire family, young and old, was punished by being sent to serve in Western Qiang for three years. Wait for pardon and return, and we will settle accounts later!" After he finished speaking, he left angrily. The historian was terrified and decided to return home, never daring to dream of the capital again.

    389

    里乘子曰：书生少登科第，不知自重。方其为文缔仙缘，果见粲者林立，未尝不自幸，从心所欲，得此奇遇；及罢于奔命，又不敢掬心告哀，乃背疏祈渎神圣，尤属糊涂胆大，真非人意料所及。观神圣所判，虽老吏断狱，亦不能如此精当。姑念平日尚无大过，暂从宽典，此正是默袒书生处。得免冥责，诚为幸事。而老叟戟手数责之词，情理兼尽，太史纵百口亦难置喙。乘一时之高兴，误毕世之功名，归老山林，春明梦断。所谓妖由人兴，孽由自作，前车可鉴，可不慎欤！愿以告天下之少年选事者。

    Explanation

    389

    里乘子曰：书生少登科第，不知自重。方其为文缔仙缘，果见粲者林立，未尝不自幸，从心所欲，得此奇遇；及罢于奔命，又不敢掬心告哀，乃背疏祈渎神圣，尤属糊涂胆大，真非人意料所及。观神圣所判，虽老吏断狱，亦不能如此精当。姑念平日尚无大过，暂从宽典，此正是默袒书生处。得免冥责，诚为幸事。而老叟戟手数责之词，情理兼尽，太史纵百口亦难置喙。乘一时之高兴，误毕世之功名，归老山林，春明梦断。所谓妖由人兴，孽由自作，前车可鉴，可不慎欤！愿以告天下之少年选事者。

    Li Chengzi said: A scholar who has recently passed the imperial examinations, lacking self-respect, when engaged in literary pursuits and making connections with the divine, indeed saw many beautiful and talented individuals, never failing to feel fortunate, acting according to his heart's desire, and gaining such a strange encounter; when exhausted from constant service, he dared not pour out his heart in sorrow, but instead wrote a petition and prayed to the sacred, which was particularly foolish and presumptuous, truly beyond what people could have imagined. Observing the judgment of the divine, even an experienced official deciding a case could not have been so precise. Considering that there were no great offenses in the past, a temporary leniency was shown, which was the place where the divine silently supported the scholar. Being spared from punishment in the afterlife, truly a fortunate thing. The old man's words of stern reprimand, both emotion and reason fully expressed, even if the historian were to speak a hundred times, he would find it hard to interject. Taking advantage of a momentary pleasure, ruining one's lifelong achievements, returning to a life in the mountains and forests, the dream of success in the capital city broken. As the saying goes, evil arises from human actions, misdeeds are self-inflicted, past events can serve as a warning, how can one not be cautious? I wish to inform the young people who are selecting their careers.

    390

    指腹为婚
    　　
    　　池阳有姊妹，幼相亲爱者，既嫁，各有娠，指腹约曰：“均是男也女也则已，脱一雌一雄，当缔姻好。”佥曰：“诺。”且誓之。既产，姊雄而妹雌，大喜，各申旧约，南山不移矣。亡何，姊夫为仇家牵讼，产业荡然，抑郁而卒。姊无以自存，尝携子往依妹。时妹已有子入塾，即留姊子伴读。先是约婚时固无媒妁，不数年两小俱已成人，妹夫某乙憎姊贫，谋与妹讳其前说，以女字同邑富家儿，嫁有日矣，而女实不知有旧约也。妹使姊为女作嫁衣，姊知妹负约而不敢言，对女时潸潸泪下。女察知有异，叩之曰：“姨与母兄弟也，儿视姨犹母，姨亦合视儿犹女。

    Explanation

    390

    Betrothed in the Womb

    In the town of Chi Yang, there were two sisters who had been close since childhood. After they were married, each became pregnant. While still pregnant, they made a pact by pointing to their bellies, saying, "If both children are boys or both are girls, then there is nothing more to it. But if one is a boy and the other a girl, then we shall form a marital alliance." All present said, "Agreed," and they swore an oath. When they gave birth, the elder sister had a son and the younger a daughter. They were greatly pleased, and both kept their old promise, as unshakable as Mount Nanshan.

    Not long after, the elder sister’s husband was dragged into a lawsuit by his enemies, and his property was completely destroyed. He died in despair. The elder sister had no means to support herself and so took her son to live with her younger sister. At that time, the younger sister already had a son who had entered school, and so she kept the elder sister’s son to study alongside her own.

    Originally, their marriage had been made without the involvement of matchmakers. Within a few years, both children had grown up. The younger sister’s husband, Mr. Yi, resented the elder sister’s poverty and conspired with his wife to conceal the previous agreement, arranging for their daughter to marry a wealthy man from the same county. The wedding was already set for a date, though the daughter was unaware of the old pact.

    The younger sister had her sister make the wedding clothes. The elder sister knew that her sister had broken the agreement, but dared not speak of it, and wept silently when she was with the girl. The girl sensed something was amiss and asked, "Aunt and Mother are siblings. I regard Aunt as my mother. Aunt should also regard me as her daughter."

    391

    儿闻男婚女嫁，人之大伦，儿嫁有日，此一生大事，姨当为儿喜，何哀之甚也？敢请其说。”姊第摇首而不肯言。女固叩之，乃拭泪喟然曰：“儿不知耶？儿固吾子妇也。今已矣，夫复何言？”女闻之大惊，穷诘得其巅末。沉吟久之，曰：“噫！我知之矣。姨诘朝须托故暂回家摒挡，倘父母使人招之，固辞以疾，俟儿自来姨家再作计较。”姊如女言，诘朝与妹别，坚订三日必返。妹以女嫁期迫，须姊代为理妆，三日不至，叠使人招之，俱辞以疾。固招不至，妹心益急，女乘间曰：“阿姨素怜爱儿，非儿自往招之不可。”妹笑曰：“老贱人自抬身价，须小妮子自往捉来。

    Explanation

    391

    The daughter heard that marriage for men and women is the great norm of human relations, and that her own wedding was approaching, which was a major event in her life. She thought that her aunt should be happy for her, so she asked, "Why are you so sad?" She dared to ask the reason. Her sister just shook her head and refused to speak. The daughter persisted in asking, and her sister wiped her tears and sighed, saying, "Don't you know? My daughter is your future wife. Now it is too late; what more can I say?" The daughter was greatly surprised and probed further to get the details. After pondering for a long time, she said, "Ah! I understand now. Auntie must make an excuse to return home temporarily to prepare. If your parents send someone to call you, firmly decline citing illness, and wait until I come to your house to discuss further." The sister followed the daughter's advice, and the next morning she parted ways with her younger sister, firmly promising to return in three days. The younger sister was in a hurry because of the wedding and needed her sister to help with the preparations. However, the sister did not arrive in three days, and the younger sister repeatedly sent someone to call her, but she all declined citing illness. Despite the firm attempts to call her, the sister did not come, and the younger sister became increasingly anxious. The daughter took the opportunity to say, "Auntie always loves and cares for me; only I can go to call her." The younger sister laughed and said, "The old woman is raising her status; only the young girl can go to fetch her."

    392

    然期迫矣，儿须速偕阿姨归，勿贪戏也。”女曰：“诺。”亟怀金而往。访知近村有贫妇新分娩，女以钱十千付姨，悄往说贫妇，愿假婴儿代哺一月，姊抱婴儿归，竟无知者。女计父必自来督趣，令姨闭门以待。妹以女连日不归，果使乙自往趣之。乙至叩门，逾时始开，亟问姊曰：“吾女何在？”姊笑以手反指曰：“坐床者非耶？”乙至房中，见女蓬首拥被坐床上，解衣哺儿乳。见父故作羞缩状，面壁默默不作一语。乙瞪视失色，顿足叹曰：“奈何！奈何！”急趋而归，具以告妻，相对愁叹而已。一时里党传为笑柄，富家闻而耻之，亟请媒退婚，索还聘物。

    Explanation

    392

    The deadline is pressing, so the child must quickly return with Auntie, without indulging in play." The girl said, "Yes." She hurriedly carried the gold and went. She inquired and found out that there was a poor woman in the nearby village who had just given birth. The girl gave ten thousand coins to Auntie and quietly went to speak to the poor woman, wishing to borrow an infant to nurse for a month. The sister carried the infant back, and nobody knew. The girl calculated that her father would surely come to supervise, so she ordered Auntie to close the door and wait. The sister-in-law, because the girl had not returned for several days, indeed sent B to go and urge her. B arrived and knocked on the door, but it did not open until after a long time. He quickly asked the sister, "Where is my daughter?" The sister smiled and pointed back with her hand, saying, "Isn't she sitting on the bed?" B entered the room and saw the girl with disheveled hair and wrapped in a blanket sitting on the bed, undressing to nurse the child. He saw her father, who pretended to be shy and withdrawn, facing the wall silently without speaking a word. B stared in shock, stamped his feet, and sighed, "What can be done! What can be done!" He hurriedly returned home and told his wife. They only looked at each other in sorrow and sighed. For a time, the neighbors passed it around as a laughing stock. The wealthy heard about it and were ashamed, quickly asked a matchmaker to break off the engagement, and demanded the return of the betrothal gifts.

    393

    某乙夫妻以女贻门户羞，暂令住姊家，俟弥月徐为之计。比及匝月，贫妇自往索儿。女诧曰：“汝以十千钱鬻我，何又索还耶？”贫妇怒曰：“汝原约假我子代哺一月，焉得不还我！岂鬻子者耶？”彼此反唇相稽，女固靳不肯还，贫妇益怒，亟鸣鼓诉之官。官拘女至，女乃泣陈所以，官肃然称叹曰：“以智全节，奇女子也！可谓诡而不离于正。”拘乙与姊子至，见姊子温文尔雅，询知尚在塾读书，大喜，叱乙曰：“汝以一女而字二夫，罪不容逭。姑念汝女贤，贷汝过。”又指姊子谓乙曰：“此诚快婿，亟归，好助奁资为二人合卺，以赎汝过，何如？”乙叩头唯唯，答称愿从尊命。又令以婴儿还贫妇，远近颂神明焉。

    Explanation

    393

    A couple named Yi, whose daughter brought shame to their family, temporarily placed her at her sister's house, planning to arrange for her marriage after a month. By the end of the month, the poor woman came to claim her child. The daughter was surprised and said, "You sold me for ten thousand coins, why are you now demanding I be returned?" The poor woman angrily replied, "You originally agreed to lend me your child to nurse for a month; how can I not return her to you! Are you selling your own child?" They argued back and forth, and the daughter, being stubborn, refused to return the child. The poor woman became increasingly angry and quickly appealed to the local official. The official summoned the daughter and, upon hearing her explanation, praised her, saying, "To preserve one's virtue with wisdom, she is a remarkable woman! She is indeed clever but remains true to what is right." The official then summoned Yi and his sister's son, who was gentle and refined, and learned that he was still studying at a private school. The official was pleased and scolded Yi, saying, "You have one daughter married to two husbands; this crime cannot be excused. However, considering your daughter's virtue, I will forgive your mistake." The official then pointed to his sister's son and said to Yi, "He is indeed an ideal son-in-law. Hurry home, prepare the dowry, and arrange the wedding for the two of them to atone for your mistake. What do you say?" Yi bowed repeatedly and agreed to follow the official's command. The official then ordered the baby to be returned to the poor woman, and both near and far praised the official's wisdom and fairness.

    394

    里乘子曰：此事吾乡啧啧称道。闻其夫妇至今尚在。其夫已贡成均，为一乡祭酒；生数子，亦有声胶庠。而富家自遭兵燹，竟荡然无存矣。即此亦可见天报贤女，而货财之不可恃也。凡憎贫者，尚其鉴之。
    　　
    　　老圃某翁
    　　
    　　老圃某翁好道，断酒茹素，鳏居六十馀年，一乡称善人焉。尝暑夜就寝，忽闻窸窣声，见无数小人从门罅入，联臂登榻，撼翁骂曰：“嗜杀贼，休安枕高卧！吾侪隐忍数十年，今决不汝恕矣！”言毕，经上翁身，自首至足，跆藉不留馀隙。或擢发，或捋须，或撮唇捉鼻、箝耳扪目；甚至有挠其股者，有呵其肢者，有嘬其肤者，有吮其乳而噬其脐者。翁痛痒备至，欲言而口不能开，欲动而手足如有所缚。微睨其人，大者长尺许，次者或七八寸、五六寸不等，有老有少，有男有女；有面目娟好美如冠玉者，有环眼虬髯狰狞凶恶者，憧憧往来，如傀儡然。

    Explanation

    394

    Li Chengzi said: This story is often praised in my hometown. I hear that the couple are still alive to this day. The husband has been admitted to the imperial academy and now serves as the head of the local school; he has several sons, who are also notable in the academies. However, the wealthy family, after suffering from war and fire, was completely destroyed. From this alone, one can see that heaven rewards virtuous women, and that wealth and property are not to be relied upon. All who despise the poor, take this as a warning.

    Old Farmer Master Wang

    Old Farmer Master Wang loved the Way, abstained from alcohol and ate only vegetarian food, and lived as a widower for over sixty years. He was regarded as a virtuous man throughout the village. One summer night, as he was about to sleep, he suddenly heard a rustling sound. He saw countless little figures entering through the crack in the door, linking arms and climbing onto the bed. They shook the old man and shouted at him: “You cruel killer, do not lie comfortably in bed! We have endured for decades, but now we will not forgive you!” After saying this, they climbed over his body, from head to foot, trampling without leaving any space. Some pulled his hair, some stroked his beard, some pinched his lips and grabbed his nose, some pinched his ears and touched his eyes; even some scratched his thigh, some blew on his limbs, some sucked his skin, and some licked his breast and bit his navel. The old man suffered extreme pain and itching, wanting to speak but unable to open his mouth, wanting to move but feeling as if his hands and feet were bound. Glancing at them, he saw that the largest were about a foot long, the next were about seven or eight inches, or five or six inches in length. There were old and young, men and women; some had beautiful, delicate features as fine as jade, while others had round eyes, twisted beards, and fierce, evil expressions. They moved back and forth in a crowd, like puppets.

    395

    鸡鸣始下榻由门罅去。如是匝月，不堪其扰。自问平生尚无大过，不知是何冤孽。闻某寺有老僧道行甚高，往求纤解。僧命翁傍坐，自趺坐禅榻，手诀诵咒。少顷，小人果络绎至，膜拜，齐伏座下。老僧喝曰：“何物么魔，敢祟善士？无说则死！”小人诉曰：“尊师听者，吾侪乃舆父亭长之俦，伏处田间，自甘蔬水，与物无忤，与人无争。不图某翁包藏祸心，视吾侪恨若仇寇，数十年来，屠戮何止十族。倘再束手听其鱼肉，日复一日，我子孙必无噍类。为此召号同侪，决意扑杀此獠，庶可消宿怨而杜后患。所谓孤注一掷，势不两立，诚出于不得已也。

    Explanation

    395

    At dawn, a ghostly figure began to descend from the bed and slip out through a crack in the door. For a whole month, the disturbance was unbearable. He questioned himself, wondering if he had committed any great sins in his life, and did not know what kind of karmic retribution he had to face. He heard that there was an elderly monk with high moral and spiritual cultivation in a certain temple, so he went to seek a solution. The monk instructed him to sit beside him, and then sat in meditation on the meditation mat, performing hand gestures and reciting mantras. After a short while, the ghosts indeed arrived one after another, knelt down, and bowed before the monk. The monk shouted, "What kind of demon are you, daring to torment a virtuous person? If you do not speak, you will die!" The ghosts pleaded, "Respected teacher, listen to us. We are the kind of people like carriage drivers and village constables, living humbly in the fields, content with simple food and water, causing no harm to others or quarreling with people. We never imagined that this old man harbored evil intentions, viewing us as enemies, and over the past few decades, he has slaughtered countless families. If we continue to submit and let him abuse us, day by day, our descendants will surely perish. Therefore, we have called upon our fellow ghosts, determined to kill this villain, in order to eliminate our long-standing grievances and prevent future troubles. This is a case of putting all our eggs in one basket, and we cannot coexist. It is truly a matter of necessity."

    396

    尊师座前不作诳语，请问某翁更有何说？”盖翁素性勤俭，平生所种瓜豆各菜，每日灌溉外，必逐柯一一谛视，遇虫辄歼除之，恐其有损根蒂花叶也。翁闻言恍然大悟，遂向老僧白其故，且告之悔。老僧嗟叹久之，为强辨曰：“汝曹所言是矣。然老衲闻，近村某甲某乙等俱与翁同业，其畦陇较多于翁，性亦蠢恶，平日遇汝曹摧残自必尤甚，汝曹何悉不与校，独与翁作难？得勿欺善而畏恶乎？”对曰：“非也。

    Explanation

    396

    Before the revered teacher, I do not speak falsely. May I ask if there is anything more to be said about the old man?" The old man is inherently frugal and thrifty. Throughout his life, the vegetables and fruits he cultivates are watered daily, and he meticulously examines each plant, promptly eliminating any pests to prevent damage to the roots, stems, and leaves. Upon hearing this, the old man suddenly realized the truth and explained the reason to the old monk, confessing his remorse. The old monk sighed for a long time and forcefully argued, "What you say is true. However, I have heard that nearby villagers like 甲 and 乙, who are in the same line of work as you, have more fields than you and are also foolish and wicked. They would surely be even more cruel to you in their daily interactions. Why do you not confront them, but instead make it difficult for this old man? Are you not taking advantage of his kindness and fearing his evil?" The old man replied, "No, that is not the case."

    397

    如甲乙等生平作恶较多，他日森罗殿上自有人与之对簿，吾侪何必作难！若某翁者，戒荤慕道，一乡称为善人，以善人而戕害物命，罪宜加凡人一等。《春秋》所以责备贤者也。吾侪修怨于翁，正是此意。”老僧点首叹曰：“善哉！善哉！”因劝翁从此改业：“待老衲为汝解脱，何如？”翁合掌答曰：“幸甚！”老僧乃宣谕曰：“老僧闻冤宜解不宜结。某翁今已忏悔，痛捐旧业，但汝曹既已怛化，何能再生？老衲愿唪经诵咒，超度汝曹，听其湿化道中，流转轮回，或化蝏蜨翔戏山林，或变鱼蝦泳游水国，各寻乐趣，永遂生机；尽释怨尤，莫事缠扰。汝曹有知，疾如律令者。”小人闻之，皆大欢喜，伏地膜拜，同宣佛号而灭。某翁此后不复从事锄筿，惟织席编屦以终馀年。此庸通和尚言者。

    Explanation

    397

    Those who have committed many evil deeds in their lives, such as Jia and Yi, will one day face judgment in the Hall of Ten Thousand Laws, where there will be someone to hold them accountable. Why should we make things difficult for ourselves? As for a certain old man, he abstained from meat and sought the Dao, and was called a virtuous person throughout the village. Yet, as a virtuous person, he harmed living beings, and his punishment should be one level higher than that of ordinary people. The Spring and Autumn Annals criticize even the virtuous for their failings. We hold a grudge against this old man, which aligns with this principle. The old monk nodded and sighed, saying, "Good! Good!" He then advised the old man to change his profession: "Let me, the humble monk, liberate you from your karma. How does that sound?" The old man clasped his hands and replied, "Thank goodness!" The old monk then proclaimed, "I have heard that grudges should be resolved, not held onto. Now that the old man has repented and deeply renounced his old ways, but since you have already undergone the transformation of the soul, how can you be reborn? I vow to chant scriptures and recite mantras to liberate you all. Allow yourselves to be reborn in the cycle of rebirth, perhaps as butterflies playing in the mountains and forests, or as fish and shrimp swimming in the aquatic realm. Each of you may find your own joy and continue to thrive; fully release all grievances and avoid being entangled in troubles. If you have any awareness, let it be done as commanded." Upon hearing this, the souls were all filled with joy, prostrated themselves, paid homage, and chanted the Buddha's name together before passing away. After this, the old man no longer engaged in the act of weeding, but instead wove mats and made sandals to spend his remaining years. This is what the ordinary monk said.

    398

    里乘子曰：或疑蠢尔微虫，焉知报怨？大约禅师饶舌，故假此恢诡之谈以警世耳。予谓不然，杨雀宋蚁，载之典籍，非虫鸟之报德者乎？报德既有可证，报怨有何足怪？况出自善人，罪宜加等；责备贤者，更为近理。子何疑焉？
    　　
    　　产蛇
    　　
    　　合肥李季荃督军鹤章言，其乡农人某，家颇小阜。妻某氏最恶生女，每产男则字之，女则溺之。年将三十，业戕女六七矣。既又有身，将分娩，腹痛甚，比产一卵，内蠕蠕动，剖之，蛇也。鳞甲金光烂然，举首，目炯炯望母，哆口舌，意似索乳。农人欲杀之，妻摇首止之曰：“此宿孽也。安知非妾平日溺女之报？倘再戕其命，结冤益深，其何以解？不如纵之，听其自然为善。”农人然其言，乃置诸筐，而放之深山丛莽中。

    Explanation

    398

    Li Chengzi said: Some may doubt that such a small, insignificant creature knows how to seek revenge. Perhaps the Zen master was talking too much, so he used this strange and exaggerated talk to warn the world. I say otherwise: the stories of the sparrow and the ant, recorded in the classics, are not examples of gratitude from insects and birds? If gratitude can be proven, why is seeking revenge so surprising? Moreover, if it comes from a virtuous person, the sin should be increased; to blame the virtuous is more in line with reason. Why do you doubt?

    Giving Birth to a Snake

    Lieutenant General Li Jiquan of Hefei said that a farmer from his village, whose family was relatively well-off, had a wife who deeply despised having daughters. Every time she gave birth to a boy, she would name him, but if it was a girl, she would drown her. By the time she was nearly thirty, she had already drowned six or seven daughters. She became pregnant again and was about to give birth when she experienced severe abdominal pain. When she gave birth, she produced an egg, which moved inside. When they cut it open, it was a snake. The snake's scales shone with golden light, and it raised its head, staring at its mother with bright eyes. It opened its mouth and stuck out its tongue, as if asking for milk. The farmer wanted to kill it, but his wife shook her head and stopped him, saying, "This is a karmic debt. How do I know this isn't punishment for my past actions of drowning my daughters? If I were to kill it again, the grudge would deepen, and how could it be resolved? It's better to let it go and let it live naturally." The farmer agreed with her, so he placed the snake in a basket and released it into the deep mountains and dense forests.

    399

    迨夜漏二下，闻户下隐隐有声，见蛇蜿蜒入，径上榻投母怀中，以口哺乳嘬吮，俨然婴儿。某氏痛彻心髓，而竟无如之何。蛇饱则蜷蟠卧枕际，饥则就乳如初。日辄三哺，某氏甚苦之，向蛇哀告曰：“我与汝类分人畜，义属母子。汝齿日长，我乳实不足以果汝腹，况汝日大则毒，未免尤甚，我不堪痛楚，命合休矣。纵系宿孽，而以子杀母，其曲在汝，汝心安乎？今与汝约，以饭代乳，何如？”蛇颔之。自是日饲从饭。蛇渐长大，不三年已粗如碗，十石瓮藉以草，蟠卧其中。日三餐必需斗米，农人家由此渐落。蛇今尚在，人多见之。究竟不知何若也。

    Explanation

    399

    When the night clock struck two, the woman heard a faint sound coming from the door. She saw a snake winding its way in, directly onto the bed and into her lap, sucking and sucking with its mouth, just like an infant. The woman felt pain that penetrated to the core, yet unable to do anything about it. When the snake was full, it coiled and lay beside the pillow; when hungry, it returned to suckle as before. Daily it would suckle three times, and the woman found it very distressing. She addressed the snake in a plea: "I and you are divided between human and animal, by nature we are mother and child. Your teeth are growing longer each day, my milk is truly insufficient to fill your stomach, and as you grow larger, your venom will inevitably become more potent. I can no longer bear the pain, and my life is at an end. Even if this is a karmic retribution from past lives, to have a child kill its mother, the fault lies with you. Is your conscience at peace? Now I make a pact with you, to substitute rice for milk. How does that sound?" The snake nodded in agreement. From then on, it was fed rice daily. The snake gradually grew larger, and within three years it was as thick as a bowl. A ten-stone jar was filled with grass, and the snake coiled and lay inside. Daily it required a basket of rice for three meals, and the farmer's family gradually fell into poverty. The snake still exists today, and many people have seen it. Ultimately, it is unknown what will happen.

    400

    里乘子曰：古语有云：“虎狼虽狠不食儿。”彼溺女者自杀所生，是诚虎狼之不若矣。或谓凡遇溺女之家，当以无故自杀儿女律科其罪，此论最确。吾愿为民父母者，所在留心教化，力换浇风，其功德岂在施药、舍棺、掩骼下哉！忆在蜀时，闻熊一峰游击云，村有媪，溺女甚多，年五十馀，摘蔬圃中，忽有二蛇缘骭而上，窜入前后两窍，媪骇晕倒地，寻毙。此亦孽报，可以为世之溺女者劝。乡民某甲，妻极悍毒，连举二子，意甚得；继生二女，恚令溺之。甲殊不忍，妻骂曰：“业有二子，恶用是赔钱货为哉！如汝不忍，我将手刃之！”甲恐，绐为诞置诸野，乃悄寄人哺育。后配农家子，家颇小阜。及二子长成，游惰荡产，举室难以自存。甲谓：“幸有赔钱货在，试往求援可乎？”妻惊，诘得巅末，悔而且愧。不得已求援于女，相依以终。然则观某甲已事，女虽赔钱货，不且贤于不肖之子哉！为父母者尚其鉴诸。礼园吴宝清识。

    Explanation

    400

    Lǐ Chéngzi said: There is an ancient saying: "Even fierce tigers and wolves do not eat their own cubs." Those who drown their daughters are killing their own offspring; truly, they are worse than tigers and wolves. Some argue that anyone who drowns their daughter should be punished as if they had killed their children for no reason, and this view is the most accurate. I wish that those in power would pay attention to moral education wherever they are, and work to reform corrupt customs. Is such virtue not greater than distributing medicine, providing coffins, or burying the dead? I recall being in Sichuan when I heard from Xiong Yifeng, a military officer, that in a village there was an elderly woman who had drowned many daughters. At the age of fifty or more, while picking vegetables in her garden, she was suddenly attacked by two snakes that climbed up her legs and entered her body through the front and back passages. The woman was terrified, fainted, and fell to the ground, eventually dying. This is also a case of karmic retribution, and it can serve as a warning to those who drown their daughters. In a village, a man named Jia had a wife who was extremely cruel. She had two sons and was very pleased with them; when she later gave birth to two daughters, she angrily ordered them to be drowned. Jia was deeply reluctant to do so, and his wife scolded him, saying, "We already have two sons; what use are these money-losing burdens! If you are unwilling to do it, I will kill them myself!" Fearing her, Jia pretended to agree and left the daughters in the fields, but secretly entrusted them to others for care. Later, they were married to farmers, and their family became slightly prosperous. When the two sons grew up, they squandered their property and lived in idleness, making it difficult for the entire household to survive. Jia said, "Fortunately, we still have these money-losing burdens; perhaps we can seek their help?" His wife was shocked and, after learning the full story, felt both regret and shame. Reluctantly, they turned to their daughters for assistance and depended on them to the end. Thus, considering what happened to Jia, though daughters are called "money-losing burdens," are they not more virtuous than unworthy sons? Let those who are parents take this as a warning. Lǐ Yuán Wú Bǎoqīng noted.

    401

    雷击某总戎
    　　
    　　丙寅春，某县令如金陵谒制军。舟次江东桥，邻泊一巨舰，上载总兵某，麾下健儿繁有徒，亦谒制军来者，气象森严，望之可畏。夜，雷雨大作，河水簸荡，某令同舟人众不能立足。正惶惧间，忽霹霹一声，巨舰凌空而去，而令舟与巨舰首尾相系，竟安然无恙。平明，三十里内村人来报，各处零骸断骼盈尺盈寸，皆为雷火烁炙，其黑如炭。船板碎若败叶，散落满地。佥谓非造大恶孽，不能干天之怒如此。后闻人言，方雷击时，惟一庖人掷置洲上，幸免于祸。且为人言，有某公子者，挟巨资挈家如都门求官。薄暮，舟行患盗，见孤洲有总兵巨舰，大纛扬风，急趣舣其旁，冀叨翼庇。总兵耽视其资，夜半刃公子，并纵兵淫辱妇女，悉杀而投诸江中。搜括财物，半犒麾下，半入己橐，卒火其舟，舟人无一得免者，不匝月即遭此报。盖麾下惟庖人不肯染指，故得幸免于祸云。

    Explanation

    401

    In the spring of the year of Bing Yin, a certain county magistrate traveled to Jinling to pay respects to the provincial governor. His boat stopped at Jiangdong Bridge, where a large ship was moored nearby, carrying General [Name]. The general's subordinates were numerous and strong, and they too came to pay respects to the provincial governor. The atmosphere was stern and imposing, and it was awe-inspiring to look at. At night, there was a great thunderstorm, and the river was turbulent. The magistrate and the people on the boat could not stand. Suddenly, a loud clap of thunder, and the large ship flew into the air, while the magistrate's boat was connected to the large ship at the front and back, and they remained unharmed. At dawn, villagers from within thirty miles came to report that everywhere there were scattered bones and fragments, filling every inch, all of which were scorched by the lightning fire, black like charcoal. The ship planks were shattered like withered leaves, scattered all over the ground. Everyone said it was not due to a great evil deed, but could not provoke such anger from heaven. Later, someone said that when the lightning struck, only one cook was thrown onto the shore and was fortunate to escape disaster. He also told others that there was a certain young master who, with a large sum of money, took his family to the capital to seek an official position. At dusk, while traveling by boat, they feared bandits and saw a lone island with a large ship of the general, a large banner fluttering in the wind. They quickly docked their boat nearby, hoping to benefit from his protection. The general coveted their wealth and, in the middle of the night, killed the young master and ordered his soldiers to rape and humiliate the women, killing them all and throwing them into the river. He seized their property, distributing half to his subordinates and putting the other half into his own bag. Finally, he set their boat on fire, and no one on the boat escaped. Within a month, he suffered this retribution. Because only the cook refused to participate, he was fortunate to escape disaster.

    402

    里乘子曰：总兵为卫民大员，行过盗跖。其麾下惟一庖人不肯染指，得免于祸。然亦赖有此硕果，藉以宣播其恶耳。吁！明明在上，残暴之徒而欲侥幸漏网，岂可得哉！
    　　
    　　崔太史
    　　
    　　蜀中崔太史晴郊，未达时，娶父执某翁女为妻，逑好甚敦。翁老且鳏，疾革时，以小女尚在，垂髫无人抚育，遗命二子，将妹寄养姊家，并托崔夫妇善视之，待成人配一士人，老夫九原感且不朽矣！崔夫妇泣涕受命。翁死后不数年，崔成进士入词馆，意气甚得。时妻妹年已及笄，慧中秀外，士族争来委禽。崔涎其色，谋于妻，求效英皇。妻不敢拂良人意，试商之妹，妹念骨肉情，不忍分离，遂首肯。定情后，崔妻颇怀妒嫉，时加白眼。尝谓亲虽姊妹，分则嫡庶，婢子不得无礼。妹齿稚，腼腆不敢声辨。

    Explanation

    402

    Li Chengzi said: The general, a high-ranking official responsible for protecting the people, behaved like a notorious outlaw. Under his command, only one cook refused to get involved and thus escaped disaster. Yet it was also thanks to this lone survivor that his evil deeds were exposed. Alas! The clear and just authority above, how could cruel and violent people possibly escape punishment!

    Cui Taishi, from Shu, known as Qingjiao, married the daughter of a certain elderly man who was his father's friend when he was not yet successful. The couple was very affectionate. The old man, who was both old and widowed, was on his deathbed and left a bequest to his two sons, asking them to take care of his younger daughter, who was still a child and had no one to nurture her. He entrusted her to the care of Cui's wife and asked them to treat her well until she grew up and could be married to a scholar. The old man hoped that in the afterlife, he would feel grateful and immortal for this. Cui and his wife wept and accepted the responsibility. Not long after the old man's death, Cui passed the imperial examinations and entered the literary office, where he was very proud. At the time, his wife's younger sister had reached the age of marriage, and she was intelligent and beautiful, with many scholars vying to marry her. Cui was attracted to her beauty and consulted his wife about it, hoping to emulate the legendary Emperor Xuan of Han. His wife dared not go against her husband's wishes and tried to discuss it with her sister. The sister, thinking of their familial bond, did not want to be separated and agreed. After they became betrothed, Cui's wife harbored jealousy and often gave her sister a cold look. She once said that although they were sisters by blood, they were now in different social positions, and the younger sister should not be disrespectful. The younger sister, being young and shy, did not dare to argue.

    403

    偶以小事忤崔意，崔遽批其颊而丑诋之。妹以不得于姊尚可忍也，不得于夫则此生无望矣。展转饮泣，中夜雉经而死。人皆叹其薄命，而诮崔薄情，崔亦懊恨无及。一日，崔方假寐，见妻之妹珊珊然来，揽其裾而诃曰：“薄情郎安得高枕无事？妾向念骨肉情，苦为汝夫妇所赚，致罹惨死。今请于帝矣，岂甘心视汝夫妇静好耶？”言讫，拉崔僵仆于地，凡榻上衾枕之属，抛掷狼籍，见者毛发森竖。众掖崔起，色若死灰，不数日寻卒。未几，妻患恶创，遍体溃秽，肉脱见骨而死，人皆称快，以为薄倖悍妒之报。

    Explanation

    403

    A minor incident offended Cui's intentions, and he promptly slapped her face and harshly insulted her. The younger sister could endure being treated poorly by her elder sister, but she could not bear her husband's treatment, for which her life was hopeless. She wept repeatedly and, in the middle of the night, hanged herself to death. Everyone pitied her for her unfortunate fate and criticized Cui for his lack of feeling; Cui also regretted it but was too late. One day, while Cui was dozing, he saw his wife's younger sister approach gracefully. She grasped his sleeve and scolded him, saying, "How can a heartless man rest soundly without worry? I once cherished our familial bond, but you and your wife deceived me, leading to my tragic death. Now I have appealed to the heavens, and how can I possibly be content to see you both live in peace?" Upon finishing her words, she dragged Cui to the ground, and everything on the bed—sheets, pillows, and all—were thrown about in disarray. Those who witnessed this were horrified. The others helped Cui up, but he looked as if he were dead. He died within a few days. Soon after, Cui's wife developed a terrible wound, her body rotted, and she died with flesh falling off and bones exposed. Everyone rejoiced, believing it was retribution for his infidelity and her own cruelty.

    404

    里乘子曰：初，太史谋之妻，求效英皇，妻以不敢拂良人意，故试商之妹，原非出自本心。向使妹或不肯，亦可报良人之命而告无罪矣，不谓妹稚齿无知，情重骨肉，遂为其夫妻所赚。妻之妒加白眼尤可说也；太史竟批颊丑诋，是何心耶？然则妹之冤忿，非饮泣雉经，请命于帝，直不能白矣。吁！岂不痛哉！
    　　
    　　王氏子
    　　
    　　滦州榛子镇有王叟者，以鬻果为业。子名马头，年十九，素极不孝，所为多不法事。父母偶尔劝戒，不惟不听，甚则反遭殴骂。故事果藏地窨，每夜分别生熟之等差，最熟者先售，最生者从缓，为可暂蓄不致烂坏也。同治甲子二月十七日，夜漏二下，叟与子入窨选果，甫出，陡见火起，黑烟充塞窨中，急同下扑灭，皆为烟熏昏仆于地。邻舍趋救，将父子先后舁出。须臾父苏，而子竟殭矣！是盖天罚不孝也。葛少莪司马所目睹，因函寄予。笔之以为不孝于亲者劝。

    Explanation

    404

    里乘子曰：起初，太史谋的妻子想要效仿英皇，但不敢违背丈夫的意愿，因此尝试了商人的妹妹，这原本并非出自她的心意。如果妹妹当时拒绝了，也可以向丈夫报告并免于责罚，没想到妹妹年纪尚小，不懂事理，非常重视家族亲情，结果被这对夫妻欺骗了。妻子的嫉妒和轻蔑尚可理解；而太史竟然动手打人并恶言相向，这是什么心态呢？这样一来，妹妹的冤屈和愤怒，除非她哭泣着自尽，向天帝申诉，否则无法昭雪。唉！这难道不可悲吗？

    王氏子

    滦州榛子镇有一位名叫王叟的老者，以卖水果为生。他的儿子叫马头，十九岁，一直非常不孝顺，经常做违法的事情。父母偶尔劝诫他，他不仅不听，反而遭到殴打和辱骂。王叟通常将水果储存在地窖中，每晚根据成熟度分类，最熟的先卖，最生的留到后面，以便暂时存放而不至于腐烂。同治甲子年二月十七日，午夜两点，王叟和他的儿子进入地窖挑选水果，刚出来就突然看到火起，黑烟充满了地窖。他们急忙下去灭火，都被烟熏得昏倒在地上。邻居们赶来救援，先后将父子俩抬了出来。不久父亲苏醒过来，但儿子却不幸去世了！这显然是上天对不孝的惩罚。此事由葛少莪司马亲眼目睹，并通过信件告知我。我写下这段文字，是为了警告那些对父母不孝的人。

    405

    雷击邵伯民
    　　
    　　邵伯民有伯仲析炊而同居者。伯素朴愿，病瘵濒死，召仲执手泣谓曰：“我病殆将不起，手足从此分矣！先人遗产，不忧饿殍，奈无子息何！顾汝嫂贤，弟所知也，幸有娠。男也，佐嫂善抚之，成人必教之读书；女也，亦善视之，为择一快婿，不使失所。汝后日子多，当分嗣我，佐汝嫂守柏舟节，吾目瞑矣。弟如重骨肉情，须念我治命，识之勿忘。”仲慰之曰：“兄第安心调摄，当不至若是。万一不幸，敢不如命？”伯点首者再，不日寻卒。

    Explanation

    405

    Lightning struck the people of Shaobo.
    In Shaobo, there were brothers who lived together but maintained separate households. The elder brother was simple and virtuous, but he was gravely ill with tuberculosis and on the brink of death.
