On that very day, the father and son, along with the mother, performed a joint worship of heaven and earth, their ancestors, and conducted the temple meeting ceremony before they were married.

    451

    毛甲
    　　
    　　颍州毛甲，能知前生事，其左手宛然猪蹄。人问之，自言前世为江西九江府人，姓李，与同里赵某至汉口作小贸易，经年折阅将尽，议同遄归。赵忽染疫，疾革，泣谓李曰：“初与君出门时，本期稍得什一。今天降灾，断难再起。囊仅存钱十馀千，烦代购薄材为之殡殓。如有馀钱，乞带付其子，幸甚！”李挥泪应诺。赵不日寻卒。李以数千为市薄材，权厝义山。尚馀钱六千文，即私入己槖。既归，但告赵子，谓其父染疫已故，柩厝某所而已。越岁，昼寝，忽见赵来索钱。拉李至野外，剥去衣服，推堕河中，听其浮沉。倏达彼岸，忽有一长人援拯上岸，以黑衫付令着之。

    Explanation

    451

    Mao Jia

    Mao Jia of Yingzhou could remember his past lives, and his left hand was exactly like a pig's hoof. When asked about it, he said that in his previous life he was a man named Li from Jiujiang Prefecture in Jiangxi Province. He had traveled to Hankou with a fellow villager named Zhao to engage in small-scale trade. After many years of losses approaching depletion, they agreed to return home quickly. Zhao suddenly contracted an illness and fell gravely ill, weeping as he told Li, "When we first went out together, I originally expected to gain a tenth part. But now disaster has befallen us, and it is impossible to recover. Only about ten thousand coins are left in my bag. Please buy a simple coffin for my burial. If there is any extra money, please take it to my son, which would be most fortunate!" Li nodded with tears. Zhao did not live long and soon died. Li used several thousand coins to buy a simple coffin and temporarily buried Zhao in a public grave. Still having six thousand coins left, Li secretly put them into his own bag. Upon returning home, he merely told Zhao's son that his father had contracted an illness and passed away, and that the coffin was placed somewhere. A year later, while taking a daytime nap, Mao Jia suddenly saw Zhao come to demand money. Zhao dragged Li to the wilderness, stripped him of his clothes, pushed him into the river, and left him to float or sink. Suddenly, he reached the other side, and a tall person pulled him up to the shore, giving him a black robe to wear.

    452

    导至一处，人烟辐辏，过一短垣，其人蓦自后推之，身忽倾跌，拭目自视，已变为小豕。闻赵子笑呼曰：“猪母又生子矣。”知身已死，变畜以偿夙逋，颇自悔恨。唯腹苦肌，见群小豕俱就母哺乳，不得已，亦就哺之。日渐壮长，年馀，居然肥腯。一日，忽见赵子率屠人至，索钱六千，付其宰割。急欲逃避，乃屠人已出索紧缚，舁置案上，遽以刀搠人喉际，直刺至心，痛不可忍。且伐毛解肉，备受苦楚，久之，肉始割尽。售讫，身复为人。见前长人来，笑谓曰：“衣可见还矣。”便为解衣，极力褫脱，左袂尚未曾褪下，又蓦自后推之，身忽倾跌，拭目自视，复变为小儿，即毛甲也，惟左手宛然猪蹄。比长，每自顾其手，念及前事，而心辄惴惴也。

    Explanation

    452

    He was led to a place where people gathered in dense clusters, passed a short wall, and was suddenly pushed from behind, causing him to fall. Wiping his eyes and looking at himself, he found that he had transformed into a small pig. He heard Zhaozi laughing and calling out, "The pig mother has given birth to another piglet!" Realizing that he had died, he understood that he had turned into an animal to repay an ancient debt, and he felt deep regret and remorse. Despite the pain in his stomach, he saw a group of small pigs nursing from their mother and, feeling helpless, he also nursed from her. Over time, he grew stronger and fatter, and after a year, he became surprisingly plump. One day, he suddenly saw Zhaozi leading a butcher to him, demanding six thousand coins to slaughter him. He desperately tried to escape, but the butcher had already tied him up tightly and placed him on a table. The butcher quickly stabbed a knife into his throat, piercing it straight to his heart, causing unbearable pain. The butcher then skinned and cut him, subjecting him to immense suffering. After a long time, the meat was finally cut off entirely. Once the meat was sold, his body returned to being a human. Seeing the tall figure from before, Zhaozi laughed and said, "Your clothes can now be returned." He then proceeded to remove his clothes, struggling to tear them off. His left sleeve had not yet been removed when he was suddenly pushed from behind again, causing him to fall. Wiping his eyes and looking at himself, he found that he had transformed back into a child, specifically Maojia, but with his left hand still clearly resembling a pig's hoof. As he grew older, he often looked at his hand, thinking of the previous events, and his heart would always be filled with anxiety.

    453

    戴明府
    　　
    　　合肥戴子开明府，谒选入都。将小妻某氏暂寄居清江，自携旧仆甲乙买舟北上。甲乙故各有所昵妓，惓惓不能忘情。知戴囊藏白金一千三百两，谋杀戴而攫其金，以充缠头之费。舟次沧州，夜漏三下，瞷戴睡熟，甲持刃入内，遽剁戴首于枕，未殊，手颤几难举刃，乙继进刃而殪之。榜人闻声起窥，大惊欲呼，甲乙遽掩其口劫之曰：“声则杀汝！”榜人禁骇不敢复声。甲乙乃舁尸投诸河。启囊各瓜分六百金，以百金并衣物犒榜人。又假戴手书寄小妻某氏，谓甲乙伺应无状，途次业俱屏逐，如来见汝，切勿再留为要。

    Explanation

    453

    Magistrate Dai of Hefei

    Magistrate Dai of Hefei, a man named Dai Zikai, went to the capital to take up a new post. He left his concubine, Miss So-and-so, to stay temporarily in Qingjiang, and he himself, accompanied by his old servants Jia and Yi, bought a boat to travel north. Jia and Yi each had a favorite courtesan, and they were deeply attached to them, unable to forget their feelings. Knowing that Dai had 1,300 taels of silver hidden in his bag, they plotted to kill Dai and seize his money to use as payment for the courtesans. When the boat reached Cangzhou, at the third watch of the night, they waited until Dai was asleep. Jia took a knife and entered the cabin, quickly chopping off Dai's head on the pillow. Dai was not yet dead, and Jia's hands were trembling, making it difficult to lift the blade. Yi then stepped forward and finished the deed, killing Dai. The boatman heard the sound and got up to look, greatly alarmed and wanting to call out. Jia and Yi quickly covered his mouth and threatened him, saying, "If you make a sound, we will kill you!" The boatman was terrified and dared not make a sound. Jia and Yi then carried the body and threw it into the river. They opened the bag and divided the 600 taels of silver equally. They gave 100 taels of silver and clothes as a reward to the boatman. They also forged a letter from Dai to send to his concubine, Miss So-and-so, stating that Jia and Yi had behaved improperly and were already dismissed, and if she saw them, she should not keep them any longer.

    454

    既甲乙来见某氏，问何不随主人北上，甲乙答称材愧驽骀，不足任主人驱策，已被摈弃矣。某氏以前得戴书，不敢再留，好言慰令俱去。甲所昵妓在清江，乙所昵妓在如皋，各携金分投所欢。他日，乙出游，遇戴友郭君月楼，诧问何不随主人北上，乙答如前，谓材愧驽骀，不足任主人驱策，已与甲俱被摈弃矣，郭颔之。忽闻耳畔有人言戴子开已被若曹杀了，郭四顾无人，大惊，心知有异，急拘乙付有司，一讯而吐其实。复遣人至清江，拘甲至所，供无异词，遂将甲乙同付大辟。其金各存妓处，分毫未用，爰取以归某氏。惟戴尸已投诸河，榜人尚稽诛待缉，鬼如有灵，知终难漏网耳。
    　　
    　　里乘卷七

    Explanation

    454

    Both "甲" and "乙" came to see "某氏" and were asked why they did not follow the owner north. "甲" and "乙" replied that they lacked ability, were inferior like slow horses, and were not capable of being used by the owner, having already been rejected. "某氏" did not dare to keep them and kindly comforted them, letting them both go. "甲" had a favored courtesan in Qingjiang, and "乙" had a favored courtesan in Rugao. Each carried money to give to their favored courtesans. Later, "乙" went out and met a friend of "戴子开," Guo Junyue. "乙" was asked why he did not follow the owner north, and he replied as before, saying that he lacked ability, was inferior like a slow horse, and was not capable of being used by the owner, having been rejected along with "甲." Guo nodded in agreement. Suddenly, he heard someone say that "戴子开" had been killed by them. Looking around, he saw no one and was greatly shocked, knowing there was something strange. He quickly arrested "乙" and handed him over to the authorities. After one interrogation, "乙" revealed the truth. "某氏" then sent someone to Qingjiang to arrest "甲" and bring him here. "甲" gave no different account. Thus, both "甲" and "乙" were sentenced to death. The money they each gave to their courtesans was kept there and not used. It was then taken back to "某氏." Only "戴子开's" body had been thrown into the river, and the boatman is still under investigation and awaiting arrest. If "戴子开" has a spirit, he knows he will eventually be caught.

    455

    某公子
    　　
    　　姑苏玄妙观，内有太岁祠，向供六十甲子神位。士女各按生年所属，前往祈祷。每岁春首，祈祷愈众，香烟烟煴，旦暮不绝。秦中某公子者，慧中秀外，素有璧人之誉。弱冠领解，人皆称其才可问鼎，公子亦顾影自负。以丧偶求凰，择选甚苛。会其父由侍御出任苏松监司，随侍游吴。尝于春日至玄妙观，见一女郎靓妆媥姺，独拜太岁；迫而视之，玉貌珊珊，盖天人也。窥其所拜甲子，计年才十七，心恋神痴，遂屈膝跽诸裙角。拜云则拜，就谛双钩，瘦不盈握，爱极忘形，不觉伏嗅其胫。女觉身后有人，回首蓦见公子，秋波微睨，红晕两颊，毫无怒色。拜毕，又频以目视公子，含笑登舆而去。公子益不自持，亟尾缀舆后，从行二里许，瞷女郎下舆入室，默识其地，怏怏而归。自幸奇遇，秘不敢遽告父母。潜托心腹友，就其地代访氏族。友复命，谓其室主人某叟，固是老儒，只二子，远游在外，家并无女郎。公子谓友代访不实，颇怀怨恚。

    Explanation

    455

    A Certain Young Nobleman

    In the Xuanmiao Temple of Suzhou, there is a shrine dedicated to the God of Age, where sixty gods representing the sixty cycles of the Chinese calendar are enshrined. Men and women, according to their birth years, go to pray. Each spring, the number of people praying increases, and the incense smoke is thick and continuous day and night. A young nobleman from Qin, known for his intelligence and beauty, had always been praised as a perfect gentleman. He passed the imperial examinations at the age of twenty and was widely regarded as capable of achieving great success. The young nobleman was also very self-confident, looking at his reflection and feeling proud. He sought a virtuous wife after the loss of his previous one, and his standards were extremely high. Coincidentally, his father, who served as an official in the imperial court, was appointed as the supervisor of Suzhou and Songjiang, and the young nobleman accompanied him on a trip to Wu. One spring day, he visited the Xuanmiao Temple and saw a young lady dressed elegantly, worshipping alone before the God of Age. He looked closely at her, and she had a graceful and beautiful appearance, seemingly a celestial being. He noticed the year she was worshipping, which indicated she was only seventeen years old. Entranced by her beauty, he knelt down at her feet. As she continued to pray, he carefully examined her delicate handwriting, which was so thin that it could not be grasped by hand. Overwhelmed by his feelings, he forgot himself and even leaned forward to smell her leg. The young lady felt someone behind her and turned around, suddenly seeing the young nobleman. She gave him a subtle glance, her cheeks blushing slightly, but showed no sign of anger. After finishing her prayer, she frequently looked at the young nobleman with a smile and got into a carriage to leave. The young nobleman became even more infatuated and quickly followed the carriage, trailing it for about two li. He saw the young lady get off the carriage and enter a room, and he silently remembered the location, returning home with a sullen expression. He felt fortunate to have met such a beautiful woman and kept it a secret, not immediately telling his parents. He secretly entrusted a trusted friend to investigate the family background of the young lady at that location. The friend reported back, saying that the owner of the house was an elderly scholar with only two sons who were traveling far away, and there was no young lady in the household. The young nobleman believed that the friend's investigation was inaccurate and felt quite resentful.

    456

    因自往前地，遍讯左右邻舍，所答一如友所言，心甚讶之，以为白昼目所亲睹，岂有差舛？坐是犹疑成疾，镇日忽忽若失，眠食俱废。延医胗治，百药罔效。友人无可如何，密以致疾之由备告其父母。观察公仅此一子，素极怜爱。商之夫人，亟召媒妁，遍为物色佳丽，意在求遂子意而速已其疾。公子闻之，太息谓友人曰：“我病殆不起矣！烦代禀二老人，不必妄费心力。”友人曰：“然则君将奈何？”公子叹曰：“我前日明明目睹，既无其人，非仙即魅。

    Explanation

    456

    Therefore, he went to the place in question and inquired of all the neighbors around him. Their responses were the same as his friend had said, and he was greatly surprised by it. He believed that what he had seen with his own eyes during the day could not possibly be mistaken. Because of this, he fell into doubt and became ill, all day long seeming lost and distracted, neglecting both sleep and food. He called in a doctor to treat him, but all medicines were ineffective. His friend, helpless, secretly informed his parents of the cause of his illness. The governor, who had only this one son, had always been very much loved by them. They consulted with his wife and hastily summoned matchmakers to seek out beautiful women everywhere, with the intention of satisfying his son's wishes and quickly curing his illness. The young man, upon hearing this, sighed and said to his friend, "My illness is likely to be fatal! Please convey this to my parents; there is no need to waste effort." His friend asked, "Then what will you do?" The young man sighed and said, "I clearly saw it with my own eyes the other day. Since there was no one there, it must be either a fairy or a demon."

    457

    我魂已为摄去，行将访彼于地下矣！又何必舍却巫山而另求别岫之云哉！”友曰：“若使巫山云在，又将如何？”公子点首笑曰：“非此不可。”友人知其情不可夺，又以言备告其父母。因共筹商，折柬召某叟来从权计议。少选叟至，观察公降阶相迎。见其白髯垂胸，举止不俗，心甚异之。谈次，公笑问叟曰：“尊丈宅中，向日可否有妖？”叟拈髯笑曰：“老朽今年政七十矣。自高曾以来，居此宅者五世矣。愧非高明，幸免鬼瞰，公何所见而有妖也？”观察不得已，以公子所见告之。

    Explanation

    457

    My soul has already been taken away, and I am about to visit him/her in the underworld! Why should I abandon Mount Wu and seek clouds from other mountains?" The friend said, "If the cloud of Mount Wu is still there, what then?" The young master nodded and smiled, saying, "Nothing else will do." The friend knew his/her feelings could not be changed and fully informed his/her parents of the situation. They discussed together and sent a folded note inviting a certain old man to come for consultation. In a short while, the old man arrived, and the official descended the steps to greet him. Seeing the old man with a white beard hanging down to his chest and a refined demeanor, he thought it very strange. During the conversation, the official smiled and asked the old man, "In your esteemed house, have there been any spirits or ghosts in the past?" The old man twirled his beard and smiled, saying, "I am an old man this year, seventy years old. Since the time of my ancestors, five generations have lived in this house. I am ashamed not to be wise, but fortunately, I have avoided the gaze of ghosts. What did you see that made you think there were spirits or ghosts?" The official, out of necessity, informed him of what the young master had seen.

    458

    且问：“尊丈可否有女？”叟曰：“老朽只有二子，糊口在外，家惟老妻督率儿妇作苦，并无一女。不然，如公子才望，方恐系援不得也。”公曰：“豚儿谬荷奖饰。君为一方耆宿，闻见较广，意中颇有淑女堪与豚儿妃匹者乎？”叟曰：“此非仓卒所可报命。无已，老朽有两甥，颇不恶，公如不论门第，或可塞责。”公问：“令亲何许人也？”叟曰：“妹夫楼某，攻媿的裔，以名诸生，曾中副本。无子，仅生二女，幼即教其读书。

    Explanation

    458

    The gentleman then asked, "May I inquire if your esteemed elder has a daughter?" The old man replied, "I am old and have only two sons who make a living outside, and at home I have only my old wife overseeing the younger women in their hard work, and we have no daughters. Otherwise, if your young master has such talent and reputation, I would fear that we would not be able to secure his support." The gentleman said, "My son falsely receives your praise. You are a respected elder in your area, with a wide range of knowledge and experience, so I wonder if you have in mind any virtuous young lady who could be a suitable match for my son?" The old man said, "This is not something that can be decided quickly. If not, I have two nephews who are quite good, and if you do not mind the family background, they might be able to fulfill the duty." The gentleman asked, "Who are your relatives?" The old man said, "My sister-in-law's husband, Mr. Lou, is from the lineage of Gong Kui, a well-known scholar, and he once passed the supplementary examination. He has no sons, only two daughters, and he has been educating them in reading and writing from a young age."

    459

    妹夫去世，吾妹极其珍爱，欲择快婿，相依以终。今两甥长成，尚称端丽，兼通翰墨。里党誉之曰‘前有三刘，后有二楼’，谓其不减孝绰三妹也。次甥近遘小恙，长甥曾寄老朽为女。容与吾妹商之，倘肯令其远嫁，与公子差堪妃匹。”公问知年甫十九，少公子二岁，大喜。亟倩叟为道地，谓：“曾寄名君女，暂请易舅为翁，即就君宅亲迎，以释痴儿之疑。凡此权宜行事，烦寄语令妹，务求曲予成全。俟结褵后再作别议，无不惟命是听。”叟为人亦甚通达，一一首肯而别。

    Explanation

    459

    My brother-in-law has passed away, and my sister cherishes him greatly. She wishes to find a suitable son-in-law for her to rely on until the end. Now, her two nephews have grown up and are still considered handsome, and they are also proficient in calligraphy and literature. The neighborhood praises them, saying, "Before there were the Three Lis, now there are the Two Lou's," indicating that they are no less than the three sisters of Xiao Chuo. The younger nephew recently fell ill, while the elder nephew was once entrusted to me as a daughter. I will discuss with my sister, and if she is willing to let him marry far away, he could be a suitable match for her son-in-law. The official learned that he is just nineteen, two years younger than his son, and was very pleased. He quickly asked the old man to arrange it, saying, "He was once entrusted to you as a daughter, temporarily change uncle to grandfather, and have the wedding at your house to resolve the child's doubts." All these temporary arrangements, please convey to my sister, and seek her understanding and support. After the wedding, we can discuss other matters, and I will follow your instructions. The old man, being very understanding, agreed to all of this and took his leave.

    460

    公乃使友风示公子曰：“顷尊公召君前所见之室主人至，皤然老叟，一乡祭酒也。再三询诘，乃知渠固有爱女，年已及笄，才貌两绝，不肯字异地人，凡有问名，悉邻舍托词以谢。尊公再三恳求，渠亦素慕君才望，允缔婚媾。君闻之颇快意否？”公子冷笑曰：“君休相戏！天下岂有此快意事？”友正色申誓，以明无欺。公子忻然推枕起坐曰：“田舍奴，我岂妄哉！我谓其中必有曲折，今果然也。但未知何日委禽？”友谓：“俟君病愈。

    Explanation

    460

    The official then sent his messenger, Friend Wind, to inform the young noble: "Recently, your esteemed father summoned the owner of the room you previously saw. He is a gray-haired old man, the respected leader of the village. After repeatedly questioning him, we learned that he indeed has a beloved daughter, who is already of marriageable age, exceptionally talented and beautiful, and refuses to marry someone from another place. Anyone who inquired about marriage was refused using excuses from neighbors. Your esteemed father repeatedly requested, and he also admired your talent and reputation, agreeing to the marriage. Are you pleased to hear this?" The young noble coldly laughed and said, "Do not jest with me! How could there be such a pleasant thing in the world?" Friend Wind solemnly swore to show no deception. The young noble joyfully pushed away his pillow and sat up, saying, "Rural servant, how could I be mistaken? I thought there must be complications, and indeed, there are. But I do not know when the gift will be presented." Friend Wind replied, "Wait until you recover from your illness."

    461

    ”公子亟披衣起立曰：“烦君代禀二老，我固无病。”友喜以语观察。亟为聘叟之长甥，如议亲迎成礼。两情倍极欢洽。晨兴，女坐床着履，公子欹枕注视，觉女新上鬟，较初见时尤为丰艳，情不自禁，戏捉女足曰：“小生初窥双钩，谓瘦不盈握，信然。”女讶曰：“妾及笄后，母教严，不轻出门。君从何处得见？”公子笑曰：“卿会打谎语！小生为卿一病几死，卿真不知耶？”女益惊曰：“君言妾实不解。请明谕，以免疑闷。”公子笑曰：“卿真不知也耶？”遂历历笑述前事。

    Explanation

    461

    The young lord hurriedly put on his clothes and stood up, saying, "Please inform the two elders on my behalf that I am certainly not ill." His friend, pleased, relayed this to the observer. They quickly arranged for the engagement of the old man's eldest nephew, and as planned, the wedding ceremony was completed. Their feelings were even more joyful and harmonious. In the morning, the young lady sat on the bed, putting on her shoes. The young lord, leaning on his pillow and watching, noticed that her newly styled hairdo looked even more beautiful and full of charm compared to when he first saw her. Unable to contain his feelings, he playfully grabbed her foot and said, "I first saw your hands and thought they were slender and not quite full-sized. Indeed, they are." The young lady was surprised and asked, "After I came of age, my mother taught me strictly and did not let me leave the house lightly. Where did you see me?" The young lord laughed and said, "You are good at telling lies! I was so sick for you that I almost died. Are you really not aware?" The young lady was even more surprised and said, "You say I don't understand. Please explain clearly to dispel my doubts." The young lord laughed and said, "Are you really not aware?" He then recounted the previous events in detail with laughter.

    462

    女闻之，愀然叹曰：“若然，是君杀吾妹矣！奈何！”公子亟叩何故，女曰：“君固不知，叟非妾父，乃舅也。初舅向吾母议婚时，语多颟顸，母殊不解。以素耳君名，勉强允诺。不料有如许委曲。”公子亟问：“究竟何谓我杀卿妹？”女叹曰：“君固不知，妾姊妹二人，幼相亲爱。妹年十七，少妾二岁，尝长枕大被，拥抱而卧，每谈衷曲，愿得一可共白首者同侍巾栉，俾免骨肉乘违。前妹以生辰，奉母命往祷太岁，顺道省视舅妗。归，私谓妾言，庙中见一郎君，仪容蕴藉，多情人也。倘我姊妹得同事之，平生之愿足矣。妾比让：痴婢休得呓语！汝匆匆一面，何由知其多情？妹谓其人情见于面，尾缀舆后，直从舅氏之门，无情者能若是乎？姊如不信，数日必有好音。妾方笑其太痴。久之，音问杳然，吾妹失望，竟至成疾，眠食俱废，医治无效。其致疾之由，惟妾知之，不敢告母。前妾嫁君时与妹作别，并以好言宽慰。待嘱君物色其人，不谓竟是老奴也。

    Explanation

    462

    The girl heard this and sighed sadly, saying, "If that is the case, then you have killed my sister! What should I do?" The young nobleman quickly asked why, and the girl said, "You certainly do not know. The old man is not my father but my uncle. When my uncle first proposed marriage to my mother, the conversation was vague and unclear, and my mother did not understand. Since she had long heard of your name, she reluctantly agreed. I did not expect there to be such complications." The young nobleman quickly asked, "What exactly does it mean that you killed my sister?" The girl sighed and said, "You certainly do not know. My sister and I were very close. She is two years younger than me and was seventeen. We used to share a large pillow and blanket, sleeping together, and often talked about our innermost feelings, wishing to find someone who could be our lifelong companion so we would not be separated from our family. Before her birthday, she went to pray to the Jade Emperor and visited my uncle and aunt. When she returned, she privately told me that in the temple, she saw a young man with a dignified appearance and a gentle demeanor. If my sister and I could serve him together, it would fulfill my lifelong wish. I laughed and said, 'Don't be silly! You just saw him briefly, how could you know he is gentle?' My sister said that his feelings were visible on his face, he followed behind in the carriage, and went directly to my uncle's house; could someone without feeling act that way? If you don't believe me, you will hear good news soon. I laughed and thought she was too foolish. After a while, there was no news, and my sister became disappointed, falling ill, unable to sleep or eat, and the medicine did not help. The cause of her illness was known only to me, and I did not dare to tell my mother. Before I married you, I said goodbye to my sister and comforted her with reassuring words. I told you to look for that person, but I didn't expect it to be the old servant."

    463

    然则据君所言，谓非君杀吾妹而何？”公子闻言，如梦方醒。乃叹曰：“今而后我知之矣。大约家君恐小生疾不能起，故商之阿舅，换羽移宫，可谓弥缝甚巧。但卿年齿较所拜甲子差长，面貌较初见时微丰，且谛视颊无微涡，不无少疑。然难弟难兄，亦何酷肖若此！”女曰：“妾姊妹面貌相肖，多疑孪生，即吾母有时亦几误认。今即若此，将焉置吾妹也？”公子笑揖女曰：“卿妹几杀小生，赖卿救之；小生几杀卿妹，亦非卿不能救之。二人之命，生死惟卿。

    Explanation

    463

    In that case, according to what you say, how can it be otherwise than that you killed my sister?" The young man, upon hearing this, was as if waking from a dream. He then sighed and said, "Now I understand. My father probably feared that I would not recover from my illness, so he consulted my uncle-in-law and devised a clever plan, which he executed very skillfully. But your age is slightly older than the person I addressed as '甲子,' your appearance is slightly fuller than when we first met, and upon close inspection, there is no faint dimple on your cheek, which leaves me somewhat doubtful. However, we are so alike, how can this be?" The woman replied, "My sisters look very similar, and they are often mistaken for twins, even by our mother. If this is the case, what should I do about my sister?" The young man smiled and bowed to the woman, saying, "Your sister almost killed me, thanks to your rescue; I almost killed your sister, and it was only you who could save her. The lives of the two of us depend entirely on you."

    464

    脱卿妹不起，誓不独生！卿其怜之。”女笑曰：“君亦无赖矣！容徐图之，必有以报命。”公子笑谢之。亡何，女归宁。见妹瘦如削玉，不胜诧惜。因私谓妹曰：“汝以姊夫为何如人也？”妹曰：“不知。”曰：“汝向所谓有情之郎君，即今之所谓姊夫者是也。”遂备述公子所言。妹闻之不禁神痴，移时乃曰：“嘻！信如姊言，窃为姊贺得人矣！姊如念畴昔之愿，不以妹为不肖，愿事姊以终身焉。不然，媵之可也。惟姊所命。”女泣慰之曰：“妹其善保玉体，姊特此归宁，正为妹事。待商之老母，当无不谐。

    Explanation

    464

    If my younger sister does not rise, I swear I will not live alone! You must pity her." The woman laughed and said, "Sir, you are quite the rogue! Give me some time to consider it, and I will surely have a way to fulfill my promise." The young man laughed and thanked her. Soon after, the woman returned to her parents' home. She saw her sister so thin that she looked like carved jade, and she was filled with surprise and concern. She then privately told her sister, "What do you think of my brother-in-law?" Her sister replied, "I don't know." The woman said, "The man you once spoke of with affection is now your brother-in-law." She then recounted everything the young man had said. Her sister, upon hearing this, was struck with a sense of wonder and said, "Ah! If it is as you say, I secretly congratulate you for finding the right person! If you remember your past wishes and do not think I am unworthy, I wish to serve you for life. Otherwise, I can be your concubine. Whatever you command." The woman comforted her, saying, "Take good care of your body, dear sister. I have come back home just for your sake. I will discuss this with our mother, and there will be no problem."

    465

    ”妹挥泪以谢。既女见母，与叙述婿家琐事。母问：“汝婿为人何若？”女曰：“婿为人温文尔雅，读书君子也。”母又问：“其兄弟几何？”女叹曰：“翁姑以男丁单寡，行将为婿谋置簉室，冀多添孙，以昌大其后。”母闻之惊曰：“我固谓仕宦之家不可与为婚姻，正为此也。况男子得新弃旧，大抵皆然。果尔，则吾儿休矣。奈何！”女频蹙曰：“儿日来亦正虑此事。

    Explanation

    465

    The sister shed tears in gratitude. After the daughter met her mother, she narrated trivial matters of her husband's family. The mother asked, "What is your husband like?" The daughter replied, "My husband is gentle, refined, and courteous, a scholarly gentleman." The mother asked again, "How many brothers does he have?" The daughter sighed and said, "The parents-in-law, due to the scarcity of male descendants, are about to arrange for the son-in-law to take a secondary wife, hoping to have more grandchildren, to greatly expand their lineage." The mother was shocked upon hearing this and said, "I have always believed that it is not advisable to marry into a family of officials, and this is exactly why. Moreover, men tend to favor new ones while abandoning the old, which is generally the case. If so, then my daughter is ruined. What shall we do?" The daughter frowned and said, "Lately, I have also been worrying about this matter."

    466

    昨筹得一策，未审母以为然否？”母问：“如何？”女曰：“母生儿姊妹二人，幼相亲爱。假使妹他日又适远地，姊妹作别，故难为情；即母依婿以终，在此在彼，究不免有所牵挂。儿愚以为与其妹归他人，不如亦适儿婿。一则儿婿免滋别议；再则姊妹得以常聚，即生子亦无分畛域；三则迎母同居，婿分属半子，二女同婿，则情逾一子。似此骨肉永得团聚，较为万全。母以为何如？”母沉思良久，曰：“汝言亦似近理，未审汝妹抱病，于意云何？”女曰：“比视妹恙，谓已小愈，具告此意，渠重同怀情，亦甚乐从。

    Explanation

    466

    Yesterday I devised a plan, but I am unsure if Mother thinks it is right. Mother asked, "How so?" I said, "Mother, you have two daughters, and we were very close when we were young. If my sister were to go to a distant place in the future, it would be awkward for us to part. Even if Mother relies on the son-in-law until the end, whether here or there, one cannot help but feel some attachment. I think that rather than my sister going to someone else, it would be better for her to marry my son-in-law. First, my son-in-law would avoid causing different opinions; second, the sisters could always be together, and even if they have children, there would be no division; third, welcoming Mother to live with us, the son-in-law would be treated like a half-son, and two daughters sharing the same son-in-law would mean their affection surpasses that of a single son. In this way, the family will always be united, which is more comprehensive. What do you think, Mother?" Mother thought for a long time and said, "Your words seem reasonable, but what about my sister's illness? What do you think about that?" I said, "I see that my sister is sick, and I think she has slightly recovered. I fully explained this idea to her, and she values the bond of being sisters, so she is very happy to agree."

    467

    ”母曰：“既儿辈乐此，吾复何说？但须与婿斟酌尽善。禀明舅姑，毋使汝妹负屈，纳采成礼，勿得草草。”女曰：“诺。”既归，公子亟问所事如何，女摇手曰：“已遭阿母谯诃，事不谐矣。君休妄想！”公子闻之，怅然变色，垂首潸潸欲涕。女遽前以罗巾为拭其面曰：“君真志诚种也。妾与君戏耳，何遽若是？”爰为笑述母言。公子大悦，起揖女曰：“卿真一尊救苦观世音也。他日卿妹来时，当以黄金铸像，香花供奉，聊报玉成之德。”女笑曰：“君亦可谓善于将将。

    Explanation

    467

    The mother said, "Since the children are happy with this, what more can I say? Just make sure to carefully consider everything with the groom to ensure it is perfect. Inform your in-laws, and do not let your sister suffer injustice. The wedding ceremony should be conducted properly, not hastily." The girl replied, "Yes." After she returned, the young man hurriedly asked her how things went. She shook her head and said, "Mother has already scolded me, and the matter is not going well. Do not have unrealistic expectations!" Upon hearing this, the young man looked disappointed, his face turning pale, and he was about to cry. The girl quickly stepped forward and wiped his face with a silk handkerchief, saying, "You are truly a person of sincere heart. I was just joking with you, why be so upset?" She then laughed and recounted what her mother had said. The young man was greatly pleased, stood up, and bowed to the girl, saying, "You are truly like the compassionate Bodhisattva Guanyin. When your sister comes to visit in the future, I will cast a golden statue and offer incense and flowers to honor her, to repay her kindness in making this arrangement successful." The girl smiled and said, "You can also be said to be good at managing people."

    468

    但愿鱼水偕老，毋忘冰人足矣。”越日，公子倩其友将此意告知父母。父母以爱怜子故，又嘉新妇之德，一如所议。择日延媒召傧，鼓吹迎其妹归。初，公与夫人以一时行权，恐新妇来不当子意。既见其伉俪情笃，老怀顿慰。及见其妹，讶与新妇酷肖。后闻友人备述始末，心始恍然。然每值两妇定省时，犹不辨其孰姊孰妹也。乃迎其母同居，公子事之，不啻所生焉。厥后，姊生二子一女，妹生三子一女，公子由词馆官至少宰，具表陈情，姊妹皆封夫人，并貤封及其母云。

    Explanation

    468

    But愿鱼水偕老，毋忘冰人足矣。”越日，公子倩其友将此意告知父母。父母以爱怜子故，又嘉新妇之德，一如所议。择日延媒召傧，鼓吹迎其妹归。初，公与夫人以一时行权，恐新妇来不当子意。既见其伉俪情笃，老怀顿慰。及见其妹，讶与新妇酷肖。后闻友人备述始末，心始恍然。然每值两妇定省时，犹不辨其孰姊孰妹也。乃迎其母同居，公子事之，不啻所生焉。厥后，姊生二子一女，妹生三子一女，公子由词馆官至少宰，具表陈情，姊妹皆封夫人，并貤封及其母云。

    May you be like fish and water, living together forever, and do not forget the matchmaker is enough.” The next day, the young master asked his friend to convey this intention to his parents. The parents, out of love for their son and admiration for the new wife's virtue, agreed as they had discussed. They chose a day to invite the matchmaker and summon the attendants, and welcomed her sister with music and ceremony. Initially, the husband and wife acted out of necessity, fearing that the new wife would not meet the son's expectations. After seeing their deep affection for each other, the old couple's hearts were suddenly comforted. Upon seeing her sister, they were surprised that she looked very similar to the new wife. Later, after hearing the friend's detailed account, their hearts finally understood. However, whenever the two women visited and inquired about their health, they still couldn't tell who was the elder sister and who was the younger sister. They then welcomed their mother to live together, and the young master treated her as if she were his own mother. Later, the elder sister had two sons and one daughter, while the younger sister had three sons and one daughter. The young master rose from a position in the Literary Office to become a Minister of State, and submitted a memorial to express his feelings. Both sisters were enfeoffed as ladies, and their mother was also enfeoffed.

    469

    里乘子曰：天下事有由死而复生，由拙而反巧者，此类是也。初，公子自幸奇遇，及一再访之，而竟无其人，方疑见神见怪，病不能兴，以为死灰决无复然之理矣。及群出下策，权宜行事，冀释其疑。是诚掩耳盗铃，不可谓不拙也。迨至穷原竟委，剥蕉见心，始知姊妹花开，天然一色，于是因错就错，卒使英皇遂愿，甥馆偕归。至是，拙者反巧，而死者亦因之俱生矣。此事吴人多能言之，每过太岁祠，辄啧啧称道不置云。

    Explanation

    469

    The Master of the Village said: In the world, there are cases where things revive from death and transform from clumsiness to skill, and this is one such instance. At first, the young man hoped for a rare opportunity, but after visiting twice, there was still no one, and he began to doubt if it was a matter of spirits or strange occurrences. He was ill and could not rise, thinking that a dead ember would never reignite. When he resorted to desperate measures and acted with temporary expedients in the hope of dispelling his doubts, this was indeed like covering one's ears while stealing a bell, and it cannot be called anything but clumsy. When he finally traced the matter to its root and uncovered the truth, he realized that the sisters' flowers bloomed naturally in the same color. Thus, he made the best of a mistake and ultimately fulfilled the emperor's wish, with the nephew returning home with him. At this point, from clumsiness to skill, and the dead were also revived because of it. Many people from Wu can tell this story, and every time they passed by the God of Age's temple, they would repeatedly praise it.

    470

    雄黄弹
    　　
    　　相传雄黄得山川灵气，三千年结成圆颗，光明滑泽，形类弹丸，俗名之曰“雄黄弹”。气辛且馥，蚺蟒触之辄死。其大者如鸡卵，小者如龙眼。愈大愈可宝贵，未易轻得。维扬有名妓杨少女者，名巽，二字巽巽，色艺冠其俦侣。年甫及笄，声誉极隆，客非具厚贽不见。楚北舒生，故世家子，容止甚都，才华丰蔚。以父执某公守扬州，为司书记。慕姬名，厚贽往见。鸨媪导至曲房，令坐少待。久之，两婢扶姬出。姬时小极新瘥，蓬首裹青绡帕，缟衣素裳，绝无修饰。而黑发白肉，明眸皓齿，光彩倍觉照人。见生，展问邦族，彼此略一酬对，意甚相洽。日昳，生欲兴辞，姬笑止之曰：“君去何太急也？如不嫌仓卒主人，即屈晚餐何如？”生喜出非望，亟答曰：“唯唯。

    Explanation

    470

    Cinnabar Pellets

    It is said that cinnabar, having acquired the vital energy of mountains and rivers, forms into round pellets over three thousand years. These pellets are bright and smooth, resembling small balls, and are commonly called "cinnabar pellets." They have a pungent and fragrant aroma, and when touched by a python or cobra, they kill them instantly. The larger ones are about the size of a chicken's egg, while the smaller ones are about the size of a lychee. The larger the cinnabar pellet, the more precious it is, and it is not easily obtained.

    In Weiyang, there was a famous courtesan named Yang Shao Nv, whose name was Xun, and her courtesy name was also Xun. Her beauty and talent surpassed those of her peers. She was just turned eighteen, and her reputation was extremely high. Visitors could not see her unless they brought a substantial gift. A scholar named Shu from the north of Chu, from an old noble family, had a very handsome appearance and demeanor, and he was endowed with abundant and rich talent. Because his father's friend, a certain official, was governing Yangzhou, he served as a secretary. He admired the courtesan's reputation and brought a substantial gift to visit her. The madam led him to a secluded room and asked him to sit and wait for a moment. After a while, two maids helped the courtesan come out. The courtesan had just recovered from a minor illness and had her hair loose, wrapped in a green silk scarf, wearing a white robe and a plain skirt, completely unadorned. With black hair, white skin, bright eyes, and white teeth, her radiance was even more noticeable. When she saw the scholar, she asked about his family and background, and they exchanged a few polite words, getting along very well. When the sun was high in the afternoon, the scholar wanted to take his leave. The courtesan smiled and stopped him, saying, "Why are you leaving so quickly? If you do not mind the hurried hospitality of the host, would you stay for dinner?" The scholar was delighted and eagerly replied, "Yes, yes."

    471

    ”饭毕，姬命婢秉烛导生入己室。室中奁具之外，琴书图画，罗列满案。生故善琴，取壁间琴解囊视之，朱弦玉轸，装潢精洁，随手挑拨，其声清越异常。姬曰：“君亦解此耶？”生曰：“偶尔为之。此琴甚古，卿从何处得来？”姬曰：“此卞玉京物也。鹾贾出兼金购以见贻。君不见腹有款识耶？”生视琴腹镌隶书“四善”二字，下有行楷书铭曰：“风吹空桑，水声浪浪。花天月明，心清意凉。”款署“乙酉春三月玉京道人”九字。生阅之，极为称赞。谓：“卿蓄此，必是高手。请试一挥，愿为洗耳。”姬曰：“久未弄此，今既遇赏音，不敢藏拙。倘有舛误，乞赐指点。”乃高卷翠袖，轻舒玉腕，为谱《合欢》之词，曰：“君何翩翩兮妾何娟娟？未见兮心相怜，既见兮心相欢。花合欢兮并蒂长眷，人合欢兮如意延年。

    Explanation

    471

    After the meal, the lady ordered a maid to hold a candle and lead the scholar into her room. Outside the dressing case in the room, musical instruments, books, paintings, and calligraphy were arranged on the table. The scholar was skilled at playing the qin, and he took the qin from the wall, opening the case to examine it. The strings were red, the tuning pegs were jade, and the decoration was refined and clean. With a casual touch, the sound was exceptionally clear and resonant. The lady said, "Do you also understand this?" The scholar replied, "I occasionally play it. This qin is very old. Where did you get it?" The lady answered, "It belongs to Bian Yujing. A salt merchant paid a high price to obtain it and presented it to me. Have you not seen the inscription on the belly of the qin?" The scholar saw the two characters "四善" (Four Virtues) engraved in clerical script on the belly of the qin, below which were inscriptions in running script: "The wind blows through empty mulberry trees, the water sounds like waves. Under the bright moon, flowers bloom, and the heart feels clear and cool." The inscription reads "Yujing Taoist in the third month of the Year of the Rooster." The scholar read it and highly praised it. He said, "You must be a master to have such a qin. Please play one piece, and I will listen attentively." The lady replied, "I haven't played it in a long time, but now that I have met someone who appreciates music, I dare not hide my clumsiness. If there are any mistakes, please give me guidance." She then raised her green sleeves high and gracefully extended her white wrists, composing a piece called "Héhuān" (Joyful Union). The lyrics went: "How graceful you are, and how beautiful I am. Before meeting, our hearts were fond of each other; after meeting, our hearts were joyful. Flowers unite in joy, growing together in eternal affection; people unite in joy, wishing for a long life."

    472

    皆大欢喜兮合人天，适所愿兮永弗谖。”弹毕，笑曰：“弦涩手生，君其勿哂。”生击节称妙。见案有薛涛笺，走笔书一绝赠姬曰：“尘海茫茫几赏音？玉人为谱白头吟。多情惟有红桥月，不照吹箫照鼓琴。”题毕，持示姬曰：“布鼓雷门，不值一笑。”姬微哂曰：“白头吟三字甚好，未审可能成谶。”生听夜漏二下，欲起作别。姬低声问：“君居停较牛奇章何如？”生会意曰：“小生有愧樊川，居停怜才，则较胜牛相。”姬面发赤，笑曰：“果尔，君何妨在此少叙。”生大喜，遂止弗去。人谓姬平日遇生客之风雅者，或陪一枰，或酬一画，至厚者赠一诗，曾未有作歌而以琴谱之者，况一见留髡，尤为异数。妒生者心殊不平，使人潜谮于太守。太守笑谢之曰：“君休饶舌，使太牢公笑我。

    Explanation

    472

    All were delighted, harmonizing humans and heaven, fulfilling one's wishes forever without forgetting. After finishing, he smiled and said, "The strings are out of tune, and my hands are unaccustomed; please do not laugh." The player clapped in admiration and saw a sheet of Xue Tao's paper on the table, writing a poem in a hurry to give to the lady:

    "In the vast sea of dust, how many true appreciators? The jade person composes the 'White-haired Lament.' Only the Red Bridge Moon is full of emotion, not illuminating the flute but the zither."

    After finishing, he held it up to show the lady and said, "A humble drum in the gate of thunder, not worth a laugh." The lady smiled slightly and said, "The phrase 'White-haired Lament' is very good, but it is uncertain if it might become a prophecy." The player listened to the night clock striking two, wanting to get up and say goodbye. The lady whispered, "How does your host compare to Niu Sengru?" The player understood and said, "I am ashamed to compare myself to Fan川, but my host values talent and is more generous than Niu Sengru." The lady's face turned red, and she smiled, "If that is so, why don't you stay a little longer to chat?" The player was very happy and stayed, not leaving. People said that the lady, in her daily life, would sometimes accompany a guest with a game of go, sometimes reciprocate with a painting, and at most, give a poem. She had never sung a song or composed a poem with a zither, and especially not kept a guest overnight, which was an unprecedented event. Those who envied the player were very unhappy and sent someone to secretly slander him to the governor. The governor laughed and thanked them, saying, "You stop talking nonsense; let the great official laugh at me."

    473

    ”生闻而喜，述于姬，两人同声称感。自是益无忌惮，夙夜必偕，殆有终焉之志。客来，姬概谢不见。媪以生为太守客，多不敢与校。亡何，太守内擢京卿。生恋姬，不肯偕行。而客囊垂罄，益不能厌媪所欲，平日妒生者又从而交谤于媪，媪益嗛之。生来，尝拒不令与姬见。姬私谓生曰：“始君为太守客，凡事得恣所欲，媪尝敢怒而不敢言。今已矣，平日妒君者皆欲报怨，媪日来颜色，君当窥之。脱不早见几，且不免于祸。”因脱金缠臂一双纳生腕，又赠白金一铤，趣令速去。且曰：“妾年方十八，君方二十有二。请待君三年，以补司勋之恨。尔时齿各未衰，为欢有日。以君才华门第，何患不出人头地。尚其怒力进取，好自为之，莫怪妾此时太恝也。千万珍重自爱。”生泣听姬言，一语一首肯，不得已，挥泪而别。

    Explanation

    473

    The young man heard and was pleased, and told it to Ji. Both of them cried out in gratitude. From then on, they became even more unrestrained, being together day and night, almost as if they had the intention of making it their final resting place. When guests came, Ji generally declined to see them. The old woman regarded the young man as a guest of the governor, and most dared not argue with her. Not long after, the governor was promoted to a high position in the capital. The young man was attached to Ji and did not want to go with him. But the guest's purse was nearly empty, and he could no longer satisfy the old woman's desires. Those who had envied the young man in the past joined in criticizing him to the old woman, and she grew more resentful. When the young man came, she often refused to let him see Ji. Ji privately told the young man, "At first, you were the governor's guest, and you could do whatever you wanted; the old woman used to dare to be angry but not to speak out. But that is over now, and those who envied you in the past all want to repay their grievances. The old woman's demeanor, you should observe it. If you do not act quickly, you will not escape disaster." She then took off a pair of golden bracelets and put them on the young man's wrist, and gave him a piece of white gold, urging him to leave quickly. She also said, "I am only eighteen, and you are twenty-two. Please wait for you for three years, to make up for the regret of the official. At that time, our teeth have not yet decayed, and we will have days of happiness. Given your talent and family background, why worry about not standing out. Continue to strive and advance, take good care of yourself. Do not blame me for being so indifferent now. Take good care of yourself." The young man listened to Ji's words with tears, agreeing with each word, and reluctantly, he wiped away his tears and said goodbye.

    474

    乃将白金略偿宿逋，趁舟旋楚。比至汉皋，计距家仅百馀里，以无资赁车，又无傔从，襆被徒行。日暮失路，寄宿邮亭。跋涉劳顿，一偃仰便已熟睡。平明，见巨蟒僵死身旁，大惊，不知何故。俄顷，土人毕至，见生，讶问姓名，多有知者。群谓此蟒伤人甚多，方苦无计诛除，今忽就歼，君身有何宝物？生言无之。群不信，试展其被，见巾裹一物，大于鸡卵。有识者曰：“此雄黄弹也。君有此至宝，岂不自知也耶？”先是，姬每于暑月，辄患虫蚁，有漂水药商赠姬此物，置诸枕畔，凡虿蠍及蝇蚋蜰蚤之属，果尽灭迹。生无事尝取把玩，别时无意携裹巾中。因识者见问，诡称祖遗，向实不知何用。其人具言其宝贵难得，群争传观，咋舌称异。爰共将死蟒剥皮刳肉，刮鳞解骨，共得大小珠无算。

    Explanation

    474

    He then used white gold to roughly settle his old debts and took a boat to return to Chu. When he arrived at Hanshui, he calculated that he was only about a hundred miles from home. Because he had no money to hire a carriage and had no attendants or companions, he wrapped his bedding and walked on foot. At dusk, he lost his way and stayed overnight in a post station. After a long and tiring journey, as soon as he lay down, he fell into a deep sleep. At dawn, he saw a huge python lying dead beside him, greatly alarmed and not knowing why. In a short while, local people all arrived, surprised and asked for his name. Many of them knew him. They said that this python had injured many people and they were struggling to find a way to eliminate it. Now it had suddenly been killed, and they asked, "What valuable item do you have on your body?" He said he had none. They did not believe him and tried to unfold his blanket, seeing an object wrapped in a cloth, larger than a chicken's egg. A knowledgeable person said, "This is a ball of realgar. You have such a precious item, how could you not know about it?" Previously, his wife often suffered from insects during the hot months, and a water merchant gave this item to his wife, placing it beside her pillow. All scorpions, mosquitoes, flies, and fleas were completely eliminated. He sometimes took it out to play with when he had nothing to do, and when he left, he accidentally carried it wrapped in a cloth. Because the knowledgeable person asked, he falsely claimed it was inherited from his ancestors, but he had actually never known its use. The person explained its great value and rarity, and they all competed to pass it around and looked at it with amazement. They then collectively skinned and cut open the dead python, scraped off the scales and removed the bones, and obtained countless pearls of various sizes.

    475

    其额上一珠大如鱼目，遂以归生。并各具酒食，延生至家，小住数日，以车送归。生至家，喜老母无恙。问知妻已物故，不胜伤感。每忆姬约，誓不再娶。将所赠缠臂金变易，营负郭田数亩，养母读书。明年，一战捷于乡，以母老，不愿应礼闱试。又越两年，计已逾约，于无人时，追忆坠欢，不禁潸焉出涕。转念业已爽约，玉人或别有主，思维展转，已拚割爱。会有事过洞庭，猝遭暴风。适巡抚某公之任楚南，官舻势在危急，某公见满湖客舟漂覆殆尽，惟生舟安稳无恙，异之。亟命榜人杈依其旁，果亦转危为安。命材官持刺召生至，叙及先世，固有世谊。因问生有何术，令舟无患，生殊茫然。公忽见生角巾上缀蟒珠，瞪视诧曰：“君从何处得定风珠？此诚希世之珍，无怪涉险不惊也。”生始恍然。

    Explanation

    475

    On his forehead, there was a pearl as large as a fish's eye. He then took her back to his home. Each prepared food and drink, inviting him to their home, where he stayed for a few days before being sent back by carriage. Upon returning home, he was glad to find his old mother unharmed. He learned that his wife had passed away, which filled him with deep sorrow. Every time he recalled the promise made to her, he swore never to remarry. He converted the gold bracelet given to him into money and cultivated several acres of land near the city wall to support his mother and himself. The following year, he won a victory in the local examination, but due to his mother's advanced age, he did not wish to take the imperial examination. Two more years passed, and the time had already exceeded the agreed period. When no one was around, he would recall past joys and could not help but shed tears. He then realized that he had already broken the agreement, and the woman he loved might have found another. After much thought, he resolved to let go of his love. One day, as he happened to pass through Dongting Lake, he suddenly encountered a fierce storm. Just then, a certain official, on a mission to the southern part of Chu, found his official boat in grave danger. The official saw all the boats in the lake capsizing almost completely, but only the protagonist's boat remained safe and sound. He found it strange and immediately ordered the boatman to follow closely beside it. Indeed, the situation turned from danger to safety. The official then ordered a clerk to bring the protagonist to him, and they talked about their ancestors, noting that there was a long-standing friendship. The official asked the protagonist what method he used to make the boat safe, and the protagonist was completely at a loss. Suddenly, the official noticed a pearl on the protagonist's headband and stared in amazement, saying, "Where did you get the wind-stilling pearl? This is truly a rare treasure of the world, it's no wonder you were not afraid of the danger." The protagonist suddenly realized that the pearl was indeed a wind-stilling pearl, a mythical object that could calm storms.

    476

    因取以献曰：“此珠先世所遗，不意竟能定风。公出入王命，此所必需，敢献以备济川之用，聊代羔雁。”公大喜。以生谊属通家，且共患难，令夫人暨众姬出见，并示以此珠之异。乃绮罗粲列，杨姬亦在其中。生与姬相视错愕，不觉双泪承睫。公评问何故，两人跽陈巅末，公亟掖起，掀髯笑曰：“数有前定。老夫方愧无以报赠珠之惠，请效合浦还珠，用答嘉贶，何如？”生顿首谢，并叩数定之说。公谓：“前过扬州，闻姬通翰墨，以三千金为脱籍，将令司内记室。便祷诸露筋祠，默问后事，得第五签云：‘碧云舒卷碧天晴，杨柳依依送客行。合浦还珠张乐地，公真一笑比河清。’今味其词，前二句明点两姓，三句暗切洞庭，四句调侃老夫，谑而不虐。谓非数有前定耶！”生亦啧啧称奇。

    Explanation

    476

    He took the pearl and presented it, saying: “This pearl was left by our ancestors, yet I did not expect it to calm the wind. Since you are entrusted with royal commands, this is essential, and I dare to present it for use in crossing rivers, to serve as a substitute for lambs and geese.” The official was greatly pleased. Because they were connected by familial ties and had shared hardships, he ordered his wife and concubines to appear, and showed them the pearl’s extraordinary nature. The women, dressed in fine silks, stood in a dazzling array, and among them was Lady Yang. The young man and Lady Yang looked at each other in astonishment, and unconsciously, tears welled up in their eyes. The official calmly inquired as to the reason, and the two of them knelt and recounted the full story. He quickly helped them up, lifting his beard in a laugh and saying: “Fate was already decided. I am only now ashamed that I have no way to repay the kindness of the pearl’s gift. I beg to emulate the return of the pearl to Hépǔ, to repay your generous offering. How does that sound?” The young man bowed deeply in thanks, and inquired further about the theory of predestination. The official said: “Previously, when I was in Yangzhou, I heard that Lady Yang was skilled in literature and calligraphy, so I spent three thousand gold pieces to release her from bondage, intending to appoint her as a secretary in the inner office. I then prayed at the Lùjīn Temple, quietly inquiring about future matters, and received the fifth oracle, which read: ‘The blue clouds unfold, the blue sky is clear; the willows sway gently as they send off travelers. The pearl returns to Hépǔ, the land of music; your Excellency’s smile is as clear as the river’s surface.’ Now, considering the words, the first two lines clearly name the two families, the third line subtly alludes to Dongting Lake, and the fourth line playfully teases me, jesting without being cruel. Can this not be said to be predestined?” The young man also expressed admiration in astonishment.

    477

    公命人送生与姬过船，姬衣饰值数千金，尽令携去，并馈金助妆。两人叩谢公与夫人，感激涕零而别。初，生母闻洞庭大风，多遭水厄，甚切倚闾之望。今见子携佳妇归，大喜。生为母备述前事。乃出金益治田庐，且蓄仆婢，居然类素封。家母无事，与姬围棋，或看姬作画弹琴，甚欢，因谓生曰：“前我以无人侍奉，故不令汝远离。兹得汝妇，事我甚孝。汝如再以我藉口不肯计偕，是恋汝妇，即为不孝。汝其奋志显扬，以慰老怀。”生重违母命，是科捷南宫，观政吏部。不数年，荐擢正郎，出守扬州，板舆迎养。旧地重来，凡向所媒蘖者，悉置不校，口碑藉甚。姬受诰命，封恭人。每鱼轩出，昔之俦侣俱侧目窃视，交口称羡。生常与姬话旧事，爰倩画师写《弹珠图》，以志不忘所自。一时题咏甚夥，传为美谈。

    Explanation

    477

    The lord ordered someone to escort the protagonist and his wife across the boat. His wife's clothing and ornaments were worth thousands of gold pieces, and he ordered them to take everything with them, along with gold to help with her dowry. The two knelt and thanked the lord and lady, leaving with tears of gratitude streaming down their faces. Initially, the protagonist's mother had been deeply worried about the severe storm in the Dongting Lake area, fearing that her son would suffer from the dangers of the water. Now, seeing her son return with a beautiful wife, she was overjoyed. The protagonist recounted all that had happened to his mother. They then took out gold to improve their fields and houses, and hired servants and maids, becoming as wealthy as a noble family. His mother, having nothing to do, played go with his wife, or watched her paint and play the qin, and they were very happy. She then said to her son, "Before, because there was no one to serve me, I did not allow you to leave. Now that you have a wife who serves me very well, if you use me as an excuse to refuse to go with her, it means you are attached to your wife, which is a sign of filial impiety. You must strive to achieve greatness and bring comfort to my old heart." The protagonist could not disobey his mother's command, and in that year, he passed the imperial examination and was assigned to observe government affairs in the Ministry of Personnel. Within a few years, he was recommended and promoted to the rank of Zhenglang, and was sent out to govern Yangzhou. His mother was welcomed and cared for with a sedan chair. Returning to the old place, he forgave and did not hold grudges against anyone who had previously slandered or provoked him, and his reputation became very good. His wife received imperial edicts and was granted the title of Gongren. Whenever she traveled in her luxurious carriage, her former companions would look at her with envy or respect and praise her unanimously. The protagonist often talked to his wife about their past, and he commissioned a painter to create a picture titled "The Bead Game," to remember their origins. At that time, many poems and inscriptions were written about it, and it became a beautiful story.

    478

    里乘子曰：昔牛奇章、裴晋国二公，怜才重义，百世而下，闻其风者，无不津津乐道。乃舒生一生而两遇其人，可谓盛矣。《弹珠图》题咏甚夥，不知至今尚在人间否？杜司勋扬州梦后，得此足以补恨。

    Explanation

    478

    Lǐ Chéngzǐ said: In ancient times, the two esteemed figures Niu Qichang and Pei Jinguo were known for their care for talent and their emphasis on righteousness. Through the ages, those who heard of their influence have all spoken of them with great interest. Shū Shēng encountered these two figures twice in his lifetime, which can truly be called fortunate. There are many inscriptions and poems dedicated to "Dàn Zhū Tú" (The Ball Diagram), but it is unknown whether it still exists in the world today. After Du Mu's dream in Yangzhou, having this would be sufficient to alleviate any regret.

    479

    有外山王
    　　
    　　明成祖文皇帝，既有天下，恶靖难诸忠，戮及十族，薄海疑惧，盗贼蜂起，帝窃患之。尝密使爪士侦察四方，又手诏疆臣，随时剿抚兼施，并许便宜行事，其英谋睿断，多史册所未载。儿时闻野老言，帝初定鼎燕京，山东巡抚某公入觐陛辞，帝赐画一轴。某公旋任展视之，见所画大海汪洋，重峦叠，中有楼阁，似是宫殿。并无题咏款识。公朝夕思索画意，忽然有悟。麾下某游击，短小精悍，素极机警。公召至，屏去左右，出另纸仿画一帧授之，曰：“此中有绿林之豪，访之最确。汝其为我生致之，宜秘宜速，功成不吝厚赏。不则勿归也。”公令素严，某不敢辞，贸贸然袖画归，泣别妻子。至登莱，孑身浮海，听其所之。值飓风，漂舟次一山下，舍舟裹糇上山，欲穷其境。经旬，见前山树木阴翳，中隐隐有楼阁，形势与画略肖。疾行十馀里至其处，见宫殿一所，巍然类王者居。门外白石铺地，洁无纤尘。

    Explanation

    479

    The King of the Outer Mountains

    The Emperor Yongle of the Ming dynasty, having secured the realm, harbored hatred for the loyalists of the Jingnan Rebellion, and in his wrath, executed them along with their ten extended families. Fear spread across the land, and bandits and rebels arose like a swarm. The emperor secretly feared this unrest. He once secretly dispatched his spies to investigate the four directions, and also personally issued a decree to the frontier generals, ordering them to employ both suppression and conciliation as circumstances required, and granting them the authority to act as they saw fit. His brilliant strategies and wise judgments were often unrecorded in the annals of history.

    When I was a child, I heard an old man from the countryside say that when the emperor first established his capital in Yanjing, a certain official who served as governor of Shandong came to court to bid farewell. The emperor presented him with a scroll. Upon taking up his post, the official unrolled the painting and saw an image of vast, boundless seas and towering, overlapping mountains, with a pavilion in the center that seemed to resemble a palace. There were no inscriptions or verses on the painting. The official pondered its meaning day and night, and suddenly came to a realization.

    One of his subordinates, a certain captain of the guard, was short in stature but sharp and alert, and always extremely vigilant. The official summoned him, dismissed his attendants, and gave him another sheet of paper, on which he had drawn a copy of the painting, saying, "Within this painting lies a leader of the greenwood bandits. You must find him with the greatest accuracy. Bring him to me alive—act in secrecy and haste. If you succeed, I will not hesitate to reward you handsomely. If you fail, you will not return."

    The official was known for his strict commands, and the captain dared not refuse. He took the drawing, concealed it in his sleeve, and returned home, weeping as he bade farewell to his wife and children. He set sail alone across the sea, allowing the winds and waves to determine his course. A typhoon struck, and his boat drifted to the foot of a mountain. He abandoned his boat, wrapped his provisions, and climbed the mountain, determined to explore its depths. After ten days, he saw that the trees ahead formed a dense shade, and in the distance, a pavilion was faintly visible—its layout roughly resembling the painting. He hurried for more than ten miles until he arrived at the place, where he saw a palace standing majestically, as if it were the residence of a king. Outside the gates, white stones paved the ground, spotless and free of the smallest speck of dust.

    480

    壮夫百数十人，超距击刺，气象雄猛。见某，叱问何来，内一人曰：“勿多言，第拘去请夫人处分。”遂以索反接其手，驱入四重门内，系诸檐梧。某惴惴不知何处，自分身死异域，即亦不畏。俄闻呵殿声，传言夫人升殿，命将某带进问话。又入一重门，见大殿左右武夫数十人，皆躬擐甲胄，佩刀剑，屏息侍立。上坐一女子，年二十以来，珠冠绣袍，颜色姣艳。叱问：“何物奸宄，敢犯秘境！汝有几首？不畏死耶！”某伏地泣陈身系客商，遭风覆舟，无意误犯，罪该万死。女子又问邦族，某谓晋产。女子喜曰：“我亦晋产，与汝有桑梓之谊，合是天意。”命左右释其缚，并令更衣授食，“在此少住，俟主人翁来，筹送汝归。”某顿首谢。左右导至客房，供给精美。惟见诸人行踪诡秘，无从诘其端绪，时切犹疑，吉凶不能自决。一夜漏二下，将就枕，忽两婢秉烛叩门，传夫人命，召某入内室。见夫人频蹙危坐，某屈膝欲拜，夫人急止之，并赐隅坐。

    Explanation

    480

    A hundred or so strong men, skilled in long-range attacks and thrusts, stood with a bold and fierce appearance. Seeing someone, they shouted and asked where he came from. One of them said, "Do not speak much, just take him away and let the lady decide." They then tied his hands behind his back with a rope and drove him into a fourfold gate, tying him to a tree near the eaves. He was trembling, not knowing where he was, and thought he would die in a foreign land, but he was not afraid. Soon he heard the sound of a procession, and it was said that the lady had ascended the hall. She ordered that he be brought in for questioning. They entered another gate and saw dozens of warriors on either side of the grand hall, all wearing armor and helmets, carrying swords, and standing silently. On the upper seat sat a woman in her twenties, wearing a jeweled crown and an embroidered robe, with a beautiful and radiant appearance. She shouted, "What kind of villain dare to trespass in this secret place! How many heads do you have? Are you not afraid of death!" He fell to the ground and wept, explaining that he was a merchant, blown off course by the wind, causing his ship to capsize, and he accidentally trespassed; he deserved to die a thousand deaths. The woman asked about his origin, and he said he was from Jin. She said happily, "I am also from Jin, and we have the bond of hometown friendship; this must be the will of heaven." She ordered the attendants to release his bindings and also ordered him to change clothes and be given food, "Stay here for a while, wait for the master to come, and he will arrange for you to return home." He bowed deeply in gratitude. The attendants led him to a guest room, where the provisions were fine and exquisite. He only saw that the people were acting mysteriously, and he could not question the beginning or end of it. He was constantly in doubt, unable to determine whether it was good or bad. At night, when the water clock struck two, as he was about to go to bed, two maids came with candles and knocked on the door, conveying the lady's command, summoning him into the inner chamber. He saw the lady with a worried expression, sitting upright. He knelt to bow, but the lady quickly stopped him and allowed him to sit in a corner.

    481

    夫人问曰：“汝知此为何地，此间主人翁为何如人耶？”某对曰：“不知。”夫人曰：“主人某甲，固海盗之魁也。此山名有外山，人民多穴居，房舍甚少。物产丰饶，家给户足，向无统属。主人翁近以威胁之，令岁供赋税。此山纵横四万馀里，主人宫室凡三十二处，每处或岁一二至，或间岁一至。主人初号有外山主，近号有外山王，其人孔武有力，日可行二千馀里；明能察远，身不在此，此间事纤末俱知，即君此来，当已备悉。”因问某究为何事，质言勿隐。某窥夫人意不恶，遂以直告。夫人叹曰：“我家大同城内，父富有巨万。以春日郊游，被主人飞劫至此。今年二十有七，已阅十一寒暑矣。他日君归，能为寄语父母，感且没齿。”某起立曰：“倘托夫人福庇，万一生还，敢不如命！”夫人曰：“主人去此年馀，默计旦夕且至。汝见时须道其实。主人尚质，稍涉虚言，恐察及隐情，则齑粉矣。切记勿忘。”嘱毕，仍命前婢送某归寝。

    Explanation

    481

    The lady asked, "Do you know what this place is, and who is the master of this place?" The narrator replied, "I do not know." The lady said, "The master is Mr. A, the leader of pirates. This mountain is called the Outer Mountain, and the people mostly live in caves, with few houses. The land is abundant in resources, and each household is self-sufficient, with no previous ruler. The master has recently threatened them, making them pay yearly tribute. This mountain stretches over 40,000 miles, and the master has 32 palaces, each visited once or twice a year or once every two years. The master was originally called the Master of the Outer Mountain and is now called the King of the Outer Mountain. He is extremely strong and powerful, capable of traveling over 2,000 miles in a day; he can see far and clearly, even when not present here, knowing every detail of this place. Even your arrival here must have been known to him." She then asked the narrator to speak frankly without hiding anything. The narrator noticed that the lady's intentions were not evil and thus spoke openly. The lady sighed and said, "My family lives in the city of Datong, and my father is very wealthy. On a spring outing, I was kidnapped by the master and brought here. I am now 27 years old and have been here for 11 years. When you return, could you please convey my gratitude and that I will never forget it to my parents?" The narrator stood up and said, "If it is due to the lady's protection, and if I manage to return alive, I dare not fail to follow your command!" The lady said, "The master left here over a year ago and is expected to return soon. When you see him, you must tell him the truth. The master is straightforward, and any slight falsehood might lead to the discovery of hidden matters, and then you will be in danger. Remember this well." After giving these instructions, she still sent a maid to escort the narrator back to bed.

    482

    亡何，西南风大作，闻众哗言：“大王行且至矣。”盖某甲制铁甲一领，能避五兵，上缀铁铃一百八颗，名曰“铁铃甲”。每披甲顺风凌空行，五十里即闻其声，使人预知有备。铃颗重一十二两，摘铃以击人，百步之外，百不失一，亦绝技也。时天色微曛，新月东上。某伏暗地窥之，见铺毡张幔，灿列灯烛，夫人华妆率众环跽门外。但闻空际铃声琅琅，自远渐近，约二刻许，一莽男子自空而下，紫面虬髯，目乌喙。脱去铁甲，内着绣裲裆，足着吉莫靴，仗剑昂然视众，略一点首。夫人率众环拜欢呼，拥簇入门。殿上鼓乐伧伫，肆筵设席。某甲上坐，夫人跽进三爵，起，坐左侧侍饮。少选，庖人进蒸豚，甲拔佩剑，脔切大嚼。徐问别后事，夫人唯唯以对。又问：“有远人来未？”夫人谓：“某月日有某至此。”甲笑谓：“我固早料及之。”即命某来问话。某至，但长揖不跽。甲问：“汝居何官？”某忆夫人所嘱，直答曰：“忝官游击，殊不称职。

    Explanation

    482

    Shortly thereafter, a strong southwest wind blew, and the crowd shouted excitedly, "The Great King is about to arrive!" It was said that a certain man named Mr. A had crafted a suit of iron armor that could withstand five types of weapons. Attached to the armor were one hundred and eight iron bells, which he called "Iron Bell Armor." Whenever he wore the armor and flew through the air with the wind, his presence could be heard from fifty miles away, giving people ample warning to prepare. Each bell weighed twelve taels, and when plucked, they could strike someone from a hundred paces, never missing. This was also a remarkable skill. At the time, the sky was faintly dim, and a new moon rose in the east. I lay hidden in the dark, watching from a distance, and saw carpets laid out and tents set up, with bright lights and candles arranged. The lady, dressed in elaborate makeup, led a group in a kneeling position outside the door. Only the sound of the bells ringing in the air could be heard, growing louder as they approached from afar, taking about two hours. A rugged man descended from the sky, with a purple face, curly beard, piercing eyes, and a black beak. He removed the iron armor, revealing embroidered armor underneath, and wore Gimo boots. He stood tall and confident, holding a sword and looking at the crowd, nodding slightly. The lady led the group in a circle, bowing and cheering, and they escorted him inside. Drums and music played continuously on the dais, and a feast was set up. Mr. A sat at the head of the table, and the lady knelt and presented him with three cups of wine. After drinking, she stood up and sat to his left, serving him. Soon, a cook brought in steamed pork, and Mr. A drew his sword, slicing and eating it heartily. He then asked about recent events, and the lady replied politely. He asked, "Has anyone from afar arrived yet?" The lady replied, "On a certain day in a certain month, a certain person arrived here." Mr. A smiled and said, "I had already anticipated this." He then ordered the person to come and speak with him. The person arrived, only bowing instead of kneeling. Mr. A asked, "What office do you hold?" The person recalled the lady's instructions and replied straightforwardly, "I am ashamed to hold the rank of a guerrilla officer, and I am not very competent."

    483

    ”又问：“汝来何为也？”曰：“巡抚某公慕大王威名，欲一望见颜色，故使末将为致殷情。”甲冷笑曰：“此燕藩之命，某公焉足知我？”某曰：“末将实奉某公令，不知其他。”甲曰：“汝胆亦非小弱，居然不远而至。岂谓我剑不利耶？”某对曰：“某公将令素严，大王所知也。明知违令死，奉令而远犯虎威亦死，等死也。违令其死速；奉令而乞怜于大王，倘怜末将之死，肯赐一行，以大王神威，行止可以自由，某公又将奈之何？且大王若去，某公方将结纳之不暇，岂敢有他图哉？果尔，则大王不过一举趾，而末将即可因之不死。他日馀生，皆出大王之赐矣。惟大王怜之！”甲沈思久之，曰：“汝且退，容细思之。”某拜谢而出。甲亦罢席。越数月，某见甲曾不述及前事，亦不敢促迫，姑耐候之。一日，忽闻甲大宴宾客，为某祖饯，某窃自庆。顷之，使者来召，某喜，从而去。见甲冠服立阼阶，某至，笑执其手，迎入大殿。

    Explanation

    483

    Another asked, "Why have you come?" He replied, "Governor so-and-so admires the Duke's reputation and wishes to see the Duke's face once, so he has sent me to convey his sincere intentions." The Duke laughed coldly and said, "This is an order from the Prince of Yan. Governor so-and-so is not worthy to know me." He said, "I truly follow the order of Governor so-and-so and do not know anything else." The Duke said, "Your courage is not small or weak, and you have come all the way here. Do you think my sword is not sharp?" He replied, "Governor so-and-so's orders are usually strict, as the Duke knows. Knowing that disobeying the order would lead to death, obeying the order and facing the Duke's wrath would also lead to death. It's the same either way. Disobeying the order would lead to quicker death; obeying the order and pleading for mercy from the Duke, if the Duke spares my life, would grant me a visit. With the Duke's divine power, I could go and come freely, and what could Governor so-and-so do about it? Moreover, if the Duke leaves, Governor so-and-so would be too busy to form alliances and would not dare to have other plans. If that is the case, then the Duke would only need to take a step, and I could be spared from death. Future survival would all be due to the Duke's grace. Only the Duke can show mercy!" The Duke pondered for a long time and said, "You may go back and let me think it over." He bowed and thanked him and left. The Duke also dismissed the feast. After several months, he saw that the Duke never mentioned the previous matter and did not dare to press for it, so he had to wait patiently. One day, suddenly heard that the Duke was hosting a grand banquet to send him off. He secretly rejoiced. Soon, an envoy came to summon him. He was happy and followed him. Seeing the Duke standing on the main steps in formal attire, he arrived, and the Duke smiled and took his hand, welcoming him into the main hall.

    484

    殿中凡设数十席，所谓三十二夫人，及部下谋士武夫，济济毕集。甲一一指道姓名，某俱与为礼。中一席某坐客位，甲坐主位，馀席按班环坐。甲飞三巨觥谓某曰：“今日为君祖饯，须满引勿辞。”某称谢立饮，亦飞三巨觥相酬。甲饮讫，乃掀髯谓众曰：“我忝据此山，十馀年矣。本期与尔曹共图大事。今燕藩部署粗定，已洞悉我底蕴，我复何望？兹某巡抚使某来通殷情，是必燕藩之所指授。已许同某一行，我其不归也矣。

    Explanation

    484

    In the hall, dozens of seats were set up, including the so-called Thirty-Two Ladies and the strategists and warriors under his command, all gathered in full assembly. One by one, he pointed out their names, and they all exchanged greetings. In the center, the guest sat in the seat of honor, while he took the host's place, with the remaining seats arranged in a circle around them. He raised three large cups and said to the guest, "Today, I am hosting a farewell banquet for you. Please drink to the full without hesitation." The guest expressed gratitude and stood to drink, also raising three large cups to reciprocate. After finishing his drink, he raised his beard and addressed the crowd, "I have humbly held this position for over ten years. I had hoped to jointly plan great matters with you all. Now, the Yan fief's arrangements are roughly established, and they have already thoroughly understood my innermost intentions. What can I hope for now? This provincial governor, sent by the Yan fief, has come to convey sincere feelings, which must be their instruction. He has already agreed to go with me, and I will not return."

    485

    ”夫人及众闻之，皆掩面而泣。佥曰：“大王何出此言？以大王神威，即永据此山，亦可优游自适。何必以千金之躯，远涉险阻也？”甲曰：“我意已决，业许之矣，尔曹毋得多言。惟与尔曹约：此去如某巡抚执礼甚恭则已，不然，我匝月必归，再作别计，或未知鹿死谁手。如匝月不归，诸夫人等俱听自便，所有子女、玉帛，尔曹可瓜分之；或入海，或入山，各自为计，慎勿系念瞻顾，徒自取苦也。”众默默相视，不置一词。俱饮不尽欢而散。
    越日，甲召某登舟，并戒众勿送。比至舟中，则大同之夫人在焉。甲指谓某曰：“是与君同乡，烦为寄语其父母，好为安置。渠所携金珠玉宝，一生吃着不尽。”某姑漫应之。问甲共有几子，甲谓：“诸夫人类生而不育，今有娠者尚八人，然我躬不阅，遑恤我后。君亦何必多问也？”某深叹其豁达。于是相与沿途共览山川形胜，甲喟然叹曰：“实不相欺，我初据此山，闻燕藩抗命，屡欲兴一旅之师，前往问罪。

    Explanation

    485

    The women and the crowd heard this and all covered their faces, weeping. They all said, "Why would you say such a thing, Your Majesty? With your divine power, even if you were to stay here forever, you could live comfortably and at ease. Why risk your precious life by venturing far into dangerous territory?" He replied, "My decision is made, it is already promised. You all must not speak further. I only make this agreement with you: if I do not return within a month, I will come back, and we will make other plans, or it may be uncertain who will be the victor. If I do not return within a month, all the wives will be free to do as they please, and all the children, wealth, and property will be divided among you. You may either go to sea or into the mountains, each to your own fate. Be careful not to look back or be attached to the past, for it will only bring you suffering." They all looked at each other silently, not saying a word. They drank but did not fully enjoy themselves and then dispersed.

    The next day, he called someone to board the boat and warned the crowd not to see him off. Upon arriving at the boat, the wife of the governor of Datong was there. He pointed to her and said to the person, "She is from the same hometown as you. Please convey a message to her parents to take good care of her. What she carries in terms of gold, pearls, and jade, will be enough for a lifetime." The person replied vaguely. When asked how many children he had, he said, "The wives generally do not bear children, but there are still eight of them who are pregnant. However, I am not around to see it, so I do not worry about what happens afterward. Why ask more questions?" The person deeply admired his openness. Thus, they traveled together along the way, admiring the mountains and rivers. He sighed and said, "I am not deceiving you, when I first settled in this mountain, I heard that the Yanshan region was defying orders, and I repeatedly wanted to raise an army to punish them."

    486

    既思故主出亡，神器有主。一家之物，仍归一家，天命有归，岂人力所可争哉！”及将至登莱，乃谓某曰：“计日达岸，烦君先驰报巡抚某公，须从我三事，可则行，否则止。”某请其说，则曰：“一、我登岸后，某公须率所属文武，郊迎于五十里外；一、我此去即于巡抚署栖止，进署时我乘舆，某公骑马作先导，洞开重门，由中道直入宅门；一、饮食务极丰腆，每日须择好梨园演剧侑觞，所需犒赏，不得少吝。只此三事，可则行，否则止。”某曰：“诺。

    Explanation

    486

    Having considered that the former ruler has gone into exile, the mandate of heaven now has a rightful possessor. The property of one family should return to that same family; heaven's decree has its destination, how can human effort contend with it!" As we approach Dengla, he then said to me, "Within days of reaching the shore, I ask you to ride ahead and inform Governor So-and-so. There are three things you must do for me; if they are acceptable, we will proceed; otherwise, we will stop." I asked him to explain, and he said, "First, after I land, Governor So-and-so must lead all the officials and military officers to meet me outside the city gates at a distance of fifty miles. Second, upon my arrival, I will stay at the governor's office, and when entering the office, I will ride in a carriage, and Governor So-and-so will ride a horse in front as a guide, opening the heavy doors wide and entering through the main gate in a direct manner. Third, the food and drink must be extremely abundant and rich, and every day, good actors from the imperial theater must perform plays to accompany the wine. The rewards and gratuities required shall not be given sparingly. These are the three things; if they are acceptable, we will proceed; otherwise, we will stop." I replied, "Yes."

    487

    ”达岸，即先驰报。寻复命曰：“某公闻大王至，大喜，所约三事，无不惟命是听。”进署后，某公果执礼甚恭，曲尽绸缪。甫匝月，甲忽谓某公曰：“闻诸公子甚佳，愿请一见。”公即令六子出拜。甲一一相之曰：“某清贵，某方面，某民社，某部曹，某卿贰。惟四公子头角峥嵘，勋业在公之上。”指所佩剑曰：“此出自吴大帝冢中，当日六剑之一，所谓‘流星’者是也。当以相赠。”公为称谢。甲笑谓四公子曰：“今夜与老夫抵足如何？”公笑曰：“童子何知，合当遣事长者。”是夕果使同寝。平明，甲起唤四公子曰：“为我谢而翁。吾事毕矣。”拔所佩剑曰：“请以相赠。”遂自刭而死。撒手以剑授公子，头虽断而身僵立不仆。四公子大骇，趋以告公。公喜，以礼殡殓之。具实入告，帝大喜，爵某公以国公；某游击超擢总戎，并予伯爵。大同夫人以父母命归某游击，封夫人。后四公子由词馆出入将相，以征虏功封爵国公。馀公子所官，亦俱如甲言。

    Explanation

    487

    Upon reaching the shore, he immediately sent a fast report. Soon, he received a further command: "The official has heard that the Great King has arrived and is greatly pleased. The three matters agreed upon, he will do as commanded." After entering the office, the official indeed conducted himself with great respect and courtesy, showing every sign of sincerity. After a month, A suddenly said to the official, "I have heard that your fourth son is excellent; I would like to meet him." The official then had his six sons come out to pay their respects. A examined each of them one by one, saying, "One is clear and noble, one is broad-minded, one is a local official, one is a departmental official, and one is a high-ranking official. Only the fourth son stands out, with achievements surpassing those of the official." Pointing to the sword he wore, he said, "This sword was found in the tomb of the First Emperor of Wu, one of the six swords of that time, known as the 'Meteor.' I will give it to you." The official thanked him. A smiled and said to the fourth son, "How about spending the night with me tonight?" The official laughed and said, "What does a child know? He should attend to the elder." That evening, the fourth son was indeed made to share the bed. At dawn, A woke up and called the fourth son, saying, "Tell your father to thank me. My task is complete." He drew the sword he wore and said, "Please accept it as a gift." Then, he cut his own throat and died. He handed the sword over to the fourth son with his own hands, and although his head was severed, his body remained standing rigidly. The fourth son was greatly alarmed and hurried to inform the official. The official was delighted and treated him with the proper funeral rites. He reported the truth to the emperor, who was very pleased and bestowed the title of Marquis of a State on the official; the general was promoted to Grand General and given the title of Marquis. The lady of Great Harmony, by the command of her parents, returned to the general and was granted the title of lady. Later, the fourth son, promoted through the literary office to become a general or chancellor, was bestowed the title of Marquis of a State for his achievements in subduing the barbarians. The other sons' positions also matched what A had said.

    488

    里乘子曰：昔永乐时，鱼台妖妇唐赛儿谋逆，或借其事撰《女仙外史》。稗说谓为靖难诸臣雪愤，今证以野老所言，或亦有因。惟某甲能知天命，甘心伏剑，使一切篝火狐鸣、妄希非分者观之，亦可爽然自失矣。

    Explanation

    488

    The narrator says: During the Yongle era, a witch named Tang Saini from Yutai County, Shandong Province, led a rebellion against the Ming dynasty. Some may have written the fictional account The History of the Female Immortal based on her story. Popular belief holds that the tale was written to clear the names of officials who supported the Jingnan rebellion. Now, based on what the old people say, there may be some truth to this. Only someone who understands the will of heaven and willingly accepts his fate by committing suicide can serve as a warning to those who spread false rumors and seek what is not rightfully theirs, causing them to suddenly feel lost or ashamed.

    489

    蒋柿姑
    　　
    　　蒋柿姑，楚北士人女也。幼失怙恃，育于孀婶。年及笄，美而慧。以针黹佐婶食用，相依为命。婶有子椒哥，少柿姑三岁，从塾师读，晚归，柿姑必课其日所业若何，意藉自求识字也。久之，竟能粗通文理，婶益爱之。媒妁议婚，高低多不就。里有某公子，素渔于色，偶见柿姑貌，艳之，冒托瓜葛亲，时来相挑。柿姑窥其意，恒避不与相见。某计贫士女当可以利诱，瞷婶他出，特华服而往。柿姑见某来，遽合其户。某脱缠臂金自窗掷，谓柿姑曰：“卿视此物好否？”柿姑大怒，掷还骂曰：“汝卿谁？谁是汝卿！汝以此为好物，盍将去献汝母，固不失为孝。不则给汝姊若妹。似此龌龊物，休污我肉也！”某知不可犯，拾取衔恨而去。婶归，柿姑且哭且诉。

    Explanation

    489

    Shigugu, a Daughter of a Scholar from Northern Chu

    Shigugu was a daughter of a scholar from northern Chu. Orphaned at a young age, she was raised by her widowed aunt. When she reached the age of marriage, she was both beautiful and intelligent. She helped her aunt with sewing and embroidery to support the household, and they relied on each other for survival. Her aunt had a son named Peiguo, who was three years younger than Shigugu and studied under a tutor. Every evening, Peiguo would return home, and Shigugu would teach him what he had learned that day, hoping to learn to read herself. Over time, she was able to understand basic literature, and her aunt loved her even more. Many marriage proposals were made through matchmakers, but Shigugu refused them all.

    There was a young nobleman from the same village who was known for his pursuit of women. One day, he saw Shigugu's beauty and became attracted to her. He pretended to be related to her and often came to court her. Shigugu noticed his intentions and always avoided meeting him. The nobleman thought that a poor scholar's daughter could be tempted with gifts. He waited until her aunt left and then went to her house in fine clothes. When Shigugu saw him coming, she quickly closed the door. The nobleman threw a bracelet through the window and said to Shigugu, "Do you think this is a good thing?" Shigugu was furious and threw the bracelet back, shouting, "Who are you calling me? Who are you calling yours! If you think this is a good thing, why not give it to your mother as a gift, which would make you a good son. Otherwise, give it to your sisters. Such a dirty thing should not defile my flesh!"

    The nobleman knew he could not force her, so he picked up the bracelet and left in anger. When her aunt returned, Shigugu cried and told her what had happened.

    490

    婶性最烈，众所敬畏，诘朝，登某之门，声其罪而痛数之，某匿不敢出，邻里再三缓颊乃已。某羞且忿，从此绝迹。婶有兄子幸生，少孤，母课之读书。十六岁，母殁，遂废读。成人后意气轩昂，性黠而介。家徒四壁，自命甚高。尝道拾遗金，坐待其人而归之，闻者多哂其痴。会岁暮，有同学友鬻业于邻翁，生居间书券。翁酬白金一流，以资润笔。友固与生善，又另赠白金一流。生得金陡制袍服，竟体华美。献岁，诣婶叩贺。婶讶曰：“几时不见，何一旦光采若此？得勿发义财耶？”盖楚人谓掘窖为发义财也。生微笑，漫应之。婶益疑为真，固诘不已。生无奈何，曰：“何必穷究，后自知之。”婶曰：“是矣，愈秘之愈久昌也。”盖楚人每掘窖，虽亲友皆秘不肯告，向有“秘一年，富万千；秘一代，富万载”之说，故婶云然也。夜间，婶悄告柿姑：“幸生大发义财，且秘不肯语我，此事愈秘愈能昌，我亦不便穷究。

    Explanation

    490

    The aunt was known for her fiery temper and was greatly respected by all. The next morning, she went to the door of a certain person, publicly accusing and severely listing their sins. The person hid and dared not come out, and only after the neighbors repeatedly mediated did the situation calm down. The person felt both ashamed and furious, and from then on, they ceased all contact. The aunt had a nephew named Xing Sheng, who was orphaned at a young age. His mother had him study diligently. At sixteen, his mother passed away, and he then stopped studying. After growing up, he was full of pride and arrogance, with a cunning but straightforward nature. His home was poor, with nothing but bare walls, yet he held himself in high regard. Once, he found lost gold on the road and sat waiting for the owner to return to give it back. Those who heard about it often mocked him for being foolish. Just before the New Year, a classmate sold his property to a neighbor, and Xing Sheng acted as an intermediary to draft the contract. The neighbor rewarded him with a piece of white gold as a tip. Since the classmate was friendly with Xing Sheng, he also gave him another piece of white gold separately. Upon receiving the gold, Xing Sheng immediately made himself a splendid robe, which made him look magnificent. During the New Year, he visited the aunt to pay his respects. The aunt was surprised and asked, "How long has it been since we last met? Why are you suddenly so splendid? Did you not make a fortune from some righteous deed?" It was said in Chu that digging a treasure trove was considered making a fortune from a righteous deed. Xing Sheng smiled and replied casually. The aunt became even more suspicious and persisted in questioning him. Xing Sheng, having no choice, said, "Why bother to investigate further? You will find out later on your own." The aunt replied, "That's true, the more you keep it secret, the longer it will last." It was said in Chu that whenever someone dug a treasure trove, even close friends would keep it a secret. There was a saying, "The more you keep it secret for a year, the richer you become; the more you keep it secret for a generation, the wealthier you become for generations." Hence, the aunt spoke like this. That night, the aunt secretly told Shi Gu, "Xing Sheng has made a great fortune from a righteous deed, and he keeps it secret, not telling me. The more he keeps it secret, the more prosperous it will be. I also don't feel like investigating it further."

    491

    惟渠年少，难保不无浪费。我见汝日长，尚未字人，时切忧虑。汝如肯嫁渠，佐治生产，一举而两善皆备。汝意云何？”柿姑面頳不语。婶又诘之，良久答曰：“如大人审得良确，儿无不惟命是听。”婶喜曰：“我审之极确，复何多疑。”越日以告幸生，命即请冰速为订盟。幸生辞曰：“此儿一生大事，家本寒素，何敢孟浪！”婶笑曰：“汝休狡狯！吾意已决。此后一切礼节，仍旧从俭，吾不汝苛也。”生迫于婶命，寻遣媒草草委禽，亲迎而归。柿姑至生家，见室磬萧条，别无长物。偶叩生是否真发义财，生但笑而不言。柿姑叹曰：“贫富，命也，妾亦不怨。男儿不读书，究无长进。君果肯下帷，妾判劳十指，亦不至冻馁，君愿之否？”生笑曰：“读书是小生分内事，有何不愿？但累卿十指，于心良不忍。卿无忧，小生自有治生法也。

    Explanation

    491

    Since he is young, there is no guarantee that he will not be wasteful. I see that you are growing older, yet you have not yet been married, and I am constantly worried.
