In their village, there were Zhou and Wei, both famous scholars. Because the Du family had many books, they all stayed overnight at their home, studying together with the new bride, getting along well in spirit. This year, Zhou also passed the provincial examination, and their friendship became even better. The auspicious date was set for late September, when the weather was cool but not yet cold.

    566

    届期，周生窃语韦生曰：“有美与慧娟天生嘉耦，得谐琴瑟，煞费周折，当此良辰定情，不知若何欢洽。我两人当设法侦听，以快所闻。”韦曰：“唯唯，是不难，青庐上是藏书之楼，我与尔预伏楼上，大好侦听。”正窃议间，适有美在屏后闻之，点首匿笑，默筹预防之法。先是，有美之乳母朱媪，有子曰阿笨，游惰无赖，酷嗜赌博，负辄盗杜家物，典鬻以偿赌逋。有美亲迎，铺张务极奢华，慧娟周卢爱女，妆奁亦颇壮观。杜素恶阿笨行止不端，患其盗物，戒阍者不许入门。阿笨果早萌胠箧之念，以杜家不许入门，心益衔之。

    Explanation

    566

    At the appointed time, Zhou whispered to Wei, "Youmei and Huijuan are a naturally good match, destined to be a perfect couple. Despite the great trouble they've endured, it's a favorable time for them to make their commitment. I wonder how they will celebrate. Let's devise a plan to eavesdrop and satisfy our curiosity." Wei replied, "Yes, yes, that won't be difficult. The library is on top of the wedding pavilion. We can secretly hide there and listen in." As they were whispering, Youmei, who was hiding behind a screen, heard them, nodded with a hidden smile, and silently planned a way to prevent their scheme. Earlier, Youmei's wet nurse, Zhu, had a son named Aben, who was idle and good-for-nothing, obsessed with gambling, and often stole from the Du family to repay his debts. Youmei's wedding was lavishly extravagant, and Huijuan's dowry was also impressive. The Du family, who disliked Aben's behavior and feared he would steal, forbade him from entering their home. Aben, indeed, had early on conceived the idea of stealing, and because the Du family wouldn't let him in, he felt even more resentment.

    567

    是日，新娘下舆，观者如堵，阿笨在人丛中慁上书楼，计俟人静，蹈隙行事。漏二下，宾客甫散。有美脱去冠服，将次就寝，蓦忆日间周生言———欲觇二人所为，以博一笑，乃蹑足轻步上楼。于时，残月初升，阿笨正凭栏凝眺，有美窥之，以为必是周生，悄从背后出两手于面，反掩其目。阿笨固强有力，意有美特来己，既惊且恨，急回身，紧抱有美颈而扼其喉，须臾气绝，倒卧楼上。

    Explanation

    567

    On that day, the bride got off the carriage, and the spectators were as thick as a wall. Aben slipped into the bookshelf area in the crowd, planning to wait until it was quiet and take advantage of an opportunity to act. By the second hour of the night, the guests had just dispersed. The beautiful woman removed her headwear and clothing, preparing to go to bed, and suddenly remembered what Zhou had said during the day—she wanted to secretly observe what the two people were doing to get a laugh, so she tiptoed lightly up the stairs. At that moment, the waning moon rose, and Aben was leaning on the railing, gazing fixedly. The beautiful woman peeked at him, thinking he must be Zhou, and quietly put her hands in front of his face, covering his eyes. Aben, being strong and powerful, thought the beautiful woman came specifically to provoke him. He was both surprised and angry, quickly turned around, tightly hugged her neck, and choked her throat. In a short time, she lost consciousness and fell unconscious on the upper floor.

    568

    时慧娟坐帏中，方命伴媪出具汤沐更衣，见有美蹑足上楼，不知何意；俄闻楼上窸窣作响，心甚讶之。阿笨见有美已死，陡起恶念，欲犯慧娟，爰脱衣履塞藏书箱下，将有美短褐靴褫下自着之，大步下楼，知新娘在帏中，亟吹灭双烛入帏，遽抱慧娟求欢。慧娟念有美平日温存，何忽狂暴？况两人逑好不易，今幸觌面，无数款曲，合当絮诉，何将烛吹灭，得勿有所不慊耶？心殊不快。阿笨近身，遂极力撑拒。阿笨知难强合，急探手脱去慧娟两腕缠臂金，并摸索头上簪珥等物，慧娟益复骇异。适伴媪秉烛携沐汤进房，阿笨恐被人窥破形迹，急以袖掩面，夺门越墙而遁。

    Explanation

    568

    At the time, Huìjuān was sitting in the curtain, having just ordered the attendant to bring out the bathing water and change her clothes. She saw Yǒuměi tiptoeing up the stairs, not knowing what it meant. Soon after, she heard a faint rustling sound coming from upstairs, which greatly surprised her. Ābèn, seeing that Yǒuměi was already dead, suddenly had an evil thought and wanted to harm Huìjuān. He took off his clothes and shoes and hid them under the book box, then took off Yǒuměi's simple clothing and boots and put them on himself. He quickly descended the stairs, knowing that the new bride was inside the curtain, and hurriedly blew out the two candles and entered the curtain. He suddenly embraced Huìjuān and made advances towards her. Huìjuān remembered Yǒuměi's kindness in daily life and wondered why he suddenly became violent. Moreover, it was not easy for the two of them to get along well, and now they had the fortunate opportunity to meet face-to-face. There were innumerable affectionate details and exchanges that should have been discussed at length. Why did he blow out the candle? Was there something unsatisfactory? She felt very unhappy. When Ābèn approached her, she resisted with great effort. Ābèn knew it was difficult to force a union and quickly reached out to take off the bracelets on Huìjuān's wrists and felt around for hairpins and other ornaments on her head. Huìjuān was even more surprised and shocked. Just then, the attendant entered with a candle and bathing water. Ābèn feared being discovered and quickly covered his face with his sleeve, rushed out the door, leaped over the wall, and fled.

    569

    伴媪不知谁何，大惊，急燃双烛，搴帏见慧娟披发汗喘，神魂不定，叩问所以，慧娟备诉顷间情状。方共诧愕，忽闻楼上欷歔有声，命伴媪烛之，则见有美赤身卧楼上，吁息不已，盖扼喉一时气绝，须臾气复流行，故得再生，然暴苏身体绵软，猝难起立。伴媪另取衣履着之，缓缓扶掖下楼，偃卧绣榻，默不一语。慧娟情不能忍，急腼腆傍身，低问所苦，有美自指其喉，摇手令其勿语。慧娟莫喻其故，只得怏怏对镜，绾发添妆以待。延至五更，有美甫能起坐出声。

    Explanation

    569

    The attendant, not knowing who it was, was greatly startled and hastily lit two candles. She pulled aside the curtain and saw Hui Juan with disheveled hair, sweating and gasping, her spirit unsettled. She questioned Hui Juan about what had happened, and Hui Juan fully recounted the events that had just transpired. Only then did they both express astonishment, when suddenly they heard sobbing from upstairs. They ordered the attendant to light the way, and then saw a beautiful woman lying naked on the upper floor, gasping for breath. It turned out she had choked on her throat for a moment, causing her to lose breath; after a short while, her breath returned, and thus she was revived. However, upon her sudden awakening, her body was weak and she could not stand up immediately. The attendant took another set of clothes and dressed her, then slowly supported her down the stairs and had her lie down on an embroidered bed, where she remained silent, not uttering a word. Hui Juan, unable to bear the situation, hurriedly approached her shyly and asked softly what her suffering was. The beautiful woman pointed to her throat and shook her hand, signaling her not to speak. Hui Juan could not understand the reason and could only sullenly face the mirror, braiding her hair and adding makeup in anticipation. It was not until the fifth watch of the night that the beautiful woman was finally able to sit up and speak.

    570

    彼此各述所见，互相慨叹。有美以为素待周生不薄，何竟如此恶作剧？继念虽劫去钗珥等物，犹幸慧娟不为所欺，又复转怒为喜。然变出意外，气血究难骤舒，竟体疼痛，未免良宵虚度矣。是夜客散，周生以中酒酣卧斋中，韦生以周生既醉，遂独自归家。及周生酒醒，见韦生已归，趁月色甚明，亦踉跄归家，将出大门，会司阍者起溺，见周生暮夜短衣着靴，仓皇径去，形迹可疑。诘旦，举室喧传昨夜之事，证以司阍所见，佥谓必周生无疑。杜固长者，以慧娟幸不受辱，又不欲暴人之过，遍戒家人，秘勿播扬。

    Explanation

    570

    Each person recounted what they had seen, and they mutually sighed. The beautiful woman thought that she had treated Zhou Sheng not poorly, so why did he end up playing such a terrible prank? Subsequently thinking, although he took away her hairpins and other ornaments by force, fortunately Hui Juan was not deceived, and then turned anger into joy. However, the change was unexpected, and it was difficult to suddenly relax, causing her whole body to ache, and a good night was wasted. That night, the guests dispersed, and Zhou Sheng, drunk from alcohol, deeply slept in his study. Wei Sheng, seeing that Zhou Sheng was already drunk, then returned home alone. When Zhou Sheng woke up from drinking, taking advantage of the bright moonlight, he also staggered back home. About to leave the main gate, he encountered the gatekeeper going to urinate. Seeing Zhou Sheng, wearing short clothes and boots, hurriedly went away, with suspicious behavior. The next day, the entire household spread the news of the previous night's events. Based on what the gatekeeper saw, all agreed that it must have been Zhou Sheng without a doubt. Du Gu, a respected elder, because Hui Juan was fortunately not humiliated, and did not want to expose someone's faults, warned all family members, keeping it secret and not spreading it.

    571

    不图卢某闻之，勃然大怒，特造杜面数其讳盗之罪，且言周系名士，所行如此，诚衣冠禽兽。似此浇风不整，转相效尤，何以为训！乃具状诉诸邑宰。邑宰素与周相契，见状大骇。招周至署，以状示之。周阅之，骇汗满面，谓与韦生曾有此说，后各归家，实无此事。小生虽不肖，亦断不肯逢场作戏，戕人之生，以图苟合者，尚祈明公察之。邑宰亦信周决无此事，慰令暂归。爰使人风示卢某，为周辨诬，欲寝其事。乃卢固执莫解，谓确有左证，复何诬枉？如邑宰左袒周生，便当赴诉大府，以求水落石出。

    Explanation

    571

    I never expected that Mr. Lu would hear of this and become extremely angry. He especially went to Mr. Du and accused him of hiding a theft, and also said that Zhou, as a distinguished scholar, had acted in such a manner, truly making him a civilized beast. Such superficial customs, if not regulated, will lead others to imitate such evil deeds, how can it serve as a model? He then prepared a written statement and complained to the magistrate. The magistrate, who usually had a good relationship with Zhou, was greatly shocked upon seeing the statement. He summoned Zhou to his office and showed him the complaint. Zhou read it and was frightened, breaking out in a cold sweat. He explained that he had once had this conversation with Wei, but after they each returned home, there was indeed no such thing. Though I am unworthy, I would never act on impulse, harm someone's life, or seek a hasty union. I still request Your Excellency to investigate this matter. The magistrate also believed that Zhou was innocent and comforted him, ordering him to temporarily return home. The magistrate then sent someone to hint to Mr. Lu, clearing Zhou's name, and wanted to put the matter to rest. But Mr. Lu remained stubborn and could not be persuaded, insisting that he had solid evidence and what more could be a false accusation? If the magistrate favored Zhou, he would file a lawsuit with the high court to bring everything to light.

    572

    展转牵缠，两年有馀，未敢定谳。亡何，邑宰任满迁去。新令某公，素号健吏，阅及此牍，反复细意寻绎。越日，集两造会审，公一一研讯，拈髯寻思久之，忽有悟。曰：“无论是否周生所为，杜家钗饰等物，固明明有人劫去，且据若曹言，有美赤身卧楼上，短褐靴被其人着去，其人自着衣履必脱藏楼上。搜得衣履，便可昭晰。”乃自率吏役，亲往楼上穷搜，果于书箱下索得破衣鞋袜数事，并腰橐内有信一函，阅之，固某某与阿笨招赌书也。公笑曰：“得之矣。”付两造观之，始各恍然。亟命拘阿笨来，一讯尽吐其实，周冤以白。遐迩颂神明焉。

    Explanation

    572

    The case was entangled and prolonged, lasting more than two years without a final verdict. Soon after, the county magistrate completed his term and was transferred to another post. The new magistrate, surnamed Gong, was known as a capable official. Upon reading the case file, he carefully and repeatedly examined it with great attention to detail. The next day, he assembled both parties for a joint trial. Gong questioned them one by one, stroking his beard while pondering for a long time, and suddenly had an insight. He said, "Regardless of whether it was done by Zhou, it is clearly evident that someone stole Jia Du's hair ornaments and other items. According to what you said, a beautiful woman was found naked on the upper floor, and the man took her short robe and boots. If he put on clothes, he must have removed them and hidden them upstairs. If we find the clothes and shoes, it can then be made clear." He then led the officials and servants himself to search the upper floor thoroughly. Indeed, under a bookcase, they found several pieces of torn clothing and shoes, along with a letter in his belt pouch. Upon reading it, it turned out to be an invitation to gamble between "某某" and Aben. Gong smiled and said, "We have it now." He showed it to both parties, and they finally understood. He immediately ordered Aben to be arrested. After one interrogation, Aben confessed everything, and Zhou's innocence was cleared. People far and near praised his divine wisdom.

    573

    里乘子曰：谚云：“好事多磨折。”观此而益信其言不谬也。夫有美慧娟，天生嘉耦，其为婚配，宜也。不谓卢某固执拒婚，业许婚矣，又必俟成名方准亲迎。向使有美遇同梁灏，将奈之何？不遇如罗隐，又将奈之何？所幸一战而捷，公然准其亲迎矣。乃侦听之谋，胠箧之举，突而凑合，致使变生意外，虚度良宵。所谓好事多磨折者，竟如是乎？邑宰为周辨诬、第欲颟顸了事，愦愦可笑。一经健吏听断，是非立见。要之读律无异读书，苟能得间，自无不析之义；特患粗心人不肯反复细意寻绎耳。彼颂神明者，岂有异术哉？

    Explanation

    573

    里乘子曰：谚云：“好事多磨折。”观此而益信其言不谬也。夫有美慧娟，天生嘉耦，其为婚配，宜也。不谓卢某固执拒婚，业许婚矣，又必俟成名方准亲迎。向使有美遇同梁灏，将奈之何？不遇如罗隐，又将奈之何？所幸一战而捷，公然准其亲迎矣。乃侦听之谋，胠箧之举，突而凑合，致使变生意外，虚度良宵。所谓好事多磨折者，竟如是乎？邑宰为周辨诬、第欲颟顸了事，愦愦可笑。一经健吏听断，是非立见。要之读律无异读书，苟能得间，自无不析之义；特患粗心人不肯反复细意寻绎耳。彼颂神明者，岂有异术哉？

    Li Chengzi said: A proverb goes, "Good things often come with difficulties and setbacks." Observing this case, one can see that the saying is indeed true. There was a beautiful and wise maiden, born as a good match, and their marriage was fitting. However, it was not expected that Lu refused the marriage stubbornly, even though it had already been promised, and insisted on waiting until he became famous before allowing the wedding ceremony. If she had met someone like Liang Hao, what could have been done? If she had not met someone like Luo Yin, what could have been done? Fortunately, she achieved victory in the first attempt, and the wedding ceremony was publicly allowed. Yet, a plan involving eavesdropping and an act of breaking into a box suddenly came together, causing unexpected changes and a wasted night. Is it really so that good things often come with difficulties and setbacks? The local magistrate tried to handle the matter carelessly, which was confusing and laughable. Once a capable official heard and judged the case, right and wrong became immediately clear. In essence, studying law is no different from studying books; if one can find an opportunity, there is nothing that cannot be analyzed; the only problem is that careless people are unwilling to repeatedly and carefully examine it. Those who praise deities, do they have any special skills?

    574

    守贞
    　　
    　　中州某甲，少失怙恃。弱冠娶妻某氏，伉俪甚敦。甲有族叔在京贸易，因挟薄资往依之，颇有营获，十年始归。夫妻久别重逢，情倍亲切。晚间灭烛登床，甲纵体入怀，忽狂叫一声顿绝。某氏大惊，急起火而烛之，则见血流殷席，甲势已阉割不留馀蒂，竟不能再苏矣。某氏抱尸大哭，骇惧悲恸，莫测所以。诘旦，亲党邻舍毕集，互相猜疑，以某氏帷薄素谨，何忽罹此惨变？乃共商议，不得不具状诉官。官咨诸幕友，谓必因奸谋杀无疑，官然其说。穷诘某氏，备受榜虐，卒不肯承。展转年馀，不能定谳，而某氏奄奄一息，寻将瘐死狱中矣。勾越有商先生者，年逾七十，精申韩家言。老游中州，善断疑狱。官厚聘延至，与议此狱。

    Explanation

    574

    Faithful Translation:

    Chastity

    A man from the Central Plains, known only as "Jia," lost his parents at a young age. At twenty, he married a woman from the "Mou" family, and their marriage was deeply harmonious. Jia had a cousin in the capital who engaged in commerce, so he took a small amount of money and went to live with him. He achieved some success and returned after ten years. When the couple reunited after a long separation, their affection was even more tender. In the evening, after extinguishing the lamp and climbing into bed, Jia lay in his wife's arms when suddenly he screamed once and died. His wife was greatly startled and quickly lit a lamp to examine the situation. She saw blood flowing dark red across the mat, and Jia's body had been completely castrated, with no part remaining, and he could not be revived at all. The wife clasped his body and wept bitterly, filled with fear, sorrow, and confusion, unable to understand what had happened. The next day, all relatives, neighbors, and kin gathered, each suspecting the other. Since the wife had always been modest and virtuous, how could she suddenly suffer such a terrible tragedy? They discussed among themselves and decided there was no choice but to submit a formal report to the authorities. The official consulted his advisors, who all believed it was undoubtedly murder by an adulterer. The official accepted this view. They interrogated the wife thoroughly, and she endured the harsh beatings, but she finally refused to admit anything. After more than a year of investigation, they could not reach a verdict, and the wife, already barely alive, soon died of illness in prison. In Gouyue there was a Mr. Shang, over seventy years old, who was well-versed in the teachings of Shang Yang and Han Fei. An elderly traveler in the Central Plains, he was skilled in resolving doubtful cases. The official offered him a generous salary to bring him in, and together they deliberated on this case.

    575

    商阅爰书，再四思忖，固疑某氏冤枉，爰招某氏至，审其举止温存，语言和婉，毫无凶悍之态，益疑之。乃屏去左右，谕令某氏毋羞毋恐，可将当日床笫情形，从直缕述，我当设法为汝雪冤。某氏见商皤然老者，温霁和蔼，知非轻薄者。闻言，感激垂涕，稽颡有声，历将当日床笫情形，一一从直缕述。商拈髯谛听，忽然思得一法。谕令某氏归房，赤体偃卧，毋容腼腆；乃索猪肉少许，切作人势状，以铁钩贯其中，命接生某媪将肉塞入某氏阴道中，以觇其变。某媪如言试之，阴道中果有一物，力衔其肉，如鱼吞饵然。急拔出视之，其物长七寸许，竟体黄毛，四足修尾，酷类鼯鼪。始悟某甲变生意外，固是物作祟也。见者无不咋舌称异，佥曰：“非商先生高见，而某氏之冤终难暴白矣。”或谓此物名守贞，亦名血鳖，孀妇暮年多有之；他如老处子，比邱尼亦间有之，大率多因旷怨郁结而成。然究不知出何书，当俟质之博雅者。

    Explanation

    575

    Mr. Shang read the official records, pondering repeatedly, and firmly suspected that Ms. A was wronged. He summoned Ms. A and observed her gentle demeanor and mild speech, finding no signs of fierceness or brutality, which only increased his suspicion. He then dismissed those around him and instructed Ms. A not to be ashamed or afraid, telling her to narrate the details of what happened in the bedroom in a straightforward manner, promising to find a way to clear her name. Ms. A saw Mr. Shang, an elderly man with white hair, who was warm, gentle, and amiable, and knew he was not a superficial person. Upon hearing his words, she was moved to tears with gratitude, bowed deeply with a sound, and recounted the events of that day in detail. Mr. Shang listened intently, his beard in hand, and suddenly thought of a method. He instructed Ms. A to return to her room, lie naked on her back, and not be shy. He then took a small piece of pork, cut it into a human-like shape, threaded it onto an iron hook, and ordered the midwife, Old Woman B, to insert the meat into Ms. A's vagina to observe any changes. Old Woman B followed his instructions, and indeed, there was an object in the vagina that firmly grasped the meat, like a fish swallowing bait. She quickly pulled it out to see it, and the object was about seven inches long, covered in yellow fur, with four legs and a long tail, closely resembling a flying squirrel. Mr. Shang finally realized that the transformation of A was unexpected and must be the work of this creature. All who saw it were astonished and called it strange. They all agreed, "It was not because of Mr. Shang's high insight, but the injustice of Ms. A would remain unexposed." Some said this creature is called "Shouzhen," also known as "Xuebie." Widows in their later years often have it, and occasionally, old virgins and nuns also have it, mostly formed due to unresolved resentment and accumulated grievances. However, it is still unclear where this comes from, and it should be verified by learned scholars.

    576

    褪壳龟
    　　
    　　扬州某甲，家颇小康。所蓄鸡鸭犬豕等物，无故多亡去，举家窃窃称怪，亦姑听之。忽有游方丐者过其门，默相其室，卒然问曰：“君家蓄物，颇多亡去不？”甲曰：“然。汝何由知之？”丐冷笑曰：“君祸不远矣。不亟亟预防，人且难保，何况物乎？”甲骇问：“汝有术可解不？”丐曰：“此妖物作祟，不知伎俩何若，试可乃已。功成，当以钱十千缗犒我作沽酒资；不则勿怨也。”甲曰：“诺。”丐命导其遍相宅中，至厨，见水瓮一具，瞪视良久，曰：“殆在是矣。”乃命市猪肉一方，煮半熟，以铁钩贯其中，绳系其端，缳柱上。将熟肉置水瓮畔，人遥从壁隙窥之。果见瓮下一物，闻肉香，探首出，张血口遽衔其肉，钩挂喉际，痛甚，急缩首欲遁，缘绳缳柱，猝不能脱。丐急出，将物缚住，令甲视之，状如守宫，长尺有半。丐曰：“此褪壳龟也。厥名曰蜥。

    Explanation

    576

    A man from Yangzhou, whose family was moderately well-off, found that many of his livestock—chickens, ducks, dogs, and pigs—were mysteriously disappearing. The whole household was quietly discussing the strange occurrences, but they merely listened and did nothing. Suddenly, a wandering beggar passed by his door, silently observing his house, and abruptly asked, "Do you have many of your livestock going missing?"

    The man was startled and asked, "Do you have a way to solve this?" The beggar replied, "This is caused by a malevolent spirit. I don't know what its tricks are, but we can try to catch it. If I succeed, you should reward me with ten thousand strings of cash for buying wine; otherwise, don't blame me."

    The beggar ordered someone to lead him around the house, and they came to the kitchen, where he saw a water jar. He stared at it for a long time and said, "It must be there." He then ordered someone to buy a piece of pork, cook it half-done, and pierce it with an iron hook, tying the hook to a rope and hanging it from a beam. He placed the half-cooked pork near the water jar and hid behind the wall to watch. Indeed, he saw something beneath the jar, which smelled the meat and stuck its head out, opening its bloody mouth to seize the meat. The hook got caught in its throat, causing great pain. It quickly retracted its head to escape but was entangled in the rope and the beam, unable to free itself. The beggar rushed out, caught the creature, and showed it to the man. The creature looked like a gecko, one and a half feet long. The beggar said, "This is a turtle that sheds its shell. Its name is 蜥."

    577

    ”因贺甲曰：“君幸遇我，此物化尚未久，易制；再过年馀，便能变化食人，即非人力所能制。君家将无噍类矣。”甲大惊。因忆家固蓄一大龟，亡去年馀，意即是物所化，试以叩丐，丐曰：“是也。”乃共觅其壳，果于猫窦得之。盖窦狭壳宽，龟偶入窦，如羝触藩，猛力向前，遂褪壳化为此物。丐谓：“此壳为化骨妙药，或去龋齿，或去痈疽腐骨，皆立效，可善藏之。”爰索利刃，将此物剁为肉泥；凡地上血迹，铲刮净尽，盛以瓦缶，坎埋深山之中。

    Explanation

    577

    因贺甲曰：“君幸遇我，此物化尚未久，易制；再过年馀，便能变化食人，即非人力所能制。君家将无噍类矣。”甲大惊。因忆家固蓄一大龟，亡去年馀，意即是物所化，试以叩丐，丐曰：“是也。”乃共觅其壳，果于猫窦得之。盖窦狭壳宽，龟偶入窦，如羝触藩，猛力向前，遂褪壳化为此物。丐谓：“此壳为化骨妙药，或去龋齿，或去痈疽腐骨，皆立效，可善藏之。”爰索利刃，将此物剁为肉泥；凡地上血迹，铲刮净尽，盛以瓦缶，坎埋深山之中。

    贺甲对他说：“你有幸遇到我，这种东西变化的时间还不长，容易控制；再过一年多，它就能变化成吃人的怪物，那时就不是人力所能控制的了。你家里将没有活物了。”贺甲大惊失色。他想起家里原来养的一只大龟，去年多已经死了，猜想可能是这只龟变化而成的，于是试着去问丐，丐说：“是的。”于是他们一起寻找那只龟的壳，果然在猫洞里找到了。原来洞口狭窄而龟壳宽大，龟偶然进入洞中，像一只羊撞上了篱笆，用尽全力向前冲，结果脱壳变成了这个怪物。丐说：“这只龟壳是一种骨化神药，可以去除龋齿，也可以去除痈疽和腐骨，效果立竿见影，可以好好保存。”于是他们找来一把利刃，将这个怪物剁成肉泥；凡地上有血迹的地方，都铲刮干净，装进瓦罐里，埋在深山之中。

    578

    笑谓甲曰：“小人为君料理尽善，可以高枕无忧矣。”甲喜，款以酒饭，酬钱十千而去。越岁，甲家客作，暑天畏热，取门支床露宿。诘旦，竟体化为水，惟发独存。甲大惊，急诉于有司。验其形迹，茫无端绪，甲坐是系狱，而家业荡然矣。忽前丐又来，见甲家状甚萧条，怪问甲妻，妻告之故。丐自批其颊曰：“此小人之过也。”盖当日杀此物时，曾有点血溅门上，以不足为患，故未拭去，不谓竟波及客作。亟赴有司，为甲讼冤。试取鸡犬置门血迹处，俱化为水。官赏丐者，而出甲于狱。

    Explanation

    578

    The beggar laughed and told Jia, "I have taken care of everything for you to perfection; you can sleep soundly without worry." Jia was pleased and treated the beggar with wine and food, paying him ten thousand coins and then leaving. The following year, a hired worker from Jia's household, fearing the heat during the summer, took a door and propped it up as a bed to sleep outdoors. The next morning, the entire body of the worker turned into water, leaving only the hair intact. Jia was greatly shocked and urgently reported the incident to the authorities. After investigating the traces, they found no clues, and as a result, Jia was imprisoned, and his family property was completely ruined. Suddenly, the previous beggar came again, seeing Jia's household in a very desolate state, he strangely asked Jia's wife about the situation, and she told him the reason. The beggar slapped his own cheeks and said, "This is the fault of the lowly one." It turned out that when he killed the creature, there was once blood splattered on the door, which he considered not a serious problem, so he did not wipe it away. He did not expect it would ultimately affect the hired worker. The beggar hurriedly went to the authorities to clear Jia's name. They tried placing chickens and dogs at the spot where the blood was splattered, and all turned into water. The official rewarded the beggar and released Jia from prison.

    579

    里乘子曰：此扬州土人为予言之，凿凿可据。并谓其壳今尚存药肆中，惜未一见之也。褪壳龟厥名曰蜥，究不知所出何书。彼丐者不惟有术，且亦博雅，有司赏之，宜也。吾愧丐者矣！
    　　
    　　某令
    　　
    　　甘肃某令，精明干练，能伺长官喜怒。尝有剧盗多名，历久缉不得，檄某令往捕。乃与长官幕友谋，出资另购七人，申报塞责。长官听幕友言，遂将七人付诸大辟。某令一日从外归，忽心如刀割，痛不可忍。俄顷，大叫一声而卒。某令素嗜饮茶，其鬼忽附妾身曰：“快烹好茶我饮。我近为七人所诉，受刑甚苦，尔曹速焚纸钱，以济我用，多多益善。”又嘱妻妾等应如何奉养老母，如何教育诸子，所遗官资应如何经营生活。

    Explanation

    579

    里乘子曰：此扬州土人为予言之，凿凿可据。并谓其壳今尚存药肆中，惜未一见之也。褪壳龟厥名曰蜥，究不知所出何书。彼丐者不惟有术，且亦博雅，有司赏之，宜也。吾愧丐者矣！

    某令

    甘肃某令，精明干练，能伺长官喜怒。尝有剧盗多名，历久缉不得，檄某令往捕。乃与长官幕友谋，出资另购七人，申报塞责。长官听幕友言，遂将七人付诸大辟。某令一日从外归，忽心如刀割，痛不可忍。俄顷，大叫一声而卒。某令素嗜饮茶，其鬼忽附妾身曰：“快烹好茶我饮。我近为七人所诉，受刑甚苦，尔曹速焚纸钱，以济我用，多多益善。”又嘱妻妾等应如何奉养老母，如何教育诸子，所遗官资应如何经营生活。

    580

    一一处分毕，便以手拊胸叹曰：“我今日悔之晚矣。”众审其声音、言笑、举止，固宛然某令也。少选鬼去，妾苏，问之茫然。后有扶鸾者降坛，系已故谢明府。谢固忠厚长者，沙书：“阴曹以我生前为人正直，命作此方土地。近为某令七人一案，日在城隍庙讯办，颇甚劳形。”人问：“公素与某令同寅，能赐袒护否？”曰：“阴律不比阳世，丝毫不敢徇情。不但某令，恐将来某幕友亦要到案对质也。”此吾邑张德昭在甘肃所目见，为予言之，并云：“现某幕友已渐颓敝矣。”
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：观某令之鬼以手拊胸叹曰：“我今日悔之晚矣。”嗟乎！千古权臣猾吏及一切小人，到撒手时，大都同不免有此一叹，惜乎当势焰炙手时，皆梦梦而不自觉耳！孽海茫茫，不早回头，虽悔何益？

    Explanation

    580

    After completing all the necessary arrangements, he placed his hand on his chest and sighed, saying, “I regret it too late today.” The people observed his voice, laughter, and mannerisms, and indeed, they were unmistakably those of the magistrate. After a short while, the ghost departed, and the woman regained consciousness, but when asked about what had happened, she was bewildered and had no memory of the event. Later, a medium descended on the altar, claiming to be the spirit of the late Magistrate Xie. Xie, a man of integrity and kindness, wrote in the sand: “The underworld appointed me as the local deity of this region because I was upright in life. Recently, I have been handling the case of seven people under the jurisdiction of a certain magistrate at the city god temple, which is quite exhausting.” Someone asked, “Since you and the magistrate were colleagues in life, could you give him some leniency?” The spirit replied, “The laws of the underworld are different from those of the human world. I dare not show favoritism in the slightest. Not only the magistrate, but I fear that his former colleague, a certain advisor, may also be brought to trial for questioning.” This account was witnessed by Zhang Dezhuang of our county while he was in Gansu, who told me about it. He also mentioned, “Now, the advisor is gradually declining.”

    The commentator Li Chengzi said: “Seeing the ghost of the magistrate place his hand on his chest and sigh, ‘I regret it too late today,’ I lament! Throughout history, powerful ministers, corrupt officials, and all manner of petty people, when they face the end of their lives, often cannot escape this same sigh of regret. What a pity that when they were at the height of their power and influence, they were all dazed and unaware!” The sea of sin is vast and boundless. If one does not turn back early, even regret will be of no avail.

    581

    孙明府
    　　
    　　山右孙翘江明府，一字兰皋，以进士为直隶肃宁县令。元旦，各庙拈香回，道遇女鬼，从至署内，声言索命。且谓明府前世亦姓孙，宰肃宁，女为强暴所污，堂判和奸，致女羞忿投环死。系某年月事，尚有旧牍可稽也。明府命吏稽旧牍，果有此案，官果姓孙，大骇，乃牒城隍，诘问：“既系前世冤，何以迟至今世甫报？”越日，明府梦城隍传谕云：“汝前世甚孝，且居官甚好，再世故复以甲科来宰是邑，鬼何敢报？今汝政声顿杀，鬼得乘间而入，汝何尤焉？”明府知冤难解，不得已，向鬼乞怜。鬼不肯，再三哀之，鬼曰：“虽贷汝命，亦不容汝在此作宰！”明府无奈，乃上其事于大府。时沈阳琦侯为直隶总督，檄保定守熊公往询，鬼附明府身，历历诉冤。守复命，琦侯异之，为抗疏暴白其事，改孙校官回籍。此道光十六年事。葛菊人、史仲皋皆能言之。

    Explanation

    581

    The Magistrate Sun

    The magistrate Sun Qiaojiang from Shanxi, known by the pseudonym Lan Gao, was appointed as the magistrate of Sucheng County in Zhili Province through the imperial examination. On New Year's Day, after offering incense at various temples and returning, he encountered a female ghost on the way, who followed him to his office and claimed to demand retribution for his death. She alleged that in a past life, he also bore the surname Sun and served as the magistrate of Sucheng County, where a woman was raped by a violent man, and the magistrate ruled the act as consensual, leading the woman to commit suicide by hanging due to humiliation and anger. She stated that this event occurred in a certain year and month and that there were still old documents to verify it. The magistrate ordered his officials to check the old documents, and indeed, there was such a case, and the official did bear the surname Sun. Greatly shocked, he then sent a petition to the City God, interrogating, "Since it is an injustice from a past life, why was it delayed until this life to be avenged?" The next day, the magistrate dreamed that the City God conveyed a message: "You were very filial in a past life, and you governed well. So in this life, you were appointed as a magistrate through the imperial examination. Why would the ghost dare to seek revenge? Now your reputation has suddenly declined, and the ghost took advantage of the opportunity. What do you have to complain about?" The magistrate realized that the injustice could not be resolved and, unable to do otherwise, pleaded with the ghost for mercy. The ghost refused, and he pleaded with her repeatedly. The ghost said, "Even if I spare your life, I will not allow you to continue as magistrate!" Having no choice, the magistrate reported the matter to the higher authorities. At that time, General Qi of Shenyang was the governor-general of Zhili Province, and he sent a summons to Governor Xiong of Baoding to investigate. The ghost possessed the magistrate's body and clearly recounted the injustice. Governor Xiong reported back, and General Qi found it extraordinary, submitting a memorial to the emperor to clarify the matter. Sun Qiaojiang was demoted to a school official and sent back to his home province. This occurred in the sixteenth year of the Daoguang era. Both Ge Juren and Shi Zhonggao could recount this event.

    582

    里乘子曰：后阅福建梁敬叔笔记亦载此，则旧案在乾隆五十一年。孙前世姓黄。保定守熊公名守谦，字虚谷。或曰河间守。
    　　
    　　倪公春岩
    　　
    　　倪公春岩廷谟，由进士出宰吾皖潜山县。廉明公正，四民爱戴，皆呼之曰“倪青天”。公尝于冬月有事至乡，忽有蝇成群飞缭舆前，左右挥之不去。意时方苦寒，那得有此？得勿冤鬼作祟耶？因默祝如有冤屈，蝇当导我为之伸冤。祝毕，蝇果群飞前导，不里许，路过一山，旋风骤起，将群蝇卷入山中。公急命停舆，自徒行入山迹之，步上山凹，见一坟新筑，湿土未燥，群蝇栖集其上，心益惊异。比呼亭长问，知为前村某甲新冢。问：“甲年几何？向作何生业？何疾而死？家中尚有何人？”亭长对谓：“甲年二十许，系病瘵而死。”又问：“甲妻年几何矣？”对曰：“小人不知，大约与甲齿相若耳。”公点首寻思，乃命舆径至甲家，直升其堂，召其妻出见问话。

    Explanation

    582

    Li Chengzi said: Later, upon reviewing the notes of Liang Jingshu of Fujian, which also recorded this account, it was determined that the old case dates back to the 51st year of the Qianlong reign. The grandson’s ancestors were previously surnamed Huang. Xiong Shouqian, governor of Baoding, was known by his courtesy name Xugu. Some say he also served as the governor of Hejian.

    Ni Chunyan

    Ni Chunyan, whose given name was Tingmo, was appointed as magistrate of Qianshan County in my province of Anhui after passing the imperial examination as a jinshi. Upright, clear, just, and fair, he was beloved by the four classes of people, who all called him “Ni Qingtian.” Once, during the winter, he had business in the countryside when suddenly a swarm of flies began to circle his carriage. His attendants tried to drive them away but could not. It seemed that the weather was bitterly cold—how could there be so many flies? Could it not be that a wronged spirit was causing this? He silently wished that if there were any grievances, the flies would guide him to right them. After his silent prayer, the flies indeed flew in front, leading the way. After about a li, they passed by a mountain, when suddenly a whirlwind arose, sweeping the swarm of flies into the mountain. The official immediately ordered the carriage to stop and walked into the mountain to follow the trail. He climbed up into a mountain hollow, where he saw a newly built tomb, its wet soil still not dry, and a swarm of flies had gathered upon it. He was even more surprised. He then called the village head to inquire, and learned that it was the new tomb of a certain person named Jia from the village ahead. He asked: “How old was Jia? What was his occupation? What illness did he die from? Are there still any family members at home?” The village head replied: “Jia was about twenty years old, and died from a wasting illness.” He asked again: “How old is Jia’s wife?” The village head replied: “I don’t know, but she is approximately the same age as Jia.” The official nodded in thought and then ordered the carriage to go directly to Jia’s home, entered the hall, and summoned his wife to speak with him.

    583

    甲妻某氏，闻公至，大惊，急毁妆斩齐出拜，伏地干泣。公见某氏态度妖荡，知非善类，乃厉声谓曰：“我至汝家非为别事，缘昨夜梦见汝夫，赤身喋血，跽我床前，诉称被汝所害，横遭惨死，哭求伸冤。汝知之乎？”某氏闻公言，急起趋入屏风后，与公抗辩，硬语牴牾。公见其暴悍，益信其必非良善。即召其族长并左右邻至，一一研诘，所对大略与亭长相同，再四钩稽，茫无端绪。公心终不释，乃执意开墓启棺检验，以决其疑。佥谓关系甚重，倘检验无据，恐于公多有不利。公笑曰：“我以穷措大，侥幸弋获科第，忝膺民社，如遇冤狱，不为伸理，其何以为民父母乎？我意已决，如检验无据，甘心坐罪，虽粉身勿悔也。”诘旦，乃率领族长、左右邻等，登山开墓启棺。

    Explanation

    583

    When the official arrived at the home of Jia, Jia's wife, surnamed a certain name, was greatly surprised upon hearing of his arrival. She hastily removed her makeup and cut her hair, then rushed out to greet him, falling to the ground and weeping bitterly. The official noticed that she had an alluring and unrestrained demeanor, which led him to suspect she was not of good character. He spoke sternly to her, saying, "I came to your house for no other reason than that last night I dreamt of your husband, who appeared before me in a bloody state, kneeling on my bed. He claimed that you had killed him, causing him to die a terrible death, and he cried out for justice. Do you know about this?" Upon hearing the official's words, the woman quickly rose and hurried behind a screen, where she confronted him with a defiant attitude, speaking harshly and contradicting him. Seeing her violent and fierce nature, the official became even more convinced that she was not a virtuous person. He immediately summoned the clan leader and the neighbors on both sides, questioning them one by one. Their responses were largely the same as those given by the village constable, and after repeated inquiries, there was still no clear lead. Despite this, the official remained unsatisfied and insisted on opening the tomb and examining the coffin to resolve his doubts. Everyone agreed that the matter was of great importance, and if there was no evidence from the examination, it might be detrimental to the official. The official smiled and said, "I, a poor scholar, have only managed to pass the imperial examinations by chance, and I now hold the responsibility of governing the people. If I encounter an unjust case and do not seek justice, how can I be a good leader for the people? My decision is made. If there is no evidence from the examination, I am willing to accept the punishment, even if it costs me my life, I will not regret it." The next morning, he led the clan leader and the neighbors on both sides up the mountain to open the tomb and examine the coffin.

    584

    甲尸以天寒丝毫未曾腐坏，伍伯承公意旨，由首而足，由腹而背，细意检验，竟无微伤，惟骨瘦如柴，确系病瘵而死，公无奈何，只得仍命盖棺封墓。某氏大声诃曰：“公以莫须有之言，开人之墓，启人之棺，翻覆人之尸骨，死者何辜，遭此屠毒！既启棺而又欲盖之，既开墓而又欲封之，此非未亡人所敢从命也！”公笑谢曰：“汝言良是，吾已具文请命于大府，甘任其罪矣。死者无辜，而令其暴露，是益重吾之罪也。吾何忍焉！”卒命盖棺封墓而去。公已具文上请，复自踵谒大府，面陈梗概。大府素重公之为人，此举殊怪公孟浪，例应登诸白简，难以袒救。公请展限三月，当密加访察，如真不得确耗，甘罪无悔。大府许之。公旋任，路经城隍庙，式舆默祝，祈神示梦。夜果梦城隍神遣人赠万年青草一盆，惊寤，不解所谓。乃易服，貌为卜人，至乡访察。

    Explanation

    584

    The corpse, due to the extreme cold, had not decayed at all. The subordinate, following the official's instructions, carefully examined it from head to toe, from the abdomen to the back, and found no signs of injury, only that the bones were as thin as a withered reed, confirming that the death was due to a wasting disease. The official, unable to find any other explanation, had no choice but to order the coffin to be closed and the tomb sealed. The widow, speaking loudly, rebuked, "The official, using unfounded accusations, has opened the tomb, opened the coffin, and turned over the bones of the deceased. What sin has the dead committed to suffer such torment? Having opened the coffin and now wanting to close it, having opened the tomb and now wanting to seal it, this is something I, a widow, dare not obey!" The official, smiling, apologized, "Your words are true. I have already submitted a written request to the high-ranking official, and I am willing to bear the blame. To leave the dead exposed, when they are innocent, only adds to my guilt. How could I do that?" Finally, he ordered the coffin to be closed and the tomb sealed, then left. The official had already submitted a written request and personally visited the high-ranking official to explain the situation. The high-ranking official, who always respected the official's character, found this action particularly reckless and should be recorded in the official documents, making it difficult to pardon. The official requested an extension of three months to secretly investigate, and if no definite information could be obtained, he was willing to accept the punishment without regret. The high-ranking official agreed. The official then resumed his duties, passing by the City God Temple, where he performed a formal act of silent prayer, asking the deity to show him a dream. That night, he indeed dreamed that the City God sent someone to give him a pot of everlasting green grass, waking him up in surprise, not understanding its meaning. He then changed his clothes and pretended to be a fortune-teller, going to the village to investigate.

    585

    日晡，见一渔人垂钓河滨，就而问路。渔人戏谓：“先生善卜，能卜小人今日钓得鱼若干，当作东道主，不则此处苦无逆旅，恐栖止无所也。”公信口戏答曰：“卜汝连得三鱼，计重五斤有馀。一尾烹以款客，两尾兑钱行沽。”果连得一鲤一鲂一鲫，鲜美可爱。渔人大喜，笑曰：“先生其神仙耶？何言之验也。寒舍不远，敢请税驾。”公笑从之。不半里，至其家，茅屋数椽，一老妪当门而坐，笑问渔人：“归何早也？”渔人笑曰：“儿幸遇神仙，卜应连得三鱼，果然。”因将鲤付妪，请烹以待客。又笑谓公曰：“此老母也，幸尚强健。年八十有二矣，齿目俱幸无损，惟重听耳。”因请公少坐，自携两鱼，就邻翁兑秫酒一大瓶而归。老妪烹鱼已熟，渔人请公南向坐，老妪西向坐，自居主位北向坐。老妪不能饮酒，饭罢独自归卧。公与渔人酒量俱豪，荐鱼劝饮，意甚相得。

    Explanation

    585

    In the late afternoon, I encountered a fisherman casting his line by the riverside and approached him to ask for directions. The fisherman playfully said, "Sir, you're good at divination. Can you tell me how many fish I will catch today? If I catch enough, I'll entertain you as the host. Otherwise, there's no inn here, and I fear we'll have nowhere to rest." I replied playfully, "I predict you'll catch three fish, weighing more than five pounds. One will be cooked to entertain guests, and the other two will be exchanged for money to buy more." Indeed, he caught a carp, a bream, and a crucian carp, all fresh and delicious. The fisherman was delighted and laughed, "Are you a god, sir? Your predictions are so accurate. My humble home isn't far; would you mind staying with us?" I laughed and agreed. After half a mile, we arrived at his house, a few thatched cottages. An old woman was sitting at the door and smiled, asking the fisherman, "Why did you return so early?" The fisherman laughed and said, "I was fortunate to meet a god, and your prediction came true—I caught three fish!" He gave the carp to the old woman to cook for the guest. He then smiled at me and said, "This is my mother. She's still quite healthy. She's eighty-two years old, and her teeth and eyes are still good, except she has a bit of hearing loss." He asked me to sit for a while and then went to exchange the two fish for a bottle of millet wine with a neighbor. The old woman had finished cooking the fish by the time he returned. The fisherman invited me to sit facing south, the old woman sat facing west, and he himself sat in the host's position facing north. The old woman couldn't drink, so after the meal, she went back to sleep alone. Both the fisherman and I had a hearty appetite for alcohol, and we enjoyed sharing the fish and toasting each other, feeling very much in agreement.

    586

    公问知渔人姓万，并问何以当此壮年，尚无妻室？渔人笑曰：“先生谓小人尚壮年耶？小人今年已六十有四矣。里党见小人生平不形老态，因共以‘万年轻’呼之。自知命薄，不乐有家室，徒以有老母在，不然早已披发入山矣。”公闻万年轻三字，顿忆前梦，因笑以言餂之曰：“君何言之激也。我相汝大运将至，如娶妻，当连得二子，老福甚隆，慎毋自弃。”时渔人酒已微醺，益复忘形。闻公言，急摇手曰：“天下最毒者莫如女子。娶妻一事，请勿再言。”公笑问何谓，渔人但摇首不语。公曰：“暮夜无人，汝有何意见？告我何害？”渔人叹曰：“先生长者，当不泄语。小人少酷嗜赌，负辄偶作穿窬，藉偿赌债。以俱罹法网，辍而改业。昨醉后，有友邀赌，大负，不得已，聊作冯妇。

    Explanation

    586

    The official asked about the fisherman's surname and why, at his age, he was still unmarried. The fisherman smiled and said, "Do you think I am still in my prime, sir? I am already sixty-four years old. The villagers see that I do not show signs of old age, so they call me 'Young Master Wan.' I know my fate is not good, and I do not wish to have a family. I only stay because my old mother is still alive; otherwise, I would have long since shaved my head and entered the mountains." Upon hearing the name "Young Master Wan," the official suddenly remembered a previous dream and tried to flatter the fisherman, saying, "Why do you speak so strongly? I foresee that your great fortune is about to arrive. If you were to marry, you would have two sons, and your old age would be very prosperous. Be careful not to abandon yourself." At that time, the fisherman was slightly tipsy and even more relaxed. Hearing the official's words, he quickly shook his hand and said, "The most dangerous thing in the world is women. Do not mention marriage again." The official laughed and asked what he meant, but the fisherman only shook his head and remained silent. The official said, "It is late at night and there is no one around. What do you think? What harm could it do to tell me?" The fisherman sighed and said, "Sir, as a respected elder, you should not reveal my words. When I was young, I was obsessed with gambling. Whenever I lost, I would sometimes steal to pay off my debts. Because of this, I was caught by the law and had to stop gambling and change my profession. Yesterday, after getting drunk, a friend invited me to gamble, and I lost heavily. I have no choice but to consider taking on a risky endeavor again."

    587

    稔知前村某甲家颇小康，久病卧床，尚易为计。时夜漏三下，甲宅仆媪俱已睡熟，万籁寂然，予由屋一跃至地，见甲房灯尚未息，姑伏窗窥之，乃不窥则已，窥之真令人骇然也。”公问：“如何？”渔人又摇首不语。公曰：“言之矣，复何嗫嚅为也！”渔人复坚属曰：“先生必无泄语，乃敢毕其词。”公正色指天信誓：“必不泄语。”渔人乃谓：“小人伏窗潜窥，但听病者卧床呻吟，其妻侧坐床前，默默若有所思，忽起身挑灯燃烛，向床后招手，一男子轻步而出，两人附耳小语。其妻出绢一匹，登床将甲口缠闭。两人又将甲缚伏行床，褫裈露尻。启盎出一小蛇，将蛇首纳入竹管，以竹管对尻，取香火炙蛇尾，蛇负痛由谷道窜入腹中，闻甲大喘一声，其气遂绝。两人相视而笑，复解甲缚，扛置床上。小人惨不忍睹，复一跃上屋，恨恨而返。至今思之，怒发犹为上指也。

    Explanation

    587

    I am well-acquainted with a moderately wealthy household in a village, where the head of the family, Mr. Jia, has been long-sick and bedridden, making it easy to plan something. It was around midnight, and the servants and maids in Mr. Jia's house were all asleep. All sounds were silent, and I jumped down from the roof. I saw that the light was still on in Mr. Jia's room, so I cautiously peered through the window. I had not peered before, but when I did, it truly shocked me. The master of the house was lying on the bed, groaning in pain. His wife sat beside the bed, silently as if deep in thought. Suddenly, she got up, lit a candle, and beckoned with her hand. A man stepped out quietly from behind the bed, and the two of them whispered into each other's ears. The wife took out a piece of silk, wrapped it around Mr. Jia's mouth, and tied him up. They then carried him onto the bed, pulled down his pants, and exposed his buttocks. They opened a jar and took out a small snake, inserted its head into a bamboo tube, and placed the tube against his buttocks. They lit incense and touched the snake's tail, causing the snake to suffer from pain and crawl into his rectum. I heard Mr. Jia take a loud breath, and then he died. The two of them looked at each other and laughed, then untied Mr. Jia, carried him back onto the bed, and left. The scene was so shocking that I could not bear to look upon it. I jumped back up to the roof and returned with deep resentment. To this day, I still remember the event, and my anger is so great that my hair stands on end.

    588

    先生试思，娶妻如此，有何恩爱？岂非天下最毒者莫如女子耶！”公曰：“甲如此惨死，其亲疏岂无一人肯为伸冤耶？”渔人叹曰：“甲虽惨死，身无微伤，何由伸冤？昨闻甲鬼托梦求倪青天伸冤，倪公开墓启棺，检验无伤，将来不惟去官，并有馀罪，此真抱屈也！”公笑曰：“汝何不投官自首？当得重赏。”渔人摇手曰：“否否，倪青天最恶穿窬，如言不见信，不惟无赏，恐反受罚，不如安分缄口为妙。”公又笑曰：“我相汝晚福甚隆，即此未尝不是机缘，汝其图之。”渔人摇首不语。诘旦，公与渔人作别。返署，急遣人拘万年轻至。公招至记室，渔人战兢伏地，不敢仰视。公拈髯笑曰：“汝第举首，尚识卜人否？”渔人仰视公，急叩首曰：“小人死罪，求公见恕。”公笑慰之曰：“我不汝罪，汝其毋恐。某甲之冤，汝肯为具控，不吝厚赏也。”渔人叩首答曰：“谨从尊命，谨从尊命。

    Explanation

    588

    The magistrate invited the fisherman to consider: "If a wife is like this, what love or affection is there? Is it not that the most poisonous thing in the world is a woman?" The magistrate asked, "For 甲, who died so cruelly, is there no one among his relatives who would seek justice for him?" The fisherman sighed and said, "Even though 甲 died so cruelly, his body had no minor injuries, how could there be any basis for seeking justice? I heard that the ghost of 甲 dreamed and asked for justice from Judge Ni. Judge Ni opened the tomb and examined the body, finding no injuries. In the future, he will not only be dismissed from office but also face additional punishment. This is truly a case of injustice!" The magistrate laughed and said, "Why don't you confess to the authorities? You will receive a generous reward." The fisherman shook his head and said, "No, no, Judge Ni hates thieves the most. If what I say is not believed, I will not only receive no reward but might even be punished. It is better to keep quiet and be content with my lot." The magistrate laughed again and said, "I see that you will have great fortune in your later years. This situation is not without its opportunities. Consider it well." The fisherman shook his head and remained silent. The next morning, the magistrate bid farewell to the fisherman. Returning to his office, he quickly sent someone to arrest Wan Qing. The magistrate summoned Wan Qing to the records room. The fisherman trembled and knelt on the ground,不敢仰视. The magistrate smiled and said, "Just lift your head. Do you still remember the fortune-teller?" The fisherman looked up at the magistrate and quickly knelt down, saying, "I am guilty of a crime, please forgive me." The magistrate smiled and comforted him, saying, "I will not punish you, do not be afraid. For the sake of 甲's injustice, if you are willing to file a complaint, I will not hesitate to reward you generously." The fisherman knelt down and replied, "I will obey your command, I will obey your command."

    589

    ”乃具词控诉。公立飞签拘某氏，并族长、左右邻至。令渔人与某氏对质，某氏犹强辨不已。公谓非再检验不可，比具文上达，并叙入万年轻之词。爰重率众登山，开墓启棺。时交仲春，甲尸已溃烂，脏腑毕见，肠中死蛇犹存。公令某氏观之，某氏犹不肯承。公怒甚，叱鞭其背，某氏娇不胜刑，乃吐其实。初，某甲得疾，某氏有中表兄时来省视。甲疾日剧，某氏料不能起，遂与表兄有私，计甲死当据其产，永为夫妇。甲虽绵惙床蓐，一息猝难遽绝。适见丐者蓄有小蛇，二人有触于心，以百钱购得，而致甲命。死果无伤可验，智亦巧矣。自是，甲冤既白，某氏凌迟处死，其中表兄亦立斩以殉。公又命族长择族中子侄，立为甲嗣，以延血食。遐迩闻之，无不称快。公感城隍示梦，刑牲致祭以酬之。又以此事非万年轻莫白，且嘉其孝，乃召母子至署，为万娶妻生子；给钱小作贸易，俾温饱以终身焉。

    Explanation

    589

    The fisherman then prepared a formal complaint. The official quickly issued a warrant to arrest the woman, along with the clan leader and the neighbors. He ordered the fisherman to confront the woman, but she still stubbornly argued without ceasing. The official declared that further investigation was necessary and prepared a written report to submit to higher authorities, including the testimony of the young man. The official then led a group to climb the mountain again and opened the tomb and coffin. By mid-spring, the body of A had rotted, and all internal organs were visible, with a dead snake still present in the intestines. The official ordered the woman to see it, but she still refused to confess. Enraged, the official lashed her back, and the woman, unable to withstand the punishment, finally confessed the truth. Initially, A fell ill, and the woman had a cousin who came to visit occasionally. As A's condition worsened, the woman anticipated that he would not recover and plotted with her cousin to take possession of his property after his death, intending to marry him. Although A was lying weakly on the bed, one breath could not be suddenly cut off. Just then, they happened to see a beggar with a small snake, which moved them to buy it for a hundred coins, leading to A's death. Indeed, there were no visible injuries to verify the cause of death, and the scheme was also clever. From then on, A's innocence was proven, and the woman was executed by slicing, while her cousin was immediately beheaded as a sacrifice. The official then ordered the clan leader to choose among the family's descendants and appoint one as A's heir to continue the lineage and offerings. People far and near heard about it and all praised it. Grateful to the City God for showing a dream, the official sacrificed animals and offered sacrifices to repay it. Recognizing that the young man's filial piety was the only reason the case could be resolved, the official summoned the mother and son to the office, arranged for the young man to marry and have children, and gave him money for small business ventures to ensure a comfortable life for the rest of his days.

    590

    里乘子曰：公自筮仕吾皖，历宰剧邑，所断奇狱甚多，皖人至今犹津津乐道之。其最著者，莫如某甲之狱。好事者已谱入传奇，播之管弦矣。偶阅《蝶阶外史》，载黎襄勤公世序宰江西时，曾雪冤狱，与此相类。顾同一用蛇致命，此由谷道窜入腹中，彼由口窜入腹中，为小异耳。
    　　
    　　张船山先生讯盗

    Explanation

    590

    里乘子曰：公自筮仕吾皖，历宰剧邑，所断奇狱甚多，皖人至今犹津津乐道之。其最著者，莫如某甲之狱。好事者已谱入传奇，播之管弦矣。偶阅《蝶阶外史》，载黎襄勤公世序宰江西时，曾雪冤狱，与此相类。顾同一用蛇致命，此由谷道窜入腹中，彼由口窜入腹中，为小异耳。

    Li Chengzi said: "The gentleman, having divined his career in our Anhui, governed busy towns and handled many unusual cases. The people of Anhui still speak of them with great interest to this day. The most famous of these cases is that of Mr. A. Those who love to tell tales have already included it in legendary stories and performed it in music. I chanced upon reading 'Butterfly Staircase Outer History,' which recounts how Li Xiangqin Gong Shi Xu, when governing Jiangxi, once cleared a wrongful case, similar to this one. Yet, both cases involved the use of a snake to cause death, with this one entering the abdomen through the rectum, and the other through the mouth—a small difference only."

    591

    遂宁张船山先生问陶，以翰林出守莱州，恃才傲上，上官以先生才望素著，皆优容之。会长白某公巡抚山东，先生来谒，公谓其无礼，心甚嗛之。语方伯曰：“莱州张守，书生结习未除。太守为一郡表率，渠能胜任耶？”方伯固与先生齐年契好，为之说曰：“张守虽系书生，闻尚不误民事。”时有剧盗桀骜狙诈，屡断屡翻，承讯官皆莫可如何。公冷笑谓方伯曰：“君谓张守不误民事，如某盗，渠能定谳，当即令其旋任；否则，予将登诸白简，莫怪老夫无情也。”方伯唯唯。出语先生，问：“君能定谳否？”先生笑曰：“有何不能。”方伯大喜。商诸廉访，即延先生至臬署讯盗。佥问：“先生计几日可以了结？”先生笑曰：“此细事耳，三日足矣。”又问：“需用何刑？”先生笑曰：“刑具俟用时再议。所最要者，金华极精干脯一大盘，绍兴佳酿一大瓮，藉此聊助舌锋，断不可少。”佥笑曰：“诺。”翌辰，先生至臬署客厅，箕坐炕上。

    Explanation

    591

    A Man of Talent and Arrogance

    Mr. Zhang Chanshan of Suining, also known as Wen, was appointed as the governor of Laizhou from his position as a Hanlin scholar. He relied on his talent and was arrogant towards his superiors. His superiors, recognizing his talent and reputation, all treated him leniently. A certain official from Changbai toured and governed Shandong. Mr. Zhang came to pay respects, but the official thought he was disrespectful and was deeply resentful. He told the governor of the province, "The governor of Laizhou, Zhang, still has the habits of a scholar. As the governor of a prefecture, can he really handle it?" The governor of the province, being of the same age as Mr. Zhang, was a good friend. He said to him, "Although Mr. Zhang is a scholar, he is still competent in handling civil affairs."

    At that time, there was a notorious bandit who was arrogant and cunning. He repeatedly broke out of prison, and the officials in charge could not do anything about it. The official laughed coldly and said to the governor of the province, "If you say that Mr. Zhang is competent in handling civil affairs, then he should be able to resolve the case of this bandit and can be returned to his post. Otherwise, I will report it to the emperor, don't blame me for being无情." The governor of the province nodded in agreement. He went out and told Mr. Zhang, asking, "Can you resolve the case?" Mr. Zhang smiled and said, "What is there that I cannot do?" The governor of the province was very pleased. He consulted with the censor and invited Mr. Zhang to the provincial government office to interrogate the bandit.

    They asked, "How many days does Mr. Zhang need to resolve the case?" Mr. Zhang smiled and said, "This is a trivial matter, three days will be enough." They asked, "What kind of punishment is needed?" Mr. Zhang smiled and said, "The punishment equipment can be discussed when it is needed. What is most important is a large plate of extremely fine dried meat from Jinhua and a large jar of fine wine from Shaoxing, which will help my tongue, and cannot be spared." They all smiled and said, "Yes."

    The next day, Mr. Zhang arrived at the provincial government office's living room and sat cross-legged on the kang.

    592

    几置金华极精干脯一大盘，阶下置绍兴佳酿一大瓮，一僮扇炉暖酒，一僮执壶侍侧，一书吏在旁录供。呼盗跽膝前，先生左手把杯，右手翻阅案牍，而问盗曰：“汝郯城人耶？”盗对曰：“然。”“汝年几何矣？”曰：“三十有七矣。”“汝居乡乎？居城乎？”曰：“居城。”“汝有父母乎？”曰：“小人不幸，父母俱亡矣。”“汝有兄弟乎？”曰：“兄弟三人，小人其长也。”“汝有妻子乎？”曰：“小人有二子，长年十八，能猎兽矣；幼年十三，尚未能猎兽也。”“汝家何业也？”曰：“无所事事也。”斯时，方伯与廉访诸公俱在屏后窃听，以先生素工言语，必能摘奸发覆，不料所问皆琐琐细事，殊与原案无涉，佥相视匿笑。又恐不能了结，无以复某公之命，深以为虑。越日，先生至臬署，又问盗曰：“汝郯城人耶？”盗对曰：“然。”“汝年几何矣？”曰：“小人三十有九，明年且四十矣。”“汝居乡乎？居城乎？”曰：“居乡。

    Explanation

    592

    A large tray of extremely fine dried meat from Jinhua was placed, and at the foot of the steps, a large vat of fine Shaoxing wine was set. One servant fanned the furnace to warm the wine, another held a wine jug at the side, and one clerk recorded the confession beside. The official called the thief and had him kneel before him. The official held a cup in his left hand and flipped through the case file in his right, asking the thief, "Are you from Tanzhou?" The thief replied, "Yes." "How old are you?" "I am thirty-seven." "Do you live in the countryside or the city?" "I live in the city." "Do you have parents?" "Unfortunately, my parents are both deceased." "Do you have siblings?" "I have three brothers, and I am the eldest." "Do you have a wife and children?" "I have two sons; the elder is eighteen and can hunt; the younger is thirteen and is not yet able to hunt." "What is your family's occupation?" "We have no occupation."

    At that moment, the Governor and the Censor, along with other officials, were secretly listening behind a screen. They knew the official was skilled in questioning and expected him to uncover the truth. However, they were surprised that the questions were mostly about trivial matters unrelated to the case. They exchanged amused glances and worried that the official might not be able to resolve the matter, which would disappoint the expectations of the higher authority. The next day, the official returned to the governor's office and asked the thief again, "Are you from Tanzhou?" The thief replied, "Yes." "How old are you?" "I am thirty-nine, and I will be forty next year." "Do you live in the countryside or the city?" "I live in the countryside."

    593

    ”“汝有父母乎？”曰：“小人父早亡，母已下堂矣。”“汝有兄弟乎？”曰：“兄弟三人，小人其次也。”“汝有妻子乎？”曰：“小人有一子一女，皆孩提也。”“汝家何业也？”曰：“薄田数亩，务农为业也。”诸公俱复窃听，以先生所问与昨无异，益复吃吃匿笑。至第三日，先生至臬署，方伯与廉访问曰：“君言三日了结，今三日矣，果能了结耶？”先生笑曰：“下官向不打诳语。今日下午，当可了结，公等请无虑也。”因传谕皂隶人等，预备刑具，听候结案。先生至客厅，依旧箕坐炕上，以干脯下酒，呼盗跽膝前。问曰：“汝郯城人耶？”盗对曰：“然。”“汝年几何矣？”曰：“去年四十，今又添一岁矣。”“汝居乡乎？居城乎？”曰：“时而居城，时而居乡也。”“汝有父母乎？”曰：“小人有母，年逾七十矣。”“汝有兄弟乎？”曰：“小人有两兄，皆亡故矣。”“汝有妻子乎？”曰：“小人有子，尚呱呱在抱也。

    Explanation

    593

    "Have you any parents?" he asked. "My father passed away early, and my mother has already come down from the hall," the man replied. "Do you have any brothers?" he asked. "I have three brothers, and I am the second one," he replied. "Do you have a wife and children?" he asked. "I have one son and one daughter, both still young children," he replied. "What is your family's occupation?" he asked. "A few acres of thin land, farming is our profession," he replied. The gentlemen all secretly listened, because what the master asked today was the same as yesterday, and they laughed secretly even more. On the third day, the master went to the office of the provincial governor. The provincial governor and the censor asked, saying, "You said it would be resolved in three days, and now it is the third day, can it really be resolved?" The master smiled and said, "I have never lied before. Today in the afternoon, it should be resolved. Please don't worry, gentlemen." He then ordered the executioners to prepare the instruments of punishment, waiting for the case to be concluded. The master went to the living room and sat cross-legged on the kang (a traditional heating platform), eating dried meat with wine. He called the thief to kneel in front of him. He asked, "Are you from Tancheng?" The thief replied, "Yes." "How old are you?" he asked. "Last year I was forty, this year I am one year older," he replied. "Do you live in the countryside or in the city?" he asked. "Sometimes in the city, sometimes in the countryside," he replied. "Do you have parents?" he asked. "I have a mother, she is over seventy years old," he replied. "Do you have brothers?" he asked. "I have two brothers, both have passed away," he replied. "Do you have a wife and children?" he asked. "I have a son, he is still a baby," he replied.

    594

    ”“汝家何业也？”曰：“无田可耕，或渔而或樵也。”诸公窃听，益复相视匿笑，谓先生所问，如老妪絮语，何能定谳？至日晡后，先生乃命僮取巨觥来，连满饮三巨觥，命将酒脯彻去，传集皂隶，准备刑具听用。先生正色危坐而语盗曰：“今当问及正案矣。我观案牍，前承讯各官所谳一一属实，汝何屡断屡翻也？”盗叩首曰：“小人实系负冤，尚求矜察。”先生拍案叱曰：“汝休矣！人谓汝桀骜狙诈，实属不谬。

    Explanation

    594

    “Your family's occupation?” they asked. He replied, “We have no land to farm, so we sometimes fish and sometimes chop wood.” The others listened secretly and exchanged secret smiles, thinking that the questions the master asked were trivial, like the chatter of an old woman, how could they possibly be used to determine a verdict? By the late afternoon, the master then ordered the servant to bring a large cup, and drank three large cups in a row. He then ordered the wine and meat to be removed and assembled the bailiffs, preparing the instruments of punishment. Sitting upright with a serious expression, the master said to the thief, “Now we will discuss the main case. I have reviewed the case records, and all the judgments made by previous officials were true. Why have you repeatedly overturned them?” The thief knelt and said, “I am truly innocent and seek your leniency and consideration.” The master slammed his hand on the table and shouted, “Enough! People say you are wild, stubborn, sly, and deceitful, and they are not wrong.”

    595

    我与汝絮语三日，皆家常琐事，汝三日所答，前后迥不相符，琐事尚如此反覆，况正案耶！汝果从直吐实，尚不愧为好汉，如再敢饰言强辩，我即将三日所答琐事，以证汝之反复，虽严刑处死，亦不为过。汝须自忖，毋自讨苦吃也！”盗犹欲强辩，先生叱左右严为用刑，毙命勿论。盗急叩头乞命，情愿吐实，誓不再翻。先生大喜，立命画供，其案遂结。方伯与廉访诸公在屏后闻之，叹服不置。比复命某公，公叹曰：“名下固无虚士，不谓张守有才如此。今而后不敢轻量天下士矣！”一时历下传为美谈云。

    Explanation

    595

    I chatted with you for three days, discussing only trivial household matters. Yet, your responses were completely inconsistent, and even in such minor details, you showed such inconsistency. How much more so in a serious case! If you truly confess and speak the truth, you will not disgrace yourself as a man of honor. But if you dare to fabricate words and argue forcefully, I will use the trivial matters you have already admitted to as evidence of your inconsistency. Even severe punishment, including execution, would not be excessive. You must reflect on yourself and not bring trouble upon yourself!” The thief still tried to argue forcefully, but the officials shouted at their attendants to apply severe punishment, and the thief’s life was not spared. The thief immediately knelt down and begged for mercy, willing to confess and swear never to change his story again. The officials were greatly pleased and immediately ordered him to write his confession, thus closing the case. The governor and the censors, who were hidden behind a screen, heard this and were filled with admiration. When they reported back to a certain official, he sighed, saying, “A person’s reputation is not empty. I did not expect Zhang Shou to be so talented. From now on, I dare not lightly judge the talents of people in the world!” For a time, this became a beautiful story in the region of Qufu.

    596

    张静山观察折狱
    　　
    　　滇南张公静山观察其仁，由进士为蜀中令。所至舆诵洋溢，计典屡膺上考。道光乙巳夏，以蓬州牧特擢新安太守。甫下车，有两姓争坟互控者，稽核旧牍，自嘉庆甲戌年兴讼，至是已三十馀年矣。公诧问书吏：“何迟久不能判断？”书吏对谓：“此案每新太守莅任，例来互控，缘两姓俱无契据，无从剖决，只合置之不理。”公叱曰：“天下岂有三十馀年不结之案！”立命传谕两姓，五日后登山验看，听候判断。翌日，公沐浴斋戒，祈祷城隍，夜宿庙中，求神示梦。五日后，亲自登山讯断。两姓俱至，一姓系望族，其人纳资以郡丞候选，衣冠华美，容止甚都；一姓系老诸生，年已七十许，貌甚寒俭。公大声谕之曰：“汝两姓为祖兴讼，历久不懈，孝思可嘉。惟闻自经具控，彼此阻祭，为汝祖者，毋乃馁而实甚，汝心安乎？”两姓皆伏地稽颡，唯唯请罪。

    Explanation

    596

    Zhang Jingshan, the Observing Official, Resolves a Dispute

    In the summer of 1835 during the Daoguang era, Zhang Gong Jingshan, a benevolent provincial official, was promoted from the magistrate of Pengzhou in Sichuan to the governor of Xin'an in Anhui. Upon taking office, he encountered a long-standing dispute between two families over a grave site. Reviewing old records, he discovered that the conflict had begun in 1804 during the Jiaqing era and had lasted for over 30 years.

    Zhang was surprised and asked the clerks, "Why has this case remained unresolved for so long?" The clerks explained, "Every time a new governor arrives, the two families file lawsuits against each other. Since both families lack documentation, it is impossible to resolve the case, and it has been left unaddressed."

    Zhang sternly replied, "Is there any case in the world that remains unresolved for over 30 years?" He immediately ordered the two families to meet him on the mountain five days later to inspect the site and await his judgment.

    The next day, Zhang bathed and fasted, prayed to the city god, and spent the night in the temple, seeking guidance through dreams. Five days later, he personally climbed the mountain to resolve the dispute. Both families arrived. One family was a prominent clan, and its representative was a candidate for the position of prefectural deputy, dressed in fine clothes and elegant attire, with a dignified demeanor. The other family was an elderly scholar, about 70 years old, looking very plain and frugal.

    Zhang spoke loudly to them, "Both of your families have been filing lawsuits for your ancestors for a long time, showing unwavering filial piety. However, I have heard that you have been filing lawsuits continuously, blocking each other's ancestral worship. Your ancestors must be starving and suffering greatly. How can your hearts be at peace?"

    Both families knelt down and bowed deeply, repeatedly asking for forgiveness.

    597

    公笑曰：“吾稽旧牍，见汝两姓名执一说，皆近情理，所恨两无契据耳。既思天下事，有一是必有一非，有一真必有一伪，非求神示梦，究不能决。昨特沐浴斋戒，祷宿城隍庙中，果见神传冢中人至，自称为某某之祖，被某某诬控，求我判断，我已许之矣。顾一经明白宣示，真伪既分，是非立决，此后，是其子孙，方准登山展祭；非其子孙，即不准过问。吾怜汝两姓皆系孝思，劳苦多年，孰真孰伪，孰是孰非，皆当别祖，过此以往，不能并至其陇矣。汝两人以为何如？”两人皆稽颡对曰：“谨从尊命。”于是阄拈，老诸生居先，郡丞次之。老诸生乃勉整敝冠，次且走伏墓前，草草三叩首毕，起身干哭，颜色忸怩，口中喃喃，不解所谓。公笑谓郡丞曰：“渠已别墓，次当轮至汝矣。”郡丞闻言，涕泪泫然，乃侧身伏拜墓前，大声泣曰：“子孙为祖宗兴讼多年，不辞劳苦。

    Explanation

    597

    The official smiled and said, "I consulted old records and found that you two surnames each hold a particular view, both of which are reasonable and logical, but what I regret is the lack of evidence. Considering the affairs of the world, if there is one that is right, there must be one that is wrong, and if there is one that is true, there must be one that is false. Without seeking divine guidance through a dream, one cannot truly decide. Yesterday, I specially bathed and fasted, prayed overnight at the city god's temple, and indeed saw the god convey someone from the grave to come. They claimed to be the ancestor of someone named..., falsely accused by someone named..., and asked me to judge. I have already agreed. Once clearly announced, truth and falsehood are already separated, and right and wrong are immediately decided. After this, only their descendants are allowed to climb the mountain for a memorial service; if not their descendants, they are not allowed to inquire. I pity that both of your surnames are filled with filial piety, having worked hard for many years. Which is true and which is false, which is right and which is wrong, all should separate their ancestors. Beyond this point, they cannot both reach their graves. What do you think?" Both of them knelt and bowed, replying, "We respectfully follow your command." Then they drew lots, with the older scholar going first, followed by the deputy magistrate. The older scholar tried to tidy up his old hat, hesitantly walked and knelt in front of the grave, hastily bowed three times and finished, stood up and sobbed, with an embarrassed appearance, mumbling and not understanding what it meant. The official smiled and said to the deputy magistrate, "He has already separated the grave, and next it will be your turn." Upon hearing this, the deputy magistrate shed tears, turned sideways and knelt and bowed in front of the grave, loudly wept, saying, "Descendants have initiated lawsuits for the ancestors for many years, not sparing effort."

    598

    今郡伯祷神得梦，一言判断，究不知真伪，可否不谬，倘所梦不实，为子孙者，此后不能与祭矣！兴念及此，能勿悲乎！”言毕，痛哭卧地，晕不能兴。斯时观者如堵，见之无不恻然太息。公笑谓众曰：“观两人别墓情形，真伪是非，汝众人当其喻之，尚待吾明白宣示乎？”众人等罗拜对曰：“微公言，小人等皆喻之矣。”因共赞郡丞为真孝子，而不直老诸生。公命众扶郡丞起，拳拳奖慰。老诸生惶愧俯首，默无一语。公谓老诸生：“汝别墓情形，众目共见，抚心自问，尚有何说？”老诸生汗流满面，自称知罪。公笑曰：“汝既知罪，吾亦不汝咎。但自今以后，凭众剖断，山归郡丞，毋得再讼，汝心甘乎？”老诸生唯唯听命，誓无反覆。公乃亲笔书判，令两姓画押。三十馀年难了葛藤，一旦斩绝，众口称快。盖此山本郡丞祖墓，老诸生侦知其久失契据，意图骗占。

    Explanation

    598

    The county magistrate prayed to the gods and had a dream, which led to a decisive judgment. However, the truth or falsehood of the dream was uncertain, and it was unclear whether the judgment was correct. If the dream was not true, the descendants would not be able to participate in ancestral worship afterward! Thinking of this, how could one not be sad?" He finished speaking, cried bitterly, and fell to the ground, fainting and unable to get up. At that moment, the onlookers were as thick as a wall, and all who saw it could not help but feel pity and sigh. The official smiled and said to the crowd, "Seeing the situation of the two people separating the tomb, the truth and falsehood, right and wrong, you all should understand it. Do you still need me to clearly announce it?" The crowd knelt and replied, "Without your words, we all understand it." They then praised the county magistrate as a true filial son and did not blame the old scholar. The official ordered the crowd to help the county magistrate up, warmly congratulating and comforting him. The old scholar was filled with shame and fear, bowed his head in silence, and said nothing. The official said to the old scholar, "Your situation in separating the tomb was seen by everyone. Reflecting on yourself, what else can you say?" The old scholar was drenched in sweat and admitted his guilt. The official smiled and said, "Since you know your guilt, I will not blame you. But from now on, rely on the judgment of the people, the mountain belongs to the county magistrate, and you must not re-litigate. Are you willing to accept this?" The old scholar nodded in agreement, swore not to change his mind, and the official then wrote the judgment by hand and had both families sign it. A dispute lasting more than thirty years was finally resolved, and everyone was delighted. It turned out that this mountain was originally the tomb of the county magistrate's ancestors, and the old scholar had discovered that the deed had been lost for a long time, intending to fraudulently take possession of it.

    599

    初与郡丞之祖兴讼，至郡丞已历三世。历任太守皆意郡丞家为望族，未免欺老诸生式微，咸有矜怜左袒之心。而孰知腐儒叵测，以朴陋文其奸诈。向非公巧以神道设教，黑白何由昭晰耶？是年秋，公举行郡试，延予襄校试卷。公固善饮，酒酣，尝为予述之，颇自得意。予叩公祈梦城隍，究竟果得梦兆否？公笑曰：“此姑妄言之耳。吾思两姓既无契据，只合令其别墓，以察其情形，果系真子孙，自有缠绵难舍之状；否则出于勉强，仓猝间难以掩著矣。大抵人即无良，于稠人广众之前，断未有甘心厚颜而真忍以他人之祖为祖者，天良未尽梏亡，只在此刻。人之所以异于禽兽也。吾悬揣此情，姑托言神梦以微察之，不谓果以此而竟决是非真伪也。”合座闻之，无不叹服。予特笔而识之，凡留心折狱者，遇疑难事，亦可以此为法。而神而明之，存乎其人，是又未可泥而不化也。

    Explanation

    599

    Initially, there was a lawsuit between the author and the grandfather of the county magistrate, which had lasted three generations by the time the county magistrate was involved. All previous county magistrates considered the county magistrate's family to be a prominent family and could not help but view the declining scholar as an old man who was being bullied, all having a sense of pity and inclination to favor the scholar. However, who would have thought that a decayed scholar is hard to fathom, covering up deceit with simplicity and crudeness. If not for the cleverness of the official using divine teachings to guide, how would the right and wrong be made clear? In that autumn, the official held the county examination and invited me to assist in examining the examination papers. The official was indeed fond of drinking, and when drinking was at its height, he often recounted the story to me, feeling quite pleased. I asked the official if he had actually received a dream omen from the city god. The official smiled and said, "This is just a casual statement. I thought since both families lacked written evidence, it would be appropriate to have them separate the graves to observe the situation. If they are indeed the true descendants, there would be a state of deep attachment and reluctance to part; otherwise, it would be out of compulsion, and in a hurry, it would be difficult to conceal it. Even if people are not good, in front of many people, there is no way they would willingly and shamelessly claim another person's ancestor as their own, humanity has not been completely extinguished, only in this moment. This is what distinguishes humans from animals. I speculated on this situation and pretended to use a divine dream to subtly investigate, who would have thought that this actually resolved the truth and falsehood." Everyone present listened and was greatly impressed. I recorded it with special emphasis, anyone who carefully deals with legal cases, when encountering difficult matters, can also use this as a method. The divine and clear understanding lies in the person, this cannot be rigidly applied without flexibility.

    600

    里乘卷九
    　　
    　　节录《土司婚礼记》
    　　
    　　向阅昔人所纪滇黔瑶僮风俗，凡男女婚配，大都不外吹芦踏歌，插竹为标；先野合生子，而后成礼，以为陋习，殊不足取。顷阅江阴陈定九先生鼎所撰《滇黔土司婚礼记》，则甚堪艳羡。兹姑撷其大略云。滇黔土司，其先本鹫氏，《左传》所载罗人、鸗人是也。楚灭宋、蔡、罗、鸗四国，俘其家室，放之南徼，遂成苗四家。各有后裔，冠、婚、丧、祭、衣食，一秉周礼，仍从周以建子之月为岁首。后鸗氏伯仲佐武侯平蛮有功，兄王于滇东，去鸟为龙氏；弟王于滇南，加凡为凤氏，同受爵于蜀汉。今宅第仍王家规模。

    Explanation

    600

    Li Cheng Volume Nine**

    Excerpt from The Wedding Ceremony of the Earthly Lords

    Previously, I read accounts of the customs of the Yao and僮 people in Yunnan and Guizhou. In general, the pairing of men and women involves nothing more than blowing reeds and dancing to songs, inserting bamboo as markers; first having a child through a wild union, then formalizing the marriage, which is considered a vulgar custom and truly not worth adopting. Recently, I read The Wedding Ceremony of the Earthly Lords written by Mr. Chen Dingjiu of Jiangyin, and it is very much enviable. Now I will briefly extract the main points. The Earthly Lords of Yunnan and Guizhou, whose ancestors were originally the Jiu clan, as recorded in the Zuo Commentary, were the Luo people and the Jiu people. The State of Chu conquered the four states of Song, Cai, Luo, and Jiu, captured their families, and exiled them to the southern frontier, thus forming the four Miao families. Each has descendants, with customs of capping, marriage, funerals, sacrifices, clothing, and food, all adhering to Zhou rituals, still following the Zhou calendar with the first month of the year being the month of Zi. Later, the elder and younger brothers of the Jiu clan assisted Zhuge Liang in subduing the barbarians and achieved merit. The elder brother became king in eastern Yunnan, changing his surname from Jiu to Long; the younger brother became king in southern Yunnan, adding Fan to his surname to become the Feng clan, both receiving titles from the Shu Han state. Now their residences still follow the scale of royal palaces.

    601

    四家多为姻好。嫡长女为嫡，长妇必媵八人，犹古诸侯一娶九女遗意也。定九先生十岁时，从季父宦滇中，逾年，季父殁，随其季庶母钱、并钱之兄伯可侨寓滇中，境甚窘。钱所存不上百金，交其兄伯可服贾，贩售建烟，藉得什一。伯可由是得与龙姓土司官交往。定九幼慧，从季庶母受书，年十四通举业，兼攻诗古文词。会诏改八股为论策，滇人虽老生宿儒，多不知论策体裁，知陈孺子幼而能文，庞首皓发，皆愿执贽门下，岁可得脩脯数百金，用遂不乏。定九冒滇籍，十六岁举于乡。初名太夏，字禹鼎，因官瓯削籍，乃以一字行，而就军功。

    Explanation

    601

    The four families often formed marital alliances. The eldest daughter was the legitimate heir, and the eldest wife was required to have eight attendants, a practice reminiscent of the ancient custom where a prince would marry nine women. Mr. Ding Jiu, at the age of ten, followed his younger uncle to serve in Yunnan. After a year, his younger uncle passed away, and Ding Jiu moved to live with his stepmother Qian and Qian's brother Bokai in Yunnan, where the circumstances were very difficult. Qian had less than a hundred gold pieces, so she entrusted her brother Bokai to engage in trade, specifically in tobacco. They earned a tenth of the profit from this trade. Bokai thus gained the opportunity to interact with officials of the Long family, a local chieftain. Ding Jiu was intelligent from a young age and received education from his stepmother. By the age of fourteen, he became proficient in the imperial examination subjects and also studied poetry, ancient prose, and literature. During a period when the imperial examination format changed from the eight-part essay to essays on policy and strategy, even experienced scholars in Yunnan were unfamiliar with the new format. It was known that Chen Ru, a young man, was already skilled in writing, and older scholars with white hair all wished to become his disciples. He could earn several hundred gold pieces annually as a teacher, and his means of support were thus not lacking. Ding Jiu claimed Yunnan as his place of origin and passed the provincial examination at the age of sixteen. He was initially named Tai Xia and given the courtesy name Yu Ding. Due to some official action, his registration was revoked, and he then used a single character as his name. Subsequently, he achieved military merit.

    602

    初，伯可因贾识龙氏土官，遂罢贾而馆于其家。龙氏极爱才，以定九幼而能文，遂字以嫡长女。十七岁毕姻，嫁资足埒王侯。龙女少定九三岁，名继桓，字幼君，美而慧；幼通僰文，工僰书，兼工绘事。媵者八人，曰兰彷，曰甄姑，曰郑重，曰琬香，曰蕙雪，曰安节，曰蕊珠，曰琼钿，皆殊色。或养于同姓，或选于良家，或由于庶产。中国士大夫娶彼嫡长女，礼亦媵八人，以王臣加于诸侯也。龙土司以定九贫，为大治第，而后成礼。第凡十三层，极轮奂之美。中十层各五楹，有头门、仪门、大堂、二堂、三堂，皆平屋。其后即书楼、妆楼、藏楼、绣楼、护楼。

    Explanation

    602

    At first, Boke, through the intermediary of a merchant, became acquainted with the local official of the Long family, and therefore dismissed the merchant and took up residence in their home. The Long family greatly valued talent, and because Dingjiu was young but already capable of writing, they betrothed him to their eldest daughter. At the age of seventeen, the marriage was completed, and the dowry was sufficient to rival that of a king or prince. The Long daughter was three years younger than Dingjiu, her name was Jihuan, and her courtesy name was Youjun. She was beautiful and intelligent, proficient in the Boshan script, skilled in writing it, and also adept at painting. There were eight concubines: Lanfang, Zhen Gu, Zhengzhong, Wanxiang, Huixue, Anjie, Ruizhu, and Qiongding, all of whom were exceptionally beautiful. Some were raised by relatives, some were selected from good families, and some came from common households. Chinese scholars and officials who married the eldest daughter of the Long family followed the custom of having eight concubines, this being a practice reserved for high-ranking officials, akin to those of feudal lords. The Long chieftain, seeing Dingjiu's poverty, built a grand mansion for him, and then the marriage was consummated. The mansion had thirteen floors, each exquisitely decorated. The middle ten floors each had five rooms, including an entrance gate, a ceremonial gate, a main hall, a second hall, and a third hall, all of which were flat-roofed buildings. Behind these were the study, dressing room, storage room, embroidery room, and guard room.

    603

    层各有厢二楹。三堂后，左右各五层，皆楼，各三楹，厢各二楹。左右各分贮四媵，媵各侍女四人，老媪一人。器物多紫檀、花梨，最爱爇沉速、安息等香。长官女有缠足者，民间多不然。而缠足极易，山有草曰“威灵仙”，煎其根，濯之数日，即纤如新月。女子尚短衣，长只齐腰；裙尚百折、或二百折，富者常穿五重，贫亦二三重。男亦然。其衷衣及裩，冬夏皆纻。处女夜卧，不脱不沐，临嫁方沐。嫁后，日一沐，沐毕必涂以苏合油，贫者以羊脂，故肤皆如凝脂。衷衣与裩相接，皆联金扣，扣以百数。裩口与罗袜接，亦密扣之。扣圆而扁，贫家以铅锡为之。

    Explanation

    603

    Each level has two side rooms with two columns. Behind the main hall, there are five floors on either side, all of which are buildings with three columns each, and two side rooms per floor. On both sides, four concubines are housed separately, each accompanied by four maids and one elderly woman. The furnishings are mostly made of rosewood and camphorwood, and they particularly enjoy burning incense such as sandalwood and myrrh. Some of the official's daughters have bound feet, but this is not common among the general populace. Foot binding is quite easy; there is a herb called "Wēilíngxiān" in the mountains. By boiling its roots and soaking the feet in the water for several days, the feet become as slender as a crescent moon. Women prefer short dresses that only reach their waist; their skirts have a hundred or two hundred pleats. Wealthy women often wear five layers, while poorer women wear two or three. Men also follow this custom. Their undergarments and trousers are made of ramie, both in winter and summer. Young unmarried women do not remove their clothes or bathe at night; they only bathe when they are about to get married. After marriage, they bathe daily and always apply scented oil after bathing; poorer women use sheep fat instead, so their skin remains smooth like congealed fat. The undergarments and trousers are fastened with numerous golden buttons, and the trousers are also secured with many buttons at the waist. The buttons are round and flat, and poorer families use lead and tin for them.

    604

    合卺定情始解，定情后，复着如故，生子始去。亲迎之日，奏蛮乐，苗女数十辈衣绯，击诸葛铜鼓，盘旋环绕于庭中，歌声如莺啼芳树，笔难形状。以朱丝一缕系婿左臂，引丝入室，系女右臂，牵女出，以锦蒙头，傧相赞拜。如礼毕，傧相乃更锦衣舞蹈，击铜鼓，讴苗词，请新人登车，举家哭送，诸媵母亦拥女泣涕登车。

    Explanation

    604

    In the wedding ceremony, the couple first drinks from a shared wine cup to seal their union, a sign of their established affection. After the initial ritual, they resume their normal attire and continue the celebration until they have a child. On the day of the wedding, Miao music is played, with dozens of Miao women dressed in bright red, striking the Zhu Ge bronze drums, and moving in a circular pattern around the courtyard. Their singing is as melodious as orioles chirping among fragrant trees, a scene that is difficult to describe fully. A red thread is tied to the groom's left arm, and the thread is led into the room to be tied to the bride's right arm, symbolically connecting the couple. The bride is then covered with a brocade cloth, and the master of ceremonies leads them in the traditional bowing ritual. After the rituals are completed, the master of ceremonies changes into brocade clothing and dances, playing the bronze drums and singing Miao songs, inviting the newlyweds to board the carriage. The entire family cries to see them off, and the attendants and mothers embrace the bride, crying as she boards the carriage.

    605

    既至婿家，一媪着绯衣，持钥启舆门，引新妇臂上朱丝付新郎，牵妇下车；侍女扶诸媵出，共拥新妇入青庐；夫妇交拜，媵皆随新妇后行礼；席地坐饮交杯，诸媵亦雁行列坐，新郎新妇各一饮，挨递诸媵。饮毕，相者击铜鼓、歌喜词，撒红豆为祝多男。奏乐毕，相者引新郎安诸媵室；绯衣媪即房门，为新妇更衣履、进香汤，凡三沐焉。相者引新郎从右安室，其俗尚右，故先右。侍者扶媵参新郎，郎坐受二拜，答二拜；老媪进媵者酒，手奉郎饮半，媵接跽饮毕，起鞠躬者四，侍女扶媵入帏，相者复引新郎安第二室，礼亦如之。左右八媵，次第安毕，相者引新郎还正室更衣。相者出，新妇出迎，鞠躬者四，新郎答以四揖，携手入帏。媵新沐毕，更衣，俱至帏中，亦鞠躬者四，新郎新妇答礼毕，告辞各归卧房。

    Explanation

    605

    Upon arriving at the groom's home, an elderly woman dressed in a red robe held a key to open the carriage door, leading the bride's arm with a red thread and handing it over to the groom, then guiding her down from the carriage. Maidservants helped the bridesmaids out, and together they escorted the bride into the blue tent. The couple performed the mutual bowing ritual, and the bridesmaids followed the bride in performing the rituals. They sat on the ground and drank from the shared cup, the bridesmaids also sitting in rows like geese. The groom and bride each took a sip, then passed the cup to the bridesmaids. After drinking, the matchmaker struck a copper drum and sang auspicious words, scattering red beans to wish for many sons. After the music stopped, the matchmaker led the groom to the rooms of the bridesmaids. The red-robed woman immediately opened the door to the bridal chamber, changing the bride's clothes and shoes, bringing her perfumed water, and bathing her three times. The matchmaker led the groom to the right room, as the custom favored the right side, so it was done first. Maidservants helped the bridesmaids greet the groom, who sat and received two bows, then returned two bows. An elderly woman brought wine for the bridesmaids, handing it to the groom for him to drink half, and the bridesmaids knelt to drink the rest, then bowed four times. The maidservants helped the bridesmaids enter the curtain, and the matchmaker led the groom to the second room, with the ritual being the same. Eight bridesmaids on both sides were settled in order. The matchmaker led the groom back to the main room to change clothes. The matchmaker left, and the bride came out to greet, bowing four times, to which the groom responded with four bows. They held hands and entered the curtain. The bridesmaids finished bathing, changed clothes, and all went to the curtain, also bowing four times. The groom and bride returned the greeting, then bid farewell and went to their respective bedrooms.

    606

    鸡鸣，诸媵栉沐，至新房递茶贺喜，候新妇妆毕，均随新郎新妇至姑寝门外递茶，姑受茶不接见，令婢辞焉；郎率妇及媵于寝门外再拜而退。第六日，张乐设席后堂，郎与妇拜天地、祖先及灶，次拜姑，次女亲，次小姑；诸媵随妇后行礼。南向一席，坐新妇；东向八席，坐诸媵；西向四席，坐诸女亲；西北向一席，则姑与小姑主焉。姑递杯箸，妇跽辞，小姑代行礼毕，妇递姑杯箸，次女亲，次小姑。饮三爵，彻席更衣。再饮三爵，妇率诸媵下堂，拜讫，随姑入室，为姑进帏帐、衾枕、衣服、首饰，奉沃浣；候姑寝，乃率媵退。

    Explanation

    606
