    At cockcrow, the attendants bathed and groomed themselves, then went to the new bridal chamber to present tea and offer congratulations. They waited until the bride was fully dressed, and then all followed the groom and bride to the door of the mother-in-law’s sleeping chamber, where they presented tea. The mother-in-law accepted the tea but did not receive them personally, instead sending a maid to decline it. The groom then led the bride and attendants in a second bow outside the mother-in-law’s chamber before retreating.

    On the sixth day, music was set up and a banquet arranged in the rear hall. The groom and bride paid homage to heaven, their ancestors, and the kitchen god, then bowed in turn to the mother-in-law, the bride’s maternal relatives, and the bride’s younger female relatives. The attendants followed the bride in performing the rites.

    A single seat facing south was reserved for the bride; eight seats facing east for the attendants; four seats facing west for the bride’s maternal relatives; and one seat facing northwest, where the mother-in-law and the bride’s younger female relatives sat.

    The mother-in-law presented the bride with a cup and chopsticks, and the bride knelt to decline them. The younger female relative performed the ritual on her behalf. Then the bride presented the cup and chopsticks to the mother-in-law, followed by the maternal relatives, and then the younger female relatives. They drank three cups of wine, then cleared the table and changed clothes. After drinking a further three cups, the bride led the attendants down from the hall, completed the bows, and entered the chamber with the mother-in-law. She presented the mother-in-law with bedding, pillows, clothing, and jewelry, as well as toiletries. After waiting for the mother-in-law to rest, the bride led the attendants in withdrawing.

    607

    自是，每鸡鸣必起栉沐，率诸媵至姑寝门，如未醒，即默候；既醒，则呼内侍女启门入，为姑着衣履，栉漱沐浣，进早膳讫，乃退。中午亦率媵奉饭。每日以一媵侍其役，旦暮为姑涤溺器、整衾枕候寝，然后退，日日如是。若有疾，则请假，俾诸媵奉事如前。三月，请设三代祖先神主，夫妇率媵谒焉。肆筵设席，大堂会男，后堂会女，夫妇执贽遍拜长者，各受贻赠而成妇焉。每室除侍女外，另有室媪，一室均受约束。又总立一室老，室老者，宗人中年老婺妇之有品德者也，聘请掌一家之事，皆听其指挥，不敢违命。其人能僰文，家中事事登簿，以报岳家；性极端严，妇及诸媵并侍女人等，稍不合则骂詈，轻则掴以掌，重则杖之，见之无不胆落。

    Explanation

    607

    From this point onward, every time the rooster crows, the narrator rises early, combs their hair, and leads all the concubines to the grandmother's bedroom. If the grandmother is not awake, they quietly wait; once she is awake, they call the inner servant girl to open the door and enter, dressing the grandmother in clothes and shoes, combing her hair, washing her face, and cleaning herself. After having breakfast, they then leave. In the middle of the day, they also lead the concubines to serve lunch. Each day, one concubine serves as an attendant, morning and evening, serving the grandmother by cleaning the chamber pot and arranging the bedding for sleep, then leaving. Day after day, this routine continues. If there is illness, then take leave, so that the other concubines continue to serve as before. After three months, requests the establishment of ancestral tablets for the three generations. Husband and wife lead the concubines to pay respects. Sets out tables and seats, men gather in the main hall, women in the back hall. Husband and wife present gifts and bow to elders, each receiving gifts and becoming a wife. Each room has a lady-in-waiting besides the attendant, all in the room are subject to rules. Also appoints a senior lady-in-waiting, who is an elderly woman of good character from the family, hired to manage household affairs. All listen to her commands and dare not disobey. She can read and write, records all household matters in a register to report to the in-laws. She has an extremely strict nature, and any slight inappropriateness results in scolding. Lightly, she slaps with her hand; heavily, she beats with a stick. Seeing her, no one does not tremble with fear.

    608

    各媵女独处，卧则不许偃仰纵横，既覆以衾，外加绣袱，四角镇以铜兽，重或二三斤，若不令其转侧者；寝后即禁复起溲溺。帏外彻夜张灯，榻前每夕轮一婢伺值，室老时行潜察，一闻鼾声，即排闼入，捉其发而扑之。每二鼓即寝，鸡初鸣，室老即击铜版者七，各室媪亦各击铜版以应之，促诸妇起。栉沐毕，俱集正室，为主妇治妆，妆毕，偕往候姑。凡有身者，立少不端，坐少不正，卧或偃仰纵横，及酣酒茹荤者，室老辄严诫之。

    Explanation

    608

    The concubines live alone, and they are not allowed to lie down in any position other than a specific one. First, they are covered with a quilt, and then a decorative coverlet is placed over it. The corners of the coverlet are weighted with heavy bronze figurines, each weighing two or three pounds, to prevent them from turning over. After sleeping, they are not allowed to get up to urinate. Lights are kept burning outside the curtain throughout the night, and a servant is assigned each night to stand by the bed. The head of the household secretly inspects them. Upon hearing snoring, the head of the household bursts in and slaps them. They go to bed at two drum beats and wake up at dawn when the head of the household strikes a copper plate seven times. The maids in each room respond by striking their own copper plates, urging the women to wake up. After washing and combing their hair, they gather in the main room to prepare the main wife for her makeup. After finishing, they accompany the main wife to greet her mother-in-law. Any pregnant woman who stands improperly, sits improperly, lies in an improper position, or indulges in alcohol and meat is severely reprimanded by the head of the household.

    609

    又妇凡为姑涤溺器、浣衣服、治裳履、整衾枕、进饮食，生子者连三日，生女者二日，未生者一日，次第挨班，无或少紊，皆室老主之。虽主妇必身亲其役，不敢假手于人，有疾请假始免。至主人欲与诸媵坐，必其卧榻乃可；若于短足几等类，室老知之，必加挞责。媵或逢怒主人，室老亦必勒其褫去下衣，当庭痛扑不宥；故举室媵婢，从无喧哗，其畏室老如三军之禀令大帅也。其俗，岳父母极爱怜婿。龙媪故时至定九家，先是定九亲迎，后龙媪初月一至，后则月两三至或四五至，至则必熟视女眉目及婿眉目，时与室老作密语。定九不解所言何事，以意揣之，似以婿年幼，尚未知与乃女定情也。又时时密问女，女辄融然面赤，俯首不答；固问，固不答，龙媪辄咨嗟顿足而去。

    Explanation

    609

    In the household, the woman performs all duties for her mother-in-law, including cleaning the chamber pot, washing clothes, mending shoes, arranging the bedding, and preparing meals. If a son is born, she serves for three consecutive days; if a daughter, for two days; and if no child is born, for one day. These duties are carried out in an orderly manner without any disorder, all overseen by the room elder. Even the main wife must personally perform these tasks and cannot delegate them to others; she can only be excused if she is ill and takes leave. When the master wishes to sit with the concubines, he must lie on his bed to do so; if he sits on a low table or similar item, the room elder will know and punish him. If a concubine angers the master, the room elder will force her to remove her lower garments and whip her severely in public, with no leniency. As a result, all concubines and maids are quiet and obedient, fearing the room elder as much as soldiers fear their commanding officer.

    The custom is that the father-in-law and mother-in-law deeply love and care for their son-in-law. The mother-in-law (龙媪) would visit the son-in-law's (定九) home. Initially, the son-in-law would go to the mother-in-law's home to bring his wife back. Later, the mother-in-law would visit once a month, then two or three times a month, or even four or five times a month. When she visited, she would closely examine the eyes of both the daughter and the son-in-law, and have secret conversations with the room elder. The son-in-law did not understand what they were talking about, but he guessed that it was because he was young and did not yet know about the relationship between him and her daughter. The mother-in-law would also ask the daughter in secret, and the daughter would blush and not answer. When pressed, the daughter still did not answer, and the mother-in-law would sigh and leave in frustration.

    610

    定九及其母均不解，惟纳闷吁叹而已。他日，又来密问女，见女不答，辄垂涕，女不得已，密附母耳语数言，龙媪幡然大喜，起抚定九肩者再，欣然含笑而去。定九有表妹钱氏，名洁，字素瑜，少定九四岁，伯可女也。亦美而慧，工书能诗，尤善长短句。初，陈母见龙媪与室老密语，不解所谓，心甚忧之。钱女以在苗中久，悉解其语，乃宽慰陈母曰：“毋忧也，无他事耳，我知之矣。”陈母叩之，又羞不能答，盖难于启口也。陈母忧疑益甚。半载后，彼此渐通汉苗语言，定九私询妇，乃母向日垂涕顿足之故，妇一以告，果不出定九所揣也。定九幼时，胆最弱怯。

    Explanation

    610

    Ding Jiu and his mother were both at a loss, only sighing and expressing their frustration. One day, he came secretly to ask his daughter, but seeing that she did not answer, he would shed tears. Unable to bear it, he secretly whispered a few words to his mother. The Dragon Lady suddenly became very happy, stood up, and patted Ding Jiu's shoulder twice, then left happily with a smile. Ding Jiu had a cousin named Qian Jie, whose given name was Jie and courtesy name was Su Yu. She was four years younger than Ding Jiu and the daughter of Bo Ke. She was also beautiful and intelligent, skilled in calligraphy and poetry, especially good at writing short poems. At first, Chen's mother saw the Dragon Lady and the master of the house whispering secretly, not understanding what it was about, and was very worried. Qian's daughter, having lived among the Miao for a long time, understood all the words, and then comforted Chen's mother, saying, "Do not worry, there is nothing else, I know." Chen's mother asked her, but she was embarrassed and could not answer, it being difficult to bring it up. Chen's mother's worry and doubt increased. After half a year, they gradually became familiar with each other's Han and Miao languages. Ding Jiu privately asked his wife about the reason why his mother had shed tears and stamped her feet the other day. His wife told him everything, and indeed, it was as Ding Jiu had suspected. When Ding Jiu was young, he had the weakest and most timid courage.

    611

    常闻舅氏伯可言，龙凤四家在苗中风气颇正，亲子弟奸僮仆妇女，杀必不宥，闻之心益悚。方合卺后，安诸媵室，自以为皆送亲来之女，我有主道，礼当安其室。至诸媵奉酒，又以为内家之人，敬我娇客耳，益不敢或犯。后见其同妇事姑，稍稍怪其为侍妾；又见其与妇同起居，若非卑贱之流，见婿辄侍立，不敢抗坐。夫妇彼此言语不通，无从问讯，竟不识其为何等人。盖诸媵半属宗人，半选良家，大都皆家臣之女。其齿以新娘居中，上而递长至四龄，下而递幼至四龄，是犹仿周制也。服饰器皿皆与主妇同，惟主妇多一金项环，耳钏则以金起花，他皆素金耳钏，此小异也。

    Explanation

    611

    I often heard my maternal uncle say that the four Dragon and Phoenix families in the Miaoyang region maintained a very upright customs. If any of their sons and daughters committed adultery with servants or female attendants, they would be executed without mercy — hearing this, my heart grew more fearful. After the wedding ceremony, I settled the concubines in their rooms, believing them all to be women sent by the family, and thinking that, as the husband, I had the authority to provide them with proper accommodation according to the rites. When the concubines offered me wine, I took it as a sign of respect from members of the inner family, thinking them to be favored guests, and thus dared not transgress in any way. Later, I saw them performing the same duties as the wife toward the mother-in-law, and gradually began to find it strange that they were only concubines. I also noticed that they lived and dined with the wife, and did not seem like lowly or humble people — when they saw the husband, they would stand in attendance and dared not sit in a position of equality. The husband and wife could not understand each other’s language, and so had no way to inquire or communicate, and I could not even tell what kind of people they were. It turned out that half of the concubines were from the noble family, and half were selected from good families — most were the daughters of household servants. Their ages were arranged with the bride in the middle, increasing by four years above and below — this was modeled after the Zhou dynasty system. Their clothing, ornaments, and utensils were the same as the wife’s, except that the wife had an additional gold necklace, and her earrings were made of gold with floral patterns, while the others had plain gold earrings — this was the only minor difference.

    612

    一夕，龙媪携酒筵来，大张花烛，于下房盛设帏帐衾枕，令媵者兰彷严妆出拜陈母，次拜定九夫妇及室老诸人，后拜龙媪，各奉酒三爵毕，归下房。日昳，龙媪去，新妇携双烛，引定九寝下房。定九笑问：“何为？”妇曰：“寒门家法，凡女适人半载不孕，即令媵妾入值，期早生子。今妾空侍巾栉六阅月矣，兰姊长，当首入侍，家慈故特送花烛来耳。且男子结褵，敝乡风俗期一年举子，不则嗣续艰矣。家慈前所以皇皇者，为妾之不娠也。”自是，间两日，兰必入值，鸡初鸣即去。

    Explanation

    612

    One night, Long Ao arrived with a banquet, lighting up the hall with flower candles, and in the lower chamber, she had lavish curtains, bedding, and quilts set up. She ordered the concubine Lan Fang to dress formally and come out to pay respects to the mother-in-law, then to pay respects to Ding Jiu and his wife, as well as the household elders, and finally to herself. Each person offered three cups of wine in turn, after which they returned to the lower chamber. At noon, Long Ao departed, and the new wife, carrying two candles, led Ding Jiu to sleep in the lower chamber. Ding Jiu smiled and asked, “What is this for?” The wife replied, “It is according to the customs of our humble family: if a woman has not conceived within half a year of marriage, a concubine is brought in to serve, in hopes of early childbirth. I have now served as a maid for six months, and since Lan is the eldest, she should be the first to enter service. That is why my mother specially brought the flower candles. Moreover, when a man marries, in our village it is customary to expect a child within a year; otherwise, it becomes difficult to continue the family line. That is why my mother was so anxious before—because I had not conceived.” From then on, every two days, Lan would enter service, departing as soon as the rooster first crowed.

    613

    《诗》所谓“戴星而往还”者，非欤？两月，兰不孕，龙媪又如前携花烛酒肴来，送甄姑入值，一切如兰仪。月馀，大妇及两媵均有孕。孕者，室老即不令入值，且有厉禁。盖苗中婴儿，最忌出痘，百无一二生者；痘气又易沾染，即壮夫染之亦无不痘，痘无不死。常因一儿出痘，祸延一乡，竟绝噍类。惟一受孕，即禁与男子接，儿生即决不出痘；故大家皆设室老，专司其事；小户则其姑严察之。凡妇今夕受胎，翌晨眉间即有一缕红丝隐约现出，大家妇人每早必参见室老，室老一见即知，曰：“若有孕矣。”立为移榻独宿，夜必扃钥，室老日夜防闲，不少疏忽；至七月胎成，方敢解严，以关系非一人家故也。

    Explanation

    613

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    614

    龙媪以三女皆孕，大悦，以次备花烛筵席，送媵者郑重、琬香、蕙雪、安节、蕊珠、琼钿六女入值乃已。八媵中惟琬香与妇里面酷似，定九每误认为妇，辄拉手与语，琬香每诮让曰：“我非乘卷娘子，郎君幸尊重。”人皆恒以为笑。他日，妇故与定九戏，以九环纳琬香项，令入寝室，定九方踞坐榻上，以为己也妇者，拉手与语，琬香翩若惊鸿，踉跄趋出，定九深讶之。少选妇入，项无环，则以为琬香也，急问娘子焉往，妇笑曰：“谁为娘子者？”辄与郎并坐于榻，定九又深讶之，以为媵向不敢与主人抗也。无何，琬香入，探环还纳妇项，顾而嗤曰：“还郎君娘子。”举室哄然大笑。

    Explanation

    614

    The dragon goddess was greatly pleased that her three daughters were all pregnant, and in turn, she prepared the wedding banquet, sending the attendants Zhongzheng, Wanxiang, Huixue, Anjie, Ruizhu, and Qiongchan into service. Among the eight attendants, only Wanxiang looked very similar to the wife inside. Dingjiu often mistook her for the wife and would take her hand to talk to her. Wanxiang would always chide him, saying, "I am not the wife, please treat me with respect, sir." Everyone always laughed about it. One day, the wife intentionally played with Dingjiu and put the nine-ringed necklace around Wanxiang's neck, sending her into the bedroom. Dingjiu was sitting on the bed, thinking she was the wife, and took her hand to talk to her. Wanxiang moved like a startled swan, staggering out of the room, and Dingjiu was deeply surprised. Soon, the wife entered, and her neck had no necklace. Dingjiu hurriedly asked where the wife had gone. The wife laughed and said, "Who is the wife?" She then sat next to Dingjiu on the bed, and Dingjiu was even more surprised, thinking that an attendant would never dare to defy the master. Soon after, Wanxiang entered, took the necklace, and put it back on the wife's neck. She looked at him and laughed, saying, "Returning the necklace to the wife." The whole household burst into laughter.

    615

    大抵苗中山峰峦相同者多，故人之面目亦多相肖，不独妇与琬香为然也。惟惜龙女十七岁即罹产难而卒。定九继娶者，即表妹钱洁也。初，洁与龙女最相亲爱，定九出游，洁即与龙女同起居，长枕大被，相拥而卧，不啻同怀姊妹。及龙女永诀时，告之父母，以服饰珍玩罄室与之，且曰：“愿二老视如己出，藉慰亲心，儿目亦瞑矣。”既殁，洁悲哀甚切。龙氏翁媪见待女真挚，爰喜而继为己女，以归定九，奁赠较倍于亲女。洁以媵中名蕊珠者，色尤艳丽，乃自教之书画，并谙九章数法，能推日月蚀，丝毫不爽。未几，定九北上，会吴藩叛滇，阻兵不能遽归。洁以忧思抑郁，寻卒。不逾年，蕊珠亦卒。定九所记甚长，予撷其十之四五如此。

    Explanation

    615

    In general, the mountains and peaks in the area are often similar, hence the faces of people are also often alike, not only women and Wang Xiang but others as well. Sadly, Long Nv died at the age of seventeen due to childbirth difficulties. Ding Jiu's second wife was his cousin, Qian Jie. Initially, Qian Jie was very close to Long Nv. When Ding Jiu traveled, Qian Jie would stay with Long Nv, sharing the same bed and pillow, embracing each other as if they were sisters. At the time of Long Nv's final farewell, she told her parents to give all her clothes, jewelry, and treasures to Qian Jie, and said, "I wish the two elders to treat her as their own daughter, to comfort the hearts of her parents, and my eyes will also close in peace." After Long Nv's death, Qian Jie was extremely sad. The Long family treated her sincerely and happily took her as their own daughter, marrying her to Ding Jiu, with a dowry even more generous than that given to a biological daughter. Qian Jie had a concubine named Rui Zhu, who was especially beautiful. She taught her painting and calligraphy herself and was familiar with the Nine Chapters on the Mathematical Art, able to predict solar and lunar eclipses without error. Shortly after, Ding Jiu went north, coinciding with Wu's rebellion in Yunnan, which delayed his return. Qian Jie died of worry and depression soon after. Within a year, Rui Zhu also died. Ding Jiu's records are very long, and I have only selected about a quarter to summarize.

    616

    里乘子曰：往予观丛书所载，才人遇合之隆，或托之神仙，或托之龙宫，甚或托之鬼怪梦寐，大抵不过《庄》、《列》寓言，未必实有其事，所谓子虚乌有，姑存其说可也。今阅定九先生所记，虽出苗中，其妆奁富厚，礼节雍容，服食起居之安适，求之世家巨室，亦不可多得。况龙女与八媵，和悦不妒，闺中善戏谑兮，诚如张京兆所云：“殆有甚于画眉者。”而继配又得钱女，遇合亦不可谓不隆，人生如此，南面不易也！惜定九不达，轻离远别，卒使冰弦再断，美同凋，岂艳福太稠，书生命薄，不足以消受耶！
    　　
    　　撷录《海上纪略》
    　　
    　　曩在都中，见达君经圃所刊郁君沧浪《裨海纪游》一书，系于役台湾，按日笔记。后附《海上纪略》数则，所志异闻异见，并滨海各国人情风俗甚详，意者亦有心人也，爰撷录以公同好。郁君名永河，武林人，官闽省牧令。达君名纶，襄平人，仕隐未详。

    Explanation

    616

    Li Chengzi said: In the past, I read in the collected volumes that the flourishing of meetings between talented people was often attributed to immortals, or to the dragon palaces, or even to ghosts and dreams. In general, these are no more than the allegorical tales of Zhuangzi and Liezi, and may not be real events. Such stories are, as the saying goes, fabrications like Zi Xu and Wu You, and it may be acceptable to preserve them for now. Now, upon reading the account of Mr. Dingjiu, I find that although she came from a modest background, her dowry was rich, her manners were dignified, and her clothing, food, and daily life were comfortable and pleasant. Seeking such a person among the great families and noble houses would be rare indeed. Furthermore, the dragon princess and her eight attendants were harmonious and not jealous, and in the inner quarters, they were skilled at jesting and teasing, as Zhang Jingzhao truly said: “It is almost as important as plucking one’s eyebrows.” Then, in a second marriage, he gained Qian’s daughter, and this union cannot be called anything less than prosperous. If life is like this, then ruling from the south is not an easy task! Alas, Dingjiu did not understand this, and lightly parted from his beloved, ultimately causing the icy strings to break again, and the beautiful flowers to wither together. Could it be that her beauty and fortune were too great, and the life of a scholar too frail, to bear such blessings?

    Extracted from Notes on the Sea (海上纪略)

    In the past, while in the capital, I saw a book published by Mr. Da Jingpu, which was a compilation by Mr. Yu Canglang titled Bihai Jiyou (裨海纪游), written during his service in Taiwan, with daily notes recorded. After the main text, several entries from Notes on the Sea were appended, detailing strange sights and unusual views, as well as the customs and manners of the coastal nations in great detail. It seems that the author was a thoughtful and observant person, so I have excerpted these passages to share with fellow enthusiasts. Mr. Yu’s name was Yonghe, a native of Wulin, who held office as a magistrate in Fujian province. Mr. Da’s name was Lünen, from Xiangping, and his career or retirement is not clearly recorded.

    617

    海吼
    　　
    　　海吼，俗呼海叫。小吼如击花鼓，点点作撒豆声，乍远乍近，若断若连，临流听之，有成连鼓琴之致。大吼如万马奔腾，钲鼓响震，三峡崩流，万鼎共沸，惟钱塘八月怒潮差可彷佛，触耳骇愕。余常濡足海岸，俯瞰溟渤，而静渌渊渟，会无波灂，不知声之何从出。然远海云气已渐兴，而风雨不旋踵至矣。海上人习闻不怪，曰是“雨征”也。若冬月吼，常不雨，多主风。
    　　
    　　天妃神
    　　
    　　海神，惟马祖最灵，即古天妃神也。凡海舶危难，有祷必应，多有目睹神兵维持、或神亲至救援者，灵异之迹，不可枚举。洋中风雨晦瞑，夜黑如墨，每于樯端现神灯示祐。又有船中忽出爝火如灯光、升樯而灭者，舟师谓是马祖火，去必遭覆败，无不奇验。船中例设马祖棍，凡值大鱼水怪欲近船，则以马祖棍连击船舷，即遁去。

    Explanation

    617

    The Roar of the Sea

    The roar of the sea, commonly called the sea's call, varies in intensity. The lighter roar sounds like the tapping of flower drums, with scattered, bean-like sounds, coming and going, sometimes breaking off and then resuming. Listening to it near the water, one can hear the refined and harmonious quality of a skilled musician playing the guqin. The powerful roar is like the galloping of thousands of horses, the thunder of drums and gongs, the cascading of waterfalls down three gorges, and the simultaneous boiling of thousands of cauldrons. The closest natural phenomenon to this is the Qiantang River tidal bore in August, which is the most powerful and awe-inspiring. I often wade into the sea and look out over the vast ocean, where the water is calm and still, with no waves or ripples. I cannot fathom where the sound originates. However, as clouds gather over the distant sea, storms quickly follow. People familiar with the sea are not surprised by this, calling it the "rain omen." During winter, the sea's roar often indicates wind rather than rain.

    The Sea Goddess

    Among the sea deities, only Matsu is the most powerful and revered, also known as the ancient Tianfei (Heavenly Consort) deity. In times of maritime danger, prayers to Matsu are answered, and many have witnessed divine soldiers protecting them or the goddess herself coming to their aid. The miraculous signs associated with Matsu are too numerous to count. At sea, during storms, the night is as dark as ink. Often, a divine light appears at the top of the mast, indicating divine protection. There are also instances where a small flame suddenly appears in the ship, rising to the mast and then disappearing. Sailors believe this to be the "Matsu fire," a sign of impending disaster. On board ships, a Matsu stick is commonly placed. Whenever large fish or sea monsters approach the ship, the Matsu stick is used to strike the ship's side, and the creatures retreat.

    618

    相传神为莆邑湄州东螺村林氏女，自童时已具神异，常于梦中飞越海上，救人于溺；至长不嫁，没后屡昭灵显，人为立庙祀之。自前代已加封号。康熙二十三年六月，王师攻克澎湖，靖海侯施烺屯兵天妃澳，入庙拜谒，见神衣半身沾湿，自对敌时，恍见神兵导引，始悟战胜，实邀神助。又澳中水泉，仅供居民数百人饮，是日驻师数万，方以无水为忧，而甘泉沸涌，汲之不竭。表上其异，奉诏加封“天后”。”至今湄州林氏宗族妇人将赴田者，辄以其儿置庙中，曰：“姑好看儿。”遂去，去常终日，儿不啼不饥，亦不出阈；至暮妇归，各认己子携去。神犹亲其宗人之子云。

    Explanation

    618

    It is said that the deity was a woman from the Lin family of Dongluo Village, Meizhou, Putian. From her childhood, she possessed supernatural abilities, often flying over the sea in her dreams to rescue those who were drowning. She remained unmarried until her death, and after her death, she repeatedly showed signs of supernatural power. People built a temple to worship her, and from previous dynasties, she had been given titles. In June 1784, the imperial army conquered Penghu, and General Shi Lang, Marquis of Pacifying the Sea, stationed his troops at Tianfei Bay. He entered the temple to pay respects and saw the deity's clothes half-wet. When facing the enemy, he suddenly saw divine soldiers leading the way, and he then realized that the victory was actually due to divine assistance. In addition, the water source in the bay could only supply hundreds of residents. On that day, with tens of thousands of troops stationed there, they were worried about the lack of water, but sweet spring water gushed forth, and it could be drawn without running out. He reported this miracle, and by imperial decree, she was posthumously titled "Tianhou." To this day, women from the Lin family of Meizhou who are going to the fields would leave their children in the temple, saying, "Please take care of my child," and then leave. They would often leave for the whole day, and the children would neither cry nor be hungry, nor leave the threshold. By evening, the mothers would return, each recognizing and taking their own child. The deity still favored the children of her clan.

    619

    木龙
    　　
    　　凡海舶中，必有一蛇，名曰“木龙”，自船成日即有之。平时会不可见，亦不知所处。若有木龙去，则舟必败。
    　　
    　　水仙王
    　　
    　　水仙王者，洋中之神，莫详姓氏，或曰帝禹、伍相、三闾大夫，不一其说。帝禹平成水土，功在万世；伍相浮鸱夷；屈子怀石自沉，宜为水神，神爽不泯。划水仙者，洋中危急，不得近岸之所为也。海舶在大洋中，不啻太虚一尘，渺无涯际，惟藉樯舵坚实，绳碇完固，庶几乘波御风，乃有依赖。每遇飓风忽至，骇浪如山，柁折樯倾，绳断底裂，技力不得施，智巧无所用，斯时惟有叩天求神，角崩稽首，以祈默宥而已。爰有水仙拯救之异。余于台郡遣二舶赴鸡笼淡水，大风折舵，舶腹中裂。王君云森居舟中，自分必死，舟师告曰：“惟有划水仙可免。

    Explanation

    619

    Wood Dragon**

    In every ship, there must be a snake, known as the "Wood Dragon," which appears on the day the ship is completed. It is usually invisible and its location is unknown. If the Wood Dragon leaves the ship, the ship will inevitably fail.

    Water God

    The Water God is a deity of the ocean, whose name and lineage are not well-known. Some say he is Emperor Yu, Wu Zixu, or Qu Yuan, each with their own legend. Emperor Yu, known for controlling floods and establishing the foundation for future generations, is a fitting water deity. Wu Zixu, who was drowned in a bag after being exiled, and Qu Yuan, who drowned himself in the Miluo River, are also considered water deities due to their immortal spirits. The ritual of invoking the Water God is performed in dire situations at sea, especially when one cannot reach the shore. A ship at sea is as insignificant as a speck of dust in the vastness of the sky, with no visible boundaries. Only with a sturdy mast, rudder, ropes, and anchors can the ship navigate the waves and winds effectively. When a typhoon suddenly strikes, the waves can be as high as mountains, breaking the rudder, mast, and ropes, and splitting the hull. Even skill and intelligence are useless in such extreme conditions. At such times, the only recourse is to pray to the heavens and gods, bowing and pleading for mercy. There have been instances where the Water God has miraculously intervened. Once, when I sent two ships from Taiwan to Keelung for fresh water, a strong wind broke the rudder and split the hull of one of the ships. Wang Yunsen, who was on board, believed he would die. The ship's master told him, "Only by invoking the Water God can you be saved."

    620

    ”遂披发与舟人共蹲舷间，以空手作拨棹势，而众口假为钲鼓声，如五日竞渡状，顷刻抵岸，众喜幸生，水仙之力也。余初不信，曰：“偶然耳，岂有徒手虚棹，而能抗海浪、逆飓风者乎？”顾君敷公曰：“有是哉！曩居台湾仕伪郑，从澎湖归，中流舟裂，业已半沉，众谋共划水仙，舟复浮出，直入鹿耳门，有红毛覆舟在焉，竟庋舟底。久之，有小舟来救，众已获拯，此舟乃沉，抑若有人暗中持之者，宁非鬼神之力乎？”迨八月初六日，有陈君一舶自省中来，半渡遭风，舟底已裂，水入艎中，首欲俯，而柁又中折，辗转巨浪中，死亡之势，不可顷刻待。有言划水仙者，徒手一拨，沉者忽浮，破浪穿风，疾飞如矢，顷刻抵南莰之白沙墩，众皆登岸，得饭一盂，稽颡沙岸，神未尝不歆也。陈君谓当时虽十帆并张，不足喻其疾。鬼神之灵，亦奇已哉！

    Explanation

    620

    Thus, with loose hair, the speaker and the boatmen sat on the side of the ship, using empty hands to simulate rowing, while the crowd imitated the sound of drums and gongs, as if it were the Dragon Boat Festival. The miraculous rescue was attributed to the power of the water deity. The speaker initially did not believe it, saying, "It was just a coincidence. How could one row with empty hands and resist the waves and hurricane?" Looking at Mr. Fu Gong, he said, "There is such a thing! In the past, while serving under the regime of Zheng Chenggong in Taiwan, returning from Penghu, the ship broke apart and was already half-sunk. The crowd collectively invoked the water deity, and the ship resurfaced, entering the harbor of Lukou. There was a Dutch shipwreck there, which was placed at the bottom of the boat. After some time, a small boat came for rescue, and the crowd was saved, but the ship sank. It seems as if someone was holding it from behind, is it not the power of the water deity?" On August 6th, Mr. Chen arrived on a ship from the mainland. Halfway through the journey, the ship encountered strong winds, and the bottom was broken, with water entering the ship and the bow starting to sink, and the rudder breaking in half. Tossed by huge waves, the ship faced imminent danger. They invoked the water deity, and the ship suddenly rose, cutting through waves and wind, flying like an arrow, and quickly reached the shore of Nankan's White Sand Pier. The survivors all landed, received a bowl of food, and bowed in gratitude on the shore, indicating that the deity was pleased. Mr. Chen said that even with ten sails fully spread, it would not adequately describe the speed. The miraculous power of the water deity is indeed extraordinary.

    621

    糠洋蕈洋
    　　
    　　糠洋，水面积糠粃半尺，香。蕈洋，水面有物，形如蕈，亦积半尺许。皆水沫所成。风涛鼓荡，不淆不徙。自浙中往日本者必过之。
    　　
    　　大昆仑
    　　
    　　林道乾，明季海寇。啸聚在郑芝龙、刘香老前。图据闽粤，不遂；又遍历琉球、吕宋、暹罗、东京、交阯诸国，无隙可乘，因过大昆仑，山名，在东京正南三千里，与暹罗海港相近。见其风景特异，欲留居之。其山最高且广，四面平壤沃土，五谷俱备，不种自生；中国果木无不有，百卉烂漫，四时皆春。但苦空山无人。道乾率舟师登山结茅，自谓海外扶余，足以据土立国。奈龙出无时，风雨倏至，屋宇人民多为摄去，海舟又倾荡不可泊。意其下必蛟龙窟，宅不可居，始弃去。复之大年，国名，在暹罗西南。攻得之，今大年王是其裔也。台湾有老人经随道乾至大昆仑者，尚得详言之。前郑成功以台湾小隘，有卜居大昆仑之志，咨访水程、风景甚悉，会病亡，不果行。

    Explanation

    621

    Kangyang and Junyang

    　　Kangyang refers to water covered with a layer of chaff half a foot thick, which emits a fragrance. Junyang refers to objects on the water surface that resemble mushrooms, also about half a foot thick, formed by water foam. Both phenomena are created by water foam. Despite the wind and waves, these layers remain unmixed and do not move from their place. Travelers from Zhejiang to Japan must pass through these areas.

    　　Great Kunlun

    　　Lin Daoqian was a pirate leader during the late Ming dynasty. He gathered followers before Zheng Zhilong and Liu Xianglao. He attempted to establish control over Fujian and Guangdong but failed. He then traveled extensively through Ryukyu, Luzon, Siam, Tokyo, and Jiaozhi, finding no opportunity, and thus passed through the Great Kunlun, a mountain located 3,000 li south of Tokyo, near the port of Siam. Seeing its unique scenery, he desired to stay there. The mountain is very high and wide, with fertile plains all around, where crops grow without planting. All Chinese fruits and trees are present, flowers bloom profusely, and it is spring all year round. However, the mountain is empty and lacks people. Lin Daoqian led his fleet to build shelters on the mountain, considering it a suitable place to establish a country. But dragons appeared unpredictably, and storms suddenly arrived, causing many houses and people to be taken away. Sea vessels also capsized and could not dock. He suspected that the area beneath the mountain was likely a lair of dragons, making it unsuitable for habitation, and thus abandoned it. He then returned to Danyan, a place located southwest of Siam. He conquered this place, and the current king of Danyan is his descendant. An elderly person from Taiwan who accompanied Lin Daoqian to Danyan can still provide detailed accounts. Before Zheng Chenggong died, he had planned to settle in Danyan but did not carry out the plan due to illness.

    622

    琉球
    　　
    　　琉球国，在闽省正东。去中国最近，然最小弱，又最贫。商舶从无贸易琉球者，以其贫且陋也。其王于诸国悉朝贡，为通货贸易计。诸国鄙其贫弱，不萌侵夺之念，彼反得以贫弱自安。其于中国，率三岁一贡，所贡硫黄、皮纸而已。其所携财货，惟螺与蚌壳。螺可为栗吹，即城头晓角是；蚌壳断之，可以镶带。外此，则有纸扇、烟筒，其制陋劣，傭儿所不顾。忆吾乡俗语，谓厌憎之物，辄曰“琉球货”，陋劣不自今日始，古语已云然矣。

    Explanation

    622

    The Ryukyu Kingdom is located directly east of Fujian Province. It is the closest to China, yet it is the smallest and weakest, as well as the poorest. There has never been trade with Ryukyu by merchant ships, because it is poor and backward. Its king pays tribute to all the countries for the sake of currency and trade. All the countries look down on its poverty and weakness, and do not harbor thoughts of invasion or plunder. It反而 can find security in its poverty and weakness. Regarding China, it regularly pays tribute every three years, and the tribute consists of sulfur and bark paper alone. The goods it carries are only conch shells and clam shells. Conch shells can be used for making wind instruments, that is, the morning trumpet on the city wall; clam shells, when cut, can be used to decorate belts. Besides these, there are paper fans and smoking pipes, whose craftsmanship is crude and inferior, even hired hands would not bother with them. A saying in my hometown refers to something disliked or hated as "Ryukyu goods," and crude and inferior is not a new phenomenon; ancient sayings have already said so.

    623

    里乘子曰：唐末五代时，各国纷争，惟高氏窃据一隅，所向称臣，人呼为高赖子，获利甚溥。琉球朝贡诸国，殆亦袭其故智欤！孟子谓：“仁者乐天，以大事小；智者畏天，以小事大。乐天者保天下，畏天者保其国。”彼琉球者，亦可谓善于保国者矣。
    　　
    　　日本
    　　
    　　日本，即古倭夷。于海外为莫强之国，恃强不通朝贡，且目中华为小邦；彼则坐受诸国朝贡，夜郎自大，由来久矣。其国事一听将军主之，国君如赘疣，垂拱而已。故其国中搆兵，惟将军是争。会无有放弑其主者，以国柄非所操篡，弑无益，虚被恶名，用是得长守其国。余谓琉球贫弱，日本不闻国政，其开创之主，殆深得黄老之学者乎？又治尚酷刑，小过辄死。死有三等；其一灌水，水满腹，则遍挞其身，令水散入肢体，又灌之，如此者三，如枵然大瓠，膨胀而死。其二悬肠，割人肛系巨竹梢，一纵而竹梢上腾，肢体倒悬，大肠尽出。

    Explanation

    623

    Li Chengzi said: At the end of the Tang Dynasty and during the Five Dynasties period, various states were in turmoil, but the Gao family seized a corner of the land, and all who faced them paid homage as vassals. People called them "Gao Lai Zi," and they gained great profit. The Ryukyu Kingdom, which paid tribute to many countries, likely followed their old strategies! Mencius said: "The benevolent delight in the Way of Heaven, using the great to control the small; the wise fear the Way of Heaven, using the small to serve the great. Those who delight in the Way of Heaven preserve the world, while those who fear the Way of Heaven preserve their state." The Ryukyu Kingdom can also be said to be skilled in preserving its state.

    Japan

    Japan, that is, the ancient Wō Yí. It was the strongest country overseas, relying on its strength and not paying tribute, and regarding China as a small state; it sat and received tributes from various countries, being arrogant like the Kingdom of Yelang, a practice that has lasted for a long time. The affairs of the country were entirely in the hands of the general, and the emperor was like an unsightly growth, merely sitting and doing nothing. Therefore, within the country, wars were fought only over the general. There were no instances of the general overthrowing or killing the ruler, as the power of the state was not something to be seized, and killing would be to no avail, only bringing a bad name. Thus, they were able to long preserve their country. I believe that the Ryukyu Kingdom, being poor and weak, and Japan, which does not hear of state affairs, had their founders perhaps deeply understand the Huang-Lao school of thought? Furthermore, their rule was characterized by harsh punishments, with minor offenses leading to death. There were three types of death: the first was water pouring, where water was filled into the abdomen, and then the body was beaten all over, causing the water to spread into the limbs, and then more water was poured, repeated three times, like a hollow gourd, swelling and dying. The second was hanging intestines, where the anus was cut and tied to the tip of a large bamboo pole, and when the pole was released, it rose, causing the body to hang upside down, and the large intestine to be completely exposed.

    624

    甚者为活烧，以罪人锁系杙上，围绕干柴，四面举火，其人辗转良久而死。又进于此为倒悬，殊不即死，三数日后，头胀如斗，五脏从口中出。皆非刑，较地狱罗鬼之惨尤甚。故其民皆畏法，有道不拾遗风。其先，大西洋人觊觎其国，以天主教之言惑之，事露，悉被夷戮。今商舶至彼，必问有无天主教人，又铸天主像，令人足践而登。若误携一人往，则以其船牵置岸上，尽纳舟人于艎底焚之，自此西洋人无复敢至日本者。其与诸国通贸易处曰“长畸岛”，男女肉色最白。中国人至彼，暴露风日中，犹能转黑为白，虽使桓宣武、黑王相公往，亦当改观。妇人妍美，白皙如玉人，中国人多流连不归者。今长畸有大唐街，皆中国人所居也。

    Explanation

    624

    The most severe form of punishment is to burn a person alive. The condemned individual is bound to a stake, surrounded by dry wood, and set on fire from all sides, causing them to suffer for a long time before dying. Another method involves hanging the person upside down, which does not result in immediate death; instead, after several days, the head swells to the size of a winnowing basket, and the internal organs burst out of the mouth. These methods are not formal punishments but are even more cruel than the torments of hell. Therefore, the people of the country fear the law and adhere to the custom of not picking up lost items. In the past, Westerners coveted the country and used the words of Christianity to deceive the people. When this was discovered, they were all brutally killed. Now, when merchant ships arrive, they must ask if there are any Christians on board. They also cast images of the Christian deity and force people to step on them and climb over them. If even one person is mistakenly brought there, the entire ship is dragged to the shore, and all the passengers are burned at the bottom of the ship. Since then, no Westerners dare to come to Japan. The place where the country trades with other nations is called "Nagasaki," where the skin of men and women is very white. Chinese people who go there, even when exposed to wind and sun, can still turn black into white, and even if General Huán Xuānwǔ or Black Wang Xianggong went there, they would also change their appearance. The women are beautiful, fair-skinned like jade, and many Chinese people linger and do not return. Now, in Nagasaki, there is a street called "Da Tang Street," where all Chinese people live.

    625

    里乘子曰：相传秦始皇使徐市浮海求仙，徐至日本，即自立国，以所携童男女三千人相为妃匹，滋生蕃衍，今其国王，仍徐苗裔。当秦焚书，惟徐所携至日本者独能幸免，故《十三经》完书皆在日本。向其国厉禁，不许将书盗卖出国；近闻其国已惑入天主教，遂目《十三经》为妖书，下令尽付诸火，凡臣民等，如敢私藏不火者，杀毋赦。丧心病狂，何其烈也！夫中华之所以为大国者，自有天地以来，所行皆五帝、三王、周公、孔子之道，天下不变，道亦不变，此中华之所以为中，所以为大也。乃日本夜郎自大，竟敢目中华为小邦，井蛙不知有天，殊属可笑。况《十三经》为五帝、三王、周公、孔子载道之书，自遭秦燔，远遗外域，已为不幸，果使彼国之人能始终宝重护惜，犹不幸中之幸；兹忽天夺其魄，尤效祖龙，绝灭遗编，务趋邪说，良可浩叹！然吾谓此或传闻之过，彼国崇正黜邪，素严号令，何忍，一旦改辄耶？

    Explanation

    625

    The Master Li Cheng says: It is said that Emperor Qin Shi Huang sent Xu Fu to sail the sea in search of immortality. Xu arrived in Japan, where he founded a state, taking with him 3,000 children to serve as spouses, leading to the multiplication and flourishing of the population. Today, the king of Japan is still a descendant of Xu. During the Qin dynasty's book-burning, only the texts Xu brought to Japan were able to escape destruction, so the complete Thirteen Classics are found in Japan. Previously, the country had strict prohibitions against exporting books; however, it is recently reported that the country has been influenced by Catholicism, and the Thirteen Classics have been labeled as heretical texts, ordered to be completely burned. Any subjects who dare to secretly keep them without burning them will be killed without mercy. How utterly insane is this! The reason China is considered a great nation is that since the beginning of time, it has followed the ways of the Five Emperors, Three Sovereigns, Duke of Zhou, and Confucius. The world has not changed, nor has the Way. This is why China is the center and the greatest. Yet, Japan, like the Kingdom of Yelang, is arrogant, daring to regard China as a small state, a frog in a well unaware of the sky, which is truly laughable. Moreover, the Thirteen Classics are the texts that carry the Way of the Five Emperors, Three Sovereigns, Duke of Zhou, and Confucius. Since they were burned during the Qin dynasty and sent far abroad, it was already unfortunate. If the people of that country could have always cherished and protected them, it would have been a lesser misfortune. Now, however, it seems that fate has taken away their sense, following the example of the First Emperor, destroying the remaining texts and promoting heretical teachings, which is truly a cause for deep sighs! Nevertheless, I believe this may be a mistake in hearsay. The country has always upheld the right and rejected the wrong, strictly enforcing its commands. How could it suddenly change its course?

    626

    红夷
    　　
    　　红毛，即荷兰国，又曰红夷，一名波斯胡，亦在西海外，实大西洋附庸也。性贪狡，能识宝气，善货殖，重利轻生，贸易无远不至。其船最大，用板两层，斫而不削，制极坚厚；中国人目为夹板船，其实圆木为之，非板也。又多巧思，为帆如蛛网盘旋，八面受风，无往不顺。较之中国帆樯，不遇顺风，则左右戗折，戗读锵，去声。因逆风从对面来，故作斜行，左右拗折，以趁风力之谓也。欹侧倾险，迂回不前之艰，不翅天垠。其在大洋中，恃船大帆巧，常行劫盗。

    Explanation

    626

    Red Barbarians

    Red-haired people, that is, the Dutch, also known as Red Barbarians, or alternatively called Persian merchants, are also located on the western coast, being vassals of the Great Western Ocean. They are greedy and cunning, able to recognize the aura of treasure, skilled in trade and commerce, valuing profit over life, and traveling far and wide in trade. Their ships are the largest, using two layers of boards, cut but not shaved, making them extremely sturdy and thick; the Chinese call them double-board ships, but in reality, they are made of circular timbers, not flat planks. They are also ingenious, with sails resembling a spider web spiraling around, receiving wind from all eight directions, and always moving smoothly. Compared to Chinese sails, if they do not encounter favorable winds, they adjust left and right, the character "戗" (qiāng) is pronounced with a fourth tone. Because the wind comes from the opposite direction, they move diagonally, twisting left and right, in order to catch the wind. Their ships are prone to leaning and dangerous, facing difficulties of going around and not moving forward, not unlike the edge of the sky. In the vast ocean, they rely on their large ships and clever sails, often engaging in piracy.

    627

    使数人坐樯巅，架千里镜四面审视，商舶虽在百里外，望见，即转柁逐之，无得脱者。常至日本贸易，日本倭知其为盗，必使中国商舶先归，计程已远，然后遣之。余友顾君敷公，能悉其理。谓彼帆虽巧，然巧于逆风，反拙于乘顺。凡物之巧者，不能兼擅，理固然也。若与中国舟航并驰顺风中，彼反后矣。故遇红毛追袭，即当转柁，随风顺行，可以脱祸；若仍行戗风，鲜不败者。况彼船大如山，小舟既畏其压，与战又仰攻不便，安能与抗？彼既恃所长，于诸国舟航，一切易视。常屡侵交阯，交阯人创为小舟，名曰“轧”，音空。

    Explanation

    627

    Have several people sit at the top of the mast, set up telescopes to examine all sides. Merchant ships, even if they are a hundred miles away, can be seen, and immediately turn the rudder to chase them, none can escape. Often go to Japan for trade, the Japanese know they are pirates, so they make Chinese merchant ships return first, calculate the distance far, and then send them. My friend Gu Junfu can fully understand the principle. He says their sails are skillful, but skillful in going against the wind, and clumsy in going with the wind. All things that are skillful cannot excel in everything, it is a natural law. If they race with Chinese ships in a tailwind, they will fall behind. Therefore, when encountering red-haired people (Dutch) chasing, one should immediately turn the rudder, follow the wind, and proceed, which can avoid disaster. If one still sails against the wind, few will not fail. Moreover, their ships are as big as mountains, small boats fear being crushed, and attacking from below is inconvenient, how can one resist? They rely on their strengths and regard the ships of other countries as easy prey. They often invade Giao Chui (Vietnam), the people of Giao Chui created small boats, called 'zhua,' pronounced as 'kong.'

    628

    船长仅三丈，舷出水面一尺，两头尖锐，仿佛端阳竞渡龙舟，以二十四人操檝，飞行水面；欲退则反其棹，变尾为首，进退惟意，俨然游龙。船中首尾各架红衣巨炮，附水施放，攻其船底，底破即沉，虽有技巧，无所施设，于是大败。至今红毛船过广南，见轧船出，即胆落而去。中国东南半壁皆大海，不无侵扰之虞，轧船之制，亦所宜讲。往时，郑成功取台湾，与红毛陆战。

    Explanation

    628

    The ship is only three zhang long, with its sides protruding one chi above the water. It is sharply pointed at both ends, resembling a dragon boat used in the Dragon Boat Festival races. With twenty-four men rowing, it flies across the water. To retreat, they simply reverse the oars, turning the stern into the bow, and move forward or backward at will, like a dragon gliding through the waves. At the bow and stern of the ship are mounted large cannons painted red, which can be fired underwater to attack the bottom of enemy ships. If the bottom is breached, the ship sinks immediately, rendering even the most skilled tactics useless, and thus they suffer a crushing defeat. To this day, when European ships pass through Guangnan and see these ships emerging, they lose their courage and flee. The southeastern half of China is all ocean, and there is always the danger of invasion. The design of these ships is also something that should be discussed. In the past, Zheng Chenggong captured Taiwan in a land battle against the Europeans.

    629

    彼所长惟火器，机发即燃，不劳点焠，尺寸小物，力侔巨炮，外此，则攻战之理皆谬；又足蹑高低，不便疾行，多被杀伤，卧不能起，将卒前取其首，辄为鸟炮所中，皆不敢近。复视其尸，盖两胫间皆缚小炮，以膝对人，其炮自发，犹以伤残之躯计毁人命，可谓至死不僵者矣。又凡所居处，下必藏火药，事急辄发其机，屋与人皆为飞灰，志不受戮辱。舟底亦然，急则自毁，帆樯之巧，终不示人，故诸国罕闻效其制者。有红毛船，遭风误过半线洋，遇浅船胶，彼知无复去之理，乃以布帆围蔽其舟，即于舟中另造小船，三日而成。郑氏视为釜鱼，方集战舰攻之，彼悉登小船，扬帆而去。良久，机发，大船自毁，人服其机警。又舟中百物俱备，造作小船需用物料，不假外求，叹不可及。

    Explanation

    629

    Their strength lies solely in firearms, which ignite automatically when the mechanism is triggered, requiring no manual ignition. A small object, yet its power equals that of a large cannon. Beyond this, all principles of attack and defense are flawed. Their feet are not suited for quick movement, making them vulnerable to injury and unable to rise. When soldiers try to take their heads, they are hit by birdshot, and none dare approach. Upon examining their bodies, one finds small cannons bound between their shins, aimed at people, which fire automatically. Even in their wounded state, they seek to destroy human life, truly being those who do not lie still even in death. In all their dwellings, below there must be hidden gunpowder. In times of urgency, they trigger the mechanism, and both house and people turn to ashes, ensuring they do not suffer humiliation. The bottom of their ships is the same, in urgency, they self-destruct. The ingenuity of their sails and masts is never shown to others, so few countries have heard of or imitated their methods. There was a red-haired ship that encountered a storm and mistakenly passed the halfway line of the sea, encountering a shallow ship that stuck. Knowing there was no way to escape, they surrounded their ship with cloth sails and built a new small boat inside, completing it in three days. The Zheng family viewed them as fish in a pot, gathering warships to attack them. They all boarded the small boat and sailed away. After a while, the mechanism triggered, and the large ship self-destructed, and people admired their quick thinking. Everything needed for building a small boat was available on board, without needing to seek external supplies, and one cannot help but admire their ingenuity.

    630

    里乘子曰：予曩游镇海，见海舶之大者，其高如山，若以轧船环而攻之，彼虽高大，必不能逞。方今寰海镜清，梯航靖谧，固无论矣；然未雨绸缪，《诗》有明训。鄙意海疆各要隘，不妨多制轧船，无事以之巡哨，有事即以之制敌，则灵利安稳，较之寻常炮船，似有过之而无不及也。

    Explanation

    630

    里乘子说：我过去曾游历镇海，见到那些巨大的海船，其高度如同山峰，如果用轧船环绕攻击它们，即使它们高大，也必定无法逞凶。如今整个海域清澈平静，远方的船只也安宁祥和，自然无需多虑；然而未雨绸缪，《诗经》中有明确的教导。我认为在沿海各重要关隘，不妨多制造一些轧船，在和平时期用于巡逻，在战时则用来对抗敌人，这样既灵活又安全，相比普通的炮船，似乎有过之而无不及。

    631

    西洋国
    　　
    　　西洋国在西海外，去中国极远。其人拗目隆准，状类红毛，然最多心计，又具坚忍之志。析理务极精微，推测象纬，历数不迨。器用小物，莫不尽其奇奥，用心之深，将夺造化之秘，欲后天地而不朽。苟有所为，则静坐默想，父死不遂，子孙继之，一世不成，十世为之。既穷其妙，必使国人共习而守之。务为人所难为。其先世多有慧人，入中国窃得六书之学。又有利马窦者，能过目成诵，终身不忘。明季来中国，三年，遍交海内文士，于中国书无所不读；多市典籍归，教其国人悉通文义。创为《七克》等书，所言虽孝悌慈让，其实似是而非；又杂载彼国事实，以济其天主教之邪说，诱人入其教中。

    Explanation

    631

    The Western Country

    The Western Country lies beyond the Western Ocean, far from China. Its people have prominent noses and a distinct appearance, resembling Europeans, yet they are highly strategic and possess a determined and resilient will. They analyze principles to the utmost subtlety, speculate on celestial phenomena, and their astronomy is not inferior. They excel in small tools and devices, revealing their profound mysteries and deep understanding, aiming to uncover the secrets of nature and outlast the heavens and earth. If they set their mind to something, they sit quietly and meditate, and even if a father does not succeed, his descendants will continue the endeavor, persisting for generations until it is accomplished. Once they have mastered the intricacies, they ensure their people learn and uphold these knowledge. They strive to do what others find difficult. Their ancestors were often wise individuals who came to China and secretly learned the principles of Chinese writing. There was also Matteo Ricci, who could read and recite texts upon first glance and never forgot them. During the late Ming dynasty, he came to China, spent three years, and befriended scholars across the country, reading all Chinese books. He purchased many classics and taught his compatriots to understand their meanings. He authored works such as The Seven Victories, which, though they discuss filial piety, fraternal respect, kindness, and yielding, are actually misleading. He also included accounts of their country's facts to support the heretical teachings of Christianity, enticing people to join their faith.

    632

    中国人士被惑，多皈其教者。凡各省郡、县、卫、所，皆有天主堂，扃闭甚密，外人曾不得窥见所有。不耕不织，所用自饶，皆以诱人入教为务，谓之“化人”。彼国多产白金。自明时已窃处粤之香山澳，虽纳贡赋，而丑类实据我边陲矣。又岁运白金巨万至香山澳，转送各省郡邑天主堂，资其所用。京师天主堂，屋宇宏丽，垣墙周复。又制为风琴、自鸣钟、刻漏、浑天仪诸器，皆神镂鬼斧，巧夺天工，为费不可量。穷年积岁，制造不辍，不藉中国一钱。余谓红毛密迩西洋，自是同类，英圭黎、咬留巴皆西海小国，宜为并兼，不足深怪。独怪吕宋在东海外，远过中国万里，亦为所踞，此其心宁有厌足乎！闽人多有逐利吕宋者，谓红毛政令，一如西洋之法。

    Explanation

    632

    Many Chinese people were influenced and converted to the Catholic religion. In every province, prefecture, county, garrison, and station, there were Catholic churches that were very tightly sealed, and outsiders could never see what was inside. The members of the religion did not engage in farming or weaving but had everything they needed, and their main purpose was to attract people to join the religion, which was called "converting people." The country (likely referring to Portugal or another European nation) produced a lot of silver. Since the Ming dynasty, they had secretly established themselves in the Macau area of Guangdong, although they paid tribute, they were actually occupying our border regions. They annually transported millions of taels of silver to Macau, which was then sent to Catholic churches in various provinces, counties, and towns to support their activities. The Catholic church in the capital was grand and surrounded by walls. They also made various instruments such as organs, self-striking clocks, water clocks, and celestial globes, all of which were ingeniously crafted, surpassing human skill, and the cost was immeasurable. They continued to manufacture these items year after year without interruption, without relying on a single coin from China. I believe the Portuguese are close to the Western countries and are of the same kind, and countries like England, Guinea, and Brazil are small Western nations, so it is not surprising that they are annexed. It is strange that Luzon (the Philippines) is far away from China, more than ten thousand miles east, and is also occupied by them, does their ambition know no bounds? Many people from Fujian went to Luzon for profit, and they said that the Portuguese government's laws were the same as those of the Western countries.

    633

    分吕宋地为二十四郡，有西洋化人共操其柄。禁民不得昼作，必使昼寝夜兴。又寝室不容闭门，夫妇共寝榻上，逻者时时绕榻前侦视。有女及笄，父母不得主婚配，必侯巴黎按选。[称僧曰巴黎。]其稍有姿者，率为巴黎所留，色衰放归，始令择配。父母死，人子不得殓埋，巴黎假度亡之说，舁置万人坑中；积久坑溢，扬灰弃之。民人有资，岁与中分，四岁之后，十不存一矣。其禁昼作者，防民之叛也。岁中分其资者，务贫其民，使不得为所欲为也。死者不令瘗埋，恐由川毓灵，欲无复生英杰与争国也。用意设想，皆在人情之外。

    Explanation

    633

    Divide Luzon into twenty-four prefectures, with Westernized individuals holding power together. Prohibit the people from working during the day, forcing them to sleep during the day and work at night. Furthermore, bedrooms cannot be closed, and couples must share a bed, with guards constantly circling around the bed for surveillance. When girls reach adulthood, their parents cannot arrange their marriages; they must wait for Paris to select them. [Paris is referred to as a monk.] Those who are slightly attractive are kept by Paris until they lose their beauty, after which they are released to choose a partner. When parents die, their children cannot bury them; Paris pretends to use the excuse of "death" to place the bodies in mass graves; over time, the graves overflow, and the bodies are cremated and discarded. The people's resources are divided equally each year, and after four years, only one in ten remains. The prohibition on daytime work is to prevent rebellion. The annual division of resources is to impoverish the people, preventing them from doing as they please. The dead are not allowed to be buried, fearing that they might give rise to new leaders, and the intention is to prevent the emergence of new heroes who might challenge the ruling power. All these measures are beyond normal human considerations.

    634

    夫秦皇焚书坑儒，禁偶语、徙豪杰以弱天下之民，欲令子孙自一世、二世以至万世，长有天下，虽同此心，未至若是酷。假令此辈得志，其祸尚忍言哉！人情莫不恋故乡而安本俗，若弃父母、捐家室，而反为利己者，已非人情矣！计中国郡、邑、卫、所，天主堂何止二千馀百区，而居堂中丑类不下三四万人，皆捐其父母妻子，远来必有所为矣！为名乎？为利乎？为游中华之名山大川、观中华之礼乐政教乎？其国君岁驱其民于中国，又岁捐金钱巨万资给之，曾无厌倦，果为朝会纳贡来乎？抑岁岁饥馑，移民以就食于中国乎？既无一于此，殆复何求？其有大欲存焉，不待智者然后知也！而堂堂天朝，曾无一人能破其奸，已为丑类齿冷。脱有不信余言者，试问日本何以禁绝丑类，不令蹈其境乎？今而后，尚有从其教而褒扬之者，请以巴黎选女之事告之。

    Explanation

    634

    The Qin Emperor burned books and buried Confucian scholars alive, prohibited private conversations, and relocated the powerful to weaken the people, intending for his descendants to rule for generations, even to the ten-thousandth generation. Though he shared this ambition, he did not reach such a level of cruelty. If this kind of person had succeeded, could one bear to speak of the disaster? Human nature is to love one's hometown and be content with one's native customs; if one abandons parents and family, and instead seeks personal gain, it has already gone against human nature! Considering the prefectures, counties, garrisons, and guard posts in China, how many Catholic churches are there, not less than two thousand, and the foreigners residing in these churches number no fewer than thirty to forty thousand, all of whom have abandoned their parents and wives, coming far from their homes—surely they must have some purpose! Is it for fame? For profit? To travel the famous mountains and rivers of China, to observe its rituals, music, governance, and education? Their king drives his people to China year after year, and annually donates millions of dollars to support them, yet he shows no signs of fatigue. Did they come to pay tribute and attend court? Or did they come year after year due to famine, to seek food in China? If they have none of these intentions, what else could they be seeking? They must have a great desire, and it does not require a wise person to know this! Yet the great Celestial Empire has not one person who can expose their deceit, and this has already made the foreigners scornful. If there are those who do not believe my words, ask why Japan bans foreigners from entering its territory. From now on, there should be no one who follows their teachings and praises them; I will tell you about the incident of selecting women in Paris.

    635

    里乘子曰：昔客宁波，有客告予言，凡入天主教者，至天主堂拜其酋长，给银三十两，比授红丸，趣吞之，自后，心日昏迷，久之，遂视祖孙父子皆为平等，除天主外，凡天地神祗祖先，一概不祀；天理人道，绝灭殆尽。有诸生某甲，素恶此教，以贫难自存，欲骗其银，又虑其丸作祟，商之妻，使预浓煎下药，以待既拜其酋长，吞丸携银归，急索药服之，果将丸泄出。爰取刀斧劈之，不能开，怒甚，投诸炉火，亦竟不灰。方惊诧间，忽闻剥喙声，启户视之，乃其酋长使人来，责甲悖教，并索取银丸，词色甚厉。甲强与辨，其人笑曰：“君休饶舌，顷丸神已归，诉汝投诸炉火，可速取还，免罹后患也。”甲无如何，遂并归之。初，甲颇能文，自后忽忽若失，文思顿踬矣。又言凡入天主教者，其家人有病，必报知酋长为之医治。

    Explanation

    635

    里乘子曰：昔客宁波，有客告予言，凡入天主教者，至天主堂拜其酋长，给银三十两，比授红丸，趣吞之，自后，心日昏迷，久之，遂视祖孙父子皆为平等，除天主外，凡天地神祗祖先，一概不祀；天理人道，绝灭殆尽。有诸生某甲，素恶此教，以贫难自存，欲骗其银，又虑其丸作祟，商之妻，使预浓煎下药，以待既拜其酋长，吞丸携银归，急索药服之，果将丸泄出。爰取刀斧劈之，不能开，怒甚，投诸炉火，亦竟不灰。方惊诧间，忽闻剥喙声，启户视之，乃其酋长使人来，责甲悖教，并索取银丸，词色甚厉。甲强与辨，其人笑曰：“君休饶舌，顷丸神已归，诉汝投诸炉火，可速取还，免罹后患也。”甲无如何，遂并归之。初，甲颇能文，自后忽忽若失，文思顿踬矣。又言凡入天主教者，其家人有病，必报知酋长为之医治。

    Li Chengzi said: Once a guest from Ningbo told me that anyone who joins the Catholic Church must go to the church to worship its chief, pay thirty taels of silver, and quickly swallow a red pill. Afterward, their heart gradually becomes confused, and over time, they begin to see ancestors, grandchildren, and parents as equals, worshipping only the Lord. They no longer honor any other deities, gods, or ancestors. The principles of heaven and human conduct are nearly extinguished. There was a scholar named A, who always disliked this religion. Due to poverty, he struggled to survive and wanted to trick the money. However, he feared the pill would cause harm, so he discussed it with his wife, who prepared a strong decoction to counteract the pill. After worshipping the chief, swallowing the pill, and taking the money home, he quickly took the medicine, and the pill was indeed expelled. He then took an axe to chop it, but could not break it open. Angered, he threw it into the fire, but it did not burn. Just as he was surprised, he suddenly heard a pecking sound. He opened the door and saw that it was a messenger from the chief, who accused A of violating the teachings and demanded the money and the pill back. His words and demeanor were very severe. A tried to argue back, but the messenger laughed and said, "Stop talking. The spirit of the pill has returned and has reported you for throwing it into the fire. Quickly retrieve it, or else you will suffer consequences." A had no choice but to return both the money and the pill. Originally, A was quite skilled in writing, but afterward, he became distracted and lost his ability to write. It is also said that anyone who joins the Catholic Church must inform the chief if a family member is ill, and the chief will treat them.

    636

    如不能起，当濒危时，酋长先入其房，闭门密幕窗棂，为之诵咒，度其生天，俟气绝乃开门，令所亲入房举哀殡殓。有某一家，素奉天主教甚虔，其母病革，酋长如例为之闭门诵咒。有戚某，不信其说，以房故有楼，悄伏其上，钻隙下窥，见酋长扶病者坐床上，以被靠其后，怀取一物塞其口；又出一针，长三寸许，刺其两目，血涔涔出；又取一小铜圆盒承其血；复袒其胸，以针刺其心，血又涔涔出，又取一小铜方盒承之。既拭去血迹，出膏药贴其两目并当胸小孔，口中喃喃诵咒，不知云何。乃开门大声呼曰：“病者已生天矣。”举家罗拜伏地称谢讫，然后入房举哀。戚某自上窥之最确。后以语人，闻者无不匿笑。然则由前而论，吞丸之作法甚诡；由后而论，取血之居心更毒。彼给银三十两者，固为欲买两盒血计耳。吾窃怪匹夫匹妇纷纷入教，一身受其害而不知；一家受其害而亦不知，趋之若狂，执迷不悟，亦不重可悲哉！

    Explanation

    636

    When someone could not be revived, at the time of their imminent death, the chieftain would enter their room first, close the door and screen the window lattice, recite incantations for them, judge if they were destined for heaven, wait until they breathed their last before opening the door, and allow relatives to enter the room to mourn and prepare for burial. There was a certain family that had always devoutly followed the Catholic faith; their mother was gravely ill. The chieftain, as usual, closed the door and recited incantations for her. There was a certain relative who did not believe in this, because the house had a second floor, he quietly hid on it, and peered through a gap. He saw the chieftain helping the sick person sit on the bed, placing a pillow behind her and took something from his chest to plug her mouth; then took out a needle, about three inches long, and pricked her eyes, blood trickled out; then took a small copper round box to catch the blood; then exposed her chest and pricked her heart with a needle, blood trickled out again, and took a small copper square box to catch it. After wiping away the blood, he took ointment and applied it to her eyes and the small hole on her chest, muttered incantations under his breath, though it is unknown what they were. Then he opened the door and loudly proclaimed, "The patient has gone to heaven!" The whole family gathered to prostrate themselves and thank him, then entered the room to mourn. The relative who had been watching from above confirmed it. Later told others, and those who heard it could not help but secretly laugh. Thus, considering the previous account, the method of swallowing a pill is very strange; considering the later account, the intent to take blood is even more sinister. He gave thirty-two taels of silver, clearly intending to buy the two boxes of blood. I secretly wonder why ordinary men and women join the religion so eagerly, unaware of the harm they are suffering; a family suffers harm and is also unaware, rushing into it crazily, stubbornly refusing to see the truth, which is truly pitiful!

    637

    宇内形势
    　　
    　　天宇外涵，大地虚悬于中，古以卵为喻，似近之矣。海水附隶于地，而包山川原隰者，又海也。其中四夷八荒，各占一区，如盆盎中贮水石然。驺衍论天地之大，如中国赤县神州，禹之所序者，才八十一分之一，外此尚有九州，凡一州，各有裨海环之，人民禽兽，莫能相通，如此者九；又有大瀛，海环其外。语近不经。夫吾人所居所谓中华大国者，以四天之下，惟中华恰居天地之中耳。不知天地之体既皆圆矣，人处宇内，头戴天而足履地，何莫非中？若必求天地之中，则惟北极天枢之下，此处如轮之毂，如磨之脐，如人身之心，庶几足以当之。然天枢之下，实在朔漠以北，去禹迹甚远。中国一区，道里虽广，若以天枢揆之，其实偏在东南，而东南半壁，又皆海也。

    Explanation

    637

    The Overall Layout of the World

    The universe encompasses everything, with the earth floating in the center, an idea anciently likened to an egg, which is close to the truth. Seawater is attached to the earth, including mountains, rivers, plains, and wetlands, and is also sea itself. Within this, the four border regions and the eight distant corners of the world each occupy their own area, like water and stones stored in a basin or vase. Zhuang Yan discussed the vastness of the heavens and the earth, comparing it to China, the central land of the world, which is one out of eighty-one parts as organized by Yu the Great. Beyond this, there are nine more regions, each surrounded by a smaller sea, with people and animals unable to communicate, such regions numbering nine. There is also the Great Eastern Sea, surrounding the entire world. This notion seems almost incredible. The great nation we inhabit, China, is under the four directions, precisely at the center of the heavens and the earth. However, not knowing that the bodies of heaven and earth are both round, people living in the universe, with their heads touching the sky and feet touching the earth, how can any place not be the center? If one must seek the center of heaven and earth, it would be under the North Pole's celestial pivot, a point compared to the hub of a wheel, the navel of a millstone, and the heart of a person, perhaps it can be considered the center. However, the celestial pivot is actually north of the northern deserts, far from the traces of Yu. One region of China, though broad in distance, if measured by the celestial pivot, is actually in the southeast, and the southeastern half, is also sea.

    638

    自辽阳为中国东北极际，缘海而南为天津，次山东之登、莱、青三郡，有沙门等五岛，与辽阳、朝鲜相望，一帆可即；次胶州；次江北安东县，黄河之水，由此入海；次狼山，扬子出焉；次祟明、上海，为吴淞、三泖、震泽诸水所归；次浙西之拓浦、海盐；次钱塘；次宁波府，有舟山，广八百里，今为定海县，又有普陀岩，观世音之香刹在焉；次台温；次沙城，为浙闽之交，过此为福宁州；次闽安镇，是闽省门户；次兴、泉、漳三郡，泉、漳间有金、厦门二山，各广数百里，商舶通外洋诸国者，悉由厦门出，入漳与粤，邻漳之南曰南澳，属粤潮郡；次惠州；次香山澳；次高南廉，渡海港为琼，琼之南为厓、为岗，皆粤东地。

    Explanation

    638

    From Liaoyang, which marks the extreme northeastern border of China, following the coast to the south leads to Tianjin, then to the three prefectures of Shandong—Deng, Lai, and Qing—where there are five islands, including Shandong, which face each other across the sea with Liaoyang and Korea, reachable in a single sail; next is Jiaozhou; then the northern bank of the Yangtze River, where the Yellow River flows into the sea; followed by Langshan, where the Yangtze River originates; then to Chongming and Shanghai, where the waters of Wu Song, San Mao, and Zhenze converge; next are the western regions of Zhejiang, including Taopu and Haizhou; then Qiantang; then Ningbo Prefecture, which has Zhoushan, an island extending for 800 li, now part of Dinghai County, and also the sacred site of Putuo Rock, where the temple of Guanyin stands; then Tai and Wen prefectures; next is Shacheng, the border between Zhejiang and Fujian, beyond which lies Fuzhou Prefecture; then Min'an Town, the gateway to Fujian Province; next are the three prefectures of Xing, Quan, and Zhang, where between Quan and Zhang lie Jinmen and Xiamen, each extending for several hundred li, and all merchant ships traveling to foreign countries pass through Xiamen, entering Zhangzhou and Guangdong; south of Zhangzhou is Nanao, which belongs to the Chaozhou prefecture of Guangdong; then Huizhou; then Xiangshan Bay; then Gao, Nan, and Lian, where crossing the sea leads to Hainan, and to the south of Hainan are the coastal areas of Ya and Gang, all part of eastern Guangdong.

    639

    自辽阳至此，中国东面已尽，琼厓之间，实为东南隅。缘海转西，则为中国之南面矣。尽粤东地而西为粤西，更西为贵州，又更西为云南省，云南者，中国西南隅也。然云南尽处，不尽于海而尽于山，崇山复嶂，倮偶、苗夷所居。又有缅甸国，皆瘴疠害人，人不能入。而海港亦仅止于粤西之某郡，未尝达云南也。自琼、厓间渡海港而南，水程七更，抵东京，[国名，本交阯也。明时黎氏为外家所据，遂另为一国。]东京又渡海港，十二更抵安南，即古交阯国。东京、安南两海港，自港口横渡虽甚广，渐西渐隘，而海亦止，盖海之支汊也。

    Explanation

    639

    From Liao Yang to here, the eastern part of China has reached its limit, and the Qiong崖 region is indeed the southeastern corner. Turning west along the coast, it becomes the southern part of China. Reaching the western part of Guangdong Province and moving further west is Guizhou Province, and even further west is Yunnan Province, which is the southwestern corner of China. However, the end of Yunnan does not reach the sea but reaches the mountains, with towering mountains and layered cliffs inhabited by the Lolo and Miao people. There is also the country of Burma, all of which are plagued by malaria and other diseases, making it difficult for people to enter. The seaports also only reach a certain county in western Guangdong and have never reached Yunnan. Crossing the sea port south of Qiong and Ya, a seven-day sea voyage reaches Tokyo, [a country named Jiaozhi. During the Ming Dynasty, the Li family was taken over by an external power, thus becoming a separate country.] From Tokyo, crossing another sea port, it takes twelve days to reach Annam, which is the ancient Jiaozhi country. The sea ports of Tokyo and Annam, although wide when crossing from the port, gradually narrow to the west, and the sea also ends, as it is a branch of the sea.

    640

    故东京、交阯山川，实与贵州、云南连属不断；汉伏波将军征交阯，立铜柱，不以海为限，而以分茅岭为限，则接壤可知。交阯之南曰柬浦寨，曰暹罗，曰六昆，曰大年，曰柔佛，曰麻六甲，凡六国，皆与中国连，中国以其鄙远，弃而不收。麻六甲为西面尽处，惟北连中国，馀三面皆海。凡海舶由厦门直指南，离东京水程七十更，安南七十二更，暹罗一百八十更；渐偏而西，历六昆、大年，又转北过柔佛，始抵麻六甲，水程二百更，至此，约已转出云南、缅甸后矣。

    Explanation

    640

    The mountains and rivers of the Eastern Capital (Tokyo) and Jiaozhi (Vietnam) are actually continuously connected with those of Guizhou and Yunnan. During the Han dynasty, General Ma Yuan (also known as Ma Wu) campaigned in Jiaozhi and established a copper pillar, not using the sea as a boundary but instead setting the Fenshuo Mountain as the limit, thus it is known that they border each other. To the south of Jiaozhi are Cambodia, Siam (Thailand), the Six Khmer, Daen, Johor, and Malacca, a total of six countries, all of which are connected to China. China abandoned them because they were distant and remote. Malacca is at the westernmost end, connected to China only in the north, with the other three sides being sea. All sea vessels from Xiamen sail directly south, a journey of seventy stages to Tokyo, seventy-two stages to Annam (Vietnam), and one hundred and eighty stages to Siam (Thailand); gradually turning westward, passing through the Six Khmer and Daen, then turning north over Johor, finally reaching Malacca, a journey of two hundred stages. By this point, it would have turned out behind Yunnan and Myanmar.

    641

    虽曰海道，皆依山而行，实未尝渡海也。自麻六甲斜指西北，四十更为咬留叭，[音葛腊八。]始渡西海。咬留叭西北为哑齐。[产黄金。凿石取之，其形正方，不假熔炼。其主无嗣，以女继统，今犹是女主。]哑齐之外，中国舟航不能往。相传尚有英圭黎，[音英鸡立，产哆啰呢、毕吱、西洋布、嘉纹席、玻璃器皿，与咬留叭等，而皆优于咬留叭。]荷兰、即红毛。大西洋等国，皆在西海外，其山最大又最远。
    自咬留叭绵亘而南，为万丹；又极南万里，为马神。[皆产胡椒、苏木、沉速、珈璃诸香。]自马神转东迤北，为文莱、[一无所产，极贫极陋。]为苏禄、[产大珠，有重三五钱者，然少光泽，中国人名为浅水珠。]为吕蒙山，至此，又在中国极东海外万里矣。又转北为文武楼山，以迄吕宋。海舶欲至马神者，仍行安南水道，既至咬留叭，依山而南，过万丹，达马神，水程四百六十更。非故迂其途也，以南海水道未谙，不敢渡耳。

    Explanation

    641

    Although it is called a sea route, it actually follows the coastline, and one has never truly crossed the sea. Starting from Malacca and heading northwest, after forty more days one reaches Bantam [pronounced Ge La Ba]. It is here that one begins to cross the Western Sea. Northwest of Bantam lies Arakan [which produces gold. The gold is extracted from the stone, taking the form of a perfect cube, without the need for smelting. The ruler has no heir, and the throne is succeeded by a female, who remains a reigning queen to this day]. Beyond Arakan, Chinese ships cannot venture. It is said that there is also Yingguili [pronounced Ying Ji Li, which produces cotton, silk, Western cloth, striped mats, and glassware, similar to Bantam, but all superior to Bantam]. The Netherlands, known as Hongmao, and the countries of the Great Western Ocean, all lie beyond the Western Sea, with the mountains there being the largest and farthest away.

    From Bantam, one continues southward, reaching Banjarmasin; then, another ten thousand miles south, one arrives at Macclesfield [which produces pepper, sappanwood, sandalwood, and other fragrances]. From Macclesfield, one turns eastward and then northward, reaching Brunei [which produces nothing, being extremely poor and backward], then Sulu [which produces large pearls, some weighing three or five taels, but they have little luster, and the Chinese call them shallow-water pearls], and finally Lushan Mountain, which is another ten thousand miles east of China. Continuing northward, one reaches Mount Wénwǔlóu, and finally Luzon. Ships bound for Macclesfield still follow the waterway of Annam, reaching Bantam, then following the coast southward, passing through Banjarmasin, and finally arriving at Macclesfield, a journey of four hundred and sixty days. This is not a deliberate detour, but because the waterway of the South China Sea is not well understood, one dare not cross.

    642

    往吕宋者，由厦门渡澎湖，循台湾、南沙、马矶，斜指东南巽方，经谢昆尾山、大小覆釜山，绕出东北，计水程七十二更。往苏禄者，从覆釜直指正南，水程一百四十更。计自咬留叭，从西北海中至极南，又转极东，再回东北，迄于吕宋，连山不断，蜿蜒数万里，较中国辽阳至云南海道，远过倍蓰。惜其割裂分据，不能统一；而城郭人民又无几也。日本者，古倭夷国，在中国正东，自其南言之，去中国甚远，由宁波渡海，水程三十五更。北接朝鲜，朝鲜与辽阳密迩，既渡鸭绿，便可驰驿而往，与中国在断续间，虽谓之连属亦可。

    Explanation

    642

    Those who go to Luzon cross from Xiamen to Penghu, following Taiwan, Nansha, and Ma Ji, steering southeast in the direction of Xun, passing through Xie Kun Wei Mountain and the Big and Small Fu Fu Mountains, bending northeast, calculating the water journey as seventy-two stages. Those who go to Sulu travel straight south from Fu Fu, with a water journey of one hundred and forty stages. Calculating from Batavia, the route goes from the northwest sea to the extreme south, then turns to the extreme east, and finally returns to the northeast, ending at Luzon. The continuous mountain ranges wind for tens of thousands of miles, farther than the sea route from Liaoyang in China to Yunnan by more than double. Alas, it is fragmented and divided, unable to be unified; and the cities and people are few. Japan, an ancient country of the Wō people, lies to the east of China. From the perspective of its southern part, it is very far from China, with a water journey of thirty-five stages from Ningbo. North to Korea, Korea is close to Liaoyang. After crossing the Yalu River, one can travel by post, with interruptions between China and Korea, even if they are considered connected.

    643

    台湾蕞尔拳石，南北三千里，东西三百里，去厦门水程十一更，中间又有澎湖为泊宿地；所处在东南五达之海，东西南北，惟意之适，实海上诸国必争之地也。以上诸国，皆有商舶往来贸易，其山川道里、风景、人物、土产，皆得悉知之。惟荷兰、大西洋远在西海外，相传有黑洋，昼夜如墨，人不能往，商舶不过，至咬留叭而止。咬留叭本非荷兰，特为红毛所占，设官守土，不知者目为红毛。英圭黎亦然，荷兰人鸷悍狡狯，大西洋又甚焉。近岁吕宋亦为红毛所据，分土番为二十四郡，红毛与西洋人杂治之，故荷兰者，大西洋之附庸也。琉球在东海，日本之南，吕宋之北，去中国不远，以其贫甚，故商贾蹙额视之，相戒不往。以余所知诸国绘为图，俾览者知其形势，以验盆中水石之喻而已。若夫驺子《终始》、《大圣》之篇所谓“大瀛海”者，渺不可稽，存而不论可也。

    Explanation

    643

    Taiwan, a small and insignificant island, stretches three thousand li north to south and three hundred li east to west. It is eleven hours' journey by water from Xiamen, with Penghu serving as an intermediate stop for overnight stays. Situated in the southeastern part of a sea that connects five directions, it can be approached from any direction, making it a coveted location for maritime powers. The countries mentioned above all have merchant ships engaged in trade, and their geography, scenery, people, and local products are well-known. However, the Netherlands and the Western Ocean lie far to the west, with the Indian Ocean, known for its deep and dark waters, being beyond the reach of most sailors and merchant ships, stopping at Batavia. Batavia was originally not Dutch territory but was occupied by Europeans, who set up officials to govern the land, often mistakenly identified as Dutch. Similarly, the region referred to as "英圭黎" is also under European control. The Dutch are known for their fierce and cunning nature, and the Western Ocean is even more so. Recently, the Philippines have also been occupied by Europeans, divided into twenty-four districts, governed by a mix of Europeans and Westerners, making the Dutch a subordinate power to the Western Ocean. Okinawa, located in the East China Sea, south of Japan and north of the Philippines, is not far from China but is avoided by traders due to its poverty. Based on my knowledge of these countries, I have created a map to illustrate their situation, serving as a metaphorical representation. As for the vast ocean described in the works of Zou Yan, it is too vast and mysterious to be fully explored, and it is best to acknowledge its existence without delving into it further.

    644

    里乘子曰：观郁君笔记，自谓于全台山川夷险、形势扼塞、番俗民情，不啻户至而足履，用特笔之，俾留意斯世斯民者知之。其略曰：台湾县易藏奸宄，事较两邑为繁。诸罗风山无民，所隶皆土著番人。番有土番、野番之别。野番在深山中，叠嶂如屏，连峰插汉，深林密箐，仰不见天，棘刺藤萝，举足触碍，盖自洪荒以来，斧斤所未入。野番生其中，巢居穴处，血饮毛茹者，种类实繁。其升高陟巅、越箐渡莽之捷，可以追惊猿、逐骇兽。平地诸番恒畏之，无敢入其境者。

    Explanation

    644

    Li Chengzi said: Observing Mr. Yu's notes, he claimed to have visited every household and treading every path in the mountains and rivers of the entire island, their dangerous and remote areas, strategic positions and chokepoints, customs of the indigenous people and the sentiments of the common folk. He wrote them down specifically so that those who care about this world and its people may know. His summary states: Taiwan County easily harbors evildoers, and matters are more complex than in the two counties. Zhu Luo Feng Mountain has no inhabitants, and those under its jurisdiction are all indigenous people. Among the indigenous people, there are distinctions between settled and wild tribesmen. The wild tribesmen live in the deep mountains, where layered peaks stand like screens, connected peaks reach up to the heavens, deep forests and dense thickets obscure the sky, thorns, thistles, and vines obstruct every step. Since the time of chaos and wilderness, the axe has not yet penetrated this land. The wild tribesmen live there, dwelling in caves and holes, drinking blood and eating raw meat, with a great variety of kinds. Their agility in climbing to the highest peaks, crossing thickets and wilds is such that they can chase startled monkeys and frightened beasts. The plains tribesmen always fear them and no one dares to enter their territory.

    645

    而野番恃其犷悍，时出剽掠，焚庐杀人已，复归其巢，莫能向迩。其杀人辄取首去，归而熟之，剔取髑髅，加以丹垩，置之当户。同类视其室髑髅多者推为雄，如梦如醉，不知向化，真禽兽耳，如虎豹遭之则噬，蛇虺撄之则啮。苟不近其穴，彼无肆毒之心，亦听其自生自槁于雨露中耳。客冬有趋利赖科者，欲通山东。土番与七人为侣，昼伏夜行，从野番中越度万山，竟达东面。东番知其唐人，争款之；又导之游各番社，禾黍芃芃，比户殷富。谓苦野番间阻，不得与山西通，欲约西番夹击之。

    Explanation

    645

    While the wild tribes relied on their fierce and savage nature, they occasionally went out to raid and plunder, burning down houses and killing people, then returning to their lairs. Nobody could approach or come near them. When they killed people, they would take the heads away, return and cook them, skewer out the skulls, and paint them with vermilion and white, placing them in front of the door. Among their peers, those who had the most skulls in their houses were considered the leaders. They were like in a dream or under the influence of wine, not knowing to submit to the right path. They were truly beasts, like tigers and leopards, they would bite; like snakes, they would strike. If one does not approach their lair, they will not have the heart to unleash their poison. They will also let them live or die by the rain and dew. Last winter, there was someone seeking profit through the government examination system, who wanted to reach Shandong. The local tribesmen accompanied him with seven others, hiding during the day and traveling at night, crossing through the wild tribes and over countless mountains, finally reaching the east. The eastern tribesmen recognized them as Chinese, and competed to welcome and entertain them. They also guided them to tour various tribal communities, where the crops were lush and abundant, and every household was prosperous. They complained about the obstacles posed by the wild tribes, preventing them from reaching Shanxi, and wanted to ally with the western tribes to attack them.

    646

    又曰：“寄语长官，若能以兵相助，则山东万人，凿山通道，东西一家，共输贡赋，为天朝民矣。”又以小舟从极南沙、马矶海道送之归。七人所得馈遗甚厚。谓番俗与山西大略相似，独平地至海较西为广。使当事者能持其议，与东番约期夹击，剿抚并施，烈泽焚山，夷其险阻，则数年之后，未必不变荆棘为坦途，而化盘瓠僰筰为良民也。若夫平地近番，冬夏一布，粗粝一饱，不识不知，无求无欲，自游于葛天无怀之世，有击壤鼓腹之遗风，亦恒往来市中，状貌无甚异，惟两目拗深瞪视似稍别。

    Explanation

    646

    Addressing the high-ranking official, it was said: "If you can provide military support, then ten thousand people from Shandong will dig through the mountains to create a passage, making east and west one family, jointly paying tribute and taxes, and becoming subjects of the Heavenly Dynasty." They were then sent back by small boat via the extreme south of the sea and the Ma Ji sea route. The seven individuals received substantial gifts. It was noted that the customs of the barbarians are roughly similar to those of Shanxi, except that the land from the coast to the sea is wider in the east. If those in charge can uphold this proposal and coordinate with the eastern barbarians to attack at an agreed time, employing both military force and pacification, burning marshes and mountains, and leveling the obstacles, then within a few years, it may not be impossible to transform thorny paths into smooth roads and civilize the tribes of Panhu and Buzuo into good citizens. As for the barbarians living near the plains, they wear the same clothing in winter and summer, content with coarse food, ignorant and unaware, with no desires or demands, freely wandering in the era of Ge Tian and Wu Huai, embodying the remnants of the simple, contented lifestyle of ancient times, and they also frequently visit the market, with no significant differences in appearance, except for their deeply set, staring eyes which seem somewhat different.

    647

    其语多作都庐辘声，呼酒曰“打刺酥”，呼烟曰“笃木固”。相传台湾空山无人，自南宋时，元人灭金，金人有浮海避元者，为飓风飘至，各择所居，耕凿自赡。远者或不相往来，数世之后，忘其所自，而语则未尝改。男女夏天裸体，惟私处围三尺布；冬寒以番毯为单衣。毯缉树皮、杂犬毛为之，亦有用麻者，厚可一钱。两幅联缀，不开领脰，衣时，以头贯之，仍露其背。又有袒挂一臂，及两幅左右互袒者。妇人衣，以一幅双叠，缝其两腋，仅蔽胸背；别以一幅缝其两端，以受臂而横担肩上。

    Explanation

    647

    The language often includes sounds that mimic the noise of a cart or carriage. They call out for alcohol as "dachisu" and for tobacco as "dumugou." It is said that Taiwan's empty mountains were uninhabited until the Southern Song period, when the Yuan Dynasty conquered the Jin Dynasty. Some Jin people fled across the sea to escape the Yuan invasion and were carried by a typhoon to Taiwan, where they chose their places to live and sustained themselves through farming and other means. Those who lived far apart might not interact with each other, and after several generations, they might forget their origins, but their language remained unchanged. Men and women often go naked in the summer, covering only their private areas with a piece of cloth about three feet long. In winter, they use a type of blanket as a single garment. The blankets are made from tree bark and mixed with dog hair, and some are also made from hemp, with a thickness of about one coin. The blankets are sewn together in two pieces without a collar or neck opening, and when worn, the blanket is put on by passing the head through it, leaving the back exposed. There are also styles where one arm is left bare, or the two pieces of the blanket are worn on opposite sides. Women's clothing consists of a single piece of fabric folded in half, with the sides sewn to cover the armpits, covering only the chest and back. Another piece of fabric is sewn at both ends to fit the arms and is worn horizontally across the shoulders.

    648

    上衣覆乳露腹；中衣横裹，仅掩私，不及膝；足不知履。以乌布围股，一身凡三截，各不相属。老人头白，则不挂一缕，箕踞往来，邻妇不避也。发如乱蓬，以青蒿为香草，日取束发，虮虱绕走其上。间有少妇施膏沐者，分两绺盘之；或有至妍者，亦露倩盼之态，但以鹿脂为膏，膻不可近。男子竞尚大耳，于成童时，向耳垂间各穿一孔，用竹贯之，日以加大，有大如盘、至于垂肩撞胸者。项间螺贝累累，盘绕数匝，五色陆离，都成光怪。胸背文以雕青，为鸟兽、网罟、虎豹文，不可名状。

    Explanation

    648

    The top garment covers the breasts but exposes the abdomen; the middle garment wraps horizontally, covering only the private parts but not reaching the knees; the feet are not covered by shoes. Using black cloth to wrap the thighs, the body appears in three separate sections that do not connect. Old men with white hair sit cross-legged and move around without any covering, and neighboring women do not avoid them. Their hair is like a tangle of thorns, and they use mugwort as a fragrant herb, tying it up daily, with lice and fleas crawling on it. Some young women apply ointment and divide their hair into two braids. Some very beautiful women also show a charming gaze, but the ointment they use is made of deer fat, which is pungent and unpleasant.
