Men compete to have large ears, piercing holes in their earlobes during adolescence and inserting bamboo tubes that gradually enlarge the ears, sometimes to the size of a plate or even hanging down to the shoulders and chest. The neck is adorned with many spiral shells, wrapped several times, with various colors and a dazzling appearance. The chest and back are decorated with blue tattoos in the form of birds, animals, nets, and tiger and leopard patterns, which are indescribable.

    649

    人无老少，不留一髭，并五毛尽去之。有疾不知医药，惟饮溪水则愈。妇人无冬夏，日浴于溪，浴毕，汲上流之水而归。有病者浴益频。孕妇始娩，即携儿赴浴。儿患痘，尽出其浆，复浴之，曰：“不若是，不愈也。”婚姻无媒妁。女已长，父母使居别室中，少年求偶者，皆来吹鼻萧、弹口琴，得女子和之，即入与乱，乱毕自去。久之，女择所爱者，乃与挽手，挽手者，以明私许之意也。明日，女告其父母，召挽手少年至，凿上腭门牙傍二齿授女，女亦凿二齿付男。期某日就妇室成婚，终身依妇以处。

    Explanation

    649

    In this society, regardless of age, no one retains even a single hair; all five types of hair—on the head, face, body, arms, and legs—are completely removed. When one is ill, they do not know about medicine, but drinking water from the stream cures them. Women bathe in the stream every day, regardless of whether it is winter or summer. After bathing, they draw water from the upstream and return home. Those who are sick bathe more frequently. When a woman gives birth, she immediately takes the baby to bathe. If the child has smallpox, all the pus is drained out, and then the child is bathed again, saying, "If not like this, it will not heal." Marriages do not involve matchmakers. When a girl grows up, her parents make her live in a separate room. Young men seeking a partner all come to play the nose flute and mouth harp, and when the girl responds, they enter and have sexual relations, and then leave on their own. After some time, the girl chooses the one she loves and holds hands with him, holding hands being a sign of private consent. The next day, the girl tells her parents, and they call the young man who held hands with her. They both bite off two teeth next to their front teeth and give them to each other. They agree on a certain day to marry, and the man will live with the woman for the rest of his life.

    650

    盖皆以门楣绍瓜瓞，父母不得有其子；故一再世，而孙且不识其祖矣。番人皆无姓氏，有以也。番室仿龟壳为制，筑土基三五尺，立栋其上，覆以茅。茅檐深远垂地，过土基方丈，雨阳不得侵；其下可舂、可炊，可坐、可卧，以贮笨车网罟农具，鸡栖豚栅，无不宜。室前后各为牖，在脊栋下，缘梯而登。室中空无所有，视有几犬，为置几榻；人惟藉鹿皮随处卧，夏并鹿皮去之，藉地而已。壁间悬葫芦，大如斗，旨蓄毯衣纳其中。竹筒数规，则新醅也。其酿法：聚男女老幼，共嚼米纳筒中，数日成酒。饮时，入清泉和之。

    Explanation

    650

    Generally speaking, they all pass on their lineage like melons and gourds, and parents cannot claim their children; thus, after one or two generations, the grandchildren may not even know their grandparents. Barbarians all lack surnames, for a reason. The houses of barbarians are modeled after turtle shells, with earth bases three to five feet high, and pillars erected on top, covered with thatch. The thatched eaves extend far and hang low to the ground, overhanging the earth base by a square zhang, so that rain and sun cannot penetrate; below, one can pound grain, cook, sit, and lie down, to store carts, nets, and farming tools, chickens roost and pigs are penned, nothing is inappropriate. Windows are made at the front and back of the room, and one climbs up by ladder. The room is empty of everything, depending on how many dogs there are, several beds are placed; people only lie on deer skins wherever they are, in summer the deer skins are removed, and one lies directly on the ground. Hemp rope is hung on the wall, as large as a dou, preserved food and woolen clothes are stored inside. Several bamboo tubes, these are new brews. Their method of brewing: men, women, old and young gather together, chewing rice and putting it into the tubes, in several days, the wine is made. When drinking, clear water is added to it.

    651

    客至，番妇倾筒中酒，先尝，然后进客；客饮尽则喜，否则愠，愠客或憎之也。又呼其邻妇，各衣毯衣，为联袂之歌以侑觞。客或狎之，亦不怒；其夫见妇为客狎，喜甚，谓己妻实都，故唐悦之。[海外皆称中国为大唐，称中国人为唐人。]若其同类为奸，则挟弓矢，侦奸人射杀之，而不怼其妇。地产五谷，番人惟食稻黍与稷，都不食麦。其饔飧不宿舂，晓起待炊而舂，既熟，聚家人手抟食之。山中多糜鹿，射得辄饮其血，肉之生熟不甚较，果腹而已。不虑风雨，行不计止宿，食云则食，坐云则坐；喜一笑，痛一颦；终岁不知春夏，老死不知年岁。

    Explanation

    651

    When a guest arrives, the foreign woman pours wine from a vessel, tasting it first before serving it to the guest. If the guest finishes the drink, she is pleased; otherwise, she becomes angry, and the angry guest may resent her. She then calls her neighboring women, each of whom wears a felt or woolen garment, and they sing a linked-arm song to entertain the guest. If the guest is familiar or intimate with her, she does not become angry; in fact, her husband, seeing his wife being familiar with the guest, is very pleased, saying that his wife is truly beautiful, and thus the Tang people are pleased with her. [Abroad, all refer to China as the Great Tang, and call the Chinese people the Tang people.] If members of their group commit crimes, they take up bows and arrows, investigate the criminals, and shoot them dead, without resenting their wife. The land produces the five grains, but the foreigners eat only rice, millet, and稷 (a type of millet), and do not eat wheat at all. Their meals are not prepared the night before, for they rise at dawn, wait until the cooking fire is ready, and then grind the grain. Once cooked, they gather the family and eat by hand. The mountains are filled with deer, and when they shoot one, they drink its blood immediately; the doneness of the meat is not much considered, for their only concern is to satisfy their hunger. They do not worry about rain or wind, nor do they count their stops or sleeping places; they eat when the clouds suggest it, and sit when the clouds suggest it. When happy, they smile; when in pain, they frown. Throughout the year, they do not know the seasons, and until old age claims them, they do not know the years.

    652

    寒然后求之衣，饥然后求之食，不预计也。村落庐舍，各为向背，无市肆贸易，有金钱无所用，故不知蓄积。虽有馀力，惟知计食而耕，秋成纳稼，计终岁所食有馀，则尽付曲蘖；来年新禾既值，又尽以所馀酿酒。番人无男女皆嗜酒，酒熟，各携所酿，聚男女酣饮，歌呼如沸，累三日夜不辍。馀粟既罄，虽饥不悔。屋必自构，衣需自织。耕田而后食，汲涧而后饮。绩麻为网，屈竹为弓，以猎以渔。盖毕世所需，罔非自为而后用之。

    Explanation

    652

    When cold, one seeks clothing; when hungry, one seeks food, without planning ahead. Village houses are built in various orientations, with no market trade. Money has no use, so they do not know how to accumulate. Though they have surplus strength, they only know to plant according to what they eat. When the harvest is in, if there is more than enough for the whole year, they give it all to make alcohol. For the next year, when new crops are harvested, they again use the surplus to brew more wine. All men and women of the tribe love drinking. When the wine is ready, everyone brings their own brew, gathers together to drink, sing, and shout, continuing for three days and nights without stopping. Even if there is no more grain left, they do not regret it. Houses must be built by oneself, and clothes must be woven by oneself. Eat after farming, and drink after fetching water from the stream. Make nets from hemp and bows from bamboo, used for hunting and fishing. All the necessities of life are made by oneself and used.

    653

    腰间一刃，行卧与俱，凡所成造，皆出于此。惟陶冶不能自为，得铁，则取涧中两石夹捶之，久亦成器，未尝不利于用。剖匏截竹，用代陶瓦，可以挹酒浆，可似胹饼。我有之，我饮食之；乡党亲戚，缓急有无不相通。邻人米烂仓红，饥者不之贷也。社有小大，户口有众寡，皆推一二人为土官，其居室、饮食、力作，皆与众等，无一毫加于众番，不似滇广土官，征赋税，操杀夺，拥兵自卫者。比其先不知有君长，自红毛始据时，平地土番悉受约束，力役输赋，不敢违法。杀人者剿灭无孑遗。

    Explanation

    653

    A sword hangs at my waist, accompanying me everywhere I go, and everything I create comes from this. Only pottery making cannot be done by myself. If iron is obtained, I take two stones from the stream and hammer it between them, and over time, it also becomes a tool. I cut gourds and chop bamboo to use as substitutes for pottery and tiles, which can be used to pour wine and soup, and to cook food. I have these, and I use them for eating and drinking. For neighbors and relatives, in times of need, there is no sharing of resources. Neighbors' rice rots in the granary, and the hungry do not borrow from them. Villages have large and small, households have many and few, and they all elect one or two people as local officials. Their living quarters, food, and labor are all equal to the common people, and not a single bit is added to the common people. Unlike the local officials of Yunnan and Guangdong, who collect taxes, wield power, and maintain their own armies, compared to their ancestors, they did not know of any king or leader. Since the time the Red-haired (Dutch) began to rule, the flatland tribes were all subject to their rules, performing labor and paying taxes, and dared not break the law. Those who kill are exterminated without a single survivor.

    654

    郑氏继至，立法尤严，诛夷不遗赤子，并田畴庐舍废之。其实土番杀人，非谋不轨也，曲蘖误之也。群饮之际，夸力争强，互不相下；杯未释手，白刃以陷其脰。间有平时睚眦，醉后修怨，旦日酒醒，曾不自知，而讨罪之师已蹑其门矣。至今大肚牛骂犬甲竹堑诸社，林莽荒秽，不见一人，诸番视此为戒，相率谓曰：“红毛强犯之无噍类，郑氏来，红毛畏之逃去。今郑氏又为皇帝剿灭，尽为臣虏，皇帝真天威矣！”故其人既愚又甚畏法。曩郑氏于诸番徭赋颇重，我朝因之。

    Explanation

    654

    The Zheng clan subsequently arrived and enacted laws especially strictly, executing and exiling without sparing even the youngest, and also abandoning their fields, houses, and shelters. In reality, the indigenous people killed each other not for plotting rebellion, but due to the intoxication caused by alcohol. During communal drinking sessions, they boasted and strove to outdo each other, refusing to yield to each other; holding the cups without letting go, they were stabbed with swords in their throats. Sometimes there were grudges from past grievances, which they settled after drinking. The next day when they sobered up, they were unaware of their actions, and the army of retribution had already arrived at their door. Today, the Dadao, Niwamai, Qianjia, and Zhujian tribes, their forests and jungles overgrown with weeds, not a single person is seen, and the various tribes view this as a warning, all saying, "The Red-haired invaders left no survivors, when the Zheng clan came, the Red-haired fled in fear. Now the Zheng clan is being eliminated by the emperor, all became subjects and captives, the emperor truly possesses the divine power!" Therefore, they are both foolish and very afraid of the law. Previously, the Zheng clan imposed heavy taxes and corvée labor on the tribes, and our dynasty continued this.

    655

    秋成输谷似易，而艰于输赋，彼终世不知白镪为何物，又安所得此以贡其上？于是仍沿包社之法，郡县有财力者，认办社课，名曰“社商”。社商又委通事夥长辈，使居社中，凡番人一粒一毫，皆有籍稽之。射得麋鹿，尽取其肉为脯，并收其皮。日本人甚需鹿皮，有贾舶收买；脯以鬻漳郡人，二者输赋有馀。然此辈欺番人愚，剥削无厌，视所有不异己物。平时事无巨细，悉呼番人男妇孩稚，供役其室无虚日；且皆纳番妇为妻妾，有求必与，有过必挞，而番人不甚怨之。

    Explanation

    655

    The autumn harvest of grain may seem easy, but the difficulty lies in paying the taxes. They have never seen silver in their lives and cannot possibly obtain it to offer to their superiors. Therefore, they still follow the old system of communal tax collection, where wealthy individuals in the county or town undertake the responsibility of collecting and paying taxes on behalf of the community, known as "社商." These "社商" entrust trusted and experienced elders to manage the community's affairs, and every detail of the indigenous people's resources is meticulously recorded. When they hunt deer, they take all the meat and make it into jerky, and collect the hides. Japanese merchants are in high demand for deer hides and actively purchase them; the jerky is sold to people from the region of Zhangzhou, and the two products generate more than enough revenue to pay taxes. However, these "社商" take advantage of the indigenous people's naivety and exploit them without limit, regarding everything as their own property. They call upon all the men, women, and children of the indigenous people, demanding constant and exhaustive labor, and take indigenous women as wives and concubines, giving in to any request and beating them for any mistake, yet the indigenous people do not greatly resent them.

    656

    苟能化以礼义，风以诗书，教以蓄有备无之道，制以衣服、饮食、冠婚、丧祭之礼，使咸知爱亲敬长、尊君亲上；启发乐生之心，潜削顽愍之性，远则百年，近则三十年，将见风俗改观，率循礼教，宁与中国之民有以异乎？古称荆蛮断发文身之俗，乃在吴越近地，今且蔚为人文渊薮；至若闽地，叛服不常，汉世再弃而复收之，自道南先生出，而有宋理学，大儒竞起南中，人固不可以常俗限，是在上之人鼓舞而化导之耳。今台郡百执事，朝廷以其海外劳吏，每三岁迁擢。

    Explanation

    656

    If one can transform through ritual and righteousness, and cultivate through poetry and books, teach the way of preparing for what is present and ready for what is absent, regulate through the rites of clothing, food, marriage, and funeral rites, and make everyone know how to love their parents, respect the elderly, honor the ruler, and be close to superiors; inspire a joy of life, and subtly diminish the stubborn and pitiable nature, in the long term it could take a hundred years, in the short term it could take thirty years, one will see a change in customs, and a general adherence to ritual and education, how much different would they be from the people of China? Anciently, it was said that the custom of the Jing and Man tribes was to cut their hair and tattoo their bodies, which was near the Wu and Yue regions, now it has become a rich source of culture; as for the Min region, it was often rebellious and compliant, in the Han dynasty, it was abandoned and then recovered, since the emergence of the Dao Nan Master, and the development of Song Neo-Confucianism, great scholars emerged in the southern regions, people cannot be limited by conventional customs, it is up to those in power to inspire and guide them. Now the hundred officials of Taiwan, the court considers them as laborers in the overseas territory, they are promoted every three years.

    657

    政令初施，人心未洽，而转眄易之，安必萧规曹随，后至者一守前人绳尺，不事更张；况席不暇暖，视一官传舍，孰肯为远效难稽之治乎？余谓欲化番人，必如周之分封，同姓及世卿采地，子孙世守；或如唐韦皋、宋张咏之治蜀，久任数十年，不责旦暮之效，然后可。噫！盖亦难言矣！然又有暗阻潜挠于中者，则社棍是也。此辈皆内地犯法奸民，逃死匿身于僻远无人之地，谋充夥长通事，为日既久，熟识番情，复解番语，父死子继，流毒无已。彼社商者，不过高卧郡邑，催饷纳课而已，社事任其播弄。故社商有亏折耗费，此辈坐享其利；社商率一二岁更易，而此辈虽死不移也。

    Explanation

    657

    When new policies are first implemented, the people's hearts are not yet aligned, and they tend to change their minds quickly. How can one expect them to follow the old rules and norms without making changes? Moreover, how can one expect them to undertake a distant and difficult governance when they view an official position as a temporary lodging and have no time to sit down and warm themselves? Who would be willing to undertake such a challenging task? I believe that to civilize or transform the barbarians, one must adopt the feudal system of the Zhou Dynasty, granting land and titles to clan members and hereditary nobles, with their descendants inheriting and protecting these lands. Alternatively, one could adopt the governance styles of Wei Gao and Zhang Yong, who held their positions for many years in Sichuan during the Tang and Song Dynasties, not demanding immediate results, and only then can success be achieved. Alas! This is indeed a difficult matter to discuss! However, there are also hidden obstacles and secret underminers within, and these are the village bullies. These people are lawbreakers from the interior regions who have fled to remote and uninhabited places, plotting to become leaders or interpreters. Having been there for a long time, they are well acquainted with the customs of the barbarians and understand their language. Fathers die and sons take over, and their harmful influence continues endlessly. The local officials, on the other hand, merely sit idly in the county, collecting taxes and duties, leaving the affairs of the community to be manipulated by others. Thus, when the local officials suffer losses or waste, these people enjoy the benefits. Local officials are replaced every one or two years, but these people remain unchanged even if they die.

    658

    此辈正利番人之愚，又甚欲番人之贫；愚则不识不知，撄夺惟意；贫则易于迫挟，力不敢抗。匪特不教之，且时时诱陷之。即有以冤诉者，而番语咮离，不能达情，听讼者仍问之通事，通事颠倒是非以对，番人反受呵责。通事又告之曰：“县官以尔违通事夥长言，故怒责尔。”于是番人益畏社棍，事之不啻帝天。其情至于无告，而上之人无由知，是举世所当哀矜者，莫番人若矣。乃以其异类，且歧视之；见其无衣，曰“是不知寒”；见其雨行露宿，曰“彼不致疾”；见其负重驰远，曰“若本耐劳”。

    Explanation

    658

    These people take advantage of the ignorance of the barbarians, and they even desire the barbarians to be poor; when they are ignorant, they do not know or understand, and thus they can seize and snatch at will; when they are poor, it is easy to coerce and intimidate them, and they dare not resist. Not only do they not educate them, but they often deceive and entrap them. Even if there are those who complain of injustice, the barbarian language is so different that they cannot convey their feelings, and the judge still asks the interpreter. The interpreter deliberately reverses right and wrong in response, and the barbarians are instead rebuked. The interpreter then tells them, "The county official is angry with you because you disobeyed what the interpreter and the headman said." Thus, the barbarians become even more fearful of the local tyrants, treating them no less than gods. Their plight has reached a point of no recourse, and those in high places have no way of knowing. There is no one in the world more deserving of pity than the barbarians. Because they are a different race, they are discriminated against; seeing that they have no clothes, they say, "They don't know cold"; seeing that they travel in the rain and sleep in the open, they say, "They won't get sick"; seeing that they carry heavy loads over long distances, they say, "They are naturally hardworking."

    659

    噫！若亦人也，其肢体皮骨，何莫非人！而云若是乎！马不宿驰，牛无偏驾，否且致疾。牛马且然，而况人乎！抑知彼苟多帛，亦重绨矣！寒胡为哉？彼苟无事，亦安居矣！暴露胡为哉？彼苟免力役，亦暇且逸矣！奔走负戴于社棍之室胡为哉？乐饱暖而苦饥寒，厌劳役而安逸豫，人之性也，异其人，何必异其性哉？郁君之言如此。予昔闻闽人言，番民性宜率，最易感化，大可为用。况番地极暖，岁谷可三熟，且土壤膏腴，旷地最多。前山东刘玉坡制军韵珂总督浙、闽时，番民叩辕，呈请发帑入境开垦，计岁入赋税甚巨。制军大悦，特疏入告，惜为长白某相国所持，其事遂寝。今证以郁君所记，果信。域中有此沃壤，摈弃诚属可惜，爰附识于此，以俟当道之采择焉。

    Explanation

    659

    Ah! If they are also human beings, their limbs and bones, what are not human? And yet, they are treated like this! Horses should not be ridden continuously, and oxen should not be driven unevenly, otherwise they will become ill. Even animals like horses and oxen suffer from overwork, how much more so would humans! Do you not know that if someone has many fine silks, they would also value coarser fabrics? Why would they endure the cold? If someone has no responsibilities, they should be able to live comfortably. Why would they expose themselves to harsh conditions? If someone is free from labor, they should have leisure and comfort. Why would they run errands for local bullies? People naturally prefer comfort and ease over hardship and labor, and this is a universal human trait regardless of individual differences. Mr. Yu's words are like this. I once heard a person from Fujian say that the people of a certain region are straightforward and easily influenced, making them valuable. Moreover, the land in this region is extremely warm, and crops can be harvested three times a year, with fertile soil and abundant land. When the governor of Shandong, Liu Yubo, was also in charge of Zhejiang and Fujian, the local people petitioned for funds to cultivate the land, expecting significant tax revenue. The governor was pleased and submitted a memorial, but the plan was ultimately rejected by a high-ranking official from Changbai. Now, confirmed by Mr. Yu's account, it is indeed true. There is such fertile land in the world, and to abandon it would truly be a waste. Therefore, I record this here for the consideration of those in power.

    660

    附录：海上占晴雨
    　　
    　　天无时无云，虽浓云叆叇，但有云脚可见，必不雨。云脚者，如画家绘水口石，其下横染一笔，为水石之界者是也。无脚之云，如画远山，但见山头，不见所止。日色被体如灼，三日内必雨。日出时，有云蔽之，辰刻后云渐散，必大晴。日初出即开朗，是日必不晴。[暑月久晴，则不拘。]日落时，西方有云气横亘天上，或作数十缕，各不相属，日从云隙中渡过，是谓“日穿经纬”，来日大晴。

    Explanation

    660

    Appendix: Weather Prediction at Sea

    Whenever the sky is perpetually cloudy, even if the clouds are thick and heavy, but there is still a visible lower edge (cloud foot), it will not rain. The cloud foot is like a painter's brushstroke indicating the boundary between water and stone, where the lower edge of the cloud meets the horizon. Clouds without a visible lower edge are like distant mountains, where only the peaks are visible, and the base is not clear. If the sun feels like it is burning on the body, it will rain within three days. If the sun is initially covered by clouds at sunrise, but the clouds gradually dissipate by辰刻 (around 7 AM to 9 AM), it will be a clear day. If the sun rises and immediately clears the sky, it will not be clear on that day. [During the hot summer months, if there is a prolonged period of clear weather, the rule does not apply.] At sunset, if there are several strands of clouds stretching horizontally across the western sky, and the sun passes through the gaps in the clouds, this is called "the sun passing through the weave of the sky," and it will be clear the next day.

    661

    或云色一片相连，其中但有一二点空窦得现红色，是谓“金乌展睛”，亦主晴。日落时，西方云色黯淡一片如墨，全无罅窦，又不见云脚者，主来日雨；如云色浓厚，当夜必雨。日落时，西北方云起，如层峦复嶂，重叠数十层，各各矗起者，主大风雨，山崩水溢之征也，应在七日之内。余三睹此云，三遭大水矣。处近山及江浒水涯间，宜防之。昧爽时雨，俗呼“开门雨”，是日主晴。昧爽，是初明时也。五更雨、鸡初鸣雨、天未明雨、天明已久雨，皆主是日苦雨不辍。晨起，雾遮山脚，主晴；云罩山头，主雨。

    Explanation

    661

    Some say that when the sky appears as a single continuous mass, with only one or two spots of red visible through gaps, it is called "the golden bird opening its eyes," which also indicates clear weather. At sunset, if the western sky is dark and uniform like ink, with no gaps and no visible cloud bases, it predicts rain the next day; if the cloud color is dense, it will rain that night. At sunset, if clouds rise in the northwest, forming layers like mountains, stacked in dozens of layers, each standing tall, it predicts a storm, with signs of landslides and flooding, which should occur within seven days. I have witnessed this cloud formation three times and experienced heavy rain on those occasions. Those living near mountains or along rivers should be cautious. Rain at the break of dawn, commonly known as "door-opening rain," predicts a sunny day. "Door-opening rain" refers to the early morning. Rain at the fifth watch, rain when the rooster crows, rain before dawn, and rain long after dawn all indicate continuous rain throughout the day. In the morning, if mist obscures the base of the mountain, it predicts clear weather; if clouds cover the mountain top, it predicts rain.

    662

    初雨如雾，虽沉晦，至午必晴。久雨后暂缀，犹见细雨如雾，纵令开朗，旋即雨至。谚日：“雨前濛濛终不雨，雨后濛濛终不晴。”久雨，夜忽开霁，星月朗洁，主来日大雨。若近暮，经见红光，然后见月，则晴。久雨后，近暮遍天红色，来日必晴。[俗云：火烧薄暮天。]雨天，辰刻看巽方，[东南隅也。]申酉看乾方，[西北隅也。]但有一线开朗，则主晴。虹霓，朝见西方，辰巳必雨。《诗》日：“朝跻于西，崇朝其雨。”古人已言之矣。

    Explanation

    662

    The first rain is like mist, although it is gloomy, it will certainly clear by noon. After a long period of rain, there is a temporary pause, but still, fine rain like mist persists. Even if it becomes clear, rain will soon follow. Proverb says: "If it is misty before the rain, it will not rain; if it is misty after the rain, it will not clear up." After a long period of rain, if it suddenly clears up at night and the stars and moon are bright and clean, it foretells heavy rain the next day. If near dusk, seeing red light before the moon appears, it will be clear. After a long period of rain, if the sky turns red at dusk, it will be clear the next day. It is said: "The sky burns at dusk." On rainy days, at the third watch, look at the southeast direction; at the seventh and eighth watches, look at the northwest direction. If there is even a slight clearing, it will be clear. If a rainbow is seen in the west in the morning, it will rain between the third and fourth watches. The Book of Songs says: "Rising in the west in the morning, it will rain all day." Ancient people have already said this.

    663

    朝霞亦然，谚云：“朝霞不出门，暮霞适千里。”暮霞必主晴也。虹霓，申酉见东方，必晴。又，断虹两端不连者，俗呼“破篷”，虽见东方，来日不免风雨。月阑，一曰“月晕”，主风雨。[谚云：“小晕风，大晕雨。”惟闽中人又有以月阑为旱兆。]望山，色极清明，翠色欲滴，三日内必主雨。若山色如隔，濛雾者，久晴之征也。柱础流汗，必雨。凭几作书，笔燥欲裂，三日内必雨。日入时，惟东西常有云气，南北必无；若北方有云，主明日风雨，甚者尤主暴风，试之，百不失一。
    　　
    　　暴风日期[或先期即至，或逾期始作，总不出前三后三、七日之内也。]
    　　
    　　正月初三日[真人暴。]初九日[玉皇暴。占风者以此暴为候。此暴有风，则遇暴期必不免，无则免一岁皆免。]十三日[刘将军暴。]十五日[上元暴。]廿四日[小妾暴。]廿八日[洗炊笼暴。]廿九日[乌狗暴。]

    Explanation

    663

    Morning clouds are similarly indicative of rain, as the saying goes: "If there are morning clouds, do not leave home; if there are evening clouds, travel a thousand miles." Evening clouds always portend clear weather. Rainbows seen between the hours of Shen and You in the east will always bring clear skies. Additionally, if the rainbow is broken and not connected at its ends, it is colloquially called "broken sail," and even if seen in the east, the following day will not escape rain or wind. A moon halo, also known as "moon ring," predicts rain or wind. [The saying goes: "A small halo predicts wind, a large halo predicts rain." Only people from Fujian believe that a moon halo is an omen of drought.] When looking at distant mountains, if they appear extremely clear and their green color seems about to drip, rain will surely come within three days. If the mountain colors appear distant and hazy, it is a sign of long-lasting clear weather. If the base of a building is sweating, rain will come. If the ink in a writing brush dries up and cracks, rain will come within three days. At sunset, clouds are always present in the east and west, but never in the north and south; if there are clouds in the north, it will bring rain or wind the next day, and in severe cases, it will bring strong winds. This has been tested and proven accurate every time.

    　　The date of a strong wind [may arrive early or late, but it will always fall within the three days before or after, or within seven days.]

    　　The third day of the first month [is the day of the True Man's storm.] The ninth day of the first month [is the day of the Jade Emperor's storm. Weather forecasters use this storm as a sign. If there is wind on this day, the storm period will surely not be avoided; if there is no wind, it will be avoided for the whole year.] The thirteenth day of the first month [is the day of the General Liu's storm.] The fifteenth day of the first month [is the day of the Lantern Festival storm.] The twenty-fourth day of the first month [is the day of the concubine's storm.] The twenty-eighth day of the first month [is the day of the washing of the cooking basket storm.] The twenty-ninth day of the first month [is the day of the black dog storm.]

    664

    二月初二日[土地暴。]初八日[张大帝暴。]十九日[观音暴。]廿五日[龙神朝天暴。一曰是二十九日。]
    　　
    　　三月初三日[玄帝暴。]初七日[阎王暴。]十五日[雷霆暴。]十八日[后土暴。]廿三日[马祖暴。]廿八日[东岳暴。又曰诸神朝天暴。]
    　　
    　　四月初一日[白龙暴。]初八日[佛祖暴。又曰太子暴。]十四日[纯阳暴。]廿三日[太保暴。]廿五日[龙神暴，太白暴。]
    　　
    　　五月初一日[南极暴。]初五日[屈原暴。]初七日[朱太尉暴。]十三日[关帝暴。]十六日[天地暴。]十八日[天师暴。]廿九日[威显王暴。]
    　　
    　　六月初六日[崔将军暴。]十二日[彭祖暴。]十九日[观音暴。]廿三日[小姨暴。]廿四日[雷公暴。此暴最狠。]廿六日[二郎神暴。]廿八日[大姨暴。]廿九日[文丞相暴。]

    Explanation

    664

    Inauspicious Days Throughout the Year

    February

    • Second day: Land God's Day (土地暴)
    • Eighth day: Emperor Zhang's Day (张大帝暴)
    • Nineteenth day: Guanyin's Day (观音暴)
    • Twenty-fifth day: Dragon God Ascending to Heaven's Day (龙神朝天暴); alternatively, the twenty-ninth day

    March

    • Third day: Xuanwu's Day (玄帝暴)
    • Seventh day: Yama's Day (阎王暴)
    • Fifteenth day: Thunder and Lightning's Day (雷霆暴)
    • Eighteenth day: Houtu's Day (后土暴)
    • Twenty-third day: Ma Zu's Day (马祖暴)
    • Twenty-eighth day: Dongyue's Day (东岳暴); alternatively, the Day of All Gods Ascending to Heaven (诸神朝天暴)

    April

    • First day: White Dragon's Day (白龙暴)
    • Eighth day: Buddha's Day (佛祖暴); alternatively, Prince's Day (太子暴)
    • Fourteenth day: Chunyang's Day (纯阳暴)
    • Twenty-third day: Taibao's Day (太保暴)
    • Twenty-fifth day: Dragon God's Day (龙神暴); alternatively, Taibai's Day (太白暴)

    May

    • First day: Nanjing's Day (南极暴)
    • Fifth day: Qu Yuan's Day (屈原暴)
    • Seventh day: Zhu Taifu's Day (朱太尉暴)
    • Thirteenth day: Guan Yu's Day (关帝暴)
    • Sixteenth day: Heaven and Earth's Day (天地暴)
    • Eighteenth day: Tianshi's Day (天师暴)
    • Twenty-ninth day: Wei Xian Wang's Day (威显王暴)

    June

    • Sixth day: Cui Jiangjun's Day (崔将军暴)
    • Twelfth day: Peng Zu's Day (彭祖暴)
    • Nineteenth day: Guanyin's Day (观音暴)
    • Twenty-third day: Xiao Yi's Day (小姨暴)
    • Twenty-fourth day: Leigong's Day (雷公暴); this day is particularly severe
    • Twenty-sixth day: Erlang Shen's Day (二郎神暴)
    • Twenty-eighth day: Da Yi's Day (大姨暴)
    • Twenty-ninth day: Wen Chengxiang's Day (文丞相暴)
    665

    七月初七日[乞巧暴。]十五日[中元暴。]十八日[王母暴。]廿一日[普庵暴。凡六七月多主台，海上人谓“六月防初，七月防半”，虽未必尽然，有时而验。]
    　　
    　　八月初五日[九皇暴。]十五日[中秋暴。又曰伽蓝暴。]廿一日[龙神大会暴。]
    　　
    　　九月初九日[重阳暴。]十七日[金龙暴。]廿七日[冷风暴。九月，自寒露至立冬止，是九月节令也。常乍晴乍阴，风雨不时，谓之九降，又曰九月乌。]
    　　
    　　十月初五日[风信暴。]初六日[天曹暴。]初十日[水仙王暴。]十五日[下元暴。]二十日[东岳朝天暴。]
    　　
    　　十一月十四日[水仙暴]廿九日[西岳朝天暴。此时朔风司令无日无风，然南风尽绝，凡背亲处，皆可泊船。]

    Explanation

    665

    On the seventh day of the seventh lunar month, there is the Qixi Festival, where women pray for skillful hands in needlework. On the fifteenth day, the Zhongyuan Festival, also known as the Ghost Festival, is celebrated with rituals to honor ancestors and appease wandering spirits. On the eighteenth day, the Queen Mother of the West is honored, a figure associated with longevity and immortality. On the twenty-first day, the Pu'an Festival is observed, though the specific details are less commonly known. Generally, in June and July, the weather is often unsettled, and there is a folk saying that warns of potential misfortunes at the beginning of June and the middle of July, though this is not always the case.

    On the fifth day of the eighth lunar month, the Nine Emperor Gods Festival is celebrated, a Taoist event honoring the nine emperors of the heavens. On the fifteenth day, the Mid-Autumn Festival, a time for family reunions and moon worship, is observed. It is also known as the "伽蓝暴" festival, which may refer to a ritual dedicated to the protection of temples and monasteries. On the twenty-first day, the Dragon Kings' Grand Assembly Festival is held, a celebration of the deities associated with water and weather.

    On the ninth day of the ninth lunar month, the Double Ninth Festival, also known as Chongyang Festival, is celebrated with mountain climbing and drinking chrysanthemum wine. On the seventeenth day, the Golden Dragon Festival is observed, possibly related to wealth and prosperity. On the twenty-seventh day, a cold front or storm, referred to as "冷风暴," is common. In September, from the time of Cold Dew to the start of Winter, the weather is characterized by sudden changes, with alternating clear and overcast skies and unpredictable rain and wind. This period is known as "九降" or "九月乌."

    On the fifth day of the tenth lunar month, the Wind Information Festival, or "风信暴," is held, possibly related to weather prediction or agricultural practices. On the sixth day, the Heavenly Court Festival, or "天曹暴," is celebrated, likely a ritual dedicated to the Heavenly Court in Chinese mythology. On the tenth day, the Water God Festival, or "水仙王暴," is observed, honoring the deity associated with water and fertility. On the fifteenth day, the Lower Yuan Festival, or "下元暴," is celebrated, marking the end of the year and the beginning of a new cycle. On the twentieth day, the Mount Tai Festival, or "东岳朝天暴," is held, a ritual dedicated to Mount Tai, the easternmost and most sacred of the Five Sacred Mountains in China.

    On the fourteenth day of the eleventh lunar month, the Water God Festival, or "水仙暴," is celebrated again, similar to the one in October. On the twenty-ninth day, the Mount Hua Festival, or "西岳朝天暴," is held, a ritual dedicated to Mount Hua, the westernmost of the Five Sacred Mountains in China. During this time, the prevailing northerly winds, or "朔风," dominate the weather, with no day free from wind. However, southerly winds are rare, and any sheltered or protected area, or "背亲处," can be used for docking ships due to the strong winds.

    666

    十二月廿四日[扫尘暴。]
    　　
    　　又每月遇箕、璧、翼、轸四宿之日，必主风；又朔望皆必有风，舟人谣曰：“朔望无好天。”又每旬之七、八、九三日多主风。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：“占晴雨”与“风暴日期”，亦郁君卷尾所载者。郁君久习海上，自谓屡试不爽，爰撷录，以告海客，倘有事海疆，俾知防患未然，或亦不无少裨焉。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：右图固摹郁君原本所绘者，海内外各处，朗若列眉，梯航者观之，亦可知所考焉。更谓分，昼夜为十更。
    　　
    　　撷录《豁意轩录闻》
    　　
    　　门人金仰之清美，吴门世家子也。高祖讳祖静，字安安，以进士官四川布政，少工书法，曾与张文敏公照同书《天台山赋》进呈，仰邀睿赏。曾祖讳宗楚，字小笏，官中书舍人，著作甚富，仰之尝出示所撰《豁意轩录闻》一帙。予爱其笔意颇类唐人说部，姑撷录数条以资谈柄。

    Explanation

    666

    On the 24th day of the 12th month [a sudden and intense cleaning event occurred].

    Additionally, on the days of the Jī, Bì, Yì, and Zhěn constellations each month, there will certainly be wind; and on the new moon and full moon, there will certainly be wind, as the sailors say: "There is no good weather on the new moon and full moon." Also, on the seventh, eighth, and ninth days of each ten-day period, there are often winds.

    The scholar said: "Predicting weather and storm dates," as recorded in the end of Mr. Yu's scroll. Mr. Yu, who has long been familiar with the sea, claims that his predictions have never failed. Therefore, I have selected and compiled this information to inform travelers, so that if they have business in coastal areas, they may know to prevent disasters before they occur, and perhaps it may be of some benefit.

    The scholar said: The diagram here is a copy of the original drawn by Mr. Yu. It clearly shows various places at home and abroad, as clear as a row of eyebrows. Travelers can also understand the information from it. Furthermore, it divides the day and night into ten watches.

    Selected and compiled from "Huo Yi Xuan Lu Wen" (Notes from the "Huo Yi Xuan").

    My disciple Jin Yangzhi is a handsome and refined young man from a prominent family in Wuxian. His great-grandfather, whose name was Zujing and courtesy name Anan, served as the governor of Sichuan through the imperial examination. He was skilled in calligraphy when young and once wrote the Tiantai Mountain Ode with Zhang Wenmin Gongzhao, presenting it to the emperor, who highly praised it. His grandfather, whose name was Zongchu and courtesy name Xiaohu, served as a secretary to the emperor and was prolific in his writings. Yangzhi once showed me a volume of his Huo Yi Xuan Lu Wen. I found his writing style very similar to that of Tang Dynasty narrative works, so I have selected a few entries to provide as topics of conversation.

    667

    城隍赴任
    　　
    　　广西某郡太守，挈家赴任。甫入境，薄暮雾起，将系缆于山侧，忽见上流一官舫扬舟而来，旗上大书曰“某府正堂”，与己衔无二，疑是前官，急呼榜人移舟相傍，遽投刺请见。将命者入告，出致词曰：“各事所事，未敢谋面也。某强之再四，乃延入。主人峨冠博带，凛然有肃杀意。某具陈姓字官职，随问：“公是何官？今将焉往？”对曰：“余亦此间郡守，奉命而之新任。”某讶曰：“得毋有两刺史耶？”曰：“非也，子隶阳而我隶阴也。”某大惊曰：“公殆为城隍之神耶？”曰：“然。”某不禁股慄，久之，魂魄始定。遂长跪哀告曰：“某家有恒产，无藉俸钱，只以老母在堂，欲以官为荣养，第风烛可惧，未识算尽何日，且当以何疾殂，乞尊神稽籍明示。

    Explanation

    667

    The Governor Takes Office

    A governor of a certain prefecture in Guangxi Province, accompanied by his family, was on his way to take up his new post. Just as he entered the territory, the mist began to rise as twilight approached, and he was about to moor his boat at the side of the mountain. Suddenly, he saw a government boat coming swiftly from upstream, with a flag prominently displayed that read "Governor of the Prefecture," identical to his own. Surprised, he called out to the boatman to move the boat closer and insisted repeatedly to be invited in. The guest, dressed in a tall hat and wide sash, had a stern and imposing demeanor. The governor fully stated his name, title, and position, and then asked, "What is your official rank? Where are you headed?" The guest replied, "I am also the governor of this prefecture, and I have been ordered to take up a new post." The governor was astonished and asked, "Are there two governors in the same place?" The guest answered, "No, you serve in the realm of the living while I serve in the realm of the dead." The governor exclaimed, "Surely you are the spirit of the city god?" The guest nodded, and the governor could not help but shiver with fear. After a while, his soul and body began to settle. He then knelt down and pleaded, "I have a stable income and do not rely on my salary. My only reason for seeking this position is to honor and support my elderly mother. However, life is fragile, and I do not know when my time will come. Moreover, by what illness will I die? I ask you to check the records and clearly show me."

    668

    ”神作色曰：“天机未可泄也。”某涕泣固请，神乃恻然曰：“我言之必遭天遣，顾怜子至孝，弗忍终讳。子母两年后，应遭雷殛，死于官舍。此是前生未报冤孽耳。”某放声大恸，更求禳解之法。神曰：“子之官后，当极人间之奉以奉之。”某曰：“若此可免于难乎？”曰：“若此可速其死，死既速，难斯免矣。”某仍拜求不止，神命左右掖之出。甫登己舟，风雨骤至，一切所见，倏为乌有。自是嗒焉若丧。既抵任，饮泣承欢，凡所以奉母者靡弗至，不逾年，母以疾善终。某仓皇解绶，扶榇还里。次岁仲夏，忽一日雷电大作，围绕府廨，震击屋舍殆遍；至暮，势渐远，俄闻霹雳一声，将城隍庙神像碎为齑粉，天色旋霁。盖雷固遍觅某母不得，审知泄机之由，乃以诛母者诛神耳。救人而自罹于法，神亦恺悌也哉！

    Explanation

    668

    The god changed his expression and said, "Heavenly secrets should not be revealed." The protagonist wept and pleaded persistently, and the god, moved with pity, said, "If I tell you, I will surely suffer divine punishment. But I pity your filial piety and cannot bear to keep it hidden. Two years from now, your mother should be struck by lightning and die in the official residence. This is due to karmic debts from a previous life." The protagonist wailed loudly and sought a way to avert the calamity. The god said, "After you take office, you should provide the utmost care to your mother." The protagonist asked, "If I do this, can I avoid the calamity?" The god replied, "If you do this, it may hasten her death. Once she dies quickly, the calamity will be averted." The protagonist continued to plead and prostrate himself, but the god ordered his attendants to escort him out. As soon as he boarded his own boat, a fierce storm arose, and everything he had seen vanished instantly. From then on, he was deeply saddened and despondent. Upon arriving at his post, he wept while trying to please his mother, providing her with the utmost care in every possible way. Within a year, his mother died peacefully of illness. The protagonist hastily resigned and carried her coffin back to his hometown. The following midsummer, suddenly one day, a fierce lightning storm erupted, surrounding the government office and shaking buildings almost everywhere. By evening, the intensity gradually decreased. Soon, a loud thunderclap was heard, and the statue of the city god in the temple was shattered into fine powder. The sky cleared shortly after. It seems that the lightning had searched for the woman in vain and, understanding the reason for the revelation of secrets, struck the god instead of the woman. Saving someone while oneself suffers a legal consequence, the god was indeed kind and gentle.

    669

    武昌徐商
    　　
    　　武昌徐商者，少故无赖。性嗜博，博又辄负，以致衣食不给，贫无立锥。会隆冬大雪，寄宿古祠内，朔风刺骨，四肢冻欲僵，生计已穷，将投汉江死。天乍明，径出门去，行数武，至石桥上，足忽滑仆地，起而视之，则雪积成冰，冰上有钱一枚。徐念捐生而获青蚨，是意外事，姑以易饼饵，亦可延片刻喘。因拾取回步，偶遇博徒某甲，询所自来，徐以实告。甲曰：“何不以此钱博？苟胜则有二，庶可少疗枵腹。”徐从之，一博而胜。甲请再，又胜；转瞬间获钱数十。徐以其半易酒食，馀者更博。后盖无弗胜者。半年之内，积至五百缗。

    Explanation

    669

    Xu Shang from Wuchang

    Xu Shang, from Wuchang, was a disreputable youth. He had a strong liking for gambling, and whenever he gambled, he always lost, which led to him being unable to provide for his clothing and food. He was extremely poor, with no place to stand. Coinciding with a severe winter snowstorm, he lodged temporarily in an ancient shrine. The north wind froze to the bone, and his limbs almost froze stiff. His means of subsistence had come to an end, and he intended to throw himself into the Han River to die. Just as the sky became light, he went directly out of the door, walking a few steps, and arrived at a stone bridge. Suddenly, he slipped and fell to the ground. He stood up and looked at it, and saw that the snow had accumulated and frozen into ice, with a coin on the ice. Xu thought that sacrificing his life and finding money was a fortunate turn of events, an unexpected event. Temporarily with it, he could exchange it for bread or food, which would prolong a moment's breath. Therefore, he picked it up and returned. He happened to meet a gambler named Jia, who asked where it came from. Xu told him truthfully. The gambler said, "Why not gamble with this money? If you win, then there would be two, and perhaps you can slightly alleviate your hunger." Xu followed his advice, and one gamble, he won. The gambler requested to gamble again, and he won again; in a moment, he gained dozens of coins. Xu used half of it to exchange for wine and food, and with the remainder, he gambled again. Later, he never lost. Within half a year, he accumulated to five hundred strings of coins.

    670

    徐遂娶妇生子，弃博而贾。设肆通衢，制诸竹木器，日取其利以为常。时有鹾商某，为群商领袖，强暴不法，肆作威福；楚中大吏尽与交，莫敢犯者。适部郎某公将出为楚运使，性刚正，疾恶如仇。奉诏后，即有锄某意。或遣急足驰告某，某大惧，寝食都废。客乃献计曰：“市中有徐姓者，故穷措大，貌酷肖君。曷不诱以利，俾代君，则君生矣。”某喜，遽命驾造徐。徐怪甚，伛偻以迎，而某亦谦抑尽礼，睹其貌与己无二。因邀徐至舍，水陆并陈，丝竹齐奏；宴罢下榻，精庐供帐，俱极华美。徐茫然不解其故。次日告归，某坚留弗许。如是者三日。徐曰：“君之高谊，已切五中。

    Explanation

    670

    Xu married and had a child, then abandoned gambling and became a merchant. He set up a shop in a busy street, crafting various bamboo and wood products, and daily earned a profit as his routine. At that time, there was a certain salt merchant, a leader among merchants, who was violent and lawless, acting with arbitrary power and favoritism; all the high officials in Chu were connected with him, and no one dared to defy him. Just then, a certain official from the Ministry was about to become the Transport Commissioner of Chu, a man with a straightforward and righteous nature, hating evil as if it were an enemy. After receiving the imperial decree, he immediately had the intention to deal with the salt merchant. Someone sent a swift messenger to inform him, and the salt merchant was greatly afraid, neglecting sleep and food. A guest then suggested a plan: "There is someone named Xu in the market, a former poor scholar who looks very much like you. Why not lure him with benefits, making him stand in for you, so that you can survive?" The official was pleased and immediately ordered his carriage to visit Xu. Xu was very surprised and bowed to greet him, and the official was humble and courteous. Seeing his appearance was no different from his own, he then invited Xu to his residence. Food and drink were laid out, and music was played; after the banquet, he provided lodging, with the rooms and furnishings all extremely luxurious. Xu was confused and did not understand the reason. The next day, he requested to return home, but the official insisted and did not allow him to leave. This went on for three days. Xu said, "Your noble friendship has deeply touched me."

    671

    顾必有以用仆者，乞明言，俾释疑虑。”某唯唯。徐固请，某始长跽陈状，且曰：“苟能相代，十万金亦易易事。即有死罪，我当竭力以营之。”徐闻言，面色如土。转念：己本馀生，既入犴狴，必难幸免。不若允其请，身纵杀，子孙弗失为富家儿，计亦良得。乃曰：“士为知己用。君诚践言，仆甘为君死。”某更喜，随集腹心数人，署券申誓，先以万金慰其家；徐亦召妻子与诀。某又以诸簿籍示徐，徐素黠，瞭如指掌。某自潜匿谨室，徐冒某名，出交宾客，乘坚策肥，恬不为怪，而宾客亦弗疑也。未几，运使公至，甫下车，徐率群商进谒。

    Explanation

    671

    If there must be someone who will use me, I beg for clear words to dispel my doubts." The speaker replied affirmatively. Xu persisted in his request, and the speaker began to kneel long and state the situation, saying, "If you can take my place, ten thousand gold is an easy matter. Even if there is a capital crime, I will do my utmost to save you." Xu heard this and his face turned as pale as soil. He thought to himself: "I am already living on borrowed time; once I enter prison, it will be difficult to escape punishment. It would be better to agree to his request. Even if I am killed, my descendants will not lose their status as wealthy children, and the plan is also well thought out." He then said, "A man is willing to serve those who understand him. If you truly keep your word, I am willing to die for you." The speaker was even more pleased and gathered a few close associates, signed a contract, and took an oath, first offering ten thousand gold to comfort his family; Xu also called his wife and children to bid farewell. The speaker then showed Xu all the important documents, which Xu understood thoroughly. The speaker hid himself in a secluded room, while Xu took his name and went out to meet guests, driving a sturdy carriage and riding a well-fed horse, acting without surprise, and the guests did not suspect anything either. Not long after, the governor-general arrived, just having arrived, Xu led the merchants to pay homage.

    672

    公叱曰：“尔恶已极，擢发难数！今将穷治，可洗颈以俟耳！”徐顿首曰：“某罪固大，然所为皆有裨于国用，任劳任怨，久已负不白冤，兹幸福星照临，昭雪知有日矣。”公曰：“尔试言之。”徐乃强辩饰非，条往事，凡公之所谓罪者，徐悉不居罪而居功，有经有权，娓娓动听。公不觉转怒为喜，怃然曰：“人言未足信，有以夫！”自是视徐如左右手，事无巨细，咸委之。明年，公内调，主人始出。徐别去，某感泣曰：“先生生死骨肉，恩莫大焉。十万金何足以长者，愿五倍以为寿。”徐力辞。某遽命辇金于其家，而以仆马送徐归。徐遂搆甲第，置沃产，日益以富。后数岁，某死，子嗣弗振，徐竟代其职云。

    Explanation

    672

    The official rebuked him, saying, "Your wickedness is beyond count, even if all your hair were pulled out! Now I will thoroughly investigate your crimes, so you may await punishment with your neck exposed!" Xu prostrated himself and said, "My crimes are indeed great, but all I have done has benefited the state. I have endured toil and hardship without complaint and have long borne an unjust accusation. Now, with the auspicious star shining upon me, I know the day of clearing my name is near." The official said, "Tell me about it." Xu then vigorously defended himself, recounting past events. For every crime the official had accused him of, Xu claimed he had instead performed a service. He spoke of both the established and the flexible, his words flowing persuasively. The official could not help but turn his anger into joy and said thoughtfully, "What people say is not to be trusted; there is reason for this!" From then on, he regarded Xu as his right and left hand, entrusting him with all matters, no matter how great or small. The following year, the official was transferred to a new post, and the master was released. Xu departed, and the master wept, saying, "Sir, you are like my flesh and blood, no greater favor has been bestowed upon me. Ten thousand gold coins are not enough to repay you; I wish to repay you five times over." Xu refused with great effort. The master immediately ordered the transportation of gold to Xu's home and sent servants and horses to escort Xu back. Xu then built a mansion and acquired fertile land, becoming increasingly wealthy. Several years later, the master died, and his descendants did not flourish. Xu eventually took over the position.

    673

    厉鬼作祟
    　　
    　　吴江乡民某甲，偶自田间归，遇少妇艳妆独行陇畔，疑是近村人家，尾其后，将瞷其所之。约半里，妇手解裙带，张皇四顾，似将如厕者，适道旁有废屋，妇推扉入，甲遥伺之。良久不出，忽闻号救声，乃蹑足往窥，但见屋内一棺，其盖大启，中有物曳妇入，甲大骇疾呼，棺遽阖，惟露衣裾一幅在外。甲奔告村众，诉之官。官至其处，破棺验视，妇身已僵，一男子尸，面色如生，左手紧抱妇项，右手握人首一，鲜血淋漓。

    673

    A Malevolent Spirit Causes Mischief

    A villager from Wujiang, surnamed Jia, happened to return from the fields one day when he encountered a young woman dressed in bright colors walking alone by the roadside. Suspecting she was from a nearby village, he followed her, intending to spy on where she was going. After about half a mile, the woman untied her skirt with her hand, looked around anxiously, as if she was going to the toilet. By the side of the road, there happened to be a dilapidated house. The woman pushed open the door and entered. Jia waited from afar. She remained inside for a long time, and then suddenly, he heard a cry for help. He tiptoed to look and saw a coffin inside the house, its lid wide open. Something inside dragged the woman into the coffin. Jia was greatly frightened and shouted out, but the coffin suddenly closed, leaving only a piece of her skirt hanging outside. Jia ran to tell the villagers and reported the incident to the authorities. The officials arrived at the scene, broke open the coffin to inspect, and found that the woman's body was already stiff. Inside the coffin was a man's body, his face looking as if he were alive. His left hand tightly held the woman's neck, and his right hand held a human head, with blood flowing profusely.

    674

    询诸父老，云：“滇贾客死江城，旅榇寄此地，几三十年矣。”官令焚尸，而以人首埋他处。移牒滇省，问境内有无怪异。先是，大理府某县民，父死未殡。一日，闻棺内有格斗声，启而视之，则尸已失其首，亦诉之官。官正束手，得牒喜甚，遂令此人赴江省，掘取所葬之首，依稀可辨，果伊父也。迹其格斗之期，即村妇被难之日。案虽结，而疑终不可解。夫以鬼杀鬼，事已大奇；乃能取首于棺中，劫妇于白昼，万里之遥，一日而两逞其暴，斯盖厉气之所结欤？

    Explanation

    674

    Inquiry was made of the elders, who said: "A merchant from Yunnan Province died in a city along the Yangtze River and his coffin was left in this place for almost thirty years." The officials ordered the body to be cremated and the head to be buried elsewhere. They sent a document to Yunnan Province, inquiring if there were any strange occurrences within the province. Previously, a resident of a certain county in Dali Prefecture had a father who died but was not yet buried. One day, he heard sounds of fighting inside the coffin, opened it to inspect, and found that the body had lost its head. He also reported this to the officials. The officials were at a loss, but upon receiving the document, they were very pleased and ordered this person to go to the city along the Yangtze River to dig up the buried head. The head was vaguely distinguishable and indeed belonged to his father. Tracing the time of the struggle, it was the same day as when a village woman suffered misfortune. Although the case was concluded, the doubt remained unresolved. That a ghost could kill another ghost is already a great marvel; yet it could take a head from a coffin and abduct a woman in broad daylight, traveling thousands of miles in one day to commit such acts of violence, surely this must be the result of malevolent energy?

    675

    金圣叹
    　　
    　　吾宗圣叹先生，绝世聪明，过目成诵。然放诞不羁，视青紫如儿戏，入泮未几，旋以六等被黜。第二年，仍以冠军获隽，如是者非一度矣。既而学使者法公海岁试苏郡，先生信笔直挥，顷刻脱稿，即以呈公。公展阅，见文体诡异。佶屈謷牙，微哂曰：“好秀才，姑退。”先生揖而进曰：“禀问大宗师，生员出署回寓道中，设遇美妇人，观者是乎？不观者是乎？”公亦不怒，徐应曰：“一看，君子；再看，小人。”先生又揖曰：“谨奉教。”乃昂然出，意中以为必又列六等也。明日，公坐堂上，独判先生为四等，即日发落，将重施夏楚。广文以其名士，为之缓颊，公弗许；士子数十人亦同声吁请，公愈怒，立召先生至。先生始惧，惶悚伏地，众亦知其不免。

    Explanation

    675

    My esteemed cousin, the renowned critic and essayist Jin Shengtan, possessed extraordinary intelligence and a remarkable memory, able to recite what he had read after a single glance. However, he was unrestrained and unconventional, regarding official rank and status as mere child's play. He entered the school (泮宫) for a short time but was soon dismissed after being ranked sixth. In the following year, he once again won first place in the imperial examinations, and such occurrences were not rare. Later, when the examiner, Officer Fāgōng Hǎi, conducted the annual examination in Suzhou, Mr. Jin wrote freely and without hesitation, finishing his essay quickly. He immediately presented it to the examiner. Upon reading it, the examiner found the writing style to be bizarre and convoluted. With a slight smile, he said, "Well done, young scholar, please step back." Mr. Jin bowed and approached, respectfully asking, "May I ask the Chief Examiner, if a candidate leaving the examination hall and returning to his residence encounters a beautiful woman, is it appropriate to look at her, or not?" The examiner remained calm and replied, "One look is the act of a gentleman; a second look is the act of a petty man." Mr. Jin bowed again and said, "I respectfully accept your instruction." He then left with a proud demeanor, thinking he would surely be ranked sixth again. The next day, the examiner sat on the main seat and alone judged Mr. Jin to be fourth rank, deciding the outcome immediately and preparing to severely punish him with the cane. The local school official, recognizing Mr. Jin as a distinguished scholar, interceded on his behalf, but the examiner refused. Dozens of candidates also collectively pleaded, but the examiner became even angrier, immediately summoning Mr. Jin. Mr. Jin began to fear, trembling and kneeling down in despair, and everyone knew that he could not escape punishment.

    676

    公一见，颜色忽霁，命左右曳起，曰：“余在京师，慕子名久矣。昨见奇搆，诚知天才，然国家名器，岂宜玩弄？牢骚之气，君子贵有以养之。子能痛改前辄，余之愿也。余岂真忍以子殿多士耶？”因于袖中出全榜，而弁冕实先生名。于是先生感悔，伏地大哭曰：“士得一知己，可以不恨。某虽不敏，请事斯语矣！”自此，公遂与先生为莫逆交。后数岁，先生以哭庙被收，弃市之日，作家信托狱卒寄妻子。临刑，大呼曰：“杀头，至痛也！灭族，至惨也！圣叹无意得此，呜呼哀哉！然而快哉！”遂引颈受戮。狱卒以信呈官，官疑其必有谤语，启缄视之，上书曰：“字付大儿看，盐菜与黄豆同吃，大有胡桃滋味。此法一传，我无遗憾矣！”官大笑日：“金先生死且侮人。”

    Explanation

    676

    The official, upon seeing him, suddenly brightened in expression and had those around him lift him up, saying, "I have long admired your name in the capital. I saw your extraordinary work yesterday and truly recognize your talent. However, the prestige of the state should not be trifled with. The feelings of dissatisfaction, a gentleman should cultivate them properly. If you can deeply regret and correct your past mistakes, that would be my wish. How could I truly bear to place you last among many scholars?" He then took out the full list from his sleeve, with the gentleman's name prominently featured. The gentleman, moved and repentant, fell to the ground and wept, saying, "A scholar can have no regrets with one true friend. Though I am not particularly wise, I will follow these words!" From then on, the official became the gentleman's closest and most harmonious friend. Several years later, the gentleman was arrested for participating in a temple mourning ritual. On the day of his execution, he wrote a letter and entrusted it to a prison guard to deliver to his family. As he faced the executioner, he shouted, "Beheading is the utmost pain! Annihilation of the clan is the utmost tragedy! I had no intention of meeting this fate, alas, how sad! Yet, how exhilarating!" He then extended his neck to receive his death. The prison guard presented the letter to the official, who suspected it must contain slanderous words. Upon opening it, he read, "Write this to my eldest son: eat salted vegetables with soybeans, and it has the flavor of walnuts. Once this method is passed down, I will have no regrets!" The official laughed and said, "Even in death, Mr. Jin mocks me."

    677

    玉兔
    　　
    　　浙江衢州某村王老媪者，贫无所赖，临溪结草庐而居。日为人浣衣，藉以糊口。溪边有巨石一方，每捣衣于上，既历年岁，石遂光润可鉴。忽有江西大贾过其地，愿以千金购之。媪喜讶兼集，知必有异，因请益无厌。贾曰：“囊中资不敷，俟我至馀杭更益千金，重来交易耳。”订期而别。媪思宝物而慢藏，未免诲盗，乃倩人运石于床下，守护惟谨。至期，贾匆匆至村，急询石所在。媪告以故，贾顿足不已，连呼可惜，曰：“我前日在数里外望见村有宝气，踪迹至此，识石中有活兔一，能吸日精月华，系希世珍，是以不靳重值。今来此地，正怪宝气已散，孰意为尔闭置幽室，三光莫睹，兔必饿死矣！”媪犹未深信，贾立召石工剖之，果得小玉兔，大如鼠，洁白无比。媪始神丧色沮，自恨福薄。以兔赠贾，贾仍予百金，俾作终老之资焉。

    Explanation

    677

    A Jade Rabbit

    In a village near Quzhou, Zhejiang, there lived an elderly woman named Wang, who was destitute and had no means of support. She lived by a stream in a thatched hut, earning a living by washing clothes for others. By the stream, there was a large stone where she often beat her clothes. Over the years, the stone became so smooth and shiny that it could be used as a mirror. One day, a wealthy merchant from Jiangxi passed by and offered to buy the stone for a thousand gold pieces. The old woman was filled with joy and surprise, suspecting that there must be something unusual about the stone, and asked for an even higher price. The merchant said, "I don't have enough money with me now, but I will return after going to Yuhang to bring another thousand gold pieces and complete the transaction." They set a date and parted ways. The old woman thought that hiding such a treasure carelessly would inevitably invite thieves, so she asked someone to move the stone under the bed and guarded it with utmost care. When the day arrived, the merchant hurried to the village and urgently asked about the stone's whereabouts. The old woman explained the situation, and the merchant stamped his feet in frustration, calling it a pity, and said, "I saw a village with an aura of treasure a few miles away the other day, and I followed the trail here, discovering a live rabbit inside the stone. This rabbit can absorb the essence of the sun and moon and is a rare treasure of the world, which is why I was willing to pay a high price. Now that I have come here, I am surprised to find that the aura of treasure has dissipated, and I never thought that you would seal it in a dark room, depriving it of the three lights, and the rabbit must surely starve to death!" The old woman still did not fully believe him, so the merchant immediately called a stonemason to cut open the stone, and indeed, they found a small jade rabbit, about the size of a mouse, as white and pure as snow. The old woman was then filled with despair and regretted her thin fortune. She gave the rabbit to the merchant, who still gave her a hundred gold pieces to serve as a means of support for the rest of her life.

    678

    继来禅师
    　　
    　　康熙间，继来禅师卓锡灵岩山寺，能知过去未来事。一日升座说法，远近男女环听。师遍观大众，忽指某甲谓曰：“子神色不详，报前世冤者至矣，其速治后事。”甲大惧，恳求禳解之方。师曰：“束草为人，遍体悉以面裹，着衣裳卧子床上，家人匿他室中，必有所睹。然此仅济一时厄耳。”甲辞归。届期如言制备，日既午，瞥见巨犬来自门外，狰狞可畏，突入甲舍，登床啮草人为齑粉，咆哮而去。次日，甲往谢师。师颦蹙曰：“一难虽过，第神色愈晦，仍不能免。”甲又长跪求救。

    Explanation

    678

    Master Jìlái

    　　During the reign of Emperor Kangxi, Master Jìlái settled in Lingyan Mountain Monastery, possessing the ability to know past and future events. One day, he ascended the dais to deliver a sermon, and people from far and near gathered to listen. The master surveyed the crowd and suddenly pointed to a certain person, saying, "Your appearance is ominous, the one who seeks revenge from your past life has arrived. Quickly prepare for your afterlife." The person was greatly frightened and earnestly sought a remedy to ward off the calamity. The master said, "Tie straw into a human form, cover the entire body with dough, dress it in clothes, and place it on your bed. Have your family hide in another room, and they will surely witness something. However, this will only help for a short time." The person then returned home. On the appointed day, as instructed, they prepared the straw figure. By noon, they suddenly saw a fierce dog come from outside, terrifying in appearance. It rushed into the person's house, leaped onto the bed, and gnawed the straw figure into powder, then howled and left. The next day, the person went to thank the master. The master frowned and said, "One difficulty has passed, but your appearance has become even more ominous, and you still cannot escape your fate." The person then knelt for a long time, begging for help.

    679

    师曰：“大数万难逃遁，我既心动，当全子尸。后月某日，子可同家众诣寺，并携二瓮来，我自有法。”甲又涕泣归。届期，黎明即至。师以二瓮对合，置大殿之中，使甲趺坐于内，外用朱符封之。师自坐佛前蒲团上，而命众侍立身后。日既午，倏有长蛇丈馀自屋而下，眼灼灼如电，先入殿内，张吻咋舌，若欲犯师，师以袖挥之。蛇遽出户，绕瓮盘旋，首触尾击，更向合口处喷以毒气，良久，蜿蜒而去。师乃命众启视，甲气已绝，身肿如泡，七窍尽流血。于是妻孥大哭，以棺殓之。

    Explanation

    679

    The master said, "Fate is hard to escape, even for those who are aware of it. Since my heart has moved, I will preserve your body. On a certain day next month, you may bring your family to the temple, and bring two jars with you. I have my own method." Jia wept and returned home. On the appointed day, he arrived at dawn. The master placed the two jars together, set them in the main hall, and made Jia sit cross-legged inside, sealing the outside with red talismans. The master sat on a mat in front of the Buddha, and ordered the others to stand behind him. When the sun was at noon, suddenly a snake over a zhang long came down from the house, its eyes bright like lightning. It first entered the hall, opened its mouth and hissed, as if it wanted to attack the master. The master waved his sleeve, and the snake quickly went out the door, winding around the jars, head touching tail, striking, and spraying poison gas at the joint. After a while, it went away winding. The master then ordered the others to open and look. Jia's breath had stopped, his body swelled like a bubble, and all seven orifices bled. Then his wife and children cried, and they buried him in a coffin.

    680

    阎和尚
    　　
    　　山东有阎和尚者，青州人。披缁后，云游四方，十年始返，至济南千佛山寺中栖身。主僧见其秽垢弗洁，令供水火之役。终日默无一语，即语，亦多诡异，人莫能解。身衣棉衲，寒暑不更。有时于灶下执爨，倦欲睡，即以两足伸置火中代薪，听其自爇，而竟曲肱为枕，安然入梦。汤既沸，爇犹不止。众怪而视之，见其状，惊甚，大声呼唤，阎乃徐徐起，拂去足上灶煤，皮肉毫无损坏。一夕，人静更深，阎忽于床上大哭。

    Explanation

    680

    A Monk Named Yan

    In Shandong, there was a monk named Yan from Qingzhou. After becoming a monk and donning the black robe, he wandered the world for ten years before returning to the Qianfo Mountain Temple in Jinan, where he resided. The head monk, seeing his filth and lack of cleanliness, assigned him the task of fetching water and tending the fire. He remained silent throughout the day, and when he spoke, it was often strange and mysterious, leaving others unable to understand him. He wore a cotton robe and showed no reaction to the cold or heat. Sometimes, while cooking at the stove, he would become tired and want to sleep, and he would stretch his feet into the fire to replace the wood, letting it burn by itself, and then bend his arm as a pillow, falling into a peaceful sleep. Even after the water boiled, the fire continued to burn. The other monks found this strange and looked at him, greatly surprised, and called out loudly. Yan slowly rose, brushed off the ashes from his feet, and his skin and flesh were not damaged at all. One night, when it was deep into the night and everyone was quiet, Yan suddenly wept loudly on his bed.

    681

    众问故，曰：“我母将死矣！”天明出门去。半月后即来，询其抵家之日，正伊母回首之时。自此，主僧免其役，稍加敬礼。郡人闻名，亦渐诣寺瞻拜。阎惟闭目趺坐，不与酬答。或有病而向其乞药者，阎辄取地下黄土，和唾为丸，附其人服之，病亦顿愈；无缘则虽求弗与也。既而远近皆知寺有活佛，奔竞若狂。时先君知府事，指为妖僧，命驱逐出境。属吏同声谏阻，先君愈怒，且将重惩。阎闻之，曰：“金大爷是君子，我当避彼。”明日遂飘然远遁，绝不复至。

    Explanation

    681

    Many people asked him for the reason, and he said, "My mother is about to die!" At dawn, he left the temple. Half a month later, he returned, and when he asked about the day he arrived home, it was the same day his mother passed away. From then on, the temple master exempted him from his duties and showed him slightly more respect. People from the prefecture heard of his fame and gradually began to visit the temple to pay respects. Yan only closed his eyes and sat in the lotus position, not responding to anyone. Some who were ill asked for medicine from him, and Yan always took yellow earth from the ground, mixed it with his spit to make pills, and gave it to the person to take. The illness was immediately cured; if they had no connection, even if they asked, he would not give it. Soon after, people near and far knew that the temple had a living Buddha and rushed to the temple like mad. At that time, my father was the prefect and pointed him out as an evil monk, ordering him to be expelled from the country. The subordinates unanimously advised against it, and my father became even angrier and would impose severe punishment. Yan heard it and said, "The Honorable Sir is a gentleman, I should avoid him." The next day, he lightly fled far away and never came back.

    682

    犬妖
    　　
    　　嘉兴章某，访所亲于中州任所，因留官舍。所居屋东厢系主人收贮食物处，户常扃。一日，章独坐，闻厢房咋咋作声，试往窥视，见有大黑犬，以首顶木几置墙隅，更顶二小几垒其上，乃跃登至巅，恣啖梁上所悬熟肉。啖既尽，下，复顶木几置故处，穿败壁而出。章异之，次日，以告主人，适是犬在阶下，闻言，怒目视章，似怀怒意。至夕，章既寝，犬忽推扉潜入，口衔索丈许，至章卧榻前，以索约榻之长短广狭，量度再四，仍衔索去。章心悸，假寐以俟。

    Explanation

    682

    Dog Spirit

    Zhang from Jiaxing visited a relative at their place of duty in the Central Plains and stayed in the official residence. The eastern wing of the house where he lived was where the owner stored food, and the door was usually locked. One day, while sitting alone, Zhang heard scratching sounds coming from the厢房. He went to peek and saw a large black dog using its head to lift a wooden table and place it in a corner of the wall. The dog then lifted two smaller tables and stacked them on top of the larger one, jumped up to the top, and freely ate the cooked meat hanging from the beams. After finishing the meat, the dog jumped down, lifted the wooden table back to its original place, and exited by breaking through a broken wall. Zhang was astonished by this, and the next day, he told the owner about it. Just as he spoke, the dog was standing at the bottom of the steps, glared at Zhang with angry eyes, as if harboring resentment. That evening, after Zhang had gone to sleep, the dog suddenly pushed open the door and sneaked in, holding a rope about a zhang long in its mouth. It went to Zhang's bed, measured the length and width of the bed with the rope, and did so repeatedly before taking the rope away. Zhang was terrified and pretended to sleep, waiting for what might happen next.

    683

    未几，犬又如前状，章乃披衣起，尾其后踪迹之。至廐旁菜圃中，见犬于隙上布索为围，四周方广与榻相若，以两足挖土成穴，深几二尺。盖欲致章殒命，且将拽其尸瘗于此地也。章大骇，奔告主人，命缚犬而杀之。呜呼！犬尚若此，何况于人？怨毒之不可结也！甚矣夫！里乘子日：予闻小笏舍人生平酷嗜吟诗，笔意在白陆之间。归田后，与沈归愚、彭芝庭两尚书结社酬唱。尝作《闺怨》诗，有“入梦幽情未明白，对花孤影又昏黄”之句，两公皆赏其裁对工巧，得未曾有。其全稿俱经两公评订，未付手民，遽煨燹，惜哉！

    Explanation

    683

    Not long after, the dog again acted as it had before. Zhang then put on his clothes and got up, following behind it to track its movements. He arrived at the vegetable garden beside the stable and saw the dog setting up ropes on the gap to form a circle. The surrounding area was square and wide, similar to a bed, and the dog used its two feet to dig a hole, deep almost two feet. It seemed to want to cause Zhang's death and would drag his body to bury it here. Zhang was greatly frightened, ran to tell the owner, and was ordered to bind and kill the dog. Alas! If a dog is like this, how much more so for humans? The grudge and hatred cannot be resolved! How severe it is! Li Chengzi said: I heard that Xiao Hu's life was deeply fond of reciting poetry, with a writing style between Bai Juyi and Lu You. After returning to the countryside, he formed a society with Shěn Guīyú and Pénɡ Zhītíng, the two Shàngshū, to exchange poetry. Once, he wrote a poem titled "Guilt of the Inner Chamber," with the lines "Entering a dream, the hidden emotions are not clear; facing flowers, a lonely shadow is dim." Both praised his skillful composition, which was unprecedented. His entire manuscript was reviewed and edited by both, but not yet given to the printers, and was suddenly burned, alas!

    684

    里乘卷十
    　　
    　　记粤寇倡乱之始
    　　
    　　粤寇倡乱之始，传闻不一，今据近是者记之。初，粤西浔州府桂平县某乡有古刹，芜废无僧主持，乡人士遂设为公所。中旧供关圣、观音诸神塑像。因共佣一人居守，奉香火兼司启钥、扫除等役。土豪韦镇，故虎而冠，素惑于天主教，崇奉甚严。其党三千馀人，议建天主堂，因嗾韦至刹，摈诸神像，改祀天主，佣者恐干众怒，拒不许。

    Explanation

    684

    Volume Ten of Li Cheng

    The Beginning of the Rebellion Led by the Cantonese Bandits

    The beginning of the rebellion led by the Cantonese bandits is reported in various ways. According to the most recent and reliable reports, it is recorded as follows: Initially, in a certain village in Gui Ping County, Xun Zhou Prefecture, Guangxi, there was an ancient temple that had become overgrown and neglected, with no monks in charge. The local people then converted it into a community center. Inside, there were statues of Guan Yu (the God of War) and Guanyin (the Bodhisattva of Compassion). They hired a person to live and guard the temple, who was responsible for offering incense and fire and managing the opening and closing of the doors, as well as cleaning. Wei Zhen, a local tyrant known for his ferocity, was deeply influenced by Catholicism and worshipped it strictly. He had over three thousand followers who planned to build a Catholic church. They incited Wei Zhen to go to the temple, where they removed all the statues of the deities and replaced them with images of the Christian God. The hired person, fearing public anger, refused to allow this change.

    685

    韦诃之曰：“尔敢抗吾耶！尔但听吾命，脱众有议之者，有吾在，不预尔事！”佣者惧其横暴，不得已从之。无何，众至刹，见之，果大哗；及诘佣者知其由，益怒，群掷天主于地蹴毁之，仍供诸神如初。韦之党耳其事，报韦，韦怒往与众理，语多悖谬。众窃议曰：“察韦氏子所言所行，殆病狂，必及于祸。吾侪须早为之计，先发制之，庶免波及乡镇。”固有巡检，才颇明干，众诣，备诉崖末，乞发弓弁往擒韦及其党之魁。巡检谓：“非细故，位卑恐干上官议孟浪好事；且韦党甚众，如不能就擒，更速激其变，必须计万全乃可行。

    Explanation

    685

    Wei said to the hired worker, "Dare you oppose me? Just listen to my command. If the crowd has any objections, with me here, it will not involve you." The hired worker, fearing Wei's arrogance, reluctantly complied. Soon, the crowd arrived at the temple, saw the situation, and indeed, there was great commotion. When they questioned the hired worker and learned the reason, they became even more angry. They all threw the idol to the ground and trampled it to pieces, and continued to worship the other gods as before. Those in Wei's faction heard about the matter and reported it to Wei. Wei, enraged, went to the crowd to explain, but his words were mostly absurd and unreasonable. The crowd privately discussed, "Examining what Wei's son said and did, it seems he is mentally deranged and will surely bring disaster upon himself. We must take early action, act first to control the situation, to prevent the situation from spreading to the towns." There was a local official who was quite capable and fair. The crowd approached him, fully explaining the situation, and requested that he send armed personnel to capture Wei and the leader of his faction. The official said, "This is not a trivial matter. My position is low, and I fear that acting rashly might incur the displeasure of my superiors. Moreover, Wei's faction is very large. If they cannot be captured, it will only hasten the outbreak of violence. We must plan thoroughly before taking action."

    686

    ”众曰：“公第声言奉郡密檄，访缉左道惑众者，不须公为力，吾侪自有计缚献麾下。然必公亲往，方能有济，否则姑息遗患，为不摧，为蛇奈何！异日事发，公亦不能辞咎。”巡检深然其言。爰率众往，乘其不备，遂擒韦并其党十馀人。星夜槛驰赴县，同谒县令，委曲白所事。令素选懦，闻众言，噤不敢声，意颇嫌众等多事。比谋诸幕宾，谓宜请郡守主裁；令如言谒守，守亦茫无灼见，惟命暂将韦等下于狱，待谳得左证，再如律处治。韦等既下狱，令殊不以为意。

    Explanation

    686

    The crowd said, "Simply declare that you are following a secret order from the prefecture to investigate and arrest those who use false doctrines to deceive the people. We do not need your assistance; we have our own plan to capture and present them to you. However, only if you personally go will it be successful. Otherwise, indulgence will leave a lasting problem; if left unchecked, it will grow into a monster; what then of the snake! In the future, if the matter comes to light, you too will not be able to escape blame." The patrol inspector deeply agreed with their words. He then led the crowd to the location, taking advantage of their unpreparedness, and they captured Wei and over a dozen of his followers. At night, they transported the captives in a cage-like vehicle to the county, where they met with the magistrate together and explained the situation in detail. The magistrate, who was usually timid, upon hearing the crowd's words, remained silent and dared not speak, feeling that the crowd was overstepping their bounds. He then consulted his advisors, who suggested that he should ask the prefect to make the final decision. The magistrate followed their advice and went to see the prefect, who also had no clear opinion, but ordered that Wei and his associates be temporarily detained in jail, to await further evidence before proceeding according to the law. After Wei and his associates were imprisoned, the magistrate paid no further attention to the matter.

    687

    既而韦在狱上书讼冤，毅然以崇奉天主教自任，不少讳；且言世上天地、君亲、祖宗、神祇皆听命于天主，可惜世人不察，吾将大倡其教，普救天下。并求释放出狱，宜反坐巡检等人结党畔道罪名云云。满纸邪说，令置不理。韦之党知令庸弱无能，将谋劫狱。乡人士侦知之，亟白令，乞为备；因谓韦所上书即可据为左证，速治其罪，无使滋蔓，令但笑谢之。韦之党闻乡人士所言，怒甚，遂劫韦等于狱。首报怨于乡人士，大肆屠掠，幸免者十之一二。此倡乱之始也。

    Explanation

    687

    Afterward, Wei, while in prison, submitted a petition to clear his name, firmly taking upon himself the responsibility of promoting the worship of the Lord without any concealment. He also stated that all things in the world, including heaven and earth, rulers, ancestors, and deities, are subject to the Lord, and it is regrettable that the world does not understand. He declared that he would widely promote this teaching and save the world, and requested to be released from prison, suggesting that the same crime of conspiring against the law should be applied to the patrol officials and others involved. The entire petition was filled with heretical teachings, and the magistrate ignored it. Wei's followers, knowing that the magistrate was weak and incompetent, planned to storm the prison. The local gentry discovered this and urgently informed the magistrate, asking for preparations. They argued that Wei's petition could be used as evidence and urged swift punishment to prevent the situation from spreading, but the magistrate merely laughed and thanked them. Hearing what the local gentry said, Wei's followers became very angry and stormed the prison to rescue Wei. They first vented their anger on the local gentry, committing widespread slaughter and plunder, sparing only a few. This was the beginning of the uprising.

    688

    记粤寇渠魁事迹
    　　
    　　洪秀全，蜒魁也。藉名捕鱼，出没海中，专务劫掠。其党散布港汊，一遇商舶经过，吹唇声号，各港闻之，顷刻蜂集。劫得货财，悉先上之洪，而后瓜分。贾客罹其害者，指不胜屈。洪素与相者杨秀清善。杨先业儒，应童子试，屡黜于有司，穷乏不能自存，遂弃儒卖卜，以资糊口；性极狡黠，能察言辨色，臆断吉凶祸福，多奇中，人颇神之。缘嗜博，博又辄负，赖洪时济其急，心德之而欲有以报。爰时以非分谄之，谋不轨。尝与洪之党饮酣，谈天下形势及用兵之法，瞭如指掌；并谬称洪相贵不可言，然舍己不胜卧龙任。洪党信其言，欣述于洪，皆顿首称贺，洪益自负。会韦逆劫狱倡乱，恐势孤不足抗大兵，知洪有异志，其党乃往说洪，奉为伪天王；以杨秀清为军师，称伪东王，韦自为大将军，僭称北王；又以萧朝贵为伪西王、冯云山为伪南王。萧、冯两贼事迹不得其详，或曰皆海洋剧盗耳。

    Explanation

    688

    The Leaders of the Guangxi Rebels

    Hong Xiuquan was a leader of the bandits from Guangxi. Pretending to fish, he wandered in the sea, devoting himself to robbery. His followers were scattered throughout the river mouths, and whenever a merchant ship passed, they would blow a signal through their lips. Upon hearing the signal, the bandits from each port would gather instantly. After robbing the cargo, they would first present all the loot to Hong Xiuquan, and then divide it among themselves. Merchants who suffered from their actions were too numerous to count. Hong Xiuquan had a longstanding friendship with Yang Xiuqing, who was a fortune teller. Yang Xiuqing was originally a scholar, but he failed the imperial examinations multiple times, falling into poverty and unable to support himself. He then abandoned his studies and took up fortune-telling to make a living. Yang was extremely cunning, skilled at reading people's expressions, and could often predict good or bad fortunes with surprising accuracy, which impressed many people. Due to his love for gambling, he often lost money, but Hong Xiuquan would help him in times of need, and Yang was grateful and wanted to repay him. At one point, Yang flattered Hong inappropriately, plotting rebellion. Once, while drinking with Hong's followers, Yang discussed the state of the world and military strategies, showing a thorough understanding. He also falsely praised Hong, saying that Hong was of such high status that it could not be expressed, but that Yang himself was not fit for the role of "Sleeping Dragon." Hong's followers believed him and excitedly reported this to Hong, who bowed and congratulated him, becoming even more self-confident. Coincidentally, Wei Changhui, another rebel leader, broke out of prison and started a rebellion. Fearing that his forces were too weak to resist the imperial army, Wei learned of Hong's secret intentions and sent his followers to persuade Hong, who was then proclaimed as the "False Heavenly King." Yang Xiuqing was made the military advisor, known as the "False Eastern King," while Wei became the "Great General" and usurped the title of "Northern King." Xiao Chaogui and Feng Yunshan were also proclaimed as the "False Western King" and "False Southern King," respectively. The details of Xiao and Feng's backgrounds are unclear, but some say they were all notorious pirates.

    689

    石达开
    　　
    　　石达开者，以财雄一方，素慕游侠，好结纳而不择其人。门下食客实繁有徒，类多无赖。石居恒惟日与健儿驰马较射、击梃舞槊以为乐。先是，其乡有峻岭，为剧盗所据，劫取过往财物，无幸脱者。有闽客挟重资经此，闻而心悸，久耳石名，因投刺踵谒，备陈衷曲，乞庇护。石怜而许之，款留少住，将择健儿卫送过岭。盗闻之，怒甚，率其党百人径至石家，欲图撺夺。石闻盗至，令开门召入，告之曰：“壮士之所欲者货财也。第念闽客挟重资抛离乡井，以谋得什一，意良苦。今壮士不谅其衷，将尽攘为己有，彼则本之不存，利将焉望？仆也滋不忍，敢为缓颊。”因准闽客资约计五千金，乃解囊如数列金于几，谓曰：“聊备不腆，敬献壮士，代客请命。

    Explanation

    689

    Shi Dakai was a man who was wealthy and influential in his region, and he had always admired the way of the wandering knights-errant. He enjoyed making connections and associations without discriminating among people. His retinue of guests and followers was numerous and substantial, most of them being ruffians or good-for-nothing. Shi Dakai spent his days riding horses, competing in archery, and practicing martial arts for amusement.

    Previously, there was a steep mountain in his hometown, occupied by notorious bandits who robbed travelers and no one escaped. There was a Fujianese traveler carrying a large sum of money passing through, who heard about it and was filled with fear. He had long heard of Shi Dakai's name, so he presented a visiting card and followed to pay respects, fully explaining his situation and begging for protection. Shi Dakai felt pity and agreed to help him, keeping him around for a short while and planning to select strong men to escort him across the mountain.

    The bandits heard about it and became very angry, leading a hundred of their men directly to Shi Dakai's home, intending to seize the opportunity. Shi Dakai heard the bandits were coming and ordered the door to be opened to invite them in, telling them, "What the brave ones want is wealth. But consider that the Fujianese traveler carried a large sum of money away from his hometown, in hopes of gaining a small profit, and his intention is very difficult. Now the brave ones do not understand his heart, they intend to take everything for themselves. If the original source is lost, how can there be any profit? I cannot bear to see this, I dare to intercede on his behalf."

    He therefore agreed to pay the Fujianese traveler about 5,000 taels of silver, took out the money, and placed it on the table, saying, "It is just a modest offering, I respectfully offer it to the brave ones, to plead on behalf of the traveler."

    690

    倘壮士矜而宥之，即不啻身受其赐。”群盗相顾叹曰：“人言石公重义轻财，信然！吾侪所为，殆非人类！今重违公命，但令闽客就道毋恐。然公所惠丰腆，受之有愧，敢辞。”石大悦，治席为闽客祖饯，兼酌群盗。酒酣，彼此倾吐肝胆，意气契洽，相见恨晚。席散，闽客拜谢而别；群盗兴辞，石仍出前金殷勤投赠，推让再三，始受其半。群盗既归，感石，谋有以报之。值石初度，因备奇珍异宝、锦绣牲醴之属往为寿。石大合乐，宴客三日，盗亦在座。有与石挟嗛者，密报邑令，谓石时藏盗于家，恐不免为地方之害。令正患盗，闻言，亟督兵役驰往，围其宅，并石与盗尽擒之，禁于狱中。石与杨秀清故为莫逆交，尝贷杨金而不责偿。杨既为伪东王，欲报石德。会石下狱，遂劫出之，称为伪翼王云。

    Explanation

    690

    If a brave man were to pity and pardon him, it would be no less than receiving a personal favor." The bandits looked at each other and sighed, saying, "People say that Shi Gong values righteousness over wealth; indeed so! What we have done is probably not human behavior! Now we must go against your order, but just let the Fujianese guest proceed without fear. However, your generous gift makes us feel ashamed to accept it, dare we refuse?" Shi Gong was greatly pleased, and prepared a feast to see the Fujianese guest off, also drinking with the bandits. When the wine was flowing freely, they poured out their hearts to each other, forming a harmonious and congenial relationship, regretting that they had met too late. After the feast, the Fujianese guest bowed and thanked before leaving; the bandits rose and took leave, and Shi Gong still gave them half of the previous gold generously and earnestly, refusing repeatedly before they accepted it. The bandits returned home, grateful to Shi Gong, and planned to repay him. On Shi Gong's birthday, they brought valuable treasures, fine silk, and sacrificial offerings to celebrate his life. Shi Gong held a grand feast, entertaining guests for three days, with the bandits also in attendance. Someone who harbored resentment against Shi Gong secretly reported to the local official, claiming that Shi Gong was harboring bandits at home, fearing that it would not be without harm to the local area. The official, who was concerned about the bandits, heard this and immediately ordered troops to rush to Shi Gong's residence, surrounding his house and capturing both Shi Gong and the bandits, imprisoning them in jail. Shi Gong had been a close friend of Yang Xiuqing, having once lent him money without demanding repayment. Yang, who was the Eastern King, wanted to repay Shi Gong's kindness. When Shi Gong was imprisoned, Yang rescued him and gave him the title of the False Winged King.

    691

    里乘子曰：粤寇之变，虽曰天运使然，而人事究亦难逃其责也。我闻金田啸聚，其初党羽不过三百馀人，向使地方有司果能设法化导，格其邪心，固善；万一若曹执迷不悟，即乘其势尚未张，一鼓歼之，消患未形，亦不为虐。所谓去莠安良，亦贤有司之责也。乃计不出此，卒使星星曤火，燎及昆冈；涓涓氿泉，荡成巨浸。迨顺流下窜，窃据金陵，十馀年来，沿江一带民无完居，屠毒生灵不可数计。

    Explanation

    691

    Lǐchéngzi said: The Taiping Rebellion, though it is said to be the result of the course of heaven, human affairs ultimately cannot escape responsibility. I heard that when the Taiping first gathered in Jintian, their number of followers was no more than several hundred. If the local officials had truly been able to devise methods to guide and transform them, correcting their misguided thoughts, that would have been good; but if they stubbornly refused to understand, then taking advantage of the situation before it became powerful, eliminating them in one decisive action, and eliminating the problem before it manifested, would also not be excessive. What is meant by removing weeds and protecting the good is also the duty of capable officials. However, since such plans were not made, it ultimately allowed small, scattered fires to burn down the great mountains; small, trickling streams to become a vast flood.
