Those returning from campaigns were numerous, and some said that the man had obtained an official position and married again. The mother heard this and told Rui Xia, who also dismissed it as false. She then decided to go herself to find the man, asking the mother and uncle to accompany her. Following the current downstream, the boat docked at Jiujiang. There was an official boat next to theirs, and on a moonlit night, a banquet was held. When they were drinking to the point of intoxication, they suddenly heard the sound of a pipa, and a guest sang loudly. Rui Xia was suddenly reminded of her long-standing preference and listened intently. When the song ended, Rui Xia smiled and said to the mother and uncle, "The singer on the neighboring boat must be the man." The mother and uncle did not deeply believe her. Upon inquiry, they sent someone to investigate, and it turned out to be true.

    731

    盖生已屡应保荐，荐擢总戎，气象赫赫。闻蕊霞至，亟来拜母与舅。夫妻相见，悲喜交集。即请过舟，置酒为贺。蕊霞诘责生何频年杳无信息，生笑曰：“弃文就武，殊乘初志。恐不满卿意，故屡欲发书，辄赧然搁笔。”蕊霞笑曰：“男儿当为雄飞。与其从毛锥子老死牍下，曷若持长枪大戟，取斗大黄金印耶？君言强作解嘲，然心亦何忍也！”因历诉其蜚语。生揖谢曰：“是皆小生之罪也。然卿何以闻歌即知为小生耶？”蕊霞笑曰：“聪非师旷，侧听歌词，确有师承，故敢决其不谬。”生笑曰：“卿真小生之钟之期也。容解柄归田，具贽拜师，从事翰墨，以期偕老，可乎？”相与一笑。

    Explanation

    731

    盖生已被多次推荐晋升，最终被提拔为总戎，气势非凡。听说蕊霞到来，他急忙去拜见母亲和舅舅。夫妻相见，悲喜交加。随即邀请蕊霞上船，备酒庆祝。蕊霞责备盖生为何多年没有消息，盖生笑着说：“我放弃了文学转而投身武职，这与我最初的志向大相径庭。恐怕没有达到你的期望，所以我多次想写信，但总是因为害羞而搁笔。”蕊霞笑道：“男子汉应当像雄鹰一样飞翔。与其在文牍中终老，不如手持长枪大戟，夺取大金印。你的话虽是强作解嘲，但心里怎能不感到不忍呢！”于是详细讲述了那些谣言。盖生拱手道歉说：“这些都是我的过错。但你怎么一听歌就知道是我呢？”蕊霞笑道：“虽然我不如师旷聪明，但侧耳倾听歌词，确实有师承，因此敢断定不差。”盖生笑道：“你真是我的钟子期啊。让我辞官归隐，准备礼物拜师，专心从事文学，希望能与你白头偕老，可以吗？”两人相视一笑。

    732

    盖生所歌者，乃《塞上五更词》，固袭蕊霞前调，故闻之而即确信为生也。予爱两词俱妙，爰倩人钞稿而并录之。其《塞上五更词》曰：一更笳吹白日落，东营吹笳西吹角。交头耳语口号传，霹雳炮鸣严锁钥。鼓冬冬，一更逢，我所思兮愿早从。举头忽见初生月，默庆天山早挂弓。吁嗟一更兮更鼓捷，梅花椿杂蒺藜铁。二更赌酒欢笑言，蜂房出入门间去门。约束酣嬉禁博塞，连营灯火星点繁。柝阁阁，二更作，我所思兮毋自薄。领队银刀仗将才，要敦诗书说礼乐。吁嗟二更兮更柝催，占星椅剑登碉台。三更欹枕念乡井，金闺人孤铁衾冷。

    Explanation

    732

    The song that 盖 is singing is the "Five Watch Poem of the Frontier," certainly imitating the previous style of 蕊霞, so when heard, one immediately believes it to be the work of 生. I love both poems equally, so I then asked someone to copy the manuscript and record both together. The "Five Watch Poem of the Frontier" goes as follows:

    At the first watch, the trumpet sounds as the sun sets, the eastern camp blows the trumpet, and the western camp blows the horn. Whispering and passing on slogans, the thunderous cannon sounds, and the gates are locked tightly. The drum beats, it is the first watch, what I think of, I wish to follow soon. Lifting my head, I suddenly see the new moon, silently, I rejoice that the bow is hung early on the Tianshan mountains. Alas, the first watch, the drum beats swiftly, the plum blossoms and thorns are mixed with iron蒺藜.

    At the second watch, they bet on wine and laugh, the bees come and go from the entrance. Restraining themselves from excessive drinking and gambling, the lights of the camps are like scattered stars. The wooden clappers sound, it is the second watch, what I think of, do not belittle yourself. Leading with silver swords, the generals are skilled, emphasizing poetry, books, and the teachings of rites and music. Alas, the second watch, the clappers urge, observing the stars, sitting on the sword, and climbing the watchtower.

    At the third watch, leaning on the pillow, thinking of the hometown, the person in the golden chamber is alone, the iron quilt is cold.

    733

    刀头悬知忆藁砧，飞身上天占破镜。漏徐徐，三更余，我所思兮休踟躇。笑脱章缝换铠甲，执殳效命拚前躯。吁嗟三更兮更漏滴，手摸创瘢数上战绩。四更擐甲枕戈眠，故乡入梦衣锦旋。鼻声雷动震四壁，旗影在地月在天。筹属属，四更速，我所思兮心暗卜。封侯有命数不奇，纵非虎头亦食肉。吁嗟四更兮更筹清，海查夜巡叱小兵。[每队派一人稽察勤惰，谓之“海查。”又合营共派一人稽察各队，谓之“总海查”。]五更矛头争淅米，宰夫霍霍弄刀匕。约束千营共一呼，将军传令众士起。香漫漫，五更浅，我所思兮志莫殚。此生未系楼兰颈，叱咤风云誓不还。吁嗟五更兮更香炧，争剉生刍喂战马。

    Explanation

    733

    At the edge of the blade, I remember my duty; I swiftly ascend to the heavens to restore order.
    The water clock drips slowly, past the third watch; what I think, do not hesitate.
    With a smile, I discard my civilian clothes and don armor; I hold the spear and dedicate my life to the front lines.
    Ah, the third watch, the water clock drips; I touch my scars and count my victories.
    At the fourth watch, I don armor and sleep with a spear as a pillow; my hometown enters my dreams, and I return in glory.
    The sound of my breathing is like thunder, shaking the four walls; the flag's shadow lies on the ground, the moon hangs in the sky.
    The counting sticks click, the fourth watch is swift; what I think, my heart secretly divines.
    Being granted a marquisate is fate, nothing strange; even if not a tiger's head, I shall still eat meat.
    Ah, the fourth watch, the counting sticks are clear; the night patrol of the sea guard chides the soldiers.
    At the fifth watch, the spear tips compete in preparing rice; the cook skillfully wields the knife and spoon.
    The command unites a thousand camps in one shout; the general gives orders, and the soldiers rise.
    The incense lingers, the fifth watch is shallow; what I think, my ambition is endless.
    In this life, I have not yet bound the neck of Loulan; I command the wind and clouds, swearing never to return.
    Ah, the fifth watch, the incense fades; we compete in cutting fresh grass to feed the war horses.

    734

    里乘子曰：曩在蜀中，每听人谈某生与蕊霞一段因缘，无不交口艳羡。予曾赋《新婚别》七古一首，云：“左弧右悦双结束，夫婿封侯妾愿足。东劳西燕双别离，夫婿从军妾心悲。嫁时线厌五色丝，穿针为君缀征衣。牵衣赠语君识之，别离最苦新婚时。烟尘作恶遮花烛，昨日欢笑今日哭。一朝镜合缺月圆，检点画眉螺黛绿。”
    　　
    　　陈氏女
    　　
    　　咸丰丁巳春，金陵蔡小石廉访宗茂陈臬秦中，夫人由苏携眷赴任。舟舣虎邱，见岸上一乞丐女郎，年才及笄，临流掩袂而泣。颜色虽极蕉萃，而举止态度不失大家风范，似欲投河而趦趄不决者。夫人意甚矜怜，姑遣纪纲赒钱五百，并询邦族。女郎言为江苏陈姓，父官皖江，遭粤寇之变，举家离散，身被掳至扬州，后只身遁出。今求举家音耗不得，苦无所依，行将问诸水滨矣。纪纲返命。夫人又命询女郎曾字人也未，女郎面赬不答。夫人乃自登岸询之曰：“吾金陵人，与汝为江苏同乡。

    Explanation

    734

    Mr. Li Cheng said: "In the past, when I was in Sichuan, I often heard people talk about the relationship between a certain man and Rui Xia, and everyone would speak of it with admiration. I once wrote a seven-character ancient poem titled 'Newly Married Farewell,' which goes: 'The left bow and the right joy, both tied up, my husband is made a marquis, and my wish is fulfilled. The eastern labor and the western swallow, both parting, my husband goes to war, and my heart is filled with sorrow. When I was married, I was tired of the five-colored silk, I sewed the war clothes for you with the needle. I held your clothes and gave you a message, which you should remember, parting is most bitter when it happens right after the wedding. The smoke and dust of war obscure the wedding candles, yesterday's laughter, today's tears. One day, the broken mirror will be whole again, and the crescent moon will be full, check the green eyebrows and the painted eyebrows.'"

    Chen's Daughter

    In the spring of the Ding Si year of the Xianfeng era, Cai Xiaoshi, the Censor-General of Zongmiao, and Chen, the Inspector of Qin, were traveling to their new posts. Their wives, accompanied by their families, were traveling from Suzhou. The boat docked at Huqiu, where they saw a beggar girl on the shore, who was about to marry. She faced the river, covering her sleeves and weeping. Although her appearance was extremely emaciated, her manners and demeanor did not lose the grace of a noble lady, as if she wanted to jump into the river but hesitated. The lady felt very compassionate and sent a servant to give her 500 coins, and also inquired about her family and clan. The girl said she was a Chen from Jiangsu, whose father was an official in Anhui, and suffered the chaos caused by the Cantonese bandits, leading to the whole family being scattered. She was captured and taken to Yangzhou, and later escaped alone. Now, she could not find any news of her family, and was in great distress and had no place to rely on, so she was about to ask for help at the riverside. The servant returned with the message. The lady then asked the girl if she had ever been betrothed, but the girl blushed and did not answer. The lady then got off the boat and asked her, "I am from Nanjing, and you are from Jiangsu, we are fellow townspeople."

    735

    脱已字人，告我何害？既不得举家音耗，如夫家尚在，我亦可代汝侦访，资送汝往。”女郎察夫人意善，低声答曰：“幼字金陵蔡氏，今亦不知何若矣。”夫人因细审其祖父并舅名字，女郎一一称述。夫人研究良确，乃瞪目谛视，亟把女郎臂，大恸失声，曰：“然则汝是吾儿妇也。噫嘻！苦煞汝矣！”盖女郎实夫人所聘子妇。亟携登舟，沐浴更衣，容光媖焕。先是，粤寇犯皖，所掠妇女，择其尤者汇送至维扬，将备伪王妃嫔之选，贼党奉为禁脔，懔不敢犯。故女郎虽陷贼窟，幸免于辱。时公子适侍母赴任，为涓吉成礼，逑好甚敦。合卺之日，吴下荐绅、先生、文人、学士耳其事者，无不啧啧称美，不期而衣冠来贺者数百人。

    Explanation

    735

    If you have already been betrothed, what harm is there in telling me? Since we have no news of your family, if your husband's family is still alive, I can also help you find them and provide the means for you to go. The young lady, upon observing that the lady's intention was good, whispered softly, "I was betrothed to a family in Jinling, the Puis of the Zai clan. I do not know what has become of them now." The lady then carefully inquired about the names of her grandparents and uncles, and the young lady recounted them one by one. After thorough investigation, the lady suddenly looked intently at her, grabbed her arm, and burst into tears, saying, "So you are my daughter-in-law! Alas! How you must have suffered!" It turned out that the young lady was indeed the daughter-in-law that the lady had betrothed to her son. She quickly took her to the boat, had her bathe and change clothes, and her appearance became radiant. Before that, the Cantonese bandits had invaded Anhui, and the women they captured were selected for their beauty and sent to Wei Yang, where they were chosen to be the concubines of the false king. The bandits treated them as forbidden delicacies and dared not touch them. Thus, though the young lady had fallen into the bandits' lair, she was fortunate to have been spared from dishonor. At that time, the young master happened to be accompanying his mother on a trip to take up her post, and they arranged a proper wedding ceremony, which was very harmonious. On the day of the wedding, the scholars, gentlemen, literati, and students in Wu heard of the matter and all praised it highly, and unexpectedly, hundreds of scholars came to congratulate them.

    736

    里乘子曰：是年春，予适游吴。有友人招饮，席间皆津津乐道此事，以为美谈。座中一客忽冷笑曰：“此女久陷贼窟，只身远遁，而诸君犹谓为全璧，吾不信也。”予正色叱曰：“恶是何言也！此女九死一生，卒能遂其旧姻，是必天鉴其苦节之贞，而后得占夫甘临之吉。此中非有鬼神呵护，乌能遇合如此之巧耶？况合卺之后，公子逑好甚敦，太璞能完，不言可喻。君谬持无稽之论，污蔑贤媛，其存心刻薄，殊伤忠厚，吾不忍闻也！”合座称善。

    Explanation

    736

    Li Chengzi said: In that spring, I happened to travel to Wu. A friend invited me to a banquet, and at the table, everyone spoke enthusiastically about this matter, considering it a beautiful story. Suddenly, one of the guests sneered and said, "This woman had been trapped in a dangerous place for a long time, and she managed to escape alone. Yet, you all still call her a perfect gem; I don't believe it." I sternly rebuked him, saying, "What kind of words are these! This woman faced death nine times and finally managed to reunite with her former husband. It must be that Heaven observed her enduring virtue and granted her a favorable outcome. Without the protection of spirits, how could such a perfect match be so serendipitous? Moreover, after their wedding, the couple's affection was very deep, and their relationship remained pure and unblemished. It's clear to see. You hold onto groundless theories, defaming virtuous women. Your heart is harsh and unkind, greatly harming loyalty and kindness. I cannot bear to hear such things!" Everyone at the table agreed with my rebuke.

    737

    山东某县幕友
    　　
    　　溧阳史仲皋言，山东某明府履任，其幕友某素吸阿芙蓉膏。初，进署斋，薄暮见一衣冠人来，略与拱手，遂分榻而卧，焚膏互吸。某以为官亲，不甚研究。如是月馀，彼此均未问讯名姓。一日，吸膏甫毕，衣冠人忽谓某曰：“一言颇骇君听，君愿闻乎？”某曰：“请试言之。”衣冠人曰：“我非人，乃鬼也。尚有苦衷，奉求援救，君其勿恐。”某曰：“相处月馀，已称熟识，虽鬼何畏？倘可以效力，断不敢辞。但言无妨。”其人乃细告以名姓里居。因言：“前任有某官者抵任，聘予为司会计。寇犯是邑，某官殉难，予亦被害。

    Explanation

    737

    A Magistrate's Assistant in Shandong

    Lianyang's Shi Zhonggao said that when a certain magistrate in Shandong took up his post, one of his assistants was a habitual user of opium paste. At first, when he entered the office, late in the evening, he saw a formally dressed man arrive, they bowed slightly to each other, shared a bed, and smoked opium together. The assistant thought the man was an official and did not inquire further. For over a month, they did not ask each other's names. One day, after finishing smoking opium, the formally dressed man suddenly said to the assistant, "There is something that will shock you, do you wish to hear it?" The assistant replied, "Please tell me." The formally dressed man said, "I am not a human, I am a ghost. I have a tragic story, I seek your help, please do not be afraid." The assistant said, "We have been together for over a month, I consider us familiar, even if you are a ghost, what is there to fear? If I can help, I will not hesitate. Just speak." The man then told him his name, hometown, and residence in detail. He continued, "The previous official who took up his post hired me as his accountant. When the bandits attacked this county, the official sacrificed himself, and I was also harmed."

    738

    寇退，邑人以予向不作威福，醵资为具衣衾棺槨，寄停某寺。初，贼未至时，予预藏衣箱数个于此屋地砖之下，并银数千两，黄金数百两。又藏洋土、洋膏于四壁。壁系剜砖，而外糊以纸，君第以竹管叩之，其声中空者是也。可将所藏物尽行掘取，银数千两当以奉赠。乞载予棺并衣箱、黄金送归予家，免使少妇孤儿空劳盼望，则受仁人之赐多矣。”某一一诺之。其人感谢叮咛而去，自是遂不复来。试以竹管叩壁，有数处声果中空，次第发之，果如所言。乃夤夜掘地，出所藏衣物，一一不谬，惟衣已糜烂，着手成灰。某如鬼所言，留银数千两，而载棺与烂衣与金送还其家。

    Explanation

    738

    The bandits retreated, and the townspeople, because I had not previously acted with tyranny or favoritism, collected funds to provide clothes, bedding, coffin, and casket, and temporarily stayed me at a certain temple. Initially, when the bandits had not yet arrived, I had already hidden several chests of clothes under the floor tiles of this house, along with several thousand taels of silver and several hundred taels of gold. I also hid foreign soil and foreign ointment on the four walls. The walls were hollowed out with bricks and covered with paper on the outside. You should simply tap them with a bamboo tube; the ones that sound hollow are the correct ones. You can dig up all the hidden items, and the several thousand taels of silver should be given as a gift. Please load my coffin, the clothes chests, and the gold and send them back to my home, so as not to let the young widow and orphan wait in vain. In this way, you will receive much benefit from the kindness of a benevolent person." He agreed to everything. The person thanked and repeatedly advised before leaving, and after that, he never came back. Try tapping the walls with a bamboo tube; several places indeed sounded hollow. Opening them in order, they indeed were as he said. So at night he dug the ground, and the hidden clothes and bedding came out, and everything matched exactly, only the clothes had rotted and turned to dust. He left several thousand taels of silver as the ghost had said, and loaded the coffin, the rotten clothes, and the gold to return to his home.

    739

    林明府
    　　
    　　吾友瑞安林若衣明府用光，吏治精练，所在有声。令蒙城时，苗逆新伏诛，邑甫克复，百事废弛，急须整顿措置，较常烦剧。会典学使者将次按临，趣各邑举行季考，君悬牌，届期猝病，寒热景作，头涔涔然如戴山，畏风，当暑覆重衾卧斗帐中，犹苦不汗。幕友见病状绵惙，请改试期。君漫应之。以厌闻人语，屏去侮甬，蒙头欲睡。倏见耆民无数，芒鞋布衣，纷沓入室，罗拜床下。君叱曰：“我方厌见宾客，谁令若曹来者？宜速退，毋聒我！”耆民拜且泣曰：“使君请毋怒。

    Explanation

    739

    The Magistrate of Lin County

    My friend Lin Ruoyi, the magistrate of Lin County, is renowned for his excellence in administrative affairs and his reputation wherever he serves. When he was the magistrate of Mengcheng, the town had just been recaptured after the recent execution of the rebel Miao, and all matters were neglected, requiring urgent organization and management, more complicated than usual. The provincial examination officials were about to arrive, urging each county to hold the quarterly exams. The magistrate posted a notice, but on the day, he suddenly fell ill, experiencing chills and fever, with a headache as if wearing a mountain, and he was afraid of the wind. In the heat, he covered himself with thick quilts in a mosquito net, still suffering from lack of sweating. His assistants saw the severe illness and asked to change the exam date. The magistrate replied indifferently, as he disliked hearing people talk, and he shut out the noise, covered his head, and wanted to sleep. Suddenly, he saw countless elderly people, wearing straw shoes and plain clothes, rushing into the room and kneeling around the bed. The magistrate shouted, "I am tired of seeing guests, who sent you all here? You should leave quickly, don't disturb me!" The elderly people knelt and wept, saying, "Please don't be angry, magistrate."

    740

    吾侪小人荷使君再造恩，分别等差，子孙不至饿殍，吾侪血食幸得不斩，悉受使君之赐。特来拜德，并无他意。顷入室时，见厉鬼立左右，吾侪业为驱去，疾当已。”并揽君袂曰：“使君可起视事，保无他虑。”君以手麾之，欻不见。顿惊寤，觉汗下如雨，竟体清爽，若释重负。亟起坐，召左右进汤粥。少许，病良瘥。先是，僧邸既歼苗逆，恶从逆党羽，檄郡县稽察逆产，概籍没入官。君商之幕友，谓逆党宜分别等差，如胁从出于不得已者，当曲宥勿论。甫有是说，而溟漠中竟已知之，殊可骇异。翌日扃试，竟无改期，人诧为神。试竣，君将为蒙民请命僧邸，会僧邸薨，遂寝。

    Explanation

    740

    We humble people bear the great grace of the governor's rebirth, differentiating and ranking us, ensuring our descendants will not starve to death, and that we fortunate ones have not been cut off from our ancestral sacrifices. All of this is due to the governor's benevolence. We come specially to express our gratitude, with no other intention. When I entered the room, I saw fierce ghosts standing on both sides, which we had already driven away, and the illness should be cured. I took the governor's sleeve and said, "Governor, you can rise and attend to your duties, assured of no further concerns." The governor waved his hand, and the ghosts suddenly disappeared. I woke up in alarm, feeling sweat pouring down like rain, and my whole body felt refreshed, as if a heavy burden had been lifted. I quickly sat up, called for the attendants to bring soup and porridge. After a short while, the illness greatly improved. Previously, the monk's residence had already exterminated the rebel Miao, and the evil followers of the rebels were issued an edict to the counties to investigate the rebels' property, to confiscate all and turn over to the government. The governor consulted with his advisors, who said that the rebels should be differentiated and ranked, and those who were forced to join out of necessity should be pardoned with leniency and not judged. As soon as this suggestion was made, and in the mysterious world it had already known, it is truly astonishing. The next day, the examination was held, with no rescheduling, and people were amazed and thought it was divine. After the examination was completed, the governor intended to plead for the people on behalf of the monk, just as the monk died, and thus it was abandoned.

    741

    赵孝廉
    　　
    　　合肥赵梧冈孝廉凤举，住西乡大潜山，与吾友王谦斋善。谦斋尝过访，适赵小极，见之喜曰：“君来大好，我正有要言相告。”谦斋叩之。曰：“昨在阴曹，至一公廨，一吏捧册请画诺，谓目下公务旁午，冥王已派予司事。恐不能久与诸君相聚矣！”谦斋慰之曰：“小极何遽若是，君毋呓语。”曰：“非呓语也。并见公廨东西各列公案数十，每案皆有一官稽册，册堆积如山。尊公东序西向坐，见予略一点首。

    Explanation

    741

    Mr. Zhao Wugang, a scholar-official, lived in the western suburbs of Hefei on Dapian Mountain, and was friends with Wang Qianzhai. Wang Qianzhai once visited him, and Zhao was very tired. Seeing Wang, he was delighted and said, "It's great that you've come. I have something important to tell you." Wang asked what it was. Zhao replied, "Yesterday, I was in the underworld and arrived at an official residence. A clerk presented a document and asked me to sign off on it. He said that I was overwhelmed with work, and the ruler of the underworld had already assigned me to manage affairs. I fear I won't be able to stay with you for long!" Wang comforted him, saying, "Why are you so tired? Don't talk nonsense." Zhao said, "I'm not talking nonsense. I also saw that the official residence had dozens of desks arranged east and west, each with an official checking documents. The documents were piled up like mountains. Your father was sitting facing west, with the east side as his back, and he nodded slightly when he saw me."

    742

    予就问起居，尊公举足示予，谓靴敝，烦寄语家人，急为更制。且谓事太烦剧，须某来为我分劳。予叩某是何人，尊公笑曰：‘此五儿乳名，君不知耶？五儿乃谦斋也。’予惊曰：‘自公去世，谦斋仔肩綦重，何能至此？’尊公沈吟久之曰：‘无已，着七儿来亦可。’恐君之七弟亦不能久存矣。”时谦斋之尊甫育泉征君下世已二年，谦斋乳名固无人知，闻之毛发森立。又强慰赵曰：“君言固尔，安知非妖梦之幻，何遽认真？”赵笑曰：“我亦岂愿认真？如五日内胡二水无恙，便是幻梦。君试识之。

    Explanation

    742

    I came to inquire about your well-being, and your esteemed father lifted his foot to show me, saying that his shoes were worn out, and asked me to convey a message to the family to hurry and make a new pair. He also said that his affairs were too busy and complicated, and needed someone to come and share the burden. I asked who this person was, and your esteemed father smiled and said, "This is the nickname of my fifth son, do you not know? The fifth son is Qianzai." I was surprised and said, "Since your father passed away, the burden on Qianzai is extremely heavy, how could he manage this?" Your esteemed father thought deeply for a long time and said, "If there is no other way, having the seventh son come as well would be fine." He expressed concern that my seventh brother may not last long. At that time, Qianzai's esteemed father, the respected Mr. Yüquan, had been deceased for two years, and Qianzai's nickname was unknown to anyone, causing my hair to stand on end. I then tried to comfort Zhao by saying, "Your words are true, but how do we know it is not a dream of demons? Why take it so seriously?" Zhao smiled and said, "I also do not wish to take it seriously. If Hu Ershui is fine within five days, it will be a dream. You try to recognize it."

    743

    ”胡二水与赵同里，相距里许，五日内忽无疾而逝，众益称异。谦斋乃谋于众曰：“据此，赵君之禄已尽。我辈不忍坐视，试联名具疏，焚诸司命，各请减寿以延其算，或可禳解。”佥曰：“诺。”联名具疏者凡十人，谦斋之七弟预焉。就灶焚之，不以告赵。越日，赵谓谦斋曰：“诸君雅谊假年，情殊可感。如能过某日某时，或可无虑。然七弟大名固可不列，尊公相需甚殷已定，命其某日前往，断不能少缓须臾矣。”众闻之益惊。姑留心待至某日某时，同往视赵。

    Explanation

    743

    Hu Ershui and Zhao were neighbors, living about a mile apart. Within five days, Zhao suddenly died without any illness, which greatly surprised everyone. Qianzai then consulted the people and said, "Based on this, Mr. Zhao's fate has run its course. We cannot bear to watch, so let us try to sign a petition, burn it to the gods of destiny, each ask to shorten our own lives to extend his, perhaps we can avert this." Everyone agreed, "Yes." Those who signed the petition were ten in total, including Qianzai's seventh brother. They burned it at the stove without telling Zhao. The next day, Zhao told Qianzai, "All of you have shown noble friendship by extending my life, and the feeling is very touching. If you can make it past a certain date and time, you may be safe. But your seventh brother's name can certainly be omitted. Your father has already been decided to need him urgently, and he has been ordered to go on a certain day. He cannot delay even a moment." The people heard this and were even more surprised. They temporarily kept an eye on the matter until a certain date and time, and went together to see Zhao.

    744

    赵晨兴，谈笑自若，及至其时，忽起立别众曰：“时已至矣，请与诸君永诀。”便命家人为其冠服，拜别太夫人，谓：“儿不孝，不能事奉以终天年。幸自颐养，毋以儿为念。”又谓其继室曰：“结褵多年，尚称静好，惟未得子女，未免抱歉。此后尚烦为我奉母课子，吾目瞑矣。”母与妻相持恸哭，赵强笑而慰劝之。又命其子当善事大母，无违母教。又遍拜诸人，言讫，一拱手，端坐榻上。众探其鼻，已无息矣。迨至某日，谦斋之七弟果卒。此谦斋为予言者。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：咸丰纪元，吾皖合肥王丈育泉、赵君云持，庐江吴丈兰轩，举孝廉方正，赴省，同寓馆舍。赵系故人，时相过从，因识王、吴两征君。既粤寇起，吴以团练殉节，功在桑梓；王、赵亦相继徂谢。今王丈已膺冥秩，吴丈与赵君当俱执事天曹，聪明正直谓之神，其然乎！

    Explanation

    744

    赵晨兴，谈笑自若，及至其时，忽起立别众曰：“时已至矣，请与诸君永诀。”便命家人为其冠服，拜别太夫人，谓：“儿不孝，不能事奉以终天年。幸自颐养，毋以儿为念。”又谓其继室曰：“结褵多年，尚称静好，惟未得子女，未免抱歉。此后尚烦为我奉母课子，吾目瞑矣。”母与妻相持恸哭，赵强笑而慰劝之。又命其子当善事大母，无违母教。又遍拜诸人，言讫，一拱手，端坐榻上。众探其鼻，已无息矣。迨至某日，谦斋之七弟果卒。此谦斋为予言者。

    里乘子曰：咸丰纪元，吾皖合肥王丈育泉、赵君云持，庐江吴丈兰轩，举孝廉方正，赴省，同寓馆舍。赵系故人，时相过从，因识王、吴两征君。既粤寇起，吴以团练殉节，功在桑梓；王、赵亦相继徂谢。今王丈已膺冥秩，吴丈与赵君当俱执事天曹，聪明正直谓之神，其然乎！

    745

    方复庵封翁
    　　
    　　吾邑方复庵封翁，孝友刚直，疾恶乐善。四十无子，父命助簉。秋试金陵，在媒媪家见一妇人，举止娴静，频蹙而泪。异之，研诘媒媪，乃知此妇质身殓姑，无资赎身，又不肯别嫁，故时陨泪。封翁嘉其志，为出资赎归其夫。越岁，生麟轩太守，梦神告曰：“汝救江宁孝妇，今赐汝子，他日当为江宁宰。”道光己酉，太守果宰江宁。封翁年八十，家居，寇至，以刃胁从。厉声叱曰：“我家世受国恩，岂降贼耶！”以杖击贼首出血。贼怒，虐报之，骂益烈。贼魁叹为强项，义而释之。太守时擢太仓牧，迎养任所，以寿终。初，太守宰江宁时，会封翁病剧，祷以身代。梦神赏其赈灾有功，增父寿一纪，恰符其数。生平忠义大节，为里党所称。大府上于朝，建坊旌之。太守名锡庆，曾知松江府事。

    Explanation

    745

    Mr. Fang Fu'an, Honored as a Deceased Husband

    Mr. Fang Fu'an, honored as a deceased husband in our county, was filial, kind to his siblings, upright, and resolute. He hated evil and delighted in good deeds. At forty, he had no son, and his father commanded him to assist in securing a wife. During the autumn examination in Jinling, he visited the home of a matchmaker and saw a woman whose demeanor was quiet and composed, yet she was often frowning and weeping. Surprised by this, he questioned the matchmaker, who then revealed that this woman had pledged her body to care for her deceased mother-in-law, had no means to redeem herself, and refused to remarry, hence her frequent tears. Mr. Fang admired her determination and provided funds to redeem her husband. The following year, he gave birth to Governor Linxuan, who dreamed of a deity who said to him: “You have rescued the filial woman of Jiangning; now I grant you a son, who one day will govern Jiangning.” In the year Jiyou of the Daoguang era, the governor indeed became the magistrate of Jiangning.

    At the age of eighty, Mr. Fang lived at home when bandits arrived and threatened him with a blade to comply. He shouted sternly, “My family has long received the grace of the state—how could I submit to bandits?” He struck the bandit leader with a staff, causing him to bleed. The bandits, enraged, cruelly retaliated, and he cursed them even more fiercely. The bandit leader, impressed by his unbending spirit, released him out of righteousness.

    At that time, the governor was promoted to magistrate of Taicang and brought his father to live with him in his place of service, where Mr. Fang passed away peacefully at an old age.

    Initially, when the governor was in charge of Jiangning, Mr. Fang fell seriously ill. The governor prayed to take his father’s illness upon himself. He dreamed of a deity who rewarded him for his efforts in disaster relief and extended his father’s life by one ji (a twelve-year cycle), which exactly matched the number of years he had been granted. Throughout his life, Mr. Fang’s great acts of loyalty and righteousness were praised by the local community. The local officials reported his virtues to the imperial court, and a stone tablet was erected in his honor.

    The governor’s name was Xi Qing, who once served as magistrate of Songjiang Prefecture.

    746

    汪态臣
    　　
    　　杭州汪渭，字态臣。幼跛一足，非杖不行。粤寇围杭，奉母避难绍兴。苦无肉食，家蓄四鸡，欲烹之。母素奉佛，戒勿杀，不听。适逢母斋期，以离乱尚何斋戒，窃笑母愚。背母啖鸡，意甚自得。四鸡尚未飨尽，贼猝至，被执，刃伤其颈者四，恰符鸡数。贼以为已死，委之而去。瞰贼去远，乃匍匐归。取帛裹创处，以避乱无处觅医药，检所携钞方，谓龙眼核去外黑衣，研极细末，敷刀伤立可止血止痛。试之果然，诚仙方也。又言方被执时，心甚了了，贼所斫四刀，惟先一刀砉然有声，力最猛，后三刀较轻，亦不甚痛楚。素诵《高王经》，创时尝梦一老妪谓之曰：“汝欲创愈，必虔诵《太上感应篇》方可，徒诵《高王经》无益也。”爰加诵《感应篇》，创果渐愈。遂自号曰“再生子”。后逢母斋期，一遵母教惟谨。予游燕台，与态臣订交于滦州署中，亲为予历历言之如此。

    Explanation

    746

    Wang Tai Chen

    　　Wang Wei, from Hangzhou, was known by the courtesy name Tai Chen. He was born with one lame foot and could not walk without a cane. When the Cantonese rebels surrounded Hangzhou, he accompanied his mother to Shaoxing to avoid the danger. Struggling with a lack of meat, he kept four chickens at home and wanted to cook them. His mother, who always practiced Buddhism, forbade killing, but he did not listen. Coincidentally, it was the mother's fasting period, and he thought, "In times of chaos, what is the point of fasting?" He secretly laughed at his mother's foolishness and ate the chicken behind her back, feeling quite pleased with himself. The four chickens were not yet finished when the bandits suddenly arrived, capturing him and slashing his neck four times, which exactly matched the number of chickens. The bandits thought he was dead and left him there. After they were far away, he waited cautiously and then crawled back home. He took cloth to wrap his wounds, as there was no place to find medicine due to the chaos. He checked the medical prescriptions he had brought and found that grinding the kernel of the lychee fruit without the outer black skin into very fine powder and applying it to knife wounds could stop bleeding and pain immediately. He tried it, and it worked as described, truly a magical formula. He also mentioned that when he was first captured, his mind was very clear. The first cut made a sharp sound and was the strongest, while the next three cuts were lighter and not very painful. He always recited the Gao Wang Jing and once dreamed of an old woman telling him, "If you want your wounds to heal, you must sincerely recite the Tai Shang Gan Ying Pian; merely reciting the Gao Wang Jing is of no benefit." He then began reciting the Gan Ying Pian, and his wounds gradually healed. Thus, he gave himself the nickname "Regenerated Son." Later, when his mother's fasting period came, he strictly followed her teachings. I traveled to Yan Platform and made friends with Tai Chen in the office in Luanzhou, and he told me in detail like this.

    747

    徽州某甲
    　　
    　　徽州某甲，避粤寇之乱，转徙浙东。尝同二人暮行，无地栖止，遂伏身死人丛中。半夜，忽见灯火辉煌，卤簿拥一金甲神至，稽在劫死人名目。鬼吏持册唱名，众尸起立，一一声应讫。既见甲等三人，详问姓名、里居，神命稽册，三人可否在劫？鬼吏遍稽，谓甲合于三日内在太末为赖麻子所杀，神颔之。须臾，卤薄拥神去。东方渐白，三人起行，在路互相嗟叹，以为怪事。

    Explanation

    747

    A man from Huizhou, fleeing the chaos caused by the Yue bandits, migrated to eastern Zhejiang. Once, he traveled at dusk with two others, having no place to stay, and hid among the corpses. At midnight, he suddenly saw a brilliant light, and a procession of halberds escorted a golden-armored deity, who checked the list of those destined to die in this calamity. Ghost officials held a register and called out names, and the corpses stood up, each responding in turn. When the deity saw the three men, he inquired in detail about their names and places of origin, and commanded the register to be checked to see if the three were destined to die. The ghost officials thoroughly checked the register and reported that the man from Huizhou was destined to be killed by Lai Mazǐ in Tai Maò within three days. The deity nodded in agreement. Shortly after, the procession of halberds escorted the deity away. As dawn approached, the three men stood up and walked, sighing and lamenting together, considering it a strange and unusual event.

    748

    三日，恰过太末，贼麕至，二人趣甲疾行，甲谓：“劫数难逃，今届三日，适到太末，宁非数乎？君等请速前往，免使波及。我将延颈待贼，了此劫数。”二人再三趣之，卒不肯行。以贼踵至，不得已，挥泪而别。甲独憩古刹。午刻，贼大至，见一人着黄袍坐俊马，貌甚魁伟，所统贼兵甚众，知是伪官，自拚一死，即亦不畏。马上人瞥见甲，命拘至马前，究问邦族。甲举首见其人满面痘瘢，心益异之，乃大声叱曰：“尔是赖麻子非耶？我命应了尔手。杀便杀却，夫复何问！”其人大惊，问：“缘何知我姓赖？”甲备告所见，并求速杀。

    Explanation

    748

    Three days later, just as they reached Taimei, the bandits gathered in large numbers. The two urged A to hurry, but A said, "Destiny is unavoidable. Today is the third day, and we have just arrived at Taimei. Is this not fate? You two please go quickly, so as not to be caught up in this. I will wait with my neck outstretched for the bandits, to finish this destiny." The two tried to urge him again, but he finally refused to leave. Seeing the bandits approaching, they had no choice but to shed tears and part. A rested at an ancient temple. At noon, the bandits arrived in large numbers. Seeing a man in a yellow robe sitting on a fine horse, who looked very imposing and commanded many bandit soldiers, A knew he was an imposter in authority. He prepared to die and was not afraid. The man on the horse caught a glimpse of A and ordered him to be arrested and brought before the horse. He questioned A about his nationality and clan. A raised his head and saw that the man had smallpox scars on his face. He was even more surprised and shouted, "Are you Lai Maizi? My destiny is to be in your hands. Kill me if you must, but why ask further?" The man was greatly startled and asked, "How do you know my surname is Lai?" A fully reported what he had seen and requested a swift execution.

    749

    赖笑曰：“我不信果有劫数，我不杀尔，何如？”甲谓：“资斧已绝，即蒙不杀，亦无生理。”赖赏给番镪五饼，麾令速行。甲感激拜谢而去。不图路遇他贼，搜掠一空。正窘迫时，又遇赖麻子，问：“何不归？”甲具告之，赖复赏给番镪十饼。不几日，又被搜掠，较前倍苦。而赖麻子大队又至，见甲问：“何又须捷若此？”甲泣对曰：“小人屡荷不杀之恩，奈命蹇途穷，屡遭攘劫，可知劫数难逃，不如早死为幸！”赖笑曰：“我不信果有劫数！”命从者解衣衣之，复厚给番镪五十饼，并给印牒。

    Explanation

    749

    Lai smiled and said, "I don't believe in fate. If I don't kill you, what do you say?" Jia replied, "My resources are exhausted. Even if I am spared, there is no hope for survival." Lai then gave Jia five pieces of foreign silver and gestured for him to leave quickly. Jia, grateful, bowed and thanked him before departing. Unluckily, on the road, he encountered another bandit who plundered him completely. When he was in dire straits, he met Lai Mazi again, who asked, "Why don't you return home?" Jia explained everything to him. Lai then gave Jia ten pieces of foreign silver. A few days later, Jia was plundered again, this time with even greater hardship. Lai Mazi's large group arrived again, and seeing Jia, he asked, "Why are you in such trouble again?" Jia wept and replied, "I have repeatedly received your grace of not being killed, but my fate is poor and the road is blocked; I have repeatedly been robbed and plundered. It is clear that my fate is unavoidable, and it would be better to die early!" Lai laughed and said, "I don't believe in fate!" He ordered his followers to remove their clothes and give them to Jia, and then generously gave Jia fifty pieces of foreign silver, along with official documents.

    750

    谓：“前途倘遇他队，以牒示之，保无他虑。”甲犹豫曰：“屡蒙恩贷再生，但册既有名，恐终不免。如赐曲全，请以刀薄画项肉，以释其疑，可乎？”赖笑而许之。遂拔佩刀在甲项微抹如爪痕，薄示其意，甲乃叩谢而去。既抵家，向人备述赖德，皆叹为奇遇。居无何，刀抹微痕渐觉痒痛，创日增剧。延医调治，迄无少效。所得番镪五十饼耗尽，而项创暴裂，颈堕落，如刀新割而死。或谓甲虽死，究为归正邱首，几疑劫册不尽可信。然遇贼于太末，死机日伏；即谓死于太末，亦可信乎！劫数难逃，非人力所可争也。如求免劫，或者恐惧修省，防祸于未然，其庶几乎？

    Explanation

    750

    The speaker said, "If you encounter another group ahead, show them the document, and there will be no other worries." Jia hesitated and said, "I have been repeatedly shown mercy and thus spared from death, but if my name is already on the list, I fear I cannot ultimately escape. If you grant me this favor, please use a knife to lightly mark the flesh of my neck, to dispel their doubts, would that be acceptable?" Lai smiled and agreed to it. He then drew a light scratch on Jia's neck with his dagger, like the mark of a claw, giving a subtle hint. Jia bowed in gratitude and left. Once he arrived home, he told people in detail about Lai's kindness, and all admired it as a rare encounter. Not long after, the light scratch on his neck gradually became itchy and painful, and the wound grew increasingly severe. He sought medical treatment, but without any improvement. The fifty pieces of foreign currency he had obtained were spent, and then the neck wound suddenly burst, and his neck fell off, as if newly cut by a knife. Some said that although Jia died, he ultimately returned to the proper order of things, almost suspecting that the list of victims was not entirely reliable. But encountering bandits in Taizhou, the seeds of death were already planted; even if one says he died in Taizhou, can that be believed? Fate is hard to escape, it is not something that can be resisted by human effort. If one seeks to avoid such fate, perhaps through fear, self-examination, and preventing disasters before they occur, one might succeed.

    751

    皖北粤东两奇女
    　　
    　　初，粤寇蔓延时，淫掠妇女不可胜计。惟皖南、粤东两奇女，不惟免辱，且能杀贼，仓卒应变，可谓急智。闻者无不拊掌称快，吾特为连类书之。先是，贼窜江南，至桐舒界，乡村男妇皇皇窜避。有女年十七八，以足纤不良于行，为贼所掠，搂坐马上。既至一山谷，贼瞷无人，抱女下马求欢。女笑曰：“固所愿也。然必须将马系住，否则奔逸奈何？”贼以为然。惟苦童山，无树木可以维絷。贼欲焰正炽，踌躇无计。女笑曰：“君何愚也！以马绳系君踝，复何虑耶？”贼大喜，如言缠绳于踝，摩挲妥帖。女急取贼所佩刀，力斫马尻，马负痛，曳贼足怒奔。贼猝不能脱，任其所之，竟不知胡所底止。女掩袂吃吃匿笑，以里党路熟，由巉道急遁，幸免于难。或谓贼为马所曳，脑裂肢解，身无完肤而斃。

    Explanation

    751

    Two Remarkable Women from Anhui and Guangdong

    At the beginning, when the bandits from Guangdong were spreading across the region, they ravished and plundered countless women. However, there were two remarkable women from southern Anhui and eastern Guangdong who not only managed to avoid being dishonored but also killed the bandits. Their quick thinking in emergencies can be called a display of intelligence. Those who heard about their deeds clapped in delight and approval, and I have specially written about them together. Previously, the bandits had fled to the southern part of Jiangnan and reached the border between Tong and Shu. Villagers, both men and women, were in a state of panic and fleeing. There was a young woman, about seventeen or eighteen years old, whose slender feet made her poor at walking. She was captured by the bandits and held on the horse. Once they arrived at a valley, the bandit saw that there was no one around and tried to hold the girl down and have sexual intercourse with her. The girl smiled and said, "It is what I have always wanted. However, it must be necessary to tie the horse up, otherwise, what if the horse runs away?" The bandit thought this was reasonable. However, in the barren hills, there were no trees to tie the horse with. The bandit's desire was at its peak, but he hesitated without knowing what to do. The girl laughed and said, "How foolish you are! To tie the horse's rope to your ankle, what else do you worry about?" The bandit was delighted and, as she said, tied the rope around his ankle, rubbing it gently and making it comfortable. The girl quickly took the bandit's knife and struck the horse's hindquarters with force. The horse felt pain, pulled the bandit's feet, and ran angrily. The bandit suddenly could not escape and left him to his fate, finally not knowing where he ended up. The girl covered her mouth and laughed secretly, because she was familiar with the local paths, and took a steep path and fled quickly, fortunately escaping danger. Some say that the bandit was dragged by the horse, his brain was split, his limbs were dismembered, and his body was completely torn apart, and he died.

    752

    又，贼犯粤东，掠一处女，劫与俱行。适有涧前阻，贼临流踌躇，无所为计。女笑曰：“水深不及骭，赤足可济。汝脱袜履，妾为携之，负妾而行，顷刻不并达彼岸耶？”贼如言负女，至中流深处，女弃袜履，急以两手抵死紧扼贼喉，贼力解女手不得，气闭遽踣。女与俱死水中。寇退，其父兄觅收女尸，水浸浃旬，面目如生，两手犹抱贼颈，十指入肉半寸许。贼尸伏女下，已腐烂，皮骨不属矣。其乡冯子良太守询，有诗以纪其事。
    　　
    　　里乘子曰：粤寇之难，妇女罹劫尤惨。闻贼恶纤足，以其不便骑马，每掠得纤足者，必褫其行缠，剖羊腹，纳足热血中，足指暴伸，顷刻新月即变莲船。惟血热于沸汤，痛彻骨髓，惨莫惨于此矣。如此二女，真可为闺阁吐气。皖南者智而黠，粤东者智而烈，谓之奇女，不亦宜乎！

    Explanation

    752

    The bandits invaded Guangdong and seized a maiden, forcibly taking her along. Just as they reached a stream blocking their path, the bandit stood by the stream, hesitating, with no plan. The girl smiled and said, "The water is not deep enough to reach the shins; we can cross barefoot. You take off your socks and shoes, and I will carry them. Carry me and go; won't we both reach the other side in a moment?" The bandit followed her words and carried her. They reached the deep part of the middle stream, and the girl discarded her socks and shoes. Quickly, she used both hands to tightly strangle the bandit's throat. The bandit struggled to free his hands but could not, and the breath was cut off, causing him to fall. The girl and the bandit both died in the water. When the bandits retreated, her father and brothers searched for and recovered her body. The body was submerged for ten days, and the face and features were as if alive. Both hands still held the bandit's neck, with fingers penetrating the flesh by about half an inch. The bandit's body lay under the girl, having rotted, with skin and bones no longer belonging together. The local magistrate Feng Ziliang inquired and wrote a poem to record the event.

    The author of the village chronicle said: The difficulty caused by the bandits in Guangdong was particularly cruel to women. It was heard that the bandits disliked small feet because it was inconvenient for riding horses. Whenever they captured someone with small feet, they would remove her foot bindings, cut open the sheep's belly, and place her feet in hot blood. The toes suddenly stretched out, and in a moment, the crescent moon shape turned into a lotus boat. Only the hot blood was as painful as boiling water, penetrating to the bone marrow, and nothing was more cruel than this. Such two women truly deserve to be a source of pride for the ladies of the household. The woman from Anhui was intelligent and cunning, while the woman from Guangdong was intelligent and fierce. To call them extraordinary women, is it not appropriate?

    753

    陈祥浦
    　　
    　　陈祥浦，浙江诸暨县农家子也。力耕养母，族党称孝焉。娶妻某氏，有殊色，同床数年，未尝人道之乐，而情好甚敦。他日，辍耕陇畔，忽来一叟，自称罗真人，传授勅勒之术，属为国效命。又赠石子五枚，使食之。祥浦畏难不敢嚼。真人白眼怒视，夺去以手出火煅之，再授，使试嚼之，香软如芋。食其三，窃怀二枚，将以遗母与妻。乃归探怀，则不可得矣。后母与妻皆死于难。祥浦食石后，仰观俯察，能测天地之秘。粤寇犯越，佐同邑包立身团练乡勇，捍卫乡里。包亦农人，曾遇白老人，即越女论剑之白猿也，授指环一枚，佩之可号召鬼神，呼唤风雨。戒令剿寇销劫，勿生贰心。后屡击贼有功，其志日骄，隐萌异念。老人怒夺指环去，遂为贼所杀。祥浦势孤，遁至北直。年馀，罗真人遣二仙童来为服役，戒茹荤酒，每闭目枯坐，可神游八表。

    Explanation

    753

    Chen Xiangpu

    Chen Xiangpu was a farmer's son from Zhuji County, Zhejiang Province. He worked hard to support his mother, and his clan and neighbors praised him for his filial piety. He married a woman of exceptional beauty, but despite being together for many years, they never experienced the pleasure of human intimacy; yet their affection was very deep. One day, while tilling the fields, an old man suddenly appeared, introducing himself as Luo Zhenren, a true man. He taught Chen Xiangpu a technique called the "Chile method" and entrusted him to serve the country. He also gave him five stone pills to eat. Chen Xiangpu was afraid of the difficulty and dared not chew them. Luo Zhenren stared at him with a white eye in anger, took the pills away, and heated them with fire in his hand. He then gave them back to Chen Xiangpu, asking him to try chewing them. They were fragrant and soft like taro. Chen Xiangpu ate three of them and secretly kept two, intending to give them to his mother and wife. When he returned home and searched his chest, the pills were no longer there. Later, his mother and wife both died in a disaster. After eating the stones, Chen Xiangpu looked up and down, able to measure the secrets of heaven and earth. When the Cantonese rebels invaded Yue, Chen Xiangpu helped his fellow townsman Bao Lishen to train local militia to defend their hometown. Bao Lishen was also a farmer who had once met an elderly white person, the white monkey mentioned in the story of the swordsmanship of the Lady of Yue. The white monkey gave him a ring, which, when worn, could summon ghosts and spirits, call forth wind and rain. He was warned to eliminate bandits and robbers and not to harbor any ulterior motives. After several successful battles against the bandits, Bao Lishen's ambitions grew, and he secretly harbored different thoughts. The old man angrily took the ring away, and Bao Lishen was subsequently killed by the bandits. Chen Xiangpu, now alone, fled to Beizhili. More than a year later, Luo Zhenren sent two immortal children to serve him, warning him not to eat meat or drink alcohol. Every time he closed his eyes and sat in meditation, he could spirit-walk the eight directions.

    754

    二童尝领其魂至四川峨眉山谒罗真人。山中有洞，白石为门，二童以手叩之，门遂开。洞中别有天地，无寒暑昼夜。有男有女，女皆二八丽姝，坐机织锦，脉脉不语；男皆赳赳健儿，镇日演武。五兵俱备，鞍马极多。罗真人年五十馀，须髯半白，坐殿中。左右二仙，闭目静坐，不知名氏。祥浦参拜罗真人，两傍设短足榻，俱挂帐幔，令趺坐其上，不饥不倦，寝食顿忘。真人谓祥浦终非仙材，命仍返世间，修身立德，终当娶妻生子，以延宗嗣。倘功行不懈，死后可成鬼仙。初，罗真人曾授祥浦一白绡帕，方五尺许，叠折至小，可纳耳内。有急时出之，则云雾四合，对面人不能见，名曰“帕云”。包败时，祥浦独以身免者，帕云之力也。真人送祥浦出洞，索还帕云，谓：“承平无需此物，藏汝处复何用耶？”祥浦初名肖云，改名朝云。归越，隐会稽山中，后不知所终。此其宗人陈西堂为予言者。

    Explanation

    754

    Two attendants once led his soul to Mount Emei in Sichuan to pay homage to Master Luo. In the mountain there was a cave, with a white stone as its door. The two attendants struck it with their hands, and the door opened. Within the cave was another world, without cold, heat, day, or night. There were men and women; the women were all young and beautiful, sitting at looms weaving brocade, gazing silently without speaking; the men were all strong and valiant warriors, practicing martial arts all day. All five kinds of weapons were present, and there were many saddles and horses. Master Luo was over fifty years old, with a beard and mustache half white, seated in the hall. On his left and right sat two immortals, eyes closed in meditation, their names unknown. Xiangpu paid homage to Master Luo, and on either side short-legged couches were set up, all draped with curtains, and he was ordered to sit cross-legged upon them. He felt neither hunger nor fatigue, and soon forgot sleep and food. The Real Person said that Xiangpu was ultimately not of immortal material, and commanded him to return to the world, to cultivate his body and establish virtue, and that in the end he would marry and have children, to continue his family line. If his virtue and deeds were not abandoned, after death he could become a ghost immortal. Originally, Master Luo had once given Xiangpu a white silk handkerchief, about five feet square, which could be folded down to a small size and placed inside the ear. When in danger, it could be brought out, and then clouds and mist would gather around, making it impossible for those across to see, and it was called the "Cloud Handkerchief." When the battle failed, it was only through the power of the Cloud Handkerchief that Xiangpu alone escaped with his life. When the Real Person sent Xiangpu out of the cave, he requested the return of the Cloud Handkerchief, saying, "In times of peace, there is no need for this item. What use would it be for you to keep it?" Xiangpu was originally named Xiao Yun, but later changed his name to Chao Yun. He returned to Yue and took refuge in the mountains of Kuaiji, after which his fate is unknown. This story was told to me by his cousin Chen Xitang.

    755

    小喜子
    　　
    　　予庚午秋试，闱中遇崇川某生，谈其乡方伯某公事甚详。初，粤寇蔓延，时某公藉团练名，勒捐敛财，鱼肉乡里，道路侧目。会制军某移节总督两江，某固某公门下士，每有所求，无不阿唯曲从。某公势益张，无恶不作。城有大佛寺，气象庄严，某公居然于此设立公廨。号令所出，顺之者吉，逆之者凶；生杀予夺，气焰逼人，真炙手可热也。尤溺于色，每日到寺中，必召妓者二十人伺应服役。有小喜子者，色尤艳。闻富翁子宗某素与小喜子善，怒甚，立拘宗某至。命跪墀下，厉声叱曰：“当此军务孔急，汝家有财，但狎妓而不助饷，何耶！”宗股栗唯唯，不敢置辨。某公叱令速捐万金赎罪。宗求少减，某公怒叱，笞四十。众荐绅佥为缓颊，卒捐如数乃已。

    Explanation

    755

    In the year of Gengwu, I took the autumn examination. Inside the examination hall, I met a student from Chongchuan who gave me a detailed account of a high-ranking official from his hometown. Initially, the Taiping rebels were spreading throughout the region. At that time, the official used the name of the local militia to forcibly collect donations and wealth, oppressing and exploiting the people of his own village and region. People looked askance at him out of fear and resentment. Later, a military governor moved to take up the position of governor-general of the two Jiang provinces. The official was a subordinate of the military governor and always agreed and complied in a servile manner whenever asked. As a result, the official's power grew even stronger, and he committed all kinds of evil deeds. In the city, there was a large and majestic Buddhist temple. The official竟然在这里设立了公廨。他的命令一出，顺从的人会得到好运，而违抗的人则会遭遇不幸；他生杀予夺，气势逼人，真是炙手可热。他尤其沉溺于色欲，每天到寺庙中，必定召集二十名妓女伺候他。其中有一位名叫小喜子的女子，容貌特别艳丽。听说一位富翁的儿子宗某平时与小喜子关系很好，这位官员非常愤怒，立即把宗某抓来。命令他跪在台阶下，厉声斥责道：“现在军务紧急，你家有钱，却只顾与妓女厮混而不助饷，这是为什么！”宗某吓得浑身发抖，唯唯诺诺，不敢辩解。官员命令他迅速捐出一万两银子赎罪。宗某请求减少一些，官员怒斥他，并打了他四十下。众位绅士们集体为他求情，最终宗某捐出了全部金额才得以解脱。

    756

    又风人言小喜子家窝藏奸宄，乃籍没其家，将小喜子交官遣配，人知为某公所眷，无敢问名。既某公召其父母至，勒令署券，为己簉室，并声叙得身价千两，其实不名一钱也。制军某既伏诛，某公亦寻为当道所劾，谓“声名狼藉，众怨沸腾”，洵实录也。某公失势闲居，姬媵甚多，争妍斗宠，小喜子独擅专房，嗾其挟重资与居别院。某公时年逾八十，乃进春药，朝夕媚之。某公一夜忽头晕苦渴，小喜子拂褥展衾，慰令假寐。自托烹茗以进，某公儽然睡熟。昧爽，呼小喜子不答，自力疾起察之，已席卷重资与所狎娈童，从后门趁海艘遁矣。某公搔首仰屋长叹，复以手捶胸自责曰：“何苦！何苦！”卧床郁郁，咯血数斗而卒。诸姬媵亦各挟所蓄，风流云散矣。

    Explanation

    756

    Rumors spread that Xiao Xizi's family was harboring criminals, leading to the confiscation of her family's property and her being handed over to the authorities for exile. People knew that a certain official favored her, but dared not ask her name. The official summoned her parents and forced them to sign a contract, making her his secondary wife. He claimed to have received a dowry of a thousand taels, but in reality, he had not a single coin. General Z was executed, and the official was soon impeached by the authorities, who accused him of having a "tattered reputation" and "boiling public resentment." This was indeed a truthful account. The official lost his power and retired, surrounded by many concubines who competed for beauty and favor. Xiao Xizi alone dominated the household and was encouraged to take a large sum of money and live in a separate building. The official, now over eighty years old, gave her spring medicine to flatter her day and night. One night, he suddenly felt dizzy and thirsty. Xiao Xizi smoothed the bedding and comforted him, urging him to rest. She claimed to be preparing tea for him, and he fell asleep. At dawn, when he called for Xiao Xizi, she did not respond. Struggling to get up, he investigated and found that she had already taken the money and her favorite boy, fleeing by sea from the back door. The official scratched his head, looked up at the roof, and sighed deeply. He struck his chest with his hand and blamed himself, saying, "What a pity! What a pity!" Lying in bed in depression, he coughed up several dou of blood and died. The other concubines also took their possessions, and they dispersed like clouds and flowing water.

    757

    里乘子曰：某公逐逐一生，无恶不作。所敛积之财，尽为小喜子席卷而去。当嗾其与居别院时，固已早具深心，惜老奴愦愦，为色所迷，堕其术中而不察耳。临卒捶胸自责“何苦何苦”，千古小人撒手时，大抵如是。闻其子孙今已式微，里人多揶揄之，良可浩叹！货悖而入者，亦悖而出，其某公之谓欤？

    Explanation

    757

    The man from Li Cheng said: "This certain official lived his life in constant pursuit of gain, committing every conceivable evil. The wealth he had accumulated was all swept away by the little thief. When he was induced to live in a separate courtyard, he had already prepared deeply in his heart, but alas, the old servant was so dull-witted, bewitched by beauty, and fell into his trap without realizing it. At the moment of death, he beat his chest in self-reproach, saying, 'Why, why?' When a villain lets go of life, generally speaking, it is like this. It is said that his descendants have now declined, and the people of the village often mock them, truly worthy of a long sigh! What comes in by wrong means will also go out by wrong means—this is surely what is meant by the certain official."

    758

    某甲
    　　
    　　某生又云，方伯有犹子某甲，素无赖，为乡党所不齿。方伯既办团练，乃倚之为左右手，相助为虐。麾下爪牙鹰犬，推巨擘焉。方伯渔于色，甲尤而效之。尝染恶创，竟体溃烂，剜劓其鼻。有某道士者走无常，见甲在阴司碓磨刀锯鼎镬，叠受惨刑，目不忍睹。或闻以告甲，乃不自悛悔，而反大怒，谓道士捏言咒己，将援妖言惑众律，斩道土以警其余。晨起，舌忽腐断堕地，口瘖不能传令，道士幸免于难。甲越日寻卒，身无完肤，臭不可迩。家不能停其柩，急舁出葬，臭血溢出棺缝，沿途滴沥，路人皆为掩鼻。或谓甲虽死，在阴司犹不免叠受惨刑。足见道士之言之不诬也。

    Explanation

    758

    A Certain Nephew

    A certain student further reported that the provincial governor had a nephew named Mr. A, who was always a troublemaker and despised by the local community. After organizing the local militia, the governor relied on him as a trusted assistant, and together they committed abuses. Among the governor's henchmen, Mr. A was considered the leading figure. The governor indulged in lust, and Mr. A emulated this behavior. Once, he contracted a severe wound, and his entire body became ulcerated, with his nose cut off. A Taoist priest, who traveled as a spirit, saw Mr. A suffering in the underworld, being ground, sawed, and boiled in cauldrons, enduring multiple tortures that were unbearable to look at. Someone heard about this and told Mr. A, but he did not repent. Instead, he became very angry, accusing the Taoist of lying and cursing him, and planned to use the law against spreading false rumors to kill the Taoist and warn others. In the morning, his tongue suddenly rotted and fell off, and he became mute, unable to give orders. The Taoist was fortunate to escape unharmed. Mr. A died the next day, his body completely disfigured, and the stench was unbearable. His family could not keep his coffin and quickly carried him out for burial. The stench and blood overflowed from the coffin, dripping along the way, and passersby all covered their noses. Some say that even after death, Mr. A still suffers multiple tortures in the underworld. This shows that the Taoist's words were not false.

    759

    里乘子曰：粤寇之乱，予间关转徙数万里，其历十三行省。所过郡邑，凡绅董之办团练捍卫乡里者，固不乏忠义之士；而藉团练名以鱼肉乡里者，亦往往有之。尝自楚入蜀，闻某县有某绅者，以挂吏议削职家居。其子甲乙等素倚父势，武断乡曲。会粤寇起，甲乙等乃籍名团练，科敛各乡输资募勇，为守城计。自将眷属并辎重细软等物预迁出城，远避僻壤，以为可万全无害矣。然后下令：合城老少妇孺人等，如敢私避出城者，杀无赦；如敢将食用等物悄运一针一芥出城者，杀无赦。先是，明季献贼出入楚、蜀，某邑弹丸，幸免失守。

    Explanation

    759

    The Master Li Cheng said: During the turmoil of the Taiping Rebellion, I traveled back and forth and moved several ten thousand miles, traversing thirteen provinces. In the places I passed through, there were indeed many loyal and righteous men among the local gentry and officials who organized militias to defend their hometowns; however, there were also those who used the name of the militia to oppress the people. Once, traveling from Hubei to Sichuan, I heard that in a certain county there was a local official who had been stripped of his position due to a court decision and was now living at home. His sons, named Jia and Yi, had always relied on their father's influence to bully the people. When the Taiping Rebellion broke out, Jia and Yi then registered under the name of the militia, levied taxes from various villages to raise funds and recruit brave men, and planned to defend the city. They moved their families and valuable belongings out of the city in advance, fleeing to remote areas, believing they could remain completely safe and harmless. Then they issued orders: all men, women, and children in the city, if anyone dares to secretly leave the city, they will be killed without mercy; if anyone dares to secretly transport food or other items out of the city, even the smallest amount, they will be killed without mercy. Before this, during the late Ming dynasty, when Zhang Xianzhong's rebels operated in Hubei and Sichuan, a small town managed to avoid capture.

    760

    甲乙等窃计献贼强暴尚不足虑，粤寇复何能为？又以自己眷属已俱出城远避，更可无虞。故敛重资而不肯募勇。亡何，谍者叠报贼至，甲乙等斥其造言惑众，杖之。贼至愈近，后谍者闻前谍者均受杖，遂箝口而遁。贼至，甲乙等弃城而逃。城陷，屠戮殆尽。贼询知甲乙等眷属辇重资远避僻壤，穷搜得之；杀劫淫虏，受祸尤惨，一家靡有孑遗矣！嗟乎！如方伯某公叔侄，报应显然，固见天道之不爽。然闻其尚能不惜出资募勇自卫，及身幸免于难；若某绅之子甲乙等，但敛资而不募勇，贻害桑梓，并灾及身家，弃城而逃，受祸尤惨。以某公较之，不犹彼胜于此欤！

    Explanation

    760

    A, B, and others secretly calculated that the violence of Xianzhong's rebels was not worth worrying about, and what could the rebels from Guangdong do again? Moreover, since their family members had all left the city to hide in remote areas, they felt even more secure. Therefore, they gathered heavy wealth but were unwilling to recruit brave men. Soon after, spies repeatedly reported that the rebels were approaching, but A, B, and others scolded them for spreading false rumors to confuse the masses and beat them. As the rebels drew closer, subsequent spies, hearing that the previous spies had all been beaten, clamped their mouths shut and fled. When the rebels arrived, A, B, and others abandoned the city and fled. The city fell, and almost everyone was slaughtered. The rebels learned that A, B, and others' family members had carried heavy wealth to hide in remote areas, and they searched thoroughly and found them; they killed, looted, raped, and abducted, and the family suffered a particularly cruel fate, with not a single member surviving! Alas! As for the official and his nephew, the retribution was clear, showing the unerring nature of heaven's justice. However, it is said that the official was still able to spare no expense to recruit brave men for self-defense and managed to escape unharmed. But as for the sons of a certain gentleman, A and B, they merely gathered wealth without recruiting brave men, bringing harm to their hometown and disaster to their families, abandoning the city and fleeing, and suffering a particularly cruel fate. Comparing the official to them, isn't he better off than they are?

    761

    朱封翁
    　　
    　　朱莲塘封翁，讳宗潘，吾皖泾县人，世居东乡黄田村。少业儒，以家中落，改而服贾，累资百万，好行其德。生平所作善事，更仆难数。性至孝，居父丧，恸几绝。咸丰六年，粤寇犯泾县，村人纷纷窜避。翁时奉母家居，母年九十有六，艰于步履。翁闻警，恐母惊悸，秘不敢告，吁天而泣，泪皆成血。贼竟他去，母得无恙，寻以无病而卒。翁年已七十，擗踊号恸，如孺子然。越岁，贼再至，村中劫掠一空。佥谓前次幸免于难，实翁孝心所感也。翁卒之前夕，村人夜行，见舆马羽葆，灯火纷繁，驰拥卤簿往村中去。试叩从役，谓：“特来迎朱封翁者。”讶之。诘朝，往探翁，适于平明考终。始悟为阴曹仪仗也。翁享寿八十有一，以季子子典观察守谟贵，累膺一品封典，诚异数也。

    Explanation

    761

    Mr. Zongpan, the Honored Old Man of Zhu Liantang

    Mr. Zongpan, the Honored Old Man of Zhu Liantang, whose given name was Zongpan, was a native of Jing County in Anhui Province, and his family had lived in the eastern village of Huangtian for generations. When he was young, he studied Confucianism, but because his family's fortunes declined, he changed his career and engaged in commerce, accumulating a fortune of millions. He was known for his kindness and virtue, performing countless good deeds throughout his life. He was extremely filial, and when his father died, he wept bitterly, almost dying from grief.

    In the sixth year of the Xianfeng era (1856), rebels from Guangdong invaded Jing County, and the villagers fled in panic. At the time, Mr. Zongpan was at home with his mother, who was ninety-six years old and had difficulty walking. Upon hearing the alarm, he was afraid that his mother would be frightened and dared not inform her. He called upon heaven and wept, and his tears turned to blood. The rebels ultimately left elsewhere, and his mother remained unharmed. Soon after, she died peacefully without illness. Mr. Zongpan, who was already seventy years old, beat his breast and wailed like a child.

    The following year, the rebels returned, and the village was raided and looted completely. Everyone believed that the first invasion had spared them due to Mr. Zongpan's filial piety. On the night before Mr. Zongpan's death, the villagers saw carriages, horses, and feathered canopies, with numerous and varied lights, rushing to the village in an imperial procession. They tried to ask the servants, who said, "They came especially to welcome Mr. Zongpan." They were surprised by this. The next morning, they went to visit Mr. Zongpan, who died peacefully at dawn. Only then did they realize that it was an imperial procession from the underworld.

    Mr. Zongpan lived to be eighty-one years old. Because his youngest son, Zidian, who held the title of governor, was of high rank, he was repeatedly granted the honor of a first-rank imperial decree. This was indeed an extraordinary event.

    762

    里乘子曰：咸丰癸丑，粤寇下窜时，予本生先妣史太恭人卧病在床。村中警报一日数至，不孝兄弟相对皇皇，无所为计。而贼竟末至。及先妣见背后，贼始至村。老屋新居，悉成灰烬。既贼蚁聚吾皖，四乡枯柩，贼或舁以筑营，或劈以为薪，白骨狼藉于道。先妣殡后，卜兆以无吉壤，暂为权厝。癸亥冬，有数贼窜至厝室，将燃棺木为御寒计，甫欲举火，而大兵猝至，贼弃而逃。而棺之前和燃灼之迹尚存，睹之不胜悚恸。谓非先妣灵爽所自为呵护耶？观封翁之孝之善，足征天道之可凭。谁非人子，能无对而生愧乎！至先妣生平懿行，前于《滦州夜话》时已略述之，兹不再赘。

    Explanation

    762

    Lichengzi said: In the year Xianfeng GuiChou (1853), when the Yueguo rebels were advancing, my mother, Lady Shi, was ill in bed. Warnings came several times a day to the village, and my ungrateful brothers were in a state of panic, having no plan to make. Yet, the bandits finally did not arrive. After my mother passed away, the bandits began to gather in our village. Both the old house and the new residence were completely destroyed. Once the bandits had gathered in our Anhui province, they carried the unburied coffins from the four villages to build their camps, or split them to use as firewood, leaving white bones scattered on the roads. After my mother's funeral, we divined and found no auspicious land, so we temporarily placed her in a provisional tomb. In the winter of GuiHai, several bandits broke into the tomb, intending to burn the coffin to keep warm. Just as they were about to light the fire, the imperial army suddenly arrived, and the bandits fled. The traces of burning and scorching on the front of the coffin still remained, and seeing this, we could not bear the shock and sorrow. Is it not said that it was the spirit of the deceased mother who protected herself? Seeing the filial piety and virtue of the deceased father, it is a sign that the way of heaven can be trusted. Who is not a son, can they not feel ashamed when facing this? As for the virtuous deeds of the deceased mother throughout her life, they were briefly mentioned in The Night Talk of Luanzhou; I will not repeat them here.
